revision.text:
{{short description|Body variation}} {{Summary style|date=August 2025}} {{Split|List of giant squid specimens and sightings|List of colossal squid specimens and sightings|Cephalopod anatomy|date=August 2025|reason=the article is very long (360k+ bytes); some sections could just be accommodated within their respective species pages, with this page used to discuss the methods for cephalopod measurement and a list of sizes}} {{Cleanup images|date=August 2025}} [[File:Giant squid Ranheim.jpg|thumb|The [[giant squid]] (''Architeuthis dux'', pictured) was for a long time thought to be the largest extant cephalopod. It is now known that the [[colossal squid]] (''Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni'') attains an even greater maximum size. The giant squid seen here measured {{convert|9.24|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length and had a [[Mantle (mollusc)|mantle]] length of {{convert|1.79|m|ft|abbr=on}}.]] [[Cephalopod]]s, which include squids and octopuses, vary enormously in size. The smallest are only about {{convert|1|cm|in}} long and weigh less than {{convert|1|g|oz}} at maturity, while the [[giant squid]] can exceed {{convert|10|m|ft}} in length and the [[colossal squid]] weighs close to half a tonne ({{convert|0.5|tonne|lb|disp=output only}}), making them the largest living [[invertebrate]]s. Living species range in mass more than three-billion-fold,{{refn|group=nb|name=massrange|The heaviest scientifically validated cephalopod specimen was a [[colossal squid]] (''Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni'') weighing {{convert|495|kg|lb|abbr=on}},[[#Rosaetal2017|Rosa ''et al.'', 2017:1871]] while hatchlings of ''[[Illex illecebrosus]]''—some of the smallest known—have a mass of around {{convert|0.00015|g|oz|abbr=on}}.[[#WoodODor2000|Wood & O'Dor, 2000:93]] The mass ratio between the two is \left ( \frac{495,000}{0.00015} \right ) = 3,300,000,000.
Comparing only adult females, the difference between the colossal squid and the pygmy squid ''[[Idiosepius thailandicus]]''—which weighs {{convert|0.20|g|oz|abbr=on}} at maturity[[#Nabhitabhata1998|Nabhitabhata, 1998:28]]—would be \left ( \frac{495,000}{0.2} \right ) = 2,475,000.}} or across nine [[orders of magnitude]], from the lightest hatchlings to the heaviest adults.[[#ODorHoar2000|O'Dor & Hoar, 2000:8]] Certain cephalopod species are also noted for having [[#Anatomical superlatives|individual body parts of exceptional size]].[[#Nilssonetal2012|Nilsson ''et al.'', 2012:683]] Cephalopods were at one time the [[largest organism|largest of all organisms]] on Earth,[[#Smithetal2016|Smith ''et al.'', 2016]] and numerous species of comparable size to the largest present day squids are known from the [[fossil record]], including enormous examples of [[ammonoid]]s, [[belemnoid]]s, [[nautiloid]]s, [[orthoceratoid]]s, [[teuthid]]s, and [[vampyromorphid]]s. In terms of mass, the largest of all known cephalopods were likely the giant shelled ammonoids and [[endocerid]] nautiloids,[[#TeichertKummel1960|Teichert & Kummel, 1960:6]] though perhaps still second to the largest living cephalopods when considering [[Tissue (biology)|tissue mass]] alone.[[#Vermeij2016|Vermeij, 2016]] Cephalopods vastly larger than either giant or colossal squids have been postulated at various times. One of these was the [[St. Augustine Monster]], a large carcass weighing several tonnes that washed ashore on the [[United States]] coast near [[St. Augustine, Florida|St. Augustine]], [[Florida]], in 1896. Reanalyses in 1995 and 2004 of the original tissue samples—together with those of [[globster|other similar carcasses]]—showed conclusively that they were all masses of the [[collagen]]ous matrix of whale [[blubber]].[[#Pierceetal1995|Pierce ''et al.'', 1995]]; [[#Pierceetal2004|2004]] Giant cephalopods have fascinated humankind for ages. The earliest surviving records are perhaps those of [[Aristotle]] and [[Pliny the Elder]], both of whom described squids of very large size.[[#Gerhardt1966|Gerhardt, 1966:171]]; [[#Muntz1995|Muntz, 1995]]; [[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:11]] Tales of giant squid have been common among mariners since ancient times, and may have inspired the monstrous [[kraken]] of [[Nordic legend]], said to be as large as an island and capable of engulfing and sinking any ship.[[#SalvadorTomotani2014|Salvador & Tomotani, 2014]] Similar tentacled [[sea monster]]s are known from other parts of the globe, including the [[Akkorokamui]] of [[Japan]] and [[Te Wheke-a-Muturangi]] of [[New Zealand]]. The [[Lusca]] of the [[Caribbean]] and [[Scylla]] in [[Greek mythology]] may also derive from giant squid sightings,[[#Ley1941|Ley, 1941]] as might eyewitness accounts of other sea monsters such as [[sea serpent]]s.[[#Lee1883|Lee, 1883]]; [[#Ellis1994b|Ellis, 1994b]] Cephalopods of enormous size [[Giant squid in popular culture|have featured prominently in fiction]].see [[#GarcinRaynal2011|Garcin & Raynal, 2011]]; [[#Barrere2017|Barrère, 2017]] Some of the best known examples include the giant squid from [[Jules Verne]]'s 1870 novel ''[[Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Seas]]'' and [[Adaptations of Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Seas|its various film adaptations]]; the giant octopus from the 1955 [[monster movie]] ''[[It Came from Beneath the Sea]]''; and the giant squid from [[Peter Benchley]]'s 1991 novel ''[[Beast (Benchley novel)|Beast]]'' and the [[The Beast (1996 film)|TV film adaptation of the same name]]. Due to its status as a [[charismatic megafauna]]l species, the giant squid has been proposed as an emblematic animal for [[marine conservation|marine invertebrate conservation]].[[#Guerraetal2011|Guerra ''et al.'', 2011]] Life-sized models of the giant squid are a common sight in [[natural history museum]]s around the world,[[#Tratz1973|Tratz, 1973]]; [[#Ellis1997a|Ellis, 1997a]], [[#Ellis1997b|b]] and preserved specimens are much sought after for display.[[#LandmanEllis1998|Landman & Ellis, 1998]]; [[#Ablett2012|Ablett, 2012]]{{TOC limit|4}} ==Size in teuthology== [[File:Standard measurements for cephalopods.jpg|thumb|500px|Standard measurements for cephalopods with a squid and octopus as examples]] ===Mantle length=== [[File:Squid mantle width.jpg|thumb|255px|Scientists measuring the mantle width of a large female giant squid of {{circa}} {{convert|2|m|ft|abbr=on}} ML]] Mantle length (ML) is the standard size measure for [[Coleoidea|coleoid]] cephalopods ([[#Shell diameter|shell diameter]] being more common for [[nautilus]]es) and is almost universally reported in the scientific literature. The [[mantle (mollusc)|mantle]] is the cephalopod's "body", lying posterior to the head and enclosing the [[viscera]]l mass and [[mantle cavity]], the latter being used for locomotion by [[jet propulsion]]. Unless otherwise indicated, mantle length is measured [[Dorsum (anatomy)|dorsally]] over the midline of the mantle (sometimes specified as ''dorsal mantle length'', DML). It is a straight-line measure, not measured over the curve of the body. In [[Decapodiformes]] (ten-limbed cephalopods), mantle length is measured from the anterior edge of the mantle (near the head), to the posterior end of the mantle or the apex of the united [[cephalopod fin|fins]], whichever is longer. In [[Octopodiformes]] (eight-limbed cephalopods), the anterior edge of the mantle is not clearly delimited dorsally due to advanced head–mantle fusion, and mantle length is therefore taken from the midpoint between the eyes to the posterior end of the mantle. When ''ventral'' mantle length is meant instead of dorsal this is always specified as such and abbreviated VML.[[#RoperVoss1983|Roper & Voss, 1983:58]] As an indication of overall size, mantle length is generally considered more reliable than total length because [[cephalopod limb]]s may easily be stretched beyond their natural length and are often damaged or missing in preserved specimens (this is particularly true of the long [[cephalopod limb|tentacles]] of many squid species).[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:106]]; [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas, 2004:62]] Nevertheless, mantle length is not equally applicable to all species. Certain [[benthic]] octopuses such as ''[[Callistoctopus ornatus]]'' are able to elongate and retract their mantles and therefore mantle length measurements, even when taken from a live specimen, may vary considerably. Another problematic case is that of the gelatinous [[Cirroteuthidae|cirroteuthids]], whose weakly muscled mantles are prone to substantial shrinkage during preservation. The interocular distance may be a more reliable standard for this group.[[#RoperVoss1983|Roper & Voss, 1983:55]] ===Total length=== Total length (TL) is measured along the [[Dorsum (anatomy)|dorsal]] midline with the [[cephalopod limb|limbs]] outstretched and in line with the body axis. It is the greatest measurable extent of a specimen from the posterior end of the mantle or [[cephalopod fin|fins]] (or [[cephalopod tail|tail]], if presentsee [[#Arkhipkinetal2015|Arkhipkin ''et al.'', 2015]]) to the apex of the longest limb. It is recommended that arms and tentacles be measured in a relaxed state so as not to exaggerate their length, but historically this practice was not always followed and some of the more extreme published [[giant squid]] measurements have been attributed to artificial lengthening of the tentacles.[[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]] Although total length is often mentioned in relation to the largest cephalopod species, it is otherwise seldom used in teuthology.[[#RoperYoung1972|Roper & Young, 1972:205]] As with mantle length, it is a straight-line measure. Total length is not to be confused with arm span—also known as ''arm spread'', ''radial span'', or ''radial spread''—which may be much larger and is often reported for octopuses (for which the arms usually constitute the vast majority of the length). In squids, total length is inclusive of the feeding tentacles, which in some species may be longer than the mantle, head, and arms combined ([[Chiroteuthidae|chiroteuthids]] such as ''[[Asperoteuthis acanthoderma]]'' being a prime example). A related measure is standard length (SL), which is the combined length of the mantle, head, and arms, excluding the often long feeding tentacles.[[#Clarke1996|Clarke, 1996:1105]]; [[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]] This measure is particularly useful for species such as the giant squid, where almost the entire bulk of the animal takes up less than half of its total length. ===Mass=== Mass (often abbreviated WT for 'weight') is reported far less frequently than either mantle or total length, and accurate records do not exist for all of the large cephalopod species. It can also vary widely depending on the state of the specimen at the time of weighing (for example, whether it was measured live or dead, wet or dry, frozen or thawed, pre- or post-fixation, with or without egg mass, and so on). [[File:Extracted giant squid beak.jpg|thumb|left|250px|Giant squid beak and associated muscles with hand for scale]] ===Methods of size determination=== In contrast to the vast majority of living cephalopods, which are wholly soft-bodied, size determination of the [[#Shell diameter|few surviving shelled species]] (in terms of shell diameter) is comparatively straightforward and can be accomplished with a high level of precision. Whatever the type of cephalopod, in the absence of whole specimens, size can often be estimated from only partial remains. For example, [[cephalopod beak]]s can be used for mantle length, total length and body mass estimation,see [[#Clarke1962|Clarke, 1962]]; [[#Wolff1981|Wolff, 1981]]; [[#Wolff1984|1984]]; [[#Grogeretal2000|Gröger ''et al.'', 2000]]; [[#Staudingeretal2009|Staudinger ''et al.'', 2009]]; [[#Potieretal2011|Potier ''et al.'', 2011]] and this method has notably been used to estimate the maximum size of the [[colossal squid]]. The [[lower rostral length]] (LRL) of the beak is often used for this purpose. The rostral length of the lower and upper beaks is the standard measure of beak size in [[Decapodiformes]]; [[hood length]] is preferred for [[Octopodiformes]].[[#Clarke1986|Clarke, 1986:11]] Mantle length has been estimated from video recordings of squid in the wild.[[#Zeidberg2004|Zeidberg, 2004:4195]]; [[#Phillips2004|Phillips, 2004:ii]] ==Early life stages== ===Hatchlings=== Hatchlings of ''[[Idiosepius thailandicus]]'', possibly the smallest extant cephalopod species at maturity, have a [[Mantle (mollusc)|mantle]] length of around {{convert|1|mm|in|abbr=on}}.[[#Nabhitabhata1998|Nabhitabhata, 1998:32]] The closely related ''[[Idiosepius pygmaeus]]'' weighs only {{convert|0.00033|g|oz|abbr=on}} upon hatching and increases in weight to {{convert|0.175|g|oz|abbr=on}} as it reaches maturity in 50 days. Even smaller are the hatchlings of the commercially important ''[[Illex illecebrosus]]'', with a mass of {{convert|0.00015|g|oz|abbr=on}}.[[#ODoretal1986|O'Dor ''et al.'', 1986:59]]; [[#WoodODor2000|Wood & O'Dor, 2000:93]] Hatchlings of the [[giant Pacific octopus]] (''Enteroctopus dofleini'')—one of the two largest octopus species—weigh {{convert|0.0253|g|oz|abbr=on}} on average.[[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:88]] At the other extreme are [[nautilus]]es, which upon hatching typically have a shell diameter of {{convert|25|mm|in|0|abbr=on}} or more (depending on the species), the largest hatchling size among extant invertebrates.[[#Grulke2014|Grulke, 2014:105]] Hatchlings of ''[[Nautilus belauensis]]'', one of the larger species, are estimated to weigh on the order of {{convert|5.9|g|oz|abbr=on}}{{refn|group=nb|name=hatchlings|[[#WoodODor2000|Wood & O'Dor (2000:93)]] elaborated on this mass estimate as follows:
There are [...] no published weights of hatchling ''[[Nautilus]]'' spp. The weight of a hatchling ''[[Nautilus belauensis|N. belauensis]]'' was estimated using hatchling shell size and a regression analysis of the cubed shell diameter versus the weight of seven young ''N. belauensis'' that weighed <50 g [{{convert|50|g|oz|disp=output only}}] [...] in addition to a single hatchling ''[[Nautilus pompilius|N. pompilius]]'' that was weighed for the present study on 24 April 1996 at the [[Waikiki Aquarium]]. The hatchling ''N. pompilius'' weighing 4.33 g [{{convert|4.33|g|oz|disp=output only}}], with a maximum shell diameter of 26.25 mm [{{convert|26.25|mm|in|disp=output only}}], fit a highly significant correlation [...] between cubed shell diameter and weight, which indicates that a hatchling ''N. belauensis'' with a 30 mm [{{convert|30|mm|in|disp=output only}}] shell diameter [...] would weigh approximately 5.9 g [{{convert|5.9|g|oz|disp=output only}}].
}} and mature at around {{convert|1.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}} after almost 4000 days, or around 11 years. ==Smallest adult size== {{Multiple image | image1 = Idiosepius paradoxus himeika06.JPG | caption1 = ''[[Idiosepius paradoxus]]'', a tiny species of squid that grows to {{convert|16|mm|in|abbr=on}} in mantle length | image2 = Tremoctopus violaceus5 (rotated).jpg | caption2 = Adult male ''[[Tremoctopus violaceus]]'' with large [[hectocotylus]] (arm modified for [[spermatophore]] transfer) | direction = vertical }} The smallest adult size among living cephalopods is attained by the so-called pygmy squids, ''[[Idiosepius]]'',{{cite web |url=https://reefs.com/2017/10/06/meet-worlds-smallest-weirdest-squid-idiosepius/ |title=Meet The World's Smallest & Weirdest Squid, ''Idiosepius'' |author=Rowlett, Joe |publisher=Reefs.com |access-date=19 January 2019|date=2017-10-06 }} and certain diminutive species of the genus ''[[Octopus (genus)|Octopus]]'', both of which weigh less than {{convert|1|g|oz}} at maturity.[[#Boletzky2003|Boletzky, 2003:19]] ''[[Idiosepius thailandicus]]'' is perhaps the smallest of all, with females averaging {{convert|10.4|mm|in|abbr=on}} in mantle length and males {{convert|5.9|mm|in|abbr=on}}. Average wet weights are around {{convert|0.20|and|0.02|g|oz|abbr=on}}, respectively. Other tiny species include members of the [[bobtail squid]] family [[Sepiolidae]]; the [[Myopsina|myopsid]] squid genera ''[[Australiteuthis]]'' and ''[[Pickfordiateuthis]]''; the [[Oegopsina|oegopsid]] squid genera ''[[Abralia]]'' and ''[[Abraliopsis]]''; the pygmy cuttlefish ''[[Sepia pulchra]]''; and the ram's horn squid, ''[[Spirula spirula]]''. ===Male dwarfism=== The octopod superfamily [[Argonautoidea]] is characterised by markedly dwarfed males.[[#Boletzky1999|Boletzky, 1999:24]]; [[#Boletzky2003|2003:20]]; [[#Normanetal2002|Norman ''et al.'', 2002:733]] The four extant genera of the group are ''[[Argonaut (animal)|Argonauta]]'', ''[[Haliphron]]'', ''[[Ocythoe]]'', and ''[[Tremoctopus]]'', all of which are exclusively [[pelagic]]. The greatest disparity in the size of the sexes is seen in the [[blanket octopus]]es of the genus ''Tremoctopus''. [[#Normanetal2002|Norman ''et al.'' (2002)]] reported a fully mature male ''[[Tremoctopus violaceus]]'' measuring {{convert|2.4|cm|in|abbr=on}} in total length and weighing a mere {{convert|0.25|g|oz|abbr=on}}. By comparison, the large females of this species reach total lengths of {{convert|2|m|ft|abbr=on}} and probably some {{convert|10|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in weight. This is the most extreme [[sexual size dimorphism]] known among non-microscopic animals,{{refn|group=nb|name=SSD|[[#Normanetal2002|Norman ''et al.'' (2002:733)]] wrote: "The most extreme examples of sexual size dimorphism come from marine or parasitic taxa where females are difficult to locate ([[#Ghiselin1974|Ghiselin 1974]])."}} with mature females being at least 10,000 times heavier than males, and likely up to 40,000 times heavier.[[#Normanetal2002|Norman ''et al.'', 2002:733]]; [[#Fairbairn2007|Fairbairn, 2007:3]] The related genera ''Argonauta'' and ''Ocythoe'' have similarly small males, but the females are not nearly as large as those of ''Tremoctopus'', and the size dimorphism is therefore less pronounced. The females of the fourth argonautoid genus, ''Haliphron'', are the largest of all up to length of {{convert|abbr=on|3.5|m}} and mass of {{convert|abbr=on|75|kg}} (and possibly the [[#Mass 2|largest octopuses of any kind]]), but the males are also much larger, at up to {{convert|30|cm|in|abbr=on}}. So, the size dimorphism is least pronounced of all.[[#Normanetal2002|Norman ''et al.'', 2002:733]] ===Extinct taxa=== Numerous species of so-called micromorphic ammonites are known.see [[#KennedyCobban1990|Kennedy & Cobban, 1990]] ''[[Maximites]]'' from the [[Upper Carboniferous]] is the smallest known ammonoid. Adult specimens reached only {{cvt|10|mm|in}} in shell diameter.{{cite web|author=Nishiguchi, M.K., R. Mapes|date=2008|url= https://bobtailsquid.ink/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/Nish2008_Book_PhylEvolMolusca_Cephlopoda.pdf|title=Cephalopoda|work=University of California Press|pages=163–199}} ==Maximum size== ===Scientifically validated records=== [[File:Colossal squid caught in February 2007.jpg|thumb|left|The largest cephalopod specimen ever recorded: a {{convert|495|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[colossal squid]]]] [[Squid]]s are the largest living cephalopods in terms of each of [[mantle (mollusc)|mantle]] length, total length, and mass, with the largest species by at least two of these measures being the [[colossal squid]], ''Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni''. Reaching an estimated {{convert|3|m|ft|abbr=on}} in mantle length and {{convert|10|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length, and weighing as much as {{convert|495|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, this species is also the largest of all [[extant taxon|extant]] [[invertebrate]]s.[[#RosaSeibel2010|Rosa & Seibel, 2010]]; [[#McClainetal2015|McClain ''et al.'', 2015]]; [[#Rosaetal2017|Rosa ''et al.'', 2017]] The only other squid that approaches these dimensions is the [[giant squid]] of the genus ''Architeuthis'', with females up to {{convert|275|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, {{convert|2.4|m|ft|abbr=on}} in mantle length, and possibly as much as {{convert|15|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length, making it likely the longest of all cephalopods.[[#McClainetal2015|McClain ''et al.'', 2015]] The two largest [[octopus]] species—''[[Enteroctopus dofleini]]'' and ''[[Haliphron atlanticus]]''—can both exceed {{convert|70|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, and the former has a maximum total length of more than {{convert|6|m|ft|abbr=on}}. [[Cirrina|Cirrate (finned) octopods]] can also reach a large size, with the largest captured specimen likely being a ''[[Cirrothauma magna]]'' of {{convert|1.7|m|ft|abbr=on}} total length and {{convert|33|cm|in|abbr=on}} mantle length,[[#Collinsetal2001|Collins ''et al.'', 2001]]; [[#CollinsVillanueva2006|Collins & Villanueva, 2006:294]] though observations from submersibles suggest that members of this group can exceed {{convert|4|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length.[[#Voss1988|Voss, 1988]]; [[#Vecchioneetal2008|Vecchione ''et al.'', 2008]] Members of the other cephalopod groups are substantially smaller, although the largest [[cuttlefish]] can exceed {{convert|10|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in weight and {{convert|50|cm|ft|abbr=on}} in mantle length. Cephalopods of comparable size to the largest present day squid are known from fossil remains, including enormous examples of [[ammonoid]]s, [[belemnoid]]s, [[nautiloid]]s, [[orthoceratoid]]s, [[teuthid]]s, and [[vampyromorphid]]s.see [[#TeichertKummel1960|Teichert & Kummel, 1960]]; [[#Stevens1988|Stevens, 1988]]; [[#Eyden2003b|Eyden, 2003b]]; [[#Larson2010|Larson, 2010]]; [[#Ibaetal2015|Iba ''et al.'', 2015]] ====Colossal squid (''Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni'')==== [[File:Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni beak.jpg|thumb|left|Beak of a colossal squid, which has the largest beak among living cephalopods]] Though a substantial number of [[colossal squid]] (''Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni'') [[List of colossal squid specimens and sightings|remains]] have been recorded ([[#Xavieretal1999|Xavier ''et al.'', 1999]] collated 188 geographical positions for whole or partial specimens caught by commercial and scientific fisheries), very few adult or subadult animals have ever been documented, making it difficult to estimate the maximum size of the species. [[#McClainetal2015|McClain ''et al.'' (2015)]] stated that only 12 "complete" specimens were known. The largest known complete specimen of the colossal squid was a mature female captured in the [[Ross Sea]] in February 2007. Its weight was initially estimated at {{convert|450|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, its mantle length at {{convert|4|m|ft|abbr=on}}, and its total length at {{convert|8|–|10|m|ft|abbr=on}}.[[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#howbig|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]] Once completely thawed the specimen was found to weigh {{convert|495|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, but to measure only {{convert|2.5|m|ft|abbr=on}} in mantle length and {{convert|4.2|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length.[[#howbig|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]] It is likely that the specimen, and particularly its tentacles, shrank considerably ''post mortem'' as a result of dehydration, having been kept in a freezer for 14 months. (As reported by the [[Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa]], specimens of ''[[Nototodarus sloanii]]'', the New Zealand arrow squid, can shrink by as much as 22% when dehydrated with alcohol solutions.) The colossal squid specimen contracted by a further 5% after several years in preservative fluid (first [[formalin]] and later [[propylene glycol]]).[[#Lovis2011|Lovis, 2011]] The fins of the 2007 Ross Sea specimen measured around {{convert|1.2|m|ft|abbr=on}} across and it had a mantle width of {{convert|98.2|cm|ft|abbr=on}}.[[#body|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]] The arms ranged in length from {{convert|0.85|to|1.15|m|ft|abbr=on}}, while the two tentacles were around {{convert|2.1|m|ft|abbr=on}} long.[[#limbs|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]] [[File:Colossal squid at Te Papa.jpg|thumb|The 2007 Ross Sea specimen as it appears now, on display at the [[Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa]]]] Beaks recovered from sperm whale stomachs indicate the existence of animals surpassing even the 2007 Ross Sea specimen. That specimen had a [[lower rostral length]] (LRL) of {{convert|42.5|mm|in|abbr=on}} and weighed {{convert|495|kg|lb|abbr=on}},[[#beak|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]] whereas the {{convert|300|kg|lb|abbr=on}} submature female from 2003 had a LRL of {{convert|37|mm|in|abbr=on}}.[[#OShea2003c|O'Shea, 2003c]] By comparison, the largest known colossal squid beak from a sperm whale stomach measured {{convert|49|mm|in|abbr=on}} in LRL. Though the number of large colossal squid specimens known to science is too small to get a good idea of the relationship between beak size and overall body size, a beak of such great size indicates a truly massive animal weighing perhaps as much as {{convert|600|–|700|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. However, the [[allometry|scaling relationship]] for this species shows considerable latitude, as demonstrated by a beak of {{convert|40|mm|in|abbr=on}} LRL extracted from an animal weighing only {{convert|160|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. ====Giant squid (''Architeuthis dux'')==== [[File:Logy bay giant squid 1873.png|thumb|left|A giant squid specimen; the extremely long feeding tentacles of the giant squid make it the longest known cephalopod, past or present.]] The maximum size of the [[giant squid]] (''Architeuthis dux'') has long been a subject of both popular debate and academic inquiry.[[#Ellis1998b|Ellis, 1998b]]; [[#Paxton2016a|Paxton, 2016a]]; [[#Bittel2016|Bittel, 2016]]; [[#Romanovetal2017|Romanov ''et al.'', 2017]] Unlike the colossal squid, the giant squid is known from a substantial number of mature specimens. The [[List of giant squid specimens and sightings|total number of recorded specimens]] (across all developmental stages) approaches a thousand, with approximately 700 documented {{as of|2015|lc=y}}, of which around 460 had been measured in some way.[[#Paxton2016a|Paxton, 2016a]] This number has since increased substantially, with 57 specimens recorded from Japanese waters during an exceptional 15-month period between 2014 and 2015.[[#Kuboderaetal2016|Kubodera ''et al.'', 2016]] [[File:Giant squid melb aquarium03.jpg|thumb|A giant squid measuring {{convert|7|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length and weighing {{convert|250|kg|lb|abbr=on}} displayed in a {{convert|3.5|tonne|lb|adj=on}} block of ice at the [[Melbourne Aquarium]]see [[#Holroyd2005|Holroyd, 2005]]]] Based on a 40-year data set of more than 50 giant squid specimens, [[#RoperShea2013|Roper & Shea (2013:114)]] suggest an average total length at maturity of {{convert|11|m|ft|abbr=on}} and a "rarely encountered maximum length" of {{convert|14|–|15|m|ft|abbr=on}}. Of the nearly 100 specimens examined by [[Clyde Roper]], the largest was "{{convert|46|ft|m}} long".[[#CerulloRoper2012|Cerullo & Roper, 2012:22]] [[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad (2008)]] give a maximum total length of {{convert|13|m|ft|abbr=on}} for females based on the examination of more than 130 specimens, measured ''post mortem'' and relaxed, as well as beaks recovered from [[sperm whale]]s (which do not exceed the size of those found in the largest complete specimens). [[Steve O'Shea]] estimated the maximum total length for males at {{convert|10|m|ft|abbr=on}}.[[#OShea2003a|O'Shea, 2003a]] Older records of {{convert|18|m|ft|abbr=on}} or more were likely exaggerated by stretching of the long [[cephalopod limb|feeding tentacles]] or resulted from inadequate measurement methods such as pacing.[[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#RoperShea2013|Roper & Shea, 2013:113]] O'Shea has stated that, given the available evidence, the highest [[upper bound]] he would consider plausible for the giant squid's total length would be {{convert|15|m|ft|abbr=on}}, and that the likelihood that there exist 20-metre giant squid is "so exceedingly remote that you couldn't justify the effort in writing about it".[[#Bittel2016|Bittel, 2016]] Including the head and [[cephalopod limb|arms]] but excluding the [[cephalopod limb|tentacles]] (standard length, SL), the species very rarely exceeds {{convert|5|m|ft|abbr=on}} according to [[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad (2008)]]. [[#Paxton2016a|Paxton (2016a)]] considers {{convert|9.45|m|ft|abbr=on}} to be the greatest reliably measured SL, based on a specimen reported by [[#Verrill1880a|Verrill (1880a:192)]], and considers specimens of {{convert|10|m|ft|abbr=on}} SL or more to be "very probable", but these conclusions have been criticised by giant squid experts.[[#Greshko2016|Greshko, 2016]] [[#OShea2003a|O'Shea (2003a)]] put the maximum weight of female giant squid at {{convert|275|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, based on the examination of some 105 specimens as well as beaks recovered from [[sperm whale]]s (which do not exceed the size of those found in the largest complete specimens). Giant squid are [[sexual dimorphism|sexually size dimorphic]], with the maximum weight for males estimated at {{convert|150|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, though heavier specimens have occasionally been reported (such as a {{convert|190|kg|lb|abbr=on}} specimen[[#Deagleetal2005|Deagle ''et al.'', 2005]] and a {{convert|163|kg|lb|abbr=on}} specimen[[#Hofilena2014|Hofilena, 2014]]). [[#RoperJereb2010a|Roper & Jereb (2010a:121)]] give a maximum weight of up to {{convert|500|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, and "possibly greater". Discredited weights of as much as a [[tonne]] ({{convert|1|tonne|lb|disp=output only}}) or more are not uncommon in older literature (see [[#Mass estimates|below]]). ====Other squid taxa==== [[File:Taningia danae (160 cm ML).jpg|thumb|Dana octopus squid]] The third-heaviest extant squid species is ''[[Taningia danae]]'', also known as the Dana octopus squid. The largest well documented specimen is a 160 cm ML mature female reported by [[#RoperVecchione1993|Roper & Vecchione (1993)]] from the North Atlantic. The original paper consistently gave the mass of this specimen as {{convert|61.4|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, but according to [[#RoperJereb2010h|Roper & Jereb (2010h:266)]] this figure is wrong and stems from a typographical error, the correct mass being {{convert|161.4|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. Another similarly large specimen—a female weighing {{convert|124|kg|lb|abbr=on}}—was reported from northern Spanish waters by [[#Gonzalezetal2003|González ''et al.'' (2003:297)]] (see also initial reports[[#Anonymous2000|[Anonymous], 2000]]; [[#Wong2000|Wong, 2000]]). In July 2010, a [[sperm whale]] was photographed off the [[Azores|Azorean]] island of [[Faial Island|Faial]] with a large squid—likely ''T. danae''—in its mouth. The specimen's maximum width, from fin tip to fin tip, was estimated at {{convert|1.5|–|2|m|ft|abbr=on}}; this would approximate its mantle length.[[#Vecchioneetal2010|Vecchione ''et al.'', 2010]] [[File:Moroteuthis robusta bronco.jpg|thumb|left|220px|''[[Onykia robusta]]'' grows to a mantle length of at least {{convert|2|m|ft|abbr=on}}]] ''[[Onykia robusta]]'', previously known as ''Moroteuthis robusta''see [[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#Bolstad2010|2010]] and sometimes called the robust clubhook squid, has a mantle length of up to {{convert|200|cm|ft|abbr=on}}.[[#Norman2000|Norman, 2000:174]]; [[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad, 2008:107]]; [[#Okutani2015b|Okutani, 2015b]] Some older records exceed this, such as the {{convert|91.5|in|cm|abbr=on}} ML reported by [[#Verrill1876|Verrill (1876:237)]] from a specimen with a total length of {{convert|14|ft|m|abbr=on}} (excluding the ends of the tentacles, which had been destroyed). [[#Nesis1987|Nesis (1987:192)]] likewise gave a maximum mantle length of {{convert|230|cm|ft|abbr=on}}, but [[#RoperJereb2010i|Roper & Jereb (2010i:364)]] wrote that "this old record might be in error", with the species commonly growing to {{convert|160|cm|ft|abbr=on}} ML. [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas (2004:66)]] provided a maximum total length of {{convert|4|–|6|m|ft|abbr=on}}. Literature sources give a maximum weight of {{convert|50|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.[[#Roperetal1984|Roper ''et al.'', 1984]]; [[#RoperJereb2010i|Roper & Jereb, 2010i:364]]; [[#Okutani2015b|Okutani, 2015b]] There exist numerous published records of large individuals of this species.see [[#Dall1873|Dall, 1873]]; [[#Verrill1876|Verrill, 1876]]; [[#Phillips1933|Phillips, 1933]]; [[#Croker1934|Croker, 1934]]; [[#Phillips1961|Phillips, 1961]]; [[#Smith1963|Smith, 1963]] [[File:Montemar calamar.png|thumb|Fisherman taking a [[Humboldt squid]] off the coast of [[Chile]]. This species is the largest squid species commonly encountered by humans, possessing a large fishery.[[#Hoff2003|Hoff, 2003:90]]]] The [[Humboldt squid]] (''Dosidicus gigas''), also known as the 'jumbo squid', grows to a maximum mantle length of at least {{convert|120|cm|ft|abbr=on}},[[#Nigmatullinetal2001|Nigmatullin ''et al.'', 2001]]; [[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'', 2010b:301]] if not {{convert|150|cm|ft|abbr=on}}.[[#Wormuth1976|Wormuth, 1976:38]]; [[#Norman2000|Norman, 2000:165]]; [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas, 2004:54]] The largest animals are found off the western coast of [[South America]]; northern populations reach {{convert|100|cm|ft|abbr=on}} ML, and in general {{convert|50|–|80|cm|ft|abbr=on}} ML is more typical for the species.[[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'', 2010b:301]] Southern populations may have a total length approaching {{convert|2.5|m|ft|abbr=on}}, and possibly up to {{convert|12|ft|m|order=flip|abbr=on}}.[[#Clarke1966|Clarke, 1966:117]]; [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas, 2004:59]] Again, specimens from the [[Northern Hemisphere]] are much smaller, with those off the [[California]]n coast reaching total lengths of less than {{convert|1.7|m|ft|abbr=on}}.[[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas, 2004:59]] The Humboldt squid commonly attains a weight of around {{convert|20|–|30|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and can reach a maximum of {{convert|50|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.[[#Nigmatullinetal2001|Nigmatullin ''et al.'', 2001:10]]; [[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'', 2010b:301]] There are anecdotal reports of much larger individual animals, including from diver [[Scott Cassell]], who has dived with Humboldt squid over 300 times (reportedly more than any other person).[[#Cassell2005|Cassell, 2005]]{{refn|group=nb|name=Scar|In particular, [[Scott Cassell]] mentioned a giant [[Humboldt squid]] he named 'Scar' (after the scars on its mantle), whose size he described thus:
Scar is over seven feet [{{convert|7|ft|m|abbr=on|disp=output only}}] in length, larger than anyone has ever proven. His body was 3 feet [{{convert|3|ft|m|abbr=on|sigfig=1|disp=output only}}] thick (He's too big to put my arms around him) and I estimated he weight {{sic}} between 230 and 250 pounds [{{convert|230|–|250|lb|kg|abbr=on|disp=output only}}].
}} ''[[Moroteuthopsis longimana]]'', previously ''Kondakovia longimana'' and sometimes known as the giant warty squid, is a little known species with a circum-[[Antarctic]] distribution in the [[Southern Ocean]]. The largest complete specimen, found floating at the surface off the [[South Orkney Islands]], had a mantle length of {{convert|108|cm|ft|abbr=on}},[[#LynnesRodhouse2002|Lynnes & Rodhouse, 2002:1087]]; see also [[#Carrington2000|Carrington, 2000]] but a damaged female specimen with an estimated mantle length of around {{convert|150|cm|ft|abbr=on}} is known.[[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad, 2008:171]] The largest complete specimen had a wet weight of {{convert|29|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. The species' maximum weight has been estimated at {{convert|50|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.[[#GuerraSegonzac2014|Guerra & Segonzac, 2014:65]] ====Largest octopodes==== [[File:Enteroctopus dolfeini.jpg|thumb|left|The giant Pacific octopus is one of the two largest octopus species]] The [[giant Pacific octopus]] (''Enteroctopus dofleini'') grows to more than {{convert|6.1|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length[[#Cosgrove1987|Cosgrove, 1987]] and at least {{convert|60|cm|ft|abbr=on}} in mantle length.[[#Norman2000|Norman, 2000:214]]; [[#Normanetal2014|Norman ''et al.'', 2014:124]] [[#Cosgrove1987|Cosgrove (1987)]] and [[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel (2009:69)]] gave a maximum confirmed weight of {{convert|71|kg|lb|abbr=on}} for a live specimen collected in the mid-1960s. [[#Normanetal2014|Norman ''et al.'' (2014:124)]] accept a maximum weight of at least {{convert|180|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, which approximates the {{convert|182.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}} reported for a specimen caught off [[Santa Barbara, California|Santa Barbara]], [[California]], in 1945, of which photographic evidence survives.[[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:67–69]] No specimens approaching this size have been reported since the middle of the 20th century, with recent specimens very rarely exceeding {{convert|50|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.[[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:71]] It is possible that the maximum size of the species has decreased over this period, perhaps due to [[bioaccumulation]] of [[toxicant]]s (see [[#Possible diminution in size|below]]).[[#Anderson2003|Anderson, 2003:3]]; [[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:71]]; [[#Yong2015|Yong, 2015]] In 2002, a giant specimen of ''[[Haliphron atlanticus]]'', the seven-arm octopus, was caught by fishermen trawling at a depth of {{convert|920|m|ft|abbr=on}} off the eastern [[Chatham Rise]], [[New Zealand]]. This specimen, the largest of this species and possibly of all octopuses, was the first validated record of ''Haliphron'' from the [[South Pacific Ocean|South Pacific]]. It had a mantle length of {{convert|0.69|m|ft|abbr=on}}, a total length of {{convert|2.90|m|ft|abbr=on}}, and a weight of {{convert|61.0|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, although it was incomplete.[[#OShea2002|O'Shea, 2002:1]]; [[#OShea2004a|2004a:9]]; [[#Finn2014a|Finn, 2014a:227]] The total length of the specimen, when complete, has been estimated at {{convert|4|m|ft|abbr=on}}, and its weight at {{convert|75|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.[[#OShea2004a|O'Shea, 2004a:9]] ===Extinct taxa=== [[File:Parapuzosia seppenradensis (Plate II).jpg|thumb|The largest known specimen of ''[[Parapuzosia seppenradensis]]'', with its describing author, [[Hermann Landois]], who reconstructed its missing living chamber with a combination of wire frame and paper.[[#Landois1895|Landois, 1895:108]] Landois assumed that the living chamber constituted one-fourth of the outer [[whorl (mollusc)|whorl]], but [[#TeichertKummel1960|Teichert & Kummel (1960:6)]] estimated that it continued for three-fourths to one full whorl, which would give an original shell diameter of around {{convert|3.5|m|ft|abbr=on}}.]] Certain extinct cephalopods rivalled or even exceeded the size of the largest living species.[[#Carnall2017|Carnall, 2017]] In particular, the subclass [[Ammonoidea]] is known to have included a considerable number of species that may be considered "giant" (defined by [[#Stevens1988|Stevens, 1988]] as those exceeding {{convert|1|m|ft|abbr=on}} in shell diameter). The largest confirmed [[ammonite]], a specimen of ''[[Parapuzosia seppenradensis]]'' discovered in a [[Germany|German]] quarry in 1895, measures {{convert|1.742|m|ft|abbr=on}} in diameter,[[#KennedyKaplan1995|Kennedy & Kaplan, 1995:21]] though its [[living chamber]] is largely missing. The diameter of the complete shell has been estimated at {{convert|2.55|m|ft|abbr=on}}, assuming the living chamber took up one-fourth of the outer [[whorl (mollusc)|whorl]].[[#Landois1895|Landois, 1895:100]] [[#TeichertKummel1960|Teichert & Kummel (1960:6)]] suggested an even larger original shell diameter of around {{convert|3.5|m|ft|abbr=on}} for this specimen, assuming the body chamber extended for three-fourths to one full whorl. In 1971 a portion of an ammonite possibly surpassing this specimen was reportedly found in a [[brickyard]] in [[Bottrop]], western Germany.[[#Beer2015|Beer, 2015]] A specimen found by Jim Rockwood, from the [[Late Triassic]] near [[Williston Lake]], [[British Columbia]], was said to measure more than {{convert|8|ft|m|abbr=on}} across, but was later determined to be a [[concretion]].[[#concretion|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]]; [[#Anonymous2008|[Anonymous], 2008]] [[File:Enchoteuthis reconstruction.png|thumb|left|Reconstructions of the [[holotype]] (FHSM 13049) and largest specimen (NDGS 241) of ''[[Enchoteuthis melanae]]'', based on ''[[Muensterella]]'' and ''[[Dosidicus]]'']] [[Heteromorph ammonite]]s are known to have exceeded {{convert|1|m|ft|abbr=on}} in length also, but since their shells were uncoiled to varying degrees, they were overall much smaller than the largest non-heteromorphs. The greatest lengths of all were reached by the [[orthocone]]s of [[Endocerida|endocerid]] [[nautiloid]]s such as ''[[Endoceras]]'', which may have exceeded {{convert|8|m|ft|abbr=on}}, although their maximum size is uncertain; while the largest well documented endocerid fossil is likely the 3-metre-long ({{convert|3|m|ft|disp=output only}}) shell fragment housed at the [[Museum of Comparative Zoology]], [[Harvard University]], there are published reports of even larger specimens. [[#Teichert1927|Teichert (1927)]] mentioned specimens up to {{convert|5|m|ft|abbr=on}} long from the [[Middle Ordovician]] [[limestone]] of [[Estonia]][[#TeichertKummel1960|Teichert & Kummel, 1960:2]] and [[#Frey1995|Frey (1995:72)]] gave a maximum shell length of {{convert|6|m|ft|abbr=on}} for the group. On the subject of endocerid size, nautiloid specialist [[Rousseau H. Flower]] wrote:
They are not all large, by any means, but specimens twelve feet [{{convert|12|ft|m|disp=output only}}] in length have been collected, and fragments of greater diameter indicate a much greater maximum length. I am not wholly inclined to discredit a report of an endoceroid found in a quarry near [[Watertown, New York|Watertown New York]], which was measured before it was broken up and found to attain a length of {{convert|30|ft|m|disp=sqbr}}.[[#Flower1955|Flower, 1955:329]]
However, the uncoiled length of the largest ammonites far exceeds that of even these giant endocerids. ''[[Parapuzosia seppenradensis]]'', the largest known ammonite species, had an estimated maximum unrolled shell length of around {{convert|60|ft|m|order=flip|abbr=on}}. It was also possibly the heaviest of all known cephalopods, past or present, with an estimated live mass of {{convert|1456|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, of which the shell would constitute 705 kg.[[#Landois1898|Landois, 1898:27]] By comparison, the largest endocerids may have weighed around {{convert|1000|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. In terms of mass, these are the largest known [[invertebrate]]s that have ever lived,[[#Grulke2014|Grulke, 2014:124]] though perhaps still second to the largest living cephalopods when considering [[Tissue (biology)|tissue mass]] alone, since in shell-bearing species the vast majority of the living tissue is restricted to the [[body chamber]], which occupies only a fraction of the internal shell volume. They might also be the largest—or at least longest—shell-bearing animals that have ever lived.{{refn|group=nb|name=largestshell|Depending on how one defines 'shell-bearing', other extinct contenders could include the aquatic turtles ''[[Archelon]]'' and ''[[Stupendemys]]'' (the former having a skeletal framework rather than a solid shell), the tortoise ''[[Megalochelys]]'', and the [[Armour (anatomy)|armoured]] mammal ''[[Glyptodon]]'' and its relatives.}} Cretaceous [[Yezo Group]] in [[Japan]] yields multiple taxa of large-sized cephalopod fossil remains. [[Oegopsida|Oegopsid]] ''[[Yezoteuthis|Yezoteuthis giganteus]]'' and ''[[Haboroteuthis|Haboroteuthis poseidon]]'' are only known from jaw elements, which is close to size of that of giant squid. Species of ''[[Nanaimoteuthis]]'', especially ''N. hikidai'' would be the largest known vampyromorph.{{Cite journal |last1=Tanabe |first1=Kazushige |last2=Misaki |first2=Akihiro |last3=Ubukata |first3=Takao |date=2014 |title=Late Cretaceous record of large soft-bodied coleoids based on lower jaw remains from Hokkaido, Japan |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |language=en |doi=10.4202/app.00052.2013 |issn=0567-7920|doi-access=free }} Unnamed, incomplete upper jaw fossil that is approximately twice as large as that of the mature giant squid is also found there.{{Cite journal |last1=Tanabe |first1=Kazushige |last2=Misaki |first2=Akihiro |date=2023-06-20 |title=Upper Cretaceous record of non-belemnitid coleoid jaws from Hokkaido, Japan, and its evolutionary implications |journal=Cretaceous Research |volume=151 |language=en |article-number=105624 |doi=10.1016/j.cretres.2023.105624 |bibcode=2023CrRes.15105624T |s2cid=259228292 |issn=0195-6671|doi-access=free }} ===Historical claims=== ====Misidentifications==== [[File:Moroteuthis robusta.jpg|thumb|left|300px|Kubota's photograph of a large ''[[Onykia robusta]]'', which was misidentified as the [[giant squid]], ''Architeuthis dux''. [[wide-angle lens|Wide-angle photography]] exaggerated the size of the squid[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:211]] such that it appeared impossibly large for the species.]] The maximum sizes of certain cephalopod species, most notably the giant squid and giant Pacific octopus, have often been misreported and exaggerated. The literature on cephalopod size has been further muddied by the frequent misattribution of various squid specimens to the giant squid genus ''Architeuthis'', often based solely on their large size. In the academic literature alone, such misidentifications encompass at least the [[Oegopsina|oegopsid]] families [[Chiroteuthidae]], [[Cranchiidae]], [[Ommastrephidae]], [[Onychoteuthidae]], and [[Psychroteuthidae]].see [[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a]]; [[#Salcedo-Vargas1999|Salcedo-Vargas, 1999]]; [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas, 2004]]{{refn|group=nb|name=Psychroteuthidae|[[#Iwai1956|Iwai (1956:139)]] reported on two small squid (92 and 104 mm ML) recovered from the "digestive canal" of a [[sperm whale]], which he identified as belonging to the genus ''[[Architeuthis]]''. [[#RoperYoung1972|Roper & Young (1972:220)]] showed that this was certainly a misidentification and attributed them instead to the family [[Psychroteuthidae]]. In a brief summary of this case, [[#Ellis1998a|Ellis (1998a:121)]] gave an erroneous total length of "8 feet" ({{convert|8|ft|m|disp=output only}}) for the larger of the two specimens (even though in an almost word-for-word identical [[footnote]] in [[#Ellis1994a|Ellis, 1994a:145]] he correctly stated the total length was "8 inches" [{{convert|8|in|cm|disp=output only}}]). This mistake was repeated by [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas (2004:67)]], giving rise to the claim of an implausibly large psychroteuthid "with about three meter total length".}} This situation is further confused by the occasional usage of the [[common name]] 'giant squid' in reference to large squids of other genera.[[#Robson1933|Robson, 1933:681]]; see for example [[#MitsukuriIkeda1895|Mitsukuri & Ikeda, 1895]]; [[#MeekGoddard1926|Meek & Goddard, 1926]]; [[#ClarkeRobson1929|Clarke & Robson, 1929]]; [[#Phillips1933|Phillips, 1933]]; [[#Croker1934|Croker, 1934]]; [[#Rees1950|Rees, 1950]]; [[#Smith1963|Smith, 1963]]; [[#Nesis1970|Nesis, 1970]] Perhaps the most notable misidentification relates to a photograph taken some time before 1993 by diver H. Kubota off southern [[Japan]].[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:211]]; [[#Norman2000|Norman, 2000:174]] The image shows a large individual of ''[[Onykia robusta]]'' (previously known as ''Moroteuthis robusta''), which appears to be sick or dying, alongside a diver in shallow water. A video of the same animal appeared in a Japanese made-for-television film. The image was published in the 1993 book ''European Seashells'' by [[Guido T. Poppe]] and Yoshihiro Goto, where it was identified as ''Architeuthis dux'', the giant squid, and said to have been taken in the [[North Atlantic]].[[#PoppeGoto1993|Poppe & Goto, 1993]] If true, this image would represent the first known photograph of a live giant squid. In ''[[The Search for the Giant Squid]]'' (1998), [[Richard Ellis (biologist)|Richard Ellis]] wrote:
For a moment, I thought that some obscure photograph had captured the most elusive image in natural history. Fortunately for those who have devoted their lives to searching for ''Architeuthis'', this was only an aberration, a case of mistaken identity.
It would be more than a decade before the true first photographs of a live giant squid in the wild were taken on 30 September 2004 by [[Tsunemi Kubodera]] and [[Kyoichi Mori]].[[#KuboderaMori2005|Kubodera & Mori, 2005]]; [[#Kubodera2010|Kubodera, 2010:25]] Kubodera and his team subsequently became the first to ''film'' a live ''adult'' giant squid on 4 December 2006,[[#Anonymous2007|[Anonymous], 2007]]; [[#Kubodera2010|Kubodera, 2010:38]] and the first to ''film'' a live giant squid ''in its natural habitat'' in July 2012.[[#Anonymous2013|[Anonymous], 2013]]; [[#NHK2013c|[NHK], 2013c]] These milestones were preceded by the first footage of a live ([[paralarva]]l) giant squid in 2001,[[#Baird2002|Baird, 2002]] and the first image of a live adult giant squid on 15 January 2002.[[#Anonymous2002a|[Anonymous], 2002a]]; [[#OShea2003d|O'Shea, 2003d]] Since then, live giant squid have been photographed and filmed on a number of occasions.''e.g.'' [[#Guerraetal2018|Guerra ''et al.'', 2018]] ====Giant squid==== [[File:Giant squid catalina.png|thumb|The "nearly perfect specimen" that was beached alive in [[Trinity Bay, Newfoundland and Labrador|Trinity Bay]], [[Newfoundland]], on 24 September 1877. A number of exceptionally large giant squid were reported from Newfoundland in the 1870s, and these were meticulously documented in a series of papers by zoologist [[Addison Emery Verrill]].[[#Coe1929|Coe, 1929:36]]; [[#GEVerrill1958|G.E. Verrill, 1958:69]]; see [[#Verrill1874a|Verrill 1874a]], [[#Verrill1874b|b]]; [[#Verrill1875a|1875a]], [[#Verrill1875b|b]], [[#Verrill1875c|c]]; [[#Verrill1876|1876]]; [[#Verrill1877|1877]]; [[#Verrill1878|1878]]; [[#Verrill1880a|1880a]], [[#Verrill1880b|b]]; [[#Verrill1881a|1881a]], [[#Verrill1881b|b]]; [[#Verrill1882a|1882a]]]] Reports of giant squid (''Architeuthis dux'') specimens reaching or even exceeding {{convert|18|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length are widespread, but no animals approaching this size have been scientifically documented in recent times.[[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#Dery2013|Dery, 2013]]; [[#Yong2015|Yong, 2015]] This is despite there being [[List of giant squid specimens and sightings|hundreds of specimens]] available for study ({{circa}} 700 documented as of 2015, of which {{circa}} 460 measured in some way), including numerous recent examples, such as the 57 specimens recorded from Japanese waters over a 15-month period in 2014–2015. It is now thought likely that such lengths were achieved by great lengthening of the two long [[cephalopod limb|feeding tentacles]], analogous to stretching elastic bands, or resulted from inadequate measurement methods such as pacing. On the subject of the oft-cited maximum size of 18 metres—or 60 feet—[[#Dery2013|Dery (2013)]] quoted giant squid experts [[Steve O'Shea]] and [[Clyde Roper]]:
If this figure [45 ft or {{convert|45|ft|m|abbr=on|disp=output only}}] seems a little short of the [[Brobdingnagian]] claims made for ''Architeuthis'' in most [[Popular science|pop-science]] stories about the animal, that's probably because virtually every general-interest article dutifully repeats the magic number of ''60'' feet. Steve O'Shea deplores the media's perpetuation of what he believes to be a credulity-straining exaggeration, based on the 19th-century biologist [[Thomas William Kirk|Thomas Kirk]]'s eyeball estimate of a specimen's length.{{refn|group=nb|name=longimanus}} In a comment on the final draft of this article, O'Shea wrote, "Kirk ''paced'' it, in his own words, for he had no ruler/measure handy, and I believe this misrepresentation has been perpetuated enough; if they were foot-on-foot, as in heel directly to toe, I would accept 57 (or 58, whatever the precise figure was), but I think perpetuating this as fact any longer is doing a disservice to science." Roper, in his comments on the final draft of this article, was even more conservative, writing, "there are no ''confirmed'' records of giant squid longer than about 45 feet [{{convert|45|ft|m|abbr=on|disp=output only}}] total length. Most are in the 25–35-foot [{{convert|25|–|35|ft|m|abbr=on|disp=output only}}] range. I have examined specimens in museums and laboratories around the world—perhaps a 100 or so—and I believe the 60-foot number comes from fear, fantasy, and pulling the highly elastic tentacles out to the near breaking point when they are measured on the shore or on deck."
=====Largest reported animals===== [[File:Distribution of total length, mantle length and mass in Architeuthis dux.png|thumb|400px|[[Frequency distribution]] of total length, mantle length, and mass in ''[[Architeuthis dux]]''. The {{convert|2000|lb|kg|abbr=on}} extreme [[outlier]] is sourced from [[#Verrill1880a|Verrill (1880a)]] and is unlikely to be accurate; the next most massive individual in the data set was only {{convert|700|lb|kg|abbr=on}}, and 95% of specimens were below {{convert|250|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.]] [[File:Linear regressions for Architeuthis dux.png|thumb|400px|[[Log–log plot|Log–log]] [[linear regression]]s for ''Architeuthis dux'': (A) total length vs. mass; (B) mantle length vs. total length; and (C) mantle length vs. mass]] [[#Paxton2016a|Paxton (2016a)]] investigated the maximum size of ''Architeuthis'' by performing a statistical analysis using data from [[List of giant squid specimens and sightings|literature records of giant squid specimens]]. He selected what he regarded as the largest size records for each of mantle length (ML), standard length (SL), and total length (TL). Paxton's study has been criticised by giant squid experts, who have called into question the reliability of some of the selected literature records. For mantle length, [[#Paxton2016a|Paxton (2016a:83)]] considered the {{convert|11|ft|m|abbr=on|sigfig=3}} reported by [[#Dell1952|Dell (1952:98)]] as the "longest measured", though "more reliably" the {{convert|9|ft|2|in|m|abbr=on|sigfig=3}} ML specimen from [[Lyall Bay]], [[New Zealand]], documented by [[#Kirk1880|Kirk (1880:312)]].{{refn|group=nb|name=Kirk1880|[[#Kirk1880|Kirk (1880:312)]] wrote of this specimen:
On 23rd of May last, the [[wikt:Ven.|Ven.]] [[Archdeacon]] Stock very kindly sent me word that three boys, named Edward R. Stock, and Frank and Walter Morrah, had that morning discovered, at [[Lyall Bay]], what they took to be a very large cuttlefish, with arms several feet long. I lost no time in proceeding to the spot, determined, if possible, to carry home the entire specimen; but judge my surprise when, on reaching the bay, I saw an animal of the size represented in the drawing now before you.* [[Victor Hugo]]'s [[Toilers of the Sea|account]] of his "[[octopus|pieuvre]]" was brought vividly to my mind, and I could not help thinking that a man would stand but a poor chance if he once got within the grasp of such a monster.
My first step after spreading out the arms, was to make a rough sketch and very careful measurements. I then proceeded to extract the so-called [[Gladius (cephalopod)|skeleton]], but found that some person or persons, who had visited the spot earlier than myself, had not been able to resist the temptation to try the temper of their knives upon its back, and had in consequence severed the [[Gladius (cephalopod)|cuttle-bone]] in various places. However, I was able, not only to procure all the pieces, but also the [[cephalopod beak|beak]], [[radula|tongue]], and some of the suckers, only a few of which remained, the greater portion of them having been torn off, either in some fierce encounter, or during the rough weather which had prevailed for some days previously.
The length of body from tip of [[cephalopod tail|tail]] to anterior margin of the mantle was 9 feet 2 inches [{{convert|9|ft|2|in|m|disp=output only}}] and 7 feet 3 inches [{{convert|7|ft|3|in|m|disp=output only}}] in circumference; the head from anterior margin of mantle to roots of arms 1-foot 11 inches [{{convert|1|ft|11|in|cm|disp=output only}}], making the total length of the body 11 feet 1 inch [{{convert|11|ft|1|in|m|disp=output only}}]. The head measured 4 feet [{{convert|4|ft|m|disp=output only}}] in circumference. The sessile arms measured 4 feet 3 inches [{{convert|4|ft|3|in|m|disp=output only}}] in length, and 11 inches [{{convert|11|in|cm|disp=output only}}] in circumference; each of these arms bore thirty-six suckers, arranged in two equal rows (as shown by the scars), and measuring from \tfrac{13}{16} to \tfrac{1}{4} of an inch [{{convert|13/16|–|1/4|in|mm|disp=output only}}] in diameter; every sucker was strengthened by a bony ring armed with from forty to sixty sharp incurved teeth. The tentacular arms had been torn off at the length of 6 feet 2 inches [{{convert|6|ft|2|in|m|disp=output only}}], which was probably less than half their original length.
The fins were posterior, and were mere lateral expansions of the mantle, they did not extend over the back as in the case with ''[[Onychoteuthis]]'', etc.; each measured 24 inches [{{convert|24|in|cm|disp=output only}}] in length and 13 inches [{{convert|13|in|cm|disp=output only}}] in width.
The [[Gladius (cephalopod)|cuttle bone]], when first extracted, measured 6 feet 3 inches [{{convert|6|ft|3|in|m|disp=output only}}] in length, and 11 inches [{{convert|11|in|cm|disp=output only}}] in width, but has since shrunk considerably; it was broadly lanceolate, with a hollow conical apex 1\tfrac{1}{8} inch [{{convert|1.125|in|mm|disp=output only}}] deep.
}} Paxton added: "A {{convert|4.5|m|ft|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} specimen from [[Mauritius]] is often mistakenly cited but consultation of the primary paper ([[#Staub1993|Staub, 1993]]) reveals an ill-defined length which is clearly not ML." The greatest measured ML of a giant squid recovered from a [[sperm whale]] is either the {{convert|2.4|m|ft|abbr=on}} reported by [[#Keil1963|Keil (1963:320)]] (though Paxton writes: "the account is confused and the 2.4 m figure probably refers to the head and ML combined") or the {{convert|6|ft|6|in|m|abbr=on|sigfig=3}} of a specimen that had been swallowed whole off the [[Azores]], detailed by [[#Clarke1955|Clarke (1955:589)]] and [[#Clarke1956|Clarke (1956:257)]]. The "longest visually estimated" ML, according to Paxton, is the {{circa}} {{convert|100|ft|m|abbr=on}} of a specimen apparently observed in the [[North Atlantic]] off [[Portugal]], attributed to a personal communication with T. Lipington. A more modest {{convert|4|m|ft|abbr=on}} ML is also given, based on a sighting in the [[Indian Ocean]] sourced to the TV documentary of [[#Lynch2013|Lynch (2013)]]. For standard length (excluding the [[cephalopod limb|tentacles]]), [[#Paxton2016a|Paxton (2016a:83)]] cited the {{convert|31|ft|m|abbr=on|sigfig=3}} of the "[[Three Arms]] specimen" documented by [[#Verrill1880a|Verrill (1880a:192)]] as the "longest measured". Among specimens recovered from sperm whales, the longest "definitely measured" SL is the {{convert|16|ft|3|in|m|abbr=on|sigfig=3}} reported by [[#Clarke1956|Clarke (1956:257)]] and the longest "visually estimated" SL is the {{circa}} {{convert|9|m|ft|abbr=on}} attributed to a photograph of a sperm whale with giant squid remains in its jaws,see [[#Hansford2009|Hansford, 2009]] though Paxton conceded that it is "[n]ot clear how much/what portion of body was eaten". For the "longest visually estimated", more extreme supposed SLs of {{circa}} {{convert|175|ft|m|abbr=on}} and {{circa}} {{convert|100|ft|m|abbr=on}} are cited to [[#Starkey1963|Starkey (1963)]] and [[#Ellis1998a|Ellis (1998a:246)]], respectively (the latter an eyewitness account by Dennis Braun). Paxton treated these last two size estimates as SLs as opposed to TLs because "squid do not generally leave their tentacles exposed except when grabbing prey and this appears to be the case for ''Architeuthis''". For total length, [[#Paxton2016a|Paxton (2016a:83)]] considered three records as candidates for the "longest measured": the {{convert|19|m|ft|abbr=on}} specimen of [[#Berzin1971|Berzin (1972:199)]], the {{convert|55|ft|2|in|m|abbr=on|sigfig=4}} specimen described by [[#Kirk1888|Kirk (1888)]] as ''Architeuthis longimanus''—a strangely proportioned animal that has been much commented on{{refn|group=nb|name=longimanus|[[File:Architeuthis longimanus Kirk, 1888.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Thomas William Kirk|T. W. Kirk]]'s sketch of the ''Architeuthis longimanus'' [[type specimen]] in lateral aspect, from [[#Kirk1888|Kirk (1888)]]. Note the extreme length of the feeding tentacles relative to the mantle and arms.]][[Thomas William Kirk]] gave the following account of this specimen in his [[species description|formal description]] of ''Architeuthis longimanus'':
[...] Early last month Mr. Smith, a local fisherman, brought to the [[Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa|[Colonial] Museum]] the beak and buccal-mass of a cuttle which had that morning been found lying on the "Big Beach" ([[Lyall Bay]]), and he assured us that the creature measured sixty-two feet [{{convert|62|ft|m|sigfig=3|disp=output only}}] in total length. I that afternoon proceeded to the spot and made a careful examination, took notes, measurements, and also obtained a sketch, which, although the terribly heavy rain and driving southerly wind rendered it impossible to do justice to the subject, will, I trust, convey to you some idea of the general outline of this most recently-arrived Devil-fish.
Measurements showed that, although Mr. Smith was over the mark in giving the total length as 62 feet [{{convert|62|ft|m|sigfig=3|disp=output only}}] (probably, not having a measure with him, he only stepped the distance), those figures were not so very far out; for, although the body was in all ways smaller than any of the hitherto-described New Zealand species, the enormous development of the very slight tentacular arms brought the total length up to 55 feet 2 inches [{{convert|55|ft|2|in|m|adj=on|disp=output only}}], or more than half as long again as the largest species yet recorded from these seas.
The length of the tentacular arms is not a very important character, as they are known to be capable of extension or retraction at the will of the animal, at least to a considerable extent.
[...] This specimen was a female, and to this fact may be due some of the points in which it differs from previous occurrences; but yet they are so considerable that I have no doubt a new subgenus at least will have to be created for its reception. In the meantime I place it under ''Architeuthis'', with the full knowledge, however, that it cannot possibly remain there permanently, the shape of the arms and the fins alone being sufficient to put it out of association with that genus. As soon as opportunity offers, I hope to make a further study and fully determine its affinities.[[#Kirk1888|Kirk, 1888:35–36]]
[[#Kirk1888|Kirk (1888:38)]] provides a table with a detailed breakdown of the specimen's various measurements. There is, however, a discrepancy between the total length of 684 in ({{convert|684|in|m|abbr=on|sigfig=4|disp=output only}}, or exactly 57 ft) given in the table—which agrees with the individual values of {{convert|71|in|m|abbr=on|sigfig=3}} for the mantle, {{convert|22|in|m|abbr=on|sigfig=2}} for the head, and {{convert|591|in|m|abbr=on|sigfig=4}} for the tentacles—and the total length of {{convert|55|ft|2|in|m|abbr=on}} given by Kirk in the body of the article. [[#Wood1982|Wood (1982:191)]] suggested that, due to the tentacles' highly retractile nature, the total length of {{convert|62|ft|m|sigfig=3}} originally reported by the fisherman "may have been correct at the time he found the squid", and that "[t]his probably also explains the discrepancy in Kirk's figures". Owing to its small mantle size, [[#Wood1982|Wood (1982:191)]] estimated that "this specimen probably weighed less than 300 lb [{{convert|300|lb|kg|disp=output only}}]". [[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad (2008)]] opined that the reported total length of {{convert|55|ft|2|in|m|abbr=on}} "simply cannot be correct" and attributed it to either "imagination" or artificial lengthening of the tentacles. They added that a female giant squid with a mantle length of {{convert|71|in|cm|abbr=on}} "measured post mortem and relaxed (by modern standards) today would have a total length of ≈32 feet [{{convert|32|ft|m|disp=output only}}]".}}—and the {{convert|55|ft|m|abbr=on|sigfig=4}} "[[Thimble Tickle Bay|Thimble Tickle]] specimen" reported by [[#Verrill1880a|Verrill (1880a:191)]], which is often cited as the largest giant squid ever recorded.{{refn|group=nb|name=ThimbleTickle|The Thimble Tickle specimen, which [[Addison Emery Verrill]] referred to as his specimen No. 18,[[#Verrill1880a|Verrill, 1880a:191]]; [[#Verrill1880b|Verrill, 1880b:285]] is often cited as the largest recorded giant squid, and has long been treated as such by [[Guinness World Records|Guinness]].see [[#Wood1982|Wood, 1982:189]]; [[#Carwardine1995|Carwardine, 1995:240]]; [[#Glenday2014|Glenday, 2014:62]] The Thimble Tickle squid was found aground offshore, alive, on 2 November 1878, near Little Bay Copper Mine, [[Thimble Tickle Bay|Thimble Tickle]], [[Notre Dame Bay]], [[Newfoundland Colony|Newfoundland]]. It was secured to a tree with a [[grapnel]] and rope and died as the tide receded. No part of the animal was retained as it was cut up for dog food. The length of the "body [..] from the beak to the extremity of the tail" (''i.e.'' mantle plus head) was said to be {{convert|20|ft|m|abbr=on|sigfig=2}}, with "one of the arms" (presumably a tentacle) measuring {{convert|35|ft|m|abbr=on|sigfig=3}}, for a total length of {{convert|55|ft|m|abbr=on|sigfig=3}}. Reverend [[Moses Harvey]] detailed the encounter in a letter to the ''[[Boston Traveller]]'' dated 30 January 1879, which was reproduced by [[#Verrill1880a|Verrill (1880a:191–192)]]:
On the 2d day of November last, Stephen Sherring, a fisherman residing in Thimble Tickle, not far from the locality where the other devil-fish [Verrill specimen No. 19; see [[#Verrill1880a|Verrill, 1880a:192]]], was cast ashore, was out in a boat with two other men; not far from the shore they observed some bulky object, and, supposing it might be part of a wreck, they rowed toward it, and, to their horror, found themselves close to a huge fish, having large glassy eyes, which was making desperate efforts to escape, and churning the water into foam by the motion of its immense arms and tail. It was aground and the tide was ebbing. From the funnel at the back of its head it was ejecting large volumes of water, this being its method of moving backward, the force of the stream, by the reaction of the surrounding medium, driving it in the required direction. At times the water from the siphon was black as ink.
Finding the monster partially disabled, the fishermen plucked up courage and ventured near enough to throw the grapnel of their boat, the sharp flukes of which, having barbed points, sunk into the soft body. To the grapnel they had attached a stout rope which they had carried ashore and tied to a tree, so as to prevent the fish from going out with the tide. It was a happy thought, for the devil fish found himself effectually moored to the shore. His struggles were terrific as he flung his ten arms about in dying agony. The fishermen took care to keep a respectful distance from the long tentacles, which ever and anon darted out like great tongues from the central mass. At length it became exhausted, and as the water receded it expired.
The fishermen, alas! knowing no better, proceeded to convert it into dog's meat. It was a splendid specimen—the largest yet taken—the body measuring 20 feet [{{convert|20|ft|m|adj=on|sigfig=2|disp=output only}}] from the beak to the extremity of the tail. It was thus exactly double the size of the New York specimen [Verrill specimen No. 14; see [[#Verrill1880a|Verrill, 1880a:189]]], and five feet [{{convert|5|ft|m|adj=on|sigfig=2|disp=output only}}] longer than the one taken by [fisherman William] Budgell [No. 19]. The circumference of the body is not stated, but one of the arms measured 35 feet [{{convert|35|ft|m|adj=on|sigfig=3|disp=output only}}]. This must have been a tentacle.
Such is the fame of the Thimble Tickle specimen that a Giant Squid Interpretation Centre and a "life-sized", {{convert|55|ft|m|adj=on}} sculpture have been built near the site of its capture.[[#Hickey2009|Hickey, 2009]] The sculpture appeared on a [[Canada|Canadian]] postage stamp issued in 2011.[[#Hickey2010|Hickey, 2010]]; [[#stamp|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]]}} Of the last one, Paxton wrote: "Sometimes mistakenly cited as {{convert|17.37|m|ft|abbr=on}} but the source is clear that it is 55 ft long." The first two records, particularly that of Berzin, are more questionable, as Paxton explained:
The accuracy of the two longest measured TLs of 19 and 16.81 m from a specimen found in the gut of a sperm whale from the Indian Ocean and from the specimen from New Zealand in 1887, respectively, should also be questioned but again are certainly not impossible. The New Zealand specimen (named ''Architeuthis longimanus'' [[#Kirk1888|Kirk, 1888]]) clearly has the largest ratio of TL to ML ever known in ''Architeuthis'' [...] which led [[[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]]] to suggest that the length was paced out and/or there was extensive post-mortem stretching. However, a re-reading of the original paper suggests that the specimen, although initially paced out, was actually measured, nevertheless the TL is at the edge of the 99.9% prediction interval range [...] and so it was certainly an unusual specimen. [[#Berzin1971|Berzin's (1972)]] Indian Ocean claim is suspect because of the roundness of the figure, the lack of detailed measurements and because in an associated photo, the mantle (whose length was not given) does not look very large compared to the men in the image. Consequently the measurement, if accurate, would represent another animal with very long tentacles.[[#Paxton2016a|Paxton, 2016a:86]]
[[File:Giant squid tentacle saved by Moses Harvey.jpg|thumb|left|The {{convert|19|ft|m|adj=on}} tentacle that fisherman Theophilus Picot hacked off a live animal on 26 October 1873. Picot estimated the total length of the squid at {{convert|60|ft|m|abbr=on}}.[[#Murray1874|Murray, 1874:121]]]] However, as [[#Paxton2016a|Paxton (2016a:86)]] pointed out, the genetic analysis of [[#Winkelmannetal2013|Winkelmann ''et al.'' (2013)]]—which concluded that there is likely a single, globally-distributed species of ''Architeuthis''—did not encompass these two specimens, and it is therefore possible that there exists a second, as yet unsampled, giant squid species with proportionately longer tentacles. The {{convert|19|m|ft|abbr=on}} total length of the Berzin specimen was later confirmed to be erroneous; according to Valentin Yukhov, who was involved in the specimen's discovery, it should have read {{convert|9|m|ft|abbr=on}}.[[#Romanovetal2017|Romanov ''et al.'', 2017]] The misprint was reproduced in the English translation published the following year and was later propagated in a number of papers on giant squid. With the Berzin specimen not being as large as originally reported, the longest giant squid recovered from a sperm whale is the {{convert|34|ft|5|in|m|abbr=on|sigfig=4}} TL individual recorded by [[#Clarke1956|Clarke (1956:257)]] (this specimen also has the longest confirmed ML and SL of any giant squid from a sperm whale).[[#Paxton2016a|Paxton, 2016a:83]] Paxton considered the "longest visually estimated" TL to be the {{convert|60|ft|m|abbr=on}} published by [[#Murray1874|Murray (1874:121)]], from an eyewitness account by fisherman Theophilus Picot, who claimed to have struck the floating animal from his boat, [[cephalopod attack|causing it to attack]]. Picot managed to hack off one of its tentacles, which was subsequently examined by a number of authors.see [[#Murray1874|Murray, 1874]]; [[#Verrill1875a|Verrill, 1875a]]; [[#Verrill1875b|Verrill, 1875b]] Perhaps the largest of all recorded giant squid specimens was the one found floating at the surface off [[Saint-Gilles, Réunion|Saint-Gilles]], [[Réunion]], on 4 March 2016. Although due to its great size the specimen could not be retrieved in its entirety, the head and arm crown were saved. Crucially, this meant the beak could be measured to estimate the mantle length and total length of the specimen. Using different [[allometric scaling]] equations,{{refn|group=nb|name=equations|[[File:Squid beak measuring.jpg|thumb|250px|Giant squid beak being measured. Isolated beaks found in the stomachs of predators such as sperm whales can be used to estimate the size of the original animal.see [[#Roeleveld2000|Roeleveld, 2000]]; [[#Cherel2003|Cherel, 2003]]; [[#Romanovetal2017|Romanov ''et al.'', 2017]]]][[#Romanovetal2017|Romanov ''et al.'' (2017)]] used the following allometric scaling equations to estimate the dorsal mantle length (DML) from the [[lower rostral length]] (LRL) of the beak, and total length (TL) from DML:
:LRL = 11.2 \times log_{10} DML - 19.3 (all values in mm)[[#Roeleveld2000|Roeleveld, 2000:185]] :DML = e^{(-1.168 + 98.031 \times LRL)} (all values in m)[[#Paxton2016a|Paxton, 2016a:85]] :TL = 0.006 + 5.117 DML (all values in m) :TL = 1.59 \times DML^{1.98} (all values in m)[[#McClainetal2015|McClain ''et al.'', 2015:Table 2]]}} the [[lower rostral length]] of the beak, at {{convert|19.74|mm|in|abbr=on}}, gave an estimated dorsal mantle length of {{convert|215.3|–|306.0|cm|ft|abbr=on}} and this, in turn, was used to estimate the total length at {{convert|11.025|–|15.664|m|ft|abbr=on}}. =====Supposed sucker scars===== [[File:Display of sperm whale and giant squid battling in the Museum of Natural History.jpg|thumb|A dramatisation of an underwater encounter between the sperm whale and giant squid, from a [[diorama]] at the [[American Museum of Natural History]]]] More extreme and outlandish giant squid size claims—belonging firmly in the realm of [[cryptozoology]]—have appeared in the works of authors such as [[Bernard Heuvelmans]], [[Willy Ley]], and [[Ivan T. Sanderson]].see [[#Sanderson1956|Sanderson, 1956]]; [[#Heuvelmans1958|Heuvelmans, 1958]]; [[#Ley1959|Ley, 1959]] The existence of these gargantuan squids is often supported by reference to the giant circular scars sometimes found on [[sperm whale]]s, which are assumed to have been inflicted by the [[cephalopod sucker|suckers]] of struggling giant squid. Sometimes these claims are accompanied by extrapolations of body size based on the [[Allometry|isometric upscaling]] of a "typical" giant squid.[[#RoperBoss1982|Roper & Boss, 1982:97]] However, such scars are not necessarily of squid origin and may instead represent fungal growths or bite marks, with [[sea lamprey]]s (''Petromyzon marinus'') being one possible source.[[#Wood1982|Wood, 1982:193]] Even in the case of genuine giant squid sucker marks it is possible that subsequent skin growth has enlarged them well beyond their original dimensions.[[#RoperBoss1982|Roper & Boss, 1982:99]]; [[#Wood1982|Wood, 1982:192]]; [[#HaszprunarWanninger2012|Haszprunar & Wanninger, 2012:R510]] [[File:A piece of sperm whale skin with Giant Squid sucker scars.JPG|thumb|left|A portion of [[sperm whale]] skin with giant squid sucker scars. In this widely reproduced image the largest sucker scars measure around an inch ({{convert|1|in|cm|disp=output only}}) across.[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:143]]]] Nevertheless, claims of enormous sucker scars are widespread in the literature. [[Richard Ellis (biologist)|Richard Ellis]] collected some of "the more egregious examples" in his book, ''[[The Search for the Giant Squid]]''.see [[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:142]] These include the claim of [[#Dozier1976|Dozier (1976)]] that "an ordinary giant squid of 50 feet [{{convert|50|ft|m|disp=output only}}] leaves teeth-ringed sucker marks measuring between three and four inches [{{convert|3|-|4|in|cm|disp=output only}}] across on a whale, but sperm whales have been captured with tentacle marks 18 inches [{{convert|18|in|cm|disp=output only}}] across." [[L. Harrison Matthews]]'s monographic treatment of the sperm whale, published in 1938, includes the following: "Nearly all male Sperm whales carry scars caused by the suckers and claws of large squids, scars caused by suckers up to 10 cm. [{{convert|10|cm|in|disp=output only}}] in diameter being common. The claw marks take the form of scratches 2–3 m. [{{convert|2|–|3|m|ft|disp=output only}}] in length, and appear to be of more frequent occurrence than sucker marks".[[#Matthews1938|Matthews, 1938]] [[#Ellis1998a|Ellis (1998a:142)]] wrote that this 10 cm figure is "so much larger than any other recorded sucker dimensions that one suspects some sort of error, either in measuring or in transcription." The subject was covered in some detail by [[#Wood1982|Wood (1982:192)]]:
Measurements of {{convert|90|ft|m|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}}, {{convert|130|ft|m|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} and even {{convert|200|ft|m|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} have been conjectured for giant squids from the size of sucker marks found on the skins of captured sperm whales, but it is dangerous to place too much reliance on this evidence. [[Addison Emery Verrill|Verrill]] says the largest suckers on the tentacles of a {{convert|32|ft|m|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} long specimen measured 1{{frac|1|4}} in [{{convert|1.25|in|cm|disp=output only}}] in diameter, and those on a 52-footer [{{convert|52|ft|m|disp=output only}}] about {{convert|2|in|cm|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}}. [[#Daniel1925|Daniel (1925)]], however, examined sucker marks on the head of one cachalot which measured 3{{frac|1|2}} in [{{convert|3.5|in|cm|disp=output only}}] across, and others measuring up to {{convert|5|in|cm|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} in diameter have been found on the skins of sperm whales captured in the North Atlantic. [[#Sanderson1956|Ivan Sanderson (1956)]] goes even further and claims that sucker marks over {{convert|18|in|cm|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} have been found on the heads of cachalots, but he does not explain how the poor whales managed to escape from the clutches of such colossi! The general consensus of opinion is that exceptionally large sucker marks, i.e. over {{convert|2|in|cm|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} in diameter, are old scars that have increased in size as the sperm whale grew.
[[File:Giant squid tentacle club.jpg|thumb|One of the paired [[tentacular club]]s of ''Architeuthis'', showing the enlarged suckers of the manus, which are the largest found on any of the giant squid's [[cephalopod limb|limbs]]]] Perhaps the most extreme published claim, ridiculed by [[#Ellis1998a|Ellis (1998a:142)]], appeared in [[Willy Ley]]'s 1959 book, ''[[Exotic Zoology]]'': "Toothed whales, vomiting in death struggle, have shown evidence of still larger [[kraken]]; in one case a 6-foot [{{convert|6|ft|m|disp=output only}}] piece of tentacle, ''with a diameter of 2 feet'' [{{convert|2|ft|m|disp=output only|sigfig=1}}; emphasis in original], has been claimed.
