Another claim goes for marks on the skin of such a whale, looking like the mark of a sucking disk over 2 feet [{{convert|2|ft|m|disp=output only|sigfig=1}}] in diameter".[[#Ley1959|Ley, 1959:210]] Marine biologist [[Frederick Aldrich]], who personally examined more than a dozen giant squid specimens, wrote that his largest specimen from [[Newfoundland]] bore tentacular suckers "approximately two inches [{{convert|2|in|cm|abbr=on|disp=output only}}] in diameter" but that "[s]uckers and their toothed armament of over twelve inches [{{convert|12|in|cm|abbr=on|disp=output only}}] in diameter have been found in the stomachs of sperm whale as indigestible wastes".[[#Aldrich1980|Aldrich, 1980:59]] This led him to entertain the idea of giant squid over {{convert|150|ft|m|abbr=on}} long,{{refn|group=nb|name=Aldrich|Aldrich restated this belief in "Monsters of the Deep", the second episode of the 1980 television series ''[[Arthur C. Clarke's Mysterious World]]'': "I believe the giant squid reach an approximate maximum size of something like one hundred and fifty feet [{{convert|150|ft|m|disp=output only}}]".[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:6–7]] [[Richard Ellis (biologist)|Richard Ellis]], apparently unaware of Aldrich's similar statements in print, commented: "It is difficult to imagine why Aldrich would have made such an irresponsible statement, unless it had to do with being on camera".[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:7]] [[Arthur C. Clarke]] himself once remarked: "it would be strange indeed if the world's biggest squid had been among the very few cast ashore to be examined and measured by naturalists. There may well be specimens more than a hundred feet [{{convert|100|ft|m|disp=output only}}] in length."[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:6]]}} and even to suggest a [[binomial name]] for this super-sized species, were it ever to be discovered: ''Architeuthis halpertius''. By comparison, giant squid suckers ''normally'' reach a maximum diameter of only a few centimetres. Based on a detailed examination of a number of large specimens from New Zealand waters, [[#Forch1998|Förch (1998:55)]] wrote that "[t]he largest suckers [...] on the sessile arms are a very constant {{convert|21|–|24|mm|in|disp=sqbr|sigfig=2}} in external diameter". In giant squid the largest suckers of all are found on the central portion of the [[tentacular club]], called the ''[[wikt:manus|manus]]'', and among the specimens examined by [[#Forch1998|Förch (1998:53)]] these reached a maximum diameter of {{convert|28|–|32|mm|in|abbr=on|sigfig=3}}. [[#Clarke1980|Clarke (1980)]] wrote: "I have not yet seen conclusive evidence to suggest that sucker scars are larger than {{convert|3.7|cm|in|disp=sqbr|sigfig=3}} across".[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:142]] According to [[#RoperBoss1982|Roper & Boss (1982:97)]], the largest suckers of the tentacular clubs reach {{convert|5.2|cm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter. =====Mass estimates===== [[File:Lifting giant squid in tarp.jpg|thumb|left|A dozen people attempting to move a [[giant squid]] weighing in excess of {{convert|200|kg|lb|abbr=on}}see [[#Feldman1999|Feldman, 1999]] for more on this specimen]] It is now accepted that the giant squid has [[#Mass 2|a maximum mass of several hundred kilograms]],[[#OShea2003a|O'Shea, 2003a]]; [[#RoperJereb2010a|Roper & Jereb, 2010a:121]] but the literature is rife with claims of much greater weights. [[#Clarke1966|Clarke (1966)]], for example, put the mass of the largest giant squid specimens at around {{convert|1|tonne|lb}}.[[#Alexander1998|Alexander, 1998:1233]] Similarly, [[Richard Ellis (biologist)|Richard Ellis]] wrote: "Where [giant] squid carcasses have actually been weighed, it appears that the longest ones—in the 50-foot [{{convert|50|ft|m|disp=output only}}] range, for example—weigh about a ton [{{convert|1|short ton|kg|disp=output only}}]."[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:106]] Much greater estimates of giant squid mass can be found in, for example, ''Natural History of Marine Animals'' by [[#MacGinitieMacGinitie1949|MacGinitie & MacGinitie (1949)]]: "two arms of ''Architeuthis'' that were {{convert|42|ft|m|disp=sqbr}} long were found, and if one reconstructed a body [...] the squid to which these arms belonged was {{convert|4.6|ft|m|disp=sqbr}} in diameter and {{convert|24|ft|m|disp=sqbr}} long, with an overall measurement of {{convert|66|ft|m|disp=sqbr}}. It would have weighed about 42{{frac|1|2}} tons [{{convert|42.5|short ton|tonne|abbr=off|disp=output only}}]." They added that a {{convert|55|ft|m|adj=on}} specimen, such as the one reported from [[Thimble Tickle Bay|Thimble Tickle]],{{refn|group=nb|name=ThimbleTickle}} "would have weighed 29{{frac|1|4}} or 30 tons [{{convert|29.25|or|30|short ton|tonne|sigfig=3|abbr=off|disp=output only}}] including the tentacles—a truly noble animal, being a little more than one-fifth the weight of the [[blue whale|largest whale]] and larger than the [[whale shark]]s and [[basking shark]]s, the largest of all fishes".[[#MacGinitieMacGinitie1949|MacGinitie & MacGinitie, 1949]] Ellis characterised these estimates as "unfounded exaggerations". In the revised edition of ''Natural History of Marine Animals'', published in 1968, the authors reduced their estimate to less than 8 tonnes.[[#MacGinitieMacGinitie1968|MacGinitie & MacGinitie, 1968]]; [[#Wood1982|Wood, 1982:190]] [[Bernard Heuvelmans]] believed that "there must be ''Architeuthis'' weighing more than 5 tons, and some even larger ones which must weigh between 2 and 27 tons, the normal weight being around 8 tons. There are good reasons to believe that there may even exist specimens twice as long as that of Thimble Tickle, which, depending upon their girth, might have weighed between 16 and 216 tons, but more likely around 64 tons."[[#Heuvelmans1958|Heuvelmans, 1958]] Ellis, who considered these estimates "utterly ridiculous", wrote:
Heuvelmans commits a fundamental error in calculating the weight of some of these monsters when he writes that "the density of living creatures is only slightly higher than that of water ... a decimetre of living flesh weighs about as much as a litre of water." That may be true for some other living creatures, but the flesh of ''Architeuthis'', saturated with [[ammonium chloride]], is ''lighter'' than water, and the giant squid is [[Neutral buoyancy|neutrally buoyant]]. (This is believed to be the reason that dead or dying squid are found floating at the surface or washed up on the beach.) His assumption, therefore, that the 55-foot-long [{{convert|55|ft|m|disp=output only}}] Thimble Tickle squid would have "probably weighed near 24 tons" is patently erroneous.[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:107]]
On the subject of the Thimble Tickle specimen's mass, [[#Wood1982|Wood (1982:190)]] referred to the work of [[Soviet Union|Soviet]] zoologist and writer [[Igor Akimushkin]]:
According to Dr [[#Akimushkin1963|Igor Akimushkin (1965)]], the Russian teuthologist, a {{convert|12|m|ft|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} long giant squid will weigh 1 tonne [{{convert|1|tonne|lb|abbr=on|disp=output only}}] if the head, mantle and arms combined make up half the total length. Since there is a [[Cube (algebra)|cubic]] relationship between the linear dimensions of ''Architeuthis'' and its volume or weight, this means the Thimble Tickle monster must have scaled about 2.8 tonnes [{{convert|2.8|tonne|lb|abbr=on|disp=output only}}] (i.e. the weight of a large bull [[hippopotamus]]), although 2 tonnes [{{convert|2|tonne|lb|abbr=on|disp=output only}}] is probably a more realistic figure.
====Giant Pacific octopus==== The maximum size of the [[giant Pacific octopus]] (''Enteroctopus dofleini'') has long been a source of debate in the scientific community, with dubious reports of specimens weighing hundreds of kilograms. =====Largest reported animals===== [[File:Distribution of mass and interocular distance in Enteroctopus dofleini.png|thumb|400px|[[Frequency distribution]] of mass and interocular distance in male and female ''[[Enteroctopus dofleini]]'']] [[File:Relationship between interocular distance and mass in Enteroctopus dofleini.png|thumb|400px|[[Log–log plot]] showing relationship between interocular distance and mass in ''Enteroctopus dofleini'']] In 1885, reporting on the longest octopus specimen reliably recorded up to that point, renowned [[Malacology|malacologist]] [[William Healey Dall]] wrote:
In 1874 I speared an octopus in the harbor of [[Iliuliuk]], [[Unalashka]], which was afterward hung, by a cord tied around the body immediately behind the arms, to one of the [[stern]] [[davit]]s of the coast survey vessel under my command. As soon as the animal died and the muscles relaxed, I noticed that the tips of the longer tentacles just touched the water. On measuring the distance with a cord, I found it to be sixteen feet [{{convert|16|ft|m|disp=output only}}], giving the creature a spread from tip to tip of the longest pair of arms, of not less than thirty-two feet [{{convert|32|ft|m|disp=output only}}]. The arms toward the tips were all exceedingly slender, but rather stout toward the body, which was somewhat over a foot [{{convert|1|ft|cm|disp=output only}}] long. The largest suckers were two and a half inches [{{convert|2.5|in|cm|disp=output only}}] in diameter; the whole creature nearly filled a large washtub. Parts of this specimen are now in the [[National Museum of Natural History|U. S. national museum]].[[#Dall1885|Dall, 1885:432]]
In an article for the [[National Marine Fisheries Service]] summarising knowledge on the giant Pacific octopus, [[#High1976|High (1976:17–18)]] wrote:
Several octopuses in excess of 100 pounds [{{convert|100|lb|kg|disp=output only}}] have been encountered and captured. Much larger ones have been reported but, like the [[Loch Ness Monster]], these usually elude the careful photographer or scientist. Most octopuses weigh less than 70 pounds [{{convert|70|lb|kg|disp=output only}}] with a stretched length of 15 feet [{{convert|15|ft|m|disp=output only}}] or less. Overall length between arms is not a suitable measure because of the animal's unusual elasticity. In the late 1950s, I interviewed a Canadian commercial diver, Jock MacLean of [[Prince Rupert, British Columbia|Prince Rupert]], [[British Columbia|B.C.]] He reported capturing an immense creature weighing 600 pounds [{{convert|600|lb|kg|sigfig=3|disp=output only}}] and measuring 32 feet [{{convert|32|ft|m|disp=output only}}] from arm tip to top. MacLean's photographs, unfortunately, were of poor quality. Smaller animals, to 400 pounds [{{convert|400|lb|kg|sigfig=3|disp=output only}}], were occasionally taken in his commercial octopus fishing endeavor.
[[#HochbergFields1980|Hochberg & Fields (1980:436)]] referenced the same specimen, writing: "the largest specimen on record with a total arm spread of 9.6 m [{{convert|9.6|m|ft|disp=output only}}] and a weight of 272 kg [{{convert|272|kg|lb|disp=output only}}]". These figures are only estimates, however, as—contrary to the above quotation from [[#High1976|High (1976:17–18)]]—it appears that this specimen was never collected and measured. Murray Newman, director of the [[Vancouver Aquarium]] for 37 years, quoted Jock MacLean in his 1994 memoir, ''Life in a Fishbowl'': "Next year [1957] in the same place, I saw one, maybe thirty-two feet [{{convert|32|ft|m|disp=output only}}] across and six hundred pounds [{{convert|600|lb|kg|sigfig=3|disp=output only}}]. Didn't go for her, though, no place to keep her!"[[#Newman1994|Newman, 1994:66]] Nevertheless, the [[false precision|misleadingly precise]] [[metric system|metric]] [[Conversion of units|conversion]] of 272 kg (for 600 lb) and the ''im''precise conversion of 9.6 m (for 32 ft; naively employing a [[conversion factor]] of 0.3 instead of [[Foot (unit)|0.3048]]) gained wide acceptance as the maximum recorded dimensions of the giant Pacific octopus, and have been much repeated.{{refn|group=nb|name=Bavendam|An example from the May 2000 issue of ''Diver'' magazine reads: "The generally accepted record was of awesome proportions, weighing 272kg and with an arm span of 9.6m."[[#Bavendam2000|Bavendam, 2000:63–64]]}} Jock MacLean is also reported to have ''captured'' a {{convert|198|kg|lb|abbr=on}} animal with an arm span of {{convert|8.5|m|ft|abbr=on}} near [[Port Hardy]], [[British Columbia]], in March 1956.[[#Newman1994|Newman, 1994:66]]; [[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:66–67]] Another giant specimen was caught off [[Santa Barbara, California|Santa Barbara]], [[California]], in 1945. Its weight was recorded as {{convert|182.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and the surviving photograph makes it possible to estimate its total length at more than {{convert|3|m|ft|abbr=on}} and arm span at {{convert|6|–|6.7|m|ft|abbr=on}}. In a book dedicated to the giant Pacific octopus, [[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel (2009:72)]] summarised knowledge on the species's maximum size as follows:
The specimen William Dall speared in 1885 {{sic}} at Iliuliuk had the largest radial span of any giant Pacific octopus ever measured. Jock MacLean's 1956 Port Hardy behemoth was the biggest ever weighed. The Santa Barbara specimen photographed in 1945 was the second heaviest. It would appear that octopuses weighing as much as {{convert|272|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and with radial spans of over {{convert|9|m|ft|abbr=off|spell=in}} are within the realm of possibility, but have never actually been documented by both measuring and weighing.
=====Possible diminution in size===== No specimens approaching these extreme sizes have been reported since the middle of the 20th century. This lack of giant individuals is corroborated by commercial octopus fishers; none of those interviewed by [[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel (2009)]] had caught a single animal weighing more than {{convert|57|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in the previous 20 years, among many thousands harvested over that period. Octopus specialist Roland Anderson, a biologist with the [[Seattle Aquarium]] for more than 30 years, had long sought, unsuccessfully, to find a giant Pacific octopus weighing more than {{convert|100|lb|kg|abbr=on}}. In an attempt to raise a truly enormous specimen, Anderson fed a number of captive males ''[[ad libitum]]''. The heaviest animal (nicknamed 'Big') attained a peak weight of {{convert|43|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and its largest suckers measured {{convert|7.9|cm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter.[[#Anderson2003|Anderson, 2003:2]]; [[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:71]] Anderson suggested the species might now be maturing at a smaller size as a result of [[toxicant]] [[bioaccumulation]], which could explain the lack of truly gigantic specimens in recent times. In particular, high concentrations of [[heavy metal (chemical element)|heavy metals]] and [[Polychlorinated biphenyl|PCBs]] have been identified in the [[digestive gland]]s of wild giant Pacific octopuses, likely originating from their preferred prey, the [[Cancer productus|red rock crab]] (''Cancer productus'').[[#Anderson2003|Anderson, 2003:3]]; [[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:71]]; [[#ScheelAnderson2012|Scheel & Anderson, 2012]] A preliminary study found that aquarium animals fed equal quantities of raw sea food and live ''C. productus'' (caught locally in [[Elliott Bay]]) matured at a smaller size, reached a lower maximum weight ({{convert|27|kg|lb|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} mean), and had higher concentrations of most heavy metals, than those fed solely on raw sea food ({{convert|36|kg|lb|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} mean, including the aforementioned {{convert|43|kg|lb|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} specimen).[[#Anderson2003|Anderson, 2003:2]] ===Largest species by measure=== Cephalopod size can be quantified in various ways. Some of the most common size measures are covered below. The following four tables list only extant species; [[#Extinct taxa 3|extinct taxa are treated separately at the end]]. ====Mantle length==== [[File:Giant squid with Clyde Roper.jpg|thumb|250px|Teuthologist [[Clyde Roper]] lying alongside a large [[giant squid]] (''Architeuthis dux'') specimen of almost {{convert|2|m|ft|abbr=on}} ML]] [[File:Fish4071 - Flickr - NOAA Photo Library.jpg|thumb|250px|Reaching a mantle length of {{convert|2|m|ft|abbr=on}}, ''[[Onykia robusta]]'' is one of the largest squid species (the specimen shown here is considerably smaller).]] [[File:Giant Homboldt squid.jpg|thumb|250px|A {{convert|52|lb|kg|abbr=on}} [[Humboldt squid]] (''Dosidicus gigas'') caught off the southern [[California]]n coast displaying deep red [[chromatophore|chromatophoric]] colouring.]] [[File:Enteroctopus dofleini in aquarium rotated.jpg|thumb|250px|''[[Enteroctopus dofleini]]'' at the [[National Aquarium in Washington, D.C.|National Aquarium]] in [[Washington, D.C.]]]] [[File:Sepia apama 2.jpg|thumb|250px|''[[Sepia apama]]'', the largest species of cuttlefish, is native to the southern coast of Australia.]] [[File:Sepia pharaonis.jpg|thumb|250px|''[[Sepia pharaonis]]'', a large and commercially important cuttlefish species from the [[Indian Ocean]]]] [[File:Bobtail squid.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Bobtail squid]]s, such as this ''[[Euprymna berryi]]'' from [[East Timor]], are some of the smallest of all cephalopods and are not known to attain a mantle length in excess of {{convert|10|cm|in|abbr=on}}.]] The list of largest cephalopods by mantle length is dominated by squids, with more than twenty species exceeding the largest-bodied octopuses and cuttlefish. The largest of all is the [[colossal squid]] (''Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni'') with an estimated maximum mantle length of {{convert|3|m|ft|abbr=on}} ([[#RoperJereb2010c|Roper & Jereb, 2010c:173]]). Even greater mantle lengths have historically been reported for the [[giant squid]] (''Architeuthis dux''), but these have been discredited (see [[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]]). {| class="wikitable" |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Oegopsida (oegopsid squids)"| [[Oegopsida]] ([[Squid|oegopsid squids]]) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mantle length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni]]''
(colossal squid) || ≈300 cm (estimate) || [[#RoperJereb2010c|Roper & Jereb (2010c:173)]] || The largest complete specimen, a mature female recovered from the [[Ross Sea]] in February 2007, had a mantle length of around 2.5 m ([[#howbig|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]])—this being the maximum scientifically documented mantle length ([[#Rosaetal2017|Rosa ''et al.'', 2017]])—and [[#Colossal squid (Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni)|several other specimens near this size]] have been recorded. However, at 42.5 mm [[lower rostral length|LRL]], its [[cephalopod beak|beak]] is considerably smaller than the largest recovered from a [[sperm whale]] stomach (49 mm LRL; [[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#howbig|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]]). Maximum mantle lengths as great as 4 m have been estimated and reported in the past ([[#Young2003|Young, 2003]]; [[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]]). There are published claims of a very large section of [[Gladius (cephalopod)|gladius]] that would suggest a colossal squid measuring 5 m or more in mantle length ([[#Wood1982|Wood, 1982:191]]; [[#Bright1989|Bright, 1989:146]]).{{refn|group=nb|name=colossalpen|[[#Wood1982|Wood (1982:191)]] provided the following details: "Dr [[Anna McClean Bidder|Anna M Bidder]] (pers. comm.) of the Department of Zoology at [[Cambridge University]], possesses a transverse slice of the [[Gladius (cephalopod)|pen]] of another ''[[Mesonychoteuthis]]'' which, judging by its width, must have come from a [[Cranchiidae|cranchid]] {{sic}} measuring at least {{convert|5|m|ft|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}} in mantle length." The same information is summarised by [[#Bright1989|Bright (1989:146)]].}} |- | ''[[Galiteuthis phyllura]]'' || ? 265–275 cm (estimate) || [[#Nesis1985|Nesis (1985)]]; [[#Nesis1987|Nesis (1987:274)]]; [[#Ellis1998a|Ellis (1998a:149)]]; [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas (2004:65)]]; [[#HovingRobison2017|Hoving & Robison (2017:47)]] || Estimate based on 40 cm long [[cephalopod limb|arm]] and 115 cm [[cephalopod limb|tentacle]] from the [[Sea of Okhotsk]].{{refn|group=nb|name=Galiteuthis|[[File:Galiteuthis phyllura.jpg|thumb|250px|Drawing of ''[[Galiteuthis phyllura]]'' from the ''[[Bulletin of the United States Fish Commission]]'' (1881), showing the proportionately short length of the arms and tentacles]][[#Ellis1998a|Ellis (1998a:148–149)]] wrote of this specimen:
The Russian vessel ''Novoulianovsk'', working in the [[Sea of Okhotsk]] in 1984, brought up the remains of a gigantic specimen of ''[[Galiteuthis phyllura]]'' from a depth of one thousand to thirteen hundred meters (thirty-three hundred to forty-three hundred feet), and [[#Nesis1985|Nesis (1985)]] said that it was "almost as large as ''[[Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni]]'' (of the same family)." Only an arm and a tentacle were collected, but they were so large (the arm was 40 cm long [15.6 inches] and the tentacle {{convert|115|cm|in|round=0.5|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}}) that Nesis was able to estimate the mantle length at {{convert|265|to|275|cm|ft|abbr=on}}, and the total length at over 4 meters (more than 13 feet). "Because of its narrow body," wrote Nesis, "we conclude that its mass is consistently lower than that of the other large squids."
[[#RoperJereb2010c|Roper & Jereb (2010c:165)]] questioned the validity of this record, writing: "this is considered a doubtful record that might refer to total length; probably the maximum mantle length is less than 400 to 500 mm". But [[Steve O'Shea]] commented:
The late, great [[Kir Nesis]] was not one to exaggerate, and must have had very good reason to cite a possible mantle length of 2.7m [{{convert|2.7|m|ft|disp=output only}}] for ''Galiteuthis phyllura''. Not only would such a monstrous mantle render this the 'largest species of ''[[Galiteuthis]]''', but it would give the species a mantle that was considerably longer than that of ''Architeuthis'', and marginally longer (0.2m [{{convert|0.2|m|ft|disp=output only}}]) than the submature Colossal Squid (''Mesonychoteuthis'') we reported last year [in 2003].
What amazes me is that the adult has never made the press (that I am aware of). Even if ''Galiteuthis'' lacks hooks on the arms it would still be a most frightening squid to bump into in the abyss. ([[#OShea2004b|O'Shea, 2004b]])
}} [[#RoperJereb2010c|Roper & Jereb (2010c:165)]] write: "this is considered a doubtful record that might refer to total length; probably the maximum mantle length is less than 400 to 500 mm". |- | ''[[Architeuthis dux]]''
(giant squid) || 240 cm (female) || [[#Landmanetal2004|Landman ''et al.'' (2004:686)]]; [[#OShea2006|O'Shea (2006)]]; [[#RoperShea2013|Roper & Shea (2013:114)]] || Dorsal mantle length of female captured off [[Tasmania]], [[Australia]], reported by [[#Landmanetal2004|Landman ''et al.'' (2004:686)]] and cited by [[#RoperShea2013|Roper & Shea (2013:114)]]. Questionable records of up to 500 cm ML can be found in older literature ([[#RoperJereb2010a|Roper & Jereb, 2010a:121]]). [[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad (2008)]] give a maximum mantle length of 225 cm based on the examination of more than 130 specimens, as well as beaks recovered from [[sperm whale]]s (which do not exceed the size of those found in the largest complete specimens). [[#Paxton2016a|Paxton (2016a)]] accepts a maximum recorded ML of 279 cm, based on the [[Lyall Bay]] specimen reported by [[#Kirk1880|Kirk (1880:312)]], but this record has been called into question as the [[Gladius (cephalopod)|gladius]] of this specimen—which should approximate the mantle in length—was said to be only 190 cm long ([[#Greshko2016|Greshko, 2016]]).{{refn|group=nb|name=Kirk1880}} |- | ''[[Onykia robusta]]''
(robust clubhook squid) || 200 cm || [[#Norman2000|Norman (2000:174)]]; [[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad (2008:107)]]; [[#Okutani2015b|Okutani (2015b)]] || [[#Kuboderaetal1998|Kubodera ''et al.'' (1998)]] give a maximum of at least 161.5 cm ML. The largest specimen seen by [[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad (2008:107)]] had a mantle length of 197 cm (USNM 816872; specimen of indeterminate sex from {{coord|51|46.9|N|177|39.7|E|name=Onykia robusta specimen (197 cm ML)}}). [[#Verrill1876|Verrill (1876:237)]] reported a specimen with a mantle length of {{convert|91.5|in|cm|order=flip|abbr=on}} and a total length of {{convert|14|ft|m|order=flip|abbr=on}} (excluding the ends of the tentacles, which had been destroyed). [[#Nesis1987|Nesis (1987:192)]] likewise gave a maximum mantle length of 230 cm, but [[#RoperJereb2010i|Roper & Jereb (2010i:364)]] wrote that "this old record might be in error", with the species commonly growing to 160 cm ML. Previously known as ''Moroteuthis robusta'' (see [[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#Bolstad2010|Bolstad, 2010]]). |- | ''[[Megalocranchia maxima]]'' || 185 cm (female) || [[#KuboderaHorikawa2005|Kubodera & Horikawa (2005:210)]] || Size of female caught off [[Motobu Peninsula]], [[Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa]], [[Japan]], identified as "''Megalocranchia'' [[cf.]] ''maxima''" (see [[#KuboderaHorikawa2005|Kubodera & Horikawa, 2005:223]] for photograph). This species is listed under the name ''[[Megalocranchia fisheri]]'' in many older sources. [[#TsuchiyaOkutani1993|Tsuchiya & Okutani (1993)]], [[#RoperJereb2010c|Roper & Jereb (2010c:171)]] and [[#Okutani2015a|Okutani (2015a)]] give maximum of 180 cm, and [[#Norman2000|Norman (2000:158)]] gives the same for ''M. fisheri''. This species may also be conspecific with ''[[Megalocranchia abyssicola]]'' ([[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas, 2004:65]]). |- | ''[[Taningia danae]]''
(Dana octopus squid) || 170 cm || [[#Nesis1982|Nesis (1982)]]; [[#RoperJereb2010h|Roper & Jereb (2010h:266)]] || The largest well documented specimen is a 160 cm ML mature female from the North Atlantic ([[#RoperVecchione1993|Roper & Vecchione, 1993:449]]). |- | ''[[Dosidicus gigas]]''
(Humboldt squid) || 150 cm || [[#Wormuth1976|Wormuth (1976:38)]]; [[#Norman2000|Norman (2000:165)]]; [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas (2004:54)]] || According to [[#Wormuth1976|Wormuth (1976:38)]], specimens reaching 150 cm ML are "not uncommon" off [[Peru]]. [[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'' (2010b:301)]] give a maximum mantle length of 120 cm for specimens off [[Chile]] and around 100 cm for northern populations, with a more typical mantle length of up to 50–80 cm. The review article of [[#Nigmatullinetal2001|Nigmatullin ''et al.'' (2001)]]—based on {{circa}} 230 published papers on the species, in addition to other catch data—also gives a maximum mantle length of 120 cm. |- | ''[[Kondakovia longimana]]''
(giant warty squid) || ≈150 cm (estimate; female) || [[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad (2008:171)]] || Estimated size of damaged female (NMV F109447; specimen with 21 mm LRL from {{coord|63|04.72|S| 62|56.02|E|name=Kondakovia longimana specimen (150 cm ML)}}). [[#OShea2003b|O'Shea (2003b)]] estimated maximum mantle length as probably exceeding 115 cm. Largest complete specimen measured 108 cm ML ([[#LynnesRodhouse2002|Lynnes & Rodhouse, 2002:1087]]; [[#RoperJereb2010i|Roper & Jereb, 2010i:366]]). |- | ''[[Mastigoteuthis cordiformis]]'' || 100 cm or more || [[#RoperJereb2010g|Roper & Jereb (2010g:253)]] || Based on unpublished reports; largest verified ML is 70 cm ([[#RoperJereb2010g|Roper & Jereb, 2010g:253]]). |- | ''[[Lepidoteuthis grimaldii]]''
(Grimaldi scaled squid) || 100 cm || [[#RoperJereb2010e|Roper & Jereb (2010e:240)]] || |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Myopsida (myopsid squids)"| [[Myopsida]] (myopsid squids) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mantle length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- |''[[Loligo forbesi]]''
(long finned squid)|| 93.7 cm ||||Maximum mantle length is 93.7 cm in males and 46.2 cm in females.[https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/marine-biodiversity-records/article/abs/azorean-loligo-forbesi-cephalopoda-loliginidae-in-captivity-transport-handling-maintenance-tagging-and-survival/B348C459B4EDFB2A530EA33DA4C88CB4 The Azorean Loligo forbesi (Cephalopoda: Loliginidae) in captivity: transport, handling, maintenance, tagging and survival][https://assets.researchsquare.com/files/rs-1926159/v1/a1b1c776-782d-4066-9c21-4b5cbb6aa7a6.pdf?c=1694444921 Body size and fin length as determinants in the geographic distribution of Loliginid squids] |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Octopoda (octopuses)"| [[Octopoda]] (octopuses) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mantle length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Haliphron atlanticus]]''
(seven-arm octopus) || 69 cm (female) || [[#OShea2002|O'Shea (2002:1)]]; [[#OShea2004a|O'Shea (2004a:9)]]; [[#Finn2014a|Finn (2014a:227)]] || Mantle length of incomplete 2.90 m TL mature female, measured defrosted and wet, prior to fixing. Isolated beaks of comparable size to that of this specimen were recorded by [[#Clarke1986|Clarke (1986:247–248)]]. The [[Sexual dimorphism|sexually dimorphic]] males reach a mantle length of over 10 cm ([[#Finn2014a|Finn, 2014a:227]]). |- | ''[[Enteroctopus dofleini]]''
(giant Pacific octopus) || at least 60 cm || [[#Norman2000|Norman (2000:214)]]; [[#Normanetal2014|Norman ''et al.'' (2014:124)]] || |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Sepiida (cuttlefish)"| [[Sepiida]] (cuttlefish) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mantle length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Sepia apama]]''
(Australian giant cuttlefish) || 50 cm || [[#Reidetal2005|Reid ''et al.'' (2005:68)]] || |- | ''[[Sepia latimanus]]''
(broadclub cuttlefish) || 50 cm || [[#Reidetal2005|Reid ''et al.'' (2005:92)]] || |- | ''[[Sepia hierredda]]'' || <50 cm || [[#Reidetal2005|Reid ''et al.'' (2005:88)]] || |- | ''[[Sepia officinalis]]''
(European common cuttlefish) || 49 cm || [[#Reidetal2005|Reid ''et al.'' (2005:99)]] || |- | ''[[Sepia pharaonis]]''
(pharaoh cuttlefish) || 42 cm || [[#Reidetal2005|Reid ''et al.'' (2005:107)]] || |- | ''[[Sepia lycidas]]''
(kisslip cuttlefish) || 38 cm || [[#Reidetal2005|Reid ''et al.'' (2005:96)]] || |- | ''[[Sepia ramani]]'' || 37.5 cm || [[#Reidetal2005|Reid ''et al.'' (2005:114)]] || |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Vampyromorphida (vampire squid)"| [[Vampyromorphida]] (vampire squid) – single extant species |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mantle length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Vampyroteuthis infernalis]]''
(vampire squid) || 13 cm || [[#Nesis1982|Nesis (1982)]]; [[#NormanFinn2014|Norman & Finn (2014:269)]] || |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Sepiolida (bobtail squids)"| [[Sepiolida]] (bobtail squids) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mantle length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Austrorossia antillensis]]'' || 9 cm || [[#ReidJereb2005|Reid & Jereb (2005:192)]] || |- | ''[[Rossia pacifica]]'' || 9 cm (female) || [[#ReidJereb2005|Reid & Jereb (2005:185)]] || Males grow to 4.5 cm in mantle length. |- | ''[[Rossia macrosoma]]'' || 8.5 cm || [[#ReidJereb2005|Reid & Jereb (2005:184)]] || More typically the mantle length is 2.0–6.0 cm. |- | ''[[Neorossia caroli]]'' || 8.3 cm (female) || [[#ReidJereb2005|Reid & Jereb (2005:190)]] || Males grow to 5.1 cm in mantle length. |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Spirulida (spirula)"| [[Spirulida]] (spirula) – single extant species |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mantle length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Spirula spirula]]''
(ram's horn squid) || rarely exceeds 4.5 cm || [[#Reid2005|Reid (2005:211)]] || |} {{Clear}} ====Total length==== [[File:Archie2.jpg|thumb|250px|'Archie', a [[giant squid]] specimen at the [[Natural History Museum, London|Natural History Museum]] in [[London]], measures {{convert|8.62|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length (see [[#Ablett2012|Ablett, 2012]] for more on this specimen)]] [[File:LongArmSquid.jpg|thumb|250px|A bigfin squid (cf. ''[[Bigfin squid|Magnapinna]]''), one of the longest known cephalopods. This specimen was filmed in October 2000 by [[DSV Alvin|DSV ''Alvin'']] in the [[Gulf of Mexico]], at {{convert|1940|m|ft|abbr=on}} depth.]] [[File:Ommastrephes_bartramii_(Neon_flying_squid),_Northern_Hawaiian_waters.jpg|thumb|250px|A freshly caught specimen of ''[[Ommastrephes bartramii]]'' from northern [[Hawaii]]an waters. This species grows to a total length of {{convert|2|m|ft|abbr=on}}.]] [[File:North Pacific Giant Octopus.JPG|thumb|250px|Large specimens of ''[[Enteroctopus dofleini]]'' can exceed {{convert|3|m|ft|abbr=on}} in total length.]] The longest scientifically documented specimens belong to the [[giant squid]], with a maximum total length of {{convert|14|–|15|m|ft|abbr=on}} ([[#RoperShea2013|Roper & Shea, 2013:114]]). Despite its proportionally shorter tentacles, the [[colossal squid]] may rival the giant squid in total length, but the species's size limits are uncertain because only a handful of mature specimens have been recorded. {| class="wikitable" |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Teuthida (squids)"| [[Teuthida]] (squids) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum total length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Architeuthis dux]]''
(giant squid) || 14–15 m (female) || [[#RoperShea2013|Roper & Shea (2013:114)]] || Based on a 40-year data set of more than 50 specimens, [[#RoperShea2013|Roper & Shea (2013:114)]] suggest an average total length at maturity of 11 m and a "rarely encountered maximum length" of 14–15 m. Of the nearly 100 specimens examined by [[Clyde Roper|Roper]], the largest was "46 feet (14 m) long" ([[#CerulloRoper2012|Cerullo & Roper, 2012:22]]). [[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad (2008)]] give a maximum total length of 13 m for females based on the examination of more than 130 specimens, measured ''post mortem'' and relaxed, as well as beaks recovered from [[sperm whale]]s (which do not exceed the size of those found in the largest complete specimens). [[Steve O'Shea|O'Shea]] estimated the maximum total length for males at 10 m ([[#OShea2003a|O'Shea, 2003a]]). Older records of 18 m or more were likely exaggerated by stretching of the long [[cephalopod limb|feeding tentacles]] or resulted from inadequate measurement methods such as pacing ([[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#RoperShea2013|Roper & Shea, 2013:113]]). [[#Paxton2016a|Paxton (2016a)]] performed a statistical analysis using [[List of giant squid specimens and sightings|literature records of giant squid specimens]] and concluded that "squid with a conservative TL of 20 m would seem likely based on current data", but the study has been heavily criticised by experts in the field ([[#Greshko2016|Greshko, 2016]]). |- | ''[[Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni]]''
(colossal squid) || approaching 9–10 m (estimate) || [[#RoperJereb2010c|Roper & Jereb (2010c:173)]] || The maximum confirmed total length for this species is 6 m ([[#Rosaetal2017|Rosa ''et al.'', 2017]]). [[List of colossal squid specimens and sightings|Two specimens of ''M. hamiltoni'']] recovered from the stomachs of [[sperm whale]]s between 1956 and 1957 off the [[South Shetland Islands]] and [[South Orkney Islands]]—both initially identified as ''Architeuthis''—reportedly measured around 10 and 12 m, respectively ([[#SweeneyRoper2001|Sweeney & Roper, 2001:56]]; see [[#Korabelnikov1959|Korabelnikov, 1959:103]] and [[#Yukhov1974|Yukhov, 1974:62]]). Estimated maximum lengths as great as 12–14 m have appeared in the popular literature (see [[#Anderton2007|Anderton, 2007]]). |- | cf. ''[[Magnapinna]]''
(bigfin squid) || ≈7 m (estimate) || [[#Vecchioneetal2001a|Vecchione ''et al.'' (2001a:2505)]]; [[#Vecchioneetal2001b|Vecchione ''et al.'' (2001b)]]; [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas (2004:67)]]; [[#RoperJereb2010f|Roper & Jereb (2010f:247)]] || Estimate based on specimen observed by commercial [[Remotely operated underwater vehicle|ROV]] operated from the oil-drilling ship ''[[Millennium Explorer]]'' in January 2000, [[Mississippi Canyon]], [[Gulf of Mexico]] ({{coord|28|37|N|88|0|W|name=Bigfin squid observed from ROV, January 2000}}), at a depth of 2195 m ([[#Vecchioneetal2001b|Vecchione ''et al.'', 2001b]]). [[#Bolstad2003|Bolstad (2003)]] gives an estimate of at least 8 m TL for the largest observed specimen. |- | ''[[Asperoteuthis acanthoderma]]'' || 5.5 m (+) || [[#TsuchiyaOkutani1993|Tsuchiya & Okutani (1993)]] || Total length of immature specimen measuring 0.45 m ML. Much larger specimens of up to 92 cm ML are known (see [[#KuboderaHorikawa2005|Kubodera & Horikawa, 2005:223]] for photograph). |- | ''[[Onykia robusta]]''
(robust clubhook squid) || {{convert|14|ft|m|order=flip|abbr=on}} (+)|| [[#Verrill1876|Verrill (1876:237)]] || Total length of specimen missing ends of tentacles, with a mantle length of {{convert|91.5|in|m|order=flip|abbr=on|sigfig=3}} according to [[#Verrill1876|Verrill (1876:237)]]. [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas (2004:66)]] give maximum total length of 4–6 m. Previously known as ''Moroteuthis robusta'' (see [[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#Bolstad2010|Bolstad, 2010]]). |- | ''[[Galiteuthis phyllura]]'' || ? over 4 m (estimate) || [[#Nesis1985|Nesis (1985)]]; [[#Ellis1998a|Ellis (1998a:149)]]; [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas (2004:65)]] || Estimate based on 0.40 m long [[cephalopod limb|arm]] and 1.15 m [[cephalopod limb|tentacle]] from the [[Sea of Okhotsk]].{{refn|group=nb|name=Galiteuthis}} [[#RoperJereb2010c|Roper & Jereb (2010c:165)]] cast doubt on the validity of this record. |- | ''[[Dosidicus gigas]]''
(Humboldt squid) || possibly up to {{convert|12|ft|m|order=flip|abbr=on}} || [[#Clarke1966|Clarke (1966:117)]]; [[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas (2004:59)]] || Specimens from the [[Northern Hemisphere]] are much smaller, with those off the [[California]]n coast reaching total lengths of less than 1.7 m ([[#GlaubrechtSalcedo-Vargas2004|Glaubrecht & Salcedo-Vargas, 2004:59]]). [[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'' (2010b:301)]] give maximum total length of close to 2.5 m for specimens off [[Chile]]. |- | ''[[Megalocranchia maxima]]'' || 2.7 m (+) (female) || [[#YoungMangold2010|Young & Mangold (2010)]] || Total length of 1.65 m ML female taken off [[Hawaii]] (see [[#YoungMangold2010|Young & Mangold, 2010]] for photograph). Larger specimens of up to 1.85 m ML have been recorded, and these clearly exceed 2.7 m TL (see [[#KuboderaHorikawa2005|Kubodera & Horikawa, 2005:223]] for photograph). |- | ''[[Taningia danae]]''
(Dana octopus squid) || 2.3 m (female) || [[#RoperVecchione1993|Roper & Vecchione (1993:444)]] || Total length of mature female measuring 160 cm in mantle length, taken from frozen specimen. |- | ''[[Kondakovia longimana]]''
(giant warty squid) || 2.25 m (+) || [[#LynnesRodhouse2002|Lynnes & Rodhouse (2002:1087)]] || Size of largest complete specimen (1.08 m ML), found floating at surface off [[South Orkney Islands]] (see also [[#Carrington2000|Carrington, 2000]]). Much larger specimens up to an estimated 1.5 m ML are known ([[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad, 2008:171]]). |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Octopoda (octopuses)"| [[Octopoda]] (octopuses) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum total length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Enteroctopus dofleini]]''
(giant Pacific octopus) || >6.1 m || [[#Cosgrove1987|Cosgrove (1987)]] || [[#Normanetal2014|Norman ''et al.'' (2014:124)]] give the maximum total length as "more than 3 m". Questionable length records of up to 9.8 m can be found in the literature (see [[#High1976|High, 1976:18]]). |- | [[Cirrina]] {{abbr|''gen. et sp. indet.''|genus and species indeterminate}} || over 4 m (estimate) || [[#Voss1988|Voss (1988)]]; [[#Vecchioneetal2008|Vecchione ''et al.'' (2008)]] || Estimate based on photographic record; finned octopods are known with certainty to reach at least 1.7 m in total length ([[#Collinsetal2001|Collins ''et al.'', 2001]]). |- | ''[[Haliphron atlanticus]]''
(seven-arm octopus) || 4 m (estimate; female) || [[#OShea2004a|O'Shea (2004a:9)]]; [[#Finn2014a|Finn (2014a:227)]] || Estimate based on incomplete 2.90 m mature female, measured defrosted and wet, prior to fixing. Isolated beaks of comparable size to that of this specimen were recorded by [[#Clarke1986|Clarke (1986:247–248)]]. Males are estimated to reach a total length of 21 cm ([[#Finn2014a|Finn, 2014a:227]]) and perhaps as much as 30 cm ([[#Normanetal2002|Norman ''et al.'', 2002:733]]). |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Vampyromorphida (vampire squid)"| [[Vampyromorphida]] (vampire squid) – single extant species |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum total length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Vampyroteuthis infernalis]]''
(vampire squid) || ≈30 cm || [[#NormanFinn2014|Norman & Finn (2014:269)]] || |} {{Clear}} ====Mass==== [[File:NZ070415 Colossal Squid 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The {{convert|495|kg|lb|adj=on}} colossal squid on display at the [[Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa]]. It is the most massive extant cephalopod on record.]] [[File:Taningia danae2.gif|thumb|250px|The largest documented specimen of ''[[Taningia danae|T. danae]]'', preserved at the [[National Museum of Natural History]]. It originally weighed {{convert|161.4|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and had a mantle length of {{convert|160|cm|ft|abbr=on}}.]] [[File:Onykia robsoni 885 mm ML.jpg|thumb|250px|A very large mature female of ''[[Onykia robsoni]]'' with a mantle length of {{convert|88.5|cm|ft|abbr=on}} and total weight of {{convert|11.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}}]] [[File:Lepidoteuthis grimaldii 617 mm ML.jpg|thumb|250px|''[[Lepidoteuthis grimaldii]]'' female measuring {{convert|61.7|cm|ft|abbr=on}} in mantle length and weighing {{convert|4.07|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, from the [[Chatham Rise]] off [[New Zealand]] (note the prominent [[Cephalopod dermal structures|dermal cushions]], likely used for [[buoyancy]])]] [[File:E dofleini closeup.jpg|thumb|250px|The maximum weight of the [[giant Pacific octopus]] (''Enteroctopus dofleini'') has been much debated, with numerous reports of specimens exceeding {{convert|100|kg|lb|abbr=on}}]] [[File:Ascarosepion latimanus (Reef cuttlefish) dark coloration.jpg|thumb|250px|''[[Sepia latimanus]]'' is the second largest cuttlefish species, closely rivalling ''[[Sepia apama|S. apama]]'' in both mantle length and mass.]] The heaviest known cephalopod, and the largest living invertebrate, is the [[colossal squid]]. The largest recorded specimen of this species, caught in the [[Ross Sea]] in 2007, weighed {{convert|495|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. However, its beak is not the largest known from this species; even bigger colossal squid beaks have been recovered from the stomachs of [[sperm whale]]s, indicating that this species can grow larger still. {| class="wikitable" |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Teuthida (squids)"| [[Teuthida]] (squids) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mass ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni]]''
(colossal squid) || 495 kg (female) || [[#howbig|[Anonymous] ({{abbr|N.d.|No date}})]]; [[#GuerraSegonzac2014|Guerra & Segonzac (2014:65)]]; [[#Rosaetal2017|Rosa ''et al.'' (2017)]] || Weight of mature female specimen caught in February 2007, measured after thawing. This specimen was originally estimated to weigh 450 kg ([[#Anderton2007|Anderton, 2007]]). Several other specimens with weights in the hundreds of kilograms have been recorded. Beaks recovered from [[sperm whale]] stomachs indicate the existence of even larger specimens, perhaps weighing as much as 600–700 kg ([[#beak|[Anonymous], {{abbr|N.d.|No date}}]]). |- | ''[[Architeuthis dux]]''
(giant squid) || 275 kg (female) || [[#OShea2003a|O'Shea (2003a)]]; [[#GristJackson2007|Grist & Jackson (2007:386)]]; [[#GuerraSegonzac2014|Guerra & Segonzac (2014:65)]] || Maximum size based on the examination of some 105 specimens by [[#OShea2003a|O'Shea (2003a)]], as well as beaks recovered from [[sperm whale]]s (which do not exceed the size of those found in the largest complete specimens). Maximum weight for males has been estimated at 150 kg ([[#OShea2003a|O'Shea, 2003a]]; see also [[#GristJackson2007|Grist & Jackson, 2007:386]]), though heavier specimens have occasionally been reported (see [[#Deagleetal2005|Deagle ''et al.'', 2005]] for 190 kg specimen, [[#Hofilena2014|Hofilena, 2014]] for 163 kg specimen). [[#RoperJereb2010a|Roper & Jereb (2010a:121)]] give a maximum weight of up to 500 kg, and "possibly greater". Discredited weights of as much as a [[tonne]] or more are not uncommon in older literature ([[#OSheaBolstad2008|O'Shea & Bolstad, 2008]]). |- | ''[[Taningia danae]]''
(Dana octopus squid) || 161.4 kg (female) || [[#RoperJereb2010h|Roper & Jereb (2010h:266)]] || Weight of 160 cm ML mature female from North Atlantic. Specimen weighed prior to freezing ([[#RoperVecchione1993|Roper & Vecchione, 1993:444]]). According to [[#RoperJereb2010h|Roper & Jereb (2010h:266)]], the previously reported maximum weight of 61.4 kg (based on the same specimen) stems from a typographical error in the original paper of [[#RoperVecchione1993|Roper & Vecchione (1993)]]. This lower value was repeated by a number of subsequent authors, including [[#Santosetal2001|Santos ''et al.'' (2001:355)]] and [[#Kuboderaetal2006|Kubodera ''et al.'' (2006:1029)]]. |- | ''[[Onykia robusta]]''
(robust clubhook squid) || 50 kg || [[#Roperetal1984|Roper ''et al.'' (1984)]]; [[#RoperJereb2010i|Roper & Jereb (2010i:364)]]; [[#GuerraSegonzac2014|Guerra & Segonzac (2014:65)]]; [[#Okutani2015b|Okutani (2015b)]] || Previously known as ''Moroteuthis robusta'' (see [[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#Bolstad2010|Bolstad, 2010]]). |- | ''[[Dosidicus gigas]]''
(Humboldt squid) || 50 kg || [[#Nigmatullinetal2001|Nigmatullin ''et al.'' (2001:10)]]; [[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'' (2010b:301)]]; [[#AnonymousNd|[Anonymous] ({{abbr|N.d.|No date}})]] || Commonly reaches a maximum weight of around 20–30 kg ([[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'', 2010b:301]]). In their introduction to the family [[Ommastrephidae]], [[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'' (2010b:269)]] give a maximum weight of 55–65 kg, but this is contradicted later in the same work by the 50 kg figure in the main species account. [[#GuerraSegonzac2014|Guerra & Segonzac (2014:65)]] give a maximum weight of 90 kg. |- | ''[[Kondakovia longimana]]''
(giant warty squid) || 50 kg (estimate) || [[#GuerraSegonzac2014|Guerra & Segonzac (2014:65)]] || Largest complete specimen, found floating at surface off [[South Orkney Islands]], had a wet weight of 29 kg and a mantle length of 108 cm ([[#LynnesRodhouse2002|Lynnes & Rodhouse, 2002:1087]]; see also [[#Carrington2000|Carrington, 2000]]), but a damaged female specimen with an estimated mantle length of around 150 cm is known ([[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad, 2008:171]]). |- | ''[[Thysanoteuthis rhombus]]''
(diamondback squid) || 30 kg || [[#Miyaharaetal2006|Miyahara ''et al.'' (2006)]]; [[#RoperJereb2010k|Roper & Jereb (2010k:385)]] || Probably exceeds the recorded mass of 30 kg according to [[#RoperJereb2010k|Roper & Jereb (2010k:385)]]. |- | ''[[Ommastrephes bartramii]]''
(neon flying squid) || 20–25 kg (female) || [[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'' (2010b:296)]] || Maximum size of specimens from North Atlantic and [[Southern Hemisphere]] (where males reach 2–2.2 kg). Females from the North Pacific are smaller (6 kg), but males may be larger (2–2.9 kg) ([[#Roperetal2010b|Roper ''et al.'', 2010b:296]]). |- | ''[[Megalocranchia maxima]]'' || {{convert|52.7|lb|kg|order=flip|abbr=on}} (+)|| [[#Anonymous2015b|[Anonymous] (2015b)]]; [[#Mosendz2015|Mosendz (2015)]] || Mass of specimen found floating dead off [[Hawaii]], estimated to measure at least {{convert|7|ft|m|order=flip|abbr=on}} TL, tentatively identified as ''[[Megalocranchia fisheri]]''. Larger specimens reaching and even exceeding 2.7 m TL have been recorded, though were apparently not weighed (see [[#KuboderaHorikawa2005|Kubodera & Horikawa, 2005:223]]; [[#YoungMangold2010|Young & Mangold, 2010]]). |- | ''[[Onykia robsoni]]''
(rugose hooked squid) || 11.1 kg (female) || [[#Vecchioneetal2011|Vecchione ''et al.'' (2011)]] || Weight of mature female (88.5 cm ML) caught in bottom trawl at 685–700 m depth over [[Chatham Rise]] ({{coord|44|21|S|175|32|E|name=Onykia robsoni specimen (88.5 cm ML)}}). Previously known as ''Moroteuthis robsoni'' (see [[#Bolstad2008|Bolstad, 2008]]; [[#Bolstad2010|Bolstad, 2010]]). |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Octopoda (octopuses)"| [[Octopoda]] (octopuses) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mass ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Enteroctopus dofleini]]''
(giant Pacific octopus) || ? >180 kg || [[#Normanetal2014|Norman ''et al.'' (2014:124)]] || [[#Cosgrove1987|Cosgrove (1987)]] and [[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel (2009:69)]] gave a maximum confirmed weight of 71 kg for a live specimen collected in the mid-1960s ([[#McClainetal2015|McClain ''et al.'', 2015]]). There exists a dubious record of a 272 kg specimen which is sometimes cited as the largest known (see [[#High1976|High, 1976:18]]; [[#HochbergFields1980|Hochberg & Fields, 1980:436]]; [[#Lewy2002|Lewy, 2002:65]]), although it was never actually collected and weighed ([[#Newman1994|Newman, 1994:66]]; [[#McClainetal2015|McClain ''et al.'', 2015]]). [[#Normanetal2014|Norman ''et al.'' (2014:124)]] accept a maximum weight of at least 180 kg, which approximates the 182.3 kg reported for a specimen caught off [[Santa Barbara, California|Santa Barbara]], [[California]], in 1945, of which photographic evidence survives ([[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:67–69]]). No specimens approaching this size have been reported since the middle of the 20th century, with recent specimens very rarely exceeding 50 kg ([[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:71]]). It is possible that the maximum size of the species has decreased over this period, perhaps due to [[bioaccumulation]] of [[toxicant]]s ([[#Anderson2003|Anderson, 2003:3]]; [[#CosgroveMcDaniel2009|Cosgrove & McDaniel, 2009:71]]). |- | ''[[Haliphron atlanticus]]''
(seven-arm octopus) || 75 kg (estimate; female) || [[#OShea2004a|O'Shea (2004a:9)]] || Estimate based on incomplete 61.0 kg mature female, measured defrosted and wet, prior to fixing. Isolated beaks of comparable size to that of the present specimen were recorded by [[#Clarke1986|Clarke (1986:247–248)]]. |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Sepiida (cuttlefish)"| [[Sepiida]] (cuttlefish) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mass ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Sepia apama]]''

(2005:99)]] || |} {{Clear}} ====Shell diameter==== [[File:Argonauta species.PNG|thumb|250px|Eggcases of six extant ''[[Argonaut (animal)|Argonauta]]'' species (not to scale)]] [[File:Argonauta hians.JPG|thumb|250px|A giant specimen of ''[[Argonauta hians]]'' from [[Taiwan]], which—at roughly {{convert|121.5|mm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter—approximates the length of the official world record size shell.]] [[File:Fish3214 - Flickr - NOAA Photo Library.jpg|thumb|250px|Young ''[[Argonauta argo|A. argo]]'' female with eggcase]] [[File:Nautilus species shells.png|thumb|250px|Nautilus shells: ''[[Nautilus macromphalus|N. macromphalus]]'' (left), ''[[Allonautilus scrobiculatus|A. scrobiculatus]]'' (centre), and ''[[Nautilus pompilius|N. pompilius]]'']] [[File:Nautilus belauensis from Palau.jpg|thumb|250px|''[[Nautilus belauensis]]'' off [[Palau]]]] [[File:Spirula fg1.jpg|thumb|250px|Two examples of the small internal shell of ''[[Spirula spirula]]'']] [[Nautilus]]es are the only extant [[cephalopod]]s with a true external shell; in other groups the shell has been internalised or lost completely. Internal shells include the [[cuttlebone]]s of cuttlefish, the [[Gladius (cephalopod)|gladii]] of squids and the vampire squid, the [[Cirrate shell|winged shells]] of cirrate octopods, and the spiral shells of ''[[Spirula]]''. Additionally, females of the octopus genus ''[[Argonaut (animal)|Argonauta]]'' secrete a specialised paper-thin eggcase in which they reside, and this is popularly regarded as a "shell", although it is not attached to the body of the animal (see [[#Finn2013|Finn, 2013]]). Cephalopod shell diameter is of interest to [[teuthologist]]s and [[conchologist]]s alike. The ''[[Registry of World Record Size Shells]]'', the most comprehensive publication on maximum shell size in marine molluscs, specifies that specimens "should be measured with [[vernier calipers|vernier type calipers]] and should reflect the greatest measurable dimension of the shell in any direction including any processes of hard shell material produced by the animal (i.e. spines, wings, keels, siphonal canals, etc.) and not including attachments, [[barnacle]]s, [[coralline algae]], or any other encrusting organisms" ([[#Pisor2008|Pisor, 2008:14]]). Unlike most other measures of cephalopod size, shell diameter can be determined with a high degree of precision and usually leaves little room for ambiguity. For this reason it is usually recorded to the nearest one-tenth of a millimetre ({{convert|0.1|mm|in|disp=output only}}), as is standard in [[conchology]]. When the ''[[Registry of World Record Size Shells]]'' changed ownership in 2008 it was launched as an online database in addition to its print publication. Subsequent rule changes meant that all records required photographic verification. Over time, older records for which photographic evidence could not be obtained were removed from the database. As a result, some records from older editions of the registry actually exceed the size of the current official record holders, sometimes by considerable margins. Where this has occurred, the largest recorded size across all editions is shown first and any discrepancies or competing records are noted thereafter. Where a reliable literature record surpasses all specimens ever included in the registry, this is given instead and the registry record(s) noted thereafter. [[#Pisor2008|Pisor (2008)]] was the fifth and final print edition of the registry published prior to the rule change, and [[#WRS|Barbier ''et al.'' ({{abbr|N.d.|No date}})]] is the current, continuously updated online database. The registry only covers the shells of [[nautilus]]es and ''[[Spirula]]'' and the eggcases of ''Argonauta''. {| class="wikitable" |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Octopoda (octopuses)"| [[Octopoda]] (octopuses) – all extant ''[[Argonaut (animal)|Argonauta]]'' species listed |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum shell diameter ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Argonauta argo]]''
(greater argonaut) || 300.0 mm || [[#Pisor2008|Pisor (2008:22)]]; [[#WRS|Barbier ''et al.'' ({{abbr|N.d.|No date}})]] || [[#Pisor2008|Pisor (2008:22)]] listed this size for a specimen from [[Australia]] (registered in 1991; in collection of [[Scripps Institution of Oceanography|SIO]]). [[#WRS|Barbier ''et al.'' ({{abbr|N.d.|No date}})]] list exactly the same size for a specimen from the [[Philippines]] (year given as 1985; in private collection of Beals Marty). For a discussion of maximum shell size in this species, see [[#Finn2013|Finn (2013:199)]]. The largest literature record (and the only one to exceed the official world record size) appears to be the rough upper limit of {{convert|12|in|mm|sigfig=4|abbr=on}} given by [[#Webb1945|Webb (1945:13)]] for the species as a whole ([[#Finn2013|Finn, 2013:199]]). Under ''[[Argonauta cygnus]]'', [[#Pisor2008|Pisor (2008:22)]] listed a 220.0 mm record for a specimen from western [[Mexico]] (acquired in 1995; in collection of [[Scripps Institution of Oceanography|SIO]]). The same specimen was previously listed under ''[[Argonauta pacificus]]'' (as ''A. pacifica'') by [[#Pisor2005|Pisor (2005:12)]]. Both ''A. cygnus'' and ''A. pacificus'' are considered synonyms of ''A. argo'' by [[#Finn2013|Finn (2013)]] and [[#Finn2014b|Finn (2014b)]]. |- | ''[[Argonauta nodosus]]''
(knobbed argonaut) || 292.0 mm || [[#Pisor2008|Pisor (2008:22)]] || Size of a specimen from [[South Australia]] (registered in 1977; in collection of [[American Museum of Natural History|AMNH]]). [[#WRS|Barbier ''et al.'' ({{abbr|N.d.|No date}})]] list a record of 204.0 mm for a specimen from the "[[Tasmania]] area", [[Australia]] (year given as 2000; in private collection of B. Henke). For a discussion of maximum shell size in this species, see [[#Finn2013|Finn (2013:167)]]; a number of unconfirmed reports put the maximum size beyond 300 mm. |- | ''[[Argonauta hians]]''
(muddy argonaut) || 121.61 mm || [[#WRS|Barbier ''et al.'' [[#HarasewychMoretzsohn2010|Harasewych & Moretzsohn (2010:632)]] give a maximum shell diameter of 268 mm for this species, but this is based on an erroneous record.{{refn|group=nb|name=error|This record is based on a shell collected in 2001 from the [[Timor Sea]] off [[Indonesia]], which was sold in 2003 as a specimen of ''N. repertus'' with a diameter of {{convert|268|mm|in|abbr=on}}. The fossil pictured is the original held at the [[Westfälisches Museum für Naturkunde]] in [[Münster]], but many casts exist.]] [[File:Parapuzosia seppenradensis (Plate I).jpg|thumb|250px|The earliest discovered fossil of ''[[Parapuzosia seppenradensis|P. seppenradensis]]'', as it appeared at the [[Westphalian Museum of Natural History]] in 1895. A human skeleton provided scale, while an oil painted background reconstructed the living chamber and animal itself, together with assorted [[Cretaceous]] sealife.]] [[File:The Biggest Ammonite Found in Bulgaria National Museum of Natural History Sofia.JPG|thumb|250px|The largest ammonite ever found in [[Bulgaria]]: a 1.44 m ''[[Parapuzosia]]'' sp. collected near [[Shumen]] in 1932. It is on display at the [[National Museum of Natural History (Bulgaria)|National Museum of Natural History]] in [[Sofia]].]] [[File:Pachydesmoceras pachydiscoide.jpg|thumb|250px|Giant fossil of ''[[Pachydesmoceras pachydiscoide]]'' from [[Mikasa, Hokkaido|Mikasa]], [[Hokkaido]], [[Japan]], exhibited at the [[National Museum of Nature and Science]] in [[Tokyo]]]] [[File:Titanites giganteus.JPG|thumb|250px|''[[Titanites giganteus]]'' at the [[Natural History Museum, London|Natural History Museum]] in [[London]]]] [[File:Baculites grandis shell.JPG|thumb|250px|The uncoiled shell of ''[[Baculites grandis]]'', a species of [[heteromorph ammonite]]]] [[File:Megateuthis 1.JPG|thumb|250px|Fossilised guards of the [[Jurassic]] belemnites ''[[Megateuthis gigantea]]'' (top two) and ''[[Megateuthis aalensis]]'']] [[File:Acroteuthis subquadrata - Naturhistorisches Museum, Braunschweig, Germany - DSC05160.JPG|thumb|250px|Fossilised rostrum of ''[[Acroteuthis subquadrata]]'']] [[File:Endoceras.JPG|thumb|250px|''[[Endoceras]]'' orthocones]] [[File:Orthoceras BW.jpg|thumb|250px|Artist's reconstruction of ''[[Orthoceras]]'' sp., an [[orthocerid]] of the [[Middle Ordovician]]]] {| class="wikitable" |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Ammonoidea (ammonoids)"| [[Ammonoidea]] (ammonoids) |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum shell diameter (length for heteromorphs) ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Parapuzosia seppenradensis]]'' || 2–3.5 m (estimate) || [[#Landois1895|Landois (1895:100)]]; [[#Landois1898|Landois (1898:27)]]; [[#TeichertKummel1960|Teichert & Kummel (1960:6)]]; [[#Summesberger1979|Summesberger (1979:128)]]; [[#Holland1987|Holland (1987:6)]]; [[#KennedyKaplan1995|Kennedy & Kaplan (1995:21)]]; [[#Lewy2002|Lewy (2002:66)]]; frim ''et al''. (2021) || Widely recognised as the largest ammonite specimen ever found ([[#Payneetal2009|Payne ''et al.'', 2009:27]]; [[#Grulke2014|Grulke, 2014:124]]). Discovered in 1895 in a quarry in [[Seppenrade]], [[Coesfeld (district)|Coesfeld]], [[Germany]], the original is on display at the [[Westfälisches Museum für Naturkunde]] in [[Münster]]. Estimate based on [[lectotype]] measuring 1.742 m in diameter ([[#KennedyKaplan1995|Kennedy & Kaplan, 1995:21]]) with an incomplete [[living chamber]], assuming living chamber took up one-fourth of the outer [[whorl (mollusc)|whorl]]. [[#TeichertKummel1960|Teichert & Kummel (1960:6)]] suggested an even larger shell diameter of around 3.5 m for this specimen, assuming the body chamber extended for three-fourths to one full whorl. [[#Landois1898|Landois (1898:27)]] estimated the total live weight at 1456 kg, of which the shell would constitute 705 kg. The fossil itself weighs around 3.5 tonnes ([[#Beer2015|Beer, 2015]]). A smaller specimen of 1.36 m was found in the same quarry some years earlier ([[#Beer2015|Beer, 2015]]). In 1971 a portion of an ammonite possibly surpassing the Seppenrade specimen was reportedly found in a [[brickyard]] in [[Bottrop]], western Germany ([[#Beer2015|Beer, 2015]]). In 2021, diameter of 2 m is estimated for the largest specimen.{{Cite journal |last1=Ifrim |first1=Christina |last2=Stinnesbeck |first2=Wolfgang |last3=González |first3=Arturo H. González |last4=Schorndorf |first4=Nils |last5=Gale |first5=Andrew S. |date=2021-11-10 |title=Ontogeny, evolution and palaeogeographic distribution of the world's largest ammonite Parapuzosia (P.) seppenradensis (Landois, 1895) |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=16 |issue=11 |article-number=e0258510 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0258510 |issn=1932-6203 |pmc=8580234 |pmid=34758037|bibcode=2021PLoSO..1658510I |doi-access=free }} |- | ''[[Parapuzosia bradyi]]'' || >1.8 m (estimate) || [[#Larsonetal1997|Larson ''et al.'' (1997:44)]]; [[#Lewy2002|Lewy (2002:66)]] || Largest known North American ammonite. Estimate based on incomplete specimen measuring 1.37 m in diameter (missing at least half a [[whorl (mollusc)|whorl]] of the [[body chamber]]). |- | [[Peltoceratinae]] {{abbr|''gen. et sp. indet.''|genus and species indeterminate}} || 1.78 m (estimate) || [[#Poulton1989|Poulton (1989)]] || Estimate based on small portion of [[whorl (mollusc)|outer whorl]] measuring 1.2 m along the venter and subtending a chord of 1.13 m. The estimate is based on the ultimate whorl height/diameter ratio of [[Titanites occidentalis|"''Titanites''" ''occidentalis'']] (about 35%), and assumes a constant rate of expansion. More crude calculations give a circular diameter of 2–2.4 m (best fit of the specimen's outline to a curve yields 2.16 m estimate). |- | ''[[Eopachydiscus]]'' sp. || 1.67 m || [[#Grulke2014|Grulke (2014:125)]] || This specimen, from the [[Albian]] [[Duck Creek Formation]] of [[Texas]], has been exhibited at the Tucson Fossil Show and in a New York auction. |- | [[Pachydesmoceras|''Pachydesmoceras'' cf. ''pachydiscoide'']] || 1.65 m (estimate) || [[#KinNiedzwiedzki2012|Kin & Niedźwiedzki (2012:19)]] || Estimate based on 0.98 m diameter specimen representing an apparently complete [[phragmocone]] (previously referred to ''[[Lewesiceras peramplum]]'' or ''[[Parapuzosia]]''). A more complete and therefore larger specimen (1.18 m diameter) consisting of a complete phragmocone and near-complete [[body chamber]] is also known ([[#KinNiedzwiedzki2012|Kin & Niedźwiedzki, 2012:17]]). |- | ''[[Lytoceras|Lytoceras taharoaense]]'' || 1.5 m || [[#Stevens1978a|Stevens (1978a:3)]]; [[#Stevens1978b|Stevens (1978b:2)]]; [[#Stevens1979a|Stevens (1979a:33)]]; [[#Stevens1979b|Stevens (1979b:6)]]; [[#Stevens1979c|Stevens (1979c:166)]]; [[#Stevens1985|Stevens (1985:153)]]; [[#Grulke2014|Grulke (2014:126)]] || Size based on essentially complete shell with only some damage to the [[aperture (mollusc)|aperture]]. |- | ''[[Mesopuzosia|Mesopuzosia mobergi]]'' || <1.5 m || [[#KinNiedzwiedzki2012|Kin & Niedźwiedzki (2012:19)]] || |- | ''[[Parapuzosia|Parapuzosia austeni]]'' || <1.5 m || [[#KinNiedzwiedzki2012|Kin & Niedźwiedzki (2012:19)]] || ''Puzosia mayoriana'' is a synonym. |- | ''[[Moutoniceras]]'' sp. || 1.47 m [heteromorph] || [[#Grulke2014|Grulke (2014:126)]] || In private collection of Wolfgang Grulke, according to whom it is likely the largest [[heteromorph ammonite]] ever found ([[#Grulke2014|Grulke, 2014:126]]). Originating from [[Morocco]] it is displayed in part of the original rock matrix with [[sympatric]] ''[[Gassendiceras]]'' heteromorphs. Its unrolled shell length would have exceeded 3 m ([[#Grulke2014|Grulke, 2014:126]]). A specimen claimed to be very slightly larger at 1.48 m was auctioned in September 2015 ([[#Anonymous2015a|[Anonymous], 2015a]]). |- | ''[[Parapuzosia|Parapuzosia bosei]]'' || 1.45 m || [[#ScottMoore1928|Scott & Moore (1928:276)]]; [[#Lewy2002|Lewy (2002:66)]] || From the [[Austin Chalk]] of the [[Rio Grande]] region, [[Texas]]. The largest known specimen was reported by [[#ScottMoore1928|Scott & Moore (1928:273)]] to be {{convert|4|ft|9|in|m|abbr=on}} in diameter and "impossible to extract from its matrix". The authors found "[m]any others only slightly smaller", of which three were collected in 1928 and deposited at [[Texas Christian University]] ([[#ScottMoore1928|Scott & Moore, 1928:273–274]]). |- | ''[[Parapuzosia|Parapuzosia americana]]'' || 1.37 m (estimate) || [[#ScottMoore1928|Scott & Moore (1928:276)]] || From the [[Austin Chalk]] of the [[Rio Grande]] region, [[Texas]]. |- | [[Corbinites|''Corbinites occidentalis'']]|| 1.37 m || [[#Frebold1957|Frebold (1957:66)]]; [[#Westermann1966|Westermann (1966)]] || Originally assigned to the genus ''[[Titanites]]''.{{Cite journal |last=Poulton |first=Terence P. |date=2023 |title=Corbinites (Subfamily Lithacoceratinae), a new genus for the giant western Canadian Late Kimmeridgian or Tithonian (Late Jurassic) ammonite Titanites occidentalis Frebold |url=https://vjs.pgi.gov.pl/article/view/34062 |journal=Volumina Jurassica |language=en |volume=21 |pages=27–38 |issn=1731-3708}} Size based on specimen consisting of an [[fossil|imprint]] and part of the last [[whorl (mollusc)|whorl]] preserved as an [[Endocast|internal mould]]. |- | ''[[Diplomoceras|Diplomoceras maximum]]'' || >1 m [heteromorph] || [[#OliveroZinsmeister1989|Olivero & Zinsmeister (1989)]] || |- | ''[[Tropaeum|Tropaeum imperator]]'' || almost 1 m || [[#Grulke2014|Grulke (2014:126)]] || Largest ammonite known from [[Australia]]. The whole animal is estimated to have been 3–5 m long ([[#Eyden2003a|Eyden, 2003a]]). The specimen is missing the apical and alveolar regions and comprises only the middle (stem) region of the rostrum. It measures 45 mm in total length by 30 mm and 25 mm across at the anterior and posterior ends, respectively. [[#Ibaetal2015|Iba ''et al.'' (2015:23)]] wrote: "In the [[Belemnitina]], the diameter of the alveolar region is generally larger than those of the apical and stem regions. Thus maximum rostrum diameter of the Teradani specimen is estimated to reach much more than 30 mm." |- | ''[[Acroteuthis]]'' sp. || ? {{abbr|TL|total length of rostrum}} ({{abbr|Dvms|maximum dorsoventral diameter of rostrum at stem region}}: 39 mm; {{abbr|Dvma|maximum dorsoventral diameter of rostrum at alveolar region}}: 42 mm) || [[#Ibaetal2015|Iba ''et al.'' (2010) || Estimated from hood length of lower jaw with proportion of vampire squid.{{Cite journal |last1=Tanabe |first1=Kazushige |last2=Hikida |first2=Yoshinori |date=2010 |title=Jaws of a New Species of''Nanaimoteuthis''(Coleoidea: Vampyromorphida) from the Tuironian of Hokkaido, Japan |journal=Paleontological Research |volume=14 |issue=2 |pages=145–150 |doi=10.2517/1342-8144-14.2.145 |bibcode=2010PalRe..14..145T |s2cid=128429338 |issn=1342-8144}} |- !colspan="4" style="background-color:silver;text-align:center;" id="Sisterdecapodiform"| [[Sister group]] of [[decapodiformes]] |- style="background-color:#cccccc;" ! scope="col" width="15%" | Species ! scope="col" width="15%" | Maximum mantle length ! scope="col" width="15%" | References ! scope="col" width="55%" | Notes |- | ''[[Longibelus|Longibelus matsumotoi]]'' (?)|| 91.1-98.9 cm (estimate) || Tanabe and Misaki (2023) | Estimated from rostral length of lower jaw, with proportion of ''[[Spirula]]'', giant squid and colossal squid. |} ==Anatomical superlatives== ===Eyes=== [[File:Giant squid eye.jpg|thumb|250px|Preserved giant squid eye]] [[File:Deep sea squid.jpg|thumb|250px|''[[Gonatus]]'' sp. swimming past a submersible during a dive to the [[Kermadec Arc]] off [[New Zealand]]. [[Gonatidae|Gonatids]] such as this have proportionately slightly larger eyes than do giant squid.[[#Schmitzetal2013a|Schmitz ''et al.'', 2013a]]]] The [[giant squid|giant]] and [[colossal squid]]s have the largest recorded [[cephalopod eye|eyes]] of any living animal, with a maximum diameter of at least {{convert|27|cm|in|abbr=on}} and a {{convert|9|cm|in|abbr=on}} [[pupil]]. This is three times the size of the largest [[fish]] eyes—up to {{convert|90|mm|in|abbr=on}} in [[swordfish]]—and more than twice the diameter of the largest [[whale]] eyes—up to {{convert|109|mm|in|abbr=on}}, {{convert|61|mm|in|abbr=on}}, and {{convert|55|mm|in|abbr=on}} in [[blue whale|blue]], [[humpback whale|humpback]], and [[sperm whale]]s, respectively—which are the largest among [[vertebrate]]s. A large colossal squid caught in 2014 and dissected at the [[Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa]] had eyes some {{convert|35|–|37|cm|in|abbr=on}} across.[[#Farquhar2014|Farquhar, 2014]]; [[#TePapa2014|[Te Papa], 2014]] There are unconfirmed reports from the 19th century of giant squid eyes up to {{convert|40|cm|ft|abbr=on}} across.[[#LandNilsson2012|Land & Nilsson, 2012:86]] Only the extinct [[Ichthyosauria|ichthyosaur]]s are known to have approached these dimensions,[[#Motanietal1999|Motani ''et al.'', 1999]]; [[#HumphriesRuxton2002|Humphries & Ruxton, 2002]] with some species having eyes up to {{convert|35|cm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter.[[#Nilssonetal2012|Nilsson ''et al.'', 2012:687]] Despite their size, the eyes of giant and colossal squids do not appear to be disproportionately large; they do not deviate significantly from the [[allometry|allometric relationship]] seen across other squid species.[[#Schmitzetal2013a|Schmitz ''et al.'', 2013a:45]] [[Sepiolidae|Sepiolids]] are noted for having exceptionally large eyes, which are much bigger relative to their mantle length than those of the giant squid; the same is true of ''[[Histioteuthis]]'' species. [[Gonatidae|Gonatids]] and the [[Loliginidae|loliginids]] ''[[Loligo]]'' and ''[[Lolliguncula]]'' also have proportionately somewhat larger eyes than ''Architeuthis''. Some sources state that the [[vampire squid]] (''Vampyroteuthis infernalis'') has the largest eyes of any animal relative to its size, with a {{convert|15|cm|in|abbr=on}} specimen having eyes around {{convert|2.5|cm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter.[[#Ellis1996|Ellis, 1996:177]]; [[#Anonymous2005a|[Anonymous], 2005a]]; though see [[#Youngetal2015|Young ''et al.'', 2015]] [[File:Iridoteuthis iris.jpg|thumb|left|250px|''[[Iridoteuthis iris]]'' is an extreme example of a [[Sepiolidae|sepiolid]] with disproportionately large eyes]] There is some debate in the scientific community as to the evolutionary reason behind the extremely large eyes of giant and colossal squids.[[#Partridge2012|Partridge, 2012]] [[#Nilssonetal2012|Nilsson ''et al.'' (2012)]] and [[#Nilssonetal2013|Nilsson ''et al.'' (2013)]] argue that it is an [[anti-predator adaptation]] for enhanced detection of [[sperm whale]]s, with the squids picking up [[plankton]] [[bioluminescence]] triggered by moving whales, perhaps from distances exceeding {{convert|120|m|ft|abbr=on}}. [[#Schmitzetal2013a|Schmitz ''et al.'' (2013a)]] and [[#Schmitzetal2013b|Schmitz ''et al.'' (2013b)]] contend that their eyes are so large due to a [[phylogenetically conserved]] developmental pattern that governs the relative dimensions of squids and their eyes, and that any [[Fitness (biology)|fitness]] benefits their size may confer in terms of predator avoidance are the result of [[exaptation]] ("pre-adaptation"). ===Neurons=== [[Squid giant axon]]s can exceed {{convert|1|mm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter: 100 to 1000 times the thickness of [[mammal]]ian [[axon]]s. The axons of the [[Humboldt squid]] (''Dosidicus gigas'') are exceptional in that they can reach a diameter of as much as {{convert|1.5|mm|in|abbr=on}}, and those of ''[[Loligo forbesii]]'' can also exceed 1 mm.[[#AdelmanGilbert1990|Adelman & Gilbert, 1990:102]] Such was the importance of Humboldt squid to [[electrophysiology]] research that when the animals migrated out of reach of [[Chile]]an fishermen in the 1970s "it led to the demise of a world-class electrophysiology laboratory" based there.[[#Scully2008|Scully, 2008]] Squid giant axon diameters do not necessarily correlate with overall body size; those of the [[giant squid]] (''Architeuthis dux'') are only {{convert|0.137|–|0.21|mm|in|abbr=on}} thick. [[File:RL squid 1.jpg|thumb|left|250px|Diagram showing the three major elements (red, green, and yellow) of the squid giant neuronal system. The arrows indicate the direction of transmission from the head ganglion towards the mantle. The funnel (light blue) is the site of rapid water expulsion following mantle contraction.]] The [[squid giant synapse]] is the largest [[chemical synapse|chemical junction]] in nature. It lies in the stellate [[ganglion]] on each side of the midline, at the posterior wall of the squid's muscular mantle. Activation of this synapse triggers a synchronous contraction of the mantle musculature, causing the forceful ejection of a jet of water from the mantle. This [[jet propulsion|water propulsion]] allows the squid to move rapidly through the water and, in the case of the so-called 'flying squids', even to jump through the surface of the water (breaking the air–water barrier) to escape predators.[[#Arata1954|Arata, 1954]]; [[#Murata1988|Murata, 1988]]; [[#Maciaetal2004|Maciá ''et al.'', 2004]]; [[#Muramatsuetal2013|Muramatsu ''et al.'', 2013]]; [[#ODoretal2013|O'Dor ''et al.'', 2013]] Many essential elements of how all chemical synapses function were first discovered by studying the squid giant synapse.see [[#Llinas1999|Llinás, 1999]] ===Photophores=== ''[[Taningia danae]]'', a very large [[Octopoteuthidae|octopoteuthid]] squid, possesses "lemon-sized" yellow [[photophore]]s at the tips of two of its [[cephalopod limb|arms]], which are the largest known light-emitting organs in the animal kingdom.[[#Ellis1998a|Ellis, 1998a:149]]; [[#Barrat2015|Barrat, 2015]] Video footage shot in 2005 in deep water off [[Japan]] shows ''T. danae'' emitting blinding [[Bioluminescence|flashes]] of light from these photophores as it attacks its prey.see [[#Kuboderaetal2006|Kubodera ''et al.'', 2006]] A pair of muscular lids surrounds each photophore and it is the withdrawal of these lids that produces the flashes. A large individual filmed from a remote submersible off [[Hawaii]] in 2015 can clearly be seen opening the lids to reveal its photophores.see [[#Barrat2015|Barrat, 2015]] It is believed that this highly manoeuvrable squid uses bright flashes to disorientate potential prey. The flashes may also serve to illuminate prey for easier capture or play a role in courtship and/or territorial displays.[[#Kuboderaetal2006|Kubodera ''et al.'', 2006:1033]] ===Reproductive organs=== [[File:Onykia ingens with erect penis.jpg|thumb|250px|A dissected male specimen of ''[[Onykia ingens]]'', showing an erect {{convert|67|cm|ft|abbr=on}} penis approximating the combined length of the mantle, head, and arms]] Extreme penis elongation has been observed in the deep water squid ''[[Onykia ingens]]''. When erect, the penis may be as long as the mantle, head, and arms combined.[[#ArkhipkinLaptikhovsky2010|Arkhipkin & Laptikhovsky, 2010:299]]; [[#Walker2010|Walker, 2010]] As such, deep water squids have the greatest known penis length relative to body size of all mobile animals, second in the entire animal kingdom only to certain sessile [[barnacle]]s.[[#ArkhipkinLaptikhovsky2010|Arkhipkin & Laptikhovsky, 2010:300]] ==See also== * [[Deep-sea gigantism]] * [[Gigantic octopus]] * [[Largest body parts]] * [[Largest organisms]] * [[Largest prehistoric organisms]] * [[List of colossal squid specimens and sightings]] * [[List of giant squid specimens and sightings]] * [[Smallest organisms]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=nb|1}} {{Clear}} ==References== ===Short citations=== {{reflist}} ===Full citations=== {{refbegin|40em|indent=yes}} * {{wikicite|ref=Anonymous1896|reference=[[[Anonymous work|Anonymous]]] (1896).
