{{About|relations between the People's Republic of China and the United States|relations between the Republic of China and the United States|Taiwan–United States relations|relations from before 1949|History of China–United States relations}} {{Very long|date=April 2025}} {{Use American English|date=February 2019}} {{Use dmy dates|date=June 2025}} {{Infobox bilateral relations|Chinese–American|China|United States | envoytitle1 = Ambassador | envoy1 = [[Xie Feng (diplomat)|Xie Feng]] | envoytitle2 = Ambassador | envoy2 = [[David Perdue]] | mission1 = [[Embassy of China, Washington, D.C.|Chinese Embassy, Washington, D.C.]] | mission2 = [[Embassy of the United States, Beijing|United States Embassy, Beijing]] | map = China United States of America Locator.svg }}
[[File:President Donald Trump greets Chinese President Xi Jinping before a bilateral meeting at the Gimhae International Airport terminal (54890669668).jpg|thumb| President [[Donald Trump]] and Chinese leader [[Xi Jinping]] at the [[APEC South Korea 2025|APEC Summit in Busan]], 2025|alt=Donald Trump and Xi Jinping stand next to each other, both wearing suits]]
The relationship between the [[People's Republic of China]] (PRC) and the [[United States]] (US) is the most important bilateral relationship in the world. It has been complex and at times tense since the [[Proclamation of the People's Republic of China|establishment of the PRC]] on 1 October 1949 and subsequent [[Kuomintang's retreat to Taiwan|retreat of the government of the Republic of China to Taiwan]]. After the normalization of relations in the 1970s, the US–China relationship has been marked by persistent disputes including China's economic policies, the [[political status of Taiwan]] and [[territorial disputes in the South China Sea]]. Despite these tensions, the two nations have significant economic ties and are deeply interconnected, while also engaging in [[strategic competition]] on the global stage. As of 2025, the US and China are the world's [[List of countries by GDP (nominal)|largest and second-largest economies by nominal GDP]]. Collectively, they account for 44.2% of the global nominal GDP.
The proclamation of the PRC in Beijing by the [[Chinese Communist Party]] (CCP) chairman [[Mao Zedong]] created a new [[Central People's Government of the People's Republic of China (1949–1954)|central government]] on the [[Mainland China|mainland]] in October 1949. Relations between the US and the new Chinese government quickly soured, culminating in direct conflict during the [[Korean War]]. The US-led [[United Nations]] intervention was met with [[China in the Korean War|Chinese military involvement]], as Beijing sent millions of Chinese fighters to prevent a US-aligned presence on its border. For decades, the US did not formally recognize the PRC, instead maintaining diplomatic relations with the [[Republic of China]] (ROC) based in Taiwan, and as such blocked the PRC's entry into the United Nations. However, shifting geopolitical dynamics, including the [[Sino-Soviet split]], the winding down of the [[Vietnam War]], as well as of the [[Cultural Revolution]], paved the way for US President [[Richard Nixon's 1972 visit to China]], ultimately marking a sea change in US–China relations. On 1 January 1979, the US formally established diplomatic relations with the PRC and recognized it as the sole legitimate [[government of China]], while maintaining unofficial ties with Taiwan within the framework of the [[Taiwan Relations Act]], an issue that remains a major point of contention between the two countries to the present day.
Every U.S. president since Nixon has toured China during his term in office, with the exception of [[Jimmy Carter]] and [[Joe Biden]]. The [[Presidency of Barack Obama|Obama administration]] signed a record number of bilateral agreements with China, particularly regarding climate change, though its broader strategy of [[East Asian foreign policy of the Barack Obama administration|rebalancing towards Asia]] created diplomatic friction. The advent of [[Xi Jinping]]'s [[General secretaryship of Xi Jinping|general secretaryship]] would prefigure a sharp downturn in these relations, which was then further entrenched upon the election of President [[Donald Trump]], who had promised an assertive stance towards China as a part of [[Donald Trump 2016 presidential campaign|his campaign]], which began to be implemented upon his taking office. Issues included China's militarization of the South China Sea, alleged manipulation of the Chinese currency, and [[Chinese espionage in the United States]].<ref name="bbc._WhyU">{{Cite news |title=Why US-China relations are at their lowest point in decades |work=BBC News |date=24 July 2020 |first=Barbara |last=Plett Usher |author-link=Barbara Plett Usher |access-date=28 July 2021 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-53517439}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/blogs/asia/2019/05/china-trade-war-sino-american-ties-torn-brick-brick-190512132227395.html |title=China-US trade war: Sino-American ties being torn down brick by brick |first=Adrian |last=Brown |date=12 May 2019 |website=Al Jazeera English |access-date=18 August 2019 |archive-date=4 September 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190904140940/https://www.aljazeera.com/blogs/asia/2019/05/china-trade-war-sino-american-ties-torn-brick-brick-190512132227395.html |url-status=dead |author-link=Adrian Brown (journalist)}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.latimes.com/politics/la-na-pol-us-china-trade-stalemate-20190531-story.html |title=For the U.S. and China, it's not a signs bill banning government use of Huawei and ZTE tech|url=https://www.theverge.com/2018/8/13/17686310/huawei-zte-us-government-contractor-ban-trump|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190529222201/https://www.theverge.com/2018/8/13/17686310/huawei-zte-us-government-contractor-ban-trump|archive-date=29 May 2019|access-date=29 May 2019|website=[[The Verge]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last1=Swanson|first1=Ana|last2=Mozur|first2=Paul|date=7 October 2019|title=U.S. Blacklists 28 Chinese Entities Over Abuses in Xinjiang |work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/10/07/us/politics/us-to-blacklist-28-chinese-entities-over-abuses-in-xinjiang.html|access-date=25 May Bars Some China Scholars From Visiting U.S. Over Spying Fears|last=Perlez|first=Jane|date=14 April 2019|work=The New York Times |author-link=Jane Perlez |access-date=14 July 2019 |issn=0362-4331|archive-date=14 July 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190714104329/https://www.nytimes.com/2019/04/14/world/asia/china-academics-fbi-visa-bans.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/07/25/us/politics/visa-restrictions-chinese-students.html|title=Visa Restrictions for Chinese Students Alarm Academia|last=Yoon-Hendricks|first=Alexandra|date=25 July 2018|work=The New York Times|access-date=14 July 2019 |issn=0362-4331|archive-date=14 July 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190714182433/https://www.nytimes.com/2018/07/25/us/politics/visa-restrictions-chinese-students.html|url-status=live}}</ref> and [[Taiwan–United States relations|strengthened relations with Taiwan]]. By early 2018, various geopolitical observers had begun to speak of a [[Second Cold War#Usage in the context of China–United States tensions|new Cold War]] between the two powers.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Luce |first=Edward |date=19 July 2019 |title=Getting acclimatised to the US-China cold war |website=[[Financial Times]] |author-link=Edward Luce |url=https://www.ft.com/content/a3062586-a9ac-11e9-984c-fac8325aaa04|access-date=18 August 2019 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20220329041503/https://www.ft.com/content/a3062586-a9ac-11e9-984c-fac8325aaa04 |archive-date=29 March 2022 |url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3022587/china-us-cold-war-inevitable-chinese-analysts-say-it-cant-be|title=Is China-US cold war inevitable? Chinese analysts say it can't be ruled out|date=14 August 2019|website=South China Morning Post |last=Lee |first=Jeong-ho |access-date=18 August 2019|archive-date=18 August 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190818082749/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3022587/china-us-cold-war-inevitable-chinese-analysts-say-it-cant-be|url-status=live}}</ref> On the last day of the Trump administration in January 2021, the U.S. officially classified the Chinese government's treatment of the [[Uyghurs]] in Xinjiang [[Persecution of Uyghurs in China|as a genocide]].<ref name="Bernstein-2021" />
Following the election of [[Presidency of Joe Biden|Joe Biden]] in [[2020 United States presidential election|2020]], tensions between the two countries remained high. Biden identified strategic competition with China as a top priority in [[Foreign policy of the Biden administration#China|his foreign policy]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/un-biden-will-try-move-past-afghanistan-with-climate-china-focus-2021-09-21/|title=At U.N., Biden promises 'relentless diplomacy,' not Cold War|first1=Trevor|last1=Hunnicutt|first2=Steve|last2=Holland|publisher=Reuters|date=21 September 2021|access-date=21 September 2021|archive-date=22 September 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210922222113/https://www.reuters.com/world/un-biden-will-try-move-past-afghanistan-with-climate-china-focus-2021-09-21/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.thedailybeast.com/white-house-pushes-us-officials-to-criticize-china-for-coronavirus-cover-up |title=White House Pushes U.S. Officials to Criticize China For Coronavirus 'Cover-Up' |work=The Daily Beast |date=21 March 2021 |first=Erin |last=Banco |access-date=21 September 2021|archive-date=22 January 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210122144352/https://www.thedailybeast.com/white-house-pushes-us-officials-to-criticize-china-for-coronavirus-cover-up|url-status=live}}</ref> His administration imposed large-scale restrictions on the sale of semiconductor technology to China,<ref>{{Cite news|last=Swanson|first=Ana|date=7 October 2022|title=Biden Administration Clamps Down on China's Access to Chip Technology |work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/10/07/business/economy/biden-chip-technology.html|access-date=11 March 2023|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=13 October 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231013150715/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/10/07/business/economy/biden-chip-technology.html|url-status=live}}</ref> boosted regional alliances against China, and expanded support for Taiwan.<ref name="Lau-2021" /><ref>{{Cite web |last1=Bush |first1=Richard C. |last2=Hass |first2=Ryan |date=1 December 2021 |title=The Biden administration is right to include Taiwan in the Summit for Democracy |author1-link=Richard C. Bush |author2-link=Ryan Hass |url=https://www.brookings.edu/blog/order-from-chaos/2021/12/01/the-biden-administration-is-right-to-include-taiwan-in-the-summit-for-democracy/|access-date=28 April 2023|website=Brookings}}</ref> However, the Biden administration also emphasized that the U.S. sought "competition, not conflict", with Biden stating in late 2022 that "there needs to not be a new Cold War".<ref>{{Cite news|date=14 November 2022|title=Xi Biden meeting: US leader promises 'no new Cold War' with China |work=BBC News |first=Tessa |last=Wong |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-63628454|access-date=11 March 2023|archive-date=4 October 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231004050514/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-63628454|url-status=live}}</ref> Despite efforts at diplomatic engagement, U.S.-China trade and political relations have reached their lowest point in years, largely due to disagreements over technology and China's military growth and human rights record.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Lawal |first1=Shola |title=Tech wars: Why has China banned exports of rare minerals to US? |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/12/4/tech-wars-why-has-china-banned-exports-of-rare-minerals-to-us |date=4 December 2024 |website=Al Jazeera English}}</ref> In his second term, President Donald Trump sharply escalated the trade war with China, raising baseline tariffs on Chinese imports to an effective 145%, prior to negotiating with China on 12 May 2025 a reduction in the tariff rate to 30% for 90 days while further negotiations take place.
==History== {{Main|History of China–United States relations}} The [[China|People's Republic of China]] (PRC) was founded on 1 October 1949.
===Korean War=== {{See also|Korean War}} On 25 June 1950, the China-aligned state of [[North Korea]] invaded US-aligned [[South Korea]].<ref name="Chen-1992">{{Cite journal|last=Chen|first=Jian|date=1992|title=China's Changing Aims during the Korean War, 1950—1951|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23613365|journal=The Journal of American-East Asian Relations|volume=1|issue=1|pages=8–41|jstor=23613365 |author-link=Chen Jian (academic) |issn=1058-3947 |archive-date=9 January 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240109153552/https://www.jstor.org/stable/23613365|url-status=live}}</ref> In response, the [[United Nations Security Council]] passed [[United Nations Security Council Resolution 82|Resolution 82]], which authorized military action against North Korea. Although the [[Soviet Union]] had veto power, at the time [[Soviet boycott of the United Nations|it was boycotting the UN Security Council]] over the UN's recognition of the [[Taiwan|ROC]] instead of the PRC as the representative of China.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/United-Nations-Security-Council/History|title=United Nations Security Council – Cold War, Peacekeeping, Veto Power |website=Britannica |access-date=29 September 2023|archive-date=12 May 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210512213111/https://www.britannica.com/topic/United-Nations-Security-Council/History|url-status=live}}</ref>
Initially, the US government saw Chinese intervention as unlikely. The People's Republic was barely a year old and it appeared that, if China was going to engage in warfare, it would be in the [[Kuomintang]]-controlled Taiwan, not Korea.<ref name="Rawnsley 2009 285–315">{{Cite journal |last=Rawnsley |first=Gary D. |date=2009 |title='The Great Movement to Resist America and Assist Korea': how Beijing sold the Korean War |author-link=Gary D. Rawnsley |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26000394|journal=Media, War & Conflict|volume=2|issue=3|pages=285–315|doi=10.1177/1750635209345186|jstor=26000394|s2cid=143193818|issn=1750-6352 |archive-date=28 January 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240128121855/https://www.jstor.org/stable/26000394|url-status=live|url-access=subscription}}</ref> The US was opposed to the PRC's interests in Taiwan and, within two days of North Korea invading the South, US forces were deployed to the [[Taiwan Strait]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 June 2011 |title=Chinese intervention in Korean War |last=Kim |first=Yun-sik |url=https://www.koreatimes.co.kr/www/nation/2024/01/160_89323.html|access-date=26 January 2024|website=Korea Times |archive-date=26 January 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240126043700/https://www.koreatimes.co.kr/www/nation/2024/01/160_89323.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
After their defeat in the [[Chinese Civil War]], parts of the [[Kuomintang in Burma|ROC army had retreated to Burma]].<ref name=":Han">{{Cite book|last=Han|first=Enze|title=The Ripple Effect: China's Complex Presence in Southeast Asia|date=2024|publisher=[[Oxford University Press]]|isbn=978-0-19-769659-0|location=New York, NY |page=65}}</ref> The US supported these ROC forces with the hope that they would harass the PRC from the [[Southwestern China|southwest]], thereby diverting Chinese resources from the Korean War.<ref name=":Han" />
It seemed to the new Chinese leadership that stopping American encroachment into Asia was an important issue. In a speech to the [[Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party]] in August 1950, Chairman [[Mao Zedong]] stated, "if the American imperialists are victorious, they will become dizzy with success, and then be in a position to threaten us."<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Morris-Suzuki |first=Tessa |date=2015 |title=Prisoner Number 600,001: Rethinking Japan, China, and the Korean War 1950–1953 |author-link=Tessa Morris-Suzuki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/43553591|journal=The Journal of Asian Studies|volume=74|issue=2|pages=411–432|doi=10.1017/S0021911814002253|jstor=43553591|issn=0021-9118 |archive-date=26 January 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240126063227/https://www.jstor.org/stable/43553591|url-status=live|url-access=subscription}}</ref> PRC Premier and Foreign Minister [[Zhou Enlai]] echoed this sentiment in a speech in September: "the Chinese people can never tolerate foreign invasion, nor allow the imperialist to invade our neighbour at will without response".<ref name="Rawnsley 2009 285–315"/> Chinese leadership could not tolerate an American-occupied state directly on its border:<ref>{{cite journal |first1=Hao |last1=Yufan |first2=Zhai |last2=Zhihai |title=China's Decision to Enter the Korean War: History Revisited |journal=[[The China Quarterly]] |date=1990 |volume=121 |pages=94–115 |doi=10.1017/S0305741000013527 |url=https://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.695.5440&rep=rep1&type=pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211210221049/https://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.695.5440&rep=rep1&type=pdf |archive-date=2021-12-10}}</ref> Zhou Enlai warned that China would intervene in the war on national security grounds; this warning was dismissed by US President [[Harry S. Truman]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Stokesbury|first=James L. |title=A Short History of the Korean War|publisher=Harper Perennial|year=1990|isbn=978-0688095130|location=New York|page=83}}</ref><ref> {{cite book|last=Offner|first=Arnold A.|title=Another Such Victory: President Truman and the Cold War, 1945–1953|publisher=Stanford University Press|year=2002|isbn=978-0804747745 |author-link=Arnold A. Offner |page=390}}</ref>
On 30 September 1950, the UN offensive under the direction of the US crossed the [[38th parallel north|38th parallel]] into North Korea.<ref>{{Cite web|last1=Lee|first1=Michael|last2=Cho|first2=Jung-woo|date=14 September 2023|title=Incheon landing was turning point for war, nation and world|url=https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2023-09-14/national/diplomacy/Incheon-landing-was-turning-point-for-war-nation-and-world/1869467|access-date=26 January 2024|website=Korea JoongAng Daily}}</ref> [[Kim Il Sung|Kim Il-Sung]] held an emergency meeting with Chinese officials, appealing for their urgent entry into the conflict.<ref name="Chen-1992"/> The UN authorized the reunification of Korea, meaning that the entire peninsula could fall into US control.<ref>{{cite journal |first=James I. |last=Matray |title=Truman's Plan for Victory: National Self-Determination and the Thirty-Eighth Parallel Decision in Korea |journal=The Journal of American History |volume=66 |issue=2 |pages=314–333 |date=1979 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1900879 |doi=10.2307/1900879}}</ref> On 19 October 1950, Chinese forces crossed into North Korea.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Lendon|first=Brad|date=25 June 2020|title=The U.S. Army once ruled Pyongyang and 5 other things you might not know about the Korean War|url=https://www.cnn.com/2020/06/24/asia/korean-war-70th-anniversary-intl-hnk/index.html|access-date=26 January 2024|website=CNN |archive-date=26 January 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240126063225/https://www.cnn.com/2020/06/24/asia/korean-war-70th-anniversary-intl-hnk/index.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
In response to the PRC's entry into the conflict, the US froze all Chinese assets in America and banned American citizens from traveling to the PRC.{{sfn|Li|2024a|p=50}} The PRC seized all American assets and properties<ref>{{cite journal |first=Charles Ford |last=Redick |title=The Jurisprudence of the Foreign Claims Settlement Commission: Chinese Claims |journal=American Journal of International Law |volume=67 |issue=4 |date=1973 |page=728 |doi=10.2307/2198570}}</ref> and began efforts to remove American cultural influence from China, including by nationalizing cultural institutions affiliated with the US.{{sfn|Li|2024a|p=3}}
In late October 1950, China began its intervention with the [[Battle of Onjong]]. During the [[Battle of the Ch'ongch'on River]], the [[People's Volunteer Army]] outflanked the [[United Nations Forces in the Korean War|UN forces]], leading to the defeat of the [[US Eighth Army]].<ref>{{cite book|last1=Cohen|first1=Eliot A. |last2=Gooch|first2=John|title=Military Misfortunes: The Anatomy of Failure in War|publisher=Free Press|year=2005|pages=165–195 |author-link=Eliot A. Cohen |isbn=978-0-7432-8082-2}}</ref> A ceasefire presented by the UN to the PRC shortly after Ch'ongch'on River, on 11 December it was rejected by the Chinese, who were now convinced of their ability to defeat the UN forces, and wanted to demonstrate China's military power by driving them out of Korea altogether.<ref>{{citation|last=Zhang|first=Shu Guang|title=Mao's Military Romanticism: China and the Korean War, 1950–1953|pages=119–126|year=1995|location=Lawrence, Kansas|publisher=[[University Press of Kansas]]|isbn=0-7006-0723-4}}</ref><ref>{{citation | last = Alexander | first = Bevin R. | author-link = Bevin Alexander | year = 1986 | title = Korea: The First War We Lost | publisher = Hippocrene Books, Inc | location = New York | isbn = 978-0-87052-135-5 | pages = 371–376 }}</ref> The Chinese achieved further victory at the [[Third Battle of Seoul]] and the [[Battle of Hoengsong]], but UN forces recovered, pushing the front back to lands around the 38th parallel by July. A stalemate followed.<ref>Birtle, Andrew James (2000). [https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Korean_War/sEZyAAAAMAAJ ''The Korean War: Years of Stalemate, July 1951–July 1953'']. US Army Center for Military History.</ref> Even though the U.S. Air Force would spend the entire war with total air supremacy, the strategic impasse ultimately lasted until the [[Korean Armistice Agreement]] that ended the fighting was signed on 27 July 1953. Since then, a [[Division of Korea|divided Korea]] has continued to feature in U.S.-China relations, with large American forces still stationed in the South.<ref>{{Cite journal |first=James I. |last=Matray |title=Beijing and the Paper Tiger: The Impact of the Korean War on Sino-American Relations |journal=International Journal of Korean Studies |volume=15 |number=1 |date=2011 |pages=155–186 |url=https://icks.org/data/ijks/1483321954_add_file_8.pdf}}</ref>
In 1952, in the midst of the Korean War, the American army surveyed Chinese [[Prisoner of war|prisoners of war]] (POWs) asking them why they believed the PRC was involved in the conflict. Of 238 respondents, 60% agreed it was for the defense of China against the US, while only 17% said it was to defend North Korea.<ref name="Rawnsley 2009 285–315"/><ref>"Beliefs of Enemy Soldiers about the Korean War". International Public Opinion Research Inc. and Operations Research Office at Johns Hopkins University (1952) p. 53.</ref>
===Vietnam War=== {{Main|Vietnam War}}
The People's Republic of China provided resources and training to [[North Vietnam]], and in the summer of 1962, Mao agreed to supply Hanoi with 90,000 rifles and guns free of charge. After the launch of America's [[Operation Rolling Thunder]] in 1965, China sent anti-aircraft units and engineering battalions to North Vietnam to repair the damage caused by American bombing, rebuild roads and railroads, and perform other engineering work, freeing additional hundreds of thousands [[People's Army of Vietnam|North Vietnamese Army]] units for combat against American forces supporting [[South Vietnam]].<ref>{{cite journal |first=Xiaorong |last=Han |title=Spoiled guests or dedicated patriots? The Chinese in North Vietnam, 1954–1978 |journal=[[International Journal of Asian Studies]] |volume=6 |issue=1 |date=2009 |pages=1–36 |doi=10.1017/S1479591409000011}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=Chen Jian |title=China's Involvement in the Vietnam War, 1964–69 |journal=The China Quarterly |volume=142 |date=1995 |pages=356–387 |doi=10.1017/S0305741000034974 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200206025446/https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/1dc1/33fcfe61f1967f65be068ba4195d1437e74e.pdf}}</ref>
The Chinese presence in North Vietnam was well known to US officials, which contributed to President [[Lyndon B. Johnson]] and Secretary of Defense [[Robert McNamara]]'s decision not to invade North Vietnam, favoring a strategy of supporting South Vietnam in defending itself instead. The possibility of direct [[China in the Vietnam War|Chinese intervention]] was also ambiguous throughout the course of the war. Mao Zedong reportedly told journalist [[Edgar Snow]] in 1965 that China had no intention of fighting to save the Hanoi regime and would not engage the U.S. military unless it crossed into Chinese territory. US troops ultimately exited Vietnam as domestic opposition to American deployment in Vietnam increased, ending US involvement in the Vietnam War.<ref>{{cite book |first=Kwan Ha |last=Yim |title=China & the US: 1964–72 |date=1975 |pages=57–82 |publisher=[[Facts on File]] |isbn=978-0-87196-207-2}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |first=Frank E. |last=Rogers |title=Sino-American Relations and the Vietnam War, 1964–66 |journal=The China Quarterly |volume=66 |date=1976 |pages=293–314 |doi=10.1017/S0305741000033695 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221210160007/http://www3.nccu.edu.tw/~lorenzo/Rogers%20Sino%20American%20Vietnam.pdf}}</ref>
===Freezing of relations (1949–1971)=== {{see also|PRC-US Ambassadorial Talks}} Between 1949 and 1971, US–China relations were uniformly hostile, with frequent propaganda attacks in both directions. At the [[1954 Geneva Conference]], US Secretary of State [[John Foster Dulles]] forbade any contact with the Chinese delegation, refusing to shake hands with Zhou Enlai, the lead Chinese negotiator.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Immerman |first=Richard H. |author-link=Richard H. Immerman |title=The United States and the Geneva Conference of 1954: A New Look |journal=Diplomatic History |volume=14 |issue=1 |date=1990 |pages=43–66 |url=https://www.academia.edu/68823703/The_United_States_and_the_Geneva_Conference_of_1954_A_New_Look_ |doi=10.1111/j.1467-7709.1990.tb00075.x}}</ref> Relations deteriorated further under [[Foreign policy of the John F. Kennedy administration#China|President John F. Kennedy]] (1961–1963).<ref>{{cite journal |first=Nicholas Anthony |last=Autiello |title=Taming the Wild Dragon: John F. Kennedy and the Republic of China, 1961–63 |journal=[[Cold War History (journal)|Cold War History]] |date=2019 |volume=21 |issue=1 |pages=71–89 |doi=10.1080/14682745.2018.1550077}} [https://issforum.org/reviews/PDF/AR955.pdf Online review]. {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200603080939/https://issforum.org/reviews/PDF/AR955.pdf|date=3 June 2020}}.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |first=Noam |last=Kochavi |title=Kennedy, China, and the Tragedy of No Chance |journal=Journal of American-East Asian Relations |volume=7 |issue=1-2 |date=1998 |pages=107–116 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23613311 |doi=10.1163/187656198X00072}}</ref> Before the [[Cuban Missile Crisis]], policymakers in Washington were uncertain whether or not China would break with the Soviet Union on the basis of ideology, national ambitions, and readiness for a role in guiding communist activities in many countries. New insight came with the [[Sino-Indian War|Sino-Indian border war]] in November 1962 and Beijing's response to the Cuban Missile Crisis. Kennedy administration officials concluded that China was more militant and more dangerous than the Soviet Union, making better relations with Moscow desirable, with both nations trying to contain Chinese ambitions. Diplomatic recognition of China remained out of the question, as a crucial veto power on the UN Security Council was held by America's ally on Taiwan. The US continued to work to prevent the PRC from taking China's seat in the [[United Nations]] and encouraged its allies not to deal with the PRC. The United States placed an embargo on trading with the PRC, and encouraged allies to follow it.<ref>{{cite journal |first=Sean M. |last=Turner |title='A Rather Climactic Period': The Sino–Soviet Dispute and Perceptions of the China Threat in the Kennedy Administration |journal=Diplomacy & Statecraft |volume=22 |issue=2 |date=2011 |pages=261–280 |doi=10.1080/09592296.2011.576538}}</ref>
The [[596 (nuclear test)|PRC developed nuclear weapons]] in 1964 and, as later declassified documents revealed, President Johnson considered preemptive attacks to halt its nuclear program. He ultimately decided the measure carried too much risk, and it was abandoned. Instead, Johnson looked for ways to improve relations. The American public seemed more open to the idea of expanding contacts with China, such as the relaxation of the trade embargo. But the War in Vietnam was raging, with China aiding North Vietnam. Mao's [[Great Leap Forward]] had failed in its goal to properly industrialize China and [[Great Chinese Famine|sparked a famine]], and his [[Cultural Revolution]] exercised hostility to the US. In the end, Johnson made no move to change the standoff.<ref>{{cite journal |first=Victor S. |last=Kaufman |title=A Response to Chaos: The United States, the Great Leap Forward, and the Cultural Revolution, 1961–1968 |journal=[[Journal of American-East Asian Relations]] |volume=7 |issue=1-2 |date=1998 |pages=73–92 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23613308 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200603092529/https://www.jstor.org/stable/23613308 |archive-date=3 June 2020 |doi=10.1163/187656198x00045}}.</ref>
Despite official non-recognition, the United States and the People's Republic of China held 136 meetings at the ambassadorial level beginning in 1954 and continuing until 1970, first in [[Geneva]] and in 1958–1970 in [[Warsaw]].<ref>Goldstein, Steven M. (2001). "Dialogue of the Deaf?: The Sino-American Ambassadorial-Level Talks, 1955–1970". In {{cite book|editor1=Robert S. Ross|editor2=Changbin Jiang|title=Re-examining the Cold War: U.S.-China Diplomacy, 1954–1973|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LUcbNCUoWSMC&pg=PA200 |publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|pages=200–237|isbn=9780674005266 |editor1-link=Robert S. Ross}}</ref>
The Cultural Revolution brought about near-complete isolation of China from the outside world and vocal denunciations of both [[American imperialism|US imperialism]] and [[Revisionism (Marxism)|Soviet revisionism]].
Beginning in 1967, the [[Foreign Claims Settlement Commission]] established the China Claims Program, in which American citizens could denominate the sum total of their lost assets and property following the Communist seizure of foreign property in 1950. American companies were reluctant to invest in China despite (future [[Paramount leader|top leader]]) [[Deng Xiaoping]]'s reassurances of a stable business environment.<ref>Hollie, Pamela G. (1 October 1979). "Thaw in China-U.S. Ties May Unfreeze '49 Assets". ''The New York Times''. p. D1.</ref>
===Rapprochement (1968–1972)=== {{see also|1972 visit by Richard Nixon to China}} The end of the 1960s brought a period of transformation. For China, when American President Johnson decided to wind down the Vietnam War in 1968, it gave China the impression that the U.S. had no interest in expanding throughout the Asia-Pacific anymore. Meanwhile, relations with the USSR rapidly worsened. This gave [[Richard Nixon]]—running for president in 1968—the idea of using that rivalry to improve Washington's relations with Moscow and Beijing, while each rival would cut back support for Hanoi.<ref>Goh, Evelyn (2005). "Nixon, Kissinger, and the 'Soviet Card' in the U.S. Opening to China, 1971–1974". ''[[Diplomatic History (journal)|Diplomatic History]]'' 29.3: 475–502. [http://www3.nccu.edu.tw/~lorenzo/Goh%20Soviet%20Card%20Opening%20to%20China.pdf online] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220302163757/http://www3.nccu.edu.tw/~lorenzo/Goh%20Soviet%20Card%20Opening%20to%20China.pdf|date=2 March 2022}}</ref>
This became an especially important concern for the People's Republic of China after the [[Sino-Soviet border conflict]] of 1969. The PRC was diplomatically isolated and the leadership came to believe that improved relations with the US would be a useful counterbalance to the Soviet threat. Zhou Enlai, the [[Premier of the People's Republic of China|Premier of China]], was at the forefront of this effort with the committed backing of Mao Zedong, Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP). In 1969, the US initiated measures to relax trade restrictions and other impediments to bilateral contact, to which China responded. However, this rapprochement process was stalled by the Vietnam War, where China was supporting the enemies of the US. Communication between Chinese and American leaders, however, was conducted through [[Socialist Republic of Romania|Romania]], [[Pakistan]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nixonfoundation.org/exhibit/the-opening-of-china/|title=The Week that Chenged the World|website=Richard Nixon Foundation|date=18 January 2017|access-date=2 November 2018|archive-date=27 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180927192245/https://www.nixonfoundation.org/exhibit/the-opening-of-china/|url-status=live}}</ref> and [[Polish People's Republic|Poland]] as intermediaries.<ref name=":Minami" />{{Rp|page=36}} The warming U.S.-Soviet and U.S.-China relationships in 1972 due to the [[triangular diplomacy]] coincided with crucial periods in the [[Paris Peace Talks]]. Many historians argue that the shifting dynamics of the strategic triangle played a critical role in changing the North Vietnamese's negotiating strategy and ultimately led to the signing of the [[Paris Peace Accords]] in January 1973.{{citation needed|date=October 2025}}
[[File:Kissinger Mao.jpg|thumb|left|Henry Kissinger, Zhou Enlai and Mao Zedong. Kissinger made two secret trips to the PRC in 1971 before Nixon's groundbreaking visit in 1972.]]
In the US, academics such as [[John K. Fairbank]] and [[A. Doak Barnett]] pointed to the need to deal realistically with the Beijing government, while organizations such as the [[National Committee on United States–China Relations]] sponsored debates to promote public awareness.<ref name=":Minami" />{{Rp|pages=36–37}} Many saw the specter of Communist China behind communist movements in [[Vietnam]], [[Cambodia]], and [[Laos]], but a growing number concluded that if the PRC would align with the U.S. it would mean a major redistribution of global power against the Soviets. Mainland China's market of nearly one billion consumers appealed to American businesses. Senator [[J. William Fulbright]], Chair of the [[United States Senate Committee on Foreign Relations|Senate Foreign Relations Committee]], held a series of hearings on the matter.<ref>Goh, Evelyn, ''Constructing the US Rapprochement with China, 1961–1974: From 'Red Menace' to 'Tacit Ally' '', Cambridge University Press, 2005</ref>
Richard M. Nixon mentioned in his [[First inauguration of Richard Nixon|inaugural address]] that the two countries were entering an era of negotiation after an era of confrontation. Although Nixon, during his 1960 presidential campaign, had vociferously supported Chiang Kai-Shek, by the second half of the decade, he increasingly began to speak of there "being no reason to leave China angry and isolated". Nixon's election as president in 1968 was initially met with hostility by Beijing—an editorial in the ''People's Daily'' denounced him as "a chieftain whom the capitalist world had turned to out of desperation".<ref>{{cite book|editor-last=Leffler|editor-first=Melvyn P.|title=The Cambridge history of the Cold War.|year=2010|publisher=Cambridge University Press|location=Cambridge, UK|isbn=978-0-521-83720-0|page=373|edition=1. publ.|editor-last2=Westad|editor-first2=Odd Arne}}</ref> Nixon believed it was in the American national interest to forge a relationship with China, even though there were enormous differences between the two countries.<ref>{{cite web|last=Dube|first=Clayton|title=Getting to Beijing: Kissinger's Secret 1971 Trip|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2483|publisher=USC US-China Institute|access-date=24 July 2011|archive-date=10 November 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131110190513/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2483|url-status=live}}</ref> He was assisted in this by his [[National Security Advisor (United States)|National Security Advisor]] [[Henry Kissinger]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Li |first=Xiaobing |title=The Cold War in East Asia |date=2018 |publisher=[[Routledge]] |isbn=978-1-138-65179-1 |location=Abingdon, Oxon}}</ref>{{Rp|page=3}} Both Nixon and Kissinger believed that relations with China would help the US exit the Vietnam War and obtain long-term strategic benefits in confrontations with the Soviet Union.<ref name=":0" />{{Rp|page=3}}
In 1971, an unexpectedly friendly encounter between the American and Chinese ping-pong athletes called [[Glenn Cowan]] and [[Zhuang Zedong]] in Japan opened the way for a visit to China, which Chairman Mao personally approved.<ref>{{cite web|last=Dube|first=Clayton|title=Sports diplomacy and back-channel negotiations|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2470|work=Talking Points, July 22 – August 3, 2011|publisher=USC US-China Institute|access-date=24 July 2011|archive-date=10 November 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131110200234/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2470|url-status=live}}</ref> In April 1971, the athletes became the first Americans to officially visit China since the communist takeover. The smooth acceptance of this created the term [[ping-pong diplomacy]] and gave confidence to both sides. Ping-pong diplomacy became one of the most prominent examples of [[people's diplomacy]] in China-US relations.<ref name=":Minami" />{{Rp|page=9}} The ping-pong diplomacy allowed reporters into the country as well, opening up communication to both sides and breaking a barrier that had been there previously. This smoothed out the start of the trade partnership that was going to happen later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Timeline: U.S. Relations With China 1949–2020|url=https://www.cfr.org/timeline/us-relations-china|access-date=9 March 2021|website=[[Council on Foreign Relations]] |archive-date=16 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220816002922/https://www.cfr.org/timeline/us-relations-china|url-status=live}}</ref> China's approach to keeping these early exchanges unofficial and conduct them through non-governmental agencies was generally well received by U.S. [[civil society]] groups and academics.{{sfn|Li|2024a|p=310}}
In July 1971, Henry Kissinger feigned illness while on a trip to Pakistan and did not appear in public for a day. He was actually on a top-secret mission to Beijing to negotiate with Chinese Premier Zhou Enlai.
Kissinger and his aides did not receive a warm welcome in Beijing, and the hotel they stayed in was equipped with pamphlets excoriating U.S. imperialism. However, the meeting with Zhou Enlai was productive, and the Chinese premier expressed his hope for improved China-US relations. He commented that the U.S. had intentionally isolated China, not vice versa, and any initiative to restore diplomatic ties had to come from the American side. Zhou spoke of the late President Kennedy's plans to restore relations with China and told Kissinger, "We are willing to wait as long as we need to. If these negotiations fail, in time another Kennedy or another Nixon will come along."<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://kisslibrary.com/book/7138CDB42A74355EA5B6?search=2011+American+Business+in+China+White+Paper&a_aid=ddmidovv&a_bid=11110007|title=2011 American Business in China White Paper by American Chamber of Commerce in China / KissLibrary: Affordable Ebooks|website=kisslibrary.com|access-date=6 December 2018}}</ref>
On 15 July 1971, President Richard Nixon revealed the mission to the world and that he had accepted an invitation to visit the PRC.<ref>Magaret MacMillan, ''Nixon and Mao: The Week That Changed The World'' (2008)</ref>
This announcement<ref>{{cite web|last=Nixon|first=Richard|title=Announcement of the President's Trip to China|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2258|work=US-China documents collection|publisher=USC US-China Institute|access-date=24 July 2011|archive-date=11 November 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131111171025/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2258|url-status=live}}</ref> caused immediate shock around the world. In the US, some hard-line [[Anti-communism|anti-communists]] (most notably [[libertarian Republican]] Arizona Senator [[Barry Goldwater]]) denounced the decision, but most public opinions supported the move and Nixon saw the jump in the polls he had been hoping for. Since Nixon had sterling anti-communist credentials he was all but immune to being called "soft on communism". Nixon and his aides wanted to ensure that press coverage offered dramatic imagery.<ref>See [http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2705 "Getting to know you: The US and China shake the world"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131111171032/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2705|date=11 November 2013}} and [http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2672 "The Week that Changed the World"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141021072557/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=2672|date=21 October 2014}} for recordings, documents, and interviews.</ref> Nixon was particularly eager for strong news coverage.
[[File:President and Mrs. Nixon visit the Great Wall of China and the Ming tombs - NARA - 194421.tif|thumb|right|President Nixon and First Lady [[Pat Nixon]] walk with the American delegation and their Chinese hosts on the [[Great Wall of China]].]] [[File:Nixon and Zhou toast.jpg|thumb|right|President Nixon and Premier [[Zhou Enlai]] toast during Nixon's 1972 visit to China]] Within the PRC there was also opposition from left-wing elements. This effort was allegedly led by [[Lin Biao]], head of the military, who died in a mysterious plane crash over [[Mongolian People's Republic|Mongolia]] while trying to defect to the Soviet Union. His death silenced most internal dissent over the visit.
Internationally, reactions varied. In the communist world, the Soviets were very concerned that two major enemies seemed to have resolved their differences, and the new world alignment contributed significantly to the policy of ''[[détente]]''.
America's [[NATO]] allies were pleased by the initiative, especially since many of them had already recognized the PRC. Throughout the Asia-Pacific, the reaction was far more mixed. Japan was annoyed that it had not been told of the announcement until fifteen minutes before it had been made, and feared that the Americans were abandoning them in favor of the PRC. A short time later, Japan also recognized the PRC and committed to substantial trade with the continental power. South Korea and South Vietnam were both concerned that peace between the US and the PRC could mean an end to American support for them against their communist enemies. Throughout the period of rapprochement, both countries had to be regularly assured that they would not be abandoned. Taiwan's Chiang Kai-Shek criticized the move, saying: "Today any international appease movement to evil power to seek for political power balance would never helpful for the world peace, instead it elongated the hardship of our 700 million people, and expand the disaster of the world."<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.ccfd.org.tw/ccef001/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=751:0003-54&catid=199:2014-06-12-03-10-05&Itemid=256|author=Kai-Shek, Chiang|title=對國民大會第五次會議開幕典禮致詞|trans-title=Speech for the Opening Ceremony of the National Assembly Fifth Meeting|website=中正文教基金會 (Chungcheng Cultural and Educational Foundation)|date=20 February 1972|access-date=3 June 2021|language=zh|quote=所以今天國際間任何與惡勢力謀求政治權力均衡的姑息舉動，絕不會有助於世界和平，而適以延長我七億人民的苦難，增大全世界的災禍！|archive-date=3 June 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210603081801/http://www.ccfd.org.tw/ccef001/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=751:0003-54&catid=199:2014-06-12-03-10-05&Itemid=256|url-status=live}}</ref>
From February 21 to 28, 1972, [[1972 Nixon visit to China|President Nixon traveled]] to [[Beijing]], [[Hangzhou]], and [[Shanghai]]. At the conclusion of his trip, the U.S. and the PRC issued the [[Shanghai Communiqué]], a statement of their respective foreign policy views. In the Communiqué, both nations pledged to work toward the full normalization of diplomatic relations. This did not lead to immediate recognition of the People's Republic of China but 'liaison offices' were established in Beijing and Washington.<ref name="Dunbabin 1996 258">{{cite book|last=Dunbabin|first=J.P.D.|title=International relations since 1945|year=1996|publisher=Longman|location=London [u.a.]|isbn=978-0-582-49365-0|page=258|edition=[Nachdr.].}}</ref> The U.S. acknowledged the PRC position that all Chinese on both sides of the Taiwan Strait maintain that [[One-China policy|there is only one China]] and that Taiwan is part of China. The statement enabled the U.S. and PRC to temporarily set aside the issue of Taiwan and open trade and communication. Also, the U.S. and China both agreed to take action against "any country" that is to establish "hegemony" in the Asia-Pacific. On several issues, such as the ongoing conflicts in Korea, Vietnam, and Israel, the U.S. and China were unable to reach a common understanding.<ref name="Dunbabin 1996 258"/>
The rapprochement with the US benefited the PRC immensely and greatly increased its security for the rest of the [[Cold War]]. It has been argued that the US, on the other hand, saw fewer benefits than it had hoped for, inasmuch as China continued to back America's enemies in Hanoi and Pyongyang. Eventually, however, the PRC's suspicion of Vietnam's motives led to a break in China-Vietnamese cooperation and, upon the Vietnamese invasion of Cambodia in 1979, the [[Sino-Vietnamese War]]. Both China and the US backed combatants in [[Africa]] against Soviet and [[Cuba]]n-supported movements. The economic benefits of normalization were slow as it would take decades for American products to penetrate the vast Chinese market. While Nixon's China policy is regarded by many as the highlight of his presidency, others such as [[William Bundy]] have argued that it provided very little benefit to the US.{{citation needed|date=February 2015}}
===Liaison Office (1973–1978)=== [[File:President Ford makes remarks in the People's Republic of China - NARA - 7062599.jpg|thumb|right|President Gerald Ford makes remarks at a Reciprocal Dinner in Beijing on 4 December 1975.]]
In May 1973, in an effort to build toward formal diplomatic relations, the U.S. and the PRC established the United States Liaison Office (USLO) in Beijing and a counterpart PRC office in Washington, D.C.<ref name=":0" />{{Rp|page=153}} From 1973 to 1978, such distinguished Americans as [[David K. E. Bruce]], [[George H. W. Bush]], [[Thomas S. Gates, Jr.]], and [[Leonard Woodcock]] served as chiefs of the USLO with the personal rank of ambassador. China made clear that it considered the Soviet Union its chief adversary and urged the US to be powerful, thereby distracting Moscow. Liaison officer George Bush concluded, "China keeps wanting us to be strong, wanting us to defend Europe, wanting us to increase our defense budgets, etc."<ref>{{cite book|editor1-first=Jeffrey A.|editor1-last= Engel|title=The China Diary of George H. W. Bush: The Making of a Global President|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jRvdwoKQOgQC&pg=PA356|year=2011|publisher=Princeton UP|page=356|isbn=978-1400829613}}</ref> Bush concluded that American engagement was essential to support markets, allies, and stability throughout the Asia Pacific and around the world.<ref>{{cite book|first=Jon|last=Meacham|title=Destiny and Power: The American Odyssey of George Herbert Walker Bush|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=iMgOCAAAQBAJ&pg=PT219|year=2015|page=219|publisher=Random House Publishing|isbn=9780812998207}}</ref>
President [[Gerald Ford]] visited the PRC in 1975 and reaffirmed American interest in normalizing relations with Beijing. Shortly after taking office in 1977, President [[Jimmy Carter]] again reaffirmed the goals of the Shanghai Communiqué. Secretary of State [[Cyrus Vance]], Carter's National Security Advisor [[Zbigniew Brzezinski]], and senior staff member of the National Security Council [[Michel Oksenberg]] encouraged Carter to seek full diplomatic and trade relations with China. Although Brzezinski sought to quickly establish a security relationship with Beijing to counter the Soviet Union, Carter sided with Vance in believing that such a deal would threaten existing U.S.-Soviet relations, including the [[Strategic Arms Limitation Talks|SALT II]] negotiations. Thus, the administration decided to cautiously pursue political normalization and not military relations.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Vance|first1=Cyrus|title=Hard Choices|url=https://archive.org/details/hardchoicescriti0000vanc|url-access=registration|date=1983|publisher=Simon and Schuster|pages=[https://archive.org/details/hardchoicescriti0000vanc/page/78 78]–79|isbn=9780671443399}}</ref> Vance, Brzezinski, and Oksenberg traveled to Beijing in early 1978 to work with Leonard Woodcock, then head of the liaison office, to lay the groundwork to do so. The US and the PRC announced on 15 December 1978,{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=86}} that the two governments would establish diplomatic relations on 1 January 1979.
===Normalization (1979–1989)=== {{see also|Visit by Deng Xiaoping to the United States|U.S.–China Agreement on Cooperation in Science and Technology}} [[File:Jimmy Carter and Deng Xiaoping participate in a meeting with U.S. and Chinese officials. - NARA - 183191.tif|thumb|US president Jimmy Carter, [[Zbigniew Brzezinski]], and other U.S. officials meet in the White House Cabinet Room with Chinese leader Deng Xiaoping, 29 January 1979.]] In the [[Joint Communiqué on the Establishment of Diplomatic Relations]], dated 1 January 1979, the United States transferred diplomatic recognition from Taipei to Beijing. The U.S. reiterated the Shanghai Communiqué's acknowledgment of the Chinese position [[One China policy|that there is only one China]], and that Taiwan is a part of China; Beijing acknowledged that the American people would continue to carry on commercial, cultural, and other unofficial contacts with the people of Taiwan.<ref>Jim Mann, ''About face: A history of America's curious relationship with China, from Nixon to Clinton'' (1999).</ref>
Taiwan, although fully expecting this step, nonetheless expressed disappointment at having not been consulted first. The reaction of the communist world was similar to 1972, with the Soviet Union and its allies in Eastern Europe mostly being noncommittal, Romania welcoming the move, and Cuba and Albania being strongly against it. North Korea issued a statement congratulating "our brotherly neighbors for ending long-hostile relations with the US".{{Quote without source|date=April 2018}}
Chinese leader Deng Xiaoping's [[1979 visit by Deng Xiaoping to the United States|January 1979 visit]] to Washington initiated a series of important, high-level exchanges which continued until the spring of 1989. This resulted in many bilateral agreements, including the 31 January 1979 Agreement on Cooperation in Science and Technology.{{sfn|Lampton|2024|pp=90–91}} Scientific cooperation greatly increased thereafter.<ref name=":Minami">{{Cite book|last=Minami|first=Kazushi|title=People's Diplomacy: How Americans and Chinese Transformed US-China Relations during the Cold War|date=2024|publisher=[[Cornell University Press]]|isbn=9781501774157|location=Ithaca, NY}}</ref>{{Rp|pages=86–87}} Since early 1979, the US and the PRC have initiated hundreds of joint research projects and cooperative programs under the Agreement on Cooperation in Science and Technology, the largest bilateral program.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1196|title=US-China Institute :: news & features :: china in u.s. campaign politics: part 6 of election '08 and the challenge of china|publisher=China.usc.edu|date=16 October 1964|access-date=2 December 2010|archive-date=5 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140305213847/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1196|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 1 March 1979, the two countries formally established embassies in each other's capitals. In 1979, outstanding private claims were resolved and a bilateral trade agreement was completed. Vice President Walter Mondale reciprocated Vice Premier Deng's visit with an August 1979 trip to China. This visit led to agreements in September 1980 on maritime affairs, civil aviation links, and textile matters, as well as a bilateral consular convention.
The threats of the [[Soviet–Afghan War|Soviet invasion of Afghanistan]] and [[Cambodian-Vietnamese War|Vietnamese invasion of Cambodia]] were major factors that brought Washington and Beijing closer than ever before.<ref name=shichor>{{cite book|first=Frederick S.|last=Starr|title=Xinjiang: China's Muslim Borderland|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GXj4a3gss8wC&pg=PA157|year=2004|publisher=M.E. Sharpe|isbn=978-0-7656-3192-3|pages=157–158|access-date=8 December 2018|archive-date=11 February 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240211032209/https://books.google.com/books?id=GXj4a3gss8wC&pg=PA157#v=onepage&q&f=false|url-status=live}}</ref> In June 1979, U.S. Secretary of Health, Education, and Welfare [[Joseph A. Califano Jr.|Josephn A. Califano Jr.]] led an American delegation to China; the trip resulted in the long-term institutionalization of health and education links between the two countries.{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=103}} U.S.-China military cooperation increased over the course of 1979 and 1980.<ref name="Crean-2024">{{Cite book|last=Crean|first=Jeffrey|title=The Fear of Chinese Power: an International History|date=2024|publisher=[[Bloomsbury Academic]]|isbn=978-1-350-23394-2|edition=|series=New Approaches to International History series|location=London, UK}}</ref>{{Rp|page=139}} In 1980, China allowed the United States to establish electronic listening stations in [[Xinjiang]] so the US could monitor Soviet rocket launches in Central Asia.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=54}} In exchange, the US authorized the sale of [[Dual-use technology|dual-use]] civilian and military technology and nonlethal military equipment to China.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=54}}
Chinese demands for advanced technology from the U.S. were not always met, in part due to opposition from Congressmen who either distrusted technology transfer to a communist nation out of principle or concern that there was no guarantee that such technology would not end up in the hands of unfriendly third parties. In 1983, the U.S. State Department changed its classification of China to "a friendly, developing nation",{{Quote without source|date=April 2018}} thereby increasing the amount of technology and armaments that could be sold. The skepticism of some U.S. congressmen was not entirely unmerited as China, during the 1980s, continued to sell arms to [[Iran]] and other states that were openly hostile to American interests.
[[File:Carter DengXiaoping (cropped).jpg|thumb|left|Chinese leader Deng Xiaoping with US President [[Jimmy Carter]]]] As a consequence of high-level and working-level contacts initiated in 1980, U.S. dialogue with the PRC broadened to cover a wide range of issues, including global and regional strategic problems, political-military questions, including arms control, UN, and other multilateral organization affairs, and international narcotics matters. New York City and Beijing became sister cities.<ref>Oksenberg, Michel (1982). "Reconsiderations: A Decade of Sino-American Relations". ''Foreign Affairs'' 61.1: 190.</ref>
High-level exchanges continued to be a significant means for developing US–PRC relations in the 1980s. President [[Ronald Reagan]] and Premier [[Zhao Ziyang]] made reciprocal visits in 1984. Reagan's visit to Beijing went well, however, a speech he made criticizing the Soviet Union and praising capitalism, democracy, and freedom of religion was not aired on Chinese state television. In July 1985, [[President of China|Chinese President]] [[Li Xiannian]] traveled to the US, the first such visit by a PRC head of state. Vice President Bush visited the PRC in October 1985 and opened the U.S. Consulate General in [[Chengdu]], the US's fourth consular post in the PRC. Further exchanges of cabinet-level officials occurred between 1985 and 1989, capped by President Bush's visit to Beijing in February 1989.
[[File:Zhao Ziyang cropped.jpg|150px|thumb|President Ronald Reagan walking with Premier [[Zhao Ziyang]] during his visit to the White House on 10 January 1984]]
Shortly after being elected president in 1980, Ronald Reagan made a speech criticizing the PRC and welcoming restoration of ties with Taiwan. These remarks aroused initial concern in Beijing, but Reagan's advisers quickly apologized for his comments, and the president-elect soon retracted them. Reagan's first two years in office saw some deterioration in U.S.-China relations due to the president's vociferous anti-communism, as well as the inability of the two nations to come to a common understanding over the [[Korean conflict]], the [[Israeli–Palestinian conflict|Israel–Palestine conflict]], or the [[Falklands War]]. In 1982, Chinese leader Deng Xiaoping, in a reiteration of Mao Zedong's "Three Worlds" theory, criticized both the U.S. and Soviet Union for imperialism. In 1983, there were quarrels over a Chinese tennis player, [[Hu Na]], who defected to the US, and over an incident where an Olympic parade float in New York City displayed the flag of Taiwan rather than the PRC's flag. Relations in the early part of 1984 were strained over the issue of US arms sales to Taiwan, but later improved.{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=142}}
By the late 1980s, China was the US's largest partner for science and technology, which had become the largest type of government-to-government exchange between the two countries.<ref name=":Minami" />{{Rp|page=88}}
In the period before the [[1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre]], a growing number of cultural exchange activities gave the American and Chinese peoples broad exposure to each other's cultural, artistic, and educational achievements. Numerous mainland Chinese professionals and official delegations visited the US each month. Many of these exchanges continued after the suppression of the Tiananmen protests.<ref>Robert Suettinger, ''Beyond Tiananmen: The Politics of US-China Relations, 1989–2000'' (Brookings Institution Press, 2004).</ref>
In the first decade after normalization, the US's policy towards China was largely driven by the Executive Branch of the United States, with the notable exception of the [[Taiwan Relations Act]].{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=207}} As a result of the Executive-branch driven approach during this period, China concluded that US Presidents primarily raised Congressional issues as a negotiating tool and that Congress was not itself a significant force in China-US relations.{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=208}} Consequently, China was slow to develop its Congressional liaison capacity.{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=208}}
== Contemporary Relations ==
=== George H. W. Bush administration (1989–1993) === {{Main|Foreign policy of the George H. W. Bush administration}} Americans who had been optimistic about the emergence of democratic characteristics in response to the rapid economic growth and China were stunned and disappointed by the brutal crackdown of the pro-democratic Tiananmen Square protests in 1989.<ref>Eric A. Hyer, "Values Versus Interests: The U.S. Response to the Tiananmen Square Massacre" (Georgetown Institute for the Study of Diplomacy, 1996. [https://www.academia.edu/download/55264904/Values_Versus_Interests_The_U.S._Response_to_the_Tiananmen_Square_Massacre.pdf online]{{dead link|date=July 2022|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> The U.S. and other governments enacted a number of measures against China's violation of [[Human rights in China|human rights]]. The U.S. suspended high-level official exchanges with the PRC and weapons exports from the U.S. to the PRC. The U.S. also imposed a number of [[economic sanctions]].<ref>{{cite web | title=The Dui Hua Foundation-Dialogue – Issue 38: Tiananmen Sanctions: 20 Years & Counting | website=The Dui Hua Foundation | date=2010-01-29 | url=https://duihua.org/dialogue-issue-38-tiananmen-sanctions-20-years-counting/ | access-date=2025-07-24}}</ref> In the summer of 1990, at the [[G7 (major advanced economies)|G7]] [[16th G7 summit|Houston summit]], the West called for renewed political and economic reforms in mainland China, particularly in the field of human rights.<ref>Robert Suettinger, ''Beyond Tiananmen: The Politics of U.S.-China Relations, 1989–2000'' ([[Brookings Institution Press]], 2004.)</ref>
The Tiananmen event disrupted the U.S.-China trade relationship, and U.S. investors' interest in mainland China dropped dramatically. Tourist traffic fell off sharply.<ref>Roehl, Wesley S. (1990). "Travel agent attitudes toward China after Tiananmen Square". ''Journal of Travel Research'' 29.2: 16–22.</ref> The Bush administration denounced the repression and suspended certain trade and investment programs on 5 and 20 June 1989, however Congress was responsible for imposing many of these actions, and the White House itself took a far less critical attitude of Beijing, repeatedly expressing hope that the two countries could maintain normalized relations.<ref>Skidmore, David; Gates, William (1997). "After Tiananmen: The struggle over U.S. policy toward China in the Bush administration". ''[[Presidential Studies Quarterly]]'': 514–539. [https://www.jstor.org/stable/27551766 in JSTOR] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170219024303/http://www.jstor.org/stable/27551766|date=19 February 2017}}</ref> Generally, Bush's preference was for sanctions which were not formalized in law in order to provide flexibility for altering or removing them.{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=210}} Some sanctions were legislated while others were executive actions. Examples include:
*The [[United States Trade and Development Agency|US Trade and Development Agency]] (TDA): new activities in mainland China were suspended from June 1989 until January 2001, when President [[Bill Clinton]] lifted this suspension. *Overseas Private Insurance Corporation (OPIC): new activities have been suspended since June 1989. *Development Bank Lending/[[International Monetary Fund]] (IMF) Credits: the US does not support development bank lending and will not support IMF credits to the PRC except for projects that address basic human needs. *[[United States Munitions List|Munitions List]] Exports: subject to certain exceptions, no licenses may be issued for the export of any defense article on the U.S. Munitions List. This restriction may be waived upon a presidential national interest determination. *Arms Imports – import of defense articles from the PRC was banned after the imposition of the ban on arms exports to the PRC. The import ban was subsequently waived by the administration and reimposed on 26 May 1994. It covers all items on the [[Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives|BATFE]]'s Munitions Import List. During this critical period, [[J. Stapleton Roy]], a career U.S. Foreign Service officer, served as ambassador to Beijing.<ref>{{cite web|title=The Future of U.S.–China Relations|url=http://www.china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=61#keynote|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140305215110/http://www.china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=61#keynote|archive-date=5 March 2014|access-date=31 August 2008|website=[[USC Annenberg School for Communication and Journalism]]}}</ref>
Debate within the United States also began on whether China should continue to receive the annual presidential waiver for [[Most favoured nation|most favored nation]] trading status under the [[Jackson–Vanik amendment|Jackson-Vanik Amendment]].{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=211}}
U.S.-China military ties and arms sales were terminated in 1989 and as of 2024 have never been restored. Chinese public opinion became more hostile to the United States after 1989, as typified by the 1996 manifesto [[China Can Say No]]. The authors called for Beijing to take more aggressive actions against the US and Japan in order to build a stronger international position. The Chinese government at first endorsed the manifesto, then repudiated it as irresponsible.<ref>Yuwu Song, ed., ''Encyclopedia of Chinese-American Relations'' (2009) pp. 56–57.</ref>
The end of the Cold War and [[dissolution of the Soviet Union]] removed the original motives underlying rapprochement between China and the US.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=51}} Motivated by concerns that the United States might curtail support for China's modernization, Deng adopted a low-profile foreign policy to live with the fact of United States hegemony and focus primarily on domestic development.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=51}}
===Clinton administration (1993–2001)=== {{Main|Foreign policy of the Clinton administration}} [[File:Clinton and jiang-1.jpg|thumb|American president [[Bill Clinton]] and Chinese leader [[Jiang Zemin]] holding a joint press conference at the White House, 29 October 1997]] Running for president in 1992, [[Bill Clinton]] sharply criticized his predecessor George H. W. Bush for prioritizing profitable trade relationships over human rights issues in China.<ref>Kerry Dumbaugh, and Richard C. Bush, ''Making China Policy: Lessons from the Bush and Clinton Administrations'' (Rowman & Littlefield, 2001).</ref> Clinton's 28 May 1993 Executive Order 128950 linked future extension of China's most favored nation trading status to China's progress on U.S.-defined human rights measures.{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=222}} China made virtually no effort to comply with the U.S. conditions and in mid-1994 Clinton changed his position,{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=223}} de-linking the China's most favored nation status from human rights issues.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=63}}
Congressional pressure, especially from the [[Republican Party (United States)|Republican Party]], prompted Clinton to approve arms sales to Taiwan, despite the strong displeasure voiced by Beijing.<ref>Yuwu Song, ed., ''Encyclopedia of Chinese-American Relations'' ([[McFarland Publishing]], 2009) p 63.</ref><ref>John W. Dietrich, "Interest groups and foreign policy: Clinton and the China MFN debates." ''Presidential Studies Quarterly'' 29.2 (1999): 280-296.</ref><ref>Jean A. Garrison, ''Making China Policy: From Nixon to GW Bush'' (Lynne Rienner, 2005)</ref>
In July 1993, a symbolic United States Congressional resolution opposed China's efforts to be selected as the host country for the 2000 Summer Olympics.<ref name="Crean-2024" />{{Rp|page=153}} The resolution became a major grievance among the Chinese public, which generally viewed the Resolution as an effort to humiliate China.<ref name="Crean-2024" />{{Rp|pages=153–154}}
[[File:United States trade with china history.gif|thuimb|left]]
In July 1993, the U.S. Navy [[Yinhe incident|stopped the ''Yinhe'', a Chinese container ship]], en route to [[Kuwait]] in international waters, cut off its GPS so that it lost direction and was forced to anchor, and held it in place for twenty-four days.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=63}} The United States incorrectly alleged that the ''Yinhe'' was carrying precursors of [[chemical weapon]]s for Iran.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=63}} It eventually forced an inspection of the ship in [[Saudi Arabia]], but found no chemical precursors.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=63}} The United States refused China's request for a formal apology and refused to pay compensation.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=63}} This incident was viewed in China as international bullying by the United States.<ref>{{cite book|url=http://www.foreignaffairs.house.gov/archives/107/72348.pdf|title=After Hainan: Next Steps for US–China Relations: Hearing Before the Subcommittee on East Asia and the Pacific of the Committee on International Relations, House of Representatives, One Hundred Seventh Congress, First Session, April 25, 2001|place=Washington|publisher=United States Government Publishing Office|page=45|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080813034751/http://www.foreignaffairs.house.gov/archives/107/72348.pdf|archive-date=13 August 2008}}</ref> Nonetheless, Chinese leader [[Jiang Zemin]] adopted a diplomatic posture of goodwill and a "sixteen-characters formula" to working with the United States: "enhancing confidence, avoiding troubles, expanding cooperation, and avoiding confrontation".{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=63}}
[[File:Anti-American Protests in Nanjing, 1999 (flickr 2543499638).jpg|thumb|Anti-American protests in Nanjing following the [[United States bombing of the Chinese embassy in Belgrade]], 1999]] In 1996, the People's Liberation Army conducted military exercises in the Taiwan Strait in an apparent effort to intimidate the Republic of China electorate before the [[1996 Republic of China presidential election|pending presidential elections]], triggering the [[Third Taiwan Strait Crisis]]. The United States dispatched two [[Carrier battle group|aircraft carrier battle groups]] to the region. Subsequently, tensions in the Taiwan Strait diminished and relations between the U.S. and the PRC improved, with increased high-level exchanges and progress on numerous bilateral issues, including human rights, [[nuclear proliferation]], and trade.
China's leader Jiang Zemin visited the United States in the fall of 1997, the first state visit to the U.S. by a paramount leader since 1979. In connection with that visit, the two sides came to a consensus on implementation of their 1985 agreement on Peaceful Nuclear Cooperation, as well as a number of other issues.<ref>Lucian W. Pye, "The United States and Asia in 1997: nothing dramatic, just incremental progress." ''Asian Survey'' 38.1 (1998): 99–106 [https://as.ucpress.edu/content/ucpas/38/1/99.full.pdf online].</ref> President Clinton visited the PRC in June 1998. He traveled extensively in mainland China, and had direct interaction with the Chinese people, including live speeches and a radio show which allowed the President to convey a sense of American ideals and values. In a speech at [[Peking University]], he referred to the 21st century as "your century",<ref>{{Cite web|title=President Clinton's Beijing University speech, 1998 US-China Institute|url=https://china.usc.edu/president-clintons-beijing-university-speech-1998|access-date=16 April 2023|website=china.usc.edu|archive-date=16 April 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230416074949/https://china.usc.edu/president-clintons-beijing-university-speech-1998|url-status=live}}</ref> and expressed his view that technology, including the internet, would help ease any tensions China's economic growth might cause.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Moore|first=Scott|title=China's next act : how sustainability and technology are reshaping China's rise and the world's future|date=2022|isbn=978-0-19-760401-4|location=New York, NY|page=1|oclc=1316703008}}</ref><ref>{{Citation|title=President Clinton at Peking University (1998)| date=9 July 2013|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6IpJZihF0Lw|access-date=16 April 2023}}</ref> President Clinton was criticized by some, however, for failing to pay adequate attention to human rights abuses in mainland China.<ref>Eckholm</ref>{{better source needed|reason=which book or article from Eckholm?|date=October 2025}} When Clinton visited Shanghai, he declared the "three nos" for United States foreign policy towards China: (1) not recognizing two Chinas, (2) not supporting Taiwanese independence, and (3) not supporting Taiwanese efforts to join international organizations for which sovereignty is a membership requirement.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=63}}
Relations were damaged for a time by the [[United States bombing of the Chinese embassy in Belgrade]] on 7 May 1999,{{sfn|Zhao|2022|pp=63–64}} which was stated by the White House to be miscoordination between intelligence and the military. The bombing created outrage among Chinese people, who did not accept the United States claim that the bombing was accidental.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|pp=63–64}} For several days, Beijing was rocked by massive anti-US demonstrations. Deeming the importance of the bilateral relationship too great to be harmed by the embassy bombing, Chinese leader Jiang Zemin sought to calm the Chinese public outrage.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|pp=63–64}} By the end of 1999, relations began to gradually improve. In October 1999, the two countries reached an agreement on compensation for families of those who were victims, as well as payments for damages to respective diplomatic properties in [[Belgrade]] and China. U.S.-China relations in 1999 were also damaged by accusations that a Chinese-American scientist at the [[Los Alamos National Laboratory]] had given U.S. nuclear secrets to Beijing.
===George W. Bush administration (2001–2009)=== [[File:Bushhujintao.jpg|thumb|American President [[George W. Bush]] and Chinese leader [[Hu Jintao]] with first ladies [[Laura Bush]] and [[Liu Yongqing]] wave from the White House in April 2006.]] {{Main|Foreign policy of the George W. Bush administration}} As a presidential candidate in 2000, George W. Bush repeatedly criticized the Clinton-Gore administration for being too friendly with China, which he warned was a strategic competitor.
In the [[Hainan Island incident]] of April 1, 2001, a U.S. [[Lockheed EP-3|EP-3]] surveillance aircraft collided mid-air with a Chinese [[Shenyang J-8]] jet fighter over the South China Sea.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=64}} China sought a formal apology, and accepted United States Secretary of State [[Colin Powell]]'s expression of "very sorry" as sufficient.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=64}} The incident nonetheless created negative feelings towards the United States by the Chinese public and increased public feelings of [[Chinese nationalism]].{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=64}}
Early on as President Bush increased arms sales to Taiwan, including 8 submarines. Bush's hostile position toward China was suddenly reversed after the [[September 11 attacks|11 September terrorist attacks]], and his friendly attitude toward Taiwan became a casualty. Soon he was calling China a strategic partner in the war on terror and postponing deals with Taiwan.<ref>Roberts, Guy (2011). "Circling the Middle Kingdom: George W. Bush and China 1999 – 2003". ''Australasian Journal of American Studies'' 30#1 pp. 57–71.</ref>
Two PRC citizens died in the attacks on the [[World Trade Center (1973–2001)|World Trade Center]].<ref>{{cite book|author=Chi Wang|title=George W. Bush and China: Policies, Problems, and Partnerships|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z_EBv8aeaRoC|year=2008|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739131640}}</ref> Chinese leader Jiang Zemin sent a telegram to Bush within hours of the attack expressing China's condolences and opposition to terror; Bush responded with a phone call the next day stating that he looked forward to working with Jiang and other world leaders to oppose terrorism.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=64}} Chinese companies and individuals also sent expressions of condolences to their American counterparts. The PRC, itself troubled by Muslim separatists in Xinjiang, offered strong public support for the [[War on Terror]] in [[APEC China 2001]]. The PRC voted in favor of [[United Nations Security Council Resolution 1373|UNSCR 1373]], publicly supported the [[United States invasion of Afghanistan|coalition campaign]] in [[Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan|Afghanistan]],<ref>{{cite web|url=https://2009-2017.state.gov/outofdate/bgn/china/29871.htm|title=U.S. State Department – China (03/03)|publisher=state.gov|access-date=6 June 2011|archive-date=25 May 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190525030335/https://2009-2017.state.gov/outofdate/bgn/china/29871.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> and contributed $150 million of bilateral assistance to [[Reconstruction in Afghanistan|Afghan reconstruction]] following the defeat of the [[Taliban]]. Shortly after the 11 September terrorist attacks, the U.S. and PRC also commenced a counterterrorism dialogue. In a March 2002 trip to Beijing, Bush articulated his desire for a "constructive, cooperative, and candid" relationship with China.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=64}} The third round of that dialogue was held in Beijing in February 2003.
In the United States, the threat of terrorist attacks by [[al-Qaeda]] greatly changed the nature of its security concerns.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=64}} It was no longer plausible to argue, as the [[Blue Team (U.S. politics)|Blue Team]] had earlier asserted, that the PRC was the primary security threat to the United States, and the need to focus on the [[Middle East]] and the War on Terror made the avoidance of potential distractions in East Asia a priority for the United States.
There were initial fears among the PRC leadership that the war on terrorism would lead to an anti-PRC effort by the U.S., especially as the U.S. began establishing bases in [[Central Asia]]n countries like [[Uzbekistan]] and [[Tajikistan]] and renewed efforts against [[Iraq]]. The Chinese government was relieved after the United States tied up major national resources with its [[2003 invasion of Iraq]].{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=65}} China believed that the United States' Middle East meant that the United States would need China's help on issues such as counterterrorism, Middle Eastern stability, and nuclear non-proliferation and viewed the United States' focus as conducive to China's emphasis on stability and domestic development.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=65}}
China and the United States worked closely on regional issues, including those pertaining to North Korea and its [[North Korea and weapons of mass destruction|nuclear weapons program]]. China has stressed its opposition to North Korea's decision to withdraw from the [[Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons]], its concerns over North Korea's nuclear capabilities, and its desire for a non-nuclear [[Korean Peninsula]]. It also voted to refer North Korea's noncompliance with its [[International Atomic Energy Agency]] obligations to the UN Security Council.
