In 2001, a presidential plane built in the United States for Chinese leader Jiang Zemin was found to have listening devices installed.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Brown|first=Kerry|title=China Incorporated: The Politics of a World Where China is Number One|date=2023|publisher=[[Bloomsbury Academic]]|isbn=978-1-350-26724-4|location=London|author-link=Kerry Brown (historian)}}</ref>{{Rp|page=53}} Chinese authorities located at least 20 devices, including one in the headboard of the presidential bed.<ref name="Chu-2002">{{Cite web|date=19 January 2002|title=Jiang's U.S.-Built Plane Is Reportedly Bugged|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2002-jan-19-mn-23654-story.html|access-date=3 November 2023|website=Los Angeles Times |archive-date=3 November 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231103135639/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2002-jan-19-mn-23654-story.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The listening devices were capable of being operated via satellite.<ref name="Chu-2002" />
In the mid-2000s, the United States focused relatively less on China issues.<ref name="Crean-2024" />{{Rp|page=158}} This approach was reinforced by the economic benefits to the United States from its relations with China, including cheaper consumer products like clothing and electronics.<ref name="Crean-2024" />{{Rp|page=158}} During this period, the United States also issued significant debt to fund its military interventions and China became the largest foreign purchaser of U.S. government debt.<ref name="Crean-2024" />{{Rp|page=158}}
Taiwan remained a volatile issue, but one that remained under control. The United States policy toward Taiwan involved emphasizing the [[Four Noes and One Without]]. On occasion the United States rebuked Republic of China (Taiwan) President [[Chen Shui-bian]] for provocative pro-independence rhetoric.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=65}} As Bush made his opposition to Taiwan independence clear, the PRC saw the United States as playing a positive role in restraining the separatist movement.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=65}} In 2005, the PRC passed the [[Anti-Secession Law]] which stated that the PRC would be prepared to resort to "non-peaceful means" if Taiwan declared formal independence. Many critics of the PRC, such as the Blue Team, argue that the PRC was trying to take advantage of the U.S. war in Iraq to assert its claims on Republic of China's territory. In 2008, Taiwan voters elected Ma Ying-jeou. Ma, representing the Kuomintang, campaigned on a platform that included rapprochement with mainland China. His election has significant implications for the future of cross-strait relations.<ref>{{cite web|date=31 March 2008|title=US-China Institute :: USCI Symposium Explores The Taiwan Vote|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=986|access-date=2 December 2010|publisher=[[USC Annenberg School for Communication and Journalism]]|archive-date=5 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140305213140/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=986|url-status=live}}</ref>
The 2003 United States invasion of Iraq and the failure of the United States to find evidence of weapons of mass destruction decreased China's respect for America's power and realism.{{sfn|Lampton|2024|p=334}}
China's paramount leader [[Hu Jintao]] visited the United States in April 2006.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=442|title=Text of Pres. Bush's welcome|publisher=China.usc.edu|access-date=2 December 2010|archive-date=11 February 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090211110418/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=442|url-status=live}}</ref> Bush visited Beijing in August for four days to attend the [[2008 Summer Olympics]]. The president and his wife Laura were accompanied by Bush's father, the former president, and his mother Barbara.<ref>Jean Edward Smith, ''Bush'' (2016) pp 646-649.</ref>
===Obama administration (2009–2017)=== {{Main|Foreign policy of the Obama administration}} [[File:Wang Qishan ,Obama Basketball S&ED.jpg|thumb|Vice Premier [[Wang Qishan]] holds the autographed basketball given to him by President Obama following their Washington meeting 28 July 2009, to discuss the outcomes of the first [[U.S.–China Strategic and Economic Dialogue]]. Looking on at left is State Councilor [[Dai Bingguo]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1557|title=Details and video from the meeting|publisher=China.usc.edu|access-date=2 December 2010|archive-date=1 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140301024717/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1557|url-status=live}}</ref>]] The [[2008 United States presidential election|2008 U.S. presidential election]] centered on issues of war and economic recession, but candidates [[Barack Obama]] and [[John McCain]] also spoke extensively regarding U.S. policy toward China.<ref>Video and documents: Obama and China [http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1198] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090330035029/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1198|date=30 March 2009}}; McCain and China [http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1197] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140305213052/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1197|date=5 March 2014}}.</ref> Both favored cooperation with China on major issues, but they differed with regard to trade policy. Obama expressed concern that the value of China's currency was being deliberately set low to benefit China's exporters. McCain argued that free trade was crucial and was having a transformative effect in China. Still, McCain noted that while China might have shared interests with the US, it did not share American values.<ref>''The Economist'' 8 June 2013</ref>
The election of Barack Obama in 2008 generated positive reactions from most locals and state-run media outlets in China.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Malcolm Moore|date=November 2008|title=Chinese entrepreneurs get Obama-mania|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/barackobama/3397615/Chinese-entrepreneurs-get-Obama-mania.html|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220112/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/barackobama/3397615/Chinese-entrepreneurs-get-Obama-mania.html|archive-date=12 January 2022|url-access=subscription|url-status=live|access-date=23 November 2020|website=The Daily Telegraph}}{{cbignore}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|date=October 2008|title=POLITICS-US: Online Poll Shows Obama a Hit in China|url=http://www.ipsnews.net/2008/10/politics-us-online-poll-shows-obama-a-hit-in-china/|access-date=23 November 2020|website=[[Inter Press Service]]|archive-date=7 November 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201107121258/http://www.ipsnews.net/2008/10/politics-us-online-poll-shows-obama-a-hit-in-china/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=29 June 2016|title=Obama's international image remains strong in Europe and Asia|url=https://www.pewresearch.org/global/2016/06/29/2-obamas-international-image-remains-strong-in-europe-and-asia/|access-date=23 November 2020|website=Pew Research Center |archive-date=24 November 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201124161345/https://www.pewresearch.org/global/2016/06/29/2-obamas-international-image-remains-strong-in-europe-and-asia/|url-status=live}}</ref> [[Presidency of Barack Obama|His presidency]] fostered hopes for increased co-operation and heightened levels of friendship between the two nations. On 8 November 2008, Chinese leader Hu Jintao and Obama shared a phone conversation in which Hu congratulated Obama on his election victory. During the conversation both parties agreed that the development of Sino-American relations is not only in the interest of both nations, but also in the interests of the international community.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.enghunan.gov.cn/wwwHome/200811/t20081109_146216.htm|title=President Hu Jintao and US President-elect Barack Obama Discuss over Telephone – Hunan Government|publisher=Enghunan.gov.cn|date=9 November 2008|access-date=2 December 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110721164349/http://www.enghunan.gov.cn/wwwHome/200811/t20081109_146216.htm|archive-date=21 July 2011}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|date=11 November 2008|title=No call from Obama seen as slight to India|url=http://www.asiaone.com/News/Latest%2BNews/Asia/Story/A1Story20081111-99718.html|access-date=2 December 2010|website=[[Asiaone.com]], [[The Statesman (India)]]|archive-date=25 February 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210225042727/https://www.asiaone.com/News/Latest%2BNews/Asia/Story/A1Story20081111-99718.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.asianewsnet.net/news.php?id=2540&sec=1|title=Asia News Network – Xchange Tool|url-status=usurped|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110615161849/http://www.asianewsnet.net/news.php?id=2540&sec=1|archive-date=15 June 2011}}</ref>
During the Obama administration, the U.S. signed more bilateral agreements with China than it had during any other U.S. administration, particularly with regard to addressing climate change.<ref name="Lewis-2023">{{Cite book|last=Lewis|first=Joanna I.|title=Cooperating for the Climate: Learning from International Partnerships in China's Clean Energy Sector|date=2023|publisher=The [[MIT Press]]|isbn=978-0-262-54482-5|location=Cambridge, Massachusetts}}</ref>{{Rp|page=2}} The two countries signed seven clean energy agreements on 17 November 2009, during Obama's visit to China, including an agreement establishing the [[US–China Clean Energy Research Center|U.S.-China Clean Energy Research Center]] (CERC).<ref name="Lewis-2023" />{{Rp|page=|pages=122–123}} CERC was the most ambitious clean energy technology cooperation mechanism between the two.<ref name="Lewis-2023" />{{Rp|page=117}} The many technical exchanges on climate issues during the Obama era helped both sides of the relationship to better understand each other's [[Greenhouse gas emissions|emissions]] models and data, leading to increased mutual trust.<ref name="Lewis-2023" />{{Rp|page=105}}
[[File:Obama and Wen Jiabao.jpg|thumb|right|Obama meets with Chinese Premier [[Wen Jiabao]] and members of the Chinese delegation after a bilateral meeting at the United Nations in New York City in September 2010.]] Following the [[2008 financial crisis]], both U.S. and Chinese governments addressed the economic downturn with massive stimulus initiatives. The Chinese expressed concern that "Buy American" components of the U.S. plan discriminate against foreign producers, including those in China.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1329|title=US-China Institute :: news & features :: making american policy toward china – scholars and policy makers on economics, security, and climate change|publisher=China.usc.edu|access-date=2 December 2010|archive-date=8 July 2012|archive-url=https://archive.today/20120708034849/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1329|url-status=live}}</ref>
As the two most influential and powerful countries in the world, there have been increasingly strong suggestions within American political circles of creating a [[Group of Two|G-2]] (Chimerica) relationship for the United States and China to work out solutions to global problems together.<ref>{{cite web|author=Boston Study Group on Middle East Peace|url=http://www.fpa.org/topics_info2414/topics_info_show.htm?doc_id=912530|title=Foreign Policy Association: Resource Library: Viewpoints: Moving the G-2 Forward|publisher=Fpa.org|access-date=2 December 2010|archive-date=9 September 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210909221729/https://www.fpa.org/topics_info2414/topics_info_show.htm?doc_id=912530|url-status=live}}</ref>
The [[Strategic Economic Dialogue]] initiated by then-US President Bush and Hu and led by U.S. Treasury Secretary [[Henry Paulson]] and Chinese Vice Premier [[Wu Yi (politician)|Wu Yi]] in 2006 was broadened by the [[Presidency of Barack Obama|Obama administration]] into the [[U.S.–China Strategic and Economic Dialogue]].<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|page=288}} It was then led by U.S. Secretary of State [[Hillary Clinton]] and U.S. Secretary of the Treasury [[Timothy Geithner]] for the United States and Vice Premier [[Wang Qishan]] and Chinese State Councilor [[Dai Bingguo]] for China. The focus of the first set of meetings in July 2009 was in response to the economic crisis, finding ways to cooperate to stem global warming and addressing issues such as the proliferation of nuclear weapons and humanitarian crises.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1557|title=Economic Crisis, Looming Environmental Threats, and Growing Nuclear Weapons Worries -- All in a Day's Work at the Strategic and Economic Dialogue 中美战略与经济对话|publisher=China.usc.edu|access-date=2 December 2010|archive-date=1 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140301024717/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1557|url-status=live}}</ref>
[[File:P011911SA-0499 (5376833854).jpg|thumb|left|Obama meets with Chinese leader [[Hu Jintao]] in January 2011.]] Obama visited China on 15–18 November 2009 to discuss economic worries, concerns over nuclear weapon proliferation, and the need for action against climate change.<ref>The aims and challenges of the trip were summarized by the USC US-China Institute: [http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1902] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140305214438/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1902|date=5 March 2014}}, [http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1873] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140305214703/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1873|date=5 March 2014}}.</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Instant Analysis: Reporting on U.S. Presidents in China|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1895|access-date=2 December 2010|publisher=China.usc.edu|archive-date=28 May 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100528104746/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1895|url-status=live}}</ref>
In January 2010, the U.S. proposed a $6.4 billion arms sale to the Republic of China (Taiwan). In response, the PRC threatened to impose sanctions on U.S. companies supplying arms to Taiwan and suspend cooperation on certain regional and international issues.<ref>{{cite news|date=30 January 2010|title=China hits back at U.S. over Taiwan weapons sale|work=BBC News|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/americas/8488765.stm|access-date=2 December 2010|archive-date=18 November 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101118221635/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/americas/8488765.stm|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 19 February 2010, Obama met with the [[14th Dalai Lama|Dalai Lama]], accused by China of "fomenting unrest in [[Tibet Autonomous Region|Tibet]]." After the meeting, China summoned the U.S. ambassador to China, [[Jon Huntsman Jr.|Jon Huntsman]],<ref>{{Cite news|last=Macartney|first=Jane|date=19 February 2010|title=China summons US Ambassador over Dalai Lama's meeting with Obama|work=[[The Times]]|location=London|url=http://www.timesonline.co.uk/tol/news/world/us_and_americas/article7033132.ece|access-date=12 July 2010}}{{dead link|date=September 2024|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> but ''[[Time (magazine)|Time]]'' has described the Chinese reaction as "muted", speculating that it could be because "the meeting came during the [[Chinese New Year]]... when most officials are on leave". Some activists criticized Obama for the relatively low profile of the visit.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,1966629,00.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100220230852/http://www.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,1966629,00.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=20 February 2010|title=In China, Muted Reaction to Dalai Lama's Visit|first=Austin|last=Ramzy|publisher=[[Time (magazine)|Time magazine]]|date=19 February 2010|access-date=12 July 2010}}</ref>
[[File:Secretary Kerry and Chinese Premier Li Shake Hands Before Their Meeting (10170270646).jpg|thumb|left|U.S. Secretary of State [[John Kerry]] speaks with Chinese Premier [[Li Keqiang]], 9 October 2013.]] In 2012, the PRC criticized Obama's new defense strategy, which it said was aimed at isolating China in East Asia.<ref>Lee, Mj. [https://www.politico.com/story/2012/01/china-fires-at-new-us-defense-plan-071233 "China fires at new U.S. defense plan."] ''[[Politico]]'', 9 January 2012.</ref> Obama is looking to increase U.S. military influence in the area with a rotating presence of forces in friendly countries.<ref>Whitlock, Craig. [https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/national-security/philippines-may-allow-greater-us-presence-in-latest-reaction-to-chinas-rise/2012/01/24/gIQAhFIyQQ_story.html "Philippines may allow greater U.S. military presence in reaction to China's rise."] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170715212230/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/national-security/philippines-may-allow-greater-us-presence-in-latest-reaction-to-chinas-rise/2012/01/24/gIQAhFIyQQ_story.html|date=15 July 2017}} ''The Washington Post'', 25 January 2012.</ref>
In March 2012, China suddenly began cutting back its purchases of oil from Iran, which along with some signs on sensitive security issues like [[Syria]] and North Korea, showed some coordination with the Obama administration.<ref>{{cite news|title=U.S. Sees Positive Signs From China on Security Issues|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/04/27/world/asia/us-is-seeing-positive-signs-from-chinese.html|access-date=9 November 2012|newspaper=The New York Times|date=26 April 2012|author=Mark Landler and Steven Lee Myers|archive-date=12 May 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120512221307/http://www.nytimes.com/2012/04/27/world/asia/us-is-seeing-positive-signs-from-chinese.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
In March 2013, the U.S. and China agreed to impose stricter sanctions on North Korea for conducting nuclear tests, which sets the stage for UN Security Council vote. Such accord might signal a new level of cooperation between the U.S. and China.<ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/2013-03-07/china-u-s-accord-sets-un-vote-on-north-korea-sanctions.html| work=Bloomberg| title=China-U.S. Accord Sets UN Vote on North Korea Sanctions| access-date=6 March 2017| archive-date=5 September 2014| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140905161716/http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2013-03-07/china-u-s-accord-sets-un-vote-on-north-korea-sanctions.html| url-status=live}}</ref>
[[File:Vice President Biden Raises a Toast in Honor of Chinese President Xi at a State Luncheon at the State Department (21723827681).jpg|thumb|U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry and Vice President [[Joe Biden]] with Chinese leader Xi Jinping, 25 September 2015]] In an effort to build a "new model" of relations, Obama met Chinese leader [[Xi Jinping]] for two days of meetings, between 6 and 8 June 2013, at the [[Sunnylands]] estate in [[Rancho Mirage]], California.<ref name="Calms Myers NYT">{{cite news|last=Calmes|first=Jackie and Steven Lee Myers|title=U.S. and China Move Closer on North Korea, but Not on Cyberespionage|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2013/06/09/world/asia/obama-and-xi-try-building-a-new-model-for-china-us-ties.html|access-date=11 June 2013|newspaper=The New York Times|date=8 June 2013|archive-date=10 June 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130610230446/http://www.nytimes.com/2013/06/09/world/asia/obama-and-xi-try-building-a-new-model-for-china-us-ties.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The summit was considered "the most important meeting between an [[American president]] and a [[Leader of the Chinese Communist Party|Chinese Communist leader]] in 40 years, since President Nixon and Chairman [[Mao Zedong|Mao]]," according to [[Joseph Nye]], a political scientist at Harvard University.<ref name="Sanger NYT">{{cite news|last=Sanger|first=David E.|title=Obama and Xi Try to Avoid a Cold War Mentality|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2013/06/10/world/asia/obama-and-xi-try-to-avoid-a-cold-war-mentality.html|newspaper=The New York Times|date=9 June 2013|access-date=6 February 2017|archive-date=24 December 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161224123355/http://www.nytimes.com/2013/06/10/world/asia/obama-and-xi-try-to-avoid-a-cold-war-mentality.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The leaders concretely agreed to [[climate change mitigation|combat climate change]] and also found strong mutual interest in curtailing North Korea's [[North Korea and weapons of mass destruction|nuclear program]].<ref name="Sanger NYT"/> However, the leaders remained sharply divided over cyber espionage and U.S. arms sales to Taiwan. Xi was dismissive of American complaints about cyber security.<ref name="FT McGregor">{{cite news|last=McGregor|first=Richard|title=Obama-Xi summit presented as a walk in the park|url=http://www.ft.com/intl/cms/s/0/a47cc686-d118-11e2-be7b-00144feab7de.html#axzz2VwWJHEKV|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221210201220/https://www.ft.com/content/a47cc686-d118-11e2-be7b-00144feab7de#axzz2VwWJHEKV|archive-date=10 December 2022|url-access=subscription|access-date=11 June 2013|newspaper=Financial Times|date=10 June 2013|url-status=live}}</ref> [[Thomas E. Donilon|Tom Donilon]], the outgoing U.S. [[National Security Advisor (United States)|National Security Adviser]], stated that cyber security "is now at the center of the relationship", adding that if China's leaders were unaware of this fact, they know now.<ref name="FT McGregor"/>
[[File:Xi Jinping and Barack Obama toast at White House state dinner September 2015.jpg|thumb|left|U.S. President Barack Obama and Chinese leader Xi Jinping in September 2015]]
Obama supported the [[One-China policy]].<ref>"[https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-taiwan-obama-idUSKBN13S013?il=0 White House: no change to 'one China' policy after Trump call with Taiwan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180711091802/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-taiwan-obama-idUSKBN13S013?il=0|date=11 July 2018}}". Reuters. 2 December 2016.</ref> In 2014, Obama stated that "We recognize Tibet as part of the People's Republic of China. We are not in favor of [[Tibetan independence movement|independence]]."<ref>"[https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/the-press-office/2014/11/12/remarks-president-obama-and-president-xi-jinping-joint-press-conference Remarks by President Obama and President Xi Jinping in Joint Press Conference] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170122023504/https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/the-press-office/2014/11/12/remarks-president-obama-and-president-xi-jinping-joint-press-conference|date=22 January 2017}}". 23 November 2014.</ref>
Beginning in 2015, China's [[People's Liberation Army Air Force]] began patrolling the South China Sea, including the disputed [[Paracel Islands|Paracel]] and [[Spratly Islands]].<ref name=":Li">{{Cite book|last=Li|first=Xiaobing|title=China under Xi Jinping: A New Assessment|publisher=[[Leiden University Press]]|year=2024|isbn=9789087284411|editor-last=Fang|editor-first=Qiang|chapter=Beijing's Military Power and East Asian-Pacific Hot Spots|editor-last2=Li|editor-first2=Xiaobing}}</ref>{{Rp|page=273}} In China's view, these disputed areas are within its [[Air defense identification zone|Air Defense Identification Zone]] (ADIZ).<ref name=":Li" />{{Rp|page=273}} The [[United States Air Force]] does not accept this view, and flies its military planes through the area without informing China.<ref name=":Li" />{{Rp|page=273}}
In May 2015, [[United States Secretary of Defense|U.S. Secretary of Defense]] [[Ashton Carter]] warned China to halt its rapid [[Territorial disputes in the South China Sea|island-building]] in the [[South China Sea]].<ref>"[https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/checkpoint/wp/2015/05/27/defense-secretarys-warning-to-china-u-s-military-wont-change-operations/ Defense secretary's warning to China: U.S. military won't change operations] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181030131220/https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/checkpoint/wp/2015/05/27/defense-secretarys-warning-to-china-u-s-military-wont-change-operations/|date=30 October 2018}}". ''[[The Washington Post]]''. 27 May 2015.</ref>
Obama hosted Xi for a bilateral meeting on the margins of the Nuclear Security Summit on 31 March 2016.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/the-press-office/2016/03/24/statement-press-secretary-bilateral-meeting-president-xi-jinping-peoples|work=[[whitehouse.gov]]|title=Statement by the Press Secretary on Bilateral Meeting with President Xi Jinping of the People's Republic of China|via=[[NARA|National Archives]]|date=24 March 2016|access-date=1 March 2021|archive-date=10 March 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210310161939/https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/the-press-office/2016/03/24/statement-press-secretary-bilateral-meeting-president-xi-jinping-peoples|url-status=live}}</ref>
===First Trump administration (2017–2021)=== {{see also|Foreign policy of the first Trump administration#China, Taiwan, and the South China Sea|Tariffs in the first Trump administration|China–United States trade war}}
[[File:President Xi Jinping Greets Secretary Tillerson (33139050550).jpg|thumb|Secretary of State [[Rex Tillerson]] shakes hands with Chinese leader Xi Jinping upon arrival in Beijing, 19 March 2017.]] [[File:Secretary Pompeo Meets With Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing (40985611020).jpg|thumb|Secretary of State [[Mike Pompeo]] shakes hands with Chinese leader Xi Jinping upon arrival in Beijing, 14 June 2018.]] During his presidential campaign, [[Donald Trump]] promised an assertive stance towards China. The presidency of Trump led to a negative shift in U.S. relations with China.<ref>{{Cite news| last1=Allen-Ebrahimian| first1=Bethany| title=Special report: Trump's U.S.-China transformation| url=https://www.axios.com/trump-china-policy-special-report-154fa5c2-469d-4238-8d72-f0641abc0dfa.html| date=19 January 2021| work=[[Axios (website)|Axios]]| access-date=31 January 2021| archive-date=29 January 2021| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210129173516/https://www.axios.com/trump-china-policy-special-report-154fa5c2-469d-4238-8d72-f0641abc0dfa.html| url-status=live}}</ref>
President-elect Trump's telephone conversation with the president of Taiwan [[Tsai Ing-wen]] on 2 December 2016 was the first such contact with Taiwan by an American president-elect or president since 1979. It provoked Beijing to lodge a diplomatic protest ("stern representations").<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-taiwan/trump-speaks-to-taiwans-president-financial-times-idUSKBN13R2NT|title=China lodges protest after Trump call with Taiwan president|date=4 December 2016|newspaper=Reuters|access-date=17 November 2017|archive-date=17 November 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171117174647/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-taiwan/trump-speaks-to-taiwans-president-financial-times-idUSKBN13R2NT|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="taiw">{{cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-china/trump-says-u-s-not-necessarily-bound-by-one-china-policy-idUSKBN1400TY|title=Trump says U.S. not necessarily bound by 'one China' policy|date=12 December 2016|newspaper=Reuters|access-date=17 November 2017|archive-date=14 November 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171114231730/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-china/trump-says-u-s-not-necessarily-bound-by-one-china-policy-idUSKBN1400TY|url-status=live}}</ref> Trump went on to clarify his move: "I fully understand the [[One-China policy|'one China' policy]], but I don't know why we have to be bound by a 'one China' policy unless we make a deal with China having to do with other things, including trade."<ref name="taiw" />
On President Trump's inauguration day, an official from the China's People's Liberation Army wrote on the official website that the American military build-up in East Asia and the Asia Pacific, and its push to arm South Korea with the [[Terminal High Altitude Area Defense|THAAD]] missile-defense system were provocative "hot spots getting closer to ignition" and that the chances of war had become "more real."<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/chinese-military-official-war-2017-1|title=Chinese military official warns that war with U.S. under Trump is becoming a 'practical reality'|website=[[Business Insider]]|access-date=7 February 2017|archive-date=8 February 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170208133600/http://www.businessinsider.com/chinese-military-official-war-2017-1|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy-defence/article/2065799/china-steps-preparedness-possible-military-conflict-us|title=China 'steps up preparedness for possible military conflict with US'|date=26 January 2017|access-date=31 January 2017|archive-date=27 February 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190227055308/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy-defence/article/2065799/china-steps-preparedness-possible-military-conflict-us|url-status=live}}</ref>
On taking office, the Trump administration stopped negotiations on a [[bilateral investment treaty]] with China which had begun in 2008.<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|page=312}} According to [[Michael Froman]], the lead negotiator during the preceding four years, the effort to reach an agreement was "more than 90 percent complete."<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|page=312}}
On 23 January, speaking about China's claims to sovereignty over the [[Spratly Islands]] in the [[South China Sea]], White House spokesman [[Sean Spicer]] said, "It's a question of if those islands are in fact in international waters and not part of China proper, then yeah, we're going to make sure that we defend international territories from being taken over by one country."<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-southchinasea/u-s-says-will-prevent-china-taking-over-territory-in-international-waters-idUSKBN1572M4|title=China says will protect South China Sea sovereignty|date=24 January 2017|newspaper=Reuters|access-date=17 November 2017|archive-date=17 November 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171117175042/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-southchinasea/u-s-says-will-prevent-china-taking-over-territory-in-international-waters-idUSKBN1572M4|url-status=live}}</ref>
[[File:President Trump's Trip to Asia (37575409684).jpg|thumb|left|President Trump arrives in China in November 2017.]] On 4 January, on a visit to Japan, U.S. Defense Secretary [[James Mattis]] reaffirmed Washington's commitment under the [[Treaty of Mutual Cooperation and Security between the United States and Japan]] to defending Japan, including the [[Senkaku Islands]] (the [[East China Sea]]) that are claimed by China.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://edition.cnn.com/2017/02/03/asia/us-defense-secretary-mattis-japan-visit/|title=Mattis: U.S. will defend Japanese islands claimed by China|first=Brad|last=Lendon|date=4 February 2017|work=CNN|access-date=7 February 2017|archive-date=13 August 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180813123854/https://edition.cnn.com/2017/02/03/asia/us-defense-secretary-mattis-japan-visit/|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 9 February, Trump spoke with China's leader Xi Jinping over the phone discussing a wide range of issues; Trump was said to have re-iterated the United States' commitment to the status quo 'one-China' policy.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-38927891|title=Trump climbdown on 'One China' threats|work=BBC News|date=10 February 2017|access-date=22 June 2018|archive-date=28 May 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180528200157/http://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-38927891|url-status=live}}</ref>
In a 3 July 2017 telephone conversation with Trump, Xi stated, "China-US relations have made great progress in recent days, but they have also been affected by some negative factors."<ref name="China-US tensions">{{cite news|last=Bodeen|first=Christopher|title=China's Xi warns Trump of 'negative factors' hurting US ties |publisher=Associated Press|date=3 July 2017|url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/chinas-xi-points-negative-factors-affecting-us-ties-071957481--politics.html|access-date=5 July 2017|archive-date=19 July 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170719095004/https://www.yahoo.com/news/chinas-xi-points-negative-factors-affecting-us-ties-071957481--politics.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> By "negative factors", [[Geng Shuang]], a Chinese government spokesmen, explained in a televised briefing: "Under the pretext of navigational freedom, the American side once again sent military vessels into the Chinese territorial waters of Xisha ([[Paracel Islands|Paracel]]) Islands. It has violated Chinese and international law, infringed upon Chinese sovereignty, and disrupted order, peace and security of the relevant waters and put in jeopardy facilities and personnel on the relevant Chinese islands. It is a serious political and military provocation. The Chinese side is strongly dissatisfied with and firmly opposed to the relevant actions by the US."<ref name="China-US tensions" />
In 2017, the Trump administration terminated the Joint Commission on Commerce and Trade (JCCT) between China and the United States.<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|pages=287–288}} The JCCT had met annually from 1983 to 2016 and had been a generally effective mechanism to address various trade issues between the two countries.<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|pages=287–288}} The Trump administration also terminated the Strategic and Economic Dialogue after holding the June 2017 meeting under the name "Comprehensive Economic Dialogue."<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|page=288}}
According to ''Chaos Under Heaven'', a book by [[Josh Rogin]], a group of officials including [[Peter Navarro]], [[Stephen Miller (political advisor)|Stephen Miller]] and [[Steve Bannon]] that wanted Trump to "speed the downfall" of the [[Chinese Communist Party]] (CCP) and that "believed in economic nationalism, the return of manufacturing from abroad, and the protection of domestic industries, even at the expense of free trade" emerged during the early stage of the Trump administration.<ref name=":Tsang&Cheung">{{Cite book |last1=Rogin |first1=Josh |author-link=Josh Rogin |title=Chaos Under Heaven: Trump, Xi, and the Battle for the Twenty-First Century |publisher=[[Mariner Books]] |year=2021 |isbn=9780358393245 |pages=27–28}}</ref>
[[File:Donald Trump and Xi Jinping meets at 2018 G20 Summit.jpg|thumb|Chinese leader [[Xi Jinping]] with U.S. President [[Donald Trump]] at the [[2018 G20 Buenos Aires summit|G20 summit in Buenos Aires]], December 2018<ref name="Xi's title">{{Cite news |last=Churchill |first=Owen |date=25 July 2020 |title=US officials now call Xi Jinping 'general secretary' instead of China's 'president' – but why? |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/world/united-states-canada/article/3094669/us-officials-switch-calling-chinas-xi-jinping |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200724220251/https://www.scmp.com/news/world/united-states-canada/article/3094669/us-officials-switch-calling-chinas-xi-jinping |archive-date=24 July 2020 |access-date=25 July 2020 |website=[[South China Morning Post]]}}</ref>]]
China enforced punitive tariffs on 128 categories of American goods on 1 April 2018 in retaliation for the [[First presidency of Donald Trump|first Trump administration]]'s national-security levies on steel and aluminum imports the previous month. The Chinese Government's response is measured, affecting $3 billion in annual trade or about 2% of U.S. goods exports to China. By late September 2018, the Trump administration had placed tariffs (25% tax increase) on $250 billion worth of Chinese goods, in an attempt to offset the trade imbalance between the two major economic world powers.
In what put additional strain on U.S.-China relations, [[Huawei]]'s vice-chair and [[Chief financial officer|CFO]] [[Meng Wanzhou]] was arrested in Canada on 1 December 2018 at the behest of U.S. authorities.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/12/05/tech/huawei-cfo-arrested-canada/index.html|title=Huawei CFO Meng Wanzhou arrested in Canada|first=Julia |last=Horowitz|website=CNN|date=5 December 2018|access-date=25 October 2019|archive-date=4 March 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200304064656/https://www.cnn.com/2018/12/05/tech/huawei-cfo-arrested-canada/index.html|url-status=live}}</ref> U.S. Senator [[Ben Sasse]] accused China of undermining [[National security of the United States|U.S. national security interests]], often "using private sector entities" to by-pass US sanctions against the sale of telecom equipment to Iran.<ref>{{cite news|title=Huawei finance chief Meng Wanzhou arrested in Canada|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/business-46462858|work=BBC News|date=6 November 2018|access-date=6 December 2018|archive-date=6 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181206170423/https://www.bbc.com/news/business-46462858|url-status=live}}</ref>
According to political analyst, Andrew Leung, "China is perceived as the antagonist and rival of the United States," and that China's economic growth is seen as a "threat to the world order underpinned by American dominance or American values."<ref>[[Al Jazeera Arabic|Al Jazeera]] ''Inside Story'', 8 December 2018, [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Bga1nIstT5Q Why is China's biggest technology company being targeted?], Minutes 12:55; 14:10-ff.</ref> He claimed, moreover, that the arrest of the [[CFO]] of Huawei on 1 December 2018 corresponded with the suspicious death on that same day of a leading Chinese national [[quantum physicist]] and venture capitalist at [[Stanford University]], [[Shoucheng Zhang]], who was on a [[H-1B visa]], giving rise to conspiracy theories. In August 2018, the U.S. government signed an update to legislation for the [[Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States|Committee on Foreign Investment in the U.S]]., broadening governmental scrutiny to vetting [[Venture capital|VC]]-backed, and especially Chinese state-funded, investments in U.S. tech startups.<ref>{{cite news|last=Ren|first=Shuli|date=9 December 2018|title=Beyond Huawei, Scientist's Death Hurts China's Technology Quest |work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/beyond-huawei-scientists-death-hurts-chinas-technologyquest/2018/12/09/a59bf62a-fc2a-11e8-a17e-162b712e8fc2_story.html|url-status=dead|access-date=8 January 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181210072316/https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/beyond-huawei-scientists-death-hurts-chinas-technologyquest/2018/12/09/a59bf62a-fc2a-11e8-a17e-162b712e8fc2_story.html|archive-date=10 December 2018}}</ref> [[File:Hong Kong IMG 20190831 155344 (48659677441).jpg|thumb|U.S. lawmakers from both parties have voiced their support for pro-democracy [[2019–20 Hong Kong protests|Hong Kong protests]] in 2019.]]
[[File:President Trump at the G20 (48162296741).jpg|thumb|Chinese leader [[Xi Jinping]] with U.S. President [[Donald Trump]] at the [[2019 G20 Osaka summit|G20 summit in Osaka]], June 2019]] [[File:Signing Ceremony Phase One Trade Deal Between the U.S. & China (49391434906).jpg|thumb|President Donald Trump and [[Vice Premier of China|Vice Premier]] [[Liu He (politician)|Liu He]] sign the Phase One Trade Deal in January 2020.]] Both sides signed the US–China Phase One trade deal on 15 January.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2020-01-15/u-s-china-sign-phase-one-of-trade-deal-trump-calls-remarkable|title=U.S. and China Sign Phase One of Trade Deal|last1=Donnan|first1=Shawn|last2=Wingrove|first2=Josh|date=15 January 2020|newspaper=Bloomberg.com|last3=Mohsin|first3=Saleha|access-date=20 January 2020|archive-date=19 January 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200119030159/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2020-01-15/u-s-china-sign-phase-one-of-trade-deal-trump-calls-remarkable|url-status=live}}</ref> Unlike other trade agreements, the agreement did not rely on arbitration through an [[intergovernmental organization]] like the [[World Trade Organization]], but rather through a bilateral mechanism.<ref>{{Cite news| last1=Davis| first1=Bob| title=U.S.-China Deal Could Upend the Way Nations Settle Disputes| url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/u-s-china-deal-could-upend-the-way-nations-settle-disputes-11579211598| date=16 January 2020| work=[[The Wall Street Journal]]| access-date=21 January 2020}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news| last1=Lawder| first1=David| title=In U.S.-China Phase 1 trade deal, enforcement may end in 'We quit'| url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trade-china-enforcement/in-u-s-china-phase-1-trade-deal-enforcement-may-end-in-we-quit-idUSKBN1ZE2T1| date=15 January 2020| work=[[Reuters]] | access-date=21 January 2020}}</ref>
====Rapid deterioration of relations (2019–2020)==== {{see also|Second Cold War}} [[Michael D. Swaine]] warned in 2019, "The often positive and optimistic forces, interests, and beliefs that sustained bilateral ties for decades are giving way to undue pessimism, hostility, and a zero-sum mindset in almost every area of engagement."<ref>Michael D Swaine, [https://carnegieendowment.org/posts/2019/01/a-relationship-under-extreme-duress-us-china-relations-at-a-crossroads?lang=en "A Relationship Under Extreme Duress: U.S.-China Relations at a Crossroads" ''Carnegie Endowment for International Peace'' (Jan. 16, 2019)]</ref>
{{blockquote|The U.S.-China relationship is confronting its most daunting challenge in the forty years since the two countries established diplomatic ties. Current trends portend steadily worsening relations over the long term, with increasingly adverse consequences for all actors involved. Specifically, Beijing and Washington are transitioning from a sometimes contentious yet mutually beneficial relationship to an increasingly antagonistic, mutually destructive set of interactions. The often positive and optimistic forces, interests, and beliefs that sustained bilateral ties for decades are giving way to undue pessimism, hostility, and a zero-sum mindset in almost every area of engagement.<ref>Michael D Swaine, [https://carnegieendowment.org/posts/2019/01/a-relationship-under-extreme-duress-us-china-relations-at-a-crossroads?lang=en "A Relationship Under Extreme Duress: U.S.-China Relations at a Crossroads" ''Carnegie Endowment for International Peace'' (Jan. 16, 2019)]</ref>}}
According to two experts on U.S.-China relations, [[Rosemary Foot (writer)|Rosemary Foot]] and Amy King, the consensus of experts is that: :The relationship began to deteriorate in the second decade of the 21st century, and that the Trump administration has accelerated the deterioration. Explanations...have ranged over a large number of factors, all of which have played some role. Some relate to changes in official personnel in both the United States and China, others to the shifts and relative power between the two countries after the [[2008 financial crisis]], and yet others to China's greater determination to reform global governance institutions and to play more of a global leadership role.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Foot|first1=Rosemary|last2=King|first2=Amy|date=1 June 2019|title=Assessing the deterioration in China–U.S. relations: U.S. governmental perspectives on the economic-security nexus|journal=China International Strategy Review|volume=1|issue=1|pages=39–50|doi=10.1007/s42533-019-00005-y|issn=2524-5635|doi-access=free}}</ref>
Foot and King emphasize China's aggressive efforts in developing cutting-edge technologies with significant military and commercial implications, while the United States sees the need to defend itself aggressively against technological theft.<ref>Foot and King, "Assessing the deterioration in China–US relations: US governmental perspectives on the economic-security nexus." (2019)</ref>
U.S. academics have made various policy prescriptions for the United States within the context of its deteriorating relationship with China.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Walt|first1=Stephen M|title=Yesterday's Cold War Shows How to Beat China Today|url=https://foreignpolicy.com/2019/07/29/yesterdays-cold-war-shows-how-to-beat-china-today/|work=Foreign Policy |date=29 July 2019|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=22 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200922181821/https://foreignpolicy.com/2019/07/29/yesterdays-cold-war-shows-how-to-beat-china-today/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Dobriansky|first1=Paula J.|title=An Allied Plan to Depend Less on China|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/an-allied-plan-to-depend-less-on-china-11588288513|agency=The Wall Street Journal|work=The Wall Street Journal|date=30 April 2020|quote=Washington and its partners in Asia should set up new supply chains, restructure trade relations, and start to create an international economic order that is less dependent on China. A multilateral "coalition of the willing" approach would better align trading ties with political and security relationships. It would also help India and nations in Southeast Asia develop more rapidly, becoming stronger U.S. partners.|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=3 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200803161319/https://www.wsj.com/articles/an-allied-plan-to-depend-less-on-china-11588288513|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Friedberg|first1=Aaron L.|title=An Answer to Aggression|url=https://www.foreignaffairs.com/articles/china/2020-08-11/ccp-answer-aggression|work=Foreign Affairs|date=September–October 2020|quote=The aims of this approach should be twofold: first, to deny Beijing its immediate objectives, imposing costs, slowing the growth of China's power and influence, and reducing the threat it can pose to democracies and to an open international system; and second, by demonstrating the futility of China's current strategy, to change the calculations of its ruling elite, forcing them to eventually rethink both their foreign and their domestic policies. This will take time, and given Xi's obvious predispositions and commitments, success may well depend on changes in the top leadership of the CCP.|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=10 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200910170234/https://www.foreignaffairs.com/articles/china/2020-08-11/ccp-answer-aggression|url-status=live}}</ref>
According to [[Lawrence J. Lau]], a major cause of the deterioration is the growing battle between China and the United States for global economic and technological dominance. More generally, he argues, "It is also a reflection of the rise of populism, isolationism, nationalism and protectionism almost everywhere in the world, including in the US."<ref>Lawrence J. Lau, "The China–US Trade War and Future Economic Relations." ''China and the World'' (Lau Chor Tak Institute of Global Economics and Finance, 2019): 1–32. quote p. 3 [http://www.igef.cuhk.edu.hk/igef_media/working-paper/IGEF/igef%20working%20paper%20no.%2072%20english%20version.pdf online] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200624111818/https://www.igef.cuhk.edu.hk/igef_media/working-paper/IGEF/igef%20working%20paper%20no.%2072%20english%20version.pdf|date=24 June 2020}}</ref> According to Ian Bremmer, the U.S. and China are in a technology cold war<ref>{{cite news|last1=Swaminathan|first1=Aarthi|title=US and China heading towards a cold war: Ian Bremmer|url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/us-and-china-heading-towards-a-cold-war-ian-bremmer-182045322.html|agency=Yahoo News|date=28 May 2020|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=17 July 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200717105629/https://finance.yahoo.com/news/us-and-china-heading-towards-a-cold-war-ian-bremmer-182045322.html|url-status=live}}</ref> and Trump's technology war against the PRC has been his administration's biggest foreign policy win, saying, "on the issue of tech decoupling that it was America out front with most allies on board."<ref>{{cite magazine|title=Trump's Biggest Foreign Policy Win So Far|url=https://time.com/5868095/trumps-biggest-foreign-policy-win/|magazine=Time|date=20 July 2020|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=9 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200909213711/https://time.com/5868095/trumps-biggest-foreign-policy-win/|url-status=live}}</ref> According to [[Greg Autry]], an academic at the University of Southern California, Trump's China policy was working, pointing to increased revenue intakes by the Treasury Department and offshoring by U.S. manufacturing supply chains from China, and crediting the administration for being the first to fully recognize that globalization had not delivered for Americans and that China was an existential threat.<ref>{{cite news|title=Opinion: President Trump's China policy is working, but you'd never know that from media reports|url=https://www.latimes.com/opinion/story/2019-09-19/trump-china-tariffs-recession-peter-navarro-trade |work=Los Angeles Times|date=20 September 2019|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=26 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200926001645/https://www.latimes.com/opinion/story/2019-09-19/trump-china-tariffs-recession-peter-navarro-trade|url-status=live}}</ref>
Former Obama administration officials [[Samantha Power]] and [[Susan Rice]] have criticized China's actions on trade, over the Meng Wenzhou affair and in Hong Kong while simultaneously criticizing the Trump administration for inadequate pushback.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Galloway|first1=Anthony|date=24 November 2019|title='I fear something worse': Obama adviser on the danger of China and the need to stick by the US|work=[[Sydney Morning Herald]]|url=https://www.smh.com.au/politics/federal/i-fear-something-worse-obama-adviser-on-the-danger-of-china-and-the-need-to-stick-by-the-us-20191121-p53cpr.html|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=28 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200828194412/https://www.smh.com.au/politics/federal/i-fear-something-worse-obama-adviser-on-the-danger-of-china-and-the-need-to-stick-by-the-us-20191121-p53cpr.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|date=3 November 2019|title=Susan Rice on Trump, impeachment and why Canada shouldn't back down to China|publisher=[[CBC News]]|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/world/susan-rice-the-national-interview-trump-impeachment-canada-china-huawei-1.5346230|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=28 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200828141649/https://www.cbc.ca/news/world/susan-rice-the-national-interview-trump-impeachment-canada-china-huawei-1.5346230|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Team Biden's Policies on China and Taiwan|url=https://thediplomat.com/2020/07/team-bidens-policies-on-china-and-taiwan/|agency=The Diplomat|date=16 July 2020|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=7 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200907165804/https://thediplomat.com/2020/07/team-bidens-policies-on-china-and-taiwan/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=China's leadership is 'not scared of Donald Trump' on trade: Susan Rice|url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/susan-rice-on-trump-and-china-125627961.html|agency=Yahoo Finance|date=18 October 2019|access-date=9 September 2020|archive-date=26 January 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210126180037/https://finance.yahoo.com/news/susan-rice-on-trump-and-china-125627961.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
In 2018, the U.S. Department of Justice initiated a "[[China Initiative]]" to "combat economic espionage".<ref>{{Cite web|last=Demers|first=John|date=11 February 2020|title=Report on the China Initiative, 2018-2019|url=https://china.usc.edu/us-department-justice-china-initiative-2018-2019|access-date=10 August 2020|website=USC U.S.-China Institute|archive-date=30 November 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211130095918/https://china.usc.edu/us-department-justice-china-initiative-2018-2019|url-status=live}}</ref> DOJ ended the program on 23 February 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Gerstein|first=Josh|title=DOJ shuts down China-focused anti-espionage program|url=https://www.politico.com/news/2022/02/23/doj-shuts-down-china-focused-anti-espionage-program-00011065|access-date=8 April 2023|website=Politico|date=23 February 2022 |archive-date=15 June 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220615063648/https://www.politico.com/news/2022/02/23/doj-shuts-down-china-focused-anti-espionage-program-00011065|url-status=live}}</ref> No one was charged or convicted of spying in any China Initiative case.<ref>{{Cite news|date=14 December 2021|title=China Initiative Set Out to Catch Spies. It Didn't Find Many |work=Bloomberg |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2021-12-14/doj-china-initiative-to-catch-spies-prompts-fbi-misconduct-racism-claims|access-date=8 April 2023|archive-date=14 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214134354/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2021-12-14/doj-china-initiative-to-catch-spies-prompts-fbi-misconduct-racism-claims|url-status=live}}</ref>
The [[Director of Policy Planning]] at the [[United States Department of State]], [[Kiron Skinner]] drew international attention in April 2019 for stating at a foreign policy forum that the U.S. competition with China would be especially bitter, because unlike the Cold War with the [[Soviet Union]] which is "a fight within the Western family", "it's the first time that we will have a great-power competitor that is not Caucasian".<ref>{{Cite news|website=Foreign Policy|url=https://foreignpolicy.com/2019/05/02/the-slip-that-revealed-the-real-trump-doctrine/|title=The Slip That Revealed the Real Trump Doctrine|date=2 May 2019|first=Paul|last=Musgrave|access-date=1 July 2021|archive-date=7 August 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190807120404/https://foreignpolicy.com/2019/05/02/the-slip-that-revealed-the-real-trump-doctrine/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|newspaper=Washington Post|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2019/05/04/because-china-isnt-caucasian-us-is-planning-clash-civilizations-that-could-be-dangerous/|title=Because China isn't 'Caucasian,' the U.S. is planning for a 'clash of civilizations.' That could be dangerous.|date=4 May 2019|first=Steven|last=Ward|access-date=1 July 2021|archive-date=25 May 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210525092227/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2019/05/04/because-china-isnt-caucasian-us-is-planning-clash-civilizations-that-could-be-dangerous/|url-status=live}}</ref>
In 2019, prominent Americans, including some with ties to the administration, formed the [[Committee on the Present Danger|Committee on the Present Danger: China (CPDC)]] to advocate for a more hawkish foreign policy against China.<ref name="Swanson">{{Cite news|last=Swanson|first=Ana|date=20 July 2019|title=A New Red Scare Is Reshaping Washington|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/07/20/us/politics/china-red-scare-washington.html|access-date=21 July 2019|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|archive-date=20 July 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190720234508/https://www.nytimes.com/2019/07/20/us/politics/china-red-scare-washington.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Rogin|first1=Josh|date=10 April 2019|title=China hawks call on America to fight a new Cold War|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2019/04/10/china-hawks-call-america-fight-new-cold-war/|access-date=12 July 2020|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|archive-date=17 June 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200617214429/https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2019/04/10/china-hawks-call-america-fight-new-cold-war/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Wu|first1=Wendy|date=26 March 2019|title=Cold War is back: Bannon helps revive U.S. committee to target 'aggressive totalitarian foe' China|url=https://www.politico.com/story/2019/03/26/steve-bannon-china-1238039|access-date=12 July 2020|newspaper=[[Politico]]|archive-date=12 July 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200712185050/https://www.politico.com/story/2019/03/26/steve-bannon-china-1238039|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 29 January 2020, the Interior Department's fleet of more than 800 Chinese-made drones, including those by DJI, were grounded, citing security concerns.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Interior Department Grounds Chinese-Made Drones, Months After It Approved Them|url=https://www.npr.org/2020/01/29/800890201/interior-department-grounds-all-of-its-drones-citing-cybersecurity-other-concern|access-date=17 November 2020|newspaper=NPR|date=29 January 2020 |last1=Chappell|first1=Bill|archive-date=28 October 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028110830/https://www.npr.org/2020/01/29/800890201/interior-department-grounds-all-of-its-drones-citing-cybersecurity-other-concern|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last1=Friedman|first1=Lisa|last2=McCabe|first2=David|date=29 January 2020|title=Interior Dept. Grounds Its Drones Over Chinese Spying Fears |work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/01/29/technology/interior-chinese-drones.html|access-date=17 November 2020|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=29 January 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200129191125/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/01/29/technology/interior-chinese-drones.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 18 February 2020, the U.S. government announced five Chinese [[state media]] firms<ref group="note">[[Xinhua News Agency]], [[China Global Television Network]], distribution company of ''[[China Daily]]'' newspaper, and the distribution company of ''[[The People's Daily]]''</ref> would be designated "foreign missions", requiring them to be legally registered with the U.S. government as a foreign government entity.<ref>{{cite news|last=Naranjo|first=Jesse|title=U.S. to treat 5 Chinese media firms as 'foreign missions'|work=[[Politico]]|date=18 February 2020|url=https://www.politico.com/news/2020/02/18/us-chinese-media-foreign-missions-115882|access-date=20 February 2020|archive-date=5 July 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200705195122/https://www.politico.com/news/2020/02/18/us-chinese-media-foreign-missions-115882|url-status=live}}</ref> On the following day, China took action against three American journalists with ''[[The Wall Street Journal]]'' by revoking their press credentials over a [[COVID-19 pandemic|coronavirus]] opinion column which their paper had run.<ref name="usatodayhjelmgaard" /> According to China, the column was [[Racism|racist]] and libelous; the CEO of the company that published the WSJ defended the article, as did the State department.<ref name="usatodayhjelmgaard">{{cite news|last=Hjelmgaard|first=Kim|title=China expels Wall Street Journal reporters over 'racist' headline on coronavirus op-ed|work=[[USA Today]]|date=19 February 2020|url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/china-expels-wall-street-journal-095750363.html|access-date=20 February 2020|archive-date=20 February 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200220004033/https://www.yahoo.com/news/china-expels-wall-street-journal-095750363.html|url-status=live}}</ref> A March 2020 article by Reuters said that Washington slashed the number of journalists allowed to work at U.S. offices of major Chinese media outlets to 100 from 160 due to Beijing's "long-standing intimidation and harassment of journalists". In response, China expelled about a dozen American correspondents with ''The New York Times'', News Corp's Wall Street Journal and the Washington Post, which prompted criticism from the State Department.<ref>{{cite news|title=China to restrict US journalists from three major newspapers|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-51938035|agency=BBC|date=18 March 2020|access-date=11 August 2020|archive-date=24 November 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211124050623/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-51938035|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="reutersfour">{{cite news|title=U.S. designates four major Chinese media outlets as foreign missions|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-media-restrictions/us-designates-four-major-chinese-media-outlets-as-foreign-missions-idUSKBN23T2Y8|work=Reuters|date=22 June 2020|access-date=11 August 2020|archive-date=17 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200817173749/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-media-restrictions/us-designates-four-major-chinese-media-outlets-as-foreign-missions-idUSKBN23T2Y8|url-status=live}}</ref> On 8 May, the U.S. moved Chinese citizens at non-American news outlets from open-ended work visas to extendable 90-day work visas<ref>{{cite news|last1=Wang|first1=Vivian|last2=Wong|first2=Edward|title=U.S. Hits Back at China With New Visa Restrictions on Journalists|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/05/09/us/politics/china-journalists-us-visa-crackdown.html|access-date=10 May 2020|work=The New York Times|date=9 May 2020|archive-date=10 May 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200510002702/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/05/09/us/politics/china-journalists-us-visa-crackdown.html|url-status=live}}</ref> and in June the State Department designated a further four Chinese media outlets as foreign embassies.<ref name="reutersfour" /> [[File:Deputy Secretary Blinken Meets With Chinese Executive Vice Foreign Minister Zhang Yesui (16313876818).jpg|thumb|In August 2020, [[Joe Biden]]'s foreign policy adviser [[Tony Blinken]] described China as a "strategic competitor".<ref>{{cite news|title=Former Obama Advisor on Iran: 'We're in a Much Worse Place' |url=https://www.pbs.org/wnet/amanpour-and-company/video/former-obama-advisor-on-iran-were-in-a-much-worse-place/|work=[[PBS]]|date=1 August 2020|archive-date=9 November 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201109040120/https://www.pbs.org/wnet/amanpour-and-company/video/former-obama-advisor-on-iran-were-in-a-much-worse-place/|url-status=live}}</ref>]]
By May 2020 relations had deteriorated as both sides were accusing the other of guilt for the [[COVID-19 pandemic|worldwide coronavirus pandemic]]. Washington has mobilized a campaign of investigations, prosecutions and export restrictions. Beijing, meanwhile, has stepped up military activities in the contested South China Sea, and launched denunciations of American Secretary of State Mike Pompeo, and publicly speculating that the American military deliberately unleashed the virus in China. In the growing aspersion, on 15 May 2020, the U.S. blocked shipments of [[Semiconductor|semi-conductors]] to Huawei, while China, for its part, has threatened to place [[Apple Inc.|Apple]], [[Boeing]], and other U.S. firms on "unreliable entities" lists,<ref>{{cite news|last1=Vallejo|first1=Justin|title=US cuts off semiconductor shipments to Huawei, China vows to retaliate|work=[[The Independent]]|date=15 May 2020|url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/us-cuts-off-semiconductor-shipments-205138512.html|access-date=17 May 2020|archive-date=21 June 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200621102135/https://www.yahoo.com/news/us-cuts-off-semiconductor-shipments-205138512.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Evans|first1=Zachary|title=China Threatens to Place Apple, Boeing, and Other U.S. Firms on 'Unreliable Entities' List|work=[[National Review]]|date=15 May 2020|url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/china-threatens-place-apple-boeing-171146613.html|access-date=17 May 2020|archive-date=17 May 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200517001255/https://www.yahoo.com/news/china-threatens-place-apple-boeing-171146613.html|url-status=live}}</ref> and has blamed the U.S. government of using state power under the excuse of national security, and of abusing export control measures to continuously oppress and contain specific enterprises of other countries.<ref>{{cite news|title=China warns US of 'all necessary measures' over Huawei rules|agency=[[Associated Press]]|date=17 May 2020|url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/china-warns-us-necessary-measures-104613919.html|access-date=17 May 2020|archive-date=19 June 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200619181209/https://www.yahoo.com/news/china-warns-us-necessary-measures-104613919.html|url-status=live}}</ref> [[Orville Schell]], the director of the Center on U.S.-China Relations at the [[Asia Society]], summed up the situation as follows: "The consequences of the breakdown in U.S.-China relations is going to be very grave for the world and for the global economy because the ability of the U.S. and China to work together was the keystone of the whole arch of [[globalization]] and global trade. With that pulled out, there's going to be a tremendous amount of disturbance", often compared to the [[Cold War]]. However [[Tony Blair]] noted there is "an interconnectedness, economically and in trade terms between the U.S. and China that just wasn't there in the US-Soviet Cold War" that makes it an imperfect analogy. He further felt the China-U.S. relations would be the "determining geopolitical relationship of the 21st century."<ref>{{cite news|last1=Lamarque|first1=Kevin|last2=Haltiwanger|first2=John|title=The U.S. and China are on the brink of a new Cold War that could devastate the global economy|publisher=[[Reuters]] via [[Business Insider]]|date=13 May 2020|url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/us-china-brink-cold-war-163108094.html|access-date=14 May 2020|archive-date=19 June 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200619211233/https://www.yahoo.com/news/us-china-brink-cold-war-163108094.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
In June 2020, [[US Ambassador to the United Nations]] [[Kelly Craft]] sent a letter to the U.N. secretary general explaining the U.S. position on China's "excessive maritime claims".<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/07/13/world/asia/south-china-sea-pompeo.html|title=U.S. Says Most of China's Claims in South China Sea Are Illegal|first1=Edward|last1=Wong|first2=Michael|last2=Crowley|date=13 July 2020|work=The New York Times|access-date=25 June 2021|archive-date=17 June 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210617002433/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/07/13/world/asia/south-china-sea-pompeo.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 17 June 2020, President Trump signed the [[Uyghur Human Rights Policy Act]],<ref>{{cite news|date=30 June 2020|title=Trump signed a law to punish China for its oppression of the Uighur Muslims. Uighurs say much more needs to be done|work=[[Business Insider]]|url=https://www.businessinsider.com/uighur-china-detention-centers-activists-urge-accountability-2020-2|access-date=5 July 2020|archive-date=14 December 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201214213003/https://www.businessinsider.com/uighur-china-detention-centers-activists-urge-accountability-2020-2|url-status=live}}</ref> which authorizes the imposition of [[United States sanctions|U.S. sanctions]] against Chinese government officials responsible for [[Xinjiang re-education camps|detention camps]] holding more than 1 million members of the country's [[Uyghurs|Uyghur]] [[Islam in China|Muslim]] minority.<ref>{{cite news|title=U.S. Congress urges Trump administration to get tougher on China's Xinjiang crackdown|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-xinjiang-congress/u-s-congress-urges-trump-administration-to-get-tougher-on-chinas-xinjiang-crackdown-idUSKBN24334S|work=Reuters|date=2 July 2020|access-date=5 July 2020|archive-date=3 November 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201103122838/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-xinjiang-congress/u-s-congress-urges-trump-administration-to-get-tougher-on-chinas-xinjiang-crackdown-idUSKBN24334S|url-status=live}}</ref> On 9 July 2020, the Trump administration imposed sanctions and visa restrictions against senior Chinese officials, including [[Chen Quanguo]], a member of CCP's powerful Politburo.<ref>{{Cite news| url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-53355697| title=US sanctions Chinese officials over Xinjiang 'violations'| work=www.bbc.com| date=9 July 2020| access-date=10 July 2020| archive-date=10 July 2020| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200710005130/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-53355697| url-status=live}}</ref>
A research paper by the [[Begin–Sadat Center for Strategic Studies]] said that Chinese state-controlled media enthusiastically covered the protests and rioting attending the [[Murder of George Floyd]], comparing the [[George Floyd protests|American protests]] to the [[2019–2020 Hong Kong protests|protests in Hong Kong]] and used the [[Violence and controversies during the George Floyd protests|rioting]] and [[Violence and controversies during the George Floyd protests|violence in the United States]] as evidence that the democratic system was hypocritical and morally bankrupt.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Yellinek|first=Roie|date=14 July 2020|title=The Chinese Media's Take on US Unrest|url=https://besacenter.org/perspectives-papers/us-unrest-china-media/|access-date=21 July 2020|website=[[Begin-Sadat Center for Strategic Studies]] |archive-date=15 July 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200715062155/https://besacenter.org/perspectives-papers/us-unrest-china-media/|url-status=live}}</ref> A report by the Australian Strategic Policy Institute said that racial tensions in the United States was a key area of focus for "a campaign of cross-platform inauthentic activity, conducted by Chinese-speaking actors and broadly in alignment with the political goal of the People's Republic of China (PRC) to denigrate the standing of the US."<ref>{{cite web|title=Covid-19 Disinformation & Social Media Manipulation|url=https://www.aspi.org.au/report/covid-19-disinformation|website=ASPI|access-date=4 August 2020|archive-date=27 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200827182923/https://www.aspi.org.au/report/covid-19-disinformation|url-status=live}}</ref>
In July 2020, the Trump administration [[Chinese Consulate-General, Houston#2020 closure|ordered the closure of the Chinese consulate in Houston]]. In response, the Chinese government ordered the closure of the [[Consulate General of the United States, Chengdu|US consulate in Chengdu]].
On 20 July 2020, the United States sanctioned 11 Chinese companies, restricting any trade deal with America for what the U.S. government said was their involvement in human rights violations in Xinjiang, accusing them specifically of using Uyghurs and other Muslim minorities in forced labor.<ref>{{Cite news|title=US sanctions 11 Chinese companies over human rights abuses in Xinjiang|url=https://edition.cnn.com/2020/07/21/business/us-sanctions-china-companies-xinjiang-intl-hnk/index.html|access-date=21 July 2020|website=[[CNN International]]|date=21 July 2020|archive-date=21 July 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200721112056/https://edition.cnn.com/2020/07/21/business/us-sanctions-china-companies-xinjiang-intl-hnk/index.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 23 July 2020, U.S. Secretary of State Mike Pompeo announced the end of what he called "blind engagement" with the Chinese government. He also criticized CCP general secretary Xi Jinping as "a true believer in a bankrupt totalitarian ideology".<ref>{{Cite news|last=Re|first=Gregg|title=Pompeo announces end of 'blind engagement' with communist China: 'Distrust but verify'|url=https://www.foxnews.com/politics/pompeo-announces-end-blind-engagement-communist-china|date=23 July 2020|access-date=23 July 2020|website=Fox News |archive-date=23 July 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200723230318/https://www.foxnews.com/politics/pompeo-announces-end-blind-engagement-communist-china|url-status=live}}</ref>
In August 2020, Washington imposed sanctions on 11 Hong Kong and Chinese officials over what it said was their role in curtailing political freedoms in Hong Kong through the imposition<ref name=ftretaliate>{{cite news|title=China imposes sanctions on US officials in retaliation for Hong Kong measures|url=https://www.ft.com/content/c2d78da3-1fcf-4678-b65d-e1d02aa48ae6|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221210/https://www.ft.com/content/c2d78da3-1fcf-4678-b65d-e1d02aa48ae6|archive-date=10 December 2022|url-access=subscription|url-status=live |work=Financial Times |date=10 August 2020}}</ref> of the [[2020 Hong Kong national security law|Hong Kong national security law]]; China retaliated<ref name=ftretaliate/> by sanctioning 6 Republican lawmakers and 5 individuals at non-profit and rights groups.<ref>{{cite news|title=U.S. imposes sanctions on Hong Kong's Lam, other officials over crackdown|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-hong-kong-sanctions/us-imposes-sanctions-on-hong-kongs-lam-other-officials-over-crackdown-idUSKCN253215|work=Reuters|date=7 August 2020|access-date=11 August 2020|archive-date=10 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200810002326/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-hong-kong-sanctions/us-imposes-sanctions-on-hong-kongs-lam-other-officials-over-crackdown-idUSKCN253215|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=China imposes sanctions on Republican U.S. lawmakers over Hong Kong|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-hongkong-sanctions/china-imposes-sanctions-on-republican-u-s-lawmakers-over-hong-kong-idUSKCN2560RN|work=Reuters|date=10 August 2020|access-date=11 August 2020|archive-date=11 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200811092920/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-hongkong-sanctions/china-imposes-sanctions-on-republican-u-s-lawmakers-over-hong-kong-idUSKCN2560RN|url-status=live}}</ref>
In September 2020 the United States had under a 29 May presidential proclamation revoked more than 1,000 visas for PRC students and researchers visas who the U.S. government said had ties to the Chinese military in order to prevent them from stealing and otherwise appropriating sensitive research.<ref>{{cite news|title=US revokes visas for 1,000 Chinese under Trump order|publisher=AFP|date=10 September 2020|url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/us-revokes-visas-1-000-230810765.html|access-date=10 September 2020|archive-date=11 January 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210111031859/https://www.yahoo.com/news/us-revokes-visas-1-000-230810765.html|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Pamuk|first=Humeyra |title=U.S. says revoked more than 1,000 visas of Chinese nationals over military links|publisher=Reuters (via Yahoo finance)|date=9 September 2020|url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/u-says-revoked-more-1-204050377.html|access-date=10 September 2020}}</ref>
On 26 September 2020, the U.S. Commerce Department put restrictions on Chinese chip maker, [[Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation]] (SMIC), following which the suppliers were required to have an export license for exporting the chip. The restrictions were imposed after the U.S. concluded that an "unacceptable risk" equipment supplied to SMIC could potentially be used for military purposes.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.theverge.com/2020/9/26/21457350/us-tightens-trade-restrictions-china-chipmaker-smic|title=US tightens trade restrictions on Chinese chipmaker SMIC|access-date=26 September 2020|website=The Verge|date=26 September 2020|archive-date=10 November 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211110004329/https://www.theverge.com/2020/9/26/21457350/us-tightens-trade-restrictions-china-chipmaker-smic|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-smic/u-s-imposes-curbs-on-exports-to-chinas-top-chipmaker-smic-idUSKBN26H0LN|title=U.S. tightens exports to China's chipmaker SMIC, citing risk of military use|access-date=26 September 2020|website=Reuters|date=26 September 2020|archive-date=26 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200926160337/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-smic/u-s-imposes-curbs-on-exports-to-chinas-top-chipmaker-smic-idUSKBN26H0LN|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 6 October 2020, Germany's ambassador to the UN, on behalf of the group of 39 countries including Germany, the U.K. and the U.S., made a statement to denounce China for its treatment of ethnic minorities and for curtailing freedoms in Hong Kong.<ref name="Wainer-2020">{{cite news|last1= Wainer|first1= David| title= Western Allies Rebuke China at UN Over Xinjiang, Hong Kong|url= https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2020-10-06/u-s-allies-step-up-china-rebuke-at-un-over-xinjiang-hong-kong|access-date=6 October 2020|newspaper=Bloomberg |date= 6 October 2020|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20201007050829/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2020-10-06/u-s-allies-step-up-china-rebuke-at-un-over-xinjiang-hong-kong| archive-date=7 October 2020}}</ref> [[File:Uyghur boycott 20Sept2020.jpg|thumb|Call for boycott of products from China's [[Xinjiang]] province, New York, 2020. The U.S. officially recognized the Chinese government's treatment of the [[Uyghurs]] in Xinjiang [[Persecution of Uyghurs in China|as a genocide]].]] On 9 October 2020, the Department of Justice disallowed the use of its fund to purchase DJI drones, which the DoJ classified as a "Covered Foreign Entity".<ref>{{Cite web|last=McNabb|first=Miriam|date=9 October 2020|title=No Department of Justice Funds for DJI Drones: DOJ Makes the Ban Official, but Some Federal Agencies May Suffer|url=https://dronelife.com/2020/10/09/no-federal-funds-for-dji-drones-doj-makes-the-ban-official-but-some-federal-agencies-may-suffer/|access-date=17 November 2020|website=DRONELIFE |archive-date=17 November 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201117152204/https://dronelife.com/2020/10/09/no-federal-funds-for-dji-drones-doj-makes-the-ban-official-but-some-federal-agencies-may-suffer/|url-status=live}}</ref>
