On 21 October 2020, the U.S. approved [[arms sales]] of $1.8 billion to Taiwan.<ref name="focustaiwan.tw">{{cite news|title=Taiwan welcomes latest U.S. arms sales|work=[[Focus Taiwan]]|url=https://focustaiwan.tw/politics/202010220008|access-date=26 October 2020|archive-date=26 October 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201026205117/https://focustaiwan.tw/politics/202010220008|url-status=live}}</ref> It involved three packages that included high technology weapons such as SLAM-ER missiles, HIMARS M142 Launchers and Recce Pods.<ref name="focustaiwan.tw"/> On 26 October 2020, [[China]] announced its intentions to impose sanctions on U.S. businesses and individuals, including [[Boeing Defense, Space & Security|Boeing]], [[Raytheon]] and [[Lockheed Martin]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://in.reuters.com/article/usa-china-taiwan/china-to-impose-sanctions-on-u-s-firms-over-taiwan-arms-sales-idINKBN27B0P0|title=China to impose sanctions on U.S. firms over Taiwan arms sales|access-date=26 October 2020|website=Reuters|date=26 October 2020|archive-date=26 October 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201026095700/https://in.reuters.com/article/usa-china-taiwan/china-to-impose-sanctions-on-u-s-firms-over-taiwan-arms-sales-idINKBN27B0P0|url-status=dead}}</ref> Taiwan welcomed the arms sales and disapproved of the sanctions.<ref name="focustaiwan.tw"/><ref name="Taiwan 'deeply regrets' China's thr">{{cite news|title=Taiwan 'deeply regrets' China's threat against U.S. arms suppliers|work=[[Focus Taiwan]]|url=https://focustaiwan.tw/politics/202010260019|access-date=26 October 2020|archive-date=30 October 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030041124/https://focustaiwan.tw/politics/202010260019|url-status=live}}</ref> Taiwan also said it would continue buying arms from America.<ref name="Taiwan 'deeply regrets' China's thr"/>
In a December 2020 report, U.S. intelligence officials claimed that China had supervised a bounty program that paid Afghan militants to kill U.S. soldiers deployed in the country.<ref>{{cite news|date=31 December 2020|title=China offered Afghan militants bounties to attack US soldiers: reports|work=[[Deutsche Welle]]|url=https://www.dw.com/en/china-offered-afghan-militants-bounties-to-attack-us-soldiers-reports/a-56103735|access-date=31 January 2021|archive-date=26 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211026172900/https://www.dw.com/en/china-offered-afghan-militants-bounties-to-attack-us-soldiers-reports/a-56103735|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 5 December 2020, the U.S. State Department ended five cultural exchange programs with China, which are - "the Policymakers Educational China Trip Program, the U.S.-China Friendship Program, the U.S.-China Leadership Exchange Program, the U.S.-China Transpacific Exchange Program and the [[Hong Kong]] Educational and Cultural Program." They described these programs as soft power propaganda tools of Chinese government.<ref>{{cite news|url= https://www.reuters.com/article/uk-usa-china-exchange-program-idUKKBN28F05Y|title= U.S. ends exchange programs with China, calling them 'propaganda'|access-date= 5 December 2020|website= Reuters|date= 5 December 2020|last1= Satter|first1= Raphael|archive-date= 5 December 2020|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20201205053101/https://www.reuters.com/article/uk-usa-china-exchange-program-idUKKBN28F05Y|url-status= live}}</ref>
==== Espionage ==== In July 2020, FBI Director [[Christopher A. Wray|Christopher Wray]] called China the "greatest long-term threat" to the United States. He said that "the FBI is now opening a new China-related counterintelligence case every 10 hours. Of the nearly 5,000 active counterintelligence cases currently under way across the country, almost half are related to China."<ref>{{cite news |date=8 July 2020 |title=FBI director: China is 'greatest threat' to US |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-53329755 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210212013511/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-53329755 |archive-date=12 February 2021 |access-date=12 July 2020 |work=BBC News}}</ref>
In December 2020, an investigation by ''[[Axios (website)|Axios]]'' was published that detailed the suspected activities of Christine Fang, a Chinese national who has been suspected by U.S. officials of having conducted political espionage for the [[Ministry of State Security (China)|Chinese Ministry of State Security]] (MSS) while she was in the United States from 2011 to 2015.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Allen-Ebrahimian|first1=Bethany|last2=Dorfman|first2=Zach|title=Exclusive: Suspected Chinese spy targeted California politicians|url=https://www.axios.com/china-spy-california-politicians-9d2dfb99-f839-4e00-8bd8-59dec0daf589.html|work=[[Axios (website)|Axios]]|date=8 December 2020|access-date=22 December 2020|archive-date=10 December 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201210121643/https://www.axios.com/china-spy-california-politicians-9d2dfb99-f839-4e00-8bd8-59dec0daf589.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Eric Swalwell Report Fits Bill of China Spy Pattern Identified By FBI|url=https://www.newsweek.com/eric-swalwell-report-fits-bill-china-spy-pattern-fbi-1554281|agency=Newsweek|date=11 December 2020|access-date=22 December 2020|archive-date=22 December 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201222232436/https://www.newsweek.com/eric-swalwell-report-fits-bill-china-spy-pattern-fbi-1554281|url-status=live}}</ref> While Fang's suspected activities prior to the Axios investigation had already drawn scrutiny from federal law enforcement agencies, the subsequent reactions to its publication drew further scrutiny from politicians and the media.<ref>{{cite news|title=17 House GOP members send letter to Pelosi urging Swalwell's removal from Intel Committee|url=https://www.foxnews.com/politics/house-gop-letter-to-pelosi-swalwells-intel-committee|work=Fox News|date=17 December 2020|access-date=22 December 2020|archive-date=22 December 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201222023501/https://www.foxnews.com/politics/house-gop-letter-to-pelosi-swalwells-intel-committee|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Murray|first1=Paul|title=There are 'many ways' in which China 'gets what it wants'|url=https://www.skynews.com.au/details/_6215959913001|agency=Sky News Australia|date=13 December 2020|access-date=22 December 2020|archive-date=14 December 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201214023611/https://www.skynews.com.au/details/_6215959913001|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|date=18 December 2020|title=EDITORIAL: Chinese infiltration: Taiwan can help|work=[[Taipei Times]]|url=https://www.taipeitimes.com/News/editorials/archives/2020/12/18/2003748913|access-date=22 December 2020|archive-date=12 January 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210112210729/https://www.taipeitimes.com/News/editorials/archives/2020/12/18/2003748913|url-status=live}}</ref>
Xu Zewei and Zhang Yu, Chinese hackers working under the direction of China's Ministry of State Security (MSS), conducted cyber intrusions between February 2020 and June 2021, including the large-scale "[[Hafnium (group)|HAFNIUM]]" campaign exploiting Microsoft Exchange Server vulnerabilities. Targeting U.S. universities, virologists, and law firms, they stole COVID-19 research and sensitive communications while operating through the MSS-linked company Shanghai Powerock Network Co. Ltd. In July 2025, under the Second Trump administration, a federal indictment was unsealed in the Southern District of Texas charging Xu and Zhang with conspiracy, wire fraud, unauthorized computer access, and identity theft. Xu was arrested in Italy and awaits extradition; Zhang remained at large.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-07-08 |title=Office of Public Affairs {{!}} Justice Department Announces Arrest of Prolific Chinese State-Sponsored Contract Hacker |url=https://www.justice.gov/opa/pr/justice-department-announces-arrest-prolific-chinese-state-sponsored-contract-hacker |access-date=2025-07-14 |website=United States Department of Justice}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-07-09 |title=Chinese state-sponsored contract hacker arrested in Italy at U.S. request, DOJ says |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/world/china/chinese-state-sponsored-contract-hacker-arrested-italy-us-request-doj-rcna217675 |access-date=2025-07-14 |website=NBC News}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last1=Singh |first1=Kanishka |last2=Ward |first2=Jasper |last3=Singh |first3=Kanishka |last4=Ward |first4=Jasper |date=2025-07-10 |title=Chinese state-sponsored contract hacker arrested in Italy at US request, DOJ says |url=https://www.reuters.com/legal/litigation/us-says-chinese-state-sponsored-contract-hacker-arrested-last-week-italy-us-2025-07-08/ |access-date=2025-07-14 |work=Reuters}}</ref>
===Biden administration (2021–2025)=== {{See also|Foreign policy of the Biden administration#China}}
Following his election, relations with the [[Presidency of Joe Biden|new Biden administration]] in 2021 included heightened tensions over trade, technology, and human rights, particularly regarding Hong Kong, and the treatment of minorities in China. In addition international tensions regarding control of the South China Sea remained high. The Biden administration largely continued the China policies of his predecessor Donald Trump,<ref name="Roach-2022"/>{{Rp|page=148}} and endorsed [[Mike Pompeo]]'s [[#Uyghurs|report]] about China committing genocide against [[Uyghurs]].<ref name="Bernstein-2021">{{Cite news|last=Bernstein|first=Brittany|date=20 January 2021|title=Incoming Secretary of State Backs Pompeo's Uyghur Genocide Designation|work=[[National Review]]|url=https://www.nationalreview.com/news/incoming-secretary-of-state-backs-pompeos-uyghur-genocide-designation/|access-date=31 January 2021|archive-date=30 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211030013954/https://www.nationalreview.com/news/incoming-secretary-of-state-backs-pompeos-uyghur-genocide-designation/|url-status=live}}</ref> However, the Biden and Xi administrations agreed to collaborate on long-term projects regarding climate change, nuclear proliferation, and the global COVID-19 pandemic.<ref>Sha Hua and Andrew Jeang, "U.S. and China Discuss Enhancing Beijing's Climate Commitments Climate envoy John Kerry says meetings with counterparts in Shanghai were productive" [https://www.wsj.com/articles/u-s-and-china-discuss-enhancing-beijings-climate-commitments-11618726162?page=1 ''Wall Street Journal'', April, 19, 2021] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210422105848/https://www.wsj.com/articles/u-s-and-china-discuss-enhancing-beijings-climate-commitments-11618726162?page=1|date=22 April 2021}}</ref>
President Biden, in his first foreign policy address, labeled China as "the most serious competitor" to the US.<ref>{{cite news|last=Churchill|first=Owen|title=In first foreign policy address, President Biden calls China the 'most serious competitor' to the US |publisher=South China Morning Post (via Yahoo! Finance)|date=5 February 2021|url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/first-foreign-policy-address-president-093000266.html|access-date=7 February 2021|archive-date=6 February 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210206165418/http://finance.yahoo.com/news/first-foreign-policy-address-president-093000266.html|url-status=live}}</ref> During his first visit to [[the Pentagon]] on 9 February 2021, Biden urged the [[United States Department of Defense]] to review its national security policy concerning China.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://apnews.com/article/joe-biden-biden-cabinet-lloyd-austin-coronavirus-pandemic-43210adc097451c97a400cfe6d35be15|title=Biden calls for China review during first Pentagon visit|first1=Robert|last1=Burns|first2=Lolita C.|last2=Baldor|first3=Aamer|last3=Madhani|publisher=Associated Press|date=10 February 2021|access-date=21 September 2021|archive-date=17 September 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210917174738/https://apnews.com/article/joe-biden-biden-cabinet-lloyd-austin-coronavirus-pandemic-43210adc097451c97a400cfe6d35be15|url-status=live}}</ref>
In 2021, the Biden administration tightened technology restrictions on China, including designating major firms as security threats,<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/us/five-chinese-companies-pose-threat-us-national-security-fcc-2021-03-12/|title=Five Chinese companies pose threat to U.S. national security -FCC|newspaper=Reuters|date=12 March 2021|via=www.reuters.com|last1=Shepardson|first1=David}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=U.S. FCC adds Russia's Kaspersky, China telecom firms to national security threat list|url=https://www.reuters.com/business/media-telecom/us-fcc-adds-ao-kaspersky-lab-china-telecom-firms-national-security-threat-list-2022-03-25|work=Reuters|access-date=28 July 2022|archive-date=27 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220727070822/https://www.reuters.com/business/media-telecom/us-fcc-adds-ao-kaspersky-lab-china-telecom-firms-national-security-threat-list-2022-03-25/|url-status=live}}</ref> adding [[supercomputing]] entities to the U.S. Entity List,<ref>{{cite news|title=US blacklists seven Chinese supercomputer groups|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/business-56685136|agency=BBC|access-date=28 July 2022|archive-date=28 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220728160359/https://www.bbc.com/news/business-56685136|url-status=live}}</ref> expanding limits on American investment in Chinese companies tied to the military,<ref>{{Cite web|date=3 June 2021|title=Executive Order on Addressing the Threat from Securities Investments that Finance Certain Companies of the People's Republic of China|url=https://bidenwhitehouse.archives.gov/briefing-room/presidential-actions/2021/06/03/executive-order-on-addressing-the-threat-from-securities-investments-that-finance-certain-companies-of-the-peoples-republic-of-china/|access-date=5 June 2021|website=The White House |archive-date=7 June 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210607102928/https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/presidential-actions/2021/06/03/executive-order-on-addressing-the-threat-from-securities-investments-that-finance-certain-companies-of-the-peoples-republic-of-china/|url-status=live}}</ref> and finalizing rules allowing the delisting of non-compliant Chinese firms from U.S. stock exchanges.<ref>{{cite news|title=U.S. SEC mandates Chinese companies detail ownership structure, audits|url=https://www.reuters.com/business/us-sec-mandates-foreign-companies-spell-out-ownership-structure-disclose-2021-12-02|work=Reuters|access-date=28 July 2022|archive-date=28 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220728185400/https://www.reuters.com/business/us-sec-mandates-foreign-companies-spell-out-ownership-structure-disclose-2021-12-02/|url-status=live}}</ref>
From 18 to 19 March 2021, [[United States–China talks in Alaska|bilateral talks in Alaska]] took place. Blinken and [[national security advisor]] [[Jake Sullivan]] met with [[Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party|CCP Politburo]] member [[Yang Jiechi]] and Chinese foreign minister [[Wang Yi (politician)|Wang Yi]]. The Americans unleashed heated attacks on China's policies regarding human rights, cyberattacks, Taiwan, and its crackdown in Xinjiang and Hong Kong. The Chinese side countered by attacking the U.S's standing in the world and defending China's sovereign rights and model of development.<ref name="wsj._Bitter_Alaska">{{Cite news|title=Bitter Alaska Meeting Complicates Already Shaky U.S.-China Ties|last=Mauldin|first=William|newspaper=[[The Wall Street Journal]]|date=19 March 2021|access-date=20 March 2021|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/bitter-alaska-meeting-complicates-already-shaky-u-s-china-ties-11616185669|archive-date=4 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211004005658/https://www.wsj.com/articles/bitter-alaska-meeting-complicates-already-shaky-u-s-china-ties-11616185669|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="BBC News-2021">{{Cite news|date=19 March 2021|title=US and China trade angry words at high-level Alaska talks |work=BBC News|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-56452471|access-date=20 March 2021|archive-date=11 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211011120555/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-56452471|url-status=live}}</ref> In the week ahead of the talks, the administration met with U.S. allies in Asia and imposed sanctions on senior Chinese officials amidst Beijing's [[2020 Hong Kong national security law|contemporaneous crackdown on Hong Kong]].<ref name="wsj._Bitter_Alaska"/>
On 22 March 2021, in conjunction with the European Union, United Kingdom and Canada, the United States imposed sanctions against Chinese officials in relation to the human rights violations in Xinjiang. The sanctions marked the first time the Biden administration took such coordinated action against Beijing.<ref>{{cite news|title=West sanctions China over Xinjiang abuses, Beijing hits back at EU|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/west-sanctions-china-over-xinjiang-abuses-beijing-hits-back-eu-2021-03-22|work=Reuters|access-date=28 July 2022|archive-date=28 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220728160345/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/west-sanctions-china-over-xinjiang-abuses-beijing-hits-back-eu-2021-03-22/|url-status=live}}</ref>
In June 2021, leaders of the [[G7]] and [[NATO]], encouraged by the United States, issued rare unanimous statements condemning China over human rights abuses, threats to Taiwan, unfair trade practices, and lack of transparency on COVID-19.<ref name="Lau-2021">{{Cite news |date=13 June 2021 |title=US and Europe converge on historic rebuke of China |first=Stuart |last=Lau |url=https://www.politico.eu/article/us-europe-rebuke-china-economic-practices-g7-human-rights-xinjiang-coronavirus/|access-date=29 March 2022 |website=[[Politico]] |archive-date=7 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211007180917/https://www.politico.eu/article/us-europe-rebuke-china-economic-practices-g7-human-rights-xinjiang-coronavirus/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>James T. Areddy, "Back-to-Back Rebukes of China Mark a Turning Point: Criticism from G-7 and NATO members represent a shift toward collective action to confront Beijing" [https://www.wsj.com/articles/back-to-back-rebukes-of-china-mark-a-turning-point-11623710320 ''Wall Street Journal'' June 15, 2021] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921161510/https://www.wsj.com/articles/back-to-back-rebukes-of-china-mark-a-turning-point-11623710320|date=21 September 2021}}</ref><ref>Sabine Siebold, Steve Holland and Robin Emmott, "NATO adopts tough line on China at Biden's debut summit with alliance" [https://www.reuters.com/world/europe/nato-welcomes-biden-pivotal-post-trump-summit-2021-06-14/ ''Reuters'' June 14, 2021] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211115132233/https://www.reuters.com/world/europe/nato-welcomes-biden-pivotal-post-trump-summit-2021-06-14/|date=15 November 2021}}</ref><ref>Karla Adam et al., "G-7 takes stronger stand against China, at U.S. urging" [https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/europe/queen-elizabeth-biden-g7/2021/06/13/078574de-c7cf-11eb-8708-64991f2acf28_story.html ''The Washington Post'' June 13, 2021] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211003104122/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/europe/queen-elizabeth-biden-g7/2021/06/13/078574de-c7cf-11eb-8708-64991f2acf28_story.html|date=3 October 2021}}.</ref>
In August 2021, China tested a nuclear-capable [[Hypersonic flight|hypersonic missile]],<ref>{{cite news|title=Biden airs hypersonic missile fears as probable ambassador labels China 'untrustworthy'|url=https://www.dw.com/en/biden-airs-hypersonic-missile-fears-as-probable-ambassador-labels-china-untrustworthy/a-59568428|work=Deutsche Welle|date=20 October 2021|access-date=24 October 2021|archive-date=28 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211028041828/https://www.dw.com/en/biden-airs-hypersonic-missile-fears-as-probable-ambassador-labels-china-untrustworthy/a-59568428|url-status=live}}</ref> highlighting advances in its strategic weapons program and raising concerns in Washington.<ref>{{cite news|title=China successfully tested hypersonic weapon in August: report|url=https://www.space.com/china-hypersonic-weapon-test-august|work=[[Space.com]]|date=17 October 2021|access-date=24 October 2021|archive-date=24 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211024180017/https://www.space.com/china-hypersonic-weapon-test-august|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 18 August 2021, Biden compared U.S. commitments in Afghanistan and Taiwan, saying that the United States was bound to defend Taiwan, though officials later clarified that policy had not changed.<ref>{{cite news|title=Did Biden's Taiwan Remarks Represent a U.S. Policy Change?|url=https://thediplomat.com/2021/08/did-bidens-taiwan-remarks-represent-a-us-policy-change|work=The Diplomat|access-date=28 July 2022|archive-date=28 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220728182636/https://thediplomat.com/2021/08/did-bidens-taiwan-remarks-represent-a-us-policy-change|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=U.S. position on Taiwan unchanged despite Biden comment - official|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/us-position-taiwan-unchanged-despite-biden-comment-official-2021-08-19|work=Reuters|access-date=28 July 2022|archive-date=29 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220729134227/https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/us-position-taiwan-unchanged-despite-biden-comment-official-2021-08-19/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Comments by Biden and others on U.S. policy of 'ambiguity' on Taiwan|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/comments-by-biden-others-us-policy-ambiguity-taiwan-2022-05-23|work=Reuters|access-date=28 July 2022|archive-date=28 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220728182638/https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/comments-by-biden-others-us-policy-ambiguity-taiwan-2022-05-23/|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 15 September 2021, the United States, the United Kingdom, and Australia formed [[AUKUS]], a security pact under which Australia will acquire nuclear-powered submarines with U.S. and British support, a move China denounced as "extremely irresponsible".<ref>See "Aukus: UK, US and Australia launch pact to counter China" (BBC, 16 September 2021) [https://www.bbc.com/news/world-58564837 online] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211210231939/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-58564837|date=10 December 2021}}</ref><ref>See also "AUKUS: The Trilateral Security Partnership Between Australia, U.K. and U.S." (U.S. Dept of Defense, 2023) [https://www.defense.gov/Spotlights/AUKUS/#:~:text=In%20September%202021%2C%20leaders%20of,building%20on%20longstanding%20and%20ongoing online] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512033323/https://www.defense.gov/Spotlights/AUKUS/#:~:text=In%20September%202021%2C%20leaders%20of,building%20on%20longstanding%20and%20ongoing|date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
Biden held his first virtual meeting with Xi on 15 November 2021.<ref>{{Cite news|url = https://apnews.com/article/biden-xi-meeting-e6013378e03727b85a8f59f71fe61f4a|title = Biden, Xi try to tamp down tension in long virtual meeting|website = [[Associated Press]]|date = 15 November 2021|access-date = 16 November 2021|archive-date = 16 November 2021|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20211116010201/https://apnews.com/article/biden-xi-meeting-e6013378e03727b85a8f59f71fe61f4a|url-status = live}}</ref>
On 24 November 2021, the Biden administration invited Taiwan to attend the '[[Summit for Democracy]]' - to be held in December 2021. [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the People's Republic of China|China's Foreign Ministry]] reacted by saying it was "firmly opposed" to the invitation.<ref>{{Cite news|date=24 November 2021|title=U.S. invites Taiwan to its democracy summit; China angered |work=Reuters|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-biden-democracy-taiwan-idUSKBN2I9062|access-date=24 November 2021}}</ref> Later that year, Biden signed a [[National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2022|defense bill]] strengthening support for Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Smith|first=Adam|date=2 December 2021|title=Text - H.R.4350 - 117th Congress (2021-2022): National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2022|url=https://www.congress.gov/bill/117th-congress/house-bill/4350/text|access-date=17 December 2021|website=Congress.gov|archive-date=30 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211230191919/https://www.congress.gov/bill/117th-congress/house-bill/4350/text|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=US Congress supports Taiwan in defense spending bill|url=https://en.rti.org.tw/news/view/id/2006672|access-date=17 December 2021|website=RTI Radio Taiwan International|language=zh-Hant-TW}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Singh|first=Kanishka|date=27 December 2021|title=U.S. President Biden signs $770 billion defense bill |work=Reuters|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/us/us-president-biden-signs-770-billion-defense-bill-2021-12-27/|access-date=30 December 2021}}</ref>
In late 2021, the Biden administration announced a diplomatic [[Concerns and controversies at the 2022 Winter Olympics#United States|boycott]] of the Beijing Olympics<ref name=uswiln>{{Cite news|last=Kanno-Youngs|first=Zolan|date=6 December 2021|title=U.S. Will Not Send Government Officials to Beijing Olympics |work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/12/06/us/politics/olympics-boycott-us.html|access-date=16 August 2023|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=4 August 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230804194427/https://www.nytimes.com/2021/12/06/us/politics/olympics-boycott-us.html|url-status=live}}</ref> and signed the [[Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act]],<ref>{{Cite web|title=Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act |url=https://www.cbp.gov/trade/forced-labor/UFLPA|access-date=7 February 2023|website=U.S. Customs and Border Protection |archive-date=7 August 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230807163603/https://www.cbp.gov/trade/forced-labor/UFLPA|url-status=live}}</ref> further highlighting U.S. criticism of China's human rights record.<ref name=uswiln/>
On 27 February 2022, the White House urged China to condemn [[2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine|Russia's invasion of Ukraine]].<ref>{{cite news|last1=Chang|first1=Richard|last2=Chiacu|first2=Doina|title=White House calls on China to condemn Russia's invasion of Ukraine|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/white-house-calls-china-condemn-russias-invasion-ukraine-2022-02-27/|access-date=27 February 2022|work=Reuters|agency=Reuters|date=27 February 2022}}</ref> China accused the United States of being responsible for the war in Ukraine.<ref>{{cite news|title=China hammers home its message of U.S. blame for Ukraine war to domestic audience|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3172228/china-hammers-home-its-message-us-blame-ukraine-war-domestic|work=South China Morning Post|date=29 March 2022|access-date=14 August 2022|archive-date=2 September 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240902164742/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3172228/china-hammers-home-its-message-us-blame-ukraine-war-domestic|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=China says the US is the 'main instigator' of the war in Ukraine|url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/8/10/china-accuses-us-as-main-instigator-of-the-war-in-ukraine|work=Al Jazeera|date=10 August 2022|access-date=14 August 2022|archive-date=14 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220814163322/https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/8/10/china-accuses-us-as-main-instigator-of-the-war-in-ukraine|url-status=live}}</ref> [[File:Tsai Presents Pelosi with Order of Propitious Clouds by Chien 01.jpg|thumb|ROC President [[Tsai Ing-wen]] with U.S. House Speaker [[Nancy Pelosi]] on 3 August 2022]] On 18 March 2022, Joe Biden and Xi Jinping directly communicated with each other for the first time since Russia's invasion of Ukraine.<ref>{{cite news|date=18 March 2022|title=US, China discuss Russia as Biden and Xi hold call|work=[[Al Jazeera Arabic|Al Jazeera]]|url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/3/18/biden-and-xi-to-talk-on-phone-as-us-presses-china-over-russia|access-date=20 March 2022|archive-date=19 March 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220319220508/https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/3/18/biden-and-xi-to-talk-on-phone-as-us-presses-china-over-russia|url-status=live}}</ref>
In May 2022, Chinese officials ordered government agencies and state-backed companies to remove personal computers produced by American corporations and replace them with equipment from domestic companies. Bloomberg said the decision was one of China's most aggressive moves to eliminate the usage of foreign technology from the most sensitive parts of its government and spur its campaign to substitute foreign technology with domestic ones.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Li|first1=Yanping|last2=Gao|first2=Yuan|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-05-06/china-orders-government-state-firms-to-dump-foreign-pcs|title=China Orders Government, State Firms to Dump Foreign PCs|work=[[Bloomberg News]]|date=6 May 2022|access-date=6 May 2022|archive-date=6 May 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220506210247/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-05-06/china-orders-government-state-firms-to-dump-foreign-pcs|url-status=live}}</ref>
In late May 2022, the State Department restored a line on its fact sheet on US-Taiwan relations which it removed earlier in the month and stated it did not support Taiwanese independence. However,<ref>{{cite news|title=U.S. puts back 'no support of Taiwan independence' statement on fact sheet|url=https://focustaiwan.tw/cross-strait/202206030004|agency=Focus Taiwan|publisher=CNA|access-date=14 November 2022|archive-date=14 November 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221114131005/https://focustaiwan.tw/cross-strait/202206030004|url-status=live}}</ref> another line which was also removed in the earlier fact sheet that acknowledged China's sovereignty claims over Taiwan was not restored while a line that stated the U.S. would maintain its capacity to resist any efforts by China to undermine the security, sovereignty and prosperity of Taiwan in a manner that was consistent with the Taiwan Relations Act was added to the updated fact sheet.
On 11 June 2022, U.S. Secretary of Defense [[Lloyd Austin]] condemned China's "provocative, destabilising" military activity near Taiwan,<ref>{{cite news|title=US blasts China's 'destabilising' military activity near Taiwan|url=https://www.france24.com/en/live-news/20220611-us-blasts-china-s-destabilising-military-activity-near-taiwan|work=France 24|date=11 June 2022|access-date=14 August 2022|archive-date=13 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813231437/https://www.france24.com/en/live-news/20220611-us-blasts-china-s-destabilising-military-activity-near-taiwan|url-status=live}}</ref> a day after China's Defence Minister [[Wei Fenghe]] warned Austin that "if anyone dares to split Taiwan from China, the Chinese army will definitely not hesitate to start a war no matter the cost."<ref>{{cite news|title='Smash to smithereens': China threatens all-out war over Taiwan|url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/6/10/china-tells-us-it-will-not-hesitate-to-start-war-over-taiwan|work=Al-Jazeera|date=10 June 2022|access-date=14 August 2022|archive-date=13 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813231524/https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/6/10/china-tells-us-it-will-not-hesitate-to-start-war-over-taiwan|url-status=live}}</ref>
In July 2022, speaker of the house [[Nancy Pelosi]] announced that she would be leading a congressional delegation to the Indo-Pacific region.<ref name="Pelosi-2022">{{Cite web|date=31 July 2022|title=Pelosi to Lead Congressional Delegation to Indo-Pacific Region|url=https://pelosi.house.gov/news/press-releases/pelosi-to-lead-congressional-delegation-to-indo-pacific-region|access-date=2 August 2022|website=Congresswoman Nancy Pelosi |archive-date=2 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220802112457/https://pelosi.house.gov/news/press-releases/pelosi-to-lead-congressional-delegation-to-indo-pacific-region|url-status=live}}</ref> She has planned to visit Singapore, Malaysia, South Korea and Japan, as well as the island of Taiwan.<ref name="Pelosi-2022" /> China has responded to this by saying "it would constitute a gross interference in China's internal affairs", and continued military exercises within Chinese territories.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2 August 2022|title=Taiwan and China step up military rhetoric as expected Pelosi visit looms|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/aug/02/taiwan-and-china-step-up-military-rhetoric-as-expected-pelosi-visit-looms|access-date=2 August 2022|website=The Guardian |archive-date=2 September 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240902163011/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/aug/02/taiwan-and-china-step-up-military-rhetoric-as-expected-pelosi-visit-looms|url-status=live}}</ref> When [[2022 visit by Nancy Pelosi to Taiwan|Pelosi visited the island the following month]], the act was strongly condemned by China.<ref>{{cite web| url=https://edition.cnn.com/2022/08/02/politics/nancy-pelosi-visit-taipei-taiwan-trip/index.html| title=US House Speaker Nancy Pelosi lands in Taiwan amid threats of Chinese retaliation| date=2 August 2022| access-date=6 August 2022| archive-date=4 August 2022| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220804025031/https://edition.cnn.com/2022/08/02/politics/nancy-pelosi-visit-taipei-taiwan-trip/index.html| url-status=live}}</ref> As a result, China severed ties in all cooperation activities with the United States in several areas, including military matters, global climate cooperation, and drug trafficking enforcement. Later the [[State Department]] summoned Chinese ambassadors to complain about Chinese aggression. China claimed that the Pelosi visit served no other purpose than to provoke China and to deteriorate Sino-American relations while the United States, pointing to past precedent, said that Pelosi had the right to visit Taiwan and attacked the Chinese response as disproportionate.<ref>[https://apnews.com/article/taiwan-china-asia-beijing-b252479810add6a225fa1e4a6d441983 "China cuts off vital US contacts over Pelosi Taiwan visit"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220805095713/https://apnews.com/article/taiwan-china-asia-beijing-b252479810add6a225fa1e4a6d441983|date=5 August 2022}} ''APnews.com.'' Retrieved 6 August 2022.</ref><ref>[https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2022/08/05/us-summons-china-ambassador/ "White House summons Chinese ambassador as crisis escalates"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220806203137/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2022/08/05/us-summons-china-ambassador/|date=6 August 2022}}. ''The Washington Post''. Retrieved 6 August 2022.</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=White House decries China rhetoric over Pelosi Taiwan visit|url=https://apnews.com/article/china-asia-beijing-malaysia-pacific-15f739b266c2c90ba41c5917f26eb980|work=AP News|access-date=14 November 2022|archive-date=8 November 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231108061737/https://apnews.com/article/china-asia-beijing-malaysia-pacific-15f739b266c2c90ba41c5917f26eb980|url-status=live}}</ref> After Pelosi's departure, the PRC began [[2022 Chinese military exercises around Taiwan|military exercises encircling Taiwan]].<ref>{{Cite news|work=Reuters|date=2 August 2022|title=China to launch 'targeted military operations' due to Pelosi visit |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china-launch-targeted-military-operations-due-pelosi-visit-2022-08-02/|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220802231811/https://www.reuters.com/world/china-launch-targeted-military-operations-due-pelosi-visit-2022-08-02/|archive-date=2 August 2022}}</ref>
On 7 October 2022, the U.S. implemented new [[United States New Export Controls on Advanced Computing and Semiconductors to China|export controls]] targeting China's ability to access and develop advanced computing and semiconductor manufacturing items.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Bureau of Industry and Security|date=7 October 2022|title=Commerce Implements New Export Controls on Advanced Computing and Semiconductor Manufacturing Items to the People's Republic of China (PRC)|url=https://www.bis.doc.gov/index.php/documents/about-bis/newsroom/press-releases/3158-2022-10-07-bis-press-release-advanced-computing-and-semiconductor-manufacturing-controls-final/file|access-date=29 October 2022|website=U.S. Department of Commerce Bureau of Industry and Security|archive-date=10 March 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240310160001/https://www.bis.doc.gov/index.php/documents/about-bis/newsroom/press-releases/3158-2022-10-07-bis-press-release-advanced-computing-and-semiconductor-manufacturing-controls-final/file|url-status=live}}</ref> The new export controls reflect the United States' ambition to counter the accelerating advancement of China's high-tech capabilities in these spaces to address foreign policy and national security concerns.<ref>{{Cite web|date=24 October 2022|title=New US Semiconductor Export Controls Signify Dramatic Shift in Tech Relations With China|url=https://www.justsecurity.org/83744/new-us-semiconductor-export-controls-signify-dramatic-shift-in-tech-relations-with-china/|access-date=29 October 2022|website=Just Security |archive-date=8 December 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221208071253/https://www.justsecurity.org/83744/new-us-semiconductor-export-controls-signify-dramatic-shift-in-tech-relations-with-china/|url-status=live}}</ref>
[[File:President Biden met with President Xi of the PRC before the 2022 G20 Bali Summit.jpg|thumb|Chinese leader [[Xi Jinping]] with U.S. President [[Joe Biden]] at the [[2022 G20 Bali summit|G20 summit in Bali]], November 2022]] On 14 November 2022, Joe Biden and Xi Jinping met on the sidelines of the [[2022 G20 Bali summit|G20 summit]] in [[Bali]] for their first in-person encounter since Biden became president. The meeting lasted for more than 3 hours and they discussed a range of issues which included tensions over Taiwan and North Korea, and the war in Ukraine.<ref>{{cite news|title=Factbox: Top takeaways from the Biden-Xi meeting in Bali|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/top-takeaways-biden-xi-meeting-bali-2022-11-14|work=Reuters|access-date=17 November 2022|archive-date=16 November 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221116101502/https://www.reuters.com/world/top-takeaways-biden-xi-meeting-bali-2022-11-14/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=November 14, 2022 Biden, Xi meet during G20 summit|url=https://www.cnn.com/asia/live-news/biden-xi-meeting-g20-summit-bali-indonesia/index.html|agency=CNN|access-date=17 November 2022|archive-date=17 November 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221117174915/https://www.cnn.com/asia/live-news/biden-xi-meeting-g20-summit-bali-indonesia/index.html|url-status=live}}</ref> [[File:Secretary Blinken Meets with Chinese State Councilor and Foreign Minister Wang Yi in Bali (52204013285).jpg|thumb|U.S. Secretary of State [[Antony Blinken]] (right) with Chinese Foreign Minister [[Wang Yi (politician)|Wang Yi]] on 9 July 2022. Blinken dismissed China's claims to be neutral in the [[Russo-Ukrainian War]] and accused China of supporting Russia.<ref>{{cite news|title=US's Blinken raises China's 'alignment with Russia' on Ukraine|url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/7/9/us-blinken-and-chinas-wang-yi-hold-talks-on-ukraine-trade|work=Al Jazeera|date=9 July 2022|access-date=26 February 2023|archive-date=20 March 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230320064107/https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/7/9/us-blinken-and-chinas-wang-yi-hold-talks-on-ukraine-trade|url-status=live}}</ref>]] Some [[geoeconomics]] experts see an acceleration of the US–China rivalry as "inevitable" given the tensions manifested openly in the last months of 2022 and early 2023. In a series of interviews with [[BBC News]] and [[Asharq News]], Nicolas Firzli, director, [[Singapore Forum|EU ASEAN Centre]], argued that "Cold War 2 with China [was] part of the Biden Doctrine, and the only remaining point of convergence between Biden and a Republic dominated Congress [...] January 2023 is the moment when things crystalized irreversibly".<ref>Nicolas Firzli: [https://now.asharq.com/program/5/الرابط/1547258/هل-تحركات-واشنطن-كافية-للحد-من-سيطرة-الصين-على-قطاع-التكنولوجيا-المتقدمة "Interview of Nicolas Firzli & Joseph A. Bosco: China and Tech Supremacy"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230218114529/https://now.asharq.com/program/5/%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%A8%D8%B7/1547258/%D9%87%D9%84-%D8%AA%D8%AD%D8%B1%D9%83%D8%A7%D8%AA-%D9%88%D8%A7%D8%B4%D9%86%D8%B7%D9%86-%D9%83%D8%A7%D9%81%D9%8A%D8%A9-%D9%84%D9%84%D8%AD%D8%AF-%D9%85%D9%86-%D8%B3%D9%8A%D8%B7%D8%B1%D8%A9-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B5%D9%8A%D9%86-%D8%B9%D9%84%D9%89-%D9%82%D8%B7%D8%A7%D8%B9-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AA%D9%83%D9%86%D9%88%D9%84%D9%88%D8%AC%D9%8A%D8%A7-%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%85%D8%AA%D9%82%D8%AF%D9%85%D8%A9|date=18 February 2023}}, ''Asharq News with [[Bloomberg News|Bloomberg]]'', 12 January 2023</ref>
On 2 February 2023, [[2023 Chinese balloon incident|a Chinese reconnaissance balloon]] was spotted flying over US airspace in the state of Montana, potentially to collect information related to nuclear silos in the area.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/02/02/us/politics/china-spy-balloon-pentagon.html|title=U.S. Detects Suspected China Spy Balloon Hovering over Northwest|website=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=2 February 2023|archive-date=4 February 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230204165824/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/02/02/us/politics/china-spy-balloon-pentagon.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Two days later, the United States shot it down over the Atlantic Ocean, citing national security concerns.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Capoot|first=Ashley|title=U.S. military shoots down suspected Chinese surveillance balloon|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2023/02/04/us-military-prepares-to-take-down-suspected-chinese-surveillance-balloon.html|access-date=4 February 2023|website=CNBC|date=4 February 2023 |archive-date=4 February 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230204200619/https://www.cnbc.com/2023/02/04/us-military-prepares-to-take-down-suspected-chinese-surveillance-balloon.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The balloon incident followed previous Chinese government actions targeting the U.S., including the [[Chinese espionage in the United States|Chinese theft of the designs]] for the [[F-35]] about fifteen years earlier and successful Chinese government-sponsored [[Office of Personnel Management data breach|cyberattacks targeting the Office of Personnel Management]] security clearance files (2015), the [[Anthem medical data breach|healthcare company Anthem]] (2015), and the [[Marriott International]] system (2018).<ref name="CompIntensifies">{{Cite news|last=Sanger|first=David E.|date=5 February 2023|title=Balloon Incident Reveals More Than Spying as Competition With China Intensifies |work=[[The New York Times]]|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/02/05/us/politics/balloon-china-spying-united-states.html|url-access=limited|access-date=10 February 2023|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=10 February 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230210105143/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/02/05/us/politics/balloon-china-spying-united-states.html|url-status=live}}</ref> In 2022, the U.S. and its allies imposed stringent additional [[export control]]s on the sale of "foundational technologies" (including [[Semiconductor device fabrication|advanced semiconductor chips]] and related technology) to China, with the aim of inhibiting any Chinese military buildup.<ref name="nytimes/2023/02/09/china-balloon-program" /> The [[Biden administration]] has also sought to maintain critical-sector supply chains independent from China.<ref name="nytimes/2023/02/09/china-balloon-program">{{cite news|last1=Wong|first1=Edward|last2=Barnes|first2=Julian E.|date=9 February 2023|title=Chinese Balloon Had Tools to Collect Electronic Communications, U.S. Says|work=[[The New York Times]]|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/02/09/us/politics/china-spy-balloon-program.html|url-access=limited|access-date=9 February 2023|archive-date=9 February 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230209231207/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/02/09/us/politics/china-spy-balloon-program.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The Beijing government expressed strong dissatisfaction and protest against the US's use of force, calling it a violation of international practice. The U.S. claimed the balloon was a violation of its sovereignty.<ref>{{Cite news|title= China blasts US for shooting down balloon|url= https://www.dawn.com/news/1735601/china-blasts-us-for-shooting-down-balloon|access-date= 6 February 2023|archive-date= 6 February 2023|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230206164514/https://www.dawn.com/news/1735601/china-blasts-us-for-shooting-down-balloon|url-status= live}}</ref> [[File:U-2 Pilot over Central Continental United States (7644960).jpg|thumb|right|A U.S. Air Force [[Lockheed U-2|U-2]] pilot takes a picture somewhere over the Central Continental U.S.; the [[2023 Chinese balloon incident|Chinese balloon]] is in the background.]] On 11 February 2023, the U.S. Commerce Department prohibited six Chinese companies connected to the aerospace programs of the Chinese army from acquiring U.S. technology without authorization from the government. These six businesses include Nanjiang Aerospace Technology of Beijing; The 48th Research Institute of China Electronics Technology Group Corporation; Technology for Dongguan Lingkong Remote Sensing; Aviation Science and Technology Group of the Eagles Men; Tian-Hai-Xiang Aviation Technology in Guangzhou; together with the Shanxi Eagles Men Aviation Science and Technology Group.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Rokus|first1=Brian|title=US restricts 6 Chinese companies tied to airships and balloons|url=https://edition.cnn.com/2023/02/11/business/spy-balloon-us-restricts-chinese-companies-intl-hnk/index.html|website=CNN |date=11 February 2023|access-date=14 February 2023|archive-date=2 September 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240902164742/https://edition.cnn.com/2023/02/11/business/spy-balloon-us-restricts-chinese-companies-intl-hnk/index.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
In April 2023, China sanctioned U.S. Representative [[Michael McCaul]] in response to a legislative trip for Taiwanese President [[Tsai Ing-wen]].<ref>{{cite web|title=China sanctions U.S. Congress member for Taiwan visit|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2023/04/14/china-sanctions-us-congress-member-for-taiwan-visit.html|website=cnbc.com|date=14 April 2023|publisher=CNBC|access-date=14 April 2023|archive-date=14 April 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230414174627/https://www.cnbc.com/2023/04/14/china-sanctions-us-congress-member-for-taiwan-visit.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
In May 2023, an American citizen living in Hong Kong named John Shing-Wan Leung was sentenced to life in prison on charges of espionage. Leung was arrested in 2021 by China's counterintelligence agency.<ref>{{Cite news|date=15 May 2023|first=Gareth|last=Evans|title=China sentences 78-year-old US citizen to life in prison for spying|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-65595221|access-date=15 May 2023|website=[[BBC News]] |archive-date=15 May 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230515100541/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-china-65595221|url-status=live}}</ref> On 23 September 2024, the Biden administration banned Chinese software from U.S. internet-connected vehicles, citing national security risks. This move builds on previous actions against Chinese tech, deepening the digital divide between the two nations.<ref>{{cite web|title=US for ban on Chinese-developed software in Internet-connected cars in country|date= 24 September 2024|url=https://www.telegraphindia.com/world/us-for-ban-on-chinese-developed-software-in-internet-connected-cars-in-country/cid/2050087 |work=The Telegraph Online| access-date=25 September 2024}}</ref>
====Attempts to fix relationship (2023–2024)====
In mid-2023, both countries started to increase meetings between high-level officials in the hope of stabilizing the relationship; on 11 May, U.S. national security adviser [[Jake Sullivan]] met with [[Wang Yi (politician)|Wang Yi]], director of the CCP [[Office of the Central Foreign Affairs Commission]], with the topics including Taiwan and Russia's invasion of Ukraine.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Hutzler|first1=Charles|last2=Youssef|first2=Nancy A.|date=11 May 2023|title=U.S., China Senior Officials Meet in Tentative Effort to Restart Ties |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]]|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/u-s-china-senior-officials-meet-in-tentative-effort-to-restart-ties-54f49ba4|access-date=8 June 2023|issn=0099-9660|archive-date=20 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230620190042/https://www.wsj.com/articles/u-s-china-senior-officials-meet-in-tentative-effort-to-restart-ties-54f49ba4|url-status=live}}</ref> On 21 May, Biden commented during the [[47th G7 summit|G7 summit in Hiroshima]] that he expected a thaw in relations with China soon, commenting that the two countries were moving towards more dialogue but "this silly balloon that was carrying two freight cars worth of spy equipment was flying over the United States and it got shot down and everything changed in terms of talking to one another".<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Thomas|first1=Ken|last2=Linskey|first2=Annie|date=21 May 2023|title=Biden Sees Potential Thaw With China After Tough G-7 Statement |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]]|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/biden-sees-potential-thaw-with-china-after-tough-g-7-statement-5c200aa9|access-date=8 June 2023|issn=0099-9660|archive-date=8 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230608232934/https://www.wsj.com/articles/biden-sees-potential-thaw-with-china-after-tough-g-7-statement-5c200aa9|url-status=live}}</ref> On 26 May, Chinese minister of commerce [[Wang Wentao]] met with U.S. secretary of commerce [[Gina Raimondo]], where Raimondo raised concerns about treatment of U.S. companies by China.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Politi|first1=James|last2=Leahy|first2=Joe|date=26 May 2023|title=US and China address trade tensions in rare high-level Washington meeting|work=[[Financial Times]]|url=https://www.ft.com/content/fd43bb75-cc29-4ba1-9cab-9c0f5dfdb6bb|access-date=8 June 2023|archive-date=8 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230608183547/https://www.ft.com/content/fd43bb75-cc29-4ba1-9cab-9c0f5dfdb6bb|url-status=live}}</ref> U.S. officials also announced in June that [[Central Intelligence Agency]] (CIA) director [[William J. Burns (diplomat)|William J. Burns]] travelled to China in May.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Martina|first1=Michael|last2=Hunnicutt|first2=Trevor|date=2 June 2023|title=CIA chief Burns visited China in May |work=[[Reuters]]|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/cia-head-visited-china-may-met-counterparts-us-official-2023-06-02/|access-date=8 June 2023|archive-date=9 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230609204122/https://www.reuters.com/world/cia-head-visited-china-may-met-counterparts-us-official-2023-06-02/|url-status=live}}</ref> However, a meeting in the [[Shangri-La Dialogue]] between Chinese minister of national defense [[Li Shangfu]] and U.S. secretary of defense [[Lloyd Austin]] failed to take place, after China rebuffed U.S. requests to meet.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Lin|first1=Chen|last2=Kapoor|first2=Kanupriya|date=4 June 2023|title=China says clash with US would be 'unbearable disaster' |work=[[Reuters]]|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/china-seeks-dialogue-over-confrontation-defence-minister-tells-security-meet-2023-06-04/|access-date=8 June 2023|archive-date=7 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230607230613/https://www.reuters.com/world/china/china-seeks-dialogue-over-confrontation-defence-minister-tells-security-meet-2023-06-04/|url-status=live}}</ref> In late June 2023, Blinken traveled to China and met with Xi; subsequent public statements by both countries were largely positive, with Xi and Blinken emphasizing that both sides have a responsibility to manage relations.<ref>{{Cite web|last1=Sim|first1=Dewey|last2=Wang|first2=Orange|date=19 June 2023|title=Xi and Blinken Cap China Trip With Vow to Steady Ties to Ward off Conflict|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3224560/antony-blinken-and-wang-yi-meet-beijing-us-and-china-push-mend-strained-relationship|access-date=21 June 2023|website=[[South China Morning Post]] |archive-date=21 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230621042425/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3224560/antony-blinken-and-wang-yi-meet-beijing-us-and-china-push-mend-strained-relationship|url-status=live}}</ref> However, relations became more contentious after Biden called Xi a "[[dictator]]".<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Hawkins |first1=Amy |date=23 June 2023 |title=China reportedly rebukes US ambassador after Biden called Xi a 'dictator' |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2023/jun/23/china-reportedly-rebukes-us-ambassador-after-biden-called-xi-a-dictator |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240902163015/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2023/jun/23/china-reportedly-rebukes-us-ambassador-after-biden-called-xi-a-dictator |archive-date=2 September 2024 |access-date=25 June 2023 |work=The Guardian |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> [[File:Yellen-He meeting 2023.jpg|thumb|U.S Treasury Secretary [[Janet Yellen]] meeting with Vice Premier [[He Lifeng]] during Yellen's trip to Beijing, 8 July 2023]] The trips further continued as between 6–9 July, United States secretary of treasury [[Janet Yellen]] visited China, her first trip to the country during her tenure as well as the first trip to the country by a U.S. Treasury secretary in four years. During the visit, she met with various Chinese officials, including former vice premier [[Liu He (politician)|Liu He]], governor of the [[People's Bank of China]] (PBC) [[Yi Gang]],<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Cash|first1=Joe|date=7 July 2023|title=Yellen holds talks with China central bank governor, ex-economy tsar |work=[[Reuters]]|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/yellen-holds-talks-with-chinas-central-bank-governor-former-economy-tsar-2023-07-07/|access-date=9 July 2023|archive-date=15 July 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230715133750/https://www.reuters.com/world/yellen-holds-talks-with-chinas-central-bank-governor-former-economy-tsar-2023-07-07/|url-status=live}}</ref> minister of finance [[Liu Kun]],<ref name="Rappeport-2023">{{Cite news|last1=Rappeport|first1=Alan|last2=Bradsher|first2=Keith|date=9 July 2023|title=China and the U.S., Still Adversaries, Are Talking. That's a Start. |work=[[The New York Times]]|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/07/09/business/janet-yellen-china-us.html|access-date=9 July 2023|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=1 September 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230901060744/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/07/09/business/janet-yellen-china-us.html|url-status=live}}</ref> CCP secretary of PBC [[Pan Gongsheng]], vice premier [[He Lifeng]], and premier [[Li Qiang]].<ref>{{Cite news|last=Dendrinou|first=Viktoria|date=9 July 2023|title=Yellen Says U.S.-China Ties on 'Surer Footing' After Critical Trip |work=[[Bloomberg News]]|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-07-09/yellen-says-us-china-ties-on-surer-footing-after-critical-trip|access-date=9 July 2023|archive-date=10 July 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230710021928/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-07-09/yellen-says-us-china-ties-on-surer-footing-after-critical-trip|url-status=live}}</ref> During her visit to, Yellen criticized China's treatment of American companies with foreign connections,<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Rappeport|first1=Alan|last2=Bradsher|first2=Keith|date=7 July 2023|title=Yellen, in Beijing, Criticizes China's Treatment of U.S. Companies |work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/07/07/business/yellen-china-companies-meetings.html|access-date=8 July 2023|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=9 July 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230709054911/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/07/07/business/yellen-china-companies-meetings.html|url-status=live}}</ref> stating to Li Qiang: "We seek healthy economic competition that is not winner-take-all but that, with a fair set of rules, can benefit both countries over time."<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Shalal|first1=Andrea|last2=Cash|first2=Joe|last3=Cash|first3=Joe|date=7 July 2023|title=Yellen criticizes China's 'punitive' actions against US companies, urges market reforms |work=[[Reuters]]|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/yellen-urges-china-adopt-market-reforms-insists-us-not-decoupling-2023-07-07/|access-date=8 July 2023|archive-date=9 July 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230709065633/https://www.reuters.com/world/yellen-urges-china-adopt-market-reforms-insists-us-not-decoupling-2023-07-07/|url-status=live}}</ref> She also said that the U.S. national security restrictions on investment in China were intended to be narrowly focused and not have broad effects on the Chinese economy.<ref name="Rappeport-2023" /> [[File:Qin-Blinken meeting (2023) 02.jpg|thumb|Antony Blinken with Chinese Foreign Minister [[Qin Gang]] in Beijing, 18 June 2023]] On 13 July 2023, Secretary of State [[Antony Blinken]] met the Director of the Office of the Central Commission for Foreign Affairs [[Wang Yi (politician)|Wang Yi]] in [[Jakarta]] at the [[ASEAN]] Post-Ministerial Conference at the request of Blinken to discuss the removal of obstacles that complicate U.S.-China relations, such as the Taiwan question and sanctions the U.S. is imposing against China's high-technology sector, as well as to promote the pragmatic approach with regard to regional cooperation. Former Minister of Foreign Affairs of China [[Qin Gang]] had just been replaced by his predecessor [[Wang Yi (politician)|Wang Yi]] due to an extramarital affair of Gang in July of the same year. This meeting also paved the way for a further encounter of China's top diplomat Wang Yi before year's end when he will be invited to meet Blinken in Washington DC. Later on the sidelines of the U.N General Assembly (UNGA) in NY on 26 September, Vice-president [[Han Zheng]] met with U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken to further nurture the strained bilateral ties of both superpowers. The absence of Xi Jinping at the [[2023 G20 New Delhi summit]] in India on 9 and 10 September was regretful, according to a statement by U.S. President Joe Biden, however, recent news reports indicate that both CCP General Secretary Xi as well as CCP Foreign Affairs Commission Office Director Wang Yi will meet Biden and Blinken before year's end to hopefully ease the strained relations between the two nations.<ref>[https://www.reuters.com/world/us-expects-blinken-will-host-chinas-top-diplomat-wang-yi-before-year-end-2023-09-11/ "US expects Blinken will host China's top diplomat Wang Yi before year-end"]. ''Reuters'' (12 September 2023). Accessed 1 October 2023.</ref>
Yellen's visit was followed by a visit by United States secretary of commerce [[Gina Raimondo]] between 27 and 30 August, where she met minister of culture and tourism [[Hu Heping]], minister of commerce [[Wang Wentao]], vice premier He Lifeng, and premier Li Qiang.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Wong|first1=Kandy|last2=Jennings|first2=Ralph|last3=Nulimaimaiti|first3=Mia|date=29 August 2023|title=As Gina Raimondo wraps up Beijing leg of China trip with high-level meetings, will it be a litmus test for relations?|work=[[South China Morning Post]]|url=https://www.scmp.com/economy/global-economy/article/3232692/gina-raimondos-trip-shifts-tone-and-cools-tensions-it-litmus-test-us-china-relations-talks-resume?module=inline&pgtype=article|access-date=3 September 2023}}</ref> Raimondo also visited Shanghai, where she met with Shanghai Communist Party secretary [[Chen Jining]], and visited [[Shanghai Disneyland]].<ref>{{Cite news|last=Shepardson|first=David|date=30 August 2023|title=US Commerce chief leaves China on upbeat note after 'uninvestible' remark |work=[[Reuters]]|url=https://www.reuters.com/markets/china-response-uss-raimondo-says-it-is-easing-market-access-foreign-firms-2023-08-30/|access-date=3 September 2023}}</ref> During the meeting, the two sides announced a [[working group]] on commercial issues and an export control enforcement "information exchange" dialogue.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Bade|first=Gavin|date=31 August 2023|title=Raimondo's 'bold move' in China|work=[[Politico]]|url=https://www.politico.com/news/2023/08/31/raimondo-china-congressional-hawks-00113601|access-date=3 September 2023}}</ref> The working group, upon its launch on 22 September 2023, was divided into two segments: one economic subgroup and one financial subgroup.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Hussein|first1=Fatima|title=United States and China launch economic and financial working groups with aim of easing tensions|url=https://apnews.com/article/us-china-working-groups-yellen-tensions-c21a6d14467893123ef70eb8a0443366|access-date=26 September 2023|publisher=AP News|date=22 September 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230922120621/https://apnews.com/article/us-china-working-groups-yellen-tensions-c21a6d14467893123ef70eb8a0443366|archive-date=22 September 2023}}</ref>
[[File:Sullivan-Wang meeting (2023).jpg|thumb|Biden's National Security Advisor [[Jake Sullivan]] with CCP Foreign Affairs Commission Office director [[Wang Yi (politician)|Wang Yi]], 28 October 2023]] On 2 November 2023, a report from the [[Wall Street Journal]] was released saying that the U.S. and China would hold [[nuclear arms]] talks, a rarity, ahead of Xi Jinping's visit to the United States.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://time.com/6330731/us-china-nuclear-arms-talks/|agency=[[Bloomberg News]]|work=[[Time (magazine)|Time]]|date=2 November 2023|title=U.S. and China to Hold Rare Nuclear Arms Talks Before Biden-Xi Meeting|access-date=2 November 2023}}</ref>
At the beginning of November 2023, insiders cautiously expressed hope for a climate agreement between [[China]] and [[United States]] ahead of the [[2023 United Nations Climate Change Conference]], similar to the agreement of 2014 which paved the way for the [[Paris Agreement]]. One contentious topic is a plan for reduction of [[methane]] emissions in China. According to China's climate envoy [[Xie Zhenhua (politician)|Xie Zhenhua]] "progress on a plan reflected the state of U.S.-China relations." Another is a reduction in [[Coal in China|coal use in China]]. China says it expands coal use for improving [[energy security]], even though many think there are better ways to improve it.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Hawkins|first1=Amy|title=China releases methane plan as hopes rise for new climate agreement with US|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2023/nov/08/china-methane-plan-climate-agreement-us|access-date=8 November 2023|newspaper=The Guardian|date=8 November 2023}}</ref> Talks between [[Janet Yellen]] and [[He Lifeng]] yielded a decision to enhance cooperation between China and the United States in several domains, including [[Climate change|climate]], [[debt relief]]. Much is expected from the meeting between Joe Biden and Xi Jinping. According to Kate Logan from the [[Asia Society Policy Institute]], cooperation between the 2 countries, can "set the stage for a successful outcome at the COP28".<ref>{{cite web|title=US, China agree to cooperate on climate change, global debt relief|url=https://www.carbonbrief.org/daily-brief/us-china-agree-to-cooperate-on-climate-change-global-debt-relief/|website=Carbon Brief|access-date=14 November 2023}}</ref>
On 15 November 2023, President Joe Biden met with Xi Jinping at the [[APEC United States 2023|2023 APEC Summit]] in San Francisco.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Hunnicutt|first1=Trevor|last2=Mason|first2=Jeff|last3=Holland|first3=Steve|last4=Mason|first4=Jeff|date=16 November 2023|title=Biden, Xi's 'blunt' talks yield deals on military, fentanyl |work=[[Reuters]]|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/biden-xi-meet-us-china-military-economic-tensions-grind-2023-11-15/|access-date=16 November 2023}}</ref> This was speculated to be their last meeting of 2023 before Biden's [[Joe Biden 2024 presidential campaign|2024 reelection campaign]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=Cheng|first=Evelyn|date=15 November 2023|title=Earth is 'big enough' for U.S. and China to succeed, Xi says as he meets Biden|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2023/11/15/xi-biden-at-apec-summit-china-us-leaders-meet-in-san-francisco.html|access-date=17 November 2023|website=CNBC}}</ref>
The U.S. and China resumed semi-official nuclear arms talks in March 2024, with China reassuring the U.S. it wouldn't use nuclear weapons over Taiwan and reaffirming its no-first-use policy. Despite broader tensions, both sides plan to continue discussions in 2025.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Torode|first1=Greg|last2=Doyle|first2= Gerry|last3=Chen|first3=Laurie|date=21 June 2024|title=U.S. and China hold first informal nuclear talks in five years|newspaper=Reuters|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/us-china-hold-first-informal-nuclear-talks-5-years-eyeing-taiwan-2024-06-21/|access-date=23 June 2024}}</ref>
In October 2024 American drone maker [[Skydio]] was sanctioned by China after its products were approved for use by [[fire department]]s in Taiwan. The Chinese government forbade components suppliers and other businesses in China from doing business with Skydio.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Irwin |first1=Kate |title=China Sanctions US Drone Maker Skydio Over Taiwan Deal |url=https://www.pcmag.com/news/china-sanctions-us-drone-maker-skydio-over-taiwan-deal |date=31 October 2024 |website=PC Magazine |access-date=31 October 2024}}</ref>
In November 2024, [[John Moolenaar]], chairman of the [[United States House Select Committee on Strategic Competition between the United States and the Chinese Communist Party]], introduced legislation to revoke [[Permanent normal trade relations|PNTR status]] for the People's Republic of China.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kelly |first=Laura |date=14 November 2024 |title=Republican introduces bill to revoke normal trade relations with China |url=https://thehill.com/policy/international/4991044-republican-proposes-ending-trade-relations-china/ |access-date=16 November 2024 |website=[[The Hill (newspaper)|The Hill]]}}</ref> The same month, the [[United States–China Economic and Security Review Commission]] unanimously recommended revocation of China's PNTR status.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Cadell |first=Cate |date=19 November 2024 |title=Congressional committee advises revoking China's trade status |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/national-security/2024/11/19/china-congress-trade-tariffs-ai/ |access-date=19 November 2024 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]}}</ref>
[[File:President Joe Biden meeting President Xi Jinping at APEC Peru 2024.jpg|thumb|Chinese leader [[Xi Jinping]] with U.S. President [[Joe Biden]] at the [[APEC Peru 2024|APEC summit in Lima]], November 2024]] On 16 November 2024, Xi Jinping and Joe Biden met on the sidelines of the [[APEC Peru 2024|APEC summit]] in [[Lima]]. They discussed stabilizing U.S.-China relations at [[APEC Peru 2024|APEC summit in Lima]], as Trump's return raises concerns over potential trade and Taiwan tensions. Beijing seeks dialogue but braces for challenges.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Frances|first=Mao|date=17 November 2024|title=Xi says he will work with Trump in last meeting with Biden|newspaper=BBC|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cx2l4dn802lo |access-date=17 November 2024}}</ref>
On 1 December 2024, China condemned a U.S. arms sale to Taiwan, valued at $385 million, which included F-16 jet parts and radar support. The sale was approved by the U.S. just before [[Lai Ching-te]] began a Pacific tour with stops in Hawaii and Guam. China criticized the sale for encouraging Taiwan's independence and damaging U.S.-China relations, and objected to Lai's U.S. transit, calling him a "separatist." Despite the lack of formal diplomatic ties, the U.S. is required by law to assist Taiwan in defending itself, which continues to provoke Beijing. Taiwan rejects China's claims of sovereignty.<ref>{{cite news|title=China blasts U.S. arms sale to Taiwan, President Lai's U.S. transit |date=1 December 2024 |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/world/china-taiwan-us-arms-sales-president-lai-ching-te-hawaii-rcna182323 |website=NBC News |access-date=1 December 2024}}</ref> After Washington announced more military aid and sales to Taiwan, China called it "playing with fire" and said such actions by the United States contradict the solemn commitments of its leaders not to support Taiwan independence.<ref>{{cite news |title=China says US 'playing with fire' by giving Taiwan more military aid |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/12/22/china-says-us-playing-with-fire-by-giving-taiwan-more-military-aid |agency=Al-Jazeera |date=22 December 2024}}</ref>
===Second Trump administration (2025–present)=== {{main|Foreign policy of the second Trump administration#China, Taiwan, and the South China Sea|Tariffs in the second Trump administration|China–United States trade war}} [[File:President Donald Trump greets Chinese President Xi Jinping before a bilateral meeting at the Gimhae International Airport terminal (54890669668).jpg|thumb|Chinese leader [[Xi Jinping]] with U.S. President [[Donald Trump]] at the [[APEC South Korea 2025|APEC summit]] in [[Busan]], October 2025]] Following his [[2024 United States presidential election|second election victory]] as President of the United States in November 2024, Donald Trump nominated Florida Senator [[Marco Rubio]] as Secretary of State and Representative [[Mike Waltz]] as National Security Advisor in his second administration. According to Neil Thomas, a fellow in Chinese politics at the [[Asia Society]] Policy Institute's Center for China Analysis, the selection of Rubio and Waltz—both known as hardliners on China—signals that Trump's foreign policy will prioritize China above all else.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/rubio-waltz-picks-puts-china-back-center-us/story?id=115787057|title=Rubio and Waltz picks put China back at the center of US foreign policy|first=Selina|last=Wang|newspaper=ABC News|date=13 November 2024|access-date=14 November 2024}}</ref> Just before [[Second inauguration of Donald Trump|Trump's second inauguration]] in January 2025, Vice President [[JD Vance]] and ally [[Elon Musk]] each held separate meetings with China's vice president [[Han Zheng]], who was in Washington attending the event as China's paramount leader [[Xi Jinping]]'s special representative.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Wang |first=Orange |date=20 January 2025 |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3295419/china-vice-president-han-meets-vance-musk-ahead-trump-inauguration |title=Han and Vance strike positive tone for US-China ties ahead of Trump inauguration |newspaper=[[South China Morning Post]] |access-date=20 January 2025 |url-status=live |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20250120045309/https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3295419/china-vice-president-han-meets-vance-musk-ahead-trump-inauguration |archive-date=20 January 2025 }}</ref> Han's presence at the capitol was seen by commentators as representative of Xi's interest in strengthening the two countries' relationship under Trump's tenure.<ref>{{Cite news |last=McCarthy |first=Simone |date=20 January 2025 |title=Who is Han Zheng, the senior Chinese official attending Trump's inauguration? |url=https://www.cnn.com/2025/01/19/china/han-zheng-trump-inauguration-intl-hnk/index.html |agency=[[CNN]] |access-date=20 January 2025 }}</ref><ref name="AP who is">{{cite news |last=Bodeen |first=Christopher |date=20 January 2025 |url=https://apnews.com/article/han-zheng-trump-inauguration-china-vice-president-5bb18d5049683d6624a9e1d4ee028104 |title=Vice President Han Zheng, China's representative at Trump's inauguration, is a trusted adviser to Xi |work=[[AP News]] |access-date=20 January 2025 }}</ref>
In March 2025, [[Beijing]] denounced [[G7]] statements accusing it of destabilizing maritime security, highlighting ongoing diplomatic friction.<ref name=":2" />
On 23 March 2025, [[Premier of China|Chinese Premier]] [[Li Qiang]] met with U.S. Senator [[Steve Daines]], a Trump ally, in Beijing. Li emphasized the need for dialogue over confrontation, while Daines carried Trump's "[[America First (policy)|America First]]" agenda, particularly on trade and fentanyl issues. The U.S. imposed 20% tariffs on Chinese imports, prompting Beijing to retaliate with 15% duties on American farm goods. China maintained that economic cooperation was vital and opposed U.S. pressure over fentanyl controls.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Krolicki |first1=Kevin |last2=Li |first2=Qiaoyi |title= China denounces Trump tariff: 'Fentanyl is America's problem'|date=2 January 2025 | work=Reuters |url= https://www.reuters.com/world/china/china-denounces-trump-tariff-fentanyl-is-americas-problem-2025-02-02/|access-date= 2 February 2025}}</ref>
Trump blamed China for the [[Opioid epidemic in the United States|opioid crisis in the United States]].<ref>{{cite news |title=How does fentanyl get into the US? |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cvg93nn1e6go |work=BBC News |date=4 March 2025}}</ref> He said the tariffs are intended to pressure China to do more to stop the flow of [[fentanyl]] into the US.<ref>{{cite news |title=Can Trump's tariffs break China's grip on manufacturing? |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cdxqeg51y36o |work=BBC News |date=5 March 2025}}</ref> [[Opioid]]s, predominantly fentanyl, have killed over 500,000 Americans since 2012.<ref>{{Cite web |author=Felbab-Brown |first=Vanda |date=17 February 2025 |title=The New War on Drugs |url=https://www.foreignaffairs.com/canada/new-war-drugs |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250226170429/https://www.foreignaffairs.com/canada/new-war-drugs |archive-date=26 February 2025 |access-date=7 March 2025 |website=[[Foreign Affairs]]}}</ref>
On 18 April 2025, the U.S. State Department accused China's Chang Guang Satellite Technology of providing satellite imagery to Iran-backed [[Houthis|Houthi forces]], allegedly aiding attacks on U.S. and international vessels in the [[Red Sea]]. Officials described the support as ongoing and "unacceptable", despite prior U.S. diplomatic engagement with Beijing.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-04-18 |title=US says Chinese satellite firm is supporting Houthi attacks on US interests |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/world/middle-east/article/3306997/us-says-chinese-satellite-firm-supporting-houthi-attacks-us-interests |access-date=2025-06-09 |website=South China Morning Post}}</ref>
Under the directive published by the [[State Department of the United States|U.S. State Department]] in 2025, the [[Paramount leader|Chinese leader]] should be referred to as "[[General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party]]", rather than "[[President of China]]" reflecting the supremacy of the [[One-party state|party over the state]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Secretary of State Marco Rubio with Hugh Hewitt Interview|url=https://www.state.gov/secretary-of-state-marco-rubio-with-hugh-hewitt/ |work=[[United States Department of State]] |author=[[Marco Rubio]] & [[Hugh Hewitt]] |date=19 March 2025| quote=Yesterday, The Wall Street Journal reported that General Secretary Xi is upset about the deal you made in Panama about the canal...}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=VOA Exclusive: State Department guidance distinguishes CCP from Chinese people |url=https://www.voanews.com/a/voa-exclusive-state-department-guidance-distinguishes-ccp-from-chinese-people/7996193.html |work=[[Voice of America]] |date=3 March 2025}}</ref>{{Better source needed|reason=The current source is insufficiently reliable ([[WP:NOTRS]]).|date=March 2025}}
In June 2025, a majority of [[119th United States Congress|U.S. senators]] supported [[secondary sanctions]] against Russia over [[Vladimir Putin]]'s unwillingness to agree to a [[Peace negotiations in the Russian invasion of Ukraine|ceasefire]] in Ukraine, which would impose 500% [[Tariffs in the second Trump administration|tariffs]] on countries that buy [[Petroleum industry in Russia|Russian oil]], [[Natural gas in Russia|natural gas]], [[Mining industry of Russia|uranium]] and other exports. China is one of the major consumers of Russian energy.<ref>{{cite news |title=US Senate may work on Russia sanctions bill this month |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/us/us-senate-may-work-russia-sanctions-bill-this-month-2025-06-02/ |work=Reuters |date=2 June 2025}}</ref>
[[Liu Jianchao]], head of the [[International Department of the Chinese Communist Party]], criticized U.S. Defense Secretary [[Pete Hegseth]] during an address at the 13th World Peace Forum in [[Beijing]], accusing him of promoting "hegemonic thinking" and inciting confrontation. Liu's comments followed Hegseth's call for U.S. allies to strengthen their militaries to counter China and his warning that Beijing was preparing to use military force to alter the balance of power in Asia. Liu rejected these remarks, emphasized China's commitment to "peaceful reunification" with Taiwan, and stated that China would firmly oppose any moves toward Taiwanese independence. The exchange highlighted ongoing tensions between China and the United States over Taiwan, regional security, and broader geopolitical disputes.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-07-03 |title=Top China Official Says US Defence Chief 'Inciting Conflict' |url=https://ground.news/article/top-china-official-says-us-defence-chief-inciting-conflict |access-date=2025-07-04 |website=Ground News}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=AFP |date=2025-07-03 |title=Top China official accuses US defence chief of 'inciting conflict' |url=http://hongkongfp.com/2025/07/03/top-china-official-accuses-us-defence-chief-of-inciting-conflict/ |access-date=2025-07-04 |website=Hong Kong Free Press}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-07-03 |title=Top China official says US defence chief 'inciting conflict' |url=https://www.france24.com/en/live-news/20250703-top-china-official-says-us-defence-chief-inciting-conflict |access-date=2025-07-04 |website=France 24}}</ref>
In October 2025, Trump cited proximity to China's nuclear facilities for demanding Afghanistan the access to [[Bagram Airfield]] which was vacated in 2021.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Quillen |first=Stephen |title=Regional powers signal objection to US reclaiming Afghanistan's Bagram base |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/10/8/regional-powers-signal-objection-to-us-reclaiming-afghanistans-bagram-base |access-date=2025-10-13 |website=Al Jazeera}}</ref>
==== Espionage cases ==== During the second Trump administration, the United States saw a series of high-profile espionage and cyber espionage cases linked to the Chinese government. These cases, from the perspective of the United States, illustrate ongoing concerns over the activities of China's [[Ministry of State Security (China)|Ministry of State Security]] (MSS), including efforts to infiltrate U.S. institutions and conduct state-sponsored cyber operations.
