In March 2025, the U.S. intelligence community's Annual Threat Assessment identified China as the top military and cyber threat, citing its growing capabilities to seize Taiwan, conduct cyberattacks, and challenge U.S. dominance in AI and space. [[Director of National Intelligence]] [[Tulsi Gabbard]] called China the "most capable strategic competitor", while Beijing released a counter-report accusing the U.S. of long-term cyber espionage.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2025-03-25 |title=China remains top military and cyber threat to US, intelligence report says |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/mar/25/china-military-cyber-threat-us-intelligence |access-date=2025-08-22 |work=The Guardian |issn=0261-3077}}</ref>
===== Cyber espionage ===== In 2025, U.S. charged at least 12 hackers in the month of March alone. The hackers reportedly sold data of US-based dissidents to the Chinese government.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-03-06 |title=US charges Chinese hackers who targeted government dissidents |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cj4nw84dzr8o |access-date=2025-07-08 |website=BBC}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-03-05 |title=Office of Public Affairs {{!}} Justice Department Charges 12 Chinese Contract Hackers and Law Enforcement Officers in Global Computer Intrusion Campaigns |url=https://www.justice.gov/opa/pr/justice-department-charges-12-chinese-contract-hackers-and-law-enforcement-officers-global |access-date=2025-07-08 |website=US Department of Justice}}</ref>
In early March 2025, for example, the [[United States Department of Justice]] indicted Chinese nationals Yin Kecheng (尹可成) and Zhou Shuai (周帅) for cyberattacks linked to APT27 (also known as Silk Typhoon). Between 2011 and 2024, they allegedly hacked U.S. defense contractors, tech firms, and government agencies to steal data for profit and on behalf of China's Ministry of State Security (MSS) and Ministry of Public Security (MPS). Zhou also collected data on telecommunications, border activity, and individuals in media, civil service, and religion under MSS guidance. The case highlighted China's state-backed hacking-for-hire model. U.S. authorities seized related infrastructure and sanctioned Zhou, his company Shanghai Heiying, and previously Yin.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-03-05 |title=District of Columbia {{!}} Chinese Nationals with Ties to the PRC Government and 'APT27' Charged in a Computer Hacking Campaign for Profit, Targeting Numerous U.S. Companies, Institutions, and Municipalities |url=https://www.justice.gov/usao-dc/pr/chinese-nationals-ties-prc-government-and-apt27-charged-computer-hacking-campaign-profit |access-date=2025-07-08 |website=US Department of Justice}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite web |last=Kaaviya |date=2025-03-13 |title=US Charges 12 Chinese Hackers For Hacking National Security Infrastructure |url=https://cybersecuritynews.com/us-charges-12-chinese-hackers/ |access-date=2025-07-08 |website=Cyber Security News}}</ref>
In July 2025, the United States Department of Justice charged two Chinese nationals, Yuance Chen and Liren "Ryan" Lai, with attempting to recruit U.S. military personnel and gather naval intelligence on behalf of China's Ministry of State Security (MSS). According to the FBI, Lai cultivated Chen as an asset due to his contacts within the U.S. military, and both men met with MSS agents abroad to coordinate their activities. In 2022, they conducted a dead drop by leaving a backpack containing $10,000 in cash in a California locker as payment for intelligence. The charges, filed under the Foreign Agent Registration Act (FARA), highlight growing U.S. concerns over Chinese efforts to infiltrate military institutions and expand naval capabilities.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Two Chinese nationals charged for trying to recruit spies in US military |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/7/2/two-chinese-nationals-charged-for-trying-to-recruit-spies-in-us-military |access-date=2025-07-02 |website=Al Jazeera}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Martinez |first=Christian |date=2025-07-02 |title=US charges two Chinese nationals with attempting to recruit US service members |url=https://www.reuters.com/legal/litigation/us-charges-two-chinese-nationals-efforts-recruit-us-service-members-2025-07-01/ |access-date=2025-07-02 |work=Reuters}}</ref>
In October 2025, China's Ministry of State Security (MSS) accused the United States of conducting a large-scale cyberattack on China's National Time Service Center. According to the agency, the [[U.S. National Security Agency]] (NSA) had launched a series of hacking operations since March 2022, extracting sensitive data by exploiting vulnerabilities in the center's mobile phones and internal networks.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2025-10-19 |title=China accuses US of cyber breaches at national time centre |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/china-accuses-us-cyber-breaches-national-time-centre-2025-10-19/ |access-date=2025-10-20 |work=Reuters}}</ref> According to Chinese state media, more than 42 specialized tools and pieces of malware were used during the operation, and attempts were also made to access the "Precise Ground Timing" system. China's Ministry of National Security warned that intrusions into such infrastructure could disrupt communications, financial systems, energy, transportation, and space launches.<ref>{{Cite news |title=China accuses US of cyberattack on national time center |url=https://apnews.com/article/china-us-cyberattacks-allegations-time-b3408ed2352c113904350f80e505ab9f |access-date=2025-10-20 |work=AP News}}</ref>
===== Military espionage ===== In July 2025, Chenguang Gong, a dual U.S.-China citizen and former engineer at a California defense contractor, pleaded guilty to stealing over 3,600 sensitive files related to U.S. missile detection and defense systems. Prosecutors stated he had long sought involvement in Chinese government Talent Programs and attempted to transfer military-grade technology to China.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-07-23 |title=China spy stole secret U.S. missile tech: DOJ |url=https://www.newsweek.com/china-spy-stole-secret-us-missile-tech-says-department-justice-2102724 |access-date=2025-08-22 |website=Newsweek}}</ref>
==== 2025 trade war ==== {{See also|Tariffs in the second Trump administration}} In April 2025, China imposed a 34% tariff on all U.S. imports, matching the rate introduced by [[Donald Trump|President Donald Trump]] earlier that week. The move came after two rounds of U.S. tariffs; each 10%; were implemented in February and March, citing China's alleged role in the fentanyl crisis. In retaliation, China also restricted exports of [[rare-earth element]]s,<ref>{{cite news |title=Why China curbing rare earth exports is a blow to the US |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c1drqeev36qo |work=BBC News |date=17 April 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=FACTBOX What strategic mineral exports has China restricted? |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/chinas-curbs-exports-strategic-minerals-2025-02-04/ |work=Reuters |date=4 April 2025}}</ref> filed a complaint with the [[World Trade Organization]], and blacklisted several U.S. firms. Additional measures included suspending imports from American agricultural and food producers and launching an anti-monopoly probe into [[DuPont|DuPont China]]. Analysts noted this marked a significant escalation in the ongoing trade tensions, reducing prospects for near-term diplomatic resolution.<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 April 2025 |title=China slaps a 34% tax on all US imports in retaliation for Trump's tariffs |url=https://apnews.com/article/china-tariff-trade-trump-earths-02cd23e913649f32985f075058e787c4 |access-date=8 April 2025 |website=AP News}}</ref> Trump threatened to impose an additional 50% tariff on Chinese goods on 9 April if China did not withdraw its retaliatory measure of a 34% tariff on all U.S. goods by 8 April. This would boost the effective 54% tariffs on China on 9 April to 104%.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Trump tariffs live updates: Trump tells Americans not to panic as stock markets open to big losses |url=https://abcnews.go.com/US/live-updates/trump-tariffs-live-updates-us-stronger-despite-market/?id=120551033 |access-date=7 April 2025 |website=ABC News}}</ref> China responded with retaliatory tariffs of 84% on U.S. goods.<ref>{{Cite web |title=China slaps retaliatory tariffs of 84% on U.S. goods in response to Trump |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2025/04/09/china-slaps-retaliatory-tariffs-of-84percent-on-us-goods-in-response-to-trump.html |access-date=9 April 2025 |website=CNBC |date=9 April 2025}}</ref> In response, Trump increased tariffs on Chinese goods to 125% on the same day. However, the White House clarified the next day that the tariff rate had risen to 145%.<ref name=":12">{{cite news |last1=Iordache |first1=Dan Mangan, Kevin Breuninger, Christina Wilkie,Megan Cassella,Jesse Pound,Ruxandra |date=10 April 2025 |title=Trump tariffs on China now total 145%, White House clarifies: Live updates |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2025/04/10/china-trump-tariffs-live-updates.html |work=CNBC}}</ref> China, in retaliation, announced an increase in tariffs on all American imports from the previous 84% to 125%, set to take effect on 12 April.<ref>{{Cite news |date=11 April 2025 |title=Breaking: China to increase tariffs on U.S. goods to 125 per cent |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-04-11/china-to-increase-us-tariffs-to-125-per-cent/105168174 |access-date=11 April 2025 |work=ABC News}}</ref> The U.S. later announced reciprocal tariffs will exclude consumer electronics from tariffs from most countries, but retained a 20% tariff on electronics from China.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Garrett |first=Luke |date=13 April 2025 |title=Trump mulls semiconductor levies after lifting reciprocal tariffs on electronics |url=https://www.npr.org/2025/04/13/g-s1-60086/semiconduct-tariffs-trump |access-date=14 April 2025 |work=NPR}}</ref>
On 10 May 2025, Trump declared a "total reset" in U.S.-China trade relations after tariff talks in [[Geneva]]. According to transcripts, witnesses saw both delegations returning after a lunch break to an UN ambassador's villa in Cologny.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/may/11/trump-claims-total-reset-in-us-china-trade-relations-after-tariff-talks-in-geneva "Trump claims 'total reset' in US-China trade relations after tariff talks in Geneva"]. ''The Guardian''. Accessed 10 May 2025.</ref> On 12 May 2025, the United States and China agreed to for a period of 90 days, reduce their tariff rates from 145% to 30% and 125% to 10% respectively by 14 May, while further negotiations take place.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Gan |first1=Nectar |last2=Bacon |first2=Auzinea |last3=Liu |first3=Juliana |date=12 May 2025 |title=US and China agree to drastically roll back tariffs |url=https://www.cnn.com/2025/05/12/business/us-china-trade-deal-announcement-intl-hnk |access-date=12 May 2025 |website=CNN}}</ref> Treasury Secretary [[Scott Bessent]] stated that the "somewhat stalled" U.S.-China trade talks necessitate direct communication between the leaders of both countries, especially as the U.S. implements technology restrictions on China.<ref>{{cite web |last1=cheng |first1=Evelyne |title=U.S.-China talks 'a bit stalled' and need Trump and Xi to weigh in, Treasury Secretary Bessent says |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2025/05/30/us-china-talks-bit-stalled-and-need-trump-and-xi-to-weigh-in-treasury-secretary-bessent-says.html |website=cnbc}}</ref>
In July 2025, [[Bloomberg News]] reported that U.S. President [[Donald Trump]] has softened his hardline tone on China to ensure a summit with [[General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party]] [[Xi Jinping]], aiming to push for a trade agreement between America and China.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Trump Softens Tone on China to Secure Xi Summit, Trade Deal|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-07-16/trump-softens-tone-on-china-to-secure-xi-summit-and-a-trade-deal|work=[[Bloomberg News|Bloomberg]]|date=July 16, 2025|accessdate=July 16, 2025}}</ref>
==Economic relations== As part of [[Foreign-exchange reserves of China|China's foreign-exchange reserves]], China has a preference for dollar-denominated assets, including [[United States Treasury security|United States treasury securities]].<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|pages=44–45}} In turn, the United States benefits because it lacks the [[National saving|domestic savings]] to fund its [[National debt of the United States|budget deficit]].<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|page=44}} After the [[2008 financial crisis]], Chinese policymakers and the general public viewed China's holdings of U.S. debt as unwisely overexposing China to volatility.<ref name="Liu-2023">{{Cite book|last=Liu|first=Zongyuan Zoe|title=Sovereign Funds: How the Communist Party of China Finances its Global Ambitions|publisher=The Belknap Press of [[Harvard University Press]]|year=2023|isbn=9780674271913|doi=10.2307/jj.2915805|jstor=jj.2915805|s2cid=259402050}}</ref>{{Rp|pages=61–62}} China remains a major holder of United States treasury securities, although the amount has decreased as of at least 2022.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Cox|first=Jeff|date=18 July 2022|title=China holdings of U.S. debt fall below $1 trillion for the first time since 2010|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2022/07/18/china-holdings-of-us-debt-fall-below-1-trillion-for-the-first-time-since-2010.html|access-date=13 February 2023|website=CNBC}}</ref>[[File:US China imports and exports Census data.svg|thumb|Imports and exports between the United States and China. U.S. census.gov data]] [[File:Foreign Holders of United States Treasury Securities.svg|thumb|Foreign holders of [[United States Treasury security|U.S. Treasury securities]] (2009)]]
In trade matters, the United States has benefitted from China's demand for United States export products, which grew rapidly from 2000 to at least 2021.<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|page=44}} As of 2021, China was the third largest market for United States export merchandise.<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|page=44}} Inexpensive Chinese exports to the United States increase the purchasing power of American consumers and American business profits.<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|page=44}} Both countries are benefitted by the demand for their respective exports to the other.<ref name="Roach-2022" />{{Rp|pages=44–45}}
In 1991, China only accounted for 1% of total imports to the United States.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://voxeu.org/article/reconsidering-china-shock-trade|title=Reconsidering the 'China shock' in trade|last1=Feenstra|first1=Robert|last2=Ma|first2=Hong|date=18 January 2018|website=VoxEU.org|last3=Sasahara|first3=Akira|last4=Xu|first4=Yuan}}</ref> For many years, China was the most important country which required an annual waiver to maintain [[free trade]] status. The waiver for the PRC had been in effect since 1980. Every year between 1989 and 1999, legislation was introduced in Congress to disapprove the President's waiver. The legislation had sought to tie free trade with China to meeting certain [[Human rights in China|human rights]] conditions that go beyond freedom of emigration. All such attempted legislation failed to pass. The requirement of an annual waiver was inconsistent with the rules of the [[World Trade Organization]] (WTO), and for the PRC to join the WTO, congressional action was needed to grant [[permanent normal trade relations]] (PNTR) to China. This was accomplished in 2000 ([[United States–China Relations Act of 2000]]), allowing China to join WTO in 2001.<ref>{{cite news|title=Waking the Sleeping Dragon|url=https://slate.com/business/2016/09/when-china-joined-the-wto-it-kick-started-the-chinese-economy-and-roused-a-giant.html?via=gdpr-consent|work=Slate|date=28 September 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Was Letting China Into the WTO a Mistake?|url=https://www.foreignaffairs.com/articles/china/2018-04-02/was-letting-china-wto-mistake|work=Foreign Affairs|date=2 April 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Normalizing Trade Relations With China Was a Mistake|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/ideas/archive/2018/06/normalizing-trade-relations-with-china-was-a-mistake/562403/|work=The Atlantic|date=8 June 2018}}</ref> China's [[most favoured nation]] (MFN) status was made permanent on 27 December 2001.<ref>{{cite news|title=President Grants Permanent Trade Status to China|url=https://georgewbush-whitehouse.archives.gov/news/releases/2001/12/20011227-2.html|via=[[NARA|National Archives]]|work=[[whitehouse.gov]]|date=27 December 2001}}</ref>
Since the [[China and the World Trade Organization|entry of China into the WTO in December 2001]], the decline in [[Manufacturing in the United States|U.S. manufacturing jobs]] has accelerated (the [[China shock]]).<ref>{{cite journal|first1=David H.|last1=Autor|first2=David|last2=Dorn|first3=Gordon H.|last3=Hanson|year=2016|title=The China Shock: Learning from Labor Market Adjustment to Large Changes in Trade|journal=Annual Review of Economics|volume=8|issue=1|pages=205–240|doi=10.1146/annurev-economics-080315-015041|s2cid=1415485|url=http://www.nber.org/papers/w21906.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|first=Jeffry|last=Bartash|title=China really is to blame for millions of lost U.S. manufacturing jobs, new study finds|url=https://www.marketwatch.com/story/china-really-is-to-blame-for-millions-of-lost-us-manufacturing-jobs-new-study-finds-2018-05-14|work=Market Watch|date=14 May 2018}}</ref> The [[Economic Policy Institute]] estimated that the trade deficit with China cost about 2.7 million jobs between 2001 and 2011, including manufacturing and other industries.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.epi.org/publication/bp345-china-growing-trade-deficit-cost/|title=The China toll: Growing U.S. trade deficit with China cost more than 2.7 million jobs between 2001 and 2011, with job losses in every state|website=Economic Policy Institute}}</ref>
The PRC and the U.S. resumed trade relations in 1972 and 1973. Direct investment by the U.S. in mainland China covers a wide range of manufacturing sectors, several large hotel projects, restaurant chains, and [[petrochemical]]s. U.S. companies have entered agreements establishing more than 20,000 equity [[joint venture]]s, contractual joint ventures, and wholly foreign-owned enterprises in mainland China. More than 100 US-based multinationals have projects in mainland China, some with multiple investments. Cumulative U.S. investment in mainland China is valued at $48 billion. The U.S. trade deficit with mainland China exceeded $350 billion in 2006 and was the United States' largest bilateral trade deficit.<ref>Graph showing U.S.-China trade [http://china.usc.edu/App_Images//trade-1978-2008-lgweb.jpg] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110813132416/http://china.usc.edu/App_Images//trade-1978-2008-lgweb.jpg|date=13 August 2011}}; source [http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1512]</ref> Some of the factors that influence the U.S. trade deficit with mainland China include:
*a shift of low-end assembly industries from [[newly industrialized country|newly industrialized countries]] in [[East Asia]] and the [[Asia-Pacific]] to mainland China. Mainland China has increasingly become the last link in a long [[global value chain|chain of value-added production]]. Because U.S. trade data attributes the full value of a product to the final assembler, mainland Chinese value added is overcounted. Using a statistical model that eliminates these global value chain-related distortions, [[OECD|Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development]] (OECD) and WTO researchers have concluded that the United States' measures may overstate the value of Chinese exports by as much as 35%.<ref name="Roach-2022">{{Cite book|last=Roach|first=Stephen|url=|title=Accidental Conflict: America, China, and the Clash of False Narratives|date=2022|publisher=[[Yale University Press]]|isbn=978-0-300-26901-7|doi=10.2307/j.ctv2z0vv2v|jstor=j.ctv2z0vv2v|s2cid=252800309|author-link=Stephen S. Roach}}</ref>{{Rp|page=39}} According to [[Pascal Lamy]]: "The statistical bias created by attributing commercial value to the last country of origin perverts the true economic dimension of the bilateral trade imbalances. This affects the political debate, and leads to misguided perceptions. Take the bilateral deficit between China and the US. A series of estimates based on true domestic content can cut the overall deficit – which was $252bn in November 2010 – by half, if not more."<ref name="MiC">{{cite news|last=Lamy|first=Pascal|date=24 January 2011|title='Made in China' tells us little about global trade|newspaper=Financial Times |url=https://www.ft.com/content/4d37374c-27fd-11e0-8abc-00144feab49a|url-status=live|url-access=subscription|access-date=7 July 2012|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221210/http://www.ft.com/cms/s/0/4d37374c-27fd-11e0-8abc-00144feab49a.html|archive-date=10 December 2022}}</ref> *US demand for labor-intensive goods exceeds domestic output: the PRC has restrictive trade practices in mainland China, which include a wide array of barriers to foreign goods and services, often aimed at protecting [[state-owned enterprise]]s. These practices include high [[tariff]]s, lack of transparency, requiring firms to obtain special permission to import goods, inconsistent application of laws and regulations, and leveraging technology from foreign firms in return for market access. Mainland China's accession to the WTO is meant to help address these barriers. *the undervaluation of the [[Renminbi]] relative to the [[United States dollar]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.imf.org/external/pubs/ft/weo/2007/01/data/weorept.aspx?sy=2004&ey=2007&sort=country&ds=.&br=1&c=924&s=PPPEX&grp=0&a=&pr1.x=62&pr1.y=9|title=World Economic Outlook Database, April 2007|publisher=Imf.org|date=14 September 2006|access-date=2 December 2010}}</ref>
Beginning in 2009, the U.S. and China agreed to hold regular high-level talks about economic issues and other mutual concerns by establishing the [[Strategic Economic Dialogue]], which meets biannually. Five meetings have been held, the most recent in December 2008. Economic nationalism seems to be rising in both countries, a point the leaders of the two delegations noted in their opening presentations.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://china.usc.edu/wu-yi-opens-third-china-us-strategic-economic-dialogue-2007|title=Wu Yi opens the Third China-US Strategic Economic Dialogue, 2007 {{pipe}} US-China Institute|website=china.usc.edu}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://china.usc.edu/us-treasury-sec-henry-paulson-opens-strategic-economic-dialogue-dec-2007|title=U.S. Treasury Sec. Henry Paulson opens Strategic Economic Dialogue, Dec. 2007 {{pipe}} US-China Institute|website=china.usc.edu}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://china.usc.edu/tensions-over-trade-part-2-election-08-and-challenge-china|title=Tensions over Trade: Part 2 of Election '08 and the Challenge of China {{pipe}} US-China Institute|website=china.usc.edu}}</ref> The United States and China have also established the high-level U.S.-China [[Senior Dialogue]] to discuss international political issues and work out resolutions.
[[File:United States Balance of Trade Deficit-pie chart.svg|thumb|United States trade deficit]] In September 2009 a trade dispute emerged between the United States and China, which came after the U.S. imposed tariffs of 35 percent on Chinese tire imports. The Chinese commerce minister accused the United States of a "grave act of trade [[protectionism]]",<ref name="businessweek1">{{cite web|date=13 September 2010|title=Obama's Tire Tariff Draws Beijing's Ire|url=http://www.businessweek.com/globalbiz/content/sep2009/gb20090913_714101.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090922172106/http://www.businessweek.com/globalbiz/content/sep2009/gb20090913_714101.htm|url-status=dead|archive-date=22 September 2009|website=[[Bloomberg Businessweek]]}}</ref> while an [[Office of the United States Trade Representative|USTR]] spokesperson said the tariff "was taken precisely in accordance with the law and our international trade agreements".<ref name="businessweek1"/> Additional issues were raised by both sides in subsequent months.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1600|title=Statistics on world trade, list of U.S.-China WTO complaints|publisher=China.usc.edu|access-date=2 December 2010}}</ref><ref>China slaps deposits/tariffs on US steel exports; US politicians rant about China [http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1920]</ref>
When a country joins the [[World Trade Organization]] it commits to keep its tariffs below the [[bound tariff rate|bound rate]], which is generally around 39 percent. China's reaction was attributed to the fact that nations usually keep their tariffs at an average of 9 percent, but when the U.S. raised its tariff on Chinese imported tires to 35 percent it was still below the average bound rate.<ref name="Trade grows as Tariffs decline">{{cite web|url=https://www.wto.org/english/thewto_e/20y_e/wto_20_brochure_e.pdf|title=Trade and Tariffs|publisher=World Trade Organization|date=2015}}</ref>
[[File:U.S.-China Strategic and Economic Dialogue (3767084375).jpg|thumb|The first round of the [[U.S.–China Strategic and Economic Dialogue]] was held in Washington, D.C., from 27 to 28 July 2009]] [[File:Countries by total wealth (trillions USD), Credit Suisse.png|thumb|Countries by total [[wealth]] (trillions USD), Credit Suisse]]
In early 2012, a [[Rare earths trade dispute|dispute over rare earth minerals]] was brought into the light between the two countries. [[Barack Obama|President Obama]] made an announcement that the United States would be one of a few countries to file a trade dispute with China. Amongst the United States, Japan and other Western European countries would also be filing disputes as well. This is simply just one of few disputes between the United States and China. It is believed by many experts, including Chris Isidore, a writer for CNN Money, that "any one of the disputes could damage the economies of both countries as well as the relationship between them".<ref name="Isidore">{{Cite news|url=https://money.cnn.com/2012/03/13/news/international/china-trade/|title=U.S. vs. China: The trade battles|last=Isidore|first=Chris|date=13 March 2012|work=CNN Money}}</ref> The dispute was filed, and China was charged with putting unfair restrictions on the exportation of rare earth minerals. These minerals were crucial and in high demand by all countries. President Obama believed the United States should have those minerals in the United States whereas China disagreed. China denied all of the said charges brought forth "saying its rules are defensible on grounds of environmental and economic sustainability, and suggests there would be consequences if the United States presses the case." It is important to understand the relationship between the United States and China, especially economically. There is not one without the other. China's state news agency commented that "past experiences have shown that policymakers in Washington should treat such issues with more prudence, because maintaining sound Sino-American trade relations is in the fundamental interests of both sides"<ref name="Isidore"/>
China was the biggest trading partner of the United States until 2019, when it dropped to the third place because of the ongoing [[China–United States trade war|trade war]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/china-lost-spot-as-top-u-s-trading-partner-in-first-half-11564749251|title=Tariff Fight Knocks Off China as Top U.S. Trading Partner|last=DeBarros|first=Paul Kiernan and Anthony|newspaper=The Wall Street Journal|date=2 August 2019 |access-date=6 August 2019}}</ref>
In November 2021, U.S. producer Venture Global LNG signed a twenty-year contract with China's state-owned [[Sinopec]] to supply [[liquefied natural gas]] (LNG).<ref>{{cite news|title=Sinopec signs China's largest long-term LNG contract with U.S. firm|url=https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/sinopec-signs-20-yr-lng-contract-with-us-venture-global-lng-2021-11-04/|work=Reuters|date=4 November 2021}}</ref> China's imports of U.S. natural gas will more than double.<ref>{{cite news|title=Sinopec signs huge LNG deals with US producer Venture Global|url=https://www.ft.com/content/00fdc70e-1482-4ce6-ba43-a802550838d4|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221210/https://www.ft.com/content/00fdc70e-1482-4ce6-ba43-a802550838d4|archive-date=10 December 2022|url-access=subscription|url-status=live|work=Financial Times|date=20 October 2021}}</ref> U.S. exports of liquefied natural gas to China and other Continental Asian countries [[2021 global energy crisis|surged in 2021]], with Continental Asian buyers willing to pay higher prices than European importers.<ref>{{cite news|title=Asian buyers outbid Europe for spot supplies of American-produced natural gas|url=https://www.ft.com/content/deaa7583-e69f-4a5f-9486-cc05c710a51a|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221210/https://www.ft.com/content/deaa7583-e69f-4a5f-9486-cc05c710a51a|archive-date=10 December 2022|url-access=subscription|work=Financial Times|date=21 September 2021}}</ref>
On 25 March 2023, Apple CEO [[Tim Cook]] made an official visit to Beijing to attend the [[China Development Forum]]. Cook praised China's innovation, and long history of cooperation with [[Apple Inc]]. During the [[COVID-19 pandemic]], Apple supplier [[Foxconn]] was heavily disrupted by workers protesting China's zero-COVID policies. Cook also presented an expanded rural education program for 100 million [[yuan (currency)|yuan]] to further improve the skill-set of Chinese workers.<ref>[https://www.reuters.com/technology/apple-ceo-praises-chinas-innovation-long-history-cooperation-beijing-visit-2023-03-25/ "Apple CEO praises China's innovation, long history of cooperation on Beijing visit"]. ''Reuters''. Accessed 25 March 2023.</ref>
Between 2020 and 2023, 70% of all [[Rare earth industry in China|rare earth]] compounds and metals imported into the United States came from China.<ref>{{cite news |title=China has a powerful card to play in its fight against Trump's trade war |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2025/04/15/business/china-trumps-trade-war-rare-earth-intl-hnk/index.html |work=CNN |date=15 April 2025}}</ref> China produces 90% of the world's [[rare-earth element]]s.<ref>{{cite news |title=China's dominance leaves US vulnerable, as critical minerals become political |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-04-25/china-dominates-rare-earths-leaving-trump-with-dud-hand/105213972 |work=ABC |date=25 April 2025}}</ref> In this context, in July 2025, Raja Krishnamoorthi, the top Democrat on the U.S. House Select Committee on the Chinese Communist Party, stated that China could "so much as flick a switch and cause major damage" to the American economy. The committee expressed concerns about the United States' economic dependence on China, particularly in relation to critical mineral exports such as rare earths used in modern technology.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2025-07-23 |title=Beijing punished Australia's loyalty to US, Scott Morrison tells Congress |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-07-24/scott-morrison-us-congress-select-committee-china-aukus/105565762 |access-date=2025-07-24 |work=ABC News}}</ref>
On 13 September 2024, the Biden administration finalized the increases of [[Economic policy of the Joe Biden administration|tariffs on Chinese exports]]. Tariffs increased to 100% on [[Electric vehicle industry in China|electric vehicle]]s, 50% on [[solar cell]]s and 25% on [[Electric vehicle battery|electric vehicle batteries]], critical minerals, steel, and aluminum.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lobosco |first=Katie |date=13 September 2024 |title=Biden finalizes increases to China tariffs |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2024/09/13/politics/china-tariffs-biden-trump/index.html |access-date=11 December 2024 |website=CNN |archive-date=12 December 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241212111044/https://edition.cnn.com/2024/09/13/politics/china-tariffs-biden-trump/index.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
In February 2025, Donald Trump signed an executive order that directed the [[Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States]] (CFIUS) to restrict Chinese investment in strategic economic areas.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Holland |first=Steve |date=22 February 2025 |title=Trump orders use of CFIUS to restrict Chinese investments in strategic areas |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/trump-use-cfius-restrict-chinese-investments-strategic-areas-white-house-2025-02-21/ |access-date=22 February 2025 |work=[[Reuters]]}}</ref> Premier [[Li Qiang]] emphasized the need for open markets and fair competition, while the U.S. accused China of unfair trade practices and inadequate fentanyl control efforts.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 March 2025 |title=China's premier says US relations at 'an important juncture' during meeting with pro-Trump senator |url=https://apnews.com/article/china-us-daines-li-qiang-tariff-fentanyl-321538fba10eb1c4d04f6146a4eb06b6 |access-date=25 March 2025 |website=AP News}}</ref>
===Currency dispute=== {{Main|Renminbi currency value|Internationalization of the renminbi}}
China engaged in [[currency manipulation]]<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Skaff|first1=Rebecca|last2=Lincoln|first2=Webb|date=March 2018|title=Understanding China's Currency Manipulation|url=https://hdl.handle.net/1969.1/166316|journal=The Takeaway|publisher=[[Bush School of Government and Public Service#Mosbacher Institute for Trade, Economics, and Public Policy|The Mosbacher Institute for Trade, Economics, and Public Policy at Texas A&M University Bush School of Government and Public Service]]|volume=9|issue=1|hdl=1969.1/166316|via=OAKTrust}}</ref> from 2003 to 2014.<ref name="Bergsten 2022">{{Cite book|last=Bergsten|first=C. Fred|title=The United States vs. China : the quest for global economic leadership|date=2022|isbn=978-1-5095-4735-7|location=Cambridge|oclc=1255691875}}</ref> Economist [[C. Fred Bergsten]], writing for the [[Peterson Institute for International Economics]], said that, during this period, "China bought more than $300 billion annually to resist upward movement of its currency by artificially keeping the exchange rate of the dollar strong and the renminbi's exchange rate weak. China's competitive position was thus strengthened by as much as 30 to 40 percent at the peak of the intervention. Currency manipulation explained most of China's large trade surpluses, which reached a staggering 10 percent of its entire GDP in 2007."<ref name="Bergsten">C. Fred Bergsten, [https://piie.com/blogs/trade-investment-policy-watch/china-no-longer-manipulating-its-currency China is No Longer Manipulating its Currency], [[Peterson Institute for International Economics]] (18 November 2016).</ref> China's currency manipulation was a point of conflict with the United States. Domestic leaders within the United States pressured the Obama administration to take a hardline stance against China and compel China to raise the value of its currency, and legislation was introduced to the [[United States Congress]] calling on the President to impose tariffs on Chinese imports until China properly values its currency.<ref name="businessweek1"/><ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.reuters.com/article/idUSTRE68L5K120100924| work=Reuters| title=House panel cranks up pressure on China currency| date=24 September 2010}}</ref> Nonetheless, the United States was not willing to label China as a "currency manipulator" at that time, on the theory that doing so would risk China's cooperation on other issues.<ref name="Bergsten 2022"/>
In 2014, China stopped manipulating its currency,<ref name="Bergsten"/><ref name="Wiseman">Paul Wiseman, [https://www.pbs.org/newshour/rundown/fact-check-china-manipulate-currency/ Fact check: Does China manipulate its currency?], Associated Press (29 December 2016).</ref> as the growth in the Chinese economy slowed and Chinese investors made more investments outside the country, leading to a drop in the yuan's value in relation to the dollar, as well as a decline in China's reserves.<ref name="Wiseman"/>
In August 2019, five years after China had stopped manipulating its currency,<ref name="Bergsten 2022"/> the [[United States Department of the Treasury|US Treasury]] designated China as a currency manipulator.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trade-china-currency-idUSKCN1UV2BK|title=U.S. designates China as currency manipulator for first time in...|date=5 August 2019|work=Reuters|access-date=6 August 2019}}</ref> Some U.S. analysts characterized the belated designation as "embarrassing", "without factual basis", or "a stretch".<ref name="Bergsten 2022"/><ref>{{Cite web|title=The irony of calling China a currency manipulator|url=https://www.yahoo.com/now/the-irony-of-calling-china-a-currency-manipulator-152537290.html|access-date=17 June 2022|website=Yahoo|date=6 August 2019}}</ref> On 13 January 2020, the United States removed the designation as part of the Phase One<ref>{{Cite web|title=Phase One|url=http://ustr.gov/phase-one|access-date=17 June 2022|website=United States Trade Representative}}</ref> efforts to reach a deal on the trade war.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/01/13/us/politics/treasury-china-currency-manipulator-trade.html|title=U.S. Says China Is No Longer a Currency Manipulator|last=Rappeport|first=Alan|date=13 January 2020|work=The New York Times|access-date=20 January 2020|issn=0362-4331}}</ref>
=== 2025 tensions === {{See also|Rare earths trade dispute}} [[File:Global rare-earth element deposits.webp|thumb|Global [[rare-earth element]] deposits]] On October 10, 2025, U.S. President Donald Trump announced that a 100% tariff on goods imported from China would take effect on November 1. This decision was viewed as part of an escalation in trade tensions between the two countries.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Trump ratchets up US-China trade war, promising new tariffs |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/trump-says-weighing-massive-increase-tariffs-chinese-imports-no-reason-meet-with-2025-10-10 |access-date=11 October 2025 |website=Reuters}}</ref>
On October 12, 2025, China's Ministry of Commerce responded to the Trump administration's new tariffs by calling them "hypocritical" and defending Beijing's decision to restrict exports of rare earth elements. The ministry stated that China's export controls were a response to a series of measures by Washington, including adding Chinese companies to a trade blacklist and imposing port fees on Chinese ships. This incident is the latest example of escalating tensions in economic and trade relations between China and the United States.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Beijing blames US for raising trade tensions, defends rare earth curbs |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/china/china-says-its-rare-earth-export-controls-are-legitimate-2025-10-12 |access-date=12 October 2025 |website=Reuters}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=China vows to stand firm against Trump's 100% tariff threat |url=https://apnews.com/article/china-us-trump-tariff-threat-trade-talks-cc4bd30c3b1bcf2eb2676bc0e66efba0 |access-date=12 October 2025 |website=AP News}}</ref> Trump issued a warning regarding the possibility of canceling a forthcoming meeting with Xi Jinping in South Korea later this month, which is scheduled to take place during the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC) summit. In a telephone conversation that occurred in October 2025 between Vice Premier He Lifeng and US Treasury Secretary Scott Besant, both parties reached an agreement to initiate a new series of trade discussions "at the earliest opportunity."<ref>{{cite web |title=China and US agree to fresh trade talks |url=https://www.france24.com/en/live-news/20251018-china-and-us-agree-to-fresh-trade-talks |website=france24}}</ref>
==Important issues== Relations between the two world powers have historically been stable, punctuated by several periods of open conflict, most notably during the [[Korean War]] and the [[Vietnam War]]. The United States and China have mutual environmental, political, economic, and security interests, such as [[climate change]] and the [[nuclear proliferation|non-proliferation of nuclear weapons]], but there remain perennial concerns, such as [[human rights in China]], as well as [[cross-strait relations]] and the US's attitude towards the [[One China|One China policy]]. China is the second largest foreign creditor of the United States, after [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.scmp.com/business/companies/article/3023034/japan-overtakes-china-biggest-creditor-us-japans-june-treasuries|title=Japan surpasses China as the biggest creditor to the US government|date=16 August 2019|website=South China Morning Post |access-date=18 August 2019}}</ref> [[Chinese expansionism|China's expansion]] in the [[Indo-Pacific]] has triggered pushback from the U.S. and its partners in the region. The two countries remain in dispute over [[South China Sea disputes|territorial issues in the South China Sea]];<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2016/07/15/world/asia/south-china-sea-dispute-arbitration-explained.html|title=The South China Sea: Explaining the Dispute|last=Fisher|first=Max|date=14 July 2016|work=The New York Times|access-date=11 March 2017|issn=0362-4331}}</ref> China claims sovereignty over large swaths of the [[South China Sea]]; the United States instead sees much of this ocean as [[international waters]], and as such claims [[Freedom of navigation|the right]] of its warships and aircraft to conduct military operations within them.<ref name="wsj._China's_Claims">{{Cite news|title=China's Claims in the South China Sea – The Short Answer|last1=Page|first1=Jeremy|last2=Moss|first2=Trefor|work=The Wall Street Journal|date=30 November 1999|access-date=30 July 2021|url= https://www.wsj.com/articles/BL-263B-4730}}</ref><ref name="apne_China_US">{{Cite news|title=China, US argue over naval activity in South China Sea|work=[[Associated Press]]|date=20 May 2021|access-date=30 July 2021|url= https://apnews.com/article/asia-pacific-china-b20b3b72604728c2e3f7498e2f492cac}}</ref>
=== Military issues ===
==== China's military expenditure and planning ==== {{Main|Military budget of China|Military budget of the United States|United States–China security cooperation}}{{Update|part=section|date=May 2023}} China's investment in its military is growing rapidly; the United States remains convinced that the PRC conceals the real extent of this expansion, a view shared by many independent analysts.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://hongkong.usconsulate.gov/uscn/others/2004/060601.htm|title= Defense Secretary Rumsfeld's Remarks at the International Institute for Strategic Studies, Singapore, 6/6/2004|website=hongkong.usconsulate.gov|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20041206021659/http://hongkong.usconsulate.gov/uscn/others/2004/060601.htm|archive-date=6 December 2004}}</ref><ref>{{cite news| url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/asia-pacific/4351422.stm| work=BBC News| title=Rumsfeld questions China spending| date=18 October 2005| access-date=7 April 2010}}</ref> China claims it spent a total of $45 billion over the course of 2007, an average of $123 million per day.<ref name="china.usc.edu">{{cite web|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=586|title=china's military spends hundreds of millions of dollars|publisher=China.usc.edu|access-date=2 December 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=586&AspxAutoDetectCookieSupport=1|title=china's military spends hundreds of millions of dollars|publisher=China.usc.edu|access-date=2 December 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090202163232/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=586&AspxAutoDetectCookieSupport=1|archive-date=2 February 2009}}</ref> That same year, the U.S. military spent $548.8 billion, or an average $1.66 billion per day. U.S. estimates of Chinese military expenditure{{when|date=September 2023}} range between $85 billion and $125 billion.
[[File:Xu and Gates.JPG|thumb|General [[Xu Caihou]] of the People's Liberation Army and U.S. Defense Secretary [[Robert Gates]] at the Pentagon]] Concerns over the Chinese military budget may come from U.S. worries that the PRC is attempting to threaten its neighbors or to challenge the United States. Concerns have been raised that China is developing a large naval base near the South China Sea and has diverted resources from the [[People's Liberation Army Ground Force]] to the [[People's Liberation Army Navy]] and to air force and missile development.<ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/majornews/1917167/Chinese-build-secret-nuclear-submarine-base.html| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080504060556/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/majornews/1917167/Chinese-build-secret-nuclear-submarine-base.html| url-status=dead| archive-date=4 May 2008| work=The Daily Telegraph| location=London| title=Chinese nuclear submarine base| first=Thomas| last=Harding| date=1 May 2008| access-date=7 April 2010}}</ref><ref name="china.usc.edu"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.sipri.org/contents/milap/milex/mex_database1.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061129014345/http://www.sipri.org/contents/milap/milex/mex_database1.html|title=SIPRI military expenditure database|archive-date=29 November 2006}}</ref>
On 27 October 2009, U.S. Defense Secretary [[Robert Gates]] praised the steps China has taken to increase transparency of defense spending.<ref>{{cite web|last=Luce|first=Dan De|url=https://www.google.com/hostednews/afp/article/ALeqM5iN273FI9Ieez9xh4hwmwO6hwIG2w|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130201200925/http://www.google.com/hostednews/afp/article/ALeqM5iN273FI9Ieez9xh4hwmwO6hwIG2w|url-status=dead|archive-date=1 February 2013|title=Time to end 'on-again-off-again' US-China ties: Pentagon|date=26 October 2009|access-date=2 December 2010}}</ref> In June 2010, however, he said that the Chinese military was resisting efforts to improve military-to-military relations with the United States.<ref>{{cite news|last=Entous|first=Adam|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/idUSTRE65220220100603?feedType=RSS&feedName=topNews|title=Gates says China's PLA may be trying to thwart ties|publisher=Reuters |date= 3 June 2010|access-date=2 December 2010}}</ref> Gates also said that the United States would "assert freedom of navigation" in response to Chinese complaints about [[US Navy]] deployments in international waters near China.{{citation needed|date=February 2023}} Admiral [[Michael Mullen]] said that the United States sought closer military ties to China but would continue to operate in the western [[Pacific Ocean|Pacific]].<ref>Pessin, Al. [https://www.voanews.com/a/us-wants-better-military-ties-to-china-but-will-continue-pacific-operations--111149499/166716.html "US Wants Better Military Ties to China, But Will Continue Pacific Operations."] ''[[Voice of America]]'', 1 December 2010.</ref>
[[File:South China Sea claims map.svg|thumb|250px|[[Territorial disputes in the South China Sea|Territorial claims]] in the [[South China Sea]]]]James R. Holmes, a specialist on China at the [[US Naval War College]], has said that China's investments towards a potential future conflict are closer to those of the United States than may first appear because the Chinese understate their spending, the internal price structures of the two countries are different, and the Chinese need to concentrate only on projecting military force a short distance from their own shores. The balance may shift to the advantage of the Chinese very quickly if they continue double-digit annual growth, and the U.S. and its allies cut back.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://thediplomat.com/the-naval-diplomat/2013/03/07/what-to-make-of-chinas-defense-spending-increase/|title=What to Make of China's Defense Spending Increase|author=James R. Holmes|work=The Diplomat}}</ref>
In line with [[power transition theory]], the idea that "wars tend to break out... when the upward trajectory of a rising power comes close to intersecting the downward trajectory of a [[American decline|declining power]]," some political scientists and international relations scholars have argued that a potential conflict between China, an [[Potential superpower#China|emerging power]], and the United States, the current [[superpower]], is all but inevitable.<ref>{{cite book|last=Kagan|first=Robert|title=The World America Made|year=2012|publisher=Knopf|location=New York|page=90}}</ref> Many academics disagree with applying power transition theory to the China-U.S. relationship.<ref name=":Ma&Kang">{{Cite book |last1=Ma |first1=Xinru |title=Beyond Power Transitions: The Lessons of East Asian History and the Future of U.S.-China Relations |last2=Kang |first2=David C. |date=2024 |publisher=[[Columbia University Press]] |isbn=978-0-231-55597-5 |series=Columbia Studies in International Order and Politics |location=New York}}</ref>{{Rp|page=29}} Robert Art states that these perspectives generally ignore [[China's peaceful rise]] strategy.<ref name=":Ma&Kang" />{{Rp|page=29}} Steve Chan concludes that the China-U.S. power transition will be more similar to the [[United Kingdom–United States relations|U.K.-U.S.]] power transition than the [[Germany–United Kingdom relations|Anglo-German]] power transition in which World War I arose.<ref name=":Ma&Kang" />{{Rp|page=29}}
==== Technological transfers and associated issues ==== On 29 March 2024, the Biden administration revised rules aimed at [[Artificial Intelligence Cold War|restricting China's access]] to U.S. [[artificial intelligence]] (AI) chips and chipmaking tools, including those from [[Nvidia]], as part of [[Artificial intelligence arms race|efforts]] to address national security concerns over Beijing's tech advancements potentially aiding its military.<ref>{{Cite news|date=29 March 2024|title=U.S. updates export curbs on AI chips and tools to China|newspaper=Reuters|url=https://www.reuters.com/technology/us-commerce-updates-export-curbs-ai-chips-china-2024-03-29/|access-date=31 March 2024|archive-date=1 April 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240401122755/https://www.reuters.com/technology/us-commerce-updates-export-curbs-ai-chips-china-2024-03-29/|url-status=live}}</ref> In August 2025, Chinese regulators informally directed domestic tech companies to halt or reduce purchases of Nvidia's H20 AI chips after U.S. Commerce Secretary [[Howard Lutnick]] made remarks perceived as insulting, stating that the U.S. deliberately sold China only downgraded technology.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Client Challenge |url=https://www.ft.com/content/b8e30c54-b71c-4113-8b3e-8f54bc36587d |access-date=2025-08-25 |website=Financial Times}}</ref>
===== China's alleged access to U.S. tech ===== China has reportedly gained access to classified U.S. military technology on multiple occasions, according to U.S. media and intelligence sources. In the early 1990s, U.S. officials alleged that China obtained American-made Stinger missiles through Pakistan, which had originally received them for distribution to Afghan Mujahideen during the [[Soviet–Afghan War]]. The acquisition was believed to have enabled China to reverse-engineer the technology or develop electronic countermeasures. In 2011, following the U.S. raid that killed Osama bin Laden, China was suspected of examining the wreckage of a stealth-modified [[Black Hawk helicopter]] that crashed during the operation. While U.S. forces attempted to destroy the aircraft with explosives, the tail section—featuring an unusually advanced design—remained largely intact, having landed on a wall surrounding the compound. Pakistan reportedly removed the tail and, amid heightened diplomatic tensions, allowed China to study it before returning it to the United States after several weeks. Reports citing U.S. sources suggested that Chinese officials had "probably" examined the remains. Analysts noted the incident highlighted China's close defense ties with Pakistan. Former U.S. counter-terrorism advisor Richard Clarke stated that any U.S. stealth technology shared by Pakistan to China would have been perceived as a "welcome gift" by Beijing. In 2021, a stealth variant of China's Z-20 helicopter, described by observers as a "Black Hawk clone", was revealed in concept form.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Mann |first=Jim |date=1992-05-08 |title=Pakistan Sent Stingers to China, U.S. Aides Say : Arms: The report shows another leak, adding to fears that the potent antiaircraft missiles could end up in terrorist hands. |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1992-05-08-mn-1923-story.html |access-date=2025-08-25 |website=Los Angeles Times}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=2011-08-15 |title=Pakistan Likely Allowed China Access to Secret U.S. Helicopter: Reports |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Blotter/pakistan-allowed-china-access-secret-us-helo-reports/story?id=14307423 |access-date=2025-08-25 |website=ABC News}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Rogoway |first=Tyler |date=2021-05-30 |title=Stealthy Variant Of China's Z-20 Black Hawk Clone Emerges In Concept Model Form |url=https://www.twz.com/40853/stealthy-variant-of-chinas-z-20-black-hawk-clone-emerges-in-concept-model-form |access-date=2025-08-25 |website=The War Zone}}</ref>
A 2020 report by the U.S. [[Office of Special Investigations (United States Department of Justice)|Office of Special Investigations]] detailed how Chinese cyber-espionage targeted U.S. military aircraft between 2008 and 2014. Su Bin, a Chinese national linked to the People's Liberation Army, facilitated hackers in stealing over 630,000 files from Boeing related to the C-17 Globemaster cargo aircraft, as well as data on the F-22 and F-35 fighter jets. Using his industry contacts, Su Bin directed the hackers toward specific individuals, companies, and technologies, translating stolen information and coordinating its delivery to the PLA's General Staff. The stolen data, much of which was unclassified but sensitive, enabled China to potentially reverse-engineer aircraft components and accelerate development, resulting in significant losses valued by U.S. prosecutors.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Cyber espionage for the Chinese government |url=https://www.osi.af.mil/News/Features/Display/Article/2350807/cyber-espionage-for-the-chinese-government/ |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20250706223949/https://www.osi.af.mil/News/Features/Display/Article/2350807/cyber-espionage-for-the-chinese-government/ |archive-date=2025-07-06 |access-date=2025-08-25 |work=Office of Special Investigations}}</ref>
===Taiwan issue=== {{Main|Taiwan independence movement|Political status of Taiwan}} Since the renewal of U.S.-China relations in early 1979, the Taiwan issue remained a major source of contention. After the announcement of the intention to establish diplomatic relations with [[China|Mainland China]] (PRC) on 15 December 1978, the [[Taiwan|Republic of China (Taiwan)]] immediately condemned the United States, leading to rampant protests in both Taiwan and in the US.<ref>Steven M. Goldstein, and [[Randall Schriver]], "An Uncertain Relationship: The United States, Taiwan and the Taiwan Relations Act." ''China Quarterly'' 165 (2001): 147–72. [http://cm.olemiss.edu/courses/pol324/goldschr.pdf online] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151120210107/http://cm.olemiss.edu/courses/pol324/goldschr.pdf|date=20 November 2015}}</ref> In April 1979, the U.S. Congress passed the [[Taiwan Relations Act]],<ref>{{Cite web|date=10 April 1979|title=Taiwan Relations Act, 1979 {{!}} US-China Institute|url=https://china.usc.edu/taiwan-relations-act-1979|website=[[USC Annenberg School for Communication and Journalism]]}}</ref> permitting unofficial relations with Taiwan to flourish and granting the right to provide Taiwan with [[List of U.S. arms sales to Taiwan|arms of a defensive character]]. Its passage prompted Deng to begin to view the United States as an insincere partner willing to abandon its prior commitments to China.{{sfn|Zhao|2022|p=54}} The expanding relationship that followed normalization was threatened in 1981 by PRC objections to the level of U.S. arms sales to the Republic of China on Taiwan. Secretary of State [[Alexander Haig]] visited China in June 1981 in an effort to resolve Chinese concerns about America's unofficial relations with Taiwan. Vice President Bush visited the PRC in May 1982. Eight months of negotiations produced the US-PRC Joint Communiqué of 17 August 1982. In this third communiqué, the U.S. stated its intention to gradually reduce the level of arms sales to the Republic of China, and the PRC described as a fundamental policy their effort to strive for a peaceful resolution to the [[political status of Taiwan|Taiwan question]].
When [[Donald Trump]] won the [[2016 United States presidential election|2016 presidential election]], contention over the Taiwan issue intensified; President Trump became the first sitting U.S. president since Jimmy Carter in 1979 to have any formal political or diplomatic contacts with Taiwan when he decided to receive a phone call from president [[Tsai Ing-wen|Tsai Ing-Wen]]. Trump expanded the duties of the US' ''de facto'' embassy in Taipei - the American Institute in Taiwan - by adding more security personnel, and oversaw increasing non-diplomatic visits of Tsai Ing-Wen and Congressmen to each other's countries/regions. In addition, American warships reportedly crossed the Taiwan strait and increased military drills with Taiwan, which mainland China views as a direct threat to its sovereignty.<ref name="cnn.com">{{cite news|title=US Navy warship challenges Chinese claims in the South China Sea|work=[[CNN]]|url=https://www.cnn.com/2019/09/13/_politics-zone-injection/us-warship-south-china-sea/index.html}}</ref><ref name="asia.nikkei.com">{{cite news|title=In Beijing rebuke, Taiwan signals closer defense ties with U.S. and Japan|work=[[Nikkei Asia]]|url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Politics/International-relations/In-Beijing-rebuke-Taiwan-signals-closer-defense-ties-with-US-and-Japan}}</ref> Chinese leader Xi Jinping told European Commission President [[Ursula von der Leyen]] in April 2023 that the United States was trying to trick China into attacking Taiwan, but he would not take the bait.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Sevastopulo|first1=Demetri|title=Xi Jinping claimed U.S. wants China to attack Taiwan|url=https://www.ft.com/content/7d6ca06c-d098-4a48-818e-112b97a9497a|agency=Financial Times|date=15 June 2024}}</ref>
Tensions persist over Taiwan, with China conducting military drills in March 2025 following U.S. statements on Taiwanese sovereignty.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |date=18 March 2025 |title=China conducts air and sea drills near Taiwan in response to U.S. and Taiwanese statements |url=https://apnews.com/article/taiwan-china-planes-ships-d7e0beea3f6164046d34d1dfa1dda71a |access-date=25 March 2025 |website=AP News}}</ref>
===Human rights=== [[File:Kurt Campbell with Chen Guangcheng at US Embassy May 1, 2012.jpg|thumb|Civil rights activist [[Chen Guangcheng]] (left) with former U.S. ambassador to China [[Gary Locke]] (center) and former Assistant Secretary of State for East Asian and Pacific Affairs [[Kurt M. Campbell]] (right) at the U.S. Embassy in Beijing on 1 May 2012]] <!-- Deleted image removed: [[File:China statue of liberty cartoon.jpg|thumb|''[[China Daily]]'' published a cartoon of a shadowed [[Statue of Liberty]] holding a tape recorder and microphone, instead of a tablet and torch]]. --> [[File:2008 Olympic Torch Relay in SF - Embarcadero 47.JPG|thumb|Pro-[[Tibet]]an protesters clash with pro-Chinese protesters in [[San Francisco]] in 2008.]] {{Main|Human rights in China|Human rights in the United States|Human Rights Record of the United States|Chinese censorship abroad}}
In 2003, the United States declared that despite some positive momentum that year and despite greater signs which showed that the People's Republic of China was willing to engage in discussions about [[human rights]] with the U.S. and other nations, there was still serious [[democratic backsliding|backsliding]]. In principle, China has acknowledged the importance of the protection of human rights and it has claimed that it has taken steps to bring its own human rights practices into conformity with international norms. Among those steps are China's signing of the [[International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights]] in October 1997, which was ratified in March 2001, and China's signing of the [[International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights]] in October 1998, which has not been ratified yet. In 2002, China released a significant number of political and religious prisoners and it also agreed to hold discussions about torture, arbitrary detention, and religion with UN experts. However, international human rights groups assert that there has been virtually no movement with regard to those promises,{{citation needed|date=February 2014}} with more people having been arrested for similar offences since then. Those groups maintain that China still has a long way to go in instituting the kind of fundamental systemic change that will protect the rights and liberties of all its citizens in mainland China. The U.S. State Department publishes an annual report on human rights around the world, which includes an evaluation of China's human rights record.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=973|title=U.S. Department of State, 2007 Human Rights in China, March 11, 2008|access-date=31 August 2008|archive-date=19 October 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141019051521/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=973|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1368|title=U.S. Department of State, 2008 Human Rights in China, February 25, 2009|access-date=26 December 2009|archive-date=11 October 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141011065606/http://www.china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1368|url-status=dead}}</ref>
In a decision that was criticized by human rights groups, the United States State Department did not list China as one of the world's worst human rights violators [[United States' Country Reports on Human Rights Practices|in its 2007 report of human rights practices in countries and regions outside the United States]].<ref name=NYT>{{cite news|last1=Cooper|first1=Helen|title=U.S. Drops China From List of Top 10 Violators of Rights|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2008/03/12/washington/12rights.html|publisher=NYT|date=12 March 2008}}</ref> However, the assistant secretary of the State Department's [[Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights, and Labor]] [[Jonathan D. Farrar]] stated that China's overall human rights record in 2007 remained poor.<ref name=NYT/>
Since 1998, China has annually published a [[Human Rights Record of the United States|White Paper]] detailing the human rights abuses by the United States<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.china.org.cn/e-white/20020313/index.htm|title=2002 PRC White Paper on US Human Rights Abuses|publisher=china.org.cn|date=11 March 2002|access-date=2 December 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=974|title=PRC State Council, Human Rights Record of the United States in 2007, March 13, 2008|access-date=31 August 2008|archive-date=2 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140302130854/http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=974|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1370|title=PRC State Council, Human Rights Record of the United States in 2008, February 27, 2009|access-date=26 December 2009|archive-date=11 October 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141011065746/http://www.china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=1370|url-status=dead}}</ref> and since 2005 has also published a White Paper on its own political system and democratic progress.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.china-embassy.org/eng/zt/zgrq/t217337.htm|title=China issues 1st white paper on democracy|publisher=China-embassy.org|date=19 October 2005|access-date=2 December 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://china.usc.edu/ShowArticle.aspx?articleID=855|title=White Paper on China's Political System, 2007|publisher=China.usc.edu|access-date=2 December 2010}}</ref>
On 27 February 2014,<ref name="news.xinhuanet.com">{{cite web|url=http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/china/2014-02/28/c_133150493.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140301022136/http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/china/2014-02/28/c_133150493.htm|url-status=dead|archive-date=1 March 2014|title=China issues report on U.S. human rights - Xinhua - English.news.cn}}</ref> the United States released its [[United States' Country Reports on Human Rights Practices|China report on human rights practices for 2013]], which, according to its executive summary, described the PRC as an authoritarian state and a place in which repression and coercion were routine.<ref>{{cite news|title=Country Reports on Human Rights Practices for 2013: China (includes Tibet, Hong Kong, and Macau)|url=https://2009-2017.state.gov/j/drl/rls/hrrpt/humanrightsreport/index.htm?year=2013&dlid=220263#wrapper|publisher=U.S. Department of State}}</ref> On 28 February 2014, China published a report on human rights in the United States that cited [[Mass surveillance in the United States|surveillance on its own citizens]], [[Human rights in the United States#Prison system|mistreatment of inmates]], gun violence, and homelessness, despite having a vibrant economy, as important issues.<ref name="news.xinhuanet.com"/>
American criticism of China on human rights, especially on the issue of the [[Xinjiang internment camps]], significantly expanded at the end of 2018 and in 2019.<ref>{{Cite news|date=23 September 2018|title=US criticises treatment of Uighurs in latest China row |newspaper=[[The Straits Times]] |agency=[[Agence France Presse]]|url=https://www.straitstimes.com/world/united-states/us-criticises-treatment-of-uighurs-in-latest-china-row-0|access-date=22 August 2019}}</ref> In March 2019, U.S. Secretary of State [[Mike Pompeo]] indirectly compared China to the [[Nazi Germany]] by saying that the roundup of Muslim minorities to into camps had not been seen "since the 1930s".<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/03/13/us/politics/state-department-human-rights-abuses.html|title=State Dept. Accuses China of Rights Abuses Not Seen 'Since the 1930s'|last=Sanger|first=David E.|date=13 March 2019|work=The New York Times|access-date=22 August 2019 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.washingtonexaminer.com/policy/defense-national-security/state-department-compares-china-to-nazi-germany-in-human-rights-briefing|title=State Department compares China to Nazi Germany in human rights briefing|date=13 March 2019|website=Washington Examiner |access-date=22 August 2019}}</ref> In May 2019, the United States government accused China of putting [[Uyghurs]] in "[[concentration camps]]".<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-china-concentrationcamps-idUSKCN1S925K|title=China putting minority Muslims in 'concentration camps,' U.S. says|date=4 May 2019|work=Reuters|access-date=22 August 2019}}</ref> The U.S. government has also considered sanctioning Chinese officials involved in the camps, including [[Chen Quanguo]], the [[Chinese Communist Party Committee Secretary]] of [[Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region|Xinjiang]] and a member of the [[19th Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party]] although no CCP Politburo member has ever been sanctioned by the U.S. government.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/china/diplomacy/article/3004602/chinas-top-xinjiang-official-chen-quanguo-should-face|title=Top Xinjiang official Chen Quanguo should face sanctions – US lawmakers|date=4 April 2019|website=South China Morning Post |access-date=22 August 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-usa-xinjiang-rights-idUSKCN1SZ22E|title=Rights group urges U.S. to sanction China over Xinjiang camps|date=29 May 2019|work=Reuters|access-date=22 August 2019}}</ref> In July 2019, [[Vice President of the United States|Vice President]] [[Mike Pence]] accused China of persecuting [[Christianity in China|Christians]], [[Islam in China|Muslims]] and [[Chinese Buddhism|Buddhists]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://trumpwhitehouse.archives.gov/briefings-statements/remarks-vice-president-pence-2nd-annual-religious-freedom-ministerial/|title=Remarks by Vice President Pence at the 2nd Annual Religious Freedom Ministerial |via=[[NARA|National Archives]]|work=[[whitehouse.gov]]|access-date=22 August 2019}}</ref>
On 4 October 2019, the [[Houston Rockets]]' general manager, [[Daryl Morey]], issued a tweet that supported the [[2019–20 Hong Kong protests]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Rockets working to mend ties with China after executive's Hong Kong tweet|url=https://www.nba.com/article/2019/10/06/cba-suspends-cooperation-rockets|work=AP|date=6 October 2019}}</ref> Morey's tweet resulted in the [[Chinese Basketball Association]]'s suspension of its relationship with the Houston Rockets and the issuance of a statement of dissatisfaction from the consulate office of China in [[Houston]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Rockets' general manager's Hong Kong comments anger China|url=https://www.apnews.com/0a660e9e10664e31bf6ee359c22058cf|work=Associated Press|date=7 October 2019}}</ref> On 6 October, both Morey and the [[NBA]] issued separate statements addressing the original tweet. Morey said that he never intended his tweet to cause any offense, and the NBA said the tweet was "regrettable".<ref>{{cite news|last=Chang|first=Joy|date=7 October 2019|title=Houston Rockets GM's Hong Kong tweet outrages Chinese fans|work=[[SCMP]]|url=https://www.scmp.com/video/hong-kong/3031915/houston-rockets-gms-hong-kong-tweet-outrages-chinese-fans}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Victor|first=Daniel|date=7 October 2019|title=Hong Kong Protests Put N.B.A. on Edge in China|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/10/07/sports/basketball/nba-china-hong-kong.html|work=NYT}}</ref> The statements were criticized by U.S. politicians and third-party observers for the perceived exercise of economic statecraft by the PRC and insufficiency of the NBA's defense of Morey's tweet.<ref name="statementcritic">Some relevant sources include:
* {{cite news|date=7 October 2019|title=Rockets' general manager's Hong Kong comments anger China|work=[[Associated Press]]|url=https://www.apnews.com/0a660e9e10664e31bf6ee359c22058cf}} * {{cite news|date=8 October 2019|title=China's vise grip on corporate America|publisher=Axios|agency=Axios|url=https://www.axios.com/china-nba-corporate-america-hong-kong-protests-cf9af430-4f8f-4968-aca3-c4b90fb5b2d1.html}} * {{cite news|date=8 October 2019|title=The NBA Should Call China's Bluff|publisher=Bloomberg|agency=Bloomberg|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2019-10-08/the-nba-should-call-china-s-bluff}} * {{cite news|date=7 October 2019|title=Sixers have a chance to show some guts about the NBA and China. They're passing it up. {{!}} Mike Sielski|publisher=Philadelphia Inquirer|agency=Philadelphia Inquirer|url=https://www.inquirer.com/sixers/philadelphia-76ers-china-daryl-morey-nba-tweet-loong-lions-20191007.html}} * {{cite news|title=CNN's Kate Bolduan Rips NBA for Silencing Exec Who Criticized China: 'This is Shocking'|work=[[Mediaite]]|url=https://www.mediaite.com/sports/cnns-kate-bolduan-rips-nba-for-silencing-exec-who-criticized-china-this-is-shocking/}} * {{cite news|date=7 October 2019|title=Opinion: The NBA was staring down a China problem with or without Daryl Morey's tweet|publisher=LAT|agency=LAT|url=https://www.latimes.com/opinion/story/2019-10-07/daryl-morey-nba-china-problem}} * {{cite news|date=7 October 2019|title=The day the NBA fluttered before China|newspaper=The Washington Post|agency=Chron|url=https://www.chron.com/opinion/article/The-day-the-NBA-fluttered-before-China-14499738.php}} * {{cite news|date=8 October 2019|title=Daryl Morey's tweet on Hong Kong shows how China is calling the shots in the NBA|work=[[NBC News]]|url=https://www.nbcnews.com/think/opinion/daryl-morey-s-tweet-hong-kong-shows-how-china-calling-ncna1063551}} * {{cite news|title=NBA Defends 'Freedom Of Speech' For Employees As China Moves To Block Games Facebook Twitter Flipboard Email|work=[[NPR]]|url=https://www.npr.org/2019/10/08/768225490/nba-defends-freedom-of-speech-for-employees-as-china-moves-to-block-block-games|quote=Critics in the U.S. are accusing the NBA of prioritizing profits over principles. And critics in China also cried foul, saying the league is being insensitive in handling a politically divisive issue.}} </ref> Critics also contrasted the league's disparate response to Morey's tweet with its history of political activism<ref name="statementcriticcompare">Some relevant sources include:
* {{cite news|date=8 October 2019|title=China's heavy-handed reaction to the NBA is just the wake-up call the world needed|work=[[CNBC]]|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2019/10/08/chinas-reaction-to-the-nba-is-the-wake-up-call-the-world-needed.html|quote=The NBA is not likely to face a debilitating series of protests from U.S. fans who may be angered by the league's agonizingly cautious and wavering responses to Morey's tweet. But the extreme attention the story has garnered does weaken the NBA's efforts to cast itself as a force for positive social change. Commissioner Adam Silver's public declaration of his "personal outrage" over the racist comments made by former L.A. Clippers owner Donald Sterling in 2014, and his pushback against the passing of the controversial transgender bathroom law in North Carolina in 2016 seem inconsistent now with a league that remains silent about China's many human rights abuses.}} * {{cite news|date=7 October 2019|title=Sixers have a chance to show some guts about the NBA and China. They're passing it up. {{!}} Mike Sielski|work=[[Philadelphia Inquirer]]|url=https://www.inquirer.com/sixers/philadelphia-76ers-china-daryl-morey-nba-tweet-loong-lions-20191007.html}} * {{cite news|date=7 October 2019|title=The World's Wokest Sports League Bows to China|publisher=NYT|agency=NYT|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/10/07/opinion/nba-china-hong-kong.html}} * {{cite news|date=7 October 2019|title=Opinion: The NBA was staring down a China problem with or without Daryl Morey's tweet|work=[[Los Angeles Times]]|url=https://www.latimes.com/opinion/story/2019-10-07/daryl-morey-nba-china-problem}} * {{cite news|title=Opinion: It's time for LeBron James to speak out on China, regardless of Nike ties|publisher=USAToday|agency=USAToday|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/columnist/josh-peter/2019/10/07/lebron-james-china-hong-kong-controversy/3903187002/}} * {{cite news|date=7 October 2019|title=The day the NBA fluttered before China|newspaper=The Washington Post|agency=Chron|url=https://www.chron.com/opinion/article/The-day-the-NBA-fluttered-before-China-14499738.php}} </ref> The statements also drew criticism from PRC state-run media for their perceived insufficiency, as Morey did not apologize.<ref>{{cite news|author1=陈远丁|author2=黄钰|author3=席莉莉|title=莫雷、NBA声明均未道歉 网友：这是对中国的无视和挑衅|trans-title=Morey & NBA did not apologize; Netizens: It's provocative behavior toward China|url=http://society.people.com.cn/n1/2019/1007/c428181-31385995.html|access-date=8 October 2019|work=人民网|date=7 October 2019|language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=央视快评：莫雷必须道歉|trans-title=Morey Must Apologize|url=http://tv.cctv.com/2019/10/07/VIDEOS2ZjZVo3Y3gnh4Ck92p191007.shtml|access-date=8 October 2019|work=CCTV|date=7 October 2019|language=zh}}</ref>
In June 2020, the White House on 31st anniversary of the Tiananmen Square massacre, asked Beijing to respect human rights, carry out its due commitments on Hong Kong, as well as flog persecution of ethnic and religious minorities.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-tiananmen-usa/white-house-on-tiananmen-anniversary-urges-china-to-respect-human-rights-idUSKBN23B3I3|title=White House, on Tiananmen anniversary, urges China to respect human rights|access-date= 4 June 2020|website=Reuters|date=4 June 2020}}</ref> On 9 July 2020, the [[United States]] announced [[US Sanctions|sanctions]] against Chinese politicians, who as per its record were responsible for [[human rights]] violations against [[Uyghurs|Muslim minorities]] in [[Xinjiang]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-us-canada-53355697|title=Xinjiang: US sanctions on Chinese officials over 'abuse' of Muslims|work=BBC News|date=9 July 2020|access-date=9 July 2020}}</ref>
On 20 July 2020, U.S. government sanctioned 11 new [[List of companies of China|Chinese companies]] from purchasing American technology and products over human rights violations in China targeting [[Uyghurs]] in the [[Xinjiang]] region.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/07/20/business/economy/china-sanctions-uighurs-labor.html|title=U.S. Imposes Sanctions on 11 Chinese Companies Over Human Rights|access-date=20 July 2020|work=The New York Times|date=20 July 2020|last1=Swanson|first1=Ana}}</ref>
Many American companies, including [[Delta Air Lines]], [[Coach New York]], [[Marriott International]], [[Calvin Klein]] and [[Tiffany & Co.]] have apologized to China after "offending" the country and China's ruling Communist Party.<ref>{{cite news|date=8 October 2019|title=The NBA landed in hot water after the Houston Rockets GM supported the Hong Kong protests. Here are other times Western brands caved to China after offending the Communist Party.|work=[[Business Insider]]|url=https://www.businessinsider.com/western-companies-apologize-china-communist-party-list-2019-10#christian-dior-after-an-employee-reportedly-used-a-map-of-china-that-did-not-include-taiwan-in-a-presentation-2}}</ref> [[File:Xinjiang Internment Map, US-Aus Gov Assessment.jpg|thumb|[[Xinjiang internment camps]]. The United States officially recognized the Chinese government's treatment of the [[Uyghurs]] in Xinjiang as a genocide.]] On 15 September 2020, the U.S. government decided to take steps to block some exports from Xinjiang, over the country's alleged [[human rights abuses]] directed mostly against Uyghurs of the region.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/business-54155809|title= Xinjiang: US to block some exports citing China's human rights abuses|work= BBC News|date= 15 September 2020|access-date= 15 September 2020}}</ref>
In 2020, Chinese diplomats increasingly adopted "[[wolf warrior diplomacy]]" to deny all accusations of human rights abuses.<ref name="vox._HowC">{{Cite news|last=Ward|first=Alex|date=21 December 2020|title=How China's 'wolf warrior' diplomats use Twitter to troll Beijing's enemies|work=[[Vox (website)|Vox]]|url=https://www.vox.com/22167626/china-wolf-warrior-twitter-online-interview|access-date=28 July 2021|archive-date=29 July 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210729005551/https://www.vox.com/22167626/china-wolf-warrior-twitter-online-interview|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="cnn._China_is">{{Cite web|title=China is embracing a new brand of wolf warrior diplomacy|last1=Westcott|first1=Ben|last2=Jiang|first2=Steven|work=CNN|date=29 May 2020|access-date=8 October 2021|url= https://www.cnn.com/2020/05/28/asia/china-wolf-warrior-diplomacy-intl-hnk/index.html}}</ref> Chinese foreign ministry spokesperson [[Zhao Lijian]] tweeted that as long as the U.S. had problems itself, it "had no right" to criticize China on human rights abuses.<ref name="cnn._China_is"/>
