revision.text:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use American English|date=July 2025}} {{Use mdy dates|date=March 2022}} {{TOC right|limit=2}}
'''Confederate monuments and memorials''' in the [[United States]] include public displays and symbols of the [[Confederate States of America]] (CSA), Confederate leaders, or Confederate soldiers of the [[American Civil War]]. Many monuments and memorials [[Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials|have been or will be removed]] under great controversy. Part of the [[commemoration of the American Civil War]], these symbols include monuments and statues, flags, [[Memorial Day#In the South|holidays]] and other observances, and the names of schools, roads, parks, bridges, buildings, counties, cities, lakes, dams, military bases, and other public structures. In a December 2018 special report, ''[[Smithsonian Magazine]]'' stated, "over the past ten years, taxpayers have directed at least $40 million to Confederate monuments—statues, homes, parks, museums, libraries, and cemeteries—and to Confederate heritage organizations."<ref name=Smithson/>
This entry does not include commemorations of pre-Civil War figures connected with the [[origins of the Civil War]] but not directly tied to the Confederacy, such as Supreme Court Justice [[Roger B. Taney]], congressman [[Preston Brooks]], [[North Carolina]] Chief Justice [[Thomas Ruffin]],<ref>{{cite news|title=NC's highest court will review courtroom portraits amid complaint about pro-slavery judge|first=Josh|last=Shaffer|date=October 25, 2018|newspaper=[[Island Packet]]|url=http://www.islandpacket.com/news/state/north-carolina/article220617020.html}}</ref> or Vice President [[John C. Calhoun]], although monuments to Calhoun "have been the most consistent targets" of vandals.<ref>{{cite news|title=Take Down the Confederate Flags, but Not the Monuments|first1=Ethan J.|last1=Kytle|first2=Blain|last2=Roberts|date=June 25, 2015|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2015/06/-confederate-monuments-flags-south-carolina/396836/|magazine=[[The Atlantic]]|access-date=December 5, 2018}}</ref>
[[Monument]]s and [[memorial]]s are listed alphabetically by state, and by city within each state. States not listed have no known qualifying items for the list.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/06/05/us/confederate-monuments-texas-trnd/index.html|title=The state leading the way in removing Confederate monuments? Texas|first1=Doug|last1=Criss|first2=Elizabeth|last2=Elkin|newspaper=[[CNN]]|date=June 5, 2018}}</ref>
==History==
===Monument building and dedications=== Memorials have been erected on public spaces (including on courthouse grounds) either at public expense or funded by private organizations and donors. Numerous private memorials have also been erected. [[File:Confederate monuments, schools and other iconography established by year.png|thumb|Chart of public symbols of the Confederacy and its leaders as surveyed by the [[Southern Poverty Law Center]]
(SPLC), by year of establishment. Most of these were put up either during the [[Jim Crow]] era or during the [[Civil Rights Movement]].{{efn|This chart is based on data from an SPLC survey which identified "1,503 publicly sponsored symbols honoring Confederate leaders, soldiers or the Confederate States of America in general." The survey excluded "nearly 2,600 markers, battlefields, museums, cemeteries and other places or symbols that are largely historical in nature."<ref name="SPLC2016pdf" />}} These two periods also coincided with the 50th and 100th anniversaries of the Civil War.{{efn|"The second spike began in the early 1950s and lasted through the 1960s, as the civil rights movement led to a backlash among segregationists."<ref name="SPLC2016pdf" />}}<ref>Cunningham, Anne, ed., ''The Confederate Flag'', p. 31 (quotes original text of SPLC report).</ref>]] According to ''[[Smithsonian Magazine]]'', "Confederate monuments aren't just heirlooms, the artifacts of a bygone era. Instead, American taxpayers are still heavily investing in these tributes today."<ref name=Smithson/> The report also concluded that the monuments were constructed and are regularly maintained in promotion of the [[Lost Cause]], white supremacist mythology, and over the many decades of their establishment, [[African American]] leaders regularly protested these memorials and what they represented.<ref name=Smithson/>
A small number of memorializations were made during the war, mainly as ship and place names. After the war, [[Robert E. Lee]] said on several occasions that he was opposed to any monuments, as they would, in his opinion, "keep open the sores of war".<ref>{{cite web |title=Actually, Robert E. Lee was against erecting Confederate memorials |url=https://www.wptv.com/news/national/actually-robert-e-lee-was-against-erecting-confederate-memorials |website=WPTV News Channel 5 West Palm |date=August 16, 2017 |publisher=CNN |access-date=February 10, 2018 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Memorialization of Robert E. Lee and the Lost Cause |url=https://www.nps.gov/arho/learn/historyculture/memorialization-of-robert-e-lee-and-the-lost-cause.htm |website=[[National Park Service]] |publisher=National Park Service |access-date=29 September 2023 |language=en |date=14 September 2021}}</ref> Nevertheless, monuments and memorials continued to be dedicated shortly after the American Civil War.<ref>{{Cite book|title=History of Hampshire County, West Virginia: from its earliest settlement to the present|last=Maxwell|first=Hu|date=1897|publisher=A.B. Boughner, printer|location=Morgantown, W. Va|ol = 23304577M}}</ref> Before 1890, most were erected in cemeteries as memorials to soldiers who died in the war.<ref name="Yawn">{{cite web |author1=Andrew J. Yawn |author2=Todd A. Price |author3=Maria Clark |title='This is not just about symbols': America's reckoning over Confederate monuments |url=https://www.tennessean.com/in-depth/news/american-south/2020/07/30/confederate-monuments-south-reckoning-point/5365176002/ |website=[[The Tennessean]] |publisher=USA Today Network |access-date=29 September 2023 |language=en-us |date=1 August 2020}}</ref> Many more monuments were dedicated in the years after 1890, when [[United States Congress|Congress]] established the first [[National Military Park]] at [[Chickamauga and Chattanooga National Military Park|Chickamauga and Chattanooga]], and by the turn of the 20th century, five battlefields from the Civil War had been preserved: Chickamauga-Chattanooga, [[Antietam National Battlefield|Antietam]], [[Gettysburg National Military Park|Gettysburg]], [[Shiloh National Military Park|Shiloh]], and [[Vicksburg National Military Park|Vicksburg]]. At Vicksburg National Military Park, more than 95% of the park's monuments were erected in the first eighteen years after the park was established in 1899.<ref name=Vicksburg>{{cite web|title=Monuments and Memorials |url=https://www.nps.gov/vick/learn/historyculture/vnmp-state-memorials.htm |website=Vicksburg National Military Park|publisher=National Park Service |access-date=September 26, 2017}}</ref> But monuments began appearing in public places with the emergence of the [[Jim Crow]] South, coinciding with the [[Post-Reconstruction American race relations|nadir of American race relations]].<ref name="Yawn" />
===Jim Crow=== {{see also|Jim Crow Museum of Racist Memorabilia}}
Confederate monument-building has often been part of widespread campaigns to promote and justify Jim Crow laws in the South.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Leib |first1=Jonathan I.|last2=Webster|first2=Gerald R.|last3=Webster|first3=Roberta H. |date=December 1, 2000|title=Rebel with a cause? Iconography and public memory in the Southern United States|journal=GeoJournal|language=en|volume=52|issue=2|pages=303–310|doi=10.1023/A:1014358204037|bibcode=2000GeoJo..52..303L |s2cid=151000497|issn=0343-2521}}</ref><ref name="CoxAnalysis">{{cite news |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/posteverything/wp/2017/08/16/the-whole-point-of-confederate-monuments-is-to-celebrate-white-supremacy/|title=Analysis – The whole point of Confederate monuments is to celebrate white supremacy|last=Cox|first=Karen L.|date=August 16, 2017|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=September 21, 2017}}</ref> According to the [[American Historical Association]] (AHA), the erection of Confederate monuments during the early 20th century was "part and parcel of the initiation of legally mandated segregation and widespread disenfranchisement across the South." According to the AHA, memorials to the Confederacy erected during this period "were intended, in part, to obscure the terrorism required to overthrow [[Reconstruction Era|Reconstruction]], and to intimidate African Americans politically and isolate them from the mainstream of public life." A later wave of monument building coincided with the [[civil rights movement]], and according to the AHA "these symbols of [[white supremacy]] are still being invoked for similar purposes."<ref>American Historical Association, [https://www.historians.org/news-and-advocacy/statements-and-resolutions-of-support-and-protest/aha-statement-on-confederate-monuments AHA Statement on Confederate Monuments] (August 2017)</ref> According to ''Smithsonian Magazine'', "far from simply being markers of historic events and people, as proponents argue, these memorials were created and funded by Jim Crow governments to pay homage to a slave-owning society and to serve as blunt assertions of dominance over African-Americans."<ref name=Smithson>{{cite magazine |url=https://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/costs-confederacy-special-report-180970731/#xqhmLkq8ks7k5PO9.99 |title=The Costs of the Confederacy |first1=Brian |last1=Palmer |first2=Seth Freed |last2=Wessler |magazine=[[Smithsonian Magazine]] |date=December 2018}}</ref>
[[File:Union, WV Confederate Memorial.jpg|thumb|upright|Confederate Soldier Statue, in [[Monroe County, West Virginia]], 2016]]
According to historian Jane Dailey from the [[University of Chicago]], in many cases, the purpose of the monuments was not to celebrate the past but rather to promote a "white supremacist future".<ref name="NPR">{{cite news|url=https://www.npr.org/2017/08/20/544266880/confederate-Participation-Trophies-were-built-to-further-a-white-supremacist-future|title=Confederate Participation Trophies Were Built To Further A 'White Supremacist Future'|website=npr.org|date=August 20, 2017|access-date=September 21, 2017|last1=Parks|first1=Miles}}</ref> Another historian, [[Karen L. Cox]], from the [[University of North Carolina at Charlotte]], has written that the monuments are "a legacy of the brutally racist Jim Crow era", and that "the whole point of Confederate monuments is to celebrate white supremacy".<ref name="CoxAnalysis" /> Another historian from UNC, James Leloudis, stated that "The funders and backers of these monuments are very explicit that they are requiring a political education and a legitimacy for the Jim Crow era and the right of white men to rule."<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.heraldsun.com/news/local/counties/durham-county/article167619947.html|title=Durham Confederate Participation Trophy: tribute to dying veterans or political tool of Jim Crow South?|newspaper=[[The Herald-Sun (Durham, North Carolina)|The Herald-Sun]] |location=[[Durham, North Carolina]]|access-date=September 21, 2017}}</ref> They were erected without the consent or even input of Southern African Americans, who remembered the Civil War far differently, and who had no interest in honoring those who fought to keep them enslaved.<ref name="SAHUpton">[http://www.sah.org/publications-and-research/sah-blog/sah-blog/2017/09/13/confederate-monuments-and-civic-values-in-the-wake-of-charlottesville Confederate Monuments and Civic Values in the Wake of Charlottesville]. [[Dell Upton]], [[Society of American Historians]], September 13, 2017</ref> According to Civil War historian Judith Giesberg, professor of history at [[Villanova University]], "White supremacy is really what these statues represent."<ref name="CSM">[https://www.csmonitor.com/USA/Politics/2017/0822/Confederate-monuments-What-to-do-with-them Confederate monuments: What to do with them?]. Grier, Peter. ''[[Christian Science Monitor]], August 22, 2017''</ref> Some monuments were also meant to beautify cities as part of the [[City Beautiful movement]], although this was secondary.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.csmonitor.com/Books/chapter-and-verse/2017/0614/Inside-the-hidden-history-of-confederate-memorials|title=Inside the hidden history of confederate memorials|last=Dotinga|first=Randy|date=June 14, 2017|work=The Christian Science Monitor|access-date=September 5, 2017|issn=0882-7729}}</ref>
In a June 2018 speech, Civil War historian [[James I. Robertson Jr.]] of [[Virginia Tech]] said the monuments were not a "Jim Crow signal of defiance" and referred to the current trend to dismantle or destroy them as an "age of idiocy" motivated by "elements hell-bent on tearing apart unity that generations of Americans have painfully constructed."<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.c-span.org/video/?448679-5/historydebate-confederate-monuments | first=James I. Jr. | last=Robertson | date=July 28, 2018|title=Debate Over Confederate Monuments | C-SPAN.org|website=www.c-span.org}}</ref> Katrina Dunn Johnson, Curator of the [[South Carolina Confederate Relic Room & Military Museum|South Carolina Confederate Relic Room and Military Museum]], states that "thousands of families throughout the country were unable to reclaim their soldier's remains--many never learned their loved ones' exact fate on the battlefield or within the prison camps. The psychological impact of such a devastating loss cannot be underestimated when attempting to understand the primary motivations behind Southern memorialization."<ref name="Johnson2009">{{cite book|author=Kristina Dunn Johnson|title=No Holier Spot of Ground: Confederate Monuments & Cemeteries of South Carolina|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=P9t-CQAAQBAJ&pg=PT9|date=April 6, 2009|publisher=Arcadia Publishing Inc.|isbn=978-1614232827|pages=9–}}</ref>
Many Confederate monuments were dedicated in the former Confederate states and [[Border states (American Civil War)|border states]] in the decades following the Civil War, in many instances by [[Ladies Memorial Association]]s, [[United Daughters of the Confederacy]] (UDC), [[United Confederate Veterans]] (UCV), [[Sons of Confederate Veterans]] (SCV), the Heritage Preservation Association, and other memorial organizations.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Winsboro|first=Irvin D.S.|date=2016|title=The Confederate Monument Movement as a Policy Dilemma for Resource Managers of Parks, Cultural Sites, and Protected Places: Florida as a Case Study|url=http://www.georgewright.org/332winsboro.pdf|journal=The George Wright Forum|volume=33|pages=217–29}}</ref><ref name="Wiggins">{{cite book | last = Wiggins | first = David N. | title = Remembering Georgia's Confederates | publisher = Arcadia | year = 2005 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=htvhsHY9BDEC&pg=PP1 | pages = 106, 108, 109, 117| isbn = 978-0738518237}}</ref><ref name="ForsythPark">[http://www.ci.savannah.ga.us/cityweb/p&tweb.nsf/4bf6a0ca45844e1685256c2f0071a3fb/298ff53f286e8d6385256c5a004a73ca?OpenDocument Confederate Monument in Forsyth Park] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110531173526/http://www.ci.savannah.ga.us/cityweb/p%26tweb.nsf/4bf6a0ca45844e1685256c2f0071a3fb/298ff53f286e8d6385256c5a004a73ca?OpenDocument|date=May 31, 2011}}, City of Savannah website, accessed April 24, 2010</ref> Other Confederate monuments are located on Civil War battlefields. Many Confederate monuments are listed on the [[National Register of Historic Places]], either separately or as contributing objects within listings of courthouses or historic districts. Art historians Cynthia Mills and Pamela Simpson argued, in ''Monuments to the [[Lost Cause]]'', that the majority of Confederate monuments, of the type they define, were "commissioned by white women, in hope of preserving a positive vision of antebellum life."<ref>{{cite book|title=Monuments to the Los Cause: Women, Art, and the Landscapes of Southern Memory|last1=Mills|first1=Cynthia|last2=Simpson|first2=Pamela H.|publisher=University of Tennessee Press|year=2003|isbn=978-1572332720|page={{Page needed|date=October 2017}}}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Gulley|first=H.E.|year=1993|title=Women and the Lost Cause: preserving a Confederate identity in the American Deep South|journal=Journal of Historical Geography|volume=19|issue=2|pages=125–41|doi=10.1006/jhge.1993.1009}}</ref>
In the late nineteenth century, technological innovations in the granite and bronze industries helped reduce costs and made monuments more affordable for small towns. Companies looking to capitalize on this opportunity often sold nearly identical copies of monuments to both the North and South.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/why-those-confederate-soldier-statues-look-a-lot-like-their-union-counterparts/2017/08/18/cefcc1bc-8394-11e7-ab27-1a21a8e006ab_story.html|title=Why those Confederate soldier participation trophies look a lot like their Union counterparts|last=Fisher|first=Marc|date=August 18, 2017|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=September 16, 2017|language=en-US|issn=0190-8286|quote=Because of technological innovations in the granite and bronze industries, the price of these participation trophies came way down}}</ref>
Another wave of monument construction coincided with the [[Civil Rights Movement]] and the [[American Civil War Centennial]]. At least thirty-two Confederate monuments were dedicated between 2000 and 2017, including at least 7 re-dedications.<ref name="GuardianChanging">{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/aug/16/confederate-monuments-civil-war-history-trump|title='Changing history'? No – 32 Confederate monuments dedicated in past 17 years|last1=Holpuch|first1=Amanda|date=August 16, 2017|work=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=August 26, 2017|last2=Chalabi|first2=Mona|language=en-GB|issn=0261-3077}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.foxnews.com/us/confederate-statue-removed-from-university-of-louisville-campus-rededicated-in-kentucky|title=Confederate participation trophy removed from University of Louisville campus rededicated in Kentucky|date=May 30, 2017|work=[[Fox News]]|access-date=August 22, 2018|language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/ocala|title=FPAN – Destination: Civil War – – Ocala|website=fpan.us|access-date=August 22, 2018|archive-date=August 22, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180822213859/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/ocala|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.myajc.com/lifestyles/rededicating-confederate-monument-peace/FCAZ8OVP51wNkgycFVoS6K/|title=Rededicating a Confederate monument to peace|work=myajc|access-date=August 22, 2018|language=en}}</ref>
===Scholarly study=== Scholarly studies of the monuments began in the 1980s. In 1983 John J. Winberry published a study which was based on data from the work of R.W. Widener.<ref name="Widener">{{Cite book|title=Confederate monuments: Enduring symbols of the South and the War Between the States|last=Widener|first=Ralph W.|publisher=Andromeda Associates|year=1982|oclc=8697924}}</ref><ref name=":2" /> He estimated that the main building period for monuments was from 1889 to 1929 and that of the monuments erected in courthouse squares over half were built between 1902 and 1912. He determined four main locations for monuments; battlefields, cemeteries, county courthouse grounds, and state capitol grounds. Over a third of the courthouse monuments were dedicated to the dead. The majority of the cemetery monuments in his study were built in the pre-1900 period, while most of the courthouse monuments were erected after 1900. Of the 666 monuments in his study 55% were of Confederate soldiers, while 28% were obelisks. Soldiers dominated courthouse grounds, while obelisks account for nearly half of cemetery monuments. The idea that the soldier statues always faced north was found to be untrue and that the soldiers usually faced the same direction as the courthouse. He noted that the monuments were "remarkably diverse" with "only a few instances of repetition of inscriptions".<ref name=":2" />
[[File:Confederate Memorial in Fulton.jpg|thumb|upright|The [[Confederate Memorial in Fulton]], Kentucky is listed on the [[National Register of Historic Places listings in Fulton County, Kentucky|National Register of Historic Places]]]]
He categorized the monuments into four types. Type 1 was a Confederate soldier on a column with his weapon at [[parade rest]], or weaponless and gazing into the distance. These accounted for approximately half the monuments studied. They are, however, the most popular among the courthouse monuments. Type 2 was a Confederate soldier on a column with rifle ready, or carrying a flag or bugle. Type 3 was an obelisk, often covered with drapery and bearing cannonballs or an urn. This type was 28% of the monuments studied, but 48% of the monuments in cemeteries and 18% of courthouse monuments. Type 4 was a miscellaneous group, including arches, standing stones, plaques, fountains, etc. These account for 17% of the monuments studied.<ref name=":2" />
Over a third of the courthouse monuments were specifically dedicated to the Confederate dead. The first courthouse monument was erected in [[Bolivar, Tennessee]], in 1867. By 1880 nine courthouse monuments had been erected. Winberry noted two centers of courthouse monuments: the Potomac counties of Virginia, from which the tradition spread to North Carolina, and a larger area covering Georgia, South Carolina and northern Florida. The diffusion of courthouse monuments was aided by organizations such as the United Confederate Veterans and their publications, though other factors may also have been effective.<ref name=":2" />
Winberry listed four reasons for the shift from cemeteries to courthouses. First was the need to preserve the memory of the Confederate dead and also recognize the veterans who returned. Second was to celebrate the rebuilding of the South after the war. Third was the romanticizing of the [[Lost Cause of the Confederacy|Lost Cause]], and the fourth was to unify the white population in a common heritage against the interests of African-American Southerners. He concluded: "No one of these four possible explanations for the Confederate monument is adequate or complete in itself. The monument is a symbol, but whether it was a memory of the past, a celebration of the present, or a portent of the future remains a difficult question to answer; monuments and symbols can be complicated and sometimes indecipherable."<ref name=":2">{{cite journal |title='Lest We Forget': The Confederate Monument and the Southern Townscape |first=John J. |last=Winberry |year=2015 |journal=Southeastern Geographer |volume=55 |number=1 |pages=19–31 |doi=10.1353/sgo.2015.0003 |s2cid=128637533 |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/article/585508|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
==The Monument Movement== The Monument Movement was a national movement of the late 19th and early 20th century. The Union and Confederate monuments were erected as community memorials. In the North and South communities came together in the time of war, contributing their men and boys (and a few documented women), then they came together again to memorialize these soldiers and their contributions to the cause as they saw it. Citizens paid subscriptions to memorials, for monument associations, taxes were issued, the GAR, Allied Orders, the United Daughters of the Confederacy, and the United Confederate Veterans all lead fundraisers.<ref name="auto">{{cite journal |last1=Blevins |first1=Ernest |title=Forever in Mourning: Union and Confederate Monuments, 1860–1920 |journal=Nineteenth Century |date=Fall 2019 |volume=39 |issue=2 |url=https://www.academia.edu/41329543}}</ref>
The monument to Confederate Colonel Francis S. Bartow was erected after First Manassas but was destroyed before or during Second Manassas. The other early monuments were Union monuments at Battle of Rowlett's Station in Munfordville, Kentucky in January 1862 for the men of the 32nd Indiana killed. It was removed for its own protection from the elements in 2008.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Bennett |first1=Alec |title="History of the 32nd Indiana Infantry Monument," |url=https://www.cem.va.gov/CEM/bloedner_monument.asp |website=July 7, 2019}}</ref> Other early Union monuments before the war ended were the [[Hazen Brigade Monument]] in Murfreesboro and the 1865 Ladd and Whitney Monument in Lowell, Massachusetts.<ref>{{cite web |title=Hazen Brigade Monument |url=https://www.nps.gov/places/hazen-brigade-monument.htm |access-date=July 7, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title="Ladd and Whitney Memorial," |url=https://macivilwarmonuments.com/tag/ladd-and-whitney-monument/ |website=June 29, 2019|date=October 31, 2018 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title="Massachusetts Civil War Monuments Project: Lowell," |url=https://macivilwarmonuments.com/2018/10/31/lowell/ |website=June–July 2019|date=October 31, 2018 }}</ref>
The Northern memorials recorded in the survey work to date lists 11 monuments erected before 1866 including the previously mentioned monuments. Another ten monuments were documented in 1866, and 11 more in 1867 by the time the first post-war Confederate monuments were erected in Romney, Hampshire County, West Virginia and Chester, Chester County, South Carolina in 1867.<ref name="auto"/> [[File:The Monument Movement- Union Confederate Monuments 1860-1920.jpg|thumb|Blevins' "Forever in Mourning" Chart of Union and Confederate Monuments, 1860–1920]]
In addition to monuments to the Union and Confederate honorees, the Monument Movement saw the placement of Revolutionary War Monuments for the 100th of the American Revolution from 1876 to 1883. In the W.H. Mullins Company catalog, The Blue and the Gray, it notes with Union and Confederate Monuments the company's recent installments of monuments for the Revolutionary War at Guilford Courthouse, North Carolina.<ref>{{cite book |last1=W.H. Mullins Company |title=The Blue and the Gray |date=1913 |publisher=The Canon Company |location=Salem, Cleveland}}</ref>
===Vandalism=== As of June 19, over 12 Confederate monuments had been vandalized in 2019, usually with paint.<ref>{{cite news |title=More than a dozen Confederate participation trophies vandalized around the country so far in 2019 |first=Charles |last=Du can |date=June 19, 2019 |newspaper=[[Charlotte Observer]] |url=https://www.charlotteobserver.com/news/state/north-carolina/article231680543.html}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Confederate monument splashed with paint-like substance, SC cops say, and 2 arrested |first=Noan |last=Feit |date=June 16, 2019 |url=https://www.thestate.com/news/nation-world/national/article231628148.html |newspaper=[[The State (newspaper)|The State]]}}</ref>{{update inline|date=June 2020}}
===Removal=== {{Main|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials}} [[File:Lee Removal.jpg|thumb|right|The Confederate Monument to Robert E. Lee was removed from its [[pedestal]] in Lee Circle in New Orleans on May 17, 2017]]
{{As of|2017|04}}, at least 60 symbols of the Confederacy had been removed or renamed since 2015, according to the [[Southern Poverty Law Center]] (SPLC).<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aljazeera.com/indepth/features/2017/08/confederate-statues-debate-170821104705027.html|title=US Confederate monuments: What is the debate about?|website=www.aljazeera.com|access-date=September 4, 2017}}</ref> At the same time, laws in various Southern states place restrictions on, or prohibit altogether, the removal of statues and memorials and the renaming of parks, roads, and schools.<ref name=Vice1>{{cite news |url=https://www.vice.com/en/article/btw-these-four-states-legally-protect-confederate-monuments/ |first=Katelyn |last=Harrop |newspaper=[[Vice (magazine)|Vice]] Impact |date=June 13, 2017 |title=BTW, These Four States Legally Protect Confederate Monuments |access-date=September 4, 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.djournal.com/news/state-news/mississippi-law-prohibits-removal-of-historical-markers/article_f4e8c3a6-82a2-54a7-bcf9-f8979d95322f.html |title=Mississippi law prohibits removal of historical markers |first=Bobby |last=Harrison |newspaper=[[Northeast Mississippi Daily Journal]] |date=May 28, 2017 |access-date=September 4, 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Cote |first1=Rachel Vorona |title=Heritage Act Keeps Confederate Flags Flying in South Carolina |url=https://jezebel.com/heritage-act-keeps-confederate-flags-flying-in-south-ca-1792769278|work=Jezebel|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref><ref name=Reynolds>{{cite news |url=http://www.13wmaz.com/mb/news/local/georgia-state-law-makes-it-difficult-to-completely-remove-or-hide-confederate-monuments/464932603 |title=Georgia state law makes it difficult to completely remove or hide Confederate monuments |first=Jacob |last=Reynolds |date=August 17, 2017 |newspaper=[[WMAZ]] |access-date=September 4, 2018}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/dc-politics/after-charlottesville-va-democrats-see-opening-to-change-114-year-old-monuments-law/2017/08/25/5e97e766-880e-11e7-a94f-3139abce39f5_story.html |title=After Charlottesville, Va. Democrats see opening to change 114-year-old monuments law |last=Olivo |first=Antonio |date=August 25, 2017 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=August 27, 2017|issn=0190-8286}}</ref>
A 2017 [[Reuters]] poll found that 54% of adults stated that the monuments should remain in all public spaces, and 27% said they should be removed, while 19% said they were unsure. The results were split along racial and political lines, with whites and Republicans preferring to keep the monuments in place, while Black Americans and Democrats were more likely to support their removal.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-protests-poll/a-majority-of-americans-want-to-preserve-confederate-monuments-reuters-ipsos-poll-idUSKCN1B12EG|title=A majority of Americans want to preserve Confederate monuments: Reuters/Ipsos poll|date=August 21, 2017|access-date=October 20, 2017|newspaper=[[Reuters]]}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.ipsos.com/en-us/news-polls/reuters-ipsos-confederate-monuments-2017-08-22|title=Reuters/Ipsos Data: Confederate Monuments|website=ipsos.com|access-date=October 20, 2017|archive-date=October 10, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171010165311/https://www.ipsos.com/en-us/news-polls/reuters-ipsos-confederate-monuments-2017-08-22|url-status=dead}}</ref> A similar 2017 poll by HuffPost/YouGov found that one-third of respondents favored removal, while 49% were opposed.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/confederate-statues-removal-polls_us_599de056e4b05710aa59841c|title=Polls Find Little Support For Confederate Statue Removal – But How You Ask Matters|first=Ariel|last=Edwards-Levy|date=August 23, 2017|access-date=October 20, 2017|via=Huff Post}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://big.assets.huffingtonpost.com/tabsHPConfederateFlag20170815.pdf|title=HuffPost: Confederate Flag, August 15–16, 2017 – 1000 US Adults|website=huffingtonpost.com|access-date=October 20, 2017}}</ref>
Support for removal increased during the [[George Floyd protests]], with 52% in favor of removal, and 44% opposed.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Klar|first=Rebecca|date=June 17, 2020|title=Poll: Majority supports removing Confederate statues from public places|url=https://thehill.com/homenews/news/503226-poll-majority-supports-removing-confederate-statues-from-public-places|access-date=August 22, 2020|newspaper=[[The Hill (newspaper)|The Hill]]|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|publisher=Quinnipiac University|title=QU Poll Release Detail|url=https://poll.qu.edu/national/release-detail?ReleaseID=3663|access-date=August 22, 2020|website=QU Poll|language=en}}</ref>
{|class="wikitable" !Time period !Number of removals<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/graphics/2020/national/confederate-monuments/ |title=Confederate monuments are falling, but hundreds still stand. Here's where. |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=June 20, 2020 |first1=Bonnie |last1=Berkowitz |first2=Adrian |last2=Blanco}}</ref> |- |1865–2009 || 2 |- |2009–2014 || 3 |- |2015 (after [[Charleston church shooting]]) || 4 |- |2016 || 4 |- |2017 (year of the [[Charlottesville car attack]]) || 36 |- |2018 || 8 |- |2019 || 4 |- |2020 (after [[murder of George Floyd]]) || 94<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.npr.org/2021/02/23/970610428/nearly-100-confederate-monuments-removed-in-2020-report-says-more-than-700-remai |title=Nearly 100 Confederate Monuments Removed In 2020, Report Says; More Than 700 Remain |work=[[NPR]] |date=February 23, 2021 |first1=Rachel |last1=Treisman}}</ref> |- |2021 || 16 |}
==Geographic distribution== Confederate monuments are widely distributed across the [[southern United States]] with a few dozen scattered throughout the border states and several hundred at Gettysburg, Pennsylvania.<ref name=":2" /> The distribution pattern follows the general political boundaries of the Confederacy.<ref name=":2" /> Of the more than 1503 public monuments and memorials to the Confederacy, more than 718 are monuments and statues. Nearly 300 monuments and statues are in Georgia, Virginia, or North Carolina. The [[Western United States|western states]] that were largely settled after the Civil War have few or no memorials to the Confederacy.
==National== {{See also|Commemoration of the American Civil War on postage stamps|l1=Civil War commemoration stamps|Vehicle registration plates of the United States#Confederate_States–affinity_plates|l2=CSA-affinity license plates}}
===United States Capitol=== {{Main|Confederate artworks in the United States Capitol}} [[File:National Statuary Hall Collection.jpg|thumb|There are six Confederate figures in the [[National Statuary Hall Collection]], in the [[United States Capitol]].]]
In the [[National Statuary Hall Collection]], housed inside the [[United States Capitol]], each state has provided statues of two citizens that the state wants to honor. Six Confederate figures are among them. The dates listed below reflect when each statue was given to the collection:<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/post-politics/wp/2015/06/23/a-field-guide-to-the-racists-commemorated-inside-the-u-s-capitol/|title=A field guide to the racists commemorated inside the U.S. Capitol|last=DeBonis|first=Mike|date=June 23, 2015|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=August 20, 2017}}</ref><ref name=":10">{{Cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/retropolis/wp/2017/08/16/how-statues-of-robert-e-lee-and-other-confederates-got-into-the-u-s-capitol/|title=How statues of Robert E. Lee and other Confederates got into the U.S. Capitol|last1=Brockell|first1=Gillian|date=August 16, 2017|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=August 20, 2017|last2=Brockell|first2=Gillian|language=en-US|issn=0190-8286}}</ref> * ''[[Statue of Zebulon Baird Vance (U.S. Capitol)|Zebulon Baird Vance]]'' (North Carolina, 1916)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aoc.gov/art/national-statuary-hall-collection/zebulon-vance|title=Zebulon Vance|website=Architect of the Capitol|access-date=August 28, 2017}}</ref> * ''[[Statue of Joseph Wheeler|Joseph Wheeler]]'' (Alabama, 1925)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aoc.gov/art/national-statuary-hall-collection/joseph-wheeler|title=Joseph Wheeler|website=Architect of the Capitol|access-date=August 28, 2017}}</ref> * ''[[Alexander Hamilton Stephens (sculpture)|Alexander Hamilton Stephens]]'' (Georgia, 1927)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aoc.gov/art/national-statuary-hall-collection/alexander-hamilton-stephens|title=Alexander Hamilton Stephens|website=Architect of the Capitol|access-date=August 28, 2017}}</ref> * ''[[Statue of Wade Hampton III|Wade Hampton III]]'' (South Carolina, 1929)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aoc.gov/art/national-statuary-hall-collection/wade-hampton|title=Wade Hampton|website=Architect of the Capitol|access-date=August 28, 2017}}</ref> * ''[[Statue of Jefferson Davis (U.S. Capitol)|Jefferson Davis]]'' (Mississippi, 1931)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aoc.gov/art/national-statuary-hall-collection/jefferson-davis|title=Jefferson Davis|website=Architect of the Capitol|access-date=August 28, 2017}}</ref> * ''[[Statue of James Z. George|James Zachariah George]]'' (Mississippi, 1931)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aoc.gov/art/national-statuary-hall-collection/james-zachariah-george|title=James Zachariah George|website=Architect of the Capitol|access-date=August 28, 2017}}</ref>
In addition to these pieces, four additional sculptures of Confederate figures have been removed since the turn of the 21st century.
* ''[[Statue of Jabez Lamar Monroe Curry|Jabez Lamar Monroe Curry]]'' (Alabama, 1908) was removed and replaced by a [[Statue of Helen Keller|statue]] of [[Helen Keller]] in 2009.<ref name="auto1">{{cite web|url=https://www.aoc.gov/art/national-statuary-hall-collection/helen-keller|title=Helen Keller|website=Architect of the Capitol|access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref> * ''[[Statue of Robert E. Lee (Valentine)|Robert E. Lee]]'' (Virginia, 1909) was removed in 2020.<ref name="Stewart"/> In January 2023, the design of a replacement statue of [[Barbara Rose Johns]] was revealed. At the time, sculptor [[Steven Weitzman (sculptor)|Steven Weitzman]] stated that the statue would be ready for installation sometime in 2024.<ref name="Stewart">{{cite news|first=Ian M.|last=Stewart|title=Barbara Rose Johns statue design unveiled|date=January 4, 2023|website=VPM|url=https://www.vpm.org/news/2023-01-04/barbara-rose-johns-statue-design-unveiled|access-date=May 30, 2023}}</ref> * ''[[Statue of Edmund Kirby Smith|Edmund Kirby Smith]]'' (Florida, 1922) was removed in 2021 and replaced by a [[Statue of Mary McLeod Bethune (U.S. Capitol)|statue]] of [[Mary McLeod Bethune]] in 2022.<ref>{{cite news|title=Mary McLeod Bethune becomes first Black American honored in U.S. Capitol's Statuary Hall|website=[[PBS NewsHour]]|date=July 13, 2022|url=https://www.pbs.org/newshour/show/mary-mcleod-bethune-becomes-first-black-american-honored-in-u-s-capitols-statuary-hall|access-date=May 30, 2023|language=en-us|archive-date=July 14, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220714151118/https://www.pbs.org/newshour/show/mary-mcleod-bethune-becomes-first-black-american-honored-in-u-s-capitols-statuary-hall|url-status=live}}</ref> * ''[[Statue of U. M. Rose|Uriah M. Rose]]'' (Arkansas, 1917)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aoc.gov/art/national-statuary-hall-collection/uriah-milton-rose|title=Uriah Milton Rose|website=Architect of the Capitol|access-date=April 7, 2018}}</ref> In 2019, the Arkansas legislature voted to replace both of its contributions to the collection, the statue of Rose and one of [[James Paul Clarke]].<ref>{{cite news|first=Ben|last=Peters|title=Johnny Cash is replacing one of the Capitol's Civil War statues|website=Roll Call|date=April 17, 2019|url=https://rollcall.com/2019/04/17/johnny-cash-is-replacing-one-of-the-capitols-civil-war-statues/|access-date=May 30, 2023}}</ref> In 2024, the statues were replaced by the statues of [[Statue of Johnny Cash|Johnny Cash]] and [[Daisy Bates (activist)|Daisy Bates]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Johnny Cash Statue, U.S. Capitol for Arkansas {{!}} AOC |url=https://www.aoc.gov/explore-capitol-campus/art/johnny-cash-statue |website=www.aoc.gov |publisher=Architect of the Capitol |access-date=11 February 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Daisy Bates Statue, U.S. Capitol for Arkansas {{!}} AOC |url=https://www.aoc.gov/explore-capitol-campus/art/daisy-bates-statue |website=www.aoc.gov |publisher=Architect of the Capitol |access-date=11 February 2025}}</ref> Rose's statue was moved to his former law firm in [[Little Rock, Arkansas]] in 2025.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Reddmann |first1=Caroline |title=Historic Statue of U.M. Rose Now On Display at Rose Law Firm |url=https://www.roselawfirm.com/historic-statue-of-u-m-rose-now-on-display-at-rose-law-firm/ |website=Rose Law Firm |access-date=11 February 2025 |date=29 January 2025}}</ref>
===Arlington National Cemetery=== {{Main|List of memorials and monuments at Arlington National Cemetery}}
[[File:Confederate Monument - S face tight - Arlington National Cemetery - 2011.jpg|thumb|upright|Confederate Memorial, Arlington National Cemetery]] * The antebellum home of [[Robert E. Lee]] during the [[American Civil War|Civil War]], [[Arlington House, The Robert E. Lee Memorial|Arlington House]], in [[Arlington County, Virginia]], overlooks [[Arlington National Cemetery]]. A [[National Park Service]] (NPS) memorial, the estate became the site of Arlington National Cemetery in part to ensure that Lee could never live there.<ref>{{cite web|title=The Beginnings of Arlington National Cemetery|url=https://www.nps.gov/arho/learn/historyculture/cemetery.htm|website=Arlington House, The Robert E. Lee Memorial|publisher=National Park Service|access-date=October 7, 2017}}</ref>
:The NPS describes the property as "the nation's memorial to [[Robert E. Lee]]. It honors him for specific reasons, including his role in promoting peace and reunion after the Civil War. In a larger sense it exists as a place of study and contemplation of the meaning of some of the most difficult aspects of American History: military service; sacrifice; citizenship; duty; loyalty; slavery and freedom."<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nps.gov/arho/index.htm|title=Arlington House, The Robert E. Lee Memorial (U.S. National Park Service)|website=www.nps.gov|access-date=October 8, 2017}}</ref> * The [[Confederate Memorial (Arlington National Cemetery)|Confederate Memorial]] at Arlington National Cemetery was a project of the [[United Daughters of the Confederacy]] authorized in 1906 by the [[United States Secretary of War]] [[William Howard Taft]] and unveiled by [[Woodrow Wilson|President Wilson]] in 1914.<ref>[http://www.arlingtoncemetery.org/Visitor_information/Confederate_Memorial.html Visitor Information: Monuments and Memorials: Confederate Memorial] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090725141115/http://www.arlingtoncemetery.org/visitor_information/Confederate_Memorial.html|date=July 25, 2009}}, Arlington National Cemetery website, accessed April 24, 2010</ref> The memorial was removed in December 2023.<ref>{{Cite news |title= Confederate memorial removed from Arlington National Cemetery|newspaper=Washington Post|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/dc-md-va/2023/12/20/confederate-statue-removal-arlington-cemetery/|date=December 20, 2023|access-date=January 8, 2023}}</ref>
===Coins and stamps=== {{See also|Commemoration of the American Civil War on postage stamps}}
* Robert E. Lee and Stonewall Jackson were portrayed by the [[US Mint]] on the [[Stone Mountain Memorial half dollar|1925 Commemorative silver US half dollar]], along with the words "Stone Mountain". The coin was a fundraiser for the [[Stone Mountain]] monument, which honors the Confederate Generals. The authorized issue was 5 million coins, to be sold at $1 each, but that proved overly optimistic and only 1.3 million coins were released, many of which ended up in circulation after being spent for face value.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.usrarecoininvestments.com/collecting/stone-mountain-halfdollar.htm|title=Information about the Stone Mountain Half Dollar coin|last=Pilitowski|first=Tom|website=U.S. Rare Coin Investments|language=en|access-date=August 23, 2017|archive-date=November 20, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181120184703/http://www.usrarecoininvestments.com/collecting/stone-mountain-halfdollar.htm|url-status=dead}}</ref> The caption on the reverse reads "Memorial to the valor of the soldier of the South". * Robert E. Lee has been commemorated on at least five US postage stamps. One 1936–37 stamp featured Generals Lee and Stonewall Jackson with Lee's home [[Stratford Hall (plantation)|Stratford Hall]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://civilwartalk.com/threads/robert-e-lee-on-u-s-postage-stamps.121649/|title=Robert E. Lee on U.S. Postage Stamps|website=civilwartalk.com|date=February 18, 2016 |access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref>
===US military===
====Bases==== {{see also|List of U.S. Army installations named for Confederate soldiers}}
Prior to 2023, there were nine major U.S. military bases named in honor of Confederate military leaders, all in former Confederate states. Following [[George Floyd protests|nationwide protests]] over the [[murder of George Floyd]] by a police officer, the [[United States Congress]] in 2021 created [[The Naming Commission]] in order to rename military assets with names associated with the Confederacy.<ref>{{Cite web|title=The Naming Commission|url=https://www.thenamingcommission.gov/|url-status=dead|access-date=2 September 2021|website=www.thenamingcommission.gov|language=en-US|archive-date=September 2, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210902200452/https://www.thenamingcommission.gov/}}</ref> The [[United States Secretary of Defense]] was required to implement a plan developed by the commission and to "remove all names, symbols, displays, monuments, and paraphernalia that honor or commemorate the Confederate States of America or any person who served voluntarily with the Confederate States of America from all assets of the [[United States Department of Defense|Department of Defense]]" within three years of the commission's creation.<ref>{{cite web |title=H.R.6395 – 116th Congress (2019–2020): National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021 |url=https://www.congress.gov/bill/116th-congress/house-bill/6395/text |work=U.S. Congress |access-date=12 February 2021 |date=1 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Homan|first=Timothy R.|date=12 February 2021|title=Pentagon, Congress appoint panel members to rename Confederate base names|url=https://thehill.com/policy/defense/538645-pentagon-congress-appoint-panel-members-to-rename-confederate-base-names|access-date=3 September 2021|website=[[The Hill (newspaper)|The Hill]]|language=en}}</ref>
By October 2023, all nine bases had officially been redesignated under new names proposed by the commission.
* Fort A.P. Hill (1941), near [[Bowling Green, Virginia]], named for Confederate General [[A. P. Hill]], was redesignated [[Fort Walker]] on 25 August 2023 in honor of [[Medal of Honor]] recipient and civilian army surgeon Dr. [[Mary Edwards Walker]]<ref name=px/><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.stripes.com/theaters/us/2023-08-25/fort-walker-ap-hill-confederate-army-11162161.html |title= Fort AP Hill renamed Fort Walker to honor Army's first female surgeon |first=Doug |last=Ware |newspaper=[[Stars and Stripes (newspaper)|Stars and Stripes]] |access-date=November 13, 2023 |date=August 25, 2023}}</ref> * Fort Benning (1917), near [[Columbus, Georgia]], named for Confederate General [[Henry L. Benning]], was redesignated [[Fort Moore]] on 11 May 2023 in honor of General [[Hal Moore]] and his wife [[Julia Compton Moore]]<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.stripes.com/branches/army/2023-05-11/fort-moore-benning-georgia-army-10087193.html |title='Our name may be changing, but our mission is not': Army's Fort Benning is now Fort Moore |first=Corey |last=Dickstein |newspaper=[[Stars and Stripes (newspaper)|Stars and Stripes]] |date=May 11, 2023}}</ref> * [[Fort Bragg]] (1918), in North Carolina, named for Confederate General [[Braxton Bragg]], was redesignated Fort Liberty on 2 June 2023 in honor of [[Liberty]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Fort Bragg in North Carolina becomes Fort Bragg, dropping name of Confederate general|work=[[CBS News]]|date=June 2, 2023|url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/fort-bragg-name-change-fort-liberty-confederate-general/}}</ref> On 14 February 2025, it was again renamed to Fort Bragg, though this time under the namesake of U.S. Army paratrooper [[Roland L. Bragg]] from World War II, who holds no relation to Braxton Bragg. * Fort Gordon (1917), near [[Augusta, Georgia]], named for Confederate General [[John Brown Gordon]], was redesignated as [[Fort Eisenhower]] on 27 October 2023 in honor of president [[Dwight D. Eisenhower]]<ref name=px >Robert Philpot [https://publicaffairs-sme.com/FamilyServingFamily/2023/03/22/exchange-teams-prepare-for-big-changes-as-9-army-posts-get-new-names/ (22 Mar 2023) Exchange Teams Prepare for Big Changes as 9 Army Posts Get New Names]</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.stripes.com/branches/army/2023-10-27/fort-eisenhower%C2%A0gordon-georgia-confederate-11850282.html |title=Final Army base stripped of Confederate name as Fort Gordon becomes Fort Eisenhower |first=Corey |last=Dickstein |newspaper=[[Stars and Stripes (newspaper)|Stars and Stripes]] |access-date=November 13, 2023 |date=October 27, 2023}}</ref> * Fort Hood (1942), in [[Killeen, Texas]], formerly named after Confederate General [[John Bell Hood]], was redesignated [[Fort Cavazos]] on 9 May 2023 in honor of General [[Richard Cavazos]]<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.stripes.com/branches/army/2023-05-09/army-fort-cavazos-hood-confederate-texas-10064502.html |title=Fort Hood becomes Fort Cavazos, paying homage to general from Texas known for warrior ethos, selfless service |first=Rose |last=Thayer |newspaper=[[Stars and Stripes (newspaper)|Stars and Stripes]] |date=May 9, 2023}}</ref> * Fort Lee (1917), in [[Prince George County, Virginia]], named after Confederate General [[Robert E. Lee]], was redesignated [[Fort Gregg-Adams]] on 27 April 2023 in honor of Lieutenant General [[Arthur J. Gregg]] and Lieutenant Colonel [[Charity Adams]]<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.stripes.com/theaters/us/2023-04-27/fort-gregg-adams-army-confederates-9942691.html |title=Fort Lee renamed Fort Gregg-Adams to honor two pioneering Black officers |first=Matthew |last=Adams |newspaper=[[Stars and Stripes (newspaper)|Stars and Stripes]] |date=April 27, 2023}}</ref> * Fort Pickett (1942), near [[Blackstone, Virginia]], a [[Virginia National Guard]] installation named for Confederate General [[George Pickett]], was redesignated [[Fort Barfoot]] on 24 March 2023 in honor of Medal of Honor recipient Colonel [[Van T. Barfoot]]<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.army.mil/article/265145/vng_installation_officially_redesignated_fort_barfoot |title=VNG installation officially redesignated Fort Barfoot |first=Mike |last=Vrabel |date=24 March 2023 |work=U.S. Army}}</ref> * Fort Polk (1941), near [[Leesville, Louisiana]], named for [[Episcopal Church (United States)|Episcopal]] [[bishop]] and Confederate General [[Leonidas Polk]], was redesignated [[Fort Johnson]] on 13 June 2023 in honor of Medal of Honor recipient Sergeant [[Henry Johnson (World War I soldier)|William Henry Johnson]]<ref name=px/><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.stripes.com/branches/army/2023-06-13/fort-polk-johnson-confederate-army-10427731.html |title=Fort Polk renamed Fort Johnson in honor of Black WWI hero |first=Rose |last=Thayer |newspaper=[[Stars and Stripes (newspaper)|Stars and Stripes]] |access-date=November 13, 2023 |date=June 13, 2023}}</ref> * Fort Rucker (1942), in [[Dale County, Alabama]], named for Confederate Colonel [[Edmund Rucker]], was redesignated [[Fort Novosel]] on 10 April 2023 in honor of Medal of Honor recipient Chief Warrant Officer 4 [[Michael J. Novosel]]<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.cnn.com/2023/04/10/us/fort-rucker-name-change-novosel/index.html |title= Fort Rucker was named for a Confederate. The Army post will now be called Fort Novosel, for a Medal of Honor recipient who rescued thousands |first=Phil |last=Gast |work=[[CNN]] |date=11 April 2023}}</ref>
====Facilities==== * Lee Barracks, named for CSA Gen. Robert E. Lee (1962), at [[United States Military Academy|U.S. Military Academy]] at West Point, New York.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/national/2-us-lawmakers-from-ny-rename-lee-barracks-at-west-point/2017/08/17/dba88a94-835e-11e7-9e7a-20fa8d7a0db6_story.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170817202356/https://www.washingtonpost.com/national/2-us-lawmakers-from-ny-rename-lee-barracks-at-west-point/2017/08/17/dba88a94-835e-11e7-9e7a-20fa8d7a0db6_story.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 17, 2017|title=2 NY lawmakers: Strip Robert E. Lee's name from West Point|last=Carola|first=Chris|date=August 17, 2017|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=August 28, 2017}}</ref> * [[United States Naval Academy|U.S. Naval Academy]] in Annapolis, Maryland: ** Buchanan House, the Naval Academy superintendent's home, named for CSA naval officer [[Franklin Buchanan]].<ref name=":11">{{Cite news|last=Witte|first=Brian|url=https://www.foxnews.com/us/confederate-names-at-naval-academy-could-face-rough-seas/|title=Confederate names at Naval Academy could face rough seas|date=August 23, 2017|work=[[Fox News]]|access-date=October 5, 2017|language=en-US}}</ref> A road near the house is also memorialized in Buchanan's name. ** Maury Hall, home to the academy's division of Weapons and Systems Engineering, named for US naval officer in charge of the Depot of Charts and Instruments at Washington and later CSA naval officer [[Matthew Fontaine Maury]].<ref name=":11" /><ref name=diana>Diana Fontaine Maury-Corbin [[s:Matthew Fontaine Maury|"Life of Matthew Fontaine Maury USN & CSN"]] ''Life of Matthew Fontaine Maury, U.S.N. and C.S.N.''</ref>
====Current ships==== * USNS ''Maury'' (T-AGS-66) (2013), named for [[Matthew Fontaine Maury]], was renamed [[USNS Marie Tharp|USNS ''Marie Tharp'']] for oceanographer [[Marie Tharp]] in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.military.com/daily-news/2023/03/08/navy-renames-survey-ship-after-pioneering-female-ocean-researcher-stripping-vessel-of-confederate.html|title=Navy Renames Survey Ship After Pioneering Female Ocean Researcher, Stripping Vessel of Confederate Ties|last=Toropin|first=Konstantin|date=March 8, 2023|publisher=[[Military.com]]|access-date=February 28, 2024}}</ref>
====Former ships==== {{See also|List of ships of the Confederate States Navy}}
* [[USS LST-60|USS ''Atchison County'' (LST-60)]], named for counties in Kansas and Missouri established in honor of Brigadier General [[David Rice Atchison]] * [[USS Brooke (FFG-1)|USS ''Brooke'' (FFG-1)]] – frigate named after Confederate marine engineer [[John Mercer Brooke]] * USS ''Buchanan'': Three U.S. Navy destroyers have been named in honor of the highest ranked Confederate Admiral [[Franklin Buchanan]] ** [[USS Buchanan (DD-131)|USS ''Buchanan'' (DD-131)]] 1919–1940 then transferred to UK Navy ** [[USS Buchanan (DD-484)|USS ''Buchanan'' (DD-484)]] 1941–1949 then transferred to Turkey's Navy ** [[USS Buchanan (DDG-14)|USS ''Buchanan'' (DDG-14)]] 1960–1991 then sank as target in 2000 * [[USS Dixon (AS-37)|USS ''Dixon'' (AS-37)]] – Submarine tender named after Confederate submarine commander [[George E. Dixon]] * [[USS Hunley (AS-31)|USS ''Hunley'' (AS-31)]] – Submarine tender named after Confederate marine engineer [[Horace Lawson Hunley]] * [[USS Maury|USS ''Maury'']] – 5 former ships have carried the Maury name dating from WWI and WWII. * [[USS Robert E. Lee (SSBN-601)|USS ''Robert E Lee'' (SSBN-601)]] (ballistic missile submarine) in honor of Gen. [[Robert E. Lee]]. * [[USS Richard L. Page|USS ''Richard L. Page'' (FFG-5)]] – frigate named after Confederate General [[Richard Lucian Page]] * [[USS Semmes|USS ''Semmes'']] – two destroyers have been named for [[Raphael Semmes]]. ** [[USS Semmes (DD-189)|USS ''Semmes'' (DD-189)]] 1920–1946 ** [[USS Semmes (DDG-18)|USS ''Semmes'' (DDG-18)]] 1962–1991 * [[USS Stonewall Jackson (SSBN-634)|USS ''Stonewall Jackson'' (SSBN-634)]] 1964–1995. * [[USS Tattnall (DDG-19)|USS ''Tattnall'' (DDG-19)]] – destroyer named after Confederate Commodore [[Josiah Tattnall III]] * [[USS Tom Green County (LST-1159)|USS ''Tom Green County'' (LST-1159)]] 1953–1972 then transferred to Spain. The namesake Texas County was named for CSA Brig Gen [[Thomas Green (general)|Thomas Green]] * [[USS Waddell|USS ''Waddell'' (DDG-24)]] – destroyer named after Confederate Captain [[James Iredell Waddell]] * {{SS|Zebulon B. Vance}}, a [[World War II]] [[liberty ship]], named for CSA Colonel and North Carolina Confederate governor [[Zebulon Baird Vance]]. *[[Liberty Ship]] #113, named for [[Joseph E. Johnston]] by the US Navy.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skylighters.org/troopships/libertyships.html|title=Troopships of World War II: Liberty Ships|website=www.skylighters.org|access-date=October 23, 2017|archive-date=August 19, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170819033955/http://www.skylighters.org/troopships/libertyships.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> * Liberty ship #5, named for [[Alexander H. Stephens]] * Liberty ship #8, named for [[Jefferson Davis]] Several ships named for Confederate leaders fell into Union hands during the Civil War. The Union Navy retained the names of these ships while turning their guns against the Confederacy: *[[USS Beauregard|''Beauregard'']] a [[privateer]] with [[letters of marque]] issued by the Confederacy, named in honor of Gen. [[P. G. T. Beauregard]]. Captured as a prize and purchased on February 24, 1862, by the Union Navy which operated it as the USS ''Beauregard''. *[[Laurent Millaudon (1856)|USS ''General Price'']] (1862) a Confederate ship sunk in battle, raised and used by the Union until sold in 1865.
==Multi-state highways== * [[Jefferson Davis Highway]], [[Arlington, Virginia]], to [[San Diego, California]]. The highway labeling (no road construction was involved) was a project of the [[United Daughters of the Confederacy]]. Never completed as originally planned, and its route is not completely clear. Markers that remain are listed under the states. :On October 16, 2018, the [[Board of Commissioners]] of [[Orange County, North Carolina]] (location of the [[University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill]], see [[Silent Sam]]), voted unanimously to repeal the county's 1959 resolution naming for Davis the portion of [[U.S. 15]] running through the county.<ref name=Us15>{{cite news |title=A Chapel Hill highway no longer honors a Confederate leader. But what about the sign? |first=Tammy |last=GRubb |newspaper=[[Herald Sun]] |date=October 16, 2018 |url=https://www.heraldsun.com/news/local/counties/orange-county/article220101200.html}}</ref> * [[Lee Highway]], [[New York City]] to [[San Francisco]]. Markers that remain are listed under the states. * [[Dixie Highway]] - a road from Canada to Miami * [[Dixie Overland Highway]] - a road from California to Georgia
==Alabama== {{main|List of Confederate monuments and memorials in Alabama}}
{{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 122 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Alabama]].
==Alaska== * [[Yukon–Koyukuk Census Area, Alaska|Yukon–Koyukuk Census Area]]: "Confederate Gulch"<ref>{{Cite GNIS|1412833|Confederate Gulch}}</ref> and "Union Gulch" both drain the side of a mineralized mountain mass northeast of [[Wiseman, Alaska|Wiseman]]. Gold was discovered in both gulches in the early 20th century, though only Union Gulch was mined.<ref>{{cite web | last = Maddren | first = A. G. | title = The Koyukuk-Chandalar Region, Alaska | publisher = United States Government Printing Office | date = 1913 | url = https://pubs.usgs.gov/bul/0532/report.pdf }}</ref>
==Arizona== {{As of|2020|August|20}}, only two Confederate related plaques on public property remain in Phoenix and Sierra Vista, [[Arizona]]. {{Further|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Arizona}}
{| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! Type of monument ! Date ! Location ! class="unsortable" | Details ! class="unsortable" | Image |- | Public || 2010 ||[[Sierra Vista, Arizona|Sierra Vista]]|| Confederate Memorial, Historical Soldiers Memorial Cemetery area of the state-owned Southern Arizona Veterans' Memorial Cemetery. The monument was erected in to honor the 21 soldiers interred in that cemetery who served in the Confederate Army during the Civil War and later fought in Indian wars in Arizona as members of the U.S. Army.<ref name="azmonuments" /><ref>{{cite web|url=https://dvs.az.gov/southern-memorial-cemetery|title=Southern Memorial Cemetery|website=Arizona Department of Veterans' Services|access-date=August 30, 2017|archive-date=October 20, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201020180829/https://dvs.az.gov/southern-memorial-cemetery|url-status=dead}}</ref>|| |- | Private || 1999 || [[Phoenix, Arizona|Phoenix]] || Arizona Confederate Veterans Monument, at [[Greenwood/Memory Lawn Mortuary & Cemetery|Greenwood Memory Lawn Cemetery]]; erected by SCV.<ref name="azmonuments"/> || [[File:CSA cemetery marker, Phoenix AZ, USA.jpg|100px]] |- | Public || 1961–2020||[[Phoenix, Arizona|Phoenix]]|| Memorial to Arizona Confederate Troops, in [[Wesley Bolin]] Park, next to the [[Arizona State Capitol]]; UDC memorial.<ref name="azmonuments">{{cite news|url=http://www.kmov.com/story/36151567/slideshow-where-are-arizonas-confederate-monuments|title=Slideshow: Where are Arizona's Confederate monuments?|publisher=KMOV|date=August 16, 2017|access-date=August 29, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170830104310/http://www.kmov.com/story/36151567/slideshow-where-are-arizonas-confederate-monuments|archive-date=August 30, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref>||[[File:CSA monument, Phoenix AZ, USA.jpg|100px]] |- | Road || 1943–2020 || ||[[Jefferson Davis Memorial Highway]] marker {{convert|50|mi|km|abbr=on}} east of Phoenix; erected by UDC. [[Tarred and feathered]] in August 2017.<ref name="azmonuments" /><ref name=":14">{{Cite news|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-protests-statues-reaction-idUSKCN1AX2TK|title=Statue defaced as U.S. Confederate monument protests grow|last=Simpson|first=Ian|date=August 18, 2017|work=[[Reuters]]|access-date=August 26, 2017}}</ref>|| |- | Public || 1984–2015 |[[Picacho Peak State Park]]||[[Battle of Picacho Pass#Gallery|A commemorative sign and a plaque]] commemorated the [[Battle of Picacho Pass]], the westernmost Confederate engagement of the war. The sign is "dedicated to Capt. Sherod Hunter's 'Arizona Rangers, Arizona Volunteers' C.S.A.", while the plaque states three Union soldiers buried on battlefield and includes both US Union and CSA flags. The sign was removed in 2015 due to deterioration of the wood and the plaque was moved onto the Union stone monument.<ref name="azmonuments" /><ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.colorlines.com/articles/black-leaders-fight-remove-arizonas-confederate-monuments|title=Black Leaders Fight to Remove Arizona's Confederate Monuments|date=June 5, 2017|author=Sameer Rao|publisher=ColorLines.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/news/national/arizona-civil-rights-leaders-call-remove-confederate-memorials-article-1.3231096|title=Arizona civil rights leaders call to remove Confederate memorials|last=Schladebeck|first=Jessica|date=June 8, 2017|work=[[New York Daily News]]|access-date=August 29, 2017|language=en}}</ref>||[[File:Picacho-Battle of Picacho Marker.jpg|100px]] |}
==Arkansas== {{see also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Arkansas}}
{{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 65 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Arkansas]].
===State capitol=== * [[Confederate Soldiers Monument (Little Rock, Arkansas)|Confederate Soldiers Monument]], also known as ''Defense of the Flag'', [[Arkansas State Capitol]] grounds, (1905).<ref name="walkhill">{{cite web|url=http://www.sos.arkansas.gov/stateCapitolInfo/Documents/a_walk_on_the_hill.pdf|title=A Walk on the Hill|website=Arkansas.gov|access-date=January 3, 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170207081554/http://www.sos.arkansas.gov/stateCapitolInfo/Documents/a_walk_on_the_hill.pdf|archive-date=February 7, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> * Confederate War Prisoners Memorial, Arkansas State Capitol grounds.<ref name="walkhill" /> * [[Monument to Confederate Women]] (or "Mother of the South"), [[Arkansas State Capitol]] grounds, Little Rock, Arkansas. Statue depicts a mother and daughter saying good-bye to their 16-year-old son and brother who is leaving to join his father in the fighting. (1913)<ref name="NPSArkansas" /><ref>{{cite book |title=Defeat and Memory at the Arkansas State Capitol: The Little Rock Monument to the Women of the Confederacy, 1896–1914 |first=Zachary |last=Elledge |publisher=Thesis, Master of Arts, [[Arkansas State University]] |year=2015 |url=https://www.academia.edu/18637571}}</ref> * [[Old State House (Little Rock)|Old State House]], several on the grounds:<ref>{{cite web|author1=Cynthia DeHaven Pitcock|title=Old State House – National Historic Landmark Nomination|url=https://www.nps.gov/nhl/find/statelists/ar/OldStateHouse.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170207034117/https://www.nps.gov/nhl/find/statelists/ar/OldStateHouse.pdf|url-status=dead|archive-date=February 7, 2017|website=National Park Service|publisher=National Register of Historic Places Registration Form|access-date=September 28, 2017|pages=6–7|date=July 29, 1997}}</ref> ** [[David O. Dodd Memorial]] (1923) ** Defenders Memorial Plaque (1932)<ref>{{cite web|author1=QuesterMark|title=Little Rock Defenders Memorial Plaque – Little Rock, Arkansas|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMAYNV_Little_Rock_Defenders_Memorial_Plaque_Little_Rock_Arkansas|website=Waymarking.com|access-date=September 28, 2017}}</ref> ** [[Thomas James Churchill|Gen. Thomas J. Churchill]] Memorial (1928) ** [[William Read Scurry|Gen. William Read Scurry]] Memorial (1928) ** Old State House Confederate Memorial<ref>{{cite web|author1=QuesterMark|title=Old State House Civil War Memorial Plaque – Little Rock, Arkansas|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMAYX4_Old_State_House_Civil_War_Memorial_Plaque_Little_Rock_Arkansas|website=Waymarking.com|access-date=September 28, 2017}}</ref>

Marmaduke|John Sappington Marmaduke]] Memorial * [[Monticello, Arkansas|Monticello]]: [[Monticello Confederate Monument]] * [[New Edinburg, Arkansas|New Edinburg]]: Captain Richard Tunball Banks Monument, 1864. * [[Newport, Arkansas|Newport]]: [[Jackson Guards Memorial]], built in 1914. Monument consists of a statue of a single Confederate soldier and a roster of the men who served in the Jackson Guards and the [[Slavery in the United States|slaves]] who supported them. The only Confederate monument in Arkansas built entirely with funds raised by private subscription, although it was built on a prominent piece of land donated by the city of Hot Springs.<ref name="NPSArkansas">{{NRHP url|id=64500015|title=National Register of Historic Places Multiple Property Registration Form: Civil War Commemorative Sculpture in Arkansas, 1886–1934}}, 1996.</ref> * [[Pea Ridge, Arkansas|Pea Ridge]]: ** Confederate Generals Memorial (1887)<ref>{{cite web|title=Confederate Generals Memorial|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WM3JVV_CONFEDERATE_GENERALS_MEMORIAL|website=Waymarking.com|publisher=silverquill|access-date=September 27, 2017}}</ref> ** Reunited Soldiery Monument (1889), one of the first to honor both Confederate and Union soldiers to be placed on a battlefield.<ref>{{cite web|title=Reunited Soldiery Monument – Pea Ridge Battlefield|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WM3JV4_Reunited_Soldiery_Monument_Pea_Ridge_Battlefield|website=Waymarking.com|publisher=silverquill|access-date=September 27, 2017}}</ref> ** Texas Memorial, Pea Ridge City Park (1964).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.thecivilwarmuse.com/index.php?page=mckissick-s-creek|title=Pea Ridge Texas Monument|website=The Civil War Muse}}</ref> * [[Pine Bluff, Arkansas|Pine Bluff]]: ** David O. ===Parks=== * [[Russellville, Arkansas|Russellville]]: [[Confederate Mothers Memorial Park]] (1921). Land for the public park was donated by UDC and includes three stone monuments, each one placed by a different Confederate veterans or memorial organization, honoring the mothers of the [[Confederate States of America|Confederacy]].<ref name=NRHP>{{cite web|url=http://www.arkansaspreservation.com/National-Register-Listings/PDF/PP0344S.pdf|title=NRHP nomination for Confederate Mothers Memorial Park|publisher=Arkansas Preservation|access-date=April 21, 2016}}</ref>

===State symbols=== [[File:Flag of Arkansas.svg|thumb|right|Flag of Arkansas since 1913]] * [[Flag of Arkansas]] The blue star above "ARKANSAS" represents the Confederate States of America and is placed above the three other stars for the countries (Spain, France and the US) to which the State belonged before statehood. The diamond shape represents the nation's only diamond mine with a border of 25 stars, symbolizing the 25th U.S. state.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.msnbc.com/msnbc/these-5-states-still-use-confederate-symbols-their-flags|title=These 5 states still use Confederate symbols in their flags|website=Msnbc.com|date=June 23, 2015 |access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref> The design of the border around the white diamond evokes the saltire found on the Confederate battle flag.<ref>Ark. Code Ann. (1987), Section 1–4–101; cited in B.F. Shearer and B.S. Shearer (2002), State Names, Seals, Flags, and Symbols, Greenwood Press, p. 54</ref>
==California== {{see also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#California}} {{As of|2020|July|23}}, there were at least four public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[California]].
===Inhabited places=== * Confederate Corners, crossroads near [[Salinas, California|Salinas]], in [[Monterey County]], fictionalized as Rebel Corners in [[John Steinbeck]]'s novel ''The Wayward Bus''. In 2018, name officially changed to [[Springtown, California]], which it had been known by prior to becoming Confederate Corners.<ref>"The Forgotten Anti-Rebel Yell in Steinbeck's ''The Wayward Bus'', Steinbeck Now. |url=http://www.steinbecknow.com/2017/10/30/anti-rebel-yell-steinbecks-wayward-bus/ 30 Oct. 2017."</ref>
===Roads=== * Los Angeles: Johnston Street in [[Lincoln Heights, Los Angeles|Lincoln Heights]], named for CSA Gen. [[Albert Sidney Johnston]]. * There are at least four remaining markers of the [[Jefferson Davis Highway]] in the state of California including the following:<ref name="archive.org">{{cite web|url=http://www.rootsweb.ancestry.com/~caudc/jdhway.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170816163806/http://www.rootsweb.ancestry.com/~caudc/jdhway.htm|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 16, 2017|title=J. D. Highway|date=August 16, 2017|website=Rootsweb.ancestry.com|access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref> ** [[Bakersfield, California|Bakersfield]] in Pioneer Village (1942, rededicated 1968) ** [[Hornbrook]] (1944) ** [[Winterhaven, California|Winterhaven]] at [[Fort Yuma]]
===Schools=== * [[Anaheim, California|Anaheim]]: [[Savanna High School]] (1961) mascot has always been [[Johnny Rebel]] and a fiberglass statue of a Confederate soldier stood in the courtyard from 1964 until 2009<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.ocregister.com/2015/02/19/school-board-rejects-bid-to-restore-rebel-statue/|title=School board rejects bid to restore 'Rebel' statue|date=February 19, 2015|website=ocregister.com|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> when it was removed due to deterioration. The school colors are red and grey and the school fields the Savanna Mighty Marching Rebel Band and Color Guard.
===Mountains and recreation=== *[[Alabama Hills]], named for {{ship|CSS|Alabama}}<ref name=kyle>Kyle, Douglas E. and Hoover, Mildred Brooke (1990). ''Historic Spots in California'', p. 122. Stanford, CA: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|0804744831}}.</ref> * Jeff Davis Peak Elevation: 9065 ft / 2763 m {{Coord|36.637|N|119.897|W|display=inline}} in the [[Mokelumne Wilderness]]<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.summitpost.org/jeff-davis-peak/155336|title=Jeff Davis Peak : Climbing, Hiking & Mountaineering : SummitPost|website=www.summitpost.org|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> mapped by the USGS in 1889; "however, it may have long been used locally, as many of the inhabitants of nearby Summit City (now abandoned [in the late 1860s]) were Confederate sympathizers during the civil war. Jefferson Davis (1809–89) was president of the Confederacy, 1861–65."<ref>Erwin E. Gudde, California Place Names</ref> * Pickett Peak: named for Confederate General [[George Pickett]]. Elevation: 9118 ft / 2779 m {{Coord|38.756|N|119.903|W|display=inline}} in National Forest, near the [[Mokelumne Wilderness]]<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.summitpost.org/pickett-peak/632459|title=Pickett Peak : Climbing, Hiking & Mountaineering : SummitPost|website=www.summitpost.org|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> * Fortuna: Pickett Peak Campground operated by the National Forest Service<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.campscout.com/campground/CA1078/|title=Camping at Pickett Peak Campground near Mad River – California|website=www.campscout.com|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> * The [[Robert E. Lee (tree)|Robert E. Lee giant sequoia]] in [[Kings Canyon National Park]] run by the [[National Park Service]].
===Mine=== [[File:Stonewall Jackson Mine, San Diego County, California.jpg|thumb|right|Stonewall Jackson Mine, San Diego County, circa 1872]] * [[San Diego County, California|San Diego County]]: Stonewall Jackson Mine (1870–1893), the richest gold mine in southern California history<ref>{{cite journal | last = Peterson | first = Richard H. | date = July 2001 | title = The Stonewall Jackson Mine, San Diego County, California | url = https://www.icmj.com/magazine/article/the-stonewall-jackson-mine-san-diego-county-california-2024/ | journal = ICMJ Prospecting and Mining Journal}}</ref>
==Colorado== [[File:Robert E. Lee Mine, Leadville, Colorado.jpg|thumb|right|Robert E. Lee Mine in Leadville. Photo by [[William Henry Jackson]].]]
===Inhabited Places=== * [[Breckenridge, Colorado|Breckenridge]]
===Schools=== * [[Keenesburg, Colorado|Keenesburg]]: Weld Central Senior High School and Weld Central Middle School share the Weld Central Rebel, a Civil-war-era-soldier which used to appear with depictions of Confederate flags. School teams are named Rebels.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.greeleytribune.com/news/local/confederate-themed-mascot-at-weld-central-high-school-stirs-up-controversy/|title=Confederate-themed mascot at Weld Central High School stirs up controversy|last=Redmond|first=James|date=August 26, 2017|work=The Greely Tribune|access-date=August 31, 2017|language=en}}</ref>
=== Monument === * Confederate monument [[Riverside Cemetery (Denver, Colorado)|Riverside Cemetery]], Denver (1973)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WM5QAN_Colorado_Confederate_Veterans_Memorial_Riverside_Cemetery_Denver_CO|title=Colorado Confederate Veterans Memorial – Riverside Cemetery, Denver, CO – American Civil War Monuments and Memorials on Waymarking.com|website=www.waymarking.com}}</ref>
===Mine=== * [[Leadville, Colorado|Leadville]]: Robert E. Lee Mine (1878)<ref>{{cite book|last=Keller|first=Steve|title=Colorado in Depth|publisher=Lulu|year=2007|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=lVbDAgAAQBAJ&pg=PP1|page=290|isbn=978-1430311942}}</ref><ref>{{Cite GNIS|1684289|Robert E Lee Mine}}</ref>
==Delaware== {{As of|2020|06|24|df=US}}, there is at least one public space with Confederate monuments in [[Delaware]]. * [[Georgetown, Delaware|Georgetown]]: [[Delaware Confederate Monument]], a private monument built on the grounds of the Georgetown Historical Society, unveiled in 2007.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.delawareonline.com/story/news/2017/08/15/delaware-leaders-make-no-moves-oust-confederate-monument/570039001/|title=Delaware leaders make no moves to oust Confederate monument|last=Fishman|first=Margie|date=August 15, 2017|website=[[The News Journal]]|access-date=August 30, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/08/30/us/confederate-monuments.html|title=A Boom in Confederate Monuments, on Private Land|last=Tavernise|first=Sabrina|date=August 30, 2017|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=August 30, 2017|language=en-US|issn=0362-4331}}</ref>
==District of Columbia== {{see also|List of Confederate monuments and memorials#National}}
{{As of|2020|06|24|df=USspeech o}}, there are at least nine public Confederate monuments in [[Washington, D.C.]], mostly in the National Statuary Hall Collection. ([[#National|See above]])
* [[Albert Pike Memorial]] (1901):<ref>{{Cite tweet | user=afruin | number=1274193224995713027| date=June 19, 2020 |first=Alex | last=Fruin|title=Tonight in #DC – statue of confederate officer Albert Pike was pulled down from its post in Judiciary Square. @shomaristone was there, reporting LIVE as it happened on @nbcwashington with @RealLeonHarris| access-date=June 20, 2020}}</ref> An outdoor statue that is owned by the [[National Park Service]] at 3rd and D Streets NW in the [[Judiciary Square]] neighborhood of Washington, D.C. Pike was a Confederate General and leading [[Freemason]] and is dressed as a Mason in the sculpture.<ref name=":10" /> The statue is a "portrait of Albert Pike as a Masonic leader and not as a general in the military."<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://siris-artinventories.si.edu/ipac20/ipac.jsp?session=1504Y31751M21.7245&profile=ariall&source=~!siartinventories&view=subscriptionsummary&uri=full=3100001~!325362~!19&ri=3&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ipp=20&spp=20&staffonly=&term=Trentanove,+Gaetano,+1858-1937,+sculptor.&index=AUTHOR&uindex=&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ri=3|title=Brigadier General Albert Pike|last=Trentanove|first=Gaetano|others=Washington Granite Monumental Company, Fonderia Galli|year=1899}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|title=The Outdoor Sculpture of Washington, D.C.: A Comprehensive Historical Guide|last=Goode|first=James M.|year=1974|publisher=Smithsonian Inst Press|isbn=978-0874741490|edition=1st Edition/ 2nd Printing|location=Washington, DC|page=228|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|title=Testament to Union: Civil War Monuments in Washington, D.C.|last1=Jacob|first1=Kathryn Allamong|last2=Remsberg|first2=Edwin H.|year= 1998|publisher=Johns Hopkins University Press|isbn=978-0801858611|location=Baltimore, Md.; London|page=62|language=en}}</ref> "Eight D.C. elected officials have asked the National Park Service to remove" the statue.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://wtop.com/dc/2017/08/dc_officials_seek_remove_statue_confederate_general/ |newspaper=WToP |date=August 16, 2017 |access-date=November 24, 2017 |title=DC officials seek to remove statue of Confederate general |first=Amanda |last=Iacone}}</ref> On June 19, 2020, protesters tore down the statue and set it on fire as part of the [[George Floyd protests]] because of Pike's association with the Confederacy.
==Florida== {{see also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Florida}}
{{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 63 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Florida]].
An August 2017 meeting of the Florida League of Mayors was devoted to the topic of what to do with Civil War monuments.<ref name="Elias">{{cite news |url=http://www.nbc-2.com/story/36170127/fort-myers-mayor-considering-options-for-removing-civil-war-pieces |access-date=October 19, 2017 |title=Fort Myers mayor considering options for removing Civil War pieces |newspaper=WBBH |first=Dave |last=Elias |date=August 18, 2017 |archive-date=October 23, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171023230820/http://www.nbc-2.com/story/36170127/fort-myers-mayor-considering-options-for-removing-civil-war-pieces |url-status=dead }}</ref>
