===State capitol=== * Confederate monument of [[Leon County, Florida|Leon County]], on the grounds of the former [[Florida State Capitol]], the "Old Capitol," now a museum.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.hmdb.org/marker.asp?marker=73047|title=Leon County Civil War Monument Historical Marker|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref> Erected 1882 by "our country women", moved to current location 1923.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.flhistoriccapitol.gov/Pages/ExhibitsAndCollections/PermanentExhibits.aspx|title=Permanent Exhibits|publisher=Florida Historic Capitol Museum|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref>
===State symbol=== [[File:Flag of Florida.svg|thumb|right|Flag of Florida since 1900]] * The current [[flag of Florida]], adopted by popular referendum in 1900, with minor changes in 1985, contains the St. Andrew's Cross. It is believed that the Cross was added in memory of, and showing support for, the Confederacy.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Williams|first1=Dave|title=Flag debate spreading across Deep South|url=http://savannahnow.com/stories/091700/LOCflaginsight.shtml#.VaunxRNVhBd|access-date=July 19, 2015|work=Savannah Morning News|date=September 17, 2000|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150722185513/http://savannahnow.com/stories/091700/LOCflaginsight.shtml#.VaunxRNVhBd|archive-date=July 22, 2015|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Ingraham|first1=Christopher|title=How the Confederacy lives on in the flags of seven Southern states|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/wonkblog/wp/2015/06/21/how-the-confederacy-lives-on-in-the-flags-of-seven-southern-states/|access-date=July 19, 2015|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|date=June 21, 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://dos.fl.gov/florida-facts/florida-state-symbols/state-flag/|title=State Flag – Florida Department of State|website=dos.myflorida.com|access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref> Others instead say there is no link with the Confederacy, but that the saltire recalls the [[Cross of Burgundy]], the emblem of [[New Spain]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Munzenrieder |first1=Kyle |title=Is Florida's State Flag "the Most Overtly Racist Symbol in the United States"? |url=https://www.miaminewtimes.com/news/is-floridas-state-flag-the-most-overtly-racist-symbol-in-the-united-states-7710371 |website=Miami New Times |access-date=June 14, 2020 |date=June 26, 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Garvin |first1=Glenn |title=Historians differ on whether Florida flag echoes Confederate banner |url=https://www.miamiherald.com/news/state/florida/article25444405.html |website=[[Miami Herald]] |date=June 24, 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=The Burgundian Saltire – 1565–1763 – Florida Department of State |url=https://dos.fl.gov/florida-facts/florida-state-symbols/state-flag/floridas-historic-flags/national-flags/the-burgundian-saltire-1565-1763/ |website=dos.myflorida.com |access-date=June 14, 2020}}</ref> However, the addition of the Cross was proposed by Governor [[Francis P. Fleming]], a former Confederate soldier, who was strongly committed to [[Racial segregation in the United States|racial segregation]].
===State holiday=== * In Florida, Robert E. Lee's birthday (January 19), Confederate Memorial Day (April 26), and Jefferson Davis's birthday (June 3) are legal holidays.<ref>{{cite web |title=The 2018 Florida Statutes, Title XXXIX, Chapter 683 |author=Florida Legislature |year=2019 |access-date=April 4, 2019 |url=http://www.leg.state.fl.us/statutes/index.cfm?App_mode=Display_Statute&URL=0600-0699/0683/Sections/0683.01.html|author-link=Florida Legislature }}</ref>
===Monuments=== ====Courthouse monuments==== [[File:Unveiling of Confederate Monument, Ocala, Florida.jpg|thumb|right|Unveiling of Confederate Monument, Ocala, 1908]] * [[Bartow, Florida|Bartow]]: 7th Florida Infantry Regiment Monument, [[Old Polk County Courthouse (Bartow, Florida)|Old Polk County Courthouse]] (1982)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/bartow|title=Bartow|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|access-date=August 15, 2017|archive-date=August 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170816060615/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/bartow|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Brooksville, Florida|Brooksville]]: Confederate Soldiers' Memorial, [[Hernando County Courthouse]] (1916)<ref>{{cite journal|url=http://digitalcommons.unf.edu/historical_architecture_main/3235/|title=Confederate Soldiers' Memorial, Brooksville, FL|journal=George Lansing Taylor Collection Main Gallery|date=January 31, 2010|access-date=August 15, 2017|last1=Taylor|first1=George}}</ref><ref name="Widener" /> * [[Defuniak Springs, Florida|Defuniak Springs]]: Florida's First Confederate Monument, [[Walton County Courthouse (DeFuniak Springs, Florida)|Walton County Courthouse]] (1871, historic marker nearby) * [[Marianna, Florida|Marianna]]: ** Battle of Marianna Monument, [[Jackson County Courthouse (Florida)|Jackson County Courthouse]] lawn (1924)<ref name="tallahassee.com">{{cite web|url=http://www.tallahassee.com/story/news/2017/08/19/big-bend-peppered-confederate-monuments/583146001/|title=Big Bend peppered with Confederate monuments|website=tallahassee.com|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Widener" />{{rp|36}} ** Confederate monument, Jackson County Courthouse lawn (1881) * [[Monticello, Florida|Monticello]]: Confederate monument, [[Jefferson County Courthouse (Monticello, Florida)|Jefferson County Courthouse]] (1899)<ref name="tallahassee.com" /><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMBW5E_Jefferson_County_Confederate_Memorial_Monticello_FL|title=Jefferson County Confederate Memorial – Monticello, FL|website=Waymarking|access-date=August 16, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Widener" />{{rp|36}} * [[Ocala, Florida|Ocala]]: Confederate monument, erected on the grounds of the [[Marion County, Florida|Marion County]] Courthouse (1908, moved to Veterans Memorial Park and rededicated 2011).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/ocala|title=Ocala|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|access-date=August 16, 2017|archive-date=August 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170816105253/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/ocala|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="Widener" />{{rp|37}} The [[Confederate flag]] that flew in front of the Marion County Courthouse was to be moved to "elsewhere on the campus".<ref name=Ocala>{{cite news |title=Marion County to relocate Confederate flag by mid-May |newspaper=[[Ocala Star-Banner]] |first=Jim |last=Ross |date=April 13, 2016 |url=https://www.ocala.com/article/LK/20160413/News/604149721/OS/}}</ref> * Tampa: The monument ''Memoria in Aeterna'' was funded by the city of Tampa and the DOC starting in 1903 and was erected in 1911 at the Hillsborough County Courthouse. In 2017 it was proposed to move it to a private cemetery in [[Brandon, Florida]].
====Other public monuments==== [[File:Hudson Park United Daughters of the Confederacy Memorial.jpg|thumb|right|Monument in Crawfordville, Florida]] * [[Crawfordville, Florida|Crawfordville, Florida, Wakulla County]]: **Confederate Monument (1987): This white obelisk is located in Hudson Park. It is inscribed on one side with an image of a Confederate flag and the words: "1861–1865. In loving memory of those from Wakulla County who served the Confederacy during the war between the states. Erected by the R. Don McLeod Chapter 2469 United Daughters of the Confederacy May 17, 1987." * [[Daytona Beach, Florida|Daytona Beach]]: ** Confederate Sun Dial Monument (1961)<ref name="Widener" /> Originally a marble base and column topped with a sundial (by the early 1980s all that remained was its base and its bronze plaque). Dedicated to the Confederate dead. Erected by United Daughters of the Confederacy in 1961. Plaque was removed by the City of Daytona Beach in 2017 after violent clashes in Charlottesville, Virginia over their Robert E. Lee monument. Was to be given to Halifax Historical Museum.<ref name=Most/> ** Two other bronze plaques were erected in Riverfront Park by the Sons of Confederate Veterans in 1979 and 1985, which listed the names of Confederate veterans buried in East Volusia County. They were mounted on a long granite wall with other plaques commemorating various US wars. They were also removed by the city in 2017 to also be given to the Halifax Historical Museum.<ref name=Most/> ** Confederate Boulder Monument (1979)<ref name="Widener" />{{rp|33}} [[File:SCV memorial in Darsey, Florida.jpg|thumb|right|Memorial in Darsey, Florida]] * Darsey, Florida, Gadsden County: ** [[Sons of Confederate Veterans]] memorial located on [[U.S. Route 27 in Florida|U.S. Route 27]]. * [[Dixie County, Florida|Dixie County]]: American Veteran Monument, Highway 98 west of [[Old Town, Florida|Old Town]], dedicated to Confederate veterans (c. 2005)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/old-town-vicinity|title=American Veteran Monument|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|publisher=University of West Florida|access-date=August 22, 2017|archive-date=July 5, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170705003709/http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/old-town-vicinity|url-status=dead}}</ref> *[[Jefferson County, Florida]]: Monument to Stonewall Jackson * [[Ellenton, Florida|Ellenton]]: ** Confederate Veterans Memorial Monument, [[Gamble Plantation Historic State Park]] (1937)<ref name="Widener" /><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/ellenton/confederate-monument|title=FPAN – Destination: Civil War – Confederate Monument|website=Flpublicarchaeology.org|access-date=April 26, 2017|archive-date=April 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170426153429/http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/ellenton/confederate-monument|url-status=dead}}</ref> ** [[Gamble Plantation Historic State Park|Judah P. Benjamin Confederate Memorial at Gamble Plantation Historic State Park]], established 1925, pursuant to agreement between UDC and State of Florida.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.floridastateparks.org/parks/judah-p-benjamin-confederate-memorial-gamble-plantation-historic-state-park|title=Judah P. Benjamin Confederate Memorial at Gamble Plantation Historic State Park|website=Floridastateparks.org|access-date=April 26, 2017|archive-date=April 27, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170427100150/https://www.floridastateparks.org/parks/judah-p-benjamin-confederate-memorial-gamble-plantation-historic-state-park|url-status=dead}}</ref> Benjamin was Attorney General, then Secretary of War, then Secretary of State of the Confederacy. Also serves as home to Florida Division of UDC.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.floridadivisionudc.org/gamble-plantation.html|title=Gamble Plantation|website=Florida Division UDC|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170426151445/http://www.floridadivisionudc.org/gamble-plantation.html|archive-date=April 26, 2017}}</ref> * [[Fernandina Beach, Florida|Fernandina Beach]]: Statue of [[David Levy Yulee]].<ref name="forwardfeldmanwhyaretherenostatues">{{cite news|last1=Feldman|first1=Ari|title=Why Are There No Statues Of Jewish Confederate Judah Benjamin To Tear Down?|url=http://forward.com/news/380453/why-are-there-no-statues-of-jewish-confederate-judah-benjamin-to-tear-down/|access-date=September 6, 2017|work=Forward|date=August 20, 2017|quote=There is only one known statue of a Jewish Confederate leader. It depicts David Levy Yulee, an industrialist, plantation owner and Confederate senator from Florida, and it shows him sitting on a bench.}}</ref> [[File:Yellow Bluff Fort SP mnmt02.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Yellow Bluff Fort Monument]] * [[Jacksonville, Florida|Jacksonville]]: ** [[Confederate Park (Jacksonville)|Confederate Park]]. It opened in 1907 as Dignan Park, named for a former chairman of the city's Board of Public Works. In 1914, the park was chosen as the location of the annual reunion of the [[United Confederate Veterans]]. The UCV chose the park as the location for a new monument to honor the Women of the Southland, and five months after the reunion the city renamed the park "Confederate Park." ** [[Florida's Tribute to the Women of the Confederacy]], in [[Confederate Park (Jacksonville)|Confederate Park]] (1915). The sculptor was [[Allen George Newman]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://siris-artinventories.si.edu/ipac20/ipac.jsp?session=14S1X138436E2.6589&profile=ariall&source=~!siartinventories&view=subscriptionsummary&uri=full=3100001~!313445~!23&ri=4&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ipp=20&spp=20&staffonly=&term=Outdoor+Sculpture+--+Florida+--+Jacksonville&index=OBJEC&uindex=&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ri=4|title=Florida's Tribute to the Women of the Confederacy|first=Allen George|last=Newman|date=January 1, 1914|website=Siris-artinventories.si.edu}}</ref> ** Confederate monument, downtown [[Hemming Park]] (1898)<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://floridapolitics.com/archives/243312-jax-chamber-backs-call-inventory-confederate-monuments|title=Jax Chamber backs Confederate monument 'inventory'; Anna Brosche modifies position|last=Gancarski|first=A.G.|date=August 18, 2017|website=floridapolitics.com|language=en-US|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Widener" />{{rp|34}} "The president of Jacksonville City Council, Anna Lopez Brosche, called for all Confederate monuments to be moved from city property to a museum. The most prominent Confederate memorial in Jacksonville is a statue of a Confederate soldier that sits atop a towering pillar in Hemming Park."<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2017/08/16/us/confederate-monuments-removed.html |access-date=October 28, 2017 |title=Confederate Monuments Are Coming Down Across the United States. Here's a List. |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=August 28, 2017}}</ref> ** Yellow Bluff Fort Monument, [[Yellow Bluff Fort Historic State Park]] (1951)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/jacksonville-evergreen-cemetery/yellow-bluff-fort/|title=To the Memory of the Confederate Soldiers who Defended Jacksonville|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|publisher=University of West Florida|access-date=August 22, 2017|archive-date=August 23, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170823121202/http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/jacksonville-evergreen-cemetery/yellow-bluff-fort/|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Key West, Florida|Key West]]: ** Confederate memorial fence at Clinton Square, built by J.V. Harris circa 1866.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.hmdb.org/marker.asp?marker=85270|title=Clinton Square Historical Marker|website=The Historical Marker Database|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref> ** Confederate memorial pavilion at Bayview Park (1924) by UDC.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://wlrn.org/post/key-west-preserves-memorials-confederate-and-union-armies|title=Key West Preserves Memorials To Confederate and Union Armies|first=Nancy|last=Klingener|work=WLRN Public Radio and Television|date=August 25, 2015|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref> ** Mallory Square named after Stephen R. Mallory<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.encirclephotos.com/image/mallory-square-signage-in-key-west-florida/|website=encircle photos|title= Mallory Square Signage in Key West, Florida}}</ref> * [[Lake City, Florida|Lake City]]: Confederate Dead of [[Battle of Olustee]], town square in front of the Columbia County Courthouse (1928)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/lake-city/downtown-square/|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|title=Lake City – Downtown Square|access-date=August 16, 2017|archive-date=August 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170816151245/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/lake-city/downtown-square/|url-status=dead}}</ref> [[File:Confederate monument in Munn Park - Lakeland, Florida.jpg|thumb|right|upright|[[United Daughters of the Confederacy]] members seated around a Confederate monument in Lakeland, 1915]] * [[Madison, Florida|Madison]]: Confederate monument, Four Freedoms Park (1909). Lists names of men who died from county. Nearby sits a monument to former slaves in the county.<ref name="tallahassee.com"/><ref name="Widener" />{{rp|35}} * [[Miami, Florida|Miami]]: Confederate monument, Confederate Circle in [[Miami City Cemetery|City Cemetery]] (1914 at the Dade County Courthouse, was moved to cemetery in 1927)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/miami/our-heroes-old-miami-cemetery/|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|title=City of Miami Cemetery|access-date=August 18, 2017|archive-date=August 19, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170819015815/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/miami/our-heroes-old-miami-cemetery/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="Widener" />{{rp|36}} [[File:Olustee Battlefield Historic State Park11.jpg|right|thumb|upright|Olustee Battlefield Historic State Park]] * [[Olustee, Florida|Olustee]]: ** Battlefield monument, [[Olustee Battlefield Historic State Park]] (1912). Inscription: Here was fought on February 20, 1864, the Battle of Ocean Pond under the immediate command of General Alfred Holt Colquitt, "Hero of Olustee." This decisive engagement prevented a Sherman-like invasion of Georgia from the south. Erected April 20, 1936, by the Alfred Holt Colquitt Chapter, United Daughters of the Confederacy Ga. Div. ** CSA [[Brigadier General]] [[Joseph Finnegan (Brigadier general)|Joseph Finnegan]] Monument, [[Olustee Battlefield Historic State Park]] (1912). "Placed by The United Daughters of the Confederacy Florida Division In Memory of Brig. Gen. Joseph Finegan Commander of the District of Middle and East Florida So well did he perform his part that a signal victory over the Federals was won in the Battle of Olustee Feb. 20, 1864" * [[Pensacola, Florida|Pensacola]]: ** Florida Square was renamed Lee Square in 1889.<ref name="Buchanan">{{cite news |title=Rally at Confederate monument in downtown Pensacola draws hundreds, one arrested |first=Drew |last=Buchanan |newspaper=The Pulse |date=August 26, 2017 |url=http://pulsegulfcoast.com/2017/08/rally-at-confederate-monument-in-downtown-pensacola-draws-hundreds-one-arrested}}</ref> ** A 50-foot monument to Our Confederate Dead, erected in 1891, is in Lee Square.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://siris-artinventories.si.edu/ipac20/ipac.jsp?session=147122JY4223C.6622&profile=ariall&uri=link=3100006~!278357~!3100001~!3100002&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ri=7&source=~!siartinventories&term=J.+F.+Manning+Company,+fabricator.&index=AUTHOR|title=J. F. Manning Company, fabricator|website=SIRIS – Smithsonian Institution Research Information System}}</ref> It commemorates Jefferson Davis, Pensacolian Confederate veterans [[Stephen R. Mallory]] (Secretary of the Confederate Navy) and [[Edward Aylesworth Perry]] (Confederate General and Governor of Florida 1885–1889), and "the Uncrowned Heroes of the Southern Confederacy." The mayor of Pensacola has called for its removal.<ref name="Buchanan"/> * [[Perry, Florida|Perry]]: Confederate monument, Taylor County Sports Complex (2007)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/perry|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|title=Perry|access-date=August 16, 2017|archive-date=August 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170816190953/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/perry|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WM7K0P_Confederate_Monument_Perry_FL|website=Waymarking|title=Confederate Monument – Perry, FL|access-date=August 16, 2017}}</ref> * [[Quincy, Florida|Quincy]]: Confederate memorial, Soldiers Cemetery within Eastern Cemetery, part of the town's National Register Historic District (2010). The memorial also notes the restoration of the historic fence.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMBT4C_Soldiers_Cemetery_Confederate_Memorial_Quincy_FL|website=Waymarking|title=Soldiers Cemetery Confederate Memorial – Quincy, FL|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.gadsdenarts.org/Portals/Gadsenden/Visit/QuincyHistoricEBook.pdf|title=On the Trail in Historic Quincy|website=Gadsden Arts Center & Museum|access-date=August 29, 2017|archive-date=December 21, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161221153508/http://www.gadsdenarts.org/Portals/Gadsenden/Visit/QuincyHistoricEBook.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[St. Augustine, Florida|St. Augustine]]: ** Confederate monument, on the [[Plaza de la Constitución]] (1879).<ref>{{cite journal|url=http://digitalcommons.unf.edu/historical_architecture_main/5064/|journal=George Lansing Taylor Collection Main Gallery|title=Confederate Memorial Obelisk, St. Augustine, FL|date=June 14, 2009|last1=Taylor|first1=George}}</ref> "The Confederate Memorial Contextualization Advisory Committee, a seven-member task force {{sic|comprised mostly|hide=y| of}} historians", in 2018 recommended to the City Commission that the monument be kept, with the addition of "some necessary context".<ref>{{cite news |title=Task force: Context needed for St. Augustine's Confederate memorial |first1=Ashley |last1=Harding |first2=Ethan |last2=Calloway |date=July 9, 2018 |newspaper=[[WJXT]] |url=https://www.news4jax.com/news/local/st-augustine/committee-hopes-for-compromise-over-fate-of-st-augustine-confederate-statues}}</ref> * [[St. Cloud, Florida|St. Cloud]]: Confederate monument, Veterans Park (2006)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.civilwaralbum.com/misc20/stcloud1.htm|website=Civil War Album|title=St. Cloud, Florida|access-date=August 17, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170817164338/http://www.civilwaralbum.com/misc20/stcloud1.htm|archive-date=August 17, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[St. Petersburg, Florida|St. Petersburg]]: Confederate monument, Greenwood Cemetery (1900)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/st.-petersburg/greenwood-cemetery/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170819063400/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/st.-petersburg/greenwood-cemetery/|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 19, 2017|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|title=St. Petersburg – Greenwood Cemetery|access-date=August 18, 2017}}</ref> * [[Tampa, Florida|Tampa]]: There is a stained-glass window donated by the United Daughters of the Confederacy in 1906 in honor of Father [[Abram Ryan]], called "Poet of the Confederacy", in the [[Sacred Heart Catholic Church (Tampa, Florida)|Sacred Heart Catholic Church]]. * [[Trenton, Florida|Trenton]]: Confederate monument, across from Gilchrist County Courthouse in Veterans' Park (2010)<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://gilchristcountyjournal.net/archived/04-29-10/04-29-10confederatevets.html/|title=Fallen Confederates honored Saturday|date=April 29, 2010|work=Gilchrist County Journal|access-date=August 17, 2017}}{{dead link|date=December 2017 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> * [[Woodville, Florida|Woodville]]: In Loving Memory Monument, [[Natural Bridge Battlefield Historic State Park]] (1922)<ref name="Widener" />{{rp|37}} A plaque placed at the base of the monument in 2000 lists the names of those who died as a result of the battle.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/natural-bridge-battlefield-monuments/confederate-and-union-soldiers-who-dies-at-natural-bridge|title=Confederate and Union Soldiers who died at Natural Bridge|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|access-date=August 30, 2017|archive-date=August 30, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170830145115/http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/natural-bridge-battlefield-monuments/confederate-and-union-soldiers-who-dies-at-natural-bridge|url-status=dead}}</ref>
====Private monuments==== * [[Alachua, Florida|Alachua]]: Confederate monument, Newnansville Cemetery (2002) by the Alachua Lions Club<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMBYBF_Newnansville_Cemetery_Confederate_Veterans_Memorial_Alachua_FL|title=Newnansville Cemetery Confederate Veterans Memorial – Alachua, FL|website=Waymarking|access-date=August 16, 2017}}</ref> * [[Bradfordville, Florida|Bradfordville]], unincorporated community in [[Leon County, Florida|Leon County]]: [[Robert E. Lee]] Monument, dedicated along Highway 319 in 1927 by UDC. Moved in the 1960s and 1990s, it is now located about a mile south of the Georgia border.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/bradfordville-robert-e.-lee-monument|title=Bradfordville|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|access-date=August 15, 2017|archive-date=February 19, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200219072606/http://www.flpublicarchaeology.org/civilwar/monuments/bradfordville-robert-e.-lee-monument|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://sites.google.com/site/tallahasseehistoricalsociety//history-of-our-society/meeting-history/approvedminutesforoctober102013meeting|title=Approved Minutes from October 10, 2013, Meeting|website=Tallahassee Historical Society|access-date=August 15, 2017}}{{Dead link|date=August 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> * [[Dade City, Florida|Dade City]]: Confederate memorial, Townsend House Cemetery (2010)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMH6ZJ_Pasco_County_Civil_War_Veterans_Memorial_Dade_City_Florida|title=Pasco County Civil War Veterans Memorial-Dade City, Florida|website=Waymarking|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Deland, Florida|Deland]]: Confederate Veteran Memorial, Oakdale Cemetery (1958)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMB9EC_Confederate_Veteran_Memorial_DeLand_FL|website=Waymarking|title=Confederate Veteran Memorial – DeLand, FL|access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref> * [[Kissimmee, Florida|Kissimmee]]: Granite obelisk in Rose Hill Cemetery, dedicated to Confederate veterans buried in Osceola County with their names listed on the monument. Erected 2002 by Sons of Confederate Veterans.<ref name=Most/> * [[Lake City, Florida|Lake City]]: ** Last Confederate War Widow, Oaklawn Cemetery, erected after her death in 1985. The memorial and the cemetery are along the Florida Civil War Heritage Trail.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/lake-city/oaklawn-cemetery/last-confederate-war-widow/|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|title=Lake City – Last Confederate War Widow|access-date=August 16, 2017|archive-date=August 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170816150503/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/lake-city/oaklawn-cemetery/last-confederate-war-widow/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://files.floridados.gov/media/32357/civilwarheritagetrail.pdf|title=Florida Civil War Heritage Trail|website=Florida Department of State}}</ref>{{rp|28}} ** Our Confederate Dead, Oaklawn Cemetery (1901, rededicated 1996). A tall obelisk in memory of the unnamed soldiers who died at the nearby [[Battle of Olustee]] or in the town's Confederate hospital. The cemetery is the focal point of the opening of Lake City's annual [[Lake City, Florida#Olustee Battle Festival|Olustee Battle Festival]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/lake-city/oaklawn-cemetery/battle-of-olustee-soldiers-plot/|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|title=Lake City – Battle of Olustee Soldiers Plot|access-date=August 16, 2017|archive-date=August 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170816151633/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/lake-city/oaklawn-cemetery/battle-of-olustee-soldiers-plot/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.floridamemory.com/items/show/9196|title=Monument in Oaklawn Cemetery : Lake City, Florida|website=Florida Memory, State Library & Archives of Florida}}</ref> * [[Leesburg, Florida|Leesburg]]: Memorial fountain made of rustic limestone, in Lone Oak Cemetery. Erected 1935 by United Daughters of the Confederacy but dedicated to soldiers of all wars. An adjacent 20-foot flagpole and inscribed granite block dedicated to Civil War veterans buried there was erected by the United Daughters of the Confederacy in 2005.<ref name=Most/> * [[Ormond Beach, Florida|Ormond Beach]]: 2011; Pilgrim's Rest Cemetery. Monument consists of a flagpole and a concrete base with an attached bronze Southern Cross of Honor and a granite slab listing the names of Confederate veterans buried there. Erected by Confederate Sons Association of Florida.<ref name=Most>{{cite news |title=Most Confederate statues in Central Florida have been relocated |url=https://www.clickorlando.com/news/local/2020/06/15/most-confederate-statues-in-central-florida-have-been-relocated/ |first=Crystal |last=Moyer |newspaper=[[WKMG-TV]] (clickorlando.com)}}</ref> * [[Oxford, Florida|Oxford]]: Upright granite slab monument in Pine Level Cemetery, listing the names of Confederate veterans buried in the cemetery. Erected 2007 by Sons of Confederate Veterans.<ref name=Most/> * [[White Springs, Florida|White Springs]]: Confederate monument and large flag, along Interstate 75 (2002)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/white-springs|website=Florida Public Archaeology Network|title=White Springs|access-date=August 18, 2017|archive-date=August 19, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170819015533/http://fpan.us/civilwar/monuments/white-springs|url-status=dead}}</ref>
===Inhabited places=== ====Counties==== * [[Baker County, Florida|Baker County]] (1861), named for [[James McNair Baker]], a lawyer and judge who was a Confederate States of America Senator from Florida.<ref name="auto2">{{cite web|url=http://info.flheritage.com/florida-names/county.cfm|title=County Name Origins|website=Florida Department of State: Division of Historical Resources|access-date=August 18, 2017}}</ref> * [[Bradford County, Florida|Bradford County]] (1861), named for Captain Richard Bradford, who was killed in the [[Battle of Santa Rosa Island]], becoming the first Confederate officer from Florida to die during the Civil War.<ref name="auto2"/> * [[Hendry County, Florida|Hendry County]] (1923), named for [[Francis A. Hendry|Francis Asbury Hendry]], a Confederate Captain and one of the first settlers in the area.<ref name="auto2"/> * [[Lee County, Florida|Lee County]] (1887), named for Robert E. Lee.<ref>{{cite book|last=Gannett|first=Henry|title=The Origin of Certain Place Names in the United States|url=https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_9V1IAAAAMAAJ|year=1905|publisher=U.S. Government Printing Office|page=[https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_9V1IAAAAMAAJ/page/n183 184]}}</ref> * [[Levy County, Florida|Levy County]] (1845), named for [[David Levy Yulee]], a Florida businessman, senator, and strong supporter of slavery, who withdrew from the U.S. Senate in 1861 and served nine months in prison after the Civil War for supporting the Confederacy. * [[Pasco County, Florida|Pasco County]] (1887), named for [[Samuel Pasco]], who fought for the CSA but spent much of the war as a prisoner of war. Pasco later became a state representative and US Senator from Florida.
====Municipalities==== * [[Bartow, Florida|Bartow]] (1862), previously Reidsville, renamed for CSA Col. [[Francis S. Bartow|Francis Bartow]].<ref name="Polk County Courthouse">{{cite web|url=http://www.jud10.org/Courthouses/Polk/polk.html|title=Polk County Courthouse |access-date=August 22, 2017|publisher=Florida 10th Judicial Circuit|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101101130759/http://jud10.org/Courthouses/Polk/polk.html|archive-date=November 1, 2010|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Brooksville, Florida|Brooksville]], previously Pierceville, took on its present name in 1856, to honor and show support for [[Preston Brooks]], a pro-slavery congressman from South Carolina who [[caning of Charles Sumner|caned and seriously injured Massachusetts Senator and abolitionist Charles Sumner]]. * [[Fort Myers, Florida|Fort Myers]], previously Fort Harvie, named for [[Abraham Myers]], [[Quartermaster General]] of the Confederate Army.<ref name="Elias"/> * [[Lee, Florida|Lee]]: named for Robert E. Lee. * [[Mayo, Florida|Mayo]], named for CSA Col. James Mayo.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://info.flheritage.com/florida-names/city2.cfm|title=City Name Origins|website=Florida Department of State: Division of Historical Resources|access-date=August 18, 2017}}</ref> * [[Perry, Florida|Perry]] (1875), named for Florida Governor and CSA Col. [[Madison S. Perry|Madison Starke Perry]].<ref name="tallahassee.com"/> * [[Starke, Florida|Starke]]. The origin of the name is unknown. A prominent theory is that it was named in honor of [[Madison S. Perry|Madison Starke Perry]], fourth [[List of Governors of Florida|governor of Florida]] and a [[Confederate States Army]] [[Colonel (United States)|colonel]]. The Division of Historical Resources of the Florida Department of State adds that the city may have been named for "Thomas Starke, a slaveholder who once owned much land around the area."<ref name="auto4">{{Cite web|url=http://www.flheritage.com/facts/reports/names/city3.cfm#S|title=Name Origins of Florida Places: Florida OCHP|website=Flheritage.com|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref> * [[Titusville, Florida|Titusville]] (1873), previously Sand Point, renamed by CSA Col. [[Henry T. Titus]], who also supplied Confederate troops.<ref name="auto4"/> * [[Yulee, Florida|Yulee]] (1893), named for [[David Levy Yulee]], a supporter of slavery and secession. See Levy County, above.
===Parks=== * [[Ellenton, Florida|Ellenton]]: [[Judah P. Benjamin]] Confederate Memorial at [[Gamble Plantation Historic State Park]] (1925)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.floridastateparks.org/park/Gamble-Plantation|title=Gamble Plantation Historic State Park|website=Florida State Parks|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Fort Walton Beach]]: Heritage Park preserves the Confederate Camp Walton named for the county it was located in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.exploresouthernhistory.com/fortwalton.html|title=Camp Walton – Confederate Fort at Fort Walton Beach, Florida|last=Cox|first=Dale|website=www.exploresouthernhistory.com|access-date=October 9, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170916140849/http://www.exploresouthernhistory.com/fortwalton.html|archive-date=September 16, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Jacksonville, Florida|Jacksonville]]: ** [[Confederate Park (Jacksonville)|Confederate Park]], opened in 1907. Originally named Dignan Park, the park was renamed when UCV chose the locale as the site for their annual reunions in 1914.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.metrojacksonville.com/article/2009-apr-urban-parks-confederate-park|title=Confederate Park History|website=Metro Jacksonville|access-date=August 22, 2017}}</ref> -now Springfield Park. ** [[Hemming Park]]/Hemming Plaza (1899) renamed in honor of Civil War veteran Charles C. Hemming, after he installed a 62-foot (19 m)-tall Confederate monument in the park in 1898.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WM2PH4|title=Confederate Memorial – Hemming Plaza – Jacksonville, FL|website=Waymarking|language=en|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://apps2.coj.net/parksinternet/parkdetails.asp?parkid=397|title=Hemming Plaza|website=apps2.coj.net|access-date=August 17, 2017|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303214056/http://apps2.coj.net/parksinternet/parkdetails.asp?parkid=397|archive-date=March 3, 2016}}</ref> -now James Weldon Johnson Park. ** [[Hemming Park station]] an elevated rail station taking its name from the park. Now James Weldon Johnson Park Station. * [[Miami]]: Robert E. Lee Park, the athletic field of Jose de Diego Middle School which replaced Robert E. Lee Middle School (1924–1989) in the [[Wynwood]] neighborhood in 1999.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://issuu.com/diegociudadreal/docs/nuevo_libro_wynwood_walls_con_porta_6dbb20717e04d7|title=Wynwood Walls "Stories Through The Walls"|page=14|date=September 11, 2015|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref> A school district spokesman has said the name is not official and requested agencies with incorrect listings update them.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.miaminewtimes.com/news/miamis-robert-e-lee-park-should-go-activists-say-9590432|title=Activists Want to Erase Wynwood's Robert E. Lee Park, but Officials Say It Doesn't Actually Exist|first=Tim|last=Elfrink|work=Miami New Times|date=August 17, 2017|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref> As of 2024, Google Maps has changed the park’s name to Jose de Diego Park. * [[Pensacola, Florida|Pensacola]]: Lee Square (1889) -now Florida Square. * [[Tampa, Florida|Tampa]]: [[Confederate Memorial Park (Tampa, Florida)|Confederate Memorial Park]], opened 2008 by the Sons of Confederate Veterans.
===Roads=== {{div col |colwidth=20em}} * Stonewall Jackson Memorial Highway, designated by UDC. Chapters placed the following markers in the state: ** [[Capps, Florida|Capps]]: Along [[U.S. Route 19 in Florida|U.S. Route 19]] in 1940.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMGXTE_Stonewall_Jackson_Memorial_Highway|website=Waymarking|title=Stonewall Jackson Memorial Highway|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref> ** [[St. Petersburg, Florida|St. Petersburg]]: Terminus marker at the intersection of Central Avenue and Bayshore Drive in 1939.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.hmdb.org/marker.asp?marker=4670|website=The Historical Marker Database|title=Stonewall Jackson Memorial Highway Terminus|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref><br />Removed by the city August 15, 2017.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.tampabay.com/blogs/baybuzz/st-pete-confederate-marker-on-waterfront-remains-mystery/2333756|title=Kriseman removes Confederate marker from St. Pete's waterfront|last=Frago|first=Charlie|date=August 15, 2017|work=Tampa Bay Times|access-date=August 17, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170816020611/http://www.tampabay.com/blogs/baybuzz/st-pete-confederate-marker-on-waterfront-remains-mystery/2333756|archive-date=August 16, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Hilliard, Florida|Hilliard]]: General Lee Road * [[Jacksonville, Florida|Jacksonville]] ** Confederate Point Road ** Confederate Street ** General Lee Road * [[Naples, Florida|Naples]]: Confederate Drive * [[Orlando, Florida|Orlando]] ** [[Edmund Kirby Smith|Kirby Smith]] Road ** Stonewall Jackson Road-renamed * [[Pensacola, Florida|Pensacola]]: Confederate Drive * [[Perry, Florida|Perry]]: North Jeff Davis Avenue * [[St. Cloud, Florida|St. Cloud]]: Robert Lee Road * [[Stuart, Florida|Stuart]]: Southeast General Lee Terrace * [[Tampa, Florida|Tampa]]: Robert E. Lee Road * [[Zephyrhills, Florida|Zephyrhills]]: ** Jeff Davis Drive ** Jubal Early Road {{div col end}}
===Schools and libraries=== * [[Gainesville, Florida|Gainesville]]: ** J.J. Finley Elementary School (1939), named for CSA Brig. Gen. [[Jesse J. Finley]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.finley75.com/finley-history.html|title=J.J. Finley Elementary School History|website=J.J. Finley Elementary School History|access-date=August 22, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170823120154/http://www.finley75.com/finley-history.html|archive-date=August 23, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> -now Carolyn Beatrice Parker Elementary School. ** Kirby-Smith Center (1939), [[Alachua County Public Schools]] administrative offices. Constructed in 1900, the building was initially the all white Gainesville Graded & High School.<ref>{{cite journal|url=http://digitalcommons.unf.edu/historical_architecture_main/4075/|journal=George Lansing Taylor Collection Main Gallery|title=Kirby Smith School 2, Gainesville, FL|date=August 3, 2009|access-date=August 19, 2017|last1=Taylor|first1=George}}</ref> In August 2017, the school board announced plans to rename the center.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.wcjb.com/content/news/Alachua-County-School-Board-Changing-Name-441354423.html|title=Alachua County School Board Changing Name|publisher=WCJB|date=August 21, 2017|access-date=August 22, 2017}}</ref> ** [[Sidney Lanier]] School. Lanier was a Confederate soldier and poet. * [[Hillsborough County, Florida|Hillsborough County]]: Robert E. Lee Elementary School aka Lee Elementary Magnet School of World Studies and Technology was built 1906 and named for Lee in 1943. A school board member pushing for a rename in 2017 noted that had Lee's army won the war "a majority of our students would be slaves."<ref>{{cite news |first=Marlene |last=Sokol |url=https://www.tampabay.com/news/education/k12/as-confederate-monuments-fall-tampas-robert-e-lee-elementary-could-get/2327176/ |title=As Confederate monuments fall, Tampa's Robert E. Lee Elementary could get name change |newspaper=[[Tampa Bay Times]] |access-date=August 26, 2017 |date=June 14, 2017}}</ref> -now Tampa Heights Elementary Magnet School. * [[Jacksonville, Florida|Jacksonville]]<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.actionnewsjax.com/news/local/duval-county-public-schools-not-considering-changes-to-schools-with-confederate-names/592886128/|title=Duval County Public Schools not considering changes to schools with Confederate names|last=Bennett|first=Erica|date=August 15, 2017|work=Action News Jax|access-date=August 17, 2017|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170816124855/http://www.actionnewsjax.com/news/local/duval-county-public-schools-not-considering-changes-to-schools-with-confederate-names/592886128|archive-date=August 16, 2017}}</ref> ** J.E.B. Stuart Middle School (1966), named for CSA Gen. [[J. E. B. Stuart]]. -now Westside Middle School. ** Jefferson Davis Middle School (1961) -now Charger Academy. ** Kirby-Smith Middle School (1924), named for CSA Gen. [[Edmund Kirby Smith]]. -now Springfield Middle School. ** [[Robert E. Lee High School (Jacksonville)|Robert E. Lee High School]] (1928) -now Riverside High School. ** Stonewall Jackson Elementary School -now Hidden Oaks Elementary School. * [[Orlando, Florida|Orlando]]: ** Robert E. Lee Middle School, renamed College Park Middle School in 2017.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.orlandosentinel.com/features/education/school-zone/os-lee-name-vote-20170214-story.html|title=Lee Middle School will become College Park Middle|last=Martin|first=Annie|date=February 14, 2017|work=Orlando Sentinel|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref> ** Stonewall Jackson Middle School was renamed Roberto Clemente Middle School in 2020, as was the road in front of the school. * [[Pensacola, Florida|Pensacola]]: Escambia High School's [[Escambia High School#Mascot Riots|Rebel mascot riots]], 1972–1977. Before a noncontroversial name was chosen, protests and violence occurred at the school and in the community, [[Cross burning|crosses were burned]] on school district members' lawns, lawsuits were filed, and the [[Ku Klux Klan]] held a rally and petitioned the school board. * [[Tampa, Florida|Tampa]]: [[Lee Elementary School of Technology / World Studies]] (1906). The school's mascot is Robert E. Lee's horse Traveller. In July 2015, students asked the school board to change the school's name.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.tampabay.com/news/humaninterest/today-in-florida-its-robert-e-lee-day/2261887/|title=Today in Florida, it's Robert E. Lee Day|newspaper=Tampa Bay Times|date=January 19, 2016|access-date=August 22, 2017}}</ref> In June 2017, a board member asked the board to consider the name change.<ref>{{cite news |first=Emerald |last=Morrow |url=https://www.wtsp.com/article/news/local/hillsborough-school-official-moves-to-rename-robert-e-lee-school/67-449726224 |title=Hillsborough school official moves to rename Robert E. Lee school |publisher=[[WTSP]] |date=June 19, 2017 |access-date=August 22, 2017}}</ref> -now Tampa Heights Elementary School
===City symbols=== * [[Hillsborough County, Florida|Hillsborough County]]: until 1997, the Hillsborough County seal included the Confederate Battle Flag.<ref name="Contorno">{{cite news |last1=Contorno |first1=Steve |title=For Tampa's Confederate monument, racist history clouds claims of heritage |url=https://www.tampabay.com/news/humaninterest/for-tampas-confederate-monument-racist-history-clouds-claims-of-heritage/2327512/ |access-date=8 August 2023 |publisher=Tampa Times |date=June 17, 2017}}</ref> * [[Panama City, Florida|Panama City]]: city flag is quite similar to the Florida state flag with a white background and the St Andrews cross echoing the Confederate Battle Flag, but with the city seal replacing the state seal.
===City holiday=== * On April 2, 2019, Ocala mayor Kent Guinn signed a declaration declaring that April 26, 2019, would be Confederate Memorial Day. He said he has done so in previous years.<ref>{{cite news |title=Mayor announces 'Confederate Memorial Day.' A city council member says it should cost him his job |first=Reis |last=Thebault |date=April 5, 2019 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/nation/2019/04/06/mayor-announces-confederate-memorial-day-city-councilor-says-it-should-cost-him-his-job/}}</ref>
===County holiday=== * In 2016, the Commission of [[Marion County, Florida|Marion County]] (county seat Ocala) declared April as Confederate History Month.<ref name=Ocala/>
==Georgia==
{{Main|List of Confederate monuments and memorials in Georgia}} {{As of|2020|June|24|df=US}}, there are at least 201 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Georgia (U.S. state)|Georgia]]. [[File:Confederate monument, Mulberry Street, Decoration Day display, circa 1877 - DPLA - bcba8ddfd2acd4f29668355f795d5ee6.jpeg|thumb|Confederate monument in Macon, Ga on Mulberry street circa 1877]]
==Hawaii== * A plaque in the [[National Memorial Cemetery of the Pacific]] commemorates Hawaiians who fought for the Union, as well as Hawaiians who fought for the Confederacy.
==Idaho== The settlement of Idaho coincided with the Civil War and settlers from Southern states memorialized the Confederacy with the names of several towns and natural features.<ref>{{Cite web|date=1983|title=History of the Boise National Forest, 1905–1976|url=https://www.fs.usda.gov/Internet/FSE_DOCUMENTS/fsbdev3_042206.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141227051827/http://www.fs.usda.gov/Internet/FSE_DOCUMENTS/fsbdev3_042206.pdf|url-status=dead|archive-date=December 27, 2014|publisher=Idaho State Historical Society|page=25}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.ktvb.com/article/news/local/legacy-of-the-civil-war-in-idaho/464654495|title=Legacy of the Civil War in Idaho|last=Parsons|first=Gretchen|date=August 16, 2017|website=www.ktvb.com|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref><ref name="confidaho">{{cite web|url=https://www.theclio.com/web/entry?id=21447|title=Lower Boise Historical Marker-Confederates in Idaho|website=Clio|access-date=August 24, 2017}}</ref>
{{As of|2020|June|24|df=US}}, there are at least three public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Idaho]].
===Inhabited places=== * [[Atlanta, Idaho|Atlanta]]: unincorporated, and its [[Atlanta Airport (Idaho)|Atlanta Airport]]. The area was named by Southerners after reports of a Confederate victory over Gen. Sherman in the [[Battle of Atlanta]], which turned to be wholly false, but the name stuck. * Confederate Gulch: unincorporated former mining community.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://idfg.idaho.gov/ifwis/fishingplanner/water/?id=3170|title=Confederate Gulch {{!}} Idaho Fishing Planner|last=Game|first=Brent Thomas, Idaho Fish and|website=idfg.idaho.gov|language=EN|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref><ref name="confidaho" /> * Grayback Gulch: unincorporated former mining community, settled by Confederate soldiers and named for the color of their uniforms. Now a U.S. Forest Service campground.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.fs.usda.gov/r04/boise/recreation|title=Boise National Forest – Grayback Campground and Group|website=www.fs.usda.gov|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref> * [[Leesburg, Idaho|Leesburg]]: an unincorporated former goldmining town settled by southerners and named for Robert E. Lee.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.ktvb.com/article/news/local/legacy-of-the-civil-war-in-idaho/464654495|title=Legacy of the Civil War in Idaho|last=Parsons|first=Gretchen|date=August 15, 2017|website=KTVB-TV|language=en|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref>
===Natural features and recreation=== *Located within [[Boise National Forest]] in [[Elmore County, Idaho|Elmore County]] are: **[[Robert E. Lee Campground]],<ref>{{cite book | title = Camp and Picnic in the National Forests of the Intermountain Region | publisher = United States Forest Service | year = 1981 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=8fUTAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA46| pages = 46}}</ref> now dispersed<ref name="idahostatesman.com">{{cite web|url=https://www.idahostatesman.com/news/northwest/idaho/article244065002.html|title=Is there a Robert E. Lee campground in the Boise National Forest?|website=www.idahostatesman.com|access-date=January 29, 2021}}</ref> **Robert E Lee Creek<ref>{{Cite GNIS|389438|Robert E. Lee Creek}}</ref> *Chattanooga Hot Springs, near Atlanta, ID<ref>labeled on public state signs</ref> *Secesh (Successionist) Summit and [[Secesh River]]<ref name="idahostatesman.com"/>
==Illinois== [[File:Confederate Mound cropped.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Confederate Monument at [[Oak Woods Cemetery]] in [[Chicago]]]] The four memorials in [[Illinois]] are in federal government cemeteries and connected with prisoners of war.
===Federal cemeteries=== * [[Alton, Illinois|Alton]]: UDC monument (1909), North Alton Confederate Cemetery. Dedicated to Confederate soldiers who died at [[Alton Military Prison]]<ref>{{cite web | title = North Alton Confederate Cemetery | publisher = U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs | url = https://www.cem.va.gov/cems/lots/north_alton.asp | access-date = October 12, 2017}}</ref> As of October 2018, it is one of 7 cemeteries with Confederate monuments that the [[Veterans Administration]] has under 24-hour guard.<ref name=Veterans/> * [[Rock Island, Illinois|Rock Island]]: UDC obelisk (2003), Rock Island Confederate Cemetery. Dedicated to Confederate soldiers who died at [[Rock Island Military Prison]]<ref>{{cite web | title = Rock Island Confederate Cemetery | publisher = National Park Service | url = https://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/illinois/rock_island_confederate_cemetery.html | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20141124072305/http://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/Illinois/Rock_Island_Confederate_Cemetery.html | url-status = dead | archive-date = November 24, 2014 | access-date = October 14, 2017}}</ref> * [[Springfield, Illinois|Springfield]]: UDC/SCV monument (2005), [[Camp Butler National Cemetery]]. Dedicated to Confederate soldiers who died at Camp Butler.<ref>{{cite web | title = Camp Butler National Cemetery | publisher = U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs | date = August 28, 2017 | url = https://www.cem.va.gov/cems/nchp/campbutler.asp}}</ref>
===Federal plot within private cemetery=== * [[Chicago]]: Confederate Mound (1895), [[Oak Woods Cemetery]]. [[Mass grave]] and monument dedicated to Confederate soldiers who died at [[Camp Douglas (Chicago)|Camp Douglas]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Confederate Mound at Oak Woods Cemetery Chicago, Illinois | publisher = National Park Service | url = https://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/illinois/confederate_mound_oak_woods_cemetery.html | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20150322235338/http://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/Illinois/Confederate_Mound_Oak_Woods_Cemetery.html | url-status = dead | archive-date = March 22, 2015 | access-date = October 12, 2017}}</ref> As of October 2018, the [[Veterans Administration]] has it under dawn to dusk guard.<ref name=Veterans/> It is No. 7 on the [[Make It Right Project]]'s 2018 list of the 10 Confederate monuments it most wants removed.<ref name="independentmediainstitute.org">{{cite web |title=Announcing the Launch of the Make It Right Project |first=Kali |last=Holloway |date=June 3, 2018 |access-date=September 10, 2018 |url=https://independentmediainstitute.org/make-it-right-project-announcement/ |publisher=Independent Media Institute}}</ref> A counter memorial, a large black granite cenotaph was erected nearby in 1896, dedicated to abolitionists and unionists of the pre-war South.<ref>{{Cite news |title=It Tells His Life Story: Abolitionist Shaft in Oakwoods Erected by T.D. Lowther |date=June 9, 1896 |work=Chicago Tribune |page=1}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=Monument makes case for statue removal |last=Grossman |first=Ron |date=September 24, 2017 |work=Chicago Tribune |department=Section 1 |page=11}}</ref>
==Indiana== {{As of|2020|06|24|df=US}}, there is at least one public space with Confederate monuments in [[Indiana]]. [[File:Crown Hill Confederates.JPG|thumb|right|Confederate monument, [[Crown Hill National Cemetery]], Indianapolis]] * Multiple locations: There are 27 historical markers/point-of-interest displays marking the route of [[John Hunt Morgan]] through Indiana.<ref>{{cite web |title=John Hunt Morgan Map of the Trail |author=Historic Hoosier Hills Research, Consetvation & Development |url=https://m.hhhills.org/Map-of-the-Trail.html |access-date=December 12, 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=John Hunt Morgan Heritage Trail in Indiana : a tour guide to the Indiana portion of Morgan's Great Raid, July 8-13, 1863 |first=Lora Schmidt |last=Cahill |year=2011 |location=[[Attica, Ohio]] |publisher=K-Hill |isbn=978-1889030227 |oclc=650094122 }}</ref> * [[Corydon, Indiana|Corydon]]: [[Corydon Battle Site]] is a memorial to both sides that fought in the [[Battle of Corydon]], the only Civil War battle in Indiana. It contains Corydon's Civil War Museum.<ref>{{cite web |title=Corydon's Civil War Museum |url=http://www.corydonbattlepark.com/museum.html |author=Harrison County Park and Recreation Department |access-date=December 2, 2018 |archive-date=July 6, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190706235423/http://www.corydonbattlepark.com/museum.html |url-status=dead }}</ref> * [[Evansville, Indiana|Evansville]]: The Confederate monument (1904) at [[Oak Hill Cemetery (Evansville, Indiana)|Oak Hill Cemetery]] marks the burial site of 24 Confederate prisoners who died at Evansville.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.courierpress.com/story/news/2017/08/17/heres-why-evansville-has-confederate-monument/104692710/|title=Evansville Confederate monument controversy-free|work=Evansville Courier & Press|date=August 17, 2017|access-date=August 28, 2017}}</ref> * [[Indianapolis]]: ** [[Confederate Soldiers and Sailors Monument (Indianapolis)|Confederate Soldiers and Sailors Monument]], [[Garfield Park (Indianapolis)|Garfield Park]],<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.indystar.com/story/news/2014/07/12/group-wants-restore-confederate-fighters-monument/12586379/|title=Group wants to restore Confederate fighters' monument|last=Ryckaert|first=Vic|date=July 12, 2014|work=Indianapolis Star|access-date=August 16, 2017|language=en}}</ref> erected, according to its inscription, "to mark the burial place of 1616 Confederate soldiers and sailors who died here as prisoners of war and whose graves cannot now be identified."<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.indystar.com/story/news/crime/2017/08/17/indianapolis-explore-removal-confederate-monument-garfield-park/578392001/ |newspaper=Indianapolis Star (IndyStar) |date=August 17, 2017 |access-date=November 24, 2017 |title=Confederate monument discussion comes to Indianapolis over Garfield Park's memorial |first=Holly V. |last=Hays}}</ref> Following protests of the [[murder of George Floyd]] in June 2020, the city announced plans to remove the monument.<ref>{{cite news |title=Confederate Tower Still in Place 1 Year After Removal Calls |date=August 2, 2018 |agency=[[Associated Press]] |newspaper=[[U.S. News & World Report]] |url=https://www.usnews.com/news/best-states/indiana/articles/2018-08-02/confederate-tower-still-in-place-1-year-after-removal-calls}}</ref> ** A granite monument was erected in 1933 at [[Crown Hill National Cemetery]], a burial site for Confederate prisoners who died at [[Camp Morton]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Crown Hill Confederate Plot | publisher = U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs | date = August 28, 2017 | url = https://www.cem.va.gov/cems/lots/crownhill.asp}}</ref> * [[Terre Haute, Indiana|Terre Haute]]: Woodlawn Monument Site (1912), Woodlawn Cemetery. Erected by the Federal Government to commemorate 11 Confederate soldiers who died in a local prison camp.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/indiana/Woodlawn_Monument_Site.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150311155153/http://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/Indiana/Woodlawn_Monument_Site.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=March 11, 2015|title=Woodlawn Monument Site|access-date=August 20, 2017}}</ref> * [[Versailles, Indiana|Versailles]]: Versailles is the location of a skirmish with [[Morgan's Raiders]]. South Ripley High School named their mascot the Raiders in honor of [[John Hunt Morgan]]'s campaign across Indiana.
==Iowa== {{As of|2020|06|24|df=US}}, there is at least one public space with Confederate monuments in [[Iowa]]. * [[Bentonsport]]: Monument to [[Lawrence Sullivan Ross]] (2007), Iowa's only Confederate general<ref name=":19">{{Cite news|url=http://www.desmoinesregister.com/story/news/local/columnists/kyle-munson/2017/08/25/americas-civil-war-over-confederate-monuments-takes-root-iowa/588650001/|title='Stupid liberals' vs. white privilege: Iowa caught up in Confederate monuments debate|last=Munson|first=Kyle|date=August 25, 2017|work=[[Des Moines Register]]|access-date=August 30, 2017|language=en}}</ref> * [[Bloomfield, Iowa|Bloomfield]]:<ref name=":19" /> Confederate Invasion of Iowa Monument (2005)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.roadsideamerica.com/story/23199|title=Confederate Invasion of Iowa Monument, Bloomfield, Iowa|website=RoadsideAmerica.com}}</ref>
==Kansas==
Veterans Memorial Park in Wichita, Kansas holds one Confederate and Union monument, a Reconciliation Memorial. "The intent of this memorial is to bring folks together and reconcile their differences," [https://www.kmuw.org/post/confederate-monuments-come-down-across-us-wichita-memorial-comes-question As Confederate Monuments Come Down Across U.S., Wichita Memorial Comes Into Question]. The Memorial is a small obelisk with text honoring North and South combatants on both sides. See [[Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Kansas]] for monuments which have been removed.
==Kentucky== {{see also|List of Civil War Monuments of Kentucky|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Kentucky}} {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 37 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Kentucky]].
===Monuments=== * [[Augusta, Kentucky|Augusta]]: [[Confederate Monument in Augusta|Confederate Monument]], Payne Cemetery, 1903. * [[Bardstown, Kentucky|Bardstown]]: [[Confederate Monument of Bardstown|Confederate Monument]], Bardstown/St. Joseph's Cemetery, 1903. * [[Bowling Green, Kentucky|Bowling Green]]: ** [[Confederate Monument of Bowling Green]], Fairview Cemetery, 1876. ** [[William F. Perry Monument]], Fairview Cemetery, 1901. * [[Brandenburg, Kentucky|Brandenburg]]: Confederate monument (2016) removed from Louisville<ref>{{cite news|url=https://latimes.com/nation/la-na-confederate-monument-laws-20170815-htmlstory.html|title=In some states, it's illegal to take down monuments or change street names honoring the Confederacy|newspaper=[[Los Angeles Times]]|access-date=September 4, 2017}}</ref> Armed protesters surrounded the monument to prevent its removal in June 2020.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Chapman |first1=Matthew |title=Armed group protects Confederate statue in Kentucky: 'This is our battle line' |url=https://www.rawstory.com/2020/06/armed-group-protects-confederate-statue-in-kentucky-this-is-our-battle-line/ |website=www.rawstory.com |date=June 13, 2020 |access-date=June 13, 2020}}</ref> * [[Cadiz, Kentucky|Cadiz]]: [[Confederate Monument (Cadiz, Kentucky)|Confederate Monument]], Trigg County Courthouse, 1913.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.trailsrus.com/monuments/reg1/cadiz.html|title=Cadiz, Ky|website=Trails-R-Us|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Covington, Kentucky|Covington]]: [[Veteran's Monument in Covington|Veteran's Monument]], Linden Grove Cemetery, 1933. It was dedicated by the American Legion and honors both Confederate and Union veterans. * [[Crab Orchard, Kentucky|Crab Orchard]]: [[Confederate Monument at Crab Orchard|Confederate Monument]], Crab Orchard Cemetery, 1872. * [[Cynthiana, Kentucky|Cynthiana]]: [[Confederate Monument in Cynthiana|Confederate Monument]], Battle Grove Cemetery, 1869. * [[Danville, Kentucky|Danville]]: [[Confederate Monument in Danville|Confederate Monument]], McDowell Park, 1910.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.trailsrus.com/monuments/reg4/danville.html|title=Danville, Ky|website=Trails-R-Us|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Eminence, Kentucky|Eminence]]: [[Confederate Soldiers Martyrs Monument in Eminence|Confederate Soldiers Martyrs Monument]], Eminence Cemetery, 1870. * [[Fairview, Kentucky|Fairview]]: Confederate monument at the [[Jefferson Davis State Historic Site|Jefferson Davis Monument State Historic Site]], a state park commemorating Davis's birthplace, completed in 1924. It contains a "35-story obelisk."<ref name="Hampson">{{cite news |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/2017/05/22/confederate-monuments-new-orleans-charlottesville-removal-race-civil-war/101870418/ |newspaper=[[USA Today]] |date=May 22, 2017 |access-date=November 24, 2017 |title=Confederate monuments, more than 700 across USA, aren't budging |first=Rick |last=Hampson}}</ref> * [[Frankfort, Kentucky|Frankfort]]: [[Confederate Monument in Frankfort|Confederate Monument]], Frankfort Cemetery, 1892. * [[Fulton, Kentucky|Fulton]]: [[Confederate Memorial in Fulton|Confederate Monument]], Fairview Cemetery, 1902. [[File:ConfederateSoldierMemorial.jpg|thumb|upright|Confederate Monument, Georgetown]] * [[Georgetown, Kentucky|Georgetown]]: [[Confederate Monument in Georgetown|Confederate Monument]], Georgetown Cemetery, 1888.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WM28P4_Confederate_Monument_Georgetown_Kentucky|title=Confederate Monument, Georgetown, Kentucky|website=Waymarking|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Graves County, Kentucky|Graves County]]: [[Camp Beauregard Memorial]] (1909), Camp Beauregard Cemetery. UDC memorial to Confederate soldiers who died at Camp Beauregard "for the Confederate State of America and were denied the glory of heroic service in a battle".<ref>{{cite web | title = Camp Beauregard Memorial | publisher = National Park Service | date = July 17, 1997 | url = https://npgallery.nps.gov/GetAsset/bfac2ae9-7f3a-4f9d-80ec-7aa4794ad34d | access-date = October 12, 2017}}</ref> * [[Glasgow, Kentucky|Glasgow]]: [[Confederate Monument in Glasgow|Confederate Monument]], Barren County Courthouse, 1905. [[File:Spring Hill Cemetery Harrodsburg Kentucky.jpg|thumb|upright|Confederate Monument, Spring Hill Cemetery, Harrodsburg]] * [[Harrodsburg, Kentucky|Harrodsburg]]: [[Confederate Monument in Harrodsburg|Confederate Monument]], Spring Hill Cemetery, 1902. * [[Hickman, Kentucky|Hickman]]: [[Confederate Memorial Gateway in Hickman|Confederate Memorial Gateway]], Hickman City Cemetery, 1913. * [[Hopkinsville, Kentucky|Hopkinsville]]: ** [[Confederate Memorial Fountain in Hopkinsville|Confederate Memorial Fountain]], Christian County Courthouse, 1911. ** [[Latham Confederate Monument]], Riverside Cemetery, 1887. * [[Horse Cave, Kentucky|Horse Cave]]: [[Unknown Confederate Soldier Monument in Horse Cave|Unknown Confederate Soldier Monument]], Old Dixie Highway, 1934. * [[Jeffersontown, Kentucky|Jeffersontown]]: [[Confederate Martyrs Monument in Jeffersontown|Confederate Martyrs Monument]], Jeffersontown City Cemetery, 1904. * [[Lawrenceburg, Kentucky|Lawrenceburg]]: [[Confederate Monument in Lawrenceburg|Confederate Monument]], Anderson County Courthouse, 1894. * [[Lexington, Kentucky|Lexington]]: ** [[Ladies' Confederate Memorial]], [[Lexington Cemetery]], 1874. ** [[Confederate Soldier Monument in Lexington]], [[Lexington Cemetery]], 1893. ** [[John C. Breckinridge Memorial]] moved from Courthouse to [[Lexington Cemetery]] Oct 2017.<ref name=Hunt/> ** [[John Hunt Morgan Memorial]] moved from Courthouse to [[Lexington Cemetery]] October 2017.<ref name=Hunt>{{cite news|url=https://www.kentucky.com/news/local/counties/fayette-county/article179392076.html|title=In a surprise move, Lexington removes controversial Confederate statues|first1=Morgan|last1=Eads|first2=Karla|last2=Ward|first3=Beth|last3=Musgrave|newspaper=[[Herald-Leader]]|date=October 17, 2017}}</ref> [[File:John B. Castleman Monument.jpg|thumb|upright|John B. Castleman Monument, Louisville]] * [[Louisville, Kentucky|Louisville]]: ** [[Confederate Monument in Louisville|Confederate Monument]], dedicated in 1895 and was placed next to the [[University of Louisville]] on city property. It was removed and re-located to a riverfront park in [[Brandenburg, Kentucky]], in December 2016.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.foxnews.com/us/confederate-statue-removed-from-university-of-louisville-campus-rededicated-in-kentucky/|title=Confederate statue removed from University of Louisville campus rededicated in Kentucky|publisher=[[Fox News]]|date=May 30, 2017|access-date=August 16, 2017}}</ref> * [[Madisonville, Kentucky|Madisonville]]: Confederate Monument, Hopkins County Courthouse, 1909.<ref name="usatodayky">{{cite news|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/2017/08/20/confederate-monuments-receive-mixed-reactions-western-ky/104795018/|title=Confederate monuments receive mixed reactions in Western KY|newspaper=[[USA Today]]|date=August 20, 2017|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Mayfield, Kentucky|Mayfield]]: ** [[Confederate Memorial in Mayfield|Confederate Monument]], Graves County Courthouse, 1920. ** [[Confederate Memorial Gates in Mayfield|Confederate Monument]], Maplewood Cemetery, 1924. * [[Maysville, Kentucky|Maysville]]: plaque commemorating [[Albert Sidney Johnston]].<ref>{{gnis|2744556|Albert Sidney Johnston Historical Marker}}</ref> * [[Midway, Kentucky|Midway]]: [[Martyrs Monument in Midway|Martyrs Monument]], Midway City Cemetery, 1890. * [[Morganfield, Kentucky|Morganfield]]: [[Confederate Monument of Morganfield|Confederate Monument]], City Cemetery/Odd Fellows Cemetery, 1870. * [[Morgantown, Kentucky|Morgantown]]: [[Confederate-Union Veterans' Monument in Morgantown|Confederate-Union Veterans' Monument]], Butler County Courthouse, 1907. * [[Mt. Sterling, Kentucky|Mt. Sterling]]: [[Confederate Monument of Mt. Sterling|Confederate Monument]], Machpelan Cemetery, 1880. * [[Munfordville, Kentucky|Munfordville]]: [[Colonel Robert A. Smith Monument]], near the Green River, 1885. * [[Murray, Kentucky|Murray]]: [[Confederate Monument, Murray, Kentucky|Confederate Monument]], Calloway County Courthouse, 1917. * [[Nancy, Kentucky|Nancy]]: [[General Felix K. Zollicoffer Monument]], Zollicoffer Park, [[Mill Springs Battlefield]], 1910. * [[Nancy, Kentucky|Nancy]]: [[Confederate Mass Grave Monument in Somerset|Confederate Mass Grave Monument]], Zollicoffer Park, [[Mill Springs Battlefield]], 1910. * [[Nicholasville, Kentucky|Nicholasville]]: Confederate Memorial, Jessamine County Courthouse, 1896.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.trailsrus.com/monuments/reg4/nicholasville.html|title=Nicholasville, Ky|website=Trails-R-Us|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Owensboro, Kentucky|Owensboro]]: [[Confederate Monument in Owensboro|Confederate Monument]], Daviess County Courthouse, 1900. * [[Owingsville, Kentucky|Owingsville]]: [[Confederate Monument in Owingsville|Confederate Monument]], Owingsville Cemetery, 1907. [[File:Tilghman Monument 2.jpg|thumb|upright|Lloyd Tilghman Statue, Paducah]] * [[Paducah, Kentucky|Paducah]]: ** [[Confederate Monument in Paducah|Confederate Monument]], Oak Grove Cemetery, 1907.<ref name="paducah">{{cite web|url=http://www.paducahky.gov/monuments-and-memorials|website=City of Paducah|title=Monuments and Memorials|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> ** First Confederate Volunteer From Paducah, marker embedded in city sidewalk.<ref name="paducah" /> ** General [[Lloyd Tilghman]] Statue, Lang Park, 1909.<ref name="paducah" /> * [[Paris, Kentucky|Paris]]: [[Bourbon County Confederate Monument]], Paris Cemetery, 1887. * [[Perryville, Kentucky|Perryville]]: ** [[Confederate Monument in Perryville|Confederate Monument]], [[Perryville Battlefield State Historic Site]], 1902. Commissioned by the state of Kentucky.<ref name="perryville">{{cite web|url=http://www.trailsrus.com/monuments/reg4/perryville.html|title=Perryville, Ky|website=Trails-R-Us|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> ** [[Unknown Confederate Dead Monument in Perryville|Unknown Confederate Dead Monument]], Goodknight Cemetery, 1928.<ref name="perryville" /> * [[Pewee Valley, Kentucky|Pewee Valley]]: [[Pewee Valley Confederate Cemetery|Pewee Valley Confederate Monument]], Pewee Valley Confederate Cemetery, 1904. * [[Princeton, Kentucky|Princeton]]: [[Confederate Soldier Monument in Caldwell|Confederate Soldier Monument]], Caldwell County Courthouse, 1912.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WM44F4_Confederate_Soldier_Monument_Princeton_KY|title=Confederate Soldier Monument-Princeton, KY|website=Waymarking|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Russellville, Kentucky|Russellville]]: [[Confederate Monument in Russellville|Confederate Monument]], Town Square, 1910. * [[Shepherdsville, Kentucky|Shepherdsville]]: John Hunt Morgan's Raid Historical Markers<ref>{{gnis|2744167|John Hunt Morgans Raid Historical Marker}}</ref>{{efn|Pair of Kentucky Historic Markers located on KY 61, near bridge crossing at Salt River, near Shepherdsville. Marker #1296, "L & N Bridge in Civil War. Destroyed three times by CSA. Partially razed on Sept. 7, 1862, by troops under Col. John Hutcheson. During the occupation of Shepherdsville, Sept. 28, Braxton Bragg's troops again destroyed it, but new bridge was up by Oct. 11. After Battle of Elizabethtown, Dec. 27, John Hunt Morgan's men moved along tracks, destroying everything on way to trestle works at Muldraugh's Hill." Marker #1413, "Morgan-on to Ohio. July 2, 1863, CSA Gen. J. H. Morgan began raid to prevent USA move to Tenn. and Va. Repulsed at Green River, July 4. Defeated a USA force at Lebanon, July 5. Moved through Bardstown, July 6. After night march, crossed here July 7. Rested troops few hours and proceeded to Brandenburg. Crossed to Indiana, July 8. He continued raid until captured in northeast Ohio, July 26." See also [[Morgan's Raid]].<ref name="Search For Markers">{{Cite web|url=https://secure.kentucky.gov/kyhs/hmdb/MarkerSearch.aspx|title=Search For Markers|website=[[Kentucky Historical Society]]|access-date=July 1, 2020}}</ref>}} * [[Somerset, Kentucky|Somerset]]: ** [[Battle of Dutton's Hill Monument]] (1875) on private property, Old Crab Orchard Road.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.lctourism.com/explore/history/item/315-dutton-s-hill|title=Dutton Hill|website=Lake Cumberland Tourism|access-date=August 23, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170824095458/http://www.lctourism.com/explore/history/item/315-dutton-s-hill|archive-date=August 24, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> Kentucky Historical Marker at roadside.{{efn|Kentucky Historic Marker located 2 mi. N. of Somerset, KY 39. Marker #712, "March 30, 1863, USA force of 1,250 under [[Quincy Adams Gillmore|General Q. A. Gillmore]] overtook 1,550 Confederate cavalry under [[John Pegram (general)|Gen. John Pegram]], here. Five-hour battle resulted. CSA driven from one position to another, withdrew during night across [[Cumberland River|Cumberland]]. Killed, wounded, missing, CSA 200 and USA 30. On nine-day expedition into Ky., CSA had captured 750 cattle and took 537 across river.".<ref name="Search For Markers"/>}} * [[Springfield, Kentucky|Springfield]]: John Hunt Morgan's Raids Historical Marker<ref>{{gnis|2744851|John Hunt Morgan's Raids Historical Marker}}</ref>{{efn |Kentucky Historic Marker located Springfield, US 150, KY 55. Marker #689, erected in 1964, "CSA Gen. [[John Hunt Morgan|John H. Morgan]]'s cavalry moved thru Springfield on raids, July 12 and December 30, 1862. On third raid, into Ohio, after [[Battle of Lebanon (Kentucky)|battle of Lebanon]], July 5, 1863, Union prisoners brought here but paroled to speed CSA movement. Confederate invasion force of 16,000 here before meeting Union Army in [[Battle of Perryville|battle at Perryville]], Oct. 8, 1862. See map other side."<ref name="Search For Markers"/>}} * [[St. Joseph, Kentucky|St. Joseph]]: [[Thompson and Powell Martyrs Monument]], St. Alphonsus Catholic Church Cemetery, 1880.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.trailsrus.com/monuments/reg2/st_joseph.html|title=St. Joseph|website=Trails-R-Us|access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Versailles, Kentucky|Versailles]]: [[Confederate Monument in Versailles|Confederate Monument]], Versailles Cemetery, 1877. * [[Winchester, Kentucky|Winchester]]: John Hunt Morgan Historical Marker<ref>{{gnis|2744749|John Hunt Morgan Historical Marker}}</ref>{{ efn|Kentucky Historic Marker #625, "Morgan's Men Here" located in Winchester, Kentucky on Courthouse lawn, US 60 & KY 627. Inscribed "CSA Gen. John H. Morgan's cavalry first raided Kentucky July, 1862. Took Cynthiana but, faced by large USA forces, withdrew. Destroyed arms here on 19th and went to Richmond. On last raid, June 1864, after two battles at Mt. Sterling, they moved by here to Lexington and to Cynthiana where they met defeat on 12th and retreated to Virginia. See map on other side." Dedicated March 9, 1964. See also [[Battle of Cynthiana]].<ref name="Search For Markers"/>}}
===Bridge=== * [[Cynthiana, Kentucky|Cynthiana]]: The [[John Hunt Morgan]] Bridge, on South Main St./[[U.S. 27]], is named for a Confederate general.
===Inhabited places=== * [[Breckinridge Center, Kentucky|Breckinridge Center]]: a census-designated place in [[Union County, Kentucky]]. Named because of nearby [[Camp Breckinridge]], which was named for [[John C. Breckinridge]], U.S. Vice President and Confederate general. * [[Lee County, Kentucky|Lee County]] (1870) * [[Confederate, Kentucky]] * [[Vanceburg, Kentucky|Vanceburg]], named for Confederate Colonel and North Carolina governor [[Zebulon Baird Vance]]. His wife was from Kentucky.
===Parks=== * [[Fairview, Kentucky|Fairview]]: [[Jefferson Davis State Historic Site]] (1957) the site includes Davis's birthplace, a memorial to Davis that includes a 341 ft obelisk (the second tallest obelisk in the world after the [[Washington Monument]]), Confederate flags and other signs and plaques.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://parks.ky.gov/parks/historicsites/jefferson-davis/|title=Jefferson Davis State Historic Site – Kentucky State Parks|website=parks.ky.gov}}</ref> * [[Lexington, Kentucky|Lexington]]: A [[historical marker]] identifying the approximate site of the birth of Confederate Brigadier General [[David Rice Atchison]] is located along [[Kentucky Route 1974|Highway 1974]] in the [[Lansdowne, Lexington|Landsdowne]] neighborhood of [[Lexington, Kentucky]].<ref>{{cite web| url=https://www.roadsideamerica.com/tip/53798| title=Lexington, Kentucky: One-Day President Birthplace| website=roadsideamerica.com| access-date=June 29, 2018}}</ref>
===Roads=== {{Div col|colwidth=20em}} * [[Elkton, Kentucky|Elkton]]: ** [[Jefferson Davis Highway]] ** Jefferson Davis Road * [[Erlanger, Kentucky|Erlanger]]: ** General Ross Drive ** General Stuart Drive ** Jefferson Davis Place ** Robert E. Lee Drive * [[Madisonville, Kentucky|Madisonville]]: Jefferson Davis Drive * [[Mayfield, Kentucky|Mayfield]]: Jeff Davis Road *[[Olive Hill, Kentucky|Olive Hill]]: John Hunt Morgan Lane * [[Raywick, Kentucky|Raywick]]: Robert Lee Road * [[Vine Grove, Kentucky|Vine Grove]]: [[Stand Watie]] Road {{div col end}}
===Highways=== * [[Jefferson Davis Highway|Jefferson Davis Memorial Highway]]: Marker located in the Fountain Avenue median in Paducah, 1935. The marker is along an auxiliary route that follows [[U.S. Route 68]] from Bowling Green, Kentucky to Paducah, Kentucky.<ref name="paducah" />
===Schools=== * [[Bedford, Kentucky|Bedford]]: Trimble County High School named their mascot the Raiders, in honor of [[John Hunt Morgan]]'s men. * [[Danville, Kentucky|Danville]]: Boyle County High School uses the Rebels nickname.<ref name="KY mascots" /> * [[Eastern, Kentucky|Eastern]]: [[Allen Central High School]]: Allen Central athletic teams are nicknamed the "Rebels." The school features various Confederate iconography<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ocala.com/news/20061210/eastern-kentucky-students-hold-onto-confederate-school-symbols|title=Eastern Kentucky students hold onto Confederate school symbols|last=Jafari|first=Samira|date=December 10, 2006|work=[[Ocala Star Banner]]|access-date=August 31, 2017|language=en}}</ref> <!--Commented out so but leave here so it does not get readded* [[Lexington, Kentucky|Lexington]]: Stonewall Elementary School (1961) is not named for Stonewall Jackson but for actual walls made from stone.<ref>kentucky.com/news/local/counties/fayette-county/article172477776.html</ref>--> * Atherton High School in Louisville used the Rebels nickname until 2021.<ref name="KY mascots" /><ref name="Ravens not Rebels">{{cite news |title=GOODBYE REBELS: Atherton High School announces new mascot |url=https://www.wdrb.com/news/goodbye-rebels-atherton-high-school-announces-new-mascot/article_d03ab5b2-207e-11ec-bed8-cfd08973aa7d.html |access-date=8 August 2023 |publisher=WDRB |date=September 28, 2021}}</ref> * [[Florence, Kentucky|Florence]]: [[Boone County High School]] uses the Rebels nickname.<ref name="KY mascots" /> * [[Liberty, Kentucky|Liberty]]: Casey County High School uses the Rebels nickname.<ref name="KY mascots" /> * [[Munfordville, Kentucky|Munfordville]]: Hart County High School named their mascot the Raiders, in honor of [[John Hunt Morgan]]'s men. Also, a large mural in the town depicts Morgan. * [[Owenton, Kentucky|Owenton]]: Owen County High School uses the Rebels nickname.<ref name="KY mascots" /> * [[Richmond, Kentucky|Richmond]]: [[Eastern Kentucky University]]. EKU athletic teams are nicknamed the "[[Eastern Kentucky Colonels|Colonels]]," a Confederate figure.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/sports/college/time-colleges-dump-old-south-nicknames-mascots-article-1.3421433|title=It's time for colleges to dump Old South nicknames and mascots|last=Phillips|first=Carron J.|date=August 18, 2017|work=[[New York Daily News]]|access-date=August 31, 2017|language=en}}</ref> * Todd County Central High School uses the nickname Rebels.<ref name="KY mascots">{{cite news |last1=Moore |first1=Josh |title=For some Kentucky high schools, Rebels nickname OK for now |url=https://www.ksba.org/protected/ArticleView.aspx?iid=6GGGI2Y&dasi=3G32 |access-date=8 August 2023 |newspaper=Herald-Leader |date=June 28, 2015 |location=Lexington, Kentucky}}</ref>
==Louisiana== {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Louisiana}}
{{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 83 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Louisiana]].
===State capitol=== * Gov. [[Francis T. Nicholls]] Statue (1934). Nicholls was a [[brigadier general]] in the Confederate Army. * Gov. [[Henry Watkins Allen]] Statue (1934). Allen was a [[brigadier general]] in the Confederate Army. He is buried on the [[Old Louisiana State Capitol]] grounds. * "Silent Sentinel" Monument, officially the Confederate Soldiers of East and West Baton Rouge Parishes Memorial. [[Plinth]] erected 1886 and statue in 1890. Dedicated by Gov. [[John McEnery]].
