Original granite and marble plinth cracked; replaced in the 1960s with a small brick plinth that was aesthetically unappealing. Formerly at North Boulevard and 3rd Street, near City Hall. In 2012, to make room for Town Square construction, it was moved to the nearby [[Old Louisiana State Capitol]], now a museum.<ref name=225magazine>{{cite magazine |title=Will Confederate Landmarks in Baton Rouge Become the Subject of Controversy? |first=David |last=Jacobs |date=October 3, 2017 |magazine=225 Magazine |url=https://www.225batonrouge.com/our-city/will-confederate-landmarks-baton-rouge-become-subject-controversy}}</ref> Plaque reads: "Erected by the men and women of East and West Baton Rouge to perpetuate the heroism and patriotic devotion of the noble soldiers from the two parishes who wore the gray and crossed the river with their immortal leaders to rest under the shade of the trees. Original monument erected 1886 A.D."
===Buildings=== [[File:Civil War Museum in New Orleans.jpg|thumb|right|Confederate Memorial Hall in New Orleans]] * [[Baton Rouge, Louisiana|Baton Rouge]]: [[Edmund Kirby Smith]] Hall at Building [[Louisiana State University]] (1965/ 2011). In 2017 "slated for demolition".<ref name=225magazine/> * [[Thibodaux, Louisiana|Thibodaux]]: [[P. G. T. Beauregard|P.G.T. Beauregard]] Hall at [[Nicholls State University]] (1961/ 2010). The school is named for Confederate General and later Governor [[Francis T. Nicholls]]. * [[New Orleans]]: **[[Gibson Hall (Tulane University)|Gibson Hall at Tulane University]] (1894), named for [[Randall L. Gibson]] ** [[Confederate Memorial Hall Museum]] (1891), the oldest museum in Louisiana<ref>{{cite web|title=Confederate Memorial Hall Museum|publisher=Confederate Memorial Hall Museum|url=http://confederatemuseum.com/|access-date=October 1, 2017}}</ref> ** [[Adolph Meyer School]] (1917), New Orleans, named for [[Confederate States Army|Confederate]] general [[Adolph Meyer]] who served nine terms in the U.S. House of Representatives and advocated for the construction of the Algiers Naval Station across the street from where the school was later built. The school was renamed in the 1990s for [[Harriet Tubman]].
===Monuments=== ====Courthouse monuments==== *[[Alexandria, Louisiana|Alexandria]]: Rapides Parish Confederate Monument (1914) * [[Benton, Louisiana|Benton]]: Confederate Soldier Monument (1910) * [[East Feliciana Parish, Louisiana|East Feliciana Parish]] – Confederate Soldiers Monument in Front of the East Feliciana Courthouse Clinton Louisiana<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.loc.gov/item/2003668483/|title=East Feliciana Parish Court House, Clinton, Louisiana | Library of Congress|date=October 14, 2015|website=Loc.gov|access-date=May 23, 2017}}</ref> * [[Franklin, Louisiana|Franklin]]: Confederate Monument (1913) * [[Lake Charles, Louisiana|Lake Charles]]: South's Defenders Monument (1915); knocked from its pedestal by [[Hurricane Laura]]<ref>{{Cite web|last=Brito|first=Christopher|date=August 28, 2020|title=Hurricane Laura topples Confederate statue, two weeks after officials voted to keep it|url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/hurricane-laura-confederate-statue-louisiana-lake-charles/|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200831075953/https://www.cbsnews.com/news/hurricane-laura-confederate-statue-louisiana-lake-charles/|archive-date=August 31, 2020|access-date=August 31, 2020|website=www.cbsnews.com|language=en-US}}</ref> * [[Opelousas, Louisiana|Opelousas]]: Confederate Monument (1920) * [[Port Allen, Louisiana|Port Allen]]: [[Henry Watkins Allen]] Statue (1962) * [[Shreveport, Louisiana|Shreveport]]: [[Caddo Parish Confederate Monument|Confederate Monument]], on grounds of the [[Caddo Parish, Louisiana|Caddo Parish]] courthouse, dedicated in 1906 by UDC, NRHP-listed.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.nps.gov/nr/feature/places/13001124.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150502074934/http://www.nps.gov/nr/feature/places/13001124.htm|url-status=dead|archive-date=May 2, 2015|title=Caddo Parish Confederate Monument|publisher=National Park Service}}</ref> Moved to private land in rural [[De Soto County, Louisiana|De Soto County]] in 2022.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.arklatexhomepage.com/news/local-news/confederate-monument-relocated-to-de-soto-parish-battlefield-site/ | title=Confederate monument relocated to de Soto Parish battlefield site | date=July 5, 2022 }}</ref> * [[St. Francisville, Louisiana|St. Francisville]]: Confederate Monument (1903). Has Confederate flag above the inscription: "In memory of West Feliciana's Confederate dead, wherever at rest. Co. C 1st Regt. La. Cavalry". * [[Tallulah, Louisiana|Tallulah]]: Confederate Monument (1912) * [[Winnfield, Louisiana|Winnfield]]: Confederate Monument (1926)

[[Richard Taylor (Confederate general)|Richard Taylor]] memorial (1994) outside Pleasant Hill American Legion Hall<ref>{{cite web | title = Battle of Pleasant Hill | publisher = Civilwaralbum.com | url = http://www.civilwaralbum.com/louisiana/pleasanthill2.htm | access-date = June 18, 2018 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20170721174756/http://www.civilwaralbum.com/louisiana/pleasanthill2.htm | archive-date = July 21, 2017 | url-status = dead }}</ref> **[[Red River Campaign]] memorial (1994)<ref>{{cite web | title = Battle of Pleasant Hill | publisher = Civilwaralbum.com | url = http://www.civilwaralbum.com/louisiana/pleasanthill.htm | access-date = June 18, 2018 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20170608023437/http://www.civilwaralbum.com/louisiana/pleasanthill.htm | archive-date = June 8, 2017 | url-status = dead }}</ref> **UDC Confederate memorial<ref>{{cite web | last = Hilton | first = Mark | title = Battle of Pleasant Hill Memorial | publisher = HMdb.com | date = July 12, 2017 | url = https://www.hmdb.org/marker.asp?marker=105407}}</ref> * [[Donaldsonville, Louisiana|Donaldsonville]]: Fort Butler Memorial (1999) * [[Gretna, Louisiana|Gretna]]: Jefferson Davis Highway Marker * [[Homer, Louisiana|Homer]]: Confederate Monument (1940) * [[Johnson Bayou, Louisiana|Johnson Bayou]]: Robert E. L Statue (1984) * [[Lafayette, Louisiana|Lafayette]]: Brig. Gen. [[Alfred Mouton|J.J. Alfred A. Mouton]] Statue (1922). There was considerable local discussion about what to do with the monument.<ref>{{cite news|title=A Monumental Question: Amid a National Conversation on Confederate Iconography, What, If Anything, Should Lafayette Do with Our Own Memorial to Jim Crow|date=February 1, 2016|last=Pierce|first=Walter|newspaper=The Independent (Lafayette, Louisiana)|url=http://theind.com/article-22610-A-MONUMENTAL-QUESTION.html}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.theadvertiser.com/story/news/local/2016/02/20/should-lafayette-remove-confederate-statue-downtown/79845228/|title=Should Lafayette remove Confederate statue from downtown?|website=theadvertiser.com|access-date=September 10, 2017}}</ref> Removed on July 18, 2021.<ref>{{Cite web|date=July 18, 2021|title=Confederate statue removed from city hall in Louisiana after 99 years|url=http://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2021/jul/18/confederate-statue-removed-city-hall-louisiana|access-date=July 18, 2021|website=[[The Guardian]]|language=en}}</ref> * [[Marthaville, Louisiana|Marthaville]]: Unknown Confederate Soldier Monument (1970) * [[Minden, Louisiana|Minden]]: Confederate Monument (1933) * [[New Orleans]]: ** Confederate Monument (1874), [[Greenwood Cemetery, New Orleans|Greenwood Cemetery]]<ref>{{cite web|last=Branley|first=Edward|title=NOLA History: Greenwood Cemetery in Mid-City|publisher=New Orleans Tourism Marketing Corporation|date=January 27, 2014|url=http://gonola.com/things-to-do-in-new-orleans/about-gonola/nola-history-greenwood-cemetery-in-mid-city|access-date=February 2, 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171001213011/http://gonola.com/things-to-do-in-new-orleans/about-gonola/nola-history-greenwood-cemetery-in-mid-city|archive-date=October 1, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> ** Army of Tennessee Tomb (1886) at [[Metairie Cemetery]], consisting of a Gothic chapel tomb containing 48 vaults, surmounted by an equestrian statue of [[Albert Sidney Johnston]]. A marble statue near the tomb's entrance shows a Confederate sergeant calling the roll. Johnston, [[P. G. T. Beauregard]], and other Confederate soldiers are entombed there.<ref>{{cite book|last=Huber|first=Leonard V.|title=New Orleans Architecture: Volume III The Cemeteries|publisher=Pelican|year=1974|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7FlJubkDKxUC&pg=PP1|page=54|isbn=978-1455609345}}</ref> ** Monument to Confederate Colonel [[Charles Didier Dreux]], first Confederate officer killed in action. At intersection of South Jefferson Davis Parkway and [[Canal Street, New Orleans|Canal Street]], across from former location of [[Jefferson Davis Monument (New Orleans, Louisiana)|Jefferson Davis Monument]].<ref name=Serrano>{{cite news |title=Who Are the other Confederate Soldiers Honored with Statues on Jefferson Davis Parkway in Mid-City? |newspaper=Midcity Messenger |date=June 29, 2015 |access-date=March 22, 2018 |first=Alicia |last=Serrano |url=https://midcitymessenger.com/2015/06/29/who-are-the-other-confederate-soldiers-honored-with-statues-on-jefferson-davis-parkway-in-mid-city/}}</ref> ** Monument to Catholic priest [[Abram J. Ryan]], called poet-priest of the Confederacy. At South Jefferson Davis Parkway and Banks Street. Erected in 1949 by the United Daughters of the Confederacy.<ref name=Serrano /> ** Monument to General [[Albert Pike]], at South Jefferson Davis Parkway and Tulane Avenue. Erected 1957. Defaced with "[[Black Lives Matter]]" in 2015.<ref name=Serrano /> * [[Plaquemine, Louisiana|Plaquemine]]: Confederate Memorial (1914) * [[Shreveport, Louisiana|Shreveport]]: Fort Humbug Confederate Memorial (1927) * [[Tangipahoa Parish, Louisiana|Tangipahoa Parish]]: Confederate monument (1907) at [[Camp Moore]], a museum and former Confederate training camp<ref>{{cite web | title = Short History of Camp Moore | publisher = Camp Moore | url = http://www.campmoorela.com/History.html | access-date = June 18, 2018}}</ref> * [[Zachary, Louisiana|Zachary]]: Port Hudson Confederate Monument (1930)

[[Clio]], [[muse]] or goddess of history, is on a monument. (Gayarré was a historian.) The monument was paid for by George Hacker Dunbar, an [[artilleryman]] during the Civil War, married to a niece of General Beauregard. The original statue was replaced in 1938, after vandals damaged it.<ref>{{cite web |title=History's Muse |first=Ned |last=Hémard |year=2012 |publisher=New Orleans Bar Association |url=http://www.neworleansbar.org/uploads/files/HistorysMuse.6-27.pdf |access-date=March 22, 2018}}</ref> ** Governor Nicholls Street ** Jefferson Davis Parkway. Originally named Hagan Avenue; name changed in 1911 to coincide with the unveiling of the [[Jefferson Davis Monument (New Orleans, Louisiana)|Jefferson Davis Monument]].<ref name="Serrano"/> -now Norman C. Francis Parkway. ** Lee Circle ** Polk Street ** Robert E. Lee Junior High School – now Neville Junior High School. * [[Longville, Louisiana|Longville]]: ** South Beauregard Elementary School ** South Beauregard High School * [[Louisiana State University]] is home of the [[LSU Tigers]] (1896) and Lady Tigers teams also known as the ''Fighting Tigers'' named for the [[Louisiana Tigers]], several Confederate Civil War regiments.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.campusreform.org/?ID=9246|title=Petition: LSU Tigers mascot a 'symbol of white oppression'|date=May 31, 2017|website=campusreform.org|access-date=September 2, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.nola.com/lsu/index.ssf/2015/06/lsus_fighting_tigers_nickname.html|title=Confederate symbols need to go but don't touch LSU's Fighting Tigers nickname: Jeff Duncan|website=nola.com|date=June 26, 2015 |access-date=September 2, 2017}}</ref> * [[Saint Bernard, Louisiana|Saint Bernard]]: P.G.T. The school's mascot, [[Colonel Tillou]], is also named for Nicholls.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://splinternews.com/here-are-all-the-racist-college-mascots-left-in-the-uni-1793853276|title=Here are all the racist college mascots left in the United States|last=Rosen|first=Michael|date=December 1, 2015|work=Splinter|access-date=September 2, 2017|language=en-US}}</ref>
===Confederate flag display=== * [[Baton Rouge, Louisiana|Baton Rouge]]: The [[Flags of the Confederate States of America#First flag: the "Stars and Bars" (1861–1863)|Stars and Bars Confederate flag]] and the [[Bonnie Blue Flag]] are flown behind City Hall, along with the [[Flag of Louisiana]].<ref name=225magazine/>
==Maryland== [[File:LoudonParkCem.ConfedMemDay.2012.flags.20120602.jpg|thumb|right|'' The Confederate Soldier'', [[Loudon Park National Cemetery]], Baltimore]] {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Maryland}}
There are at least 7 confederate monuments on public land. They are generally in or near cemeteries.
As of December 27, 2022 there is one statue on a large stone of General Robert E. Lee at the Antietam battlefield, visible from the road. It was on private land adjacent to the park, and was donated with the land.
The "Talbot Boys" statue in Easton, Maryland was the last Confederate monument removed from public property on March 14, 2022.
===State symbols=== [[File:Flag of Maryland.svg|thumb|Flag of Maryland since 1904]] * [[Flag of Maryland]] (1904). The state flag of Maryland features the red-and-white Crossland Banner, the unofficial state flag of Maryland used by secessionists and Confederates during the American Civil War.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.washingtonian.com/2015/08/19/marylands-flag-confederate-symbol-crossland-banner-calvert-family/|title=Maryland's Flag Has a Subtle Symbol of Confederate Sympathy|last=Beaujon|first=Andrew|date=August 19, 2015|website=Washingtonian|access-date=August 25, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://ggwash.org/view/38356/marylands-flag-may-be-more-symbolic-than-you-realize|title=Maryland's flag may be more symbolic than you realize|last=Johnson|first=Matt|date=June 5, 2015|work=Greater Greater Washington|access-date=August 29, 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.baltimoresun.com/features/baltimore-insider-blog/bs-fe-maryland-flag-confederate-20170803-story.html|title=Activist draws attention to Maryland flag's Confederate ties during #NoConfederate campaign|last=Tkacik|first=Christina|date=August 3, 2017|work=[[The Baltimore Sun]]|access-date=August 29, 2017|language=en-US|archive-date=August 29, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170829204634/http://www.baltimoresun.com/features/baltimore-insider-blog/bs-fe-maryland-flag-confederate-20170803-story.html|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://baltimore.cbslocal.com/2017/08/02/maryland-flag-confederate-history/|title=Does Maryland's Flag Have Confederate Ties? Social Media Campaign Sparks Controversy|date=August 2, 2017|website=CBS Baltimore|access-date=August 29, 2017}}</ref> The current state flag started appearing after the Civil War as a form of reconciliation. The flag became official in 1904. * The former state song "[[Maryland, My Maryland]]" calls on the state to join the [[Confederate States of America|Confederacy]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/08/25/us/monuments-confederacy-remove-rename.html|title=Far From Dixie, Outcry Grows Over a Wider Array of Monuments|last=Gabriel|first=Trip|date=August 25, 2017|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=June 14, 2018|language=en}}</ref> Prior to 2021, the [[Maryland General Assembly]] voted nine times to repeal, replace, or alter the state song, all without any success. In 2017, the [[Mighty Sound of Maryland]], the marching band of the [[University of Maryland at College Park]], stopped playing the song.<ref>[https://www.afro.com/umd-band-stops-playing-pro-confederate-song/ "UMD Band Stops Playing pro-Confederate Song"], ''[[Baltimore Afro-American|Afro]]'', Baltimore, Associated Press, August 30, 2017. Retrieved on September 4, 2017.</ref> In March 2021, both houses of the Maryland General Assembly voted by substantial margins to abandon "Maryland, My Maryland" as the state song. On May 18, 2021, governor [[Larry Hogan]] signed the bill officially repealing the state song.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Maryland Repeals State Song That Called Lincoln A 'Tyrant'|url=https://www.npr.org/2021/05/20/983057655/maryland-repeals-state-song-that-called-lincoln-a-tyrant|access-date=June 20, 2021|website=NPR.org|date=May 20, 2021|language=en|last1=Neuman|first1=Scott}}</ref> Since then, Maryland has had no official state song.
===Monuments=== ====Public monuments==== * [[Baltimore]]: Confederate monuments at [[Loudon Park National Cemetery]] include: ** ''The Confederate Soldier'' (1874)<ref>{{cite web | title = Baltimore's Confederate Memory & Monuments | publisher = Baltimore's Civil Rights Heritage | url = https://baltimoreheritage.github.io/civil-rights-heritage/confederate-memory/ | access-date = October 14, 2017}}</ref> ** Fort McHenry Monument (1912), dedicated to Confederate soldiers who died at [[Fort McHenry]], when it was a prisoners-of-war camp.<ref>{{cite web | title = Loudon Park National Cemetery | publisher = U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs | date = August 28, 2017 | url = https://www.cem.va.gov/cems/nchp/loudonpark.asp}}</ref> * [[Scotland, St. Mary's County, Maryland|Scotland]] ([[St. Mary's County, Maryland|St. Mary's County]]): Confederate Soldiers and Sailors Monument (1876), and Point Lookout Confederate Cemetery Monument (1910), located at [[Point Lookout State Park|Point Lookout Confederate Cemetery]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Point Lookout Confederate Cemetery|publisher=U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs|date=August 28, 2017|url=https://www.cem.va.gov/cems/lots/point_lookout.asp}}</ref> As of October 2018, it is one of 7 cemeteries with Confederate monuments that the [[Veterans Administration]] has under 24-hour guard.<ref name="Veterans" /> * [[Washington County, Maryland|Washington County]]: ** Robert E. Lee Statue at Antietam Creek, [[Antietam National Battlefield]] (2003)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nps.gov/anti/learn/historyculture/mnt-lee.htm|title=Monument to Gen. Robert E. Lee|website=National Park Service|language=en|access-date=August 26, 2017}}</ref> The statue is attempting to be removed by legislation through H.R.970 (2019)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.congress.gov/bill/116th-congress/house-bill/970/text|title=H.R.970 – Robert E. Lee Statue Removal Act|website=US Congress|language=en|access-date=September 24, 2019}}</ref> and the National Park Service acknowledges the inaccuracies of the statue and educates those in the park accordingly. ** [[Samuel Garland, Jr.]] Monument (1993)<ref>{{cite web|title=Brigadier General Samuel Garland|publisher=Stone Sentinels|url=http://stonesentinels.com/less-known/south-mountain/brigadier-general-samuel-garland/|access-date=September 19, 2017}}</ref>
====Private monuments==== [[File:Monument to the Unknown Confederate Soldiers Mount Olivet Cemetery06262012.JPG|thumb|right|upright|Monument to the Unknown Confederate Soldiers, Frederick, Maryland]] * [[Beallsville, Maryland|Beallsville]]: Memorial to Confederate soldiers at [[Beallsville, Maryland#Monocacy Cemetery|Monocacy Cemetery]] (1911; replaced 1975).<ref>{{cite web|title=Monocacy Cemetery|publisher=Maryland Historical Trust|date=September 15, 2004|url=https://apps.mht.maryland.gov/medusa/PDF/Montgomery/M;%2017-1-1.pdf}}</ref> * [[Frederick, Maryland|Frederick]]: Monument to the Unknown Confederate Soldiers (1881), [[Mount Olivet Cemetery (Frederick)|Mount Olivet Cemetery]]<ref>{{cite book|last=Scharf|first=John Thomas|title=History of Western Maryland: Being a History of Frederick, Montgomery, Carroll, Washington, Allegany, and Garrett Counties from the Earliest Period to the Present Day, Including Biographical Sketches of Their Representative Men|publisher=Louis H. Everts|year=1882|url=http://www.ebooksread.com/authors-eng/j-thomas-john-thomas-scharf/history-of-western-maryland--being-a-history-of-frederick-montgomery-carroll-ahc.shtml|page=551|volume=1}}</ref> * [[Fox's Gap]], [[Frederick County, Maryland]]: North Carolina Monument (2003): The monument is a life sized bronze figure of a wounded Confederate color bearer on a base of black granite. It was created by sculptor [[Gary Casteel]] for the Living History association of Mecklinburg, North Carolina, and unveiled on October 18, 2003. It is dedicated to all the North Carolina troops who fought in the [[Battle of South Mountain]]. Fox's Gap is the southernmost battlefield of the Battle of South Mountain. The property is owned by the Central Maryland Heritage League, a battlefield protection group.<ref>{{cite web | title = North Carolina Monument on South Mountain | url = https://stonesentinels.com/less-known/south-mountain/north-carolina-monument/ |access-date = December 17, 2022}}</ref> [[File:North Carolina Memorial Fox's Gap.jpg|thumb|alt=North Carolina Memorial at Fox's Gap|North Carolina Memorial at Fox's Gap (2003)]] * [[White's Ferry]], [[Montgomery County, Maryland|Montgomery County]]: Confederate Monument, a granite pedestal. [[File:CSA Monument at White's Ferry.jpg|thumb|The base of the CSA monument moved from Rockville, MD, to White's Ferry, MD.]] :The original monument, a bronze life-sized Confederate soldier on this pedestal, was originally donated by the UDC and the United Confederate Veterans, and built by the Washington firm of Falvey Granite Company at a cost of {{US$|3600|1913}}. The artist is unknown.<ref name=Rockville/> The inscription says "To Our Heroes of Montgomery Co. Maryland That We Through Life May Not Forget to Love The Thin Gray Line / Erected A.D. 1913 / 1861 CSA 1865."<ref name=Landmarks/> because [[Uniforms of the Confederate States Armed Forces|Confederate uniforms]] are gray. The Rockville dedication was on June 3, 1913, Jefferson Davis's birthday,<ref name=Landmarks>{{cite web|title=The Confederate Monument in Rockville|date=March 26, 2017|first=Allen|last=Browne|url=http://allenbrowne.blogspot.com}}</ref> and was attended by 3,000 out of a county population of 30,000.<ref>{{cite news|title=Montgomery County decides to hide, instead of confront, its ugly history |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/montgomery-county-decides-to-hide-instead-of-confront-its-ugly-history/2017/03/16/e8bb9b18-08c0-11e7-93dc-00f9bdd74ed1_story.html|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|first=Eugene L.|last=Meyer|date=March 17, 2017}}</ref> It was originally located in a small triangular park<ref name=Turque>{{Cite news |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/md-politics/confederate-statue-moved-from-rockville-courthouse-over-the-weekend/2017/07/24/cc80fae4-70a1-11e7-9eac-d56bd5568db8_story.html?tid=hybrid_collaborative_1_na |title=Confederate statue moved from Rockville courthouse over the weekend |last=Turque |first=Bill |date=July 24, 2017 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |access-date=August 15, 2017|issn=0190-8286}}</ref> called Courthouse Square. In 1971, urban renewal led to the elimination of the Square, and the monument was moved to the east lawn of the Red Brick Courthouse (no longer in use as such), facing south.<ref>{{cite web|title=The History and Future of the Rockville Confederate Soldier Statue|date=July 16, 2015|first=Miriam|last=Bunow|publisher=Peerless Rockville Historic Preservation Ltd.|url=http://www.peerlessrockville.org/2015/07/16/the-history-and-future-of-the-rockville-confederate-soldier-statue/}}</ref> In 1994 it was cleaned and waxed by the Maryland Military Monuments Commission.<ref name=Rockville>{{cite web|title=Historic District Commission Staff Report: Certificate of Approval HDC2016-00756, 29 Courthouse Square|author=City of Rockville|date=September 10, 2015|url=http://rockvillemd.gov/DocumentCenter/View/12674/9-15_Item_IV-A_HDC2016-00756-Updaed|access-date=June 29, 2020|archive-date=October 2, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181002215302/http://rockvillemd.gov/DocumentCenter/View/12674/9-15_Item_IV-A_HDC2016-00756-Updaed|url-status=dead}}</ref> The monument was defaced with "[[Black Lives Matter]]" in 2015; a wooden box was built over it to protect it.<ref>{{cite news|title=Montgomery boxes Confederate statue to protect it from vandalism|first=Bill|last=Turque|date=August 3, 2015|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/md-politics/montgomery-boxes-confederate-statue-to-protect-it-from-vandalism/2015/08/03/a7306142-39fa-11e5-b3ac-8a79bc44e5e2_story.html}}</ref> The monument was removed in July 2017 from its original location outside the Old Rockville Court House to private land<ref name=Turque/> at [[White's Ferry]] in [[Dickerson, Maryland]].<ref>{{cite news|title=New spot for Confederate statue: site of historic ferry|first=Bill|last=Turque|date=February 28, 2017|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/montgomery-finds-spot-for-confederate-statue-site-of-historic-ferry/2017/02/28/1de4fc08-fdf4-11e6-8f41-ea6ed597e4ca_story.html}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|date=March 22, 2017|first=James W.|last=Loewen|title=ROCKVILLE'S CONFEDERATE MONUMENT BELONGS AT WHITE'S FERRY|newspaper=[[History News Network]]|url=https://historynewsnetwork.org/blog/153908}}</ref> The statue was removed from the pedestal in June 2020, but the pedestal urging people to "Love The Thin Gray Line" remains.
===Inhabited places=== * Confederate Hills: A residential community near [[Antietam National Battlefield]] where the streets are all named after Confederate generals, including: [[James Longstreet|Longstreet Circle]], [[Charles D. Anderson|General Anderson Court]], [[Lawrence O'Bryan Branch|General Branch Court]], [[John Brown Gordon|General Gordon Circle]], [[A. P. Hill|General Hill Circle]], [[Robert E. Lee|General Lee Drive]], [[Stonewall Jackson|General Stonewall Jackson Circle]], and [[J. E. B. Stuart|General Stuart Court]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Saving History from Sprawl: Threats to Civil War Battlefields | publisher = Kaid Benfield Archive | date = December 4, 2008 | url = http://kaidbenfieldarchive.com/20081204-saving-history-from-sprawl-threats-to-civil-war-battlef.html}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | title = Road Listings | publisher = Washington County, Maryland, Planning Department | date = May 27, 1994 | url = http://msa.maryland.gov/megafile/msa/stagser/s1800/s1876/000000/000024/pdf/msa_s1876_000024.pdf}}</ref>
===Roads=== * [[Potomac, Maryland|Potomac]]: ** [[Jubal Early]] Court ** [[J. E. B. Stuart]] Road
===Ferry=== [[File:Gen. Jubal A. Early Ferry.jpg|thumb|right|''Gen. Jubal A. Early'']] [[File:WhitesFerryBoat - renamed.jpg|thumb|right|The renamed White's Ferry ferryboat]] *[[Montgomery County, Maryland|Montgomery County]]: A passenger and vehicle ferry, formerly named ''Gen. Jubal A. Early'' (1954), connected [[Montgomery County, Maryland]], and [[Loudoun County, Virginia]]. Owned by [[White's Ferry]], it was named for Confederate General [[Jubal Early]] until June 2020 when it was renamed Historic White's Ferry.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Tan|first=Rebecca|date=July 4, 2020|title=A Confederate statue is toppled in rural Maryland, then quietly stored away|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/whites-ferry-confederate-statue/2020/07/04/e717b18e-bb3c-11ea-bdaf-a129f921026f_story.html|access-date=January 5, 2021|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]}}</ref> White's Ferry was the only ferry still in operation on the [[Potomac River]] until it ceased operations in Dec 2020.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Beecher|first=Katie|date=December 30, 2020|title=White's Ferry Ceases Operations Across the Potomac|url=https://www.montgomerymag.com/2020/12/30/whites-ferry-ceases-operations-across-the-potomac/|access-date=January 5, 2021|website=Montgomery Magazine}}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last1 = Campbell | first1 = Douglas E.| last2 = Sherman | first2 = Thomas B. | title = On the Potomac River | publisher = Lulu | year = 2014 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=Z4URBwAAQBAJ&pg=PP1|isbn=978-1304698728| page = 48}}</ref>

VIEW TO NORTHEAST. - Point Lookout Confederate Cemetery, Point Lookout, Ridge, St. HALS MD-7-2.tif|Point Lookout Confederate Cemetery Monument (center) and Confederate Soldiers and Sailors Monument Monument to the falling of Samuel Garland.jpg|[[Samuel Garland, Jr.]] Monument Confederate_Memorial,_Silver_Spring,_MD.jpg|Confederate Memorial in [[Silver Spring, Maryland|Silver Spring]] to 17 unknown soldiers buried at Grace Episcopal Church Cemetery after the [[Battle of Fort Stevens]] </gallery>
==Massachusetts== {{As of|May 2019}}, all public memorials had been removed.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.mvtimes.com/2019/07/15/civil-war-plaques-display/|title=Civil War plaques are on display|first=Brian|last=Dowd|date=July 15, 2019}}</ref>
===Private memorials=== * [[Cambridge, Massachusetts|Cambridge]] ** Memorial Hall, [[Harvard University]]. Stained-glass windows to commemorate various figures, among them: *** Honor and Peace Window (1900). There is no inscription, but a Harvard University page ([http://www.fas.harvard.edu/~memhall/staingls.html Memorial Hall]) explaining the windows says: "This window commemorates those who surrendered their lives in the War of the Rebellion." Portrays two warriors, one with sword high in triumph, one kneeling in defeat, who from the ribbons can be seen to be from different but related countries. *** Student and Soldier Window (1889). Soldier wears gray uniform.
==Michigan== {{As of|2020|06|29|df=US}}, there is at least one known public monument of a confederate soldier in Michigan. It is located in [[Allendale, Michigan]], a town in Ottawa County. A part of the [[Allendale Charter Township, Michigan#Parks|Veterans Garden of Honor]] (1998) which features nine life sized statues of soldiers from various wars, the statue in question depicts a union soldier and a confederate soldier back to back with a young slave at their feet holding a plaque reading "Freedom to Slaves," and the date January 5, 1863.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Fisher |first1=Jada |title=Confederate soldier statue likely to stay in West Michigan veterans display |url=https://www.mlive.com/news/grand-rapids/2020/06/confederate-soldier-statue-likely-to-stay-in-west-michigan-veterans-display.html |work=[[MLive]] |date=June 23, 2020 |language=en}}</ref>
==Minnesota== [[Murray County Central School District|Murray County Central High School]] in Slayton, and United South Central High School in Wells both use a Rebel mascot and the nickname "Rebels."<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.pioneer.org/blogs/compass-stories/examining-rebel-mascots/ | title=Examining Rebel Mascots }}</ref>
==Mississippi== {{Main|List of Confederate monuments and memorials in Mississippi}} {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 147 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Mississippi]].
==Missouri== {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Missouri}} {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there were at least 19 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Missouri]].
===Monuments===
====Courthouse monuments==== [[File:Atchison-statue.jpg|thumb|upright|Statue of David Rice Atchison in front of the [[Clinton County, Missouri|Clinton County]] Courthouse, Plattsburg, Missouri]] * [[Cape Girardeau, Missouri|Cape Girardeau]]: [[Confederate War Memorial]] (1931) * [[Columbia, Missouri|Columbia]]: Confederate Monument (1935). Relocated to the [[Centralia, Missouri|Centralia]] Battlefield in September 2015. * [[Huntsville, Missouri|Huntsville]]: Captain Delaney S. Washburn Memorial (1976). * [[Neosho, Missouri|Neosho]]: Confederate Captain of Missouri Monument. * [[Palmyra, Missouri|Palmyra]]: [[Palmyra massacre|Palmyra Massacre]] Monument (1907). * [[Plattsburg, Missouri|Plattsburg]]: Statute of CSA Brig Gen [[David Rice Atchison]] who was later a US Senator
====Other public monuments==== [[File:Forest-hill-kc.jpg|thumb|right|UDC monument at Forest Hill and Calvary Cemetery, Kansas City, Missouri]] [[File:Union Confederate Monument, Union Cemetery, Kansas City, Missouri.jpg|thumb|right|[[Union Confederate Monument]], [[Union Cemetery (Kansas City, Missouri)|Union Cemetery]], [[Kansas City, Missouri]]]] * [[Brookline, Missouri|Brookline]]: [[Missouri State Guard]] Monument (2001) * [[Centralia, Missouri|Centralia]]: Boone County Confederate Memorial (1935) * [[Dexter, Missouri|Dexter]]: Frenchman's Spring Monument (1996). A natural gathering place for soldiers. In July 1861, 2,000 soldiers from 15 southeast Missouri counties met to organize as the First Division, Missouri State Guard – the pro-Confederate state [[Militia (United States)|militia]], known as the Swamp Fox Brigade. * [[Higginsville, Missouri|Higginsville]]: [[Confederate Memorial State Historic Site]]; also called "''Lion of Lucerne''" (1906) * [[Kansas City metropolitan area|Kansas City]]: **UDC monument at Forest Hill and Calvary Cemetery to CSA soldiers killed at the [[Battle of Westport]]. Buried nearby is CSA General [[Joseph O. Shelby]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Shelby's Last Stand | publisher = HMdb.org | url = https://www.hmdb.org/marker.asp?marker=26692 | access-date = October 8, 2018}}</ref> **[[Union Confederate Monument]], [[Union Cemetery (Kansas City, Missouri)|Union Cemetery]] (1911) **Monument to the "Loyal Women of the Old South".<ref name="news-leader.com">{{Cite web|url=https://www.news-leader.com/story/news/politics/2017/08/17/no-apparent-plans-yet-remove-confederate-monuments-springfield-and-missouri/572212001/|title=No apparent plans yet to remove Confederate monuments in Springfield and Missouri|first=Will|last=Schmitt|website=Springfield News-Leader}}</ref> The memorial to Confederate women, a 1934 gift by the United Daughters of the Confederacy, was defaced by graffiti on August 18, 2017, and boxed up two days later in preparation for its removal. The monument was removed on August 25, 2017.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Keegan |first1=Charlie |title=Watch: City crews remove Confederate monument on Ward Parkway |url=https://www.kshb.com/news/local-news/crews-to-remove-confederate-monument-on-ward-parkway |website=KSHB |date=August 25, 2017 |access-date=September 6, 2020}}</ref> * [[Keytesville, Missouri|Keytesville]]: Sterling Price Monument, [[Price Park]] (1915) * [[Lone Jack, Missouri|Lone Jack]]: Cemetery monument to Confederate soldiers who died in the [[Battle of Lone Jack]]<ref>{{cite web | title = Lone Jack Battlefield Museum and Soldier's Cemetery | publisher = Lone Jack Historical Society | url = http://www.historiclonejack.org/museum.html | access-date = November 16, 2016 | archive-date = October 22, 2016 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20161022114600/http://historiclonejack.org/museum.html | url-status = dead }}</ref> * [[Marshall, Missouri|Marshall]]: Confederate Monument of Saline (1901) * [[Neosho, Missouri|Neosho]]: Considered the state capital by Confederates, has two memorials<ref name="news-leader.com"/> **Marker on court house lawn states that Missouri separated from the Union<ref name="news-leader.com"/> **Memorial at Oddfellows Cemetery (1904) near a mass grave<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.neoshodailynews.com/article/20150412/NEWS/150419749%3ftemplate=ampart|title=Neosho Daily News – Neosho, MO|first=Wes|last=Franklin|website=www.neoshodailynews.com|access-date=August 18, 2023|archive-date=August 10, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200810015936/https://www.neoshodailynews.com/article/20150412/NEWS/150419749%3Ftemplate=ampart|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Roanoke, Missouri|Roanoke]]: Shelby's Homecoming Monument (1997) * [[Springfield, Missouri|Springfield]]: Two monuments are located at [[Springfield National Cemetery]]: the Monument to Confederate Soldiers of Missouri and General [[Sterling Price]]<ref name="news-leader.com"/> (1901), and a UDC granite marker to the unknown Confederate dead at the [[Battle of Wilson's Creek]] (1958).<ref>{{cite web | title = Springfield National Cemetery – Springfield, Missouri | publisher = National Park Service | url = https://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/missouri/springfield_national_cemetery.html | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20151202070828/http://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/missouri/springfield_national_cemetery.html | url-status = dead | archive-date = December 2, 2015 | access-date = November 16, 2017}}</ref> In late August 2017, someone threw paint on it; as of October 2018, it is one of 7 cemeteries with Confederate monuments that the [[Veterans Administration]] has under 24-hour guard.<ref name=Veterans>{{cite news |title=Feds spending millions to beef up security at Confederate monuments, cemeteries |agency=[[Associated Press]] |date=October 15, 2018 |newspaper=[[NBC News]] |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/feds-spending-millions-beef-security-confederate-monuments-cemeteries-n920316}}</ref> * [[St. Louis]]: ** [[Jefferson Barracks National Cemetery#Memorial to the Confederate Dead|Memorial to the Confederate Dead]], [[Jefferson Barracks National Cemetery]] (1988)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/missouri/jefferson_barracks_national_cemetery.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151212031723/http://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/Missouri/Jefferson_Barracks_National_Cemetery.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=December 12, 2015|title=Jefferson Barracks National Cemetery, Saint Louis, Missouri|website=National Park Service|access-date=August 25, 2017}}</ref> * [[Waverly, Missouri|Waverly]]: [[Joseph O. Shelby|CSA Gen. Joseph O. Shelby]] Statue (2009). * [[West Alton, MO|West Alton]]: Confederate Memorial, Lincoln Shields Recreation Area (1909).<ref name="Alton">[http://www.greatriverroad.com/Cities/Alton/Confederate.htm Visitors Guide to the Confederate Prison Site & Confederate Memorials Alton, Illinois] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150626114118/http://www.greatriverroad.com/Cities/Alton/Confederate.htm |date=June 26, 2015 }}, Visitors Guide to the Middle Mississippi River Valley, accessed June 25, 2015</ref>

Breckinridge]], although the name was misspelled.<ref>{{cite book|author=Bill Earngey|title=Missouri Roadsides: The Traveler's Companion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xcxWsmxRzVEC&pg=PA109|isbn=978-0826210210|year=1995|publisher=University of Missouri Press }}</ref>

(1915) * [[Higginsville, Missouri|Higginsville]]: Confederate Memorial State Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://mostateparks.com/park/confederate-memorial-state-historic-site|title=Confederate Memorial State Historic Site|date=December 10, 2010|website=mostateparks.com}}</ref>

===Schools=== * [[Southland C-9 School District]]: Rebelman mascot, school colors red, gray, and white.
==Montana== {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Montana}} {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 2 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Montana]].
* [[Beaverhead County, Montana|Beaverhead]]: Confederate Dam<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.mytopo.com/locations/index.cfm?fid=798737|title=Confederate Dam, Beaverhead County, Montana, Dam [Everson Creek USGS Topographic Map] by MyTopo|website=www.mytopo.com|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.anyplaceamerica.com/directory/mt/beaverhead-county-30001/dams/confederate-dam-798737/|title=Confederate Dam Topo Map in Beaverhead County, Montana|website=www.anyplaceamerica.com|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref> * Beaverhead: Jeff Davis Creek<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.anyplaceamerica.com/directory/mt/beaverhead-county-30001/streams/jeff-davis-creek-785432/|title=Jeff Davis Creek Topo Map in Beaverhead County, Montana|website=www.anyplaceamerica.com|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref> * [[Confederate Gulch and Diamond City|Confederate Gulch]]: historic gold mining camp founded in 1864 by Confederate soldiers, including parolees.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=08rAI9NEbcYC&q=beaverhead+confederate+dam&pg=PA38|title=Montana Place Names from Alzada to Zortman|last1=Aarstad|first1=Rich|last2=Arguimbau|first2=Ellie|last3=Baumler|first3=Ellen|last4=Porsild|first4=Charlene|last5=Shovers|first5=Brian|publisher=Montana Historical Society|year=2009|isbn=978-0975919613|page=53|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=http://helenair.com/confederate-gulch/article_6ee6aeb6-2674-11e5-8bda-bbf3c8a903e0.html|title=Confederate Gulch|newspaper=Independent Record|date=July 9, 2015|access-date=August 25, 2017}}</ref>
==Nevada== {{As of|2020|06|24|df=US}}, there is at least one public space with Confederate monuments in [[Nevada]]. * [[Jeff Davis Peak]] in the [[Snake Range]] mountains of [[White Pine County, Nevada|White Pine County]] was named in 1855 after [[Jefferson Davis]]. The name was replaced after the George Floyd Protests.
==New Jersey== [[File:Confederate Monument, Finn's Point National Cemetery, NJ.jpg|thumb|upright|Confederate Monument (1910), Finn's Point National Cemetery.]] There are at least two public spaces dedicated to the Confederacy in [[New Jersey]]. * [[Pennsville Township, New Jersey|Pennsville Township]]: Confederate Monument (1910), [[Finn's Point National Cemetery]]. Commemorates the 2,436 Confederate prisoners-of-war who died at [[Fort Delaware]].<ref>Melanie Burney, [http://www.philly.com/philly/news/new_jersey/for-this-son-of-both-confederate-and-union-soldiers-its-only-history-20170829.html "Honoring Union and Confederacy,"] ''The Philadelphia Inquirer'', August 29, 2017.</ref> As of October 2018, it is one of 7 cemeteries with Confederate monuments that the [[Veterans Administration]] has under 24-hour guard.<ref name=Veterans/> *[[Princeton, New Jersey|Princeton]]: Princeton’s Civil War Memorial (1920s), [[Nassau Hall]] at [[Princeton University]]. Commemorates 70 alumni who died in the war, including 34 who fought for the Confederacy.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.nj.com/salem/2017/08/confederate_symbols_in_new_jersey.html | title=9 surprising N.J. Ties to Confederacy you probably didn't know | date=August 16, 2017 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://slavery.princeton.edu/stories/civil-war-memorial | title=Princeton's Civil War Memorial }}</ref>
==New Mexico== {{As of|2020|06|24|df=US}}, there is at least one public space with Confederate monuments in [[New Mexico]].
* [[Santa Fe, New Mexico|Santa Fe]]: Confederate memorial (1993), [[Santa Fe National Cemetery]]. Granite and bronze memorial dedicated to 31 Confederate soldiers discovered in shallow graves in 1987 at [[Glorieta Pass Battlefield]], then re-interred at Santa Fe National Cemetery.<ref>{{cite web | title = Santa Fe National Cemetery | publisher = U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs | date = August 28, 2017 | url = https://www.cem.va.gov/cems/nchp/santafe.asp}}</ref><ref>{{cite news | last = Haederle | first = Michael | date = May 17, 1993 | title = Putting to Rest New Mexico's Civil War Past : Confederate Soldiers are Reinterred. They Died for a Dream of Seizing the Denver Mint's Gold | url = https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1993-05-17-mn-36413-story.html | work = Los Angeles Times}}</ref> * [[Socorro, New Mexico|Socorro]]: Confederate monument (2012) entitled ''Victory Awaits You''<ref>{{cite web | title = For Southern Independence | publisher = HMdb.org | url = https://www.hmdb.org/marker.asp?marker=64055 | access-date = October 8, 2018}}</ref>
==New York== [[File:Confederate monument Elmira NY.jpg|thumb|upright|Confederate Monument, [[Woodlawn National Cemetery]], Elmira, New York]] {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 3 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[New York (state)|New York]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ny.curbed.com/2017/8/16/16152376/confederate-street-names-nyc-brooklyn|title=New York pols push to remove Confederate street names in Brooklyn (updated)|last=Plitt|first=Amy|date=August 16, 2017|website=Curbed NY|access-date=August 17, 2017|quote=Memorializing Confederate generals has no place in 2017}}</ref>
===Monuments===
==== Public monuments ====
* [[The Bronx]]: Busts of Stonewall Jackson and Robert E. Lee were in the [[Hall of Fame for Great Americans]] at [[Bronx Community College]]. The college removed the busts in 2020.<ref name=":13" /><ref name="After" /> * [[Central Park]]: [[J. Marion Sims]]. In November 2017, the cover of Harper's Magazine featured [[J. C. Hallman]]'s article "Monumental Error," about the Central Park monument of controversial surgeon – and Confederate spy – J. Marion Sims.<ref>{{Cite magazine|date=November 1, 2017|title=[Essay] {{!}} Monumental Error, by J. C. Hallman|url=https://harpers.org/archive/2017/11/monumental-error/|access-date=September 2, 2020|magazine=Harper's Magazine|volume=November 2017 |language=en|last1=Hallman |first1=J. C. }}</ref> The timing coincided with the work New York City Mayor [[Bill de Blasio|Bill De Blasio]]'s committee on monuments, and Hallman's article was distributed to members of New York's Public Design Commission. The commission voted unanimously to remove Sims's statue, and it was removed in April 2018.<ref>{{Cite web|date=November 11, 2019|title=The Cry of Alice {{!}} J.C. Hallman|url=https://thebaffler.com/latest/the-cry-of-alice-hallman|access-date=September 2, 2020|website=The Baffler|language=en-US}}</ref> Hallman has since written articles about Sims's statue in Montgomery, Alabama, and is working on a book, ''The Anarcha Quest'', about Sims and his so-called "first cure," ''Anarcha Westcott''.<ref>{{Cite web|date=April 21, 2018|title=J Marion Sims: controversial statue taken down but debate still rages|url=http://www.theguardian.com/artanddesign/2018/apr/21/j-marion-sims-statue-removed-new-york-city-black-women|access-date=September 2, 2020|website=[[The Guardian]]|language=en}}</ref>
====Private monuments==== * [[Brooklyn]]: A tree at St. John's Episcopal Church bears a plaque, installed by UDC in 1912, reading "This tree was planted by CSA Gen. Robert Edward Lee, while stationed at Fort Hamilton."<ref>{{cite magazine|url=https://newyorker.com/magazine/2017/06/19/a-confederate-general-in-brooklyn|title=A Confederate General in Brooklyn|last=Sullivan|first=Robert|magazine=The New Yorker|date=June 19, 2017|access-date=August 25, 2017}}</ref> The plaque was removed in 2017.<ref name=":13">{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2017/08/16/us/confederate-monuments-removed.html?action=click&contentCollection=U.S.&module=RelatedCoverage&region=EndOfArticle&pgtype=article|title=Confederate Monuments Are Coming Down Across the United States. Here's a List|author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.-->|date=August 22, 2017|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=August 25, 2017}}</ref> * [[Elmira, New York|Elmira]]: UDC monument (1937) at [[Woodlawn National Cemetery]], dedicated to Confederate soldiers who died in [[Elmira Prison]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Woodlawn National Cemetery, Elmira, New York | publisher = National Park Service | url = https://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/new_york/Woodlawn_National_Cemetery.html | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20150627074737/http://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/New_York/Woodlawn_National_Cemetery.html | url-status = dead | archive-date = June 27, 2015 | access-date = October 9, 2017}}</ref> As of October 2018, it is one of 7 cemeteries with Confederate monuments that the [[Veterans Administration]] has under 24-hour guard.<ref name=Veterans/> * [[Hastings-on-Hudson]]: Confederate marker at Mount Hope Cemetery<ref>{{cite news|title=Confederate marker in Hastings draws concern|last=McKinney|first=Michael P.|date=August 19, 2017|newspaper=The Journal News|page=1}}</ref>
===Roads=== * [[Fort Hamilton]], [[Brooklyn]]: ** General Lee Avenue. The avenue was renamed to John Warren Avenue in 2022, to honor a 22-year-old lieutenant in the Army who was killed in the Vietnam War in January 1969.<ref>{{cite web |date=2022-06-02 |title=Fort Hamilton avenue named for Confederate General Robert E. Lee renamed for Black Vietnam War hero John Warren |url=https://abc7ny.com/fort-hamilton-general-lee-avenue-john-warren-who-was/11873644/ |access-date=2022-06-20 |website= ABC7 New York |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220602080357/https://abc7ny.com/fort-hamilton-general-lee-avenue-john-warren-who-was/11873644/ |archive-date=2 June 2022 |url-status=dead}}</ref> ** Stonewall Jackson Drive. The road was later renamed to Washington Road in 2022, shortly after the renaming of General Lee Avenue. :Governor [[Andrew Cuomo]] had twice requested the Army, unsuccessfully, to have these streets renamed.<ref name="After" /> * [[Throggs Neck|Throggs Neck, The Bronx]]: Longstreet Avenue, named for CSA Gen. James Longstreet<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/new-york/bronx/battle-gettysburg-heroes-give-names-streets-bronx-article-1.397481|title=Gettysburg addresses run thru city|last=O'Shaugnessy|first=Patrice|date=August 24, 2009|work=[[New York Daily News]]|access-date=August 28, 2017|language=en}}</ref>
==North Carolina==
{{main|List of Confederate monuments and memorials in North Carolina}}
{{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 164 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[North Carolina]].
==Ohio== {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Ohio}} {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 5 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Ohio]].
===Historical marker=== * In 2013, the state of Ohio erected 32 historical markers marking the [[John Hunt Morgan]] Heritage Trail.<ref>{{cite web |title=John Hunt Morgan Heritage Trail in Ohio Historical Markers |publisher=Historical Markers Database |url=https://www.hmdb.org/results.asp?SeriesID=364}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Ohio's new John Hunt Morgan Heritage Trail follows Confederate army's push across a panicked state (video) |date=July 6, 2018 |url=https://www.cleveland.com/travel/index.ssf/2013/07/ohios_new_john_hunt_morgan_her.html |newspaper=[[Cleveland Plain Dealer]]}}</ref>
===Monuments=== [[File:Camp chase 2.jpg|right|thumb|[[Confederate Soldier Memorial (Columbus, Ohio)|Confederate Soldier Memorial]], Camp Chase, Columbus]] [[File:P5240017 Johnsons Island Conf Cemetery.jpg|thumb|right|''The Lookout'' (1910), [[Johnson's Island]], Ottawa County<ref name="CSC">{{cite web | title = Confederate Stockade Cemetery | publisher = U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs | date = August 28, 2017 | url = https://www.cem.va.gov/cems/lots/confederate_stockade.asp}}</ref>]] * [[Columbus, Ohio|Columbus]]: [[Camp Chase]] Cemetery's [[Confederate Soldier Memorial (Columbus, Ohio)|Confederate Soldier Memorial]]. Dedicated in 1902. Statue on top was toppled and decapitated by vandals in August 2017. The vandals took the head.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://twitter.com/i/web/status/899974698964058112 |title=A confederate statue was knocked down at Camp Chase Cemetery in west Columbus. Officers are investigating.pic.twitter.com/e5Nm4sMhxf |author=[[WSYX|WSYX ABC]] |date=August 22, 2017 |access-date=August 26, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/ohio/camp_chase_confederate_cemetery.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150303080027/http://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/national_cemeteries/Ohio/Camp_Chase_Confederate_Cemetery.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=March 3, 2015|title=Camp Chase Confederate Cemetery – Civil War Era National Cemeteries: A Discover Our Shared Heritage Travel Itinerary|website=www.nps.gov|access-date=August 26, 2017}}</ref> The Veterans Administration stated that the statue will be repaired.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.dispatch.com/news/20170822/vandals-decapitate-confederate-soldier-statue-at-camp-chase-cemetery|title=Vandals decapitate Confederate soldier statue at Camp Chase cemetery|first=Beth|last=Burger|newspaper=[[Columbus Dispatch]]|access-date=September 1, 2017}}</ref> As of October 2018, it is one of 7 cemeteries with Confederate monuments that the [[Veterans Administration]] has under 24-hour guard.<ref name=Veterans/> The statue was repaired by McKay Lodge Art Conservation Laboratory in [[Oberlin, Ohio|Oberlin]] and reinstalled on March 30, 2019.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.dispatch.com/news/20190501/repaired-statue-of-confederate-soldier-reinstalled-at-camp-chase-cemetery |title=Repaired statue of Confederate soldier reinstalled at Camp Chase cemetery|first=Mark|last=Ferenchik|website=The Columbus Dispatch}}</ref> * [[Pomeroy, Ohio|Pomeroy]]: Statue of Confederate general [[John Hunt Morgan]] * [[Sandusky Bay]]: Four UDC monuments are located at Confederate Stockade Cemetery on [[Johnson's Island]], the first facility built by the Union Army solely for imprisoning Confederate soldiers<ref name="CSC"/> As of October 2018, it is one of 7 cemeteries with Confederate monuments that the [[Veterans Administration]] has under 24-hour guard.<ref name=Veterans/> * [[West Point, Columbiana County, Ohio|West Point]]: Marker for [[Battle of Salineville]], the northernmost point Confederate forces reached.
=== Inhabited places ===
* Confederate Hills, a neighborhood in [[Batavia Township, Ohio|Batavia Township]] named for the Confederate cause that is home to roads named for a CSA leader and various southern locations, notably [[Stanton Hall]] and the [[Natchez Trace]].
===Roads=== * Batavia Township: **Stonewall Ridge, memorializing CSA Gen. [[Stonewall Jackson]]. *[[Day Heights, Ohio|Day Heights]]: **Colonel Mosby Drive, memorializing CSA Col. [[John S. Mosby]]. *[[Fairfield, Ohio|Fairfield]]: **Robert E Lee Drive, memorializing CSA Gen. Robert E. Lee. **Stonewall Lane, memorializing CSA Gen. Stonewall Jackson. *[[Mount Repose, Ohio|Mt. Repose]]: **Beauregard Court, memorializing CSA Gen. [[P. G. T. Beauregard]]. **Jeb Stuart Drive, memorializing CSA Gen. [[J. E. B. Stuart]]. **Monassas Run Road, memorializing the CSA victory at the battle at Manassas, known to the North as Bull Run. **Stonewall Jackson Drive, memorializing CSA Gen. Stonewall Jackson.
===Schools=== * [[Cleveland]]: John Adams High School uses the Rebels team name, but the mascot more closely resembles a [[cavalier]] than a Confederate soldier.<ref name=":25">{{Cite news|url=http://articles.cleveland.com/metro/index.ssf/2017/08/willoughby_south_high_school_d.amp|title=Willoughby South High School drops rebel mascot, keeps name|last=Bamforth|first=Emily|date=August 17, 2017|work=cleveland.com|access-date=September 5, 2017|language=en}}</ref> * [[McConnelsville, Ohio|Mcconnelsville]]: [[Morgan High School (McConnelsville, Ohio)|Morgan High School]] is named for Confederate General [[John Hunt Morgan]].{{citation needed|date=May 2024}} Their nickname is the "Raiders". * [[Willoughby, Ohio|Willoughby]]: Willoughby South High School dropped its Confederate uniformed mascot and removed all remaining Confederate imagery from the school while retaining the Rebels team name and school colors grey and blue. In 1993 the school dropped ''Stars and Bars'' as the school song and removed Confederate imagery from school uniforms.<ref name=":25" />
==Oklahoma== {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 13 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Oklahoma]].
===Buildings=== * [[Ardmore, Oklahoma|Ardmore]]: Oklahoma Confederate Home, operated as OK Confederate Home from 1911 to 1942. Renamed Oklahoma Veterans Center after last residing confederate veteran passed.<ref>{{cite web|last=Schulze|first=Bruce|title=Oklahoma Veterans Center|publisher=Civil War Album|url=http://www.civilwaralbum.com/indian/ardmore_vet1.htm|access-date=September 8, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170908155451/http://www.civilwaralbum.com/indian/ardmore_vet1.htm|archive-date=September 8, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="Lees">{{cite web|last=Lees|first=William B.|title=Oklahoma's Civil War Monuments and Memorial Landscapes|publisher=William B. Lees|date=2004|url=http://uwf.edu/wlees/Monuments.pdf|access-date=September 8, 2017|archive-date=June 14, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160614235541/http://uwf.edu/wlees/monuments.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref>
===Monuments=== [[File:Polson Cemetery, Historical Marker for Stand Watie (Degataga Oo-Watee).JPG|thumb|upright|[[Stand Watie]] Monument, Polson Cemetery, [[Delaware County, Oklahoma|Delaware County]]]] [[File:GENERAL VIEW OF FRONT ELEVATION WITH OBELISK MONUMENT IN FOREGROUND, FROM WEST - Cherokee National Capitol Building, 101-29 South Muskogee Avenue, Tahlequah, Cherokee County, OK HABS OKLA,11-TAHL,2-2.tif|thumb|upright|Confederate Monument at [[Cherokee National Capitol]]]] * [[Ardmore, Oklahoma|Ardmore]]: ** Confederate Monument (1910) at Oklahoma Veterans Center (formerly Oklahoma Confederate Home) has pair (CSA and Union) monuments.<ref name="Lees"/>{{rp|14}} ** Confederate Monument (2013) at Rose Hill Cemetery<ref>{{cite news|last=Kerr|first=Kevin|date=June 23, 2013|title=Monument to Confederate Soldiers Dedicated Saturday|url=http://www.ardmoreite.com/article/20130623/NEWS/130629911|work=Daily Ardmoreite}}</ref> * [[Atoka, Oklahoma|Atoka]]: Two monuments are located at the Confederate Cemetery for soldiers killed in the [[Battle of Middle Boggy]]<ref name="Lees"/><ref>{{Cite GNIS|1091611|Confederate Cemetery}}</ref> * [[Delaware County, Oklahoma|Delaware County]]: Two monuments to CSA General [[Stand Watie]] at Polson Cemetery; the first erected c. 1910–20, and the second erected in 1971<ref name="Lees"/> * [[Durant, Oklahoma|Durant]]: ** Confederate Soldier Statue at Bryan County Courthouse, erected in 1917 by UDC. Dedicated in 1918.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/2017/05/22/confederate-monuments-new-orleans-charlottesville-removal-race-civil-war/101870418/|title=Confederate monuments, more than 700 across USA, aren't budging|last=Hampson|first=Rick|date=May 22, 2017|work=[[USA Today]]|access-date=August 15, 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.civilwaralbum.com/indian/durant1.htm|title=Confederate Soldiers Monument, Durant, Oklahoma|website=Civilwaralbum.com|access-date=August 15, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170815101046/http://www.civilwaralbum.com/indian/durant1.htm|archive-date=August 15, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> ** Confederate Monument at Highland Cemetery<ref name="Lees"/> * [[Hugo, Oklahoma|Hugo]]: Rose Hill * [[Mayes County, Oklahoma|Mayes County]]: Confederate Monument at site of [[Second Battle of Cabin Creek]] (1961)<ref name="Lees"/> * [[Oklahoma City]]: Confederate Monument in Fairlawn Cemetery (1923)<ref name="Lees"/> * [[Oktaha, Oklahoma|Oktaha]]: Memorial in Oktaha Cemetery to Confederate soldiers who died in the [[Battle of Honey Springs]] (1940)<ref name="Lees"/> * [[Rentiesville, Oklahoma|Rentiesville]]: ** Confederate Soldiers Monument (1983) in [[Battle of Honey Springs|Honey Springs Battlefield]] by UDC and Children of the Confederacy.<ref name="Lees"/> ** Texas Confederates Memorial in Honey Springs Battlefield by UDC<ref name="Lees"/> * [[Tahlequah, Oklahoma|Tahlequah]]: ** Confederate Monument at [[Cherokee National Capitol]] (1913).<ref name="Lees"/> Removed in 2020.<ref name="Time for a change">{{cite news |date=June 13, 2020 |title='Time for a change:' Cherokee Nation removes monuments dedicated to Confederate soldiers |url=https://www.tulsaworld.com/news/time-for-a-change-cherokee-nation-removes-monuments-dedicated-to-confederate-soldiers/article_901f5b5f-7bc8-5a51-9220-279a7046a5e2.html |work=Tulsa World |access-date=June 15, 2020}}</ref> ** [[Stand Watie]] Monument (1971).<ref name="Lees"/> Removed in 2020.<ref name="Time for a change"/> * [[Wynnewood, Oklahoma|Wynnewood]]: Confederate Soldier monument (2004)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.thelostogle.com/2017/08/16/4-confederate-monuments-in-oklahoma-that-should-be-torn-down/|title=5 Oklahoma Confederate monuments that must be torn down…|last=Lucas|date=August 16, 2017|website=The Lost Ogle|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref>
===Schools=== [[File:Robert E. Lee School, Durant, OK.jpg|thumb|right|Robert E. Lee School in [[Durant, Oklahoma]]]] * [[Durant, Oklahoma|Durant]]: Robert E. Lee Elementary School<ref>{{cite book|last=Sprague|first=Donovin Arleigh|title=Durant|publisher=Arcadia|year=2013|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=CZYrBqi8cdgC&pg=PP1|page=65|isbn=978-0738590981}}</ref> * [[Oklahoma City]]: school board studying renaming in 2017 ** Robert E. Lee Elementary School (1910)<ref name=":5">{{Cite news|url=http://newsok.com/article/5560216|title=OKC school district responds to violent racial protests|last=Willert|first=Tim|date=August 15, 2017|work=NewsOK.com|access-date=August 17, 2017|language=en-US}}</ref> -now Adelaide Lee Elementary School. ** Jackson Elementary School (1910)<ref name=":5" /> -now Mary Golda Ross Enterprise Elementary School. ** Wheeler Elementary School (1910)<ref name=":5" /> ** [[Stand Watie]] Elementary School (1930)<ref name=":5" /> -now Esperanza Elementary School. * [[Pauls Valley, Oklahoma|Pauls Valley]]: Lee Elementary School
===Inhabited places=== * [[Jackson County, Oklahoma|Jackson County]] (1907) sources dispute if the name is for the CSA General or President Jackson *[[Roger Mills County, Oklahoma|Roger Mills County]] (1907), named for [[Roger Q. Mills]], Confederate colonel and later Congressman and U.S. Senator. * Town of [[Stonewall, Oklahoma|Stonewall]] (1874) for Stonewall Jackson
===Roads=== * [[Jay, Oklahoma|Jay]]: Stand Watie Road
==Oregon== {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are no public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Oregon]].
==Pennsylvania== {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 3 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Pennsylvania]].
===Monuments=== {{See also|List of monuments of the Gettysburg Battlefield#Confederate monuments}} [[File:Robert-E-Lee-by-Sievers.jpg|thumb|upright|''Virginia State Monument'' (1917), Gettysburg Battlefield.]] [[File:CONFEDERATE SOLDIERS and SAILORS MONUMENT (cropped).jpg|thumb|upright|''Confederate Soldiers and Sailors Monument'' (1911), Philadelphia National Cemetery.]] * [[Gettysburg, Pennsylvania|Gettysburg]]: [[Gettysburg Battlefield]]. In addition to the monuments listed below, the battlefield features monuments to specific Confederate units.<ref>[http://www.gettysburg.stonesentinels.com/Confederate.php Confederate Monuments at Gettysburg], StoneSentinels.com website, accessed April 24, 2010</ref> ** [[Alabama State Monument (Gettysburg)|Alabama State Monument]] (1933), [[Joseph Urner]], sculptor. ** Arkansas State Monument (1966) ** "Armistead's Last Stand" Marker, for Brig. Gen. [[Lewis A. Armistead]] (1887) ** [[Army of Northern Virginia]] Marker (1908) ** Culp Brothers Memorial (2013), Gary Casteel, sculptor, near entrance Gettysburg Heritage Center. Honors brothers who fought on opposite sides: Confederate Private Wesley Culp and Union Lieutenant William Culp ("Brother against Brother").<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.hmdb.org/marker.asp?marker=66634|title=Culp Brothers Memorial|last1=Stelly|first1=Karl|website=HMdb.org The Historical Marker Database|publisher=Bill Pfingsten|access-date=October 2, 2017}}</ref> ** Lt. Gen. Richard S. Ewell's Headquarters Marker (1920) ** Florida State Monument (1963) ** Georgia State Monument (1961) ** [[High Water Mark of the Rebellion Monument]] (1892) ** Lt. Gen. [[Ambrose P. Hill]]'s Headquarters Marker (1920) ** Gen. [[Robert E. Lee]] Equestrian Statue (1917), atop the Virginia State Monument ** Gen. [[Robert E. Lee]] Headquarters Marker (1920) ** Lt. Gen. [[James Longstreet]] Equestrian Statue (1998) ** Lt. Gen. [[James Longstreet]] Headquarters Marker (1907) ** Louisiana State Monument (1971), [[Donald De Lue]], sculptor ** Maryland State Monument (1994). Honors Maryland soldiers on both the Union and Confederate sides. ** Mississippi State Monument (1973), Donald De Lue, sculptor ** [[North Carolina Monument (Gettysburg)|North Carolina State Monument]] (1929), [[Gutzon Borglum]], sculptor. Borglum was also the first sculptor on the [[Stone Mountain#Confederate Memorial Carving]] project. ** North Carolina Memorial Tablet ** Soldiers and Sailors of the Confederacy Monument (1965), Donald De Lue, sculptor ** South Carolina State Monument (1963) ** Tennessee State Monument (1982) ** Texas State Monument (1964) ** [[Virginia State Monument (Gettysburg)|Virginia State Monument]] (1917), [[Frederick William Sievers]], sculptor. Features a larger-than-life sculpture group, "Virginia to Her Sons at Gettysburg;" and is topped by an equestrian statue of Gen. [[Robert E. Lee]]. The [[National Park Service]] says the monument will not be removed.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.wusa9.com/news/history/park-service-says-gettysburg-confederate-statues-here-to-stay/464893816 |newspaper=WUSA |date=August 16, 2017 |access-date=November 24, 2017 |title=Park Service says Gettysburg Confederate statues here to stay |first=Bruce |last=Leshan}}</ref> * [[McConnellsburg, Pennsylvania|McConnellsburg]] ** Confederate Soldiers Monument, dedicated in 1929. ** Last Confederate Bivouac Monument, dedicated in 1930. * Philadelphia: ''Confederate Soldiers and Sailors Monument'' (1911), [[Philadelphia National Cemetery]]. Commemorates 184 Confederate prisoners of war who died in Philadelphia area hospitals and camps.
===Roads=== * Gettysburg: Confederate Avenue * McConnellsburg: Confederate Lane
==Rhode Island== {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are no public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Rhode Island]].
==South Carolina==
{{main|List of Confederate monuments and memorials in South Carolina}}
{{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 194 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[South Carolina]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.argusleader.com/story/news/2020/07/07/gettysburg-south-dakota-removes-confederate-flag-police-patch/5389838002/ | title=Gettysburg removes Confederate flag from police patch and logo, city confirms }}</ref>
==South Dakota==
In July 2020 the Confederate flag was removed from the patch of Gettysburg South Dakota police officers.
{{As of|2020|06|24|df=US}}, there is at least one public space with Confederate monuments in [[South Dakota]].
* [[Gettysburg, South Dakota|Gettysburg]]: The Gettysburg police uniforms feature a patch with overlapping U.S. and Confederate flags and a civil-war era cannon along with the city's name, in a nod to the city's namesake, [[Gettysburg, Pennsylvania]], site of the famous [[Battle of Gettysburg]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Calls Renewed to Remove Confederate Flag from Police Patch|work=[[Associated Press]]|url= https://apnews.com/40ad85a2f6617bd7a3087d84a4cb1467|access-date=June 21, 2012}}</ref> The historical reference logo for the police emblem and uniform patch was designed in 2009.<ref>{{Cite web|date=July 14, 2015|title=Gettysburg won't remove Confederate flag from police patch|url=https://apnews.com/1ee92e5ff9164cff9b2bb37d6b2acea4|access-date=June 12, 2020|website=[[Associated Press|AP News]]}}</ref>
==Tennessee== {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Tennessee|Nathan Bedford Forrest#Historical reputation and legacy}} {{further|Tennessee in the American Civil War}} {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 105 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Tennessee]]. The [[Tennessee Heritage Protection Act]] (2016) and a 2013 law restrict the removal of statues and memorials.<ref name=Vice1/>
The [[Tennessee legislature]] designated [[Confederate Decoration Day]], the origin of [[Memorial Day]], as June 3, and in 1969<ref>{{citation |last=Staples |first=Brent |date=January 9, 2018 |title=Monuments to White Supremacy |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/01/09/opinion/monuments-white-supremacy-tennessee.html |work=[[The New York Times]]}}</ref> designated January 19 and July 13, their birthdays, as Robert E. Lee Day and [[Nathan Bedford Forrest]] day respectively.
===State capitol=== * [[Nathan Bedford Forrest Bust]]. On display in the Capital rotunda since 1978. Former governor [[Bill Haslam]] wished to remove it, but he was not supported by the Legislature or the Capitol Commission. "In 2010, the state moved the Forrest bust from outside the doors of the House of Representatives' chamber to its current location between the legislature's two chambers. It was relocated in order to make room for a bust of Sampson Keeble, Tennessee's first black legislator."<ref>{{cite news |title=Tennessee's bust of Confederate Gen. Nathan Bedford Forrest likely isn't going anywhere soon |first=Natalie |last=Allison |newspaper=[[The Tennessean]] |date=January 2, 2019 |url=https://www.tennessean.com/story/news/politics/2019/01/02/bill-lee-nathan-bedford-forrest-bust-capitol-tennessee-confederate/2283203002/}}</ref> In January 2019 a group of students [[demonstration (protest)|demonstrated]] at the capital, calling for its removal.<ref>{{cite news |title=Students ask Governor to remove Nathan Bedford Forrest bust from capital |date=January 31, 2019 |newspaper=[[WTVF]] (NewsChannel5) |url=https://www.newschannel5.com/news/students-ask-governor-to-remove-nathan-bedford-forrest-bust-from-capitol}}</ref>
===Buildings=== * [[Greeneville, Tennessee|Greeneville]]: General Morgan Inn, located at the spot where Confederate general [[John Hunt Morgan]] was killed. * [[Harrogate, Tennessee|Harrogate]]: [https://www.knoxnews.com/story/news/education/2022/02/12/timeline-lincoln-memorial-universitys-biggest-moments/9318140002/ Timeline: Learn more about the history of the Lincoln Memorial University] Grant Lee Building at Lincoln Memorial University was named in honor of the two famous civil war generals. Lincoln Memorial University was named in honor of Abraham Lincoln. * [[Murfreesboro, Tennessee|Murfreesboro]]: Forrest Hall at [[Middle Tennessee State University]]. The Tennessee Board of Regents has unanimously recommended the name change, on the recommendation of a campus task force, and the university president, but it has yet to pass the Tennessee Historical Commission, which plans "public hearings."<ref>{{cite web |title=MTSU Will Seek Approval to Rename Forrest Hall |first=Brian |last=Wilson |access-date=January 8, 2018 |newspaper=The Tennessean |date=April 28, 2016 |url=https://www.tennessean.com/story/news/2016/04/28/mcphee-calls-forrest-hall-renaming/83641504/}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wkrn.com/2017/04/24/process-still-ongoing-to-change-name-of-mtsus-forrest-hall/|title=Process still ongoing to change name of MTSU's Forrest Hall|last=Flowers|first=Larry|date=April 24, 2017|website=WKRN.com|access-date=August 17, 2017}}</ref>

/> * [[Dover, Tennessee|Dover]]: [[Fort Donelson]]. The Confederate fort was named for CSA General [[Daniel Smith Donelson]] but captured by Union General Grant in 1862, who retained the Fort's name saying "Fort Donelson will hereafter be marked in Capitals on the maps of our United Country..." Also contains Confederate Monument donated by United Daughters of the Confederacy in 1933.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nps.gov/fodo/index.htm|title=Fort Donelson National Battlefield (U.S. National Park Service)|website=www.nps.gov|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> * [[Dresden, Tennessee|Dresden]]: [[Weakley County, Tennessee|Weakley County]] Confederate Monument (1915) * [[Dyersburg, Tennessee|Dyersburg]]: ** Confederate Monument (1905) ** United Confederate Veterans Civil War Plaques (1926)<ref name="Tennessean" /> * [[Erwin, Tennessee|Erwin]]: War Memorial<ref name="Tennessean" /> * [[Fayetteville, Tennessee|Fayetteville]]: ** Civil War Memorial in memory of the three thousand Confederate soldiers of Lincoln County (1906) ** Women's Monument to those who kept up the responsibilities of farms and businesses during the Civil War (1904)<ref name="Tennessean" /> * [[Franklin, Tennessee|Franklin]]: [[Confederate monument (Franklin, Tennessee)|"Our Confederate Soldiers" Monument]] (1899), UDC monument known locally as "Chip" memorializes soldiers who died in the [[Battle of Franklin (1864)|Battle of Franklin]]. Since by state law it cannot be removed, the city of Franklin, with "broad support", wants to install historic markers "depicting the experience of the African-Americans before, during, and after the Civil War." The UDC opposes this, claiming ownership of the Public Square. As of December 2018, the issue is in litigation.<ref>{{cite news |title=Tennessee City Fights Daughters of the Confederacy Over Monuments |date=December 12, 2018 |first=Daniel |last=Jackson ====Private monuments==== * [[Nashville, Tennessee|Nashville]] ** [[Nathan Bedford Forrest Statue]], made of [[fiberglass]] over foam, 25 feet high, on private land<ref name=Garrison>{{cite news |title=Forrest statue land owner fires back at blocking efforts |first=Joey |last=Garrison |date=June 23, 2015 |newspaper=The Tennessean |access-date=January 6, 2018 |url=http://www.tennessean.com/story/news/local/davidson/2015/06/23/forrest-statue-land-owner-fires-back-blocking-efforts/29161883/}}</ref> near [[Interstate 65]], installed in 1998, built with private money. It is surrounded by [[Modern display of the Confederate flag#The vernacular "Confederate flag"|Confederate battle flags]], constituting what the owner calls "Confederate Flag Park." (No government recognizes it as a park, and the entrance is chained shut with a "[[Trespassing|No Trespassing]]" sign.) The giant statue is visible from the highway to anyone entering the city from the south.<ref name=ignore /> It has been called "hideous"<ref name=ignore>{{cite news |title=A Monument the Old South Would Like to Ignore |first=Margaret |last=Renkl |date=January 29, 2018 |access-date=January 29, 2018 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/01/29/opinion/south-monuments-nashville.html}}</ref> and "ridiculous."<ref>{{cite news |title=Kershaw's Death in ''The Times'' |first=Bruce |last=Barry |date=September 27, 2010 |access-date=January 29, 2018 |newspaper=[[Nashville Scene]] |url=https://www.nashvillescene.com/news/pith-in-the-wind/article/13035656/kershaws-death-in-the-times}}</ref> There have been numerous calls for its removal. Tennessee Governor [[Bill Haslam]] said: "It's not a statue that I like and [ [[sic]] ] that most Tennesseans are proud of in any way."<ref name=block /> Former Nashville Mayor [[Megan Barry]] called the statue "an offensive display of hatred."<ref name=block /> In 2015, Nashville's Metro Council voted to petition the [[Tennessee Department of Transportation]] to plant obscuring vegetation;<ref name="washposttheterrifyingconfederatestatue">{{cite news|last1=Holley|first1=Peter|title=The 'terrifying' Confederate statue some Tennesseans want to hide|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/morning-mix/wp/2015/06/25/is-this-the-weirdest-confederate-statue-in-dixie/|access-date=September 2, 2017|newspaper=The Washington Post|date=June 25, 2015}}</ref> the Department declined, because it is private land.<ref name=ignore /> ("Never mind that the T.D.O.T. itself removed the obscuring vegetation back in 1998, when the statue was first erected."<ref name=ignore /><ref name=block>{{cite news |title=Metro Council asks state to block view of I-65 Forrest statue |first=Joey |last=Garrison |newspaper=[[The Tennessean]] |date=July 7, 2015 |access-date=January 8, 2018 |url=https://www.tennessean.com/story/insession/2015/07/08/metro-council-asks-state-to-block-view-of-i-65-forrest-statue/29848961/}}</ref>) There has been occasional vandalism; in December 2017 it was covered in "pussy-hat pink" paint,<ref name=ignore /> which Bill Dorris, current owner of the land, says he intends to leave.<ref>{{cite news |title=Vandals cover Nathan Bedford Forrest statue with pink paint |author=WKRN web staff |newspaper=[[WKRN]] |date=December 27, 2017 |access-date=January 8, 2018 |url=http://wkrn.com/2017/12/27/vandals-cover-nathan-bedford-forrest-statue-with-pink-paint/}}</ref> He also said that if trees are planted to block the view from I-65, he "would make the statue taller."<ref name=Garrison /> It was sculpted, at no charge, by notorious racist [[Jack Kershaw]], an attorney for [[James Earl Ray|Martin Luther King's murderer]], famous for having said "Somebody needs to say a good word for [[Slavery in the United States|slavery]]."<ref>{{cite news |title=Blockage sought of I-65 Nathan Bedford Forrest statue |first=Joey |last=Garrison |date=June 22, 2015 |newspaper=The Tennessean |url=http://www.tennessean.com/story/insession/2015/06/22/blockage-sought-of-i-65-nathan-bedford-forrest-statue/29128551/ |access-date=January 5, 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Jack Kershaw Is Dead at 96; Challenged Conviction in King's Death |first=Douglas |last=Martin |date=September 24, 2010 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=January 29, 2018 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2010/09/24/us/24kershaw.html}}</ref>
