Lee Drive ** Stonewall Jackson Driv * [[Oak Hill, Tennessee|Oak Hill]]: Stonewall Jackson Court * [[Pulaski, Tennessee|Pulaski]] ** Sam Davis Avenue ** Sam Davis Trail * [[Sardis, Tennessee|Sardis]]: Jeff Davis Lane * [[Smyrna, Tennessee|Smyrna]] ** Jeb Stuart Drive ** Lee Lane ** Longstreet Drive ** Robert E. Lee Lane ** Sam Davis Road ** Stonewall Drive {{div col end}}
===Schools=== * [[Chapel Hill, Tennessee|Chapel Hill]]: Forrest High School * [[Nashville, Tennessee|Nashville]]: [[Father Ryan High School]], named for [[Abram Ryan]], called "Poet of the Confederacy". * [[Paris, Tennessee|Paris]]: [[Robert E. Lee School (Paris, Tennessee)|Robert E. Lee School]] – now called Paris Academy for the Arts. * [[Sewanee: The University of the South]]: "Nowhere is the issue of Confederate remembrance more nettlesome than at Sewanee, whose origin[s] are entwined with the antebellum South and the Confederacy."<ref name="McWhirter" /> Confederate flags are in stained glass windows of the chapel, as is the [[seal (emblem)|Seal]] of the Confederacy.<ref name="McWhirter">{{cite web |title = Colleges suffer identity crisis |first = Cameron |last = McWhirter |newspaper = Atlanta Journal-Constitution |date = February 12, 2005 |url = http://www.leonidaspolk.org/Colleges%20suffer%20identity%20crisis%20%20ajc_com.htm |access-date = December 12, 2017 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20060228062721/http://www.leonidaspolk.org/Colleges%20suffer%20identity%20crisis%20%20ajc_com.htm |archive-date = February 28, 2006 }}</ref> It benefited greatly at its founding by a large gift from [[John Armfield]], at one time co-owner of [[Franklin and Armfield]], the largest and most prosperous slave trading enterprise in the country. Students as late as 1871 were required to wear uniforms of "cadet gray cloth".<ref>From a poster included at [[Sewanee: The University of the South]].</ref> Confederate flags hung in the chapel from its dedication in 1909 until the mid-1990s when they were removed "reportedly to improve acoustics".<ref name="auto10">{{Cite web|url=http://www.freep.com/story/opinion/contributors/2015/06/27/confederate-flag-church/29365297/|title=Confederate flag's history is 'sick' and 'twisted'|last=Danaher|first=William|date=June 27, 2015|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> There is an official portrait hanging at the University of Bishop [[Leonidas Polk]], "an ardent defender of slavery,"<ref name="McWhirter" /> who was in charge of the celebration of the cornerstone laying in 1857, and said the new university will "materially aid the South to resist and repel a fanatical domination which seeks to rule over us."<ref name="Carry Me Back: The Domestic Slave Trade in American Life">{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-dbFUlQvcRYCe|title=Carry Me Back: The Domestic Slave Trade in American Life|author=Steven Deyle|publisher=Oxford University Press|access-date=June 20, 2013|year=2013|pages=205–07}}{{Dead link|date=August 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> He resigned his ecclesiastical position to become a major general in the Confederate army (called "Sewanee's Fighting Bishop"), and died in battle in 1864. His official portrait at the University depicts him dressed as a bishop with his army uniform hanging nearby. However, his portrait was moved from Convocation Hall to Archives and Special Collections in 2015.<ref>{{cite news |title=Sewanee, Polk, and the Old South |date=March 22, 2016 |first=Fleming |last=Smith |access-date=January 1, 2017 |url=https://thesewaneepurple.org/2016/03/22/sewanee-polk-and-the-old-south/ |newspaper=The Sewanee Purple}}</ref> The Confederate flag was also emblazoned on the university mace that led processions marking the beginning and ending of the term from 1965 until 1997. At a special chapel service to celebrate Jefferson Davis' birthday, the [[Sewanee: The University of the South#Mace controversy|Ceremonial Mace]] was consecrated to the memory of [[Nathan Bedford Forrest]], the first Grand Wizard of the Ku Klux Klan, by Bishop [[Charles C. J. Carpenter]] of Alabama – one of the clergy who opposed Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s activities in Birmingham in 1963 (see [[A Call for Unity]]), prompting King to write his "[[Letter from a Birmingham Jail]]" in response.<ref name="auto10" /> **The Vice Chancellor is the chief academic officer at the university; the chancellor is a bishop of the Episcopal church. Jefferson Davis and Robert E. Lee both turned down offers of the position.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dspace.sewanee.edu/bitstream/handle/11005/2621/sewanee2008com.pdf|title=DSpace|website=dspace.sewanee.edu}}</ref> (Sewanee has a portrait of Davis.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.tnportraits.org/davis-jefferson-sewanee.htm|title=Jefferson Davis – Unknown – The University of the South – Sewanee, Tennessee|website=www.TNPortraits.org|access-date=October 6, 2017|archive-date=November 20, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081120042301/http://www.tnportraits.org/davis-jefferson-sewanee.htm|url-status=dead}}</ref>) The first vice chancellor was Rt. Rev. [[Charles Todd Quintard]], called "chaplain of the Confederacy". He compiled the ''Confederate Soldiers' Pocket Manual of Devotions'' (Charleston, 1863).<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.org/details/07794796.3866.emory.edu|title=The Confederate soldiers' pocket manual of devotions|first1=C. T. (Charles Todd)|last1=Quintard|first2=J. Durelle|last2=Boles|first3=printer|last3=Evans & Cogswell|last4=J. Durelle Boles Collection of Southern Imprints. GEU|date=October 6, 1863|publisher=Charleston [S.C.] : Printed by Evans & Cogswell|access-date=October 6, 2017|via=Internet Archive}}</ref> **The university's chief donor was [[John Armfield]], at the time co-owner of [[Franklin and Armfield]], the largest slave-trading firm in the U.S. He purchased the site and gave the university an endowment of $25,000 a year. In addition to Polk, Bishop [[Stephen Elliott (bishop)|Stephen Elliott]], the first and only Presiding Bishop of the [[Protestant Episcopal Church in the Confederate States of America]], and Bishop [[James Hervey Otey]], later prominent in the Confederacy, were significant founders of the university. Generals [[Edmund Kirby Smith]], [[Josiah Gorgas]], and [[Francis A. Shoup]] were prominent in the university's postbellum revival and continuance. [[File:Calhoun Hall.jpg|thumb|right|Calhoun Hall, named for slave owner and Confederate supporter W. H. Calhoun.]] * [[Vanderbilt University]]: **Calhoun Hall, named for [[William Henry Calhoun]], a slave owner, Odd Fellows Grand Master and silversmith who was arrested for his Confederate support on the order of Union Brigadier General [[Robert Byington Mitchell]] pending the release of Union prisoners in 1863.<ref name="mesdabio">{{cite web|title=Cup. Artist/Maker: Calhoun, William Henry|url=http://mesda.org/item/collections/cup/21231/|website=Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts|access-date=April 22, 2018|archive-date=April 24, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180424090756/http://mesda.org/item/collections/cup/21231/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="nashdailyunionspecialorders">{{cite news|title=Special Orders No. 46|url=https://www.newspapers.com/image/86535271/?terms=%22william%2Bh.%2Bcalhoun%22|access-date=April 23, 2018|work=The Nashville Daily Union|date=February 28, 1863|page=3|via=[[Newspapers.com]]|url-access=registration }}</ref> **Portrait of Confederate General [[Edmund Kirby Smith]] by [[Cornelius Hankins]] in the Wyatt Center.<ref name="tennportraitedmundkirbysmith">{{cite web|title=Vanderbilt Collection – Peabody Campus – Wyatt Center: Edmund Kirby Smith|url=http://www.tnportraits.org/vand-smith-edmund-kirby.htm|website=Tennessee Portrait Project|publisher=National Society of Colonial Dames of America in Tennessee|access-date=May 3, 2018|archive-date=September 15, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150915152756/http://www.tnportraits.org/vand-smith-edmund-kirby.htm|url-status=dead}}</ref> Smith served as the chancellor of the [[University of Nashville]] from 1870 to 1875.<ref name="tennportraitedmundkirbysmith"/> **Marble bust of Confederate Colonel and Board of Trust Treasurer [[Edmund William Cole]] in Kirkland Hall, the administration building.<ref name="tnportraitprojectcole">{{cite web|title=Vanderbilt Collection – Kirkland Hall: Anna Virginia Russell (Mrs. E.W.) Cole 1846–1926|url=http://tnportraits.org/cole-mrs-ew.htm|website=Tennessee Portrait Project|publisher=National Society of Colonial Dames of America in Tennessee|access-date=September 30, 2017|archive-date=September 15, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150915173058/http://www.tnportraits.org/cole-mrs-ew.htm|url-status=dead}}</ref> He is also the namesake of the annual Cole Lecture.<ref name="divinityschoolcolelectures">{{cite web|title=Cole Lectures|url=https://divinity.vanderbilt.edu/news/lectures/cole.php|website=Divinity School|publisher=Vanderbilt University|access-date=September 19, 2017|archive-date=October 2, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171002165606/https://divinity.vanderbilt.edu/news/lectures/cole.php|url-status=dead}}</ref> **The campus includes several buildings tied to slavery and the Confederacy, like McTyeire Hall (named for pro-slavery essayist [[Holland Nimmons McTyeire]]) and Elliston Hall (named for Elizabeth Boddie Elliston of the [[Burlington (Nashville, Tennessee)|Burlington Plantation]], who owned slaves).<ref name="vanderbiltpoliticalbubthelegacy">{{cite news|last1=Bub|first1=Sydney|last2=Mediratta|first2=Avi|title=The Legacy of Slavery At Vanderbilt|url=http://www.vanderbiltpoliticalreview.com/the-legacy-of-slavery-at-vanderbilt/|access-date=August 31, 2017|work=Vanderbilt Political Review|date=October 5, 2016}}</ref><ref name="vanderbilthistoricalreviewthelegacyofslavery">{{cite news|last1=Fuselier|first1=Kathryn|last2=Yee|first2=Robert|title=The Legacy of Slavery at Vanderbilt: Our Forgotten Past|url=http://vanderbilthistoricalreview.com/legacy-of-slavery/|access-date=September 19, 2017|work=Vanderbilt Historical Review|date=October 17, 2016|archive-date=October 2, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171002120504/http://vanderbilthistoricalreview.com/legacy-of-slavery/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="usatodaybeyondyale">{{cite news|last1=Epstein Ojalvo|first1=Holly|title=Beyond Yale: These other university buildings have ties to slavery and white supremacy|url=http://college.usatoday.com/2017/02/13/yale-university-buildings-slavery-white-supremacy/|access-date=April 7, 2018|work=[[USA Today]]|date=February 13, 2017|quote=But in 2012, a new college hall was dedicated to Elizabeth Boddie Elliston, whose family owned slaves and who, according to the university website, "donated segments of her plantation for the formation of the Vanderbilt campus."}}</ref>
===Tourist sites=== * [[Pigeon Forge, Tennessee|Pigeon Forge]]: "Rebel Railroad" was a small theme park built in 1961, its main attraction being a simulated Confederate steam train which afforded "'good Confederate citizens' the opportunity to ride a five mile train route through 'hostile' territory and to help repel a Yankee assault on the train". Rebel Railroad was purchased in 1970 by [[Art Modell]], owner of the [[Cleveland Browns]].<ref>{{cite book | last = Knight | first = Gladys L. | title = Pop Culture Places: An Encyclopedia of Places in American Popular Culture | publisher = ABC-CLIO | year = 2014 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=kheDBAAAQBAJ&pg=PP1| pages = 252| isbn = 978-0313398834 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last = Martin | first = C. Brenden | title = Tourism in the Mountain South: A Double-edged Sword | publisher = University of Tennessee Press | year = 2007 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=jC3ag3FT0e4C&pg=PP1| pages = 158, 159| isbn = 978-1572335752 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web | title = Dollywood History | publisher = Dollywood | url = https://www.dollywood.com/About-Us/Dollywood-History | access-date = November 7, 2017 | archive-date = November 8, 2017 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20171108095530/https://www.dollywood.com/About-Us/Dollywood-History | url-status = dead }}</ref> In 2018 it is operating under the name [[Dollywood]]. * [[Morristown, Tennessee|Morristown]], General Longstreet Headquarters Museum<ref>{{cite web | title = General Longstreet Headquarters Museum | publisher = General James Longstreet Headquarters Museum | url = http://www.longstreetmuseum.com/visit.htm | access-date = July 8, 2020 | archive-date = February 18, 2020 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20200218111817/http://www.longstreetmuseum.com/visit.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
==Texas== {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Texas}} {{further|Texas in the American Civil War}} {{As of|2020|June|24}}, there are at least 205 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Texas]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=http://www.co.waller.tx.us/upload/common/docs/Veterans/memorial_book.pdf|title=Directory of Memorials, Monuments, and Statues for Veterans|publisher=Texas Veterans Commission|year=1985|location=Austin}}</ref> "Nowhere has the national re-examination of Confederate emblems been more riven with controversy than the Lone Star State."<ref name=Politico />
===State capitol=== * "The Texas Capitol itself is a Confederate monument," according to then-[[Texas General Land Office|Land Commissioner]] [[Jerry E. Patterson]].<ref name=observer >{{cite news |title=The Hidden Confederate History of the Texas Capitol: An Unofficial Guide |first1=Kelsey |last1=Jukam |first2=John |last2=Savage |first3=Alisa |last3=Semiens |date=February 17, 2015 |url=https://www.texasobserver.org/hidden-confederate-history-texas-capitol-unofficial-guide/ |newspaper=Texas Observer |access-date=January 2, 2018}}</ref> The [[Texas Confederate Museum]] was once housed in the Capitol. ** [[Confederate Soldiers Monument (Austin, Texas)|Confederate Soldiers Monument]] (1903) features four bronze figures representing the Confederate artillery, cavalry, infantry, and navy. A bronze statue of Jefferson Davis stands above them.<ref>{{cite news | access-date = October 29, 2017 | title = The Hidden Confederate History of the Texas Capitol: An Unofficial Guide | url = https://www.texasobserver.org/hidden-confederate-history-texas-capitol-unofficial-guide/ | work = Texas Observer}}</ref> The inscription reads: "Died for state rights guaranteed under the constitution. The people of the South, animated by the spirit of 1776, to preserve their rights, withdrew from the federal compact in 1861. The North resorted to coercion. The South, against overwhelming numbers and resources, fought until exhausted."<ref name=":24" /> ** [[Hood's Texas Brigade]], a monument "to memorialize those [who] fought for the Confederacy".<ref name="chronicle" /> "The monument includes a depiction of a Confederate soldier, quotes by Confederate leaders, a flag of the Confederacy and the Confederate battle flag."<ref name=Johnson>{{cite news|title=State Rep. Eric Johnson Wants to Spur a Discussion of Confederate Symbols by Removing Capitol Plaque|first=Rhonda|last=Fanning|date=August 17, 2017|access-date=June 2, 2018|url=http://www.texasstandard.org/stories/state-rep-eric-johnson-wants-to-spur-a-discussion-on-confederate-symbols-by-removing-capitol-plaque/|newspaper=Texas Standard|publisher=[[Moody College of Communication]]}}</ref> These are the only Confederate flags currently (2017) visible in the Capitol.<ref>{{citation|url=https://www.texasobserver.org/hidden-confederate-history-texas-capitol-unofficial-guide/|newspaper=Texas Observer|date=February 17, 2015|access-date=November 24, 2017|title=The Hidden Confederate History of the Texas Capitol: An Unofficial Guide|first1=Kelsey|last1=Jukam|first2=John|last2=Savage|first3=Alisa|last3=Semiens}}</ref> Representative [[Eric Johnson (Texas politician)|Eric Johnson]] has called for its removal.<ref name=Johnson /> ** [[Terry's Texas Rangers Monument]], a monument "to memorialize those [who] fought for the Confederacy"<ref name="chronicle" /> (1907).
===State symbols=== [[File:Reverse of the Seal of Texas.svg|thumb|right|[[Seal of Texas]]]] * The reverse side of the [[Seal of Texas]] (1992) includes "the unfurled flags of the Kingdom of France, the Kingdom of Spain, the United Mexican States, the Republic of Texas, the Confederate States of America, and the United States of America". The Confederate flag is rendered as the [[Flags of the Confederate States of America#First national flag: "The Stars and Bars|Stars and Bars]].
===State holiday=== * [[Confederate Memorial Day|Confederate Heroes Day]] is celebrated on January 19. State employees have the day off. * April is Confederate History Month in Texas.<ref name=Madison>{{cite news |title=No commotion as county passes Confederate History Month measure |date=February 25, 2020 |first=Roy R. |last=Reynolds |newspaper=[[Madisonville Meteor]] ([[Madisonville, Texas]]) |url=http://madisonvillemeteor.com/stories/no-commotion-as-county-passes-confederate-history-month-measure,32241 |access-date=February 27, 2020 |archive-date=February 27, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200227095941/http://madisonvillemeteor.com/stories/no-commotion-as-county-passes-confederate-history-month-measure,32241 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
===Buildings=== * Austin ** The [[John H. Reagan]] state office building is located at 1400 Congress.<ref name="chronicle">{{cite news |url=https://www.austinchronicle.com/news/2017-08-18/a-guide-to-confederate-monuments-in-austin/ |newspaper=Austin Chronicle |date=August 18, 2017 |access-date=November 24, 2017 |title=A Guide to Confederate Monuments in Austin}}</ref>
===Monuments=== Many monuments were donated by pro-Confederacy groups like [[Daughters of the Confederacy]]. County governments at the time voted to accept the gifts and take ownership of the statues.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.texasconfederateveterans.com/Texas%20Confederate%20Monuments.htm|title=Texas Confederate Statues and Monuments|website=Texasconfederateveterans.com|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://birdshooter.smugmug.com/TexasCourthouses/Confederate-Soldier-Monuments-/|title=Confederate Soldier Monuments on Texas Courthouse Squares |first=Wayne |last=Wendel |website=Birdshooter.smugmug.com |access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref> [[File:Confederate monument detail Cooke County Courthouse Gainesville Wiki (1 of 1) (cropped).jpg|thumb|Detail of Cooke County Courthouse monument. Inscription reads “''no nation rose so white and fair none fell so pure of crime''”<ref name="auto5">{{Cite web |last=Campbell |first=Steve |title=Gainesville's dark past still stirring passions |url=https://www.statesman.com/story/news/2012/10/15/gainesvilles-dark-past-still-stirring-passions/9853690007/ |access-date=2024-10-26 |website=Austin American-Statesman |date=October 15, 2012 |language=en-US}}</ref>]]
====Courthouse monuments==== * [[Alpine, Texas|Alpine]]: Confederate Colonel [[Henry Percy Brewster]] (1963)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.hmdb.org/m.asp?m=50296|title=Lawrence Sullivan Ross Historical Marker|website=www.hmdb.org}}</ref> * [[Aspermont, Texas|Aspermont]]: Historical marker, "County Named for Confederate Hero Stonewall Jackson", Stonewall County Courthouse (1963) * [[Bastrop, Texas|Bastrop]]: Monuments at [[Bastrop County Courthouse and Jail Complex|Bastrop County Courthouse]] include: ** Confederate Soldiers' Monument (1910)<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.statesman.com/news/local/little-discussion-over-bastrop-county-confederate-monument/p6MRnoiCQA2THMjC4DtmmI/|title=Little discussion over Bastrop County Confederate monument|last=Beck|first=Jillian|date=July 13, 2015|work=Austin American-Statesman|access-date=August 16, 2017|publisher=Cox Media Group}}</ref> ** Historical marker, "Home Town of Texas Confederate Major [[Joseph D. Sayers]]" (1963)<ref>{{Cite web | url = https://atlas.thc.state.tx.us/Details/5021012388 | title = Details – Home Town of Texas Confederate Major Joseph D. Sayers – Atlas Number 5021012388 –Texas Historical Commission | website=atlas.thc.state.tx.us | access-date = August 20, 2017}}</ref> * [[Bay City, Texas|Bay City]]: Confederate Soldiers' Monument (1913), Matagorda County Courthouse<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.texasconfederateveterans.com/matagordacountyUCV.htm|title=Matagorda County Confederate Monument|website=Texas Confederate Veterans|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://birdshooter.smugmug.com/TexasCourthouses/Confederate-Soldier-Monuments-/i-VpvMRCd/A|title=Matagorda County Courthouse: Civil War Veterans Monument|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref> * [[Belton, Texas|Belton]]: Confederate Soldiers' Monument, Bell County Courthouse<ref>{{cite web|url=https://birdshooter.smugmug.com/TexasCourthouses/Confederate-Soldier-Monuments-/i-4CGcxTW/A|title=Confederate Soldier Monument|website=Birdshooter.smugmug.com|access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref> * [[Bonham, Texas|Bonham]]: Confederate Soldiers' Monument (1905), Fannin County Courthouse<ref>{{cite web|url=https://birdshooter.smugmug.com/TexasCourthouses/Fannin-County-Courthouse/|title=Fannin County: Bonham, Texas |first=Wayne |last=Wendel|website=Birdshooter.smugmug.com|access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref> * [[Bryan, Texas|Bryan]]: Commemorative marker, erected 1965, to the [[Brazos County, Texas|Brazos County]] Confederate Commissioners Court.<ref>{{cite web |title=Details for Brazos County Confederate Commissioners Court |publisher=[[Texas Historical Commission]] |url=https://atlas.thc.state.tx.us/Details/5041008667 |access-date=August 10, 2018}}</ref> * [[Comanche, Texas|Comanche]]: Confederate Soldiers' Monument (2002), Comanche County Courthouse<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://birdshooter.smugmug.com/TexasCourthouses/Confederate-Soldier-Monuments-/i-b4BXzBM/A|title=Confederate Veterans Memorial, Comanche County Courthouse|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref> * [[Corsicana, Texas|Corsicana]]: ''Call to Arms'' (Confederate Soldiers' Monument), by [[Louis Amateis]] (1907), Navarro County Courthouse.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://birdshooter.smugmug.com/TexasCourthouses/Confederate-Soldier-Monuments-/i-N92QrdR/A|title=Confederate Memorial, Navarro County Courthouse Square|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.texasconfederateveterans.com/navarrocountyMonument.htm|title=Navarro County Confederate Monument|website=Texas Confederate Veterans|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> A Civil War bugler stands in uniform holding a bugle to his mouth with his proper right hand. He holds a sword in his proper left hand at his side. He wears a hat with a feather in it and knee-high boots. A bedroll is slung over his proper left shoulder and strapped across his chest and proper right hip. The sculpture is mounted on a rectangular base.<ref name=Corsicana>{{cite web |title=Call To Arms, (sculpture) |author=Smithsonian American Art Museum |access-date=May 7, 2018 |url=https://siris-artinventories.si.edu/ipac20/ipac.jsp?session=L5X58624D5091.204&profile=ariall&uri=full=3100001~!338592~!3&ri=4&aspect=Keyword&menu=search&source=~!siartinventories&ipp=20&spp=20&staffonly=&term=Confederate&index=.TW&uindex=&aspect=Keyword&menu=search&ri=4}}</ref> "Isaac O'Haver was a member of Co K of the 17th VA Cavalry. He was a 17 year-old bugler for his unit. He was born Sep. 20, 1844 and died at the age of 27 on March 30, 1872. He is buried at the Ladoga Cemetery."<ref>{{cite web |title=Isaac O'Haver |date=September 1, 2009 |url=https://johnmangus.wordpress.com/2009/09/}}</ref> The plaques on the monument read: **South side: The Call to Arms Erected 1907 by Navarro chapter United Daughters of the Confederacy To commemorate the valor and heroism of our Confederate Soldiers It is not in the power of mortals to command success The Confederate Soldier did more – he deserved it. "But their fame on brightest pages penned by poets and by pages Shall go sounding down the ages" **West side: "Nor shall your glory be fought while fame her record keeps or honor points the hollowed spot where valor proudly sleeps" "Tell it as you may It never can be told Sing it as you Will It never can be sung The Story of the Glory of the men who wore the gray" **East side: "It is a duty we owe the dead who died for us: – But where memories can never die – It is a duty we owe to posterity to see that our children shall know the virtues And rise worthy of their sires". **North side: The soldiers of the Southern Confederacy fought valiantly for The liberty of state bequeathed them By their forefathers of 1776 "Who Glorified Their righteous cause and they who made The sacrifice supreme in That they died To keep their country free"<ref name=Corsicana /> * [[Clarksville, Texas|Clarksville]]: Confederate Soldiers' Monument, Red River County County Courthouse<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://birdshooter.smugmug.com/TexasCourthouses/Confederate-Soldier-Monuments-/i-bVPcMXn/A|title=Confederate Soldier Memorial on the Courthouse Square, Clarksville, Texas, Red River County|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref> [[File:CSA_monument,_Denton,_Texas.jpeg|thumb|upright|[[Denton, Texas]]]] * [[Denton, Texas|Denton]]: [[Denton Confederate Soldier Monument]], Denton County Courthouse.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://texashistory.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metapth34974/|title=Confederate Soldiers' Monument – The Portal to Texas History|website=Texashistory.unt.edu|access-date=May 23, 2017}}</ref> Cost $2,000; a project of the Denton Chapter, UDC. Dedicated June 3, 1918, [[Jefferson Davis]]'s birthday.<ref>{{cite web |title=Confederate Soldiers' Monument |year=2007 |url=http://texashistory.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metapth34974/ |access-date=September 14, 2018 |publisher=University of North Texas Libraries, The Portal to Texas History |page=7}}</ref> It had "whites only" drinking fountains on each side.<ref name=Vice/> In 2015 it was defaced with the words "THIS IS RACIST" in red paint.<ref>{{cite news |title=Denton County to keep Confederate monument |first=Mark |last=Smith |date=February 8, 2018 |newspaper=Cross Timbers Gazette |url=http://www.crosstimbersgazette.com/2018/02/08/denton-county-to-keep-confederate-monument/}}</ref> The twenty-year campaign of a Denton resident, Willie Hudspeth, to have the monument removed was the subject of a ''[[Vice (magazine)|Vice]]'' news video in 2018.<ref name="Vice">{{cite AV media|url=https://video.vice.com/en_us/video/two-decade-fight-bring-down-confederate-monument-protest/5a96d9ecf1cdb3463b5675d1|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180307201853/https://video.vice.com/en_us/video/two-decade-fight-bring-down-confederate-monument-protest/5a96d9ecf1cdb3463b5675d1|url-status=dead|archive-date=March 7, 2018|title=Inside the Two-Decade Fight to Bring Down a Confederate Monument|people=Lee Adams, Host; Dylan Coburn, Producer|publisher=[[Vice (magazine)|Vice]]|year=2018}}</ref> After the wave of [[Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials|Confederate monument removals]] that followed the 2017 [[Unite the Right rally]] in [[Charlottesville, Virginia]], and in large part as a result of Hudspeth's campaign, a county 15-person Confederate Memorial Committee met for three months in 2017–18 and recommended "adding context" – two video kiosks and a large plaque, "with interviews about local veterans and the history of slavery"<ref>{{cite news |title=30 turn out to protest recommendation |first=Julia |last=Falcon |newspaper=[[Denton Record-Chronicle]] |date=February 5, 2018 |url=https://www.dentonrc.com/news/turn-out-to-protest-recommendation/article_a8d6ac77-de0d-5718-b77f-438cbde98a98.html}}</ref> – to the monument rather than removing it, a suggestion accepted unanimously by the county commissioners. Once the nature of the historical context has been determined, approval of the [[Texas Historical Commission]] will be required.<ref>{{cite news |title=Judge does not expect Confederate Monument context to be added until 2019 |date=May 30, 2018 |first=Devin |last=Rarden |newspaper=[[North Texas Daily]] |url=https://www.ntdaily.com/judge-does-not-expect-confederate-monument-context-to-be-added-until-2019/}}</ref> As of September 2018, "the county still does not have a timeline for completing the project and...there were no updates to report".<ref name=Laferney>{{cite news |title=Removal of Denton County's Confederate statue a priority for national group |first=Dalton |last=LaFerney |url=https://www.dentonrc.com/news/denton_county/removal-of-denton-county-s-confederate-statue-a-priority-for/article_c6bbe538-91a3-5116-a53f-cb9ebf4dead6.html |newspaper=[[Denton Record-Chronicle]] |date=September 9, 2018}}</ref> The video caught the attention of Kali Holloway, director of the [[Make It Right Project]], which is working to remove Confederate monuments. She added the Denton monument to the group's "top 10 list" of monuments they consider priorities.<ref name="independentmediainstitute.org"/><ref name=Laferney/> The statue was removed in June 2020.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confederate soldier statue removed from Denton County Courthouse-on-the-Square|url=https://www.wfaa.com/article/news/local/denton-confederate-soldier-statue-removed-from-town-square/287-f38d4eb3-4155-4457-8303-ec159fc1a6a1|access-date=June 28, 2020|website=wfaa.com|date=June 25, 2020 |language=en-US}}</ref> * [[Fort Worth, Texas|Fort Worth]]: [[Confederate Monument (Fort Worth, Texas)|Monument to "Confederate Soldiers and their Descendents]]" (1953), Tarrant County Courthouse<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://birdshooter.smugmug.com/TexasCourthouses/Confederate-Soldier-Monuments-/i-nB966qR/A|title=Confederate Soldiers Memorial, Tarrant County, Fort Worth, TX|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref> [[File:"Dignified Resignation" - Galveston, Texas.jpg|thumb|upright|''Dignified Resignation'' in [[Galveston, Texas]]]] * [[Galveston, Texas|Galveston]]: ''Dignified Resignation'' (1909) by [[Louis Amateis]] at the Galveston County Courthouse. With his back turned to the US flag while carrying a [[Flags of the Confederate States of America|Confederate flag]], it is the only memorial in Texas to feature a Confederate sailor.<ref name="Morris Little">{{cite book|last=Little|first=Carol Morris|title=A Comprehensive Guide to Outdoor Sculpture in Texas|publisher=University of Texas Press|year=1996|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NgEAMI9BjkYC&pg=PP1|pages=53, 122, 216, 229, 247, 350|isbn=978-0292760363}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.texasconfederateveterans.com/Galveston%20Confederate%20Monument.htm|title=Galveston County Confederate Monument|website=Texas Confederate Veterans|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> It was "erected to the soldiers and sailors of the Confederate States of America." An inscription on the plaque reads, "there has never been an armed force which in purity of motives intensity of courage and heroism has equaled the army and navy of the Confederate States of America."<ref name=":24">{{Cite news|url=http://www.mysanantonio.com/news/local/article/Deadly-rally-accelerates-removal-of-Confederate-11819879.php|title=Deadly rally accelerates removal of Confederate statues, including S.A.'s memorial|last=Holland|first=Jesse J.|date=August 15, 2017|work=[[San Antonio Express-News]]|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> * [[Gainesville, ====Other public monuments==== [[File:Confederate Memorial Plaza, Anderson, Texas.jpg|thumb|upright|Confederate Memorial Plaza in [[Anderson, Texas]]]] [[File:Confederate Dead monument in front of Texas State Capitol-front view.JPG|thumb|upright|[[Confederate Soldiers Monument (Austin, Texas)|Confederate Soldiers Monument]], Austin]] [[File:Confederate Monument, Wiess Park, Beaumont, Texas.jpg|thumb|upright|Confederate Monument, Beaumont]] * [[Alpine, Texas|Alpine]]: CSA Gen. [[Lawrence Sullivan Ross|Lawrence "Sul" Ross]] Monument (1963) * [[Anderson, Texas|Anderson]]: Confederate Memorial Plaza (2010).<ref>{{cite web|title=Statue in Confederate Memorial Plaza|publisher=Texashistory|url=https://texashistory.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metapth473262/|access-date=September 1, 2017|date=February 14, 2010}}</ref> The plaza beside the Grimes County courthouse flies a Confederate flag behind a gate with metal lettering reading "Confederate Memorial Plaza." A metal statue depicts one of several Grimes County residents who fought with the 4th Texas volunteer infantry brigade in Virginia.<ref name=":24" /> * [[Athens, Texas|Athens]]: Henderson County Confederate Monument (1964) * [[Austin, Texas|Austin]]: ** [[Hood's Texas Brigade Monument]], Texas State Capitol ** Littlefield Fountain, University of Texas, commemorates [[George W. Littlefield]], a university regent and CSA officer. An inscription reads, "To the men and women of the Confederacy who fought with valor and suffered with fortitude that states [sic] rights be maintained." ** Texas Confederate Women's and Men's Historical Markers, at 3710 Cedar St. and 1600 W. Sixth, commemorate campgrounds built to house and care for widows, wives, and veterans of the Confederacy.<ref name="chronicle" /> * [[Beaumont, Texas|Beaumont]]: "Our Confederate Soldiers" Monument (1912). Removed in June 2020.<ref>{{cite web |title=City of Beaumont takes down confederate monument at Wiess Park |url=https://www.12newsnow.com/article/news/community/city-of-beaumont-takes-down-confederate-monument-at-weiss-park/502-30b73946-4d9e-40ca-a5f0-2fb4e748cf29 |website=12newsnow.com |publisher=KBMT |date=June 29, 2020 |quote=The City of Beaumont removed a Confederate statue Monday. The statue had been in a downtown park since 1912. Crews removed the statue around 10 a.m. June 29. The city council had voted 6-1 on Tuesday, June 23 to remove the statue at Wiess Park and store it in a warehouse.}}</ref> * [[Clarksville, Texas|Clarksville]]: Confederate Soldier Monument (1912) * [[Cleburne, Texas|Cleburne]]: [[Statue of Patrick Cleburne (Cleburne, Texas)|Cleburne Monument]] (2015) [[Confederate Memorial Arch (Cleburne, Texas)|Confederate Arch]] (1922) * [[Coleman, Texas|Coleman]]: Hometown of Texas CSA Col. James E. McCord Monument (1963) * [[College Station, Texas|College Station]]: A statue of [[Lawrence Sullivan Ross]], Confederate general and former president of A&M University is located on the campus of [[Texas A&M University]]. In August 2017 the Chancellor of the university, John Sharp, confirmed that the university will not be removing the statue from the campus.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.dallasnews.com/news/higher-education/2017/08/21/amid-debate-confederate-monuments-texas-am-will-remove-sul-ross-statue|title=Amid debate over Confederate monuments, Texas A&M will not remove Sul Ross statue|date=August 21, 2017|work=[[The Dallas Morning News]]|access-date=August 28, 2017|language=en}}</ref> * [[Corpus Christi, Texas|Corpus Christi]]: ''Queen of the Sea'' (1914; restored 1990), [[bas-relief]] by [[Pompeo Coppini]]; UDC-sponsored Confederate memorial featuring an allegorical female figure – representing Corpus Christie – holding keys of success while receiving blessings from Mother Earth and Father Neptune, who are standing next to her.<ref name="Morris Little"/> "Coppini was abhorrent of war", and in ''Queen of the Sea'' "he crafted a sculpture that symbolized peace and captured the spirit of Corpus Christi".<ref>{{cite news|last=King|first=Jeff|title=Queen of the Sea – A Spirited Sculpture|url=http://thebendmag.com/community/history/queen-of-the-sea-%E2%80%93-a-spirited-sculpture/|work=The Bend|access-date=September 30, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171001031311/http://thebendmag.com/community/history/queen-of-the-sea-%E2%80%93-a-spirited-sculpture/|archive-date=October 1, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[El Paso, Texas|El Paso]]: ** Hometown of Texas CSA Capt. James W. Magoffin Monument (1964) ** CSA Maj. Simeon Hart Monument (1964) * [[Farmersville, Texas|Farmersville]]: Confederate Soldier Monument (1917), Farmersville City Park<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMM8EY_Confederate_Memorial_Farmersville_TX|title=Confederate Memorial – Farmersville, TX|website=Waymarking|access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref> * [[Fort Worth, Texas|Fort Worth]]: Confederate Soldier Memorial (1939), Oakwood Cemetery<ref name="Morris Little"/> * [[Gainesville, Texas|Gainesville]] Confederate Heroes Statue (1908) in Leonard Park<ref>{{cite web|url=http://sonsofconfederateveterans.blogspot.com/2012/02/statue-to-be-restored-in-texas.html|title=Sons of Confederate Veterans: Statue to Be Restored in Texas|last=Rand|first=Chuck|date=February 21, 2012|website=Sonsofconfederateveterans.blogspot.com|access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://collections.si.edu/search/tag/tagDoc.htm?recordID=siris_ari_326107&hlterm=confederate|title=Confederate Heroes, (sculpture)|date=1993|website=Art Inventories Catalog, Smithsonian American Art Museums|access-date=September 5, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170905142734/http://collections.si.edu/search/tag/tagDoc.htm?recordID=siris_ari_326107&hlterm=confederate|archive-date=September 5, 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Gonzales, Texas|Gonzales]]: Confederate Soldiers' Monument, Confederate Square. Dedicated on June 3, 1909. To "our Confederate dead."<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.gonzalesinquirer.com/news/confederate-monument-rededicated-turns/article_5cfafc77-3e32-5246-8e61-85a18d437833.html|title=Confederate Monument rededicated, turns 100|last=Maxwell|first=Nikki|date=July 16, 2009|work=Gonzales Inquirer|access-date=August 16, 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WMGDN7_Gonzales_Confederate_Monument_Gonzales_TX|title=Gonzales Confederate Monument – Gonzales, TX – American Civil War Monuments and Memorials|website=Waymarking|access-date=August 19, 2017}}</ref> * [[Greenville, Texas|Greenville]]: Confederate Soldier Monument (1926) * [[Holliday, Texas|Holliday]]: Stonewall Jackson Camp 249 Monument (1999) * [[Houston]]: ** [[Spirit of the Confederacy]] (1908, in present location since 1925), by [[Louis Amateis]].<ref name="Morris Little"/> Erected by United Daughters of the Confederacy.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.houstontx.gov/parks/artinparks/spiritoftheconfederacy.html|title=Spirit of The Confederacy|website=Houstontx.gov|access-date=August 15, 2017}}</ref> ** [[Statue of Richard W. Dowling]], located at the entrance to [[Hermann Park]]. Erected in 1905, the monument was in front of City Hall until 1958, when it was moved to its present location. In August 2017 Andrew Schneck was arrested at the statue with bomb materials.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/post-nation/wp/2017/08/21/texas-man-charged-with-trying-to-bomb-a-confederate-statue-in-houston/|title=Texas man charged with trying to bomb a Confederate statue in Houston|last=Berman|first=Mark|date=August 21, 2017|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=September 5, 2017}}</ref> * [[Kermit, Texas|Kermit]]: Col. C.M. Winkler Monument (1963) * [[Marshall, Texas|Marshall]]: ** Confederate Capitol of Missouri Monument (1963) ** Confederate Monument (1906) ** Home of Last Texas Confederate Gov. [[Pendleton Murrah]] Monument (1963) * Miami: Col. O.M. Roberts Monument (1963) [[File:Palestine August 2017 46 (John H. Reagan Monument).jpg|thumb|right|[[John Henninger Reagan|John H. Reagan]] Memorial in Palestine, Texas. The allegorical figure seated beneath Reagan represents the [[Lost Cause of the Confederacy]].<ref name="Morris Little"/>]] * [[Palestine, Texas|Palestine]]: [[John Henninger Reagan|John H. Reagan]] Memorial (1911) by [[Pompeo Coppini]]<ref name="Morris Little"/> * [[Sabine Pass, Texas|Sabine Pass]]: [[Sabine Pass Battleground State Historic Site]]. Where the Sabine River enters the Gulf of Mexico, 47 Confederate men held off a large Union attack, destroying two Union boats and capturing hundreds of prisoners. Today the site is managed by the [[Texas Historical Commission]], and it hosts battle [[American Civil War reenactment|reenactments]] in honor of the Confederate soldiers. * [[Sherman, Texas|Sherman]]: Confederate Soldier Monument (1897) * [[Tomball, Texas|Tomball]]: Confederate Powder Mill Marker Monument (1966) * [[Victoria, Texas|Victoria]]: Confederate Monument (1912). [[Pompeo Coppini]]'s statue, called ''The Last Stand'', and sometimes ''The Firing Line'', sits in [[DeLeon Plaza and Bandstand|De Leon Plaza]]. The inscription reads: "On civilization's height / Immutable they stand". The $5,000 cost was raised by the [[United Daughters of the Confederacy]].
====Private monuments==== [[File:Confederate Veterans Memorial Plaza, Palestine, Texas.jpg|thumb|right|Confederate Veterans Memorial Plaza, Palestine, Texas]] * [[Austin, Texas|Austin]]: Confederate monument, [[Oakwood Cemetery (Austin, Texas)|Oakwood Cemetery]]. Erected in 2016 by the Sons of Confederate Veterans.<ref>{{cite news |newspaper=[[Austin American-Statesman]] |title=Even as Confederate monuments fell, a new one rose in Austin |first=Michael |last=Barnes |date=August 25, 2018 |url=https://www.mystatesman.com/news/local/even-confederate-monuments-fell-new-one-rose-austin/r43uDZbhUzvPlk7wV93pvJ/}}</ref> * [[Belton, Texas|Belton]]: Monument to Confederate Sargeant Jacob Hemphill. Erected 2016 by Sons of Confederate Veterans.<ref name=SCV>{{cite web |title=Monuments |author=Sons of Confederate Veterans, Texas Division |author-link=Sons of Confederate Veterans |date=2018 |access-date=August 7, 2018 |url=http://scvtexas.org/Monuments.html |archive-date=August 9, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180809153016/http://scvtexas.org/Monuments.html |url-status=dead }}</ref> * [[Crowley, Texas|Crowley]]: "Confederate Veterans Memorial Monument honoring The Confederate Veterans of Crowley and the surrounding area interred at the Crowley Cemetery." Erected 2011 by the Sons of Confederate Veterans.<ref name=SCV /> * [[Hempstead, Texas|Hempstead]]: The Liendo Plantation was a center for Confederate recruiting efforts and held Union prisoners during the war. Now it holds battle reenactments and demonstrations of Civil War era Confederate life at its annual Civil War Weekend. * [[Orange, Texas|Orange]]: The [[Confederate Memorial of the Wind]], located on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive, but visible from [[I-10]], has been under construction since 2013, and will be the largest Confederate monument built since 1916, according to the [[Sons of Confederate Veterans]].<ref name=Politico>{{cite web|last=Savage|first=John|url=https://www.politico.com/magazine/story/2016/08/texas-confederacy-rising-again-214159/|title=Where the Confederacy Is Rising Again|website=Politico.com|date=August 10, 2016|access-date=May 23, 2017}}</ref> A center stone ring is held aloft by 13 pillars, one for each state that seceded. There are twenty commemorative flagpoles. * [[Palestine, Texas|Palestine]]: Confederate Veterans Memorial Plaza (2013), funded by the Sons of the Confederate Veterans<ref>{{cite news|last=Vernon|first=Cheril|date=April 13, 2013|title=Confederate Veterans Memorial Plaza Dedicated in Palestine|url=http://www.palestineherald.com/archives/confederate-veterans-memorial-plaza-dedicated-in-palestine/article_0ce22066-47ad-51e2-8e2a-9b0234b8271b.html|work=Palestine Herald-Press}}</ref>
===Inhabited places=== ====Counties==== {{Further|List of counties in Texas}} * [[Brewster County, Texas|Brewster County]] (1887) named for [[Henry Percy Brewster]] * [[Culberson County, Texas|Culberson County]] (1911) named for CSA Lieutenant Colonel [[David B. Culberson]] * [[Donley County, Texas|Donley County]] (1882) named for [[Stockton P. Donley]], a lawyer who enlisted in the Confederate Army. Just after the war in 1866, he was elected to the Supreme Court of Texas, but was removed by the Reconstruction military government. * [[Ector County, Texas|Ector County]] (1891) named for CSA General [[Mathew Ector]], who later served as a judge. * [[Foard County, Texas|Foard County]] (1891) named for Robert L. Foard, an attorney and Confederate Major.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://tshaonline.org/handbook/online/articles/ffo01|title=Foard, Robert Levi|first=JJackson, Charles|last=Christopher|date=June 12, 2010|website=tshaonline.org|access-date=September 4, 2017}}</ref> * [[Gray County, Texas|Gray County]] (1902) named for [[Peter W. Gray]], a lawyer and legislator who attended the 1861 Texas State Secession Convention, and voted to leave the union. Shortly after, Gray was elected to the Confederate House of Representatives. * [[Gregg County, Texas|Gregg County]] (1873) named for CSA General [[John Gregg (CSA)|John Gregg]], who was a delegate to the Texas Secession Convention in 1861 and was elected to represent Texas in the [[Provisional Confederate Congress]] in Montgomery, Alabama, and later in Richmond, Virginia. He was killed in action at the [[Siege of Petersburg]] in 1864. * [[Hemphill County, Texas|Hemphill County]] and [[Hemphill, Texas|Hemphill]] city (1877) named for [[John Hemphill (senator)|John Hemphill]], Chief Justice of the Texas Supreme Court from 1841 to 1858 until he was elected to the US Senate when [[Sam Houston]] refused to support secession. He was expelled from the Senate when Texas seceded in 1861 and then represented Texas in the [[Provisional Confederate Congress]]. * [[Hood County, Texas|Hood County]] (1866) named for CSA General [[John Bell Hood]] * [[Jeff Davis County, Texas|Jeff Davis County]] (1887) named for [[Jefferson Davis]]. * [[Lee County, Texas|Lee County]] (1874) named for Robert E. Lee. * [[Lubbock County, Texas|Lubbock County]] named for CSA colonel [[Thomas Saltus Lubbock]] * [[Madison County, Texas|Madison County]]: In 2020 County Commissioners designated April as Confederate History and Heritage Month.<ref name=Madison/> * [[McCulloch County, Texas|McCulloch County]], for CSA general [[Benjamin McCulloch]] * [[Ochiltree County, Texas|Ochiltree County]] (1889) named for secessionist politician [[William Beck Ochiltree]]. * [[Oldham County, Texas|Oldham County]] (1881) named for [[William Simpson Oldham Sr.]], a Confederate politician. * [[Randall County, Texas|Randall County]] (1889) named for CSA Brig. Gen. [[Horace Randal]]. * [[Reagan County, Texas|Reagan County]] (1903) named for CSA [[Postmaster General]] [[John H. Reagan]]. * [[Reeves County, Texas|Reeves County]] (1884) named for CSA officer [[George R. Reeves]]. * [[Roberts County, Texas|Roberts County]] (1889) named for [[Oran Milo Roberts]], who was unanimously elected president of the 1861 Secession Convention, a meeting that he had been influential in calling. In 1862 he resigned the Supreme Court seat and was elected colonel of the Eleventh Texas infantry in the Confederate Army. He was Governor of Texas from 1879 to 1883. * [[Scurry County, Texas|Scurry County]] (1884) named for secessionist and CSA Gen. [[William Read Scurry]]. * [[Starr County, Texas|Starr County]] Confederate official [[James Harper Starr]] * [[Stephens County, Texas|Stephens County]], originally Buchanan County, for U.S. President [[James Buchanan]], in 1861 was renamed for CSA Vice-President [[Alexander Stephens]]. * [[Stonewall County, Texas|Stonewall County]] (1888) named for CSA General Stonewall Jackson. * [[Sutton County, Texas|Sutton County]] (1890) named for CSA officer [[John S. Sutton]]. * [[Terrell County, Texas|Terrell County]] (1905) named for CSA acting General [[Alexander W. Terrell]]. * [[Terry County, Texas|Terry County]] (1904) named for CSA Col. [[Benjamin Franklin Terry]] . * [[Tom Green County, Texas|Tom Green County]] (1875) named for CSA Brig. Gen. [[Thomas Green (general)|Thomas Green]], KIA (killed in action). * [[Upton County, Texas|Upton County]] (1910) named for brothers John C. and William F. Upton, both colonels in the Confederate Army. * [[Val Verde County, Texas|Val Verde County]] (1885) Confederate victory in the [[Battle of Val Verde]] * [[Winkler County, Texas|Winkler County]] (1910) named for CSA Col. Clinton M. Winkler.
====Municipalities==== * City of [[Cleburne, Texas|Cleburne]] (1871) * City of [[Fort Davis, Texas|Fort Davis]]: named for Jefferson Davis. * City of [[Granbury, Texas|Granbury]] (1887) * City of [[Hemphill, Texas|Hemphill]]: named for judge and Confederate politician [[John Hemphill (Senator)|John Hemphill]] (see Hemphill County as well) * City of [[Lubbock, Texas|Lubbock]], named for [[Thomas Saltus Lubbock]], a colonel in the Confederate Army * City of [[Robert Lee, Texas|Robert Lee]] * City of [[Stonewall, Texas|Stonewall]]: named for Stonewall Jackson.
===Museums=== {{See also|Texas Confederate Museum}} * [[Columbus, Texas|Columbus]]: [[Confederate Memorial Museum]]. Located in an unusual building, built in 1883, that formerly was the town's [[water tower]], and in the base its [[Fire Station]]. It is a [[Recorded Texas Historic Landmark]]. [[Curator|Curated]] by UDC, which purchased the building from the town in 1926, when its water system was modernized and the unneeded tower building put up for sale.<ref>{{cite web |title=Confederate Museum in a Water Tower |author=The Team at roadsideamerica.com |url=https://www.roadsideamerica.com/story/29968 |access-date=November 25, 2018}}</ref> According to the UDC it holds a collection of artifacts from Civil War soldiers of Columbus and [[Colorado County, Texas|Colorado County]]. It is a [[Recorded Texas Historic Landmark]], dedicated, according to UDC, to the veterans of all wars, although the museum holds only Confederate artifacts.<ref>{{cite web |title=Columbus: Confederate Memorial Museum |author=Texas Historical Commission |url=http://texasindependencetrail.com/plan-your-adventure/historic-sites-and-cities/sites/confederate-memorial-museum |access-date=November 24, 2018 |author-link=Texas Historical Commission |archive-date=November 27, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181127195443/http://texasindependencetrail.com/plan-your-adventure/historic-sites-and-cities/sites/confederate-memorial-museum |url-status=dead }}</ref> * [[White Settlement, Texas|White Settlement]]: [[Texas Civil War Museum]], opened 2006. It is pro-Confederacy,<ref name=Lee>{{cite news |title=Trip to Texas Civil War Museum shows why Dallas should never send its Robert E. Lee statue there |newspaper=[[Dallas News]] |first=Robert |last=Wilonsky |date=April 24, 2018 |url=https://www.dallasnews.com/opinion/commentary/2018/04/24/trip-texas-civil-war-museum-shows-dallas-never-send-robert-e-lee-statue}}</ref> and the UDC has permanently one of the three seats on its board. Has material from the former [[Texas Confederate Museum]] in Austin, which closed in 1988.
===Parks=== * [[Confederate Reunion Grounds State Historic Site]], [[Limestone County, Texas|Limestone County]], near [[Mexia, Texas]] * [[Davis Mountains State Park]] (1938) named for the mountain range * [[Davis Mountains]] (geographic feature in West Texas around and named for Fort Davis) * [[Fort Worth, Texas|Fort Worth]]: Jefferson Davis Park.<ref name="Kennedy">{{cite news |title=A Confederate flag display comes down. But it was a tiny one, and the mayor wonders – why now? |first=Bud |last=Kennedy |newspaper=[[Fort Worth Star-Telegram]] |date=August 17, 2017 |url=http://www.star-telegram.com/opinion/opn-columns-blogs/bud-kennedy/article167883357.html}}</ref> -now Unity Park. * [[Holliday, Texas|Holliday]]: Stonewall Jackson Campground * [[Lakeside, Tarrant County, Texas|Lakeside, Tarrant County]]: Confederate Park. The two Confederate flags displayed on each side of the park's marker were removed by the Texas Department of Public Transportation in 2017. Marker text:{{blockquote|Site of Confederate Park // Local businessman [[Khleber Miller Van Zandt|Khleber M. Van Zandt]] organized the Robert E. Lee Camp of the United Confederate Veterans in 1889. By 1900 it boasted more than 700 members. The Club received a 25-year charter to create the Confederate Park Association in 1901, then purchased {{convert|373|acres}} near this site for the "recreation, refuge and relief of Confederate soldiers" and their families. Opening events included a picnic for veterans and families on June 20, 1902, and a statewide reunion September 8–12, 1902, with 3,500 attendees. The park thrived as a center for the civil and social activities on Texas Confederate organizations. By 1924 the numbers [ [[sic]] ] of surviving veterans had greatly diminished, and the Confederate Park Association dissolved when its charter expired in 1926.}}<ref name="Kennedy" /> * [[Palestine, Texas|Palestine]]: [[John Henninger Reagan|John H. Reagan]] Park
