Lee Road and Jeff Davis Avenue were renamed, the city's Equity Office recommended changing the names of seven more streets: ***Littlefield Street. Commemorates [[George W. Littlefield]], a CSA officer.<ref name="Austin"/> ***Tom Green Street<ref name="Austin"/> ***Sneed Cove, named for [[Richard A. Sneed]], Confederate soldier and co-founder of the [[Ku Klux Klan]].<ref name="Austin"/> ***Reagan Hill Drive<ref name="Austin"/> ***Dixie Drive<ref name="Austin"/> ***Plantation Road<ref name="Austin"/> ***Confederate Avenue<ref name="Austin">{{cite web |last1=Winkle |first1=Kate |title=Austin outlines Confederate streets slated for renaming |url=https://www.kxan.com/news/local/austin/austin-outlines-confederate-streets-slated-for-renaming/ |website=KXAN Austin |date=July 27, 2018}}</ref> * Conroe: ** Beauregard Drive ** Jubal Early Lane ** Stonewall Jackson Drive * El Paso: Robert E. Lee Road – now Buffalo Soldier Road * Hamilton: Stonewall Jackson Road * Hillsboro: Confederate Drive * Hemphill: ** Confederate Street ** Stonewall Street * Holliday: Stonewall Road * Houston: ** Robert E. Lee Road – now Unison Road. ** Robert Lee Road ** Sul Ross St, Named for [[Lawrence Sullivan Ross]], Confederate general and former president of Texas A&M University. ** Tuam Street, a major artery named for CSA Gen. Dowling's birthplace, [[Tuam]], Ireland. * Hunt: Robert E. Lee Road * Jacksonville: Jeff Davis Street * Kermit East Winkler Street * [[Lakeside, Tarrant County, Texas|Lakeside]] Confederate Park Road * League City: Jeb Stuart Drive * Levelland: Robert Lee Street * Liberty: Confederate Street * Livingston: Robert E. Lee Road * Marshall: ** Jeff Davis Street ** Stonewall Drive * Missouri City ** Beauregard Court ** Bedford Forrest Drive ** Breckinridge Court ** Confederate Drive ** Pickett Place * Richmond: ** Jeb Stuart Drive ** Jeff Davis Drive ** Stonewall Drive * Ridgley: Bedford Forrest Lane * Roma: Robert Lee Avenue * San Antonio: ** Beauregard Street ** Robert E. Lee Drive * Sterling City: Robert Lee Highway * Sweetwater: Robert Lee Street * Tyler: ** Jeb Stuart Drive ** Jeff Davis Drive * Victoria: Robert E. Lee Road {{div col end}} Note: "There are similarly named streets in towns and cities across east Texas, notably Port Arthur and Beaumont, as well as memorials to Dowling and the Davis Guards, not least at Sabine Pass, where the battleground is now preserved as a state park"
===Schools=== * [[Abilene, Texas|Abilene]]: ** Jackson Elementary School – now Dr. Jose Alcorta Sr. Elementary School. ** Johnston Elementary School – now Eugene Purcell Elementary School. ** Lee Elementary School (1961) -now Robert and Sammye Stafford Elementary School. * [[Amarillo, Texas|Amarillo]]: ** Lee Elementary School (was renamed Park Hills Elementary School in 2019) ** [[Tascosa High School]]. Confederacy iconography was dropped in 1974. The school dropped its mascot, Johnny Reb, and stopped playing "Dixie" as their fight song. The Dixieland Singers became the Freedom Singers. Miss Southern Belle became Tascosa Belle. The "Rebel" nickname remained, but other ties to the Civil War disappeared.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.chron.com/news/houston-texas/texas/article/A-brief-history-on-controversial-mascots-at-Texas-6387176.php|title=A brief history on controversial mascots at Texas high schools|last=Levin|first=Matt|date=July 15, 2015|work=Houston Chronicle|access-date=August 31, 2017}}</ref> * [[Austin, Texas|Austin]]: ** [[John H. Reagan High School (Austin, Texas)|John H. Reagan Early College High School]] (1965) – now Northeast Early College High School. ** [[William B. Travis High School (Austin, Texas)|William B. Travis High School]], home of the "Rebels".<ref>{{Cite thesis|last=Dirickson|first=Perry|title=School Spirit or School Hate: The Confederate Battle Flag, Texas High Schools, and Memory, 1953–2002|date=2006|publisher=[[University of North Texas]]|url=https://digital.library.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metadc5467/m2/1/high_res_d/thesis.pdf}}</ref> Dropped "Dixie" as its song in 2012.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.statesman.com/article/20121016/NEWS/310169633|title=One local high school drops 'Dixie,' while another keeps it|website=www.statesman.com|access-date=September 10, 2017}}</ref> ** Zachary Taylor Fulmore Middle School – now Sarah Beth Lively Middle School. ** [[Sidney Lanier High School]]: [[Sidney Lanier]], poet of the Confederacy, was a private in the Confederate Army.<ref name="austinchronicle.com">{{cite news |url=https://www.austinchronicle.com/news/2017-08-18/a-guide-to-confederate-monuments-in-austin/ |newspaper=[[Austin Chronicle]] |date=August 18, 2017 |access-date=November 10, 2017 |title=Guide to Confederate Monuments in Austin}}</ref> -now Juan Navarro High School. ** Johnston High School: Named for [[Albert Sidney Johnston]], Confederate general killed in the [[Battle of Shiloh]]. The school closed in 2008; [[Eastside Memorial High School]] is now (2017) at that location.<ref name="austinchronicle.com"/> * [[Bryan, Texas|Bryan]]: ** [[Bryan Independent School District#Elementary schools|Sul Ross Elementary School]]: Named for [[Lawrence Sullivan Ross]], Confederate general and former president of Texas A&M University.<ref>{{Cite web|title=School Profile – About Us – Ross Elementary|url=https://ross.bryanisd.org/apps/pages/index.jsp?uREC_ID=178658&type=d&pREC_ID=375368|access-date=June 11, 2020|website=ross.bryanisd.org|archive-date=June 11, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200611201102/https://ross.bryanisd.org/apps/pages/index.jsp?uREC_ID=178658&type=d&pREC_ID=375368|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Buda, Texas|Buda]]: ** [[Jack C. Hays High School]]. The school uses the "Rebel" nickname for its athletic teams.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://kxan.com/2015/07/10/hays-hs-looks-at-whether-rebels-mascot-needs-changing/|title=Hays HS looks at whether 'Rebels' mascot needs changing|last=News|first=KXAN|date=July 11, 2015|website=KXAN.com|access-date=September 3, 2017}}</ref> Mascot "Colonel Jack" no longer has a Confederate flag belt buckle but still dresses in grey. The school dropped the Confederate flag as an official symbol in 2010 and the school district banned it from all district property in 2012.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://kylelife.com/2015/07/28/hays-high-school-loses-dixie/|title=Hays High School Loses "Dixie"|date=July 28, 2015|website=KyleLife.com|access-date=September 4, 2017}}</ref> In 2015 it replaced the school song "Dixie". * [[Baytown, Texas|Baytown]]: ** [[Lee College]] (1934) Lee College dropped “Rebels” as their nickname/mascot, and rebranded as “Navigators” in 2022.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lee.edu/news/2022/mascot.php|title=A Focused Future: Lee Rebrands Mascot to Navigators | Lee College|website=www.lee.edu}}</ref> ** [[Lee High School (Baytown, Texas)|Lee High School]] (1928) [[File:Stonewall Jackson Elementary School, Dallas, Texas.jpg|thumb|right|Stonewall Jackson Elementary School, Dallas]] * [[Dallas]]: **[[Albert Sidney Johnston]] Elementary School – now Cedar Crest Elementary School. ** John H. Reagan Elementary School – now Bishop Arts STEAM Academy. ** Robert E. Lee Elementary School – now Geneva Heights Elementary School. ** Stonewall Jackson Elementary School (1939) – now Mockingbird Elementary School. **Sidney Lanier Expressive Arts Vanguard Elementary School – now Jesús Moroles Expressive Arts Vanguard Elementary School. * [[Denton, Texas|Denton]]: Lee Elementary School (1988), renamed Alice Moore Alexander Elementary School in 2017 * [[Eagle Pass, Texas|Eagle Pass]]: Robert E. Lee Elementary School – now Juan N. Seguin Elementary School. * [[Edinburg, Texas|Edinburg]]: Lee Elementary School * [[El Paso, Texas|El Paso]]: Lee Elementary School -now Sunrise Mountain Elementary School. * [[Evadale, Texas|Evadale]]: [[Evadale High School]]. The school uses a Confederate flag-inspired crest. Its athletic teams are nicknamed the "Rebels".<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.myhighplains.com/news/state/texas-high-school-defends-rebels-confederate-flag/210630316|title=Texas High School Defends Rebels Confederate Flag|date=July 13, 2015|work=Myhighplains.com|access-date=September 3, 2017|language=en-US}}</ref> * [[Fort Davis, Texas|Fort Davis]]: ** Fort Davis AEC School ** [[Fort Davis High School]] * [[Gainesville, Texas|Gainesville]]: Robert E. Lee Intermediate School – now Gainesville Intermediate School. * [[Grand Prairie, Texas|Grand Prairie]]: Robert E. Lee Elementary School (1948) -now Delmas F. Morton Elementary School. * [[Houston]]: ** [[Davis High School (Houston)|Davis High School]] (1926). In 2016, the Houston school board voted to rename the school.<ref name=Renames>{{cite news |title=District Renames Seven Schools |newspaper=[[Austin American-Statesman]] |date=May 14, 2016 |page=B5 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/31481967/renaming_schools_removing_confederates/}}</ref> -now Northside High School. ** Dowling Middle School (1968), named for CSA Maj. [[Richard W. Dowling]]. In 2016, the Houston school board voted to rename the school.<ref name=Renames/> -now Audrey H. Lawson Middle School. ** Thomas "Stonewall" Jackson Middle School to Yolanda Black Navarro Middle School of Excellence. ** [[Sydney Lanier]], Confederate poet and soldier. In 2016, the Houston school board voted to rename the school.<ref name=Renames/> -now Bob Lanier Middle School. ** Lee High School to Margaret Long Wisdom High School. ** [[John H. Reagan High School (Houston, Texas)|John H. Reagan High School]] (1926). In 2016, the Houston school board voted to rename the school.<ref name=Renames/> -now Heights High School. ** Johnston Middle School (1959), named for [[Albert Sidney Johnston]]. In 2016, the Houston school board voted to rename the school.<ref name=Renames/> -now Meyerland Performing and Visual Arts Middle School. * [[Marshall, Texas|Marshall]]: Robert E. Lee Elementary School – closed in 2018. * [[Midland, Texas|Midland]]: ** [[Lee Freshman High School]] (1961) – now Midland Freshman High School. ** [[Lee High School (Midland, Texas)|Lee High School]] (1961). The school's athletic teams are nicknamed the "Rebels". Lee High School had used the Confederate flag in the past.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.dallasnews.com/news/news/2015/08/01/midland-lee-alum-no-longer-proud-of-schools-rebel-mascot|title=Midland Lee alum no longer proud of school's rebel mascot|date=August 1, 2015|work=Dallas News|access-date=August 31, 2017|language=en}}</ref> -now Legacy High School. * [[North Richland Hills]], home of the [[Richland High School (Texas)|Richland High School]] "Rebels" and "Dixie Belles". The school mascot is "Johnny Rebel".<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.mysanantonio.com/news/local/article/Texas-high-school-defends-Confederate-mascot-as-6386001.php|title=Texas high school defends Confederate mascot as coach gets slap for racial remarks|last=Fechter|first=Joshua|date=July 15, 2015|work=San Antonio Express-News|access-date=August 31, 2017}}</ref> * [[Port Arthur, Texas|Port Arthur]]: Lee Elementary School (1959)-now Lakeview Elementary School. * [[Robert Lee, Texas|Robert Lee]]: ** Robert Lee Elementary School ** Robert Lee High School * [[Rosenberg, Texas|Rosenberg]]: B. F. Terry High School. Named for Confederate hero [[Benjamin Franklin Terry]]. * [[San Angelo, Texas|San Angelo]]: Lee Middle School (1949)-now Lone Star Middle School. * [[San Antonio]]: [[Robert E. Lee High School (San Antonio)|Robert E. Lee High School]] (1958). After voting against a name change in 2015, the school board voted in August 2017 to change the name of the school.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Caruba|first=Lauren|date=August 30, 2017|title=NEISD board votes to drop name of Lee High School|agency=San Antonio Express-News|url=http://www.mysanantonio.com/news/education/article/NEISD-board-votes-to-drop-name-of-Lee-High-School-12159774.php|website=MySanAntonio.com|publisher=Hearst Communications, Inc.|access-date=September 7, 2017|quote=North East Independent School District trustees – prodded by a fervent national debate over memorializing the Confederacy that some said had become a distraction for students – voted 7–0 Tuesday to change the name of Robert E. Lee High School.}}</ref> In October, district trustees voted 5-2 to name the school Legacy of Educational Excellence, or LEE High School.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.mysanantonio.com/news/education/article/Lee-High-School-to-keep-school-colors-mascot-12354817.php|title=Lee High School to keep school colors, mascot|work=San Antonio Express-News|access-date=November 20, 2017}}</ref> Its mascot is currently the Volunteer and the school colors are red and grey. Its pep squad, currently called the Southern Belles, were once called the Confederates. Its varsity dance team and junior varsity drill team are respectively named the Rebel Rousers and Dixie Drillers.<ref name=":24" /> * [[Stonewall, Texas|Stonewall]]: Stonewall Elementary School * [[Tyler, Texas|Tyler]]: ** Hubbard Middle School (1964), named for Confederate Col. [[Richard B. Hubbard]] ** [[Robert E. Lee High School (Tyler, Texas)|Robert E. Lee High School]] (1958). Called "the city's most radioactive Confederate symbol," the possible renaming of the school was the subject of active discussion at meetings in August and September 2017. In 1970, as a result of a statewide federal desegregation order, the school had to get rid of "its Confederate-themed mascot (the Rebels), fight song ("Dixie"), and prized Confederate flag (so large that it required twenty boys to carry). Its beloved Rebel Guard, a squadron of boys handpicked by an American-history teacher to dress in replica Confederate uniforms at football games and fire a cannon named Ole Spirit after touchdowns, had to find a new name. Same for the Rebelettes drill team."<ref>{{cite magazine |title=To Be Black at Robert E. Lee High School |date=September 19, 2017 |last=Raja |first=Tasneem |magazine=The Atlantic |url=https://www.newyorker.com/news/news-desk/what-is-it-like-to-be-a-black-student-at-robert-e-lee-high-school?silverid=MzEwMTkwMTIwMzEyS0 |access-date=September 21, 2017 }}</ref> -now Tyler Legacy High School.
=== Other memorials === * [[Granbury, Texas|Granbury]]: The city holds an annual General Granbury's Birthday Bash dedicated to Confederate General [[Hiram B. Granbury]]
==Utah== {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Utah}} *[[Dixie (Utah)|Dixie]], a region of [[Washington County, Utah]]; The first president of the Washington [[Stake (Latter Day Saints)|Stake]] was Robert Dockery Covington, a slave owner from North Carolina and Mississippi. There were other Southern settlers; crops such as tobacco and cotton would grow there.<ref>{{cite book |first=Andrew |last=Larson |title=I Was Called to "Dixie:" The Virgin River Basin: Unique Experiences on Mormon Pioneering |publisher=Dixie College Foundation |location=St. George, Utah |year=1992 |page=185}}</ref> *[[Dixie National Forest]] * Dixie State University, The former name of [[Utah Tech University]] [[St. George, Utah]]. * Large word "DIXIE"<ref>{{Citation|title=English: The St. George Utah Sugarloaf newly painted with the word "Dixie." |date=1914-12-31 |url=https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:DIXIE_writtten_over_1914.jpg |access-date=2022-06-09}}</ref> painted on sandstone cliffs nearby, painted yearly by [[Dixie High School (Utah)|Dixie High School]]. St. George Utah *[[Dixie Technical College]]
==Vermont== {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Vermont}}
==Virginia== {{Main|List of Confederate monuments and memorials in Virginia}} {{further|Virginia in the American Civil War}} {{See also|List of memorials and monuments at Arlington National Cemetery|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Virginia}}
{{As of|2020|June|24}}, there were at least 241 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[Virginia]], more than in any other state.<ref name=":21">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/aponline/2017/08/22/us/ap-us-teaching-the-civil-war.html|title=Civil War Lessons Often Depend on Where the Classroom Is|agency=[[Associated Press]]|date=August 22, 2017|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=August 31, 2017|language=en-US|issn=0362-4331}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/virginia-politics/in-the-former-capital-of-the-confederacy-the-debate-over-statues-is-personal-and-painful/2017/08/27/87002bc4-8998-11e7-a94f-3139abce39f5_story.html|title=In the former capital of the Confederacy, the debate over statues is personal and painful|last=Schneider|first=Gregory S|date=August 27, 2017|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=August 31, 2017}}</ref> Virginia also has numerous schools, highways, roads and other public infrastructure named for Confederates. Some have been removed since. [[Lee-Jackson Day]] ceased to be a State holiday in 2020.
==Washington State== {{See also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Washington (state)}}
{{As of|2020|June|24}}, only one public space contains a Confederate connected monument in [[Washington (state)|Washington]].
* Bellingham: [[Pickett House (Bellingham, Washington)|Pickett House]], listed on the [[National Register of Historic Places]]. [[File:Jefferson Davis Park 2-4 (28233582279).jpg|thumb|3rd Flag of the Confederacy and the Bonnie Blue Flag at the Jefferson Davis Park, 2018]] At least two private properties contain a Confederate memorial or fly a CSA flag: * [[Clark County, Washington|Clark County]]: Near [[Ridgefield, Washington|Ridgefield]] is [[Jefferson Davis Park, Washington|Jefferson Davis Park]] (2007), established by the [[Sons of Confederate Veterans|SCV]] to hold the [[Jefferson Davis Highway|Jeff Davis Highway]] markers from Blaine and Vancouver. Flags of the Confederacy are also displayed there.<ref>{{citation|title=Confederate symbols also blight the Northwest|author=Knute Berger|date=June 22, 2015|publisher=[[Crosscut.com]]|url=http://crosscut.com/2015/06/confederate-symbols-also-blight-the-northwest/}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://scvpacnw.wordpress.com/jefferson-davis-park/|title=Jefferson Davis Park|date=June 27, 2014|website=wordpress.com|access-date=August 21, 2017}}</ref> * Seattle: [[United Confederate Veterans Memorial]], [[Lake View Cemetery (Seattle)|Lake View Cemetery]]. Erected in 1926 by United Daughters of the Confederacy.<ref>{{cite news|title=Seattle's own monument to the Confederacy was erected on Capitol Hill in 1926 – and it's still there|date=August 16, 2017|newspaper=[[The Seattle Times]]|first=Christine | last=Clarridge|url=http://www.seattletimes.com/nation-world/nation-politics/seattles-own-monument-to-the-confederacy-was-erected-on-capitol-hill-in-1926-and-its-still-there/}}</ref> In October 2018, the Make It Right Project put up a billboard in Seattle, saying: "Hey Seattle, there's a Confederate Memorial in your backyard".<ref>{{cite news |first=Zosha |last=Millman |newspaper=[[Seattle Post-Intelligencer]] |date=November 8, 2018 |url=https://www.seattlepi.com/seattlenews/article/Confederate-Statue-Seattle-Capitol-Hill-Cemetery-13375703.php |title=Campaign to take down Seattle's confederate memorial gets a billboard}}</ref> After years of calls to remove the monument and numerous acts of vandalism against it, the monument was toppled by unknown persons, apparently on July 3, 2020. In the process, the lower ends of both formerly vertical columns were broken in multiple places.<ref>{{cite web |title=Huge Confederate monument toppled at Seattle's Lake View Cemetery |url=https://komonews.com/news/local/confederate-monument-toppled-at-seattles-lake-view-cemetery |website=KOMO News |date=July 4, 2020 |access-date=July 4, 2020}}</ref> The wreckage was discovered by visitors to the cemetery on July 4.
==West Virginia== {{As of|2020}} there were 21 public spaces with Confederate monuments in [[West Virginia]].
===State capitol=== * Bust of [[Stonewall Jackson]] (born in West Virginia, at that time part of Virginia) by [[Bryant Baker]], located "in the rotunda of the [[West Virginia State Capitol]]." (1959)<ref name="auto3">{{cite web|url=http://www.waymarking.com/waymarks/WM8FBF_Thomas_J_Stonewall_Jackson_Charleston_West_Virginia|title=Thomas J. "Stonewall" Jackson – Charleston, West Virginia – Statues of Historic Figures on Waymarking.com|website=www.waymarking.com}}</ref> * Statue of Stonewall Jackson by [[Moses Ezekiel]]<ref name="Ezekiel, Moses Jacob p. 71">Ezekiel, Moses Jacob, Moses Jacob Ezekiel: Memoirs from the Bath of Diocletian, ed. by Joseph Gutmann and Stanley F. Chyet, Wayne State University, Detroit, 1975 p. 71</ref> on the State Capitol grounds (1910) placed by UDC Chapter No. 151<ref name="auto7">''Biennial Report of the Department of Archives and History of the State of West Virginia'', Charleston, 1911, pp. 275–79.</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Charleston's Civil War soldier statues erected with private funds |first=Rick |last=Steelhammer |date=August 22, 2017 |newspaper=[[Charleston Gazette-Mail]] |url=https://www.wvgazettemail.com/news/charleston-s-civil-war-soldier-statues-erected-with-private-funds/article_301056fc-a75d-5bb8-81a3-fd72b1c4015b.html}}</ref>
===Monuments=== {{See also|Confederate Cemetery at Lewisburg}} [[File:Plaque on base of Monument.JPG|thumb|Bronze plaque commemorating the site of Pettigrew's death.]] [[File:Confederate Memorial Romney WV 2015 06 08 01.jpg|thumb|upright|First Confederate Memorial (1867), Romney, West Virginia]]
* [[Bunker Hill, West Virginia]]: Monument marking the death of Brig.-Gen. [[James Johnston Pettigrew]], wounded on July 14, 1863, near [[Falling Waters, West Virginia|Falling Waters]] during the retreat after the [[Battle of Gettysburg]]. He died at [[Edgewood (Bunker Hill, West Virginia)|Edgewood]] on July 17, 1863.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.hmdb.org/m.asp?m=2615|title=James Johnston Pettigrew Monument Historical Marker|website=www.hmdb.org}}</ref> * [[Clarksburg, West Virginia|Clarksburg]]: Bronze [[equestrian statue]] of Stonewall Jackson created by [[Charles Keck]] (1953) by the [[United Daughters of the Confederacy]] (UDC). Jackson was born in Clarksburg. * [[Charles Town, West Virginia|Charles Town]]: Portraits of Lee and Jackson hang in the courtroom in which [[John Brown (abolitionist)|John Brown]] was tried and sentenced to death.<ref>{{cite news |title=OPINION: Finally righting a wrong at our historic courthouse |newspaper=Spirit of Jefferson |date=December 12, 2018 |url=http://www.spiritofjefferson.com/news/article_12744016-fe3a-11e8-9019-1bf8fbd8c778.html}}</ref> * [[Charleston, West Virginia|Charleston]] – See [[West Virginia State Capitol]], above. * [[Harpers Ferry, West Virginia|Harpers Ferry]]: [[Heyward Shepherd]] Monument (1931). Although Shepherd was a black freeman working for the railway when killed in [[John Brown's raid on Harper's Ferry]], the monument was erected by UDC and [[Sons of Confederate Veterans]] (SCV). They called the project the "Faithful Slave Memorial" for many years and saw it as a way to emphasize their idea that blacks enjoyed being slaves and that men like Shepherd were victims of those seeking to free slaves.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Johnson|first1=Mary|date=1997|title=An "Ever Present Bone of Contention": The Heyward Shepherd Memorial|url=http://www.wvculture.org/history/journal_wvh/wvh56-1.html|journal=West Virginia History|volume=56|pages=1–26}}</ref> * [[Hinton, West Virginia|Hinton]]: Confederate Soldier Monument, Summers County Courthouse (dedicated May 1914)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theclio.com/web/entry?id=26028|title=Hinton Confederate Monument|website=Clio|access-date=August 25, 2017}}</ref> The base of the monument carries the inscription: "(North base:) This monument erected in honor of American valor as displayed by the Confederate soldiers from 1861 to 1865, and to perpetuate to remotest ages the patriotism and fidelity to principles of the heroes who fought and died for a lost cause. (East base:) sacred to the memory of the noble women of the Confederacy, who suffered more and lost as much, with less glory, than the Confederate soldier. (South base:) erected in the year 1914 by Camp Allen Woodrm Confederate veterans and Camp Bob Christian sons of Confederacy veterans and their friends. (West base:) This monument is dedicated to the Confederate soldiers of Greenbrier and New River valleys who followed Lee and Jackson.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://siris-artinventories.si.edu/ipac20/ipac.jsp?session=150A534R89557.3044&profile=ariall&uri=link=3100008~!183260~!3100001~!3100002&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ri=1&source=~!siartinventories&term=Outdoor+Sculpture+--+West+Virginia+--+Hinton&index=OBJEC|title=Confederate Monument|last=Unknown|date=October 24, 2017|via=siris-artinventories.si.edu Library Catalog}}</ref> * [[Lewisburg, West Virginia|Lewisburg]]: Confederate Monument (1906) The Confederate "monument was erected by the United Daughters of the Confederacy at a cost of $2,800. The monument was originally located on the campus of the [[Greenbrier College]], but moved to its present location when U.S. Route 60 was relocated."<ref>{{cite web|url=https://siris-artinventories.si.edu/ipac20/ipac.jsp?session=IF08558P17829.3377&profile=ariall&uri=link=3100008~!183265~!3100001~!3100002&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ri=1&source=~!siartinventories&term=Outdoor+Sculpture+--+West+Virginia+--+Lewisburg&index=OBJEC|title=Confederate Monument|first1=W.|last1=Sivers|first2=William Ludwell|last2=Sheppard|first3=William R.|last3=Mason|first4=William J.|last4=Strealey|date=October 24, 2017|via=siris-artinventories.si.edu Library Catalog}}</ref> It is now located on the lawn of the old public library in Lewisburg. Some residents have suggested interpretive signage for the statue.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://mountainmessenger.com/confederation-conversation-monumental-questions-arise-in-lewisburg/|title=Confederation conversation, monumental questions arise in Lewisburg|date=September 29, 2017}}</ref> The inscription on the base reads, "In memory of our Confederate dead."<ref>{{cite web|url=http://wvhistoryonview.org/catalog/wvulibraries:3862|title=Confederate Monument, Lewisburg, W. Va. – West Virginia History OnView – WVU Libraries|first=WVU|last=Libraries|website=wvhistoryonview.org|access-date=January 3, 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180612141255/http://wvhistoryonview.org/catalog/wvulibraries:3862|archive-date=June 12, 2018|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Mingo, West Virginia|Mingo]]: Confederate Soldier Monument (1913/2013) The inscription reads in part, "TO THE MEMORY OF THE CONFEDERATE SOLDIERS OF RANDOLPH COUNTY AND VICINITY THIS INCLUDES ALL SOLDIERS WHO DIED IN VALLEY MOUNTAIN"<ref>{{cite web|url=https://siris-artinventories.si.edu/ipac20/ipac.jsp?session=15085EUD91161.3417&profile=ariall&source=~!siartinventories&view=subscriptionsummary&uri=full=3100001~!310949~!1&ri=2&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ipp=20&spp=20&staffonly=&term=Outdoor+Sculpture+--+West+Virginia+--+Mingo&index=OBJEC&uindex=&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ri=2|title=Confederate Soldier|last=Unknown|date=October 24, 2017|via=siris-artinventories.si.edu Library Catalog}}</ref> * [[Parkersburg, West Virginia|Parkersburg]]: Confederate Soldier Monument, (1908) The monument was created by [[Leon Hermant]] and the inscription reads in part, " IN MEMORY OF OUR CONFEDERATE DEAD ERECTED BY PARKERSBURG CHAPTER UNITED DAUGHTERS OF CONFEDERACY"<ref>{{cite web|url=https://siris-artinventories.si.edu/ipac20/ipac.jsp?session=15085D3W5211M.3421&profile=ariall&source=~!siartinventories&view=subscriptionsummary&uri=full=3100001~!310895~!0&ri=2&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ipp=20&spp=20&staffonly=&term=Outdoor+Sculpture+--+West+Virginia+--+Parkersburg&index=OBJEC&uindex=&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ri=2|title=Confederate Soldier|first1=Leon|last1=Hermant|first2=Fred A.|last2=Lang|first3=Jules|last3=Berchem|via=siris-artinventories.si.edu Library Catalog}}</ref> * [[Romney, West Virginia|Romney]]: [[First Confederate Memorial]] (1867) Carved on the main facade are the words, "The daughters of Old Hampshire erect this tribute of affection to her heroic sons who fell in defense of Southern Rights." * [[Union, West Virginia|Union]]: Monroe County Confederate Soldier Monument (1901); marble statue inscribed "There is a true glory and a true honor. The glory of duty done, the honor of integrity of principle. R. E. Lee"<ref>{{cite web|url=https://siris-artinventories.si.edu/ipac20/ipac.jsp?session=1J085639G13Q7.3430&profile=ariall&uri=link=3100008~!183290~!3100001~!3100002&aspect=Browse&menu=search&ri=1&source=~!siartinventories&term=Outdoor+Sculpture+--+West+Virginia+--+Union&index=OBJEC|title=Monroe County Confederate Soldier|last1=Unknown|first2=Andy|last2=Dixon|via=siris-artinventories.si.edu Library Catalog}}</ref>
===Inhabited places=== * [[Bartow, West Virginia|Bartow]], initially an 1861 Confederate encampment, Camp Bartow, named for the late Confederate Colonel [[Francis S. Bartow|Francis Bartow]].<ref name=Hamil>{{cite book|last=Kenny|first=Hamill|title=West Virginia Place Names: Their Origin and Meaning, Including the Nomenclature of the Streams and Mountains|url=http://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=mdp.39015009099824;view=1up;seq=125|year=1945|publisher=The Place Name Press|location=Piedmont, WV}}</ref>{{rp|97}} * [[Harding, West Virginia|Harding]], named for CSA Maj. French Harding.<ref name=Hamil />{{rp|297}} * [[Hotchkiss, West Virginia|Hotchkiss]], named for Stonewall Jackson's map maker [[Jedediah Hotchkiss]] * [[Linden, West Virginia|Linden]], named for CSA Capt. Charles Linden Broadus.<ref name=Hamil />{{rp|374}} * [[Thurmond, West Virginia|Thurmond]], named for CSA Captain W. D. Thurmond<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nps.gov/nr/twhp/wwwlps/lessons/28thurmond/28facts1.htm|title=Thurmond: A Town Born from Coal Mines and Railroads {{!}} Reading 1|publisher =National Park Service|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200126155540/https://www.nps.gov/nr/twhp/wwwlps/lessons/28thurmond/28facts1.htm|archive-date=January 26, 2020}}</ref> * [[Welch, West Virginia|Welch]], named for CSA Capt. Isaiah A. Welch.<ref>{{cite book|last=Capace|first=Nancy|title=Encyclopedia of West Virginia|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=K30UKW0aewgC&pg=PA196|year=1999|publisher=North American Book Dist LLC|isbn=978-0403098439|page=196}}</ref>
===Parks and water features=== * [[Weston, West Virginia|Weston]]: [[Stonewall Jackson Lake State Park]] (1990) named for [[Stonewall Jackson Lake]] named for Confederate General [[Stonewall Jackson]].
===Roads=== {{Div col|colwidth=20em}} * [[Charles Town, West Virginia|Charles Town]]: ** Beauregard Boulevard ** Lee Way * [[Charleston, West Virginia|Charleston]]: ** Beauregard Street ** Jackson Street ** Lee Street ** Dixie Street * [[Craigsville, West Virginia|Craigsville]]: General Lee Street * [[Elkins, West Virginia|Elkins]]: Robert E. Lee Avenue * [[Flatwoods, West Virginia|Flatwoods]]: Stonewall Jackson Highway * [[Inwood, West Virginia|Inwood]]: Jubal Early Avenue * [[Philippi, West Virginia|Philippi]]: Porterfield Road {{div col end}}
===Schools=== * [[Charleston, West Virginia|Charleston]]: Stonewall Jackson Middle School occupies the building that housed the former Stonewall Jackson High School. **The name was changed to West Side Middle School in July 2020.
==Wisconsin== {{see also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Wisconsin}} * [[Prairie du Chien, Wisconsin|Prairie du Chien]]: [[United Daughters of the Confederacy]] (UDC) monument to [[Jefferson Davis]] at Fort Crawford Cemetery Soldiers' Lot. Davis served briefly at [[Fort Crawford]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Fort Crawford Cemetery Soldiers' Lot | publisher = U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs | date = September 15, 2017 | url = https://www.cem.va.gov/cems/lots/fort_crawford.asp }}</ref> The text on the plaque reads, "JEFFERSON DAVIS, 1808–1889, Lieutenant United States Army, Assigned Fort Crawford 1831, Served here with distinction during Black Hawk War, Hero in Mexican War 1846–1848, United States Congressman, Senator, Secretary of War, President Confederate States of America, 1861–1865, Erected by The United Daughters of the Confederacy"<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wisconsinhistoricalmarkers.com/2013/07/jefferson-davis.html|title=Jefferson Davis|website=www.wisconsinhistoricalmarkers.com|access-date=October 21, 2017|archive-date=October 22, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171022084927/http://www.wisconsinhistoricalmarkers.com/2013/07/jefferson-davis.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> * [[Wisconsin Dells, Wisconsin|Wisconsin Dells]]: The Confederate spy [[Belle Boyd]] (1844–1900) is buried in Spring Grove Cemetery in Wisconsin Dells. She would go on tour in the United States and speak about being a spy for the Confederacy. She also wrote a book about her career. She was to speak at a [[Grand Army of the Republic]] (GAR) Post in Kilbourne City (now Wisconsin Dells) when she died from a heart attack. Members of the local GAR served as pallbearers at her funeral and was buried at the cemetery. Her grave is marked with a Confederate flag.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.baraboopubliclibrary.org/files/local/dewelvol4/13%20confederate%20spy%20buried%20in%20dells%20cemetery%20001.pdf|title=Confederate Spy Buried in Dells Cemetery}}</ref>
==Wyoming== ===Natural features=== * [[Yellowstone National Park]]: The [[Lamar River]] (named 1884–85) is named for [[Lucius Quintus Cincinnatus Lamar II|L.Q.C. Lamar]], a secessionist who drafted the instrument of Mississippi's secession and raised a regiment for the Confederates with his own money. He served as a Confederate ambassador to Russia. The river was named while he served as the [[United States Secretary of the Interior]] after the war. The Lamar Valley and other park features or administrative names which contain Lamar are derived from this original naming.<ref>Haines, Aubrey L. Yellowstone Place Names-Mirrors of History. Niwot, Colorado: University Press of Colorado. pp. 106–107. {{ISBN|087081382X}}.</ref>
==International== ===Brazil=== <!-- [[WP:NFCC]] violation: [[File:Confedarado.jpg|thumb|alt=Jimmy Carter at Confederado monument|In 1972, Georgia Governor [[Jimmy Carter]] visited the confederate monument in [[Americana, São Paulo]], Brazil.]] --> * In 1865, at the end of the American Civil War, a substantial number of [[Southern United States|Southerners]] left the South; many moved to other parts of the United States, such as the [[American Old West|American West]], but a few left the country entirely. The most popular country of [[Southern United States|Southerners]] emigration was Brazil, which still allowed slavery and wanted to encourage cotton production.<ref name=Herbert>{{cite news|url=http://www.washingtontimes.com/news/2009/dec/17/confederados-forge-new-cultural-identity/|title=Confederados forge new cultural identity|first=Paul N|last=Herbert|work=[[The Washington Times]]|date=December 17, 2009|access-date=December 22, 2015|archive-date=February 16, 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110216141150/http://www.washingtontimes.com/news/2009/dec/17/confederados-forge-new-cultural-identity/|url-status=dead}}</ref> These emigrants were known as [[Confederados]]. A Confederate monument was erected in the city of [[Americana, São Paulo|Americana]], São Paulo state, Brazil.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.sleuthsayers.org/2012/01/brazilian-confederacy.html?m=1|title=The Brazilian Confederacy|website=Sleuthsayers.org}}</ref>
===Canada=== {{see also|Removal of Confederate monuments and memorials#Canada}} * [[Kitchener, Ontario]]: [[Eastwood Collegiate Institute]] (1956), a public high school, replaced its [[Johnny Rebel]] mascot and Confederate imagery, perceived as associated with white bigotry, with Rebel Lion in 1999. The school retains the Rebel name for its teams.{{Citation needed|date=November 2023}}
===Ireland=== * [[Tuam]]: Ireland commemorated CSA Major [[Richard W. Dowling]], who was born in the Tuam, with a bronze memorial plaque on the Town Hall bearing his image and life story. Text of plaque: "Major Richard W. (Dick) Dowling C.S.A., 1837–1867 Born Knock, Tuam; Settled Houston Texas, 1857; Outstanding business and civic leader; Joined Irish Davis Guards in American Civil War; With 47 men foiled Invasion of Texas by 5000 federal troops at Sabine Pass, 8 Sept 1863, a feat of superb gunnery; formed first oil company in Texas; Died aged 30 of yellow fever. This plaque was unveiled by Col. J.B. Collerain 31 May 1998"
===Scotland=== * [[Edinburgh]]: [[Dean Cemetery]], obelisk for Scottish-born CSA Colonel Robert A. Smith, with a Confederate marker and Confederate flags.<ref name="scotsmanedinburghslittleknown">{{cite news|last1=Millar|first1=Stephen|title=Edinburgh's little-known Confederate memorial – and why it must stay|url=http://www.scotsman.com/heritage/people-places/edinburgh-s-little-known-confederate-memorial-and-why-it-must-stay-1-4547413|access-date=September 6, 2017|work=The Scotsman|date=August 31, 2017}}</ref>

<ref name="After">{{cite news |title=Here are the Confederate memorials that will be removed after Charlottesville |author=CNN |date=August 18, 2017 |url=https://www.wptv.com/news/national/here-are-the-confederate-memorials-that-will-be-removed-after-charlottesville |newspaper=[[WPTV]] |author-link=CNN |access-date=February 12, 2018}}</ref>
<ref name="SPLC2016pdf">{{cite web|url=https://www.splcenter.org/sites/default/files/com_whose_heritage.pdf|title=Whose heritage? Public Symbols of the Confederacy|last1=Gunter|first1=Booth|last2=Kizzire|first2=Jamie|editor-last=Gunter|editor-first=Booth|website=[[Southern Poverty Law Center]] |date=April 21, 2016 |access-date=June 24, 2020}}</ref>
}}
==Further reading== {{Div col|colwidth=30em}} * {{cite book|title=Beyond the Battlefield: Race, Memory, and the American Civil War|last1=Blight|first1=David W.|publisher=University of Massachusetts Press|year=2002|isbn=978-1558493612}} * {{cite book|title=Race and Reunion: The Civil War in American Memory|last1=Blight|first1=David W.|publisher=Belknap Press|year=2002|isbn=978-0674008199|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/racereunion00davi}} * {{cite book|title=The Public Art of Civil War Commemoration: A Brief History with Documents|last1=Brown|first1=Thomas J.|publisher=Bedford/St. Martin's|year=2004|isbn=978-0312397913}} * {{cite book|title=Civil War Canon: Sites of Confederate Memory in South Carolina|last1=Brown|first1=Thomas J.|publisher=University of North Carolina Press|year=2015|isbn=978-1469620954}} * {{cite book|title=Dixie's Daughters: The United Daughters of the Confederacy and the Preservation of Confederate Culture|last1=Cox|first1=Karen L.|publisher=University Press of Florida|year=2003|isbn=978-0813028125}} * {{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=5SJvUWYDBhUC|title=The Myth of the Lost Cause and Civil War History|author1=Gary W. Gallagher|author2=Alan T. Nolan|author-link1=Gary W. Gallagher|publisher=Indiana University Press|year=2000|isbn=978-0253338228}} * {{cite book|title=Georgia's Confederate Monuments: In Honor of a Fallen Nation|last1=Hagler|first1=Gould B. Jr.|date=2014|publisher=Mercer University Press|isbn=978-0881464665}} * {{cite book |title=Civil War sites, memorials, museums, and library collections : a state-by-state guidebook to places open to the public |url=https://archive.org/details/civilwarsitesmem0000gelb |url-access=registration |first=Doug |last=Gelbert |location=[[Jefferson, North Carolina]] |publisher=[[McFarland & Co]] |year=1997 |isbn=0786403195}} * {{cite book|title=Remembering North Carolina's Confederates|last1=Hardy|first1=Michael C.|publisher=Arcadia|year=2006|isbn=978-0738542973}} * {{cite news |title=The South's Confederate-monument problem is not going away |first=Monica |last=Hesse |date=May 8, 2016 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/lifestyle/style/the-souths-confederate-monument-problem-is-not-going-away/2016/05/08/b0258e4a-05af-11e6-a12f-ea5aed7958dc_story.html}} * {{cite journal |title=What Should Happen to Confederate Statues in the U.S. |first=Laura |last=Itzkowitz |date=June 24, 2020 |journal=[[Architectural Digest]] |url=https://www.architecturaldigest.com/story/what-should-happen-to-confederate-statues-in-the-us}} * {{cite book|title=Remembering the Civil War: Reunion and the Limits of Reconciliation|last1=Janney|first1=Caroline E.|date=2016|publisher=University of North Carolina Press|isbn=978-1469629896}} * {{cite book|title=No Holier Spot of Ground: Confederate Monuments & Cemeteries of South Carolina|last1=Johnson|first1=Kristina Dunn|publisher=The History Press|year=2009|isbn=978-1596293977}} * {{cite magazine |title=Public Memory and Street Names in the South: Who Gets Remembered? |first=Caroline |last=Klibanoff |date=June 22, 2017 |magazine=[[Medium (website)|Medium]] |url=https://medium.com/@cklibanoff/public-memory-and-street-names-in-the-south-who-gets-remembered-de8f7cb9e1e8}} * {{cite book|title=Recalling Deeds Immortal: Florida Monuments to the Civil War|author1=Lees, William B.|author2=Gaske, Frederick P.|publisher=University Press of Florida|year=2014|isbn=978-0813049960}} * {{cite magazine |title=The South's Love for Confederate Street Names, Mapped |first=Tanvi |last=Misra |date=August 25, 2017 |magazine=[[CityLab (web magazine)|CityLab]] |url=https://www.citylab.com/equity/2017/08/confederate-street-names-in-the-south-mapped/537771/}} * {{cite book|title=Confederate Monuments at Gettysburg|last1=Martin|first1=David G.|publisher=Da Capo Press|year=1995|isbn=978-0938289487}} * {{cite magazine |last=Moltke-Hansen |first=David |title=Honoring the Confederate Defeat the Georgia Way |magazine=[[Georgia Historical Quarterly]] |volume=101 |number=1 |year=2017 |pages=1–23}} * {{cite book|title=Honoring the Civil War Dead: Commemoration and the Problem of Reconciliation|last1=Neff|first1=John R.|publisher=University Press of Kansas|year=2005|isbn=978-0700613663}} * {{cite book|title=Confederate Symbols in the Contemporary South|author1=Richardson, William D.|author2=McNinch-Su, Ron|publisher=University Press of Florida|year=2001|isbn=978-0813021003|editor1-last=Martinez|editor1-first=J. Michael}} * {{cite news |title=In Monument Debate, Calls for an Overdue Reckoning on Race and Southern Identity |first1=Campbell |last1=Robertson |first2=Alan |last2=Blinder |first3=Richard |last3=Fausset |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=August 18, 2017 |access-date=December 6, 2017 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/08/18/us/confederate-monuments-southern-history.html}} * {{Cite book|title=Standing Soldiers, Kneeling Slaves: Race, War, and Monument in Nineteenth-century America |last=Savage|first=Kirk|publisher=Princeton University Press|year=1997|isbn=978-0691009476|location=Princeton, NJ|oclc=36470304}} * {{cite news |first=Caroline Randall |last=Williams |title=You Want a Confederate Monument? My Body Is a Confederate Monument |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=June 26, 2020 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/06/26/opinion/confederate-monuments-racism.html}} * {{cite book |title=Baptized in Blood: The Religion of the Lost Cause, 1865–1920 |last1=Wilson|first1=Charles|date=2009|publisher=University of Georgia Press|isbn=978-0820334257|edition=2nd}} {{Div col end}}
==External links== {{commons category|Monuments and memorials of the Confederate States of America}} * [https://www.google.com/maps/d/viewer?mid=1yDVZz93PUT3wDXpl4I-qmEQiSWqNtpzA&ll=38.19943672148613%2C-96.88488949999999&z=5 Whose Heritage? Public Symbols of the Confederacy] (interactive map, 2019) * [https://www.washingtonpost.com/graphics/2020/national/confederate-monuments/ Confederate monuments are falling, but hundreds still stand. Here's where.] (2020 map from ''The Washington Post'' showing additions and removals)
{{American Civil War}} {{Authority control}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Confederate Monuments and Memorials, List of}} [[Category:American Civil War-related lists]] [[Category:Confederate States of America monuments and memorials|*]] [[Category:Lists of American Civil War monuments and memorials|Confederate States Of America]] [[Category:Lists of monuments and memorials by subject]] [[Category:Lost Cause of the Confederacy]] [[Category:Sons of Confederate Veterans]] [[Category:United Daughters of the Confederacy monuments and memorials]]
