And his inability to enjoy meaningful relations with anyone except animals, small children and cartoon characters has become ridiculous. This is, in short, a singularly maladjusted young man."<ref name="Hoskyns/Vogue">{{cite magazine|first=Barney|last=Hoskyns|title=The Madness of Michael|magazine=[[Vogue (magazine)|Vogue]]|date=December 1987}} Available at [https://www.rocksbackpages.com/Library/Article/the-madness-of-michael Rock's Backpages] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160909001628/http://www.rocksbackpages.com/Library/Article/the-madness-of-michael |date=September 9, 2016 }} (subscription required).</ref> The ''Rolling Stone'' editors also attribute the media speculation partly to the singer's elusiveness and obsession with privacy, and add: "the massive public soul-searching the [1993] allegations against Jackson inspired were but one indication of the almost inestimable role he has played in shaping not only pop music but pop culture."{{sfn|Romanowski|George-Warren|1995|p=484}}
Susan Fast, writing for the same publication, gives a more sympathetic view of Jackson: "While some of [his] difference was demonstrated through what was viewed in the mass media as 'eccentric' behavior […] it was really his more substantive(sic), underlying differences that were most troubling—racial, gendered, able-bodied/disabled, child/teenager/adult, adult man who loved children, father/mother." She writes that Jackson's persona was "so unsettling to the hegemonic order that it had to be contained through ridicule, misinterpretation, [[sensationalism]], and finally criminal [[indictment]]".<ref>Fast, p. 261.</ref> It is generally regarded as unusual for a man to want to be a single parent, Fast continues, to adore children like a mother; the thought of a man obsessed with cosmetics and appearance agitated the public to disbelieve the idea of him being an object of [[Heterosexuality|heterosexual]] desire. Fast argues that such perceptions, which stemmed from anxieties of [[masculinity]], despite the fact that he created highly heterosexual art like "Black or White" and "[[In the Closet]]";<ref>Fast, p. 264.</ref> extended to the public perception of [[Trial of Michael Jackson|Jackson's alleged child molestation]] rooted in absence of masculinity.<ref>Fast, p. 265.</ref>
Scholars have described the widely acknowledged and often polarizing perception of Jackson as a [[Postmodernism|postmodern]] spectacle, causing the "real Michael Jackson" to remain elusive. In an article for ''Popular Music & Society'', Brian Rossiter commented: <blockquote>The media, aware of the marketable potential of Jackson's ambiguities, consistently used them to manufacture the notion of an authentic or private self behind his public persona. […] Audiences were always given liberty to select which Michael Jackson they deemed to be the real or authentic one […].<ref>Rossiter, p. 205.</ref></blockquote>

influence on the pop stars that followed him is like trying to trace the influence of oxygen and gravity. So vast, far-reaching and was his impact".<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Keyes |first=J. Edward |date=2009-07-08 |title=Michael Jackson's Indelible Pop Legacy |url=https://www.rollingstone.com/feature/michael-jacksons-indelible-pop-legacy-105243/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240918015851/https://www.rollingstone.com/feature/michael-jacksons-indelible-pop-legacy-105243/ |archive-date=2024-09-18 |access-date=2024-12-09 |magazine=Rolling Stone |language=en-US}}</ref> Jackson's use of diverse genres extended his reach from singers, to rappers, composers, and DJs such as [[David Guetta]] and [[Mark Ronson]] who studied and reinterpreted his work.<ref>{{Cite web |last= |last2= |date=2023-09-05 |title=The Impact Michael Jackson Had on the Culture of Music |url=https://jammin1057.com/2023/09/05/the-impact-michael-jackson-had-on-the-culture-of-music/ |access-date=2024-12-09 |website=jammin1057.com |language=en}}</ref><ref name="davidguetta1">{{Cite web |title=Artist of the Week: David Guetta {{!}} iHeart |url=https://blog.iheart.com/post/artist-week-david-guetta |access-date=2024-12-12 |website=blog.iheart.com |language=en}}</ref><ref name="markronson1">{{Cite web |title=Rhymefest & Mark Ronson - Man In The Mirror (Dedication to Michael Jackson) - Crate Kings |date=January 2008 |url=https://cratekings.com/rhymefest-mark-ronson-man-in-the-mirror-dedication-to-michael-jackson/ |access-date=2024-11-16 |language=en-US}}</ref> As gathered on ''[[WhoSampled]]'', Jackson's music has been officially [[Cover version|covered]], [[remix]]ed or [[Sampling (music)|sampled]] a combined estimate of 4,380 times (not including unverified releases) by other artists; which is among the top ten most reinterpreted music artists in modern history.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Most Covered Artists {{!}} WhoSampled |url=https://www.whosampled.com/most-covered-artists/ |access-date=2024-12-09 |website=[[WhoSampled]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Most Remixed Artists {{!}} WhoSampled |url=https://www.whosampled.com/most-remixed-artists/ |access-date=2024-12-09 |website=[[WhoSampled]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Most Sampled Artists {{!}} WhoSampled |url=https://www.whosampled.com/most-sampled-artists/ |access-date=2024-12-09 |website=[[WhoSampled]]}}</ref> Over the years, hundreds of artists have specifically cite Michael Jackson as being their early inspiration, or reference him in their creative process, or as a specified influence:
{{Columns-list|colwidth=20em|* [[Aaliyah]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sutherland |first=William |title=Aaliyah Remembered: Her Life & the Person Behind the Mystique |publisher=[[Trafford Publishing]] |year=2006 |isbn=978-1-4120-5062-3 |location=Victoria, B.C. |pages=8–10 |language=en}}</ref>

|url=https://www.facebook.com/theworldmusicawards/posts/1046879338726512/ |access-date=November 15, 2024 |website=World Music Awards - Official Facebook}}</ref><ref name="zaynmalik2">{{Cite web |date=2016-07-15 |title=Zayn Malik's UV tattoo |url=https://www.toronto.com/things-to-do/zayn-maliks-uv-tattoo/article_9eb07b1e-31c3-5cac-8fae-2976e08cf8d5.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241209061916/https://www.toronto.com/things-to-do/zayn-maliks-uv-tattoo/article_9eb07b1e-31c3-5cac-8fae-2976e08cf8d5.html |archive-date=2024-12-09 |access-date=2024-12-08 |website=Toronto.com |language=en}}</ref> * [[Zendaya]]<ref name="zendaya">{{Cite magazine |date=November 2, 2015 |title=Zendaya: I'm Not a Role Model 'Because I'm Not Playing a Role' |url=https://time.com/4093271/zendaya-interview-influential-teen/ |magazine=Time}}</ref> * [[Zion & Lennox]]<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-07-12 |title=Zion y Lennox sacaron el Michael Jackson que llevan dentro |url=https://www.univision.com/shows/premios-juventud/zion-y-lennox-sacaron-el-michael-jackson-que-llevan-dentro-video |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241213211403/https://www.univision.com/shows/premios-juventud/zion-y-lennox-sacaron-el-michael-jackson-que-llevan-dentro-video |archive-date=2024-12-13 |access-date=2024-12-13 |website=Univision}}</ref> * [[Zoe Wees]]<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-10-27 |title=Digital Cover: Zoe Wees |url=https://hungermag.com/editorial/digital-cover-zoe-wees |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241213211626/https://hungermag.com/editorial/digital-cover-zoe-wees |archive-date=2024-12-13 |access-date=2024-12-13 |website=hungermag.com |language=en}}</ref>}}
=== Earliest inspiration === Major soloists have spoken of Michael Jackson's pivotal effect on their dream of becoming a singer or as an early inspiration:
{{multiple image | direction = horizontal | align = center | footer = | image1 = David Guetta 5, 2012.jpg | image2 = POS17 @Kristsll-198 (35166606654).jpg | image3 = James Morrison (7830412734).jpg | image4 = Jason Derulo - Superbloom Festival 2023 - DSC4045.jpg | image5 = Untold 2024 - Lenny Kravitz 13.jpg | total_width = 500 | caption1 = [[David Guetta]] | caption2 = [[Cigarettes After Sex]] | caption3 = [[James Morrison (singer)|James Morrison]] | caption4 = [[Jason Derulo]] | caption5 = [[Lenny Kravitz]] | perrow = 6 | caption_align = center | header = | alt1 = }}
{{multiple image | direction = horizontal | align = center | footer = | image1 = Matty Healy, MainSquare Festival 2014, Arras, France (14597634051).jpg | image2 = P20230704CS-0962 (53235069464) (cropped).jpg | image3 = Robin Thicke 2014.jpg | image4 = Glasto24 2806 300624 (43 of 382) (53838024114) (cropped).jpg | total_width = 400 | caption1 = [[Matty Healy]] | caption2 = [[Ne-Yo]] | caption3 = [[Robin Thicke]] | caption4 = [[Tom Chaplin]] | perrow = 4 | caption_align = center | header = | alt1 = }}
*[[Beyoncé]] went to see Jackson in concert for the first time at age 5 and claims to have realized her sole purpose, saying "If it wasn't for Michael Jackson, I would never ever have performed."<ref name="beyonce3" /><ref name="beyonce1" /><ref name="beyonce2" /> *[[Celine Dion]], who had posters of Jackson in her bedroom growing up, cited Jackson as a major motivation for her to learn English and fueled her desire to want to be a megastar.<ref name="celinedion" /> *[[Chris Brown]] has claimed Michael Jackson as his biggest inspiration of all time,<ref name="chrisbrown1" /> stating "Michael Jackson is the reason why I do music and the reason I am an entertainer [...] Being able to see Michael Jackson's success, to be able to inspire the world as well as his talent and musical ability, his eye for detail – automatically inspires me to try to be better and try to be great."<ref name="chrisbrown2" /><ref name="chrisbrown3" /> *[[Chris Martin]] of [[Coldplay]] has said Jackson was his early introduction to music through ''Bad'' and ''Off the Wall'' album,<ref name="chrismartin1" /><ref name="chrismartin2" /> and dedicated a letter in Martins' handwriting on Coldplay's official website, "Michael Jackson was the best of the best. His music and performances made the world a brighter place. His light will shine on forever."<ref name="chrismartin3" /> *[[Ciara]] explained, "Whenever someone asks me who inspires me to do what I do, I always say Michael. That's it for me. He's everything to me [...] I feel it's important for me to continue to let my generation know how important he was to music."<ref name="ciara1" /><ref name="ciara2" /> *[[Dave Keuning]], lead guitarist of [[The Killers]], says listening to "Thriller" made him love music and inspired him to become a musician.<ref name="davekeuning" /> [[David Guetta]]'s beginning in [[audio mixing]] was a "beat mash paying homage to Michael Jackson's "Billie Jean".<ref name="davidguetta1" /> Guetta names him as a primary influence.<ref name="davidguetta3" /> He voiced regret they had not collaborated before his passing, after his Jackson-inspired 2014 single "[[Bad (David Guetta and Showtek song)|Bad]]" went number one in several countries.<ref name="davidguetta2" /> *[[Eddie Vedder]] from [[Pearl Jam]] has said that listening to Jackson's music got him into singing "And my parents became the new foster parents for seven kids in this group home. They were mostly African-American kids and some Irish kids. [...] I started singing to Michael Jackson records." Eddie also notes that his first album was "[[Got to Be There|Got To Be There]]".<ref name="Letkemann">{{Cite web |last=Letkemann |first=Jessica |date=2009-06-26 |title=Michael Jackson Influenced Pearl Jam Too |url=http://www.twofeetthick.com/2009/06/michael-jackson-influenced-pearl-jam-too/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090627212130/http://www.twofeetthick.com/2009/06/michael-jackson-influenced-pearl-jam-too/ |archive-date=2009-06-27 |access-date=2024-12-22 |website=www.twofeetthick.com}}</ref> *[[Greg Gonzalez]] of [[Cigarettes After Sex]] credits Jackson as his primary influence to become a musician, "Yeah for me I started young, I think I was born the year that ''Thriller'' came out and pretty much Michael Jackson became like this immediate idol, and I wanted to be a performer; just sing and perform and just seeing everything that he was doing."<ref name="greggonzalez" /> *[[Icona Pop]]'s initial track that made them want to make music was [[Black or White]] by Michael Jackson. "I think it was because it's such a powerful pop song. Even though I was really young when I first heard it, I really felt what it was all about. I guess at even such an early age I knew that I wanted to be making music and I can thank Michael Jackson for that."<ref name="iconapop" /> *[[Iron & Wine]]'s earliest impression of music was with his friend to "Beat It", saying "I still remember, to this day sitting on his couch" narrating their amazed reaction listening on a [[cassette tape]].<ref name="ironandwine" /> *[[James Morrison (singer)|James Morrison]] explained "I was four or five and I used to watch ''[[Moonwalker]]'' every day, there was a clip of him singing "Man in the Mirror" at a concert where everyone's lighters are out and that was a massive moment in my life. Something clicked in my head, which was 'I want to do that, I want to sing.' [...] Michael Jackson was my first love singing wise."<ref name="jamesmorrison" /> *[[Janet Jackson]], his sister, says he inspired her in many ways and helped her perform,<ref name="janetjackson1" /><ref name="janetjackson2" /> recalling <blockquote>Michael and I would tape every old musical, and we made a collage. All the dance numbers, we put them all on one tape and we used to just watch them and watch them. He was Fred Astaire, i was Ginger Rogers, and we'd learn every routine. Our mirror was the reflection off the window [...] Seeing all the things that he's accomplished, and saying okay I've got to keep growing, I've got to keep pushing forward, move on, on and upward. [...] And I think he's the greatest there is.<ref>{{Cite AV media |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X1R_5teWWNc |title=My Brother, Michael Jackson {{!}} Janet In Her Own Words {{!}} Full Documentary (4K 2160p) (14:02, 26:28) |date=2024-01-11 |last= |access-date=2025-01-25 |via=YouTube}}</ref></blockquote> *[[Jason Derulo]] states, "He is the reason I am who I am today. When I was four years old, I saw him for the first time. I saw how he moved the crowd and how people were just so touched."<ref name="jasonderulo" /> *[[Jeremiah Green]] of [[Modest Mouse]], said that he was a big fan of Jackson musically. When he was asked what he wanted to be as a grown up, he responded, "Well, duh, dude, I might be Michael Jackson".<ref name="jeremiahgreen" /> *[[Kevin Parker (musician)|Kevin Parker]] of [[Psychedelic music|psychedelic]] super project [[Tame Impala]] has said of one of his earliest introductions to music, <blockquote>My brother Steve, who was a few years older than me, had Bad on tape and I remember listening to Smooth Criminal and just thinking it was the coolest thing ever. I must have been five or six at the time and I remember walking around school by myself thinking I was Michael Jackson. I wasn't dancing, exactly – more like walking musically. It seemed like a good idea at the time.<ref name="kevinparker1" /></blockquote> Additionally, he says, "Michael Jackson's one of my favorite artists of my whole life [...] In fact, I think he is my favorite."<ref name="kevinparker2" /> *[[Lenny Kravitz]], "Michael Jackson, just because he was the first thing that made me want to play music. The Jackson 5 was monumental to me — in my development, in my music, in my childhood, in my adult life." He mentions seeing him in concert, "It was magical. He was six or seven years old. It was crazy. I had a picture in my bedroom that my father took of them that night on stage. It was my earliest memory of what changed my life."<ref name="lennykravitz" /> *[[Matty Healy]], frontman of [[The 1975]], cites Jackson as his earliest musical influence, "Michael Jackson [...] The first gig I went to was Michael Jackson at [[Wembley Stadium|Wembley]] when I was six. It was the ''HIStory'' tour. Watching him catalysed a real drive in me. He was such a phenomenal performer."<ref name="mattyhealy1" /><ref name="mattyhealy2" /> *[[Ne-Yo]] said in 2008, "Michael Jackson is one of the reasons why I sing."<ref name="neyo1" /> Additionally, on 2019 [[Larry King Live]] and 2024, "Without Michael Jackson, there would be no Ne-Yo." Particularly for his ''[[Libra Scale]]'' album, he referenced Jackson's need for quality was a motivation to make his music better.<ref name="neyo2" /> *[[Pat Monahan]] from [[Train (band)|Train]] described that he left his basketball career behind after listening to Jackson's music in a [[carpool]] ride back home. He claims the whole car including himself was singing to a song by Jackson, until the others in the car turned to him in shock from his vocal quality.<ref name="patmonahan1" /> He also named ''Off the Wall'' as one of five albums he couldn't live without.<ref name="patmonahan2" /> *[[Robin Thicke]], "Michael's music was one of the first things that made me want to become an artist. When I was on the school bus at seven or eight years old I would sing Michael songs to get attention. It was one of the first times I knew that I wanted to be an artist."<ref name="essencegen" /> *[[Tom Chaplin]] of [[Keane (band)|Keane]] says, "[<nowiki/>[[Bad (album)|Bad]]] was the first album that had a huge impact, and kick-started feelings of making music myself."<ref name="tomchaplin" /> *[[Troye Sivan]] credits Jackson for making performing his dream career, after seeing footage of the [[Bad (tour)|Bad tour]] on VHS tape.<ref name="troyesivan" /> *[[Usher (musician)|Usher]] has on many occasions named Jackson as his biggest influence, "I wouldn't be who I am today without Michael Jackson. [...] You can't say you are an artist in this century and wasn't inspired by Michael ... and I'm always gonna remember. I'll be a fan for life." They performed together at the [[Michael Jackson: 30th Anniversary Celebration|Michael Jackson: 30th Anniversary Special]] for "[[You Rock My World]]".<ref name="usher" /> *[[Victoria Monét]] was asked what made her want to be a musician, said,<blockquote>It was after watching a Michael Jackson concert on tape when I was around six or seven. He had a certain magic about him, seeing how many people were having a great time in the crowd, it was like they had no worries, they were just so overjoyed to be celebrating together in the one space. To child-me, it looked like there were millions of people in the audience, and they were so emotional – people bursting into tears, or passing out – the passion he exuded in every move. [...] So I was like, 'I wanna do that!'.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Johnston |first=Kathleen |date=2020-08-21 |title=Victoria Monét is ready for her moment |url=https://www.gq-magazine.co.uk/culture/article/victoria-monet-interview |archive-url=https://archive.today/20250112221831/https://www.gq-magazine.co.uk/culture/article/victoria-monet-interview |archive-date=2025-01-12 |access-date=2025-01-12 |website=British GQ |language=en-GB}}</ref></blockquote> *[[The Weeknd]] referenced Michael Jackson in a Complex magazine article, he says "He's everything to me, so you're going to hear it in my music. ''Off the Wall'' was the album that inspired me to sing."<ref name="theweeknd1" /><ref name="theweeknd2" /> Tesfaye covered "[[Dirty Diana]]" re-titled "D.D." on his third mixtape [[Echoes of Silence]]. The Weeknd also cites Jackson's [[falsetto]] on [[Don't Stop 'Til You Get Enough|Don't Stop 'til You Get Enough]] helped him train his own falsetto.<ref name="theweeknd2" />
=== Specific creative methods === Various musicians have referenced Michael Jackson for specific creative methods.
{{multiple image | direction = horizontal | align = center | footer = | image1 = Alesso playing live at Ushuaia Ibiza.jpeg | image2 = BrandyNorwoodJuly04.jpg | image3 = Camila Cabello at iHeartRadio Jingle Ball 2013.jpg | image4 = Common at BRIC Celebrate Brooklyn (27813357667) (cropped).jpg | image5 = Hanson 2022 Toronto RGB Tour.jpg | image6 = Jack White - Roskilde Festival 2012 - Orange Stage.jpg | total_width = 600 | caption1 = [[Alesso]] | caption2 = [[Brandy Norwood]] | caption3 = [[Camila Cabello]] | caption4 = [[Common (rapper)|Common]] | caption5 = [[Hanson (band)|Hanson]] | caption6 = [[Jack White]] | perrow = 6 | caption_align = center | header = }}
{{multiple image | direction = horizontal | align = center | footer = | image1 = Meghan_Trainor_2015_(cropped).jpg | image2 = PinkPantheress, 2022.jpg | image3 = SkyFerrSthBnk160622 (28 of 29) (52153299610).jpg | image4 = Sufjan_Stevens_performing_at_Pitchfork,_2016.jpg | image5 = Justice_Sónar_2008_-_2600809995.jpg | image6 = Will.i.am in 2018.jpg | total_width = 600 | caption1 = [[Meghan Trainor]] | caption2 = [[PinkPantheress]] | caption3 = [[Sky Ferreira]] | caption4 = [[Sufjan Stevens]] | caption5 = [[Justice (band)|Xavier de Rosnay]] (left) | caption6 = [[will.i.am]] | perrow = 6 | caption_align = center | header = }}
*[[Alesso]] described to [[DJ Mag]] that disc jockeys must constantly reinvent their music but it should still sound like them. So, he harnessed Jackson who is creatively his biggest influence, "Always evolving, always coming up with new ideas but still managed to stay himself the whole time".<ref name="alesso1" /><ref name="alesso2" /> *[[Brandy Norwood|Brandy]], who admires Jackson, has influenced her to stack vocals in an unconventional way for ''[[Full Moon (Brandy album)|Full Moon]]''.<ref name="brandynorwood" /> *[[Camila Cabello]] credits Michael with inspiring the content of her debut album, ''Camila''.<ref name="camilacabello1" /> She also explains the music video for "Thriller" inspired her to make an extended version of "Havana".<ref name="camilacabello2" /> *[[Charlie Puth]]'s virtual music class on [[Sound recording and reproduction|sound reproduction]] uses Quincy Jones and Michael Jackson's vocal recording trick as a focused [[case study]] on 2-note phrasing (also [[Tie (music)|tie]] notes) using [[Pro Tools]].<ref name="charlieputh" /> *[[Clean Bandit]]'s Jack says of him and Grace's emphasis to focus on their music videos was from Jackson, "I used to watch so much MTV 2 and VH1 Classics when I was like 15 or 14. I guess the first music video I had was the ''[[Michael Jackson albums discography|Michael Jackson Greatest Hits]]'' on VHS [...] I completely wore out that tape. Since I started working with Grace [...] we realised that she had worn out the same tape. I think Michael Jackson music videos are the pinnacle of that medium."<ref name="cleanbandit" /> *[[Common (rapper)|Common]] has said watching Jackson on Motown 25 stuck with him as a performer for his energy, "I can see it now. I remember where I was; a friend videotaped it and I rewound it and watched it so many times. It was one of those moments where it was out of body in a way, because his energy was so incredible. [...] It became part of the breakdance culture at some point, too." He highlights this move motivated him to use energetic movements when he's on stage.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Joshi |first=Tara |date=2021-09-24 |title=Common's teenage obsessions: 'Michael Jordan gave me the shoes off his feet' |url=https://www.theguardian.com/music/2021/sep/24/common-teenage-obsessions-michael-jordan-gave-me-the-shoes-off-his-feet#:~:text=When%20I%20was%2013,%20I,impacted%20our%20culture%20so%20much. |archive-url=https://archive.today/20250121025434/https://www.theguardian.com/music/2021/sep/24/common-teenage-obsessions-michael-jordan-gave-me-the-shoes-off-his-feet |archive-date=January 21, 2025 |access-date=2025-01-21 |work=[[The Guardian]] |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> *[[Hanson (band)|Hanson]], the group popular for "MMMBop", implement the term "apply the "Michael Jackson theory" during their production process to emphasize their songs must have the standard of "lots of groove and melody".<ref name="hanson" /> *Both members of [[Honne (band)|Honne]], describes that Michael Jackson is a main influence to their music, "For me, growing up I loved Michael Jackson." They borrowed 80s style drum and snare elements, like the [[Simmons SDS-V|Simmons snare]] that Michael Jackson used.<ref name="honne1" /><ref name="honne2" /> *[[Imogen Heap]] appeared on ''Thrillercast'' [[podcast]] in 2008,<ref name="imogenheap1" /><ref name="imogenheap2" /> to talk in depth about Jackson's methodology, <blockquote>As a singer, the way that you would breathe, people kind of dip down their breaths, when it comes down to their vocal take, I love the way he incorporates it. The breaths are just as important as the vocal melody. [...] Michael Jackson's collection of words that don't exist in the English language or perhaps any language apart from Michael Jackson's own special [[dictionary]]. Words like ''shamone'', I have no idea what shamone means but what's great about that is it leaves a door wide open for people like myself to also make up entirely nonsensical words and create even whole songs about them!<ref name="imogenheap3" /></blockquote> *[[Jack White]] of [[The White Stripes]] has praised him numerous times, "["Let God in the room"] [...] I saw Michael Jackson say that once. I thought, Wow, I've been saying that to myself for a long time, and I was glad to hear him say that. The ultimate thing to do is to relinquish all control."<ref name="jackwhite1" /> "And I want to write like Michael Jackson would write—instead of writing parts on the instruments or humming melodies, you think of them. To do everything in my head and to do it in silence and use only one room."<ref name="jackwhite2" /> He copied Jackson's melody-before-lyrics approach specifically for ''[[Boarding House Reach]]''.<ref name="jackwhite3" /> *[[James Bay (singer)|James Bay]] mentioned as a child he was obsessed with Michael Jackson, attributing his trademark wide-brimmed hat was inspired by the image of Jackson's single silvery glove.<ref name="jamesbay1" /><ref name="jamesbay2" /> *[[Junior Senior]]'s "[[Move Your Feet]]" was a song often mistaken for Jackson's voice. Junior Senior said in a 2005 [[The Reykjavík Grapevine]] interview that this wasn't the listener's imagination, it was intentional.<ref name="juniorsenior" /> *[[Meghan Trainor]] listed Jackson amongst four other entertainers who inspired her, <blockquote>Just how much he was involved with every music video, every photo shoot, every production. I didn't know any of that. I've been watching recently because I'm like, 'Am I doing too much stuff?' I'm involved in every little thing you see (in my career). I looked it up because I was like, 'Am I alone?' and I was like, 'No, that's what Michael did. You're doing the right thing.<ref name="meghantrainor" /></blockquote> *[[Muse (band)|Muse]]'s song "[[Time Is Running Out (Muse song)|Time Is Running Out]]" was specifically created during a studio session where the band wanted to have a sound similar to "Billie Jean".<ref name="muse" /> *[[The Newton Brothers]] infused retro music of Radiohead and Michael Jackson for their composition work in the ''[[X-Men '97]]'' soundtrack.<ref name="thenewtonbrothers" /> *[[Maxwell (musician)|Maxwell]] covered "[[Thriller (album)|The Lady in My Life]]" at the [[2022 Billboard Music Awards]]. He said ever since, he wanted to make such songs like Jackson that were used at his friends weddings to walk down the aisle to, just like Lady in My Life.<ref name="maxwell1" /><ref name="maxwell2" /> *[[Pharrell Williams]] has expressed admiration for Jackson, having once said "I met Michael at his video shoot in California. I told him from the age of 6 I would burn holes in my socks moonwalking in the bathroom." Williams stated he was motivated to write songs for Jackson, though it would eventually end up on Justin Timberlake's album ''[[Justified (album)|Justified]]''.<ref name="pharrellwilliams1" /><ref name="pharrellwilliams2" /> *[[PinkPantheress]] created songs on [[GarageBand]] in her college dormitory. Influenced by Jackson's music style, she became a viral sensation from her Michael Jackson-remix track "Just A Waste" on [[TikTok|Tiktok]] in 2020. It both sampled [[Off the Wall (Michael Jackson song)|Off the Wall]] and lyrically copied [[Wanna Be Startin' Somethin']]. It never officially released due to [[Estate of Michael Jackson|his estate]] rejecting authorization but helped her land a record deal with [[Parlophone]] four months later.<ref name="pinkpantheress1" /><ref name="pinkpantheress2" /><ref name="pinkpantheress3" /> *[[Roddy Ricch]] credits Michael Jackson as inspiration behind his Grammy-nominated song, "[[The Box (Roddy Ricch song)|The Box]]," explaining on [[HBO]]'s [[The Shop]] that he saw Jackson do the iconic "EE RR" adlib, "I seen Michael Jackson do it...That's what made me want to do it. Cause he was in the studio one time and he was talking about some song that he made but he started beatboxing and he said he put that in the beat."<ref name="roddyricch" /> [[Sky Ferreira]]'s grandmother was Jackson's hair stylist, and she mentions he suggested at age 9 that she join a gospel choir. She was saddened by his passing, and recalls his advice, "He was like, 'Don't focus on things that are just around you – you need to look back to the history of music.' And that's what I did."<ref name="skyferreira" /> *[[Sufjan Stevens]] expressed admiration for Michael Jackson in a 2011 interview by stating "I watched the Michael Jackson documentary ''[[Michael Jackson's This Is It|This Is It]]'' [...] just the work that goes into that kind of production and how invested the dancers were and also how hands-on Michael Jackson was. [...] I realized for the first time that all of his music is based on physical ideas. They'd be working through something, and he would explain a musical gesture with his body." Jackson's techniques influenced his tour, ''[[The Age of Adz]]''.<ref name="sufjanstevens" /> *[[Justice (band)|Xavier de Rosnay from Justice]] says electro hit single [[D.A.N.C.E.|D.A.N.C.E]] was a creative homage to Michael Jackson, "The music of Michael Jackson is something we believe in. So, we built the lyrics mainly around the titles of his songs." [[P.Y.T. (Pretty Young Thing)|P.Y.T.]], [[ABC (The Jackson 5 song)|A.B.C.]], [[Black or White]], [[Working Day and Night]] and [[Invincible (Michael Jackson album)|Whatever Happens]] are lyrics in the song.<ref name="xavierderosnay" /> *Composer and rapper [[Will.i.am]] for [[Thriller 40 (film)|Thriller 40 documentary]] did a recording session breakdown of the track layering on "[[Beat It]]", "It shows the freedom and how comfortable he felt in the studio without worrying about people's judgement or like 'What's that' cause you know he was bringing freaking skill. You knew he was bringing ultra talent, and imagination for him for him to be like 'You know what I feel like yawning right here.'"<ref name="william1" /> In another interview, he predicts the future of "perfecting" music, "It's a peek of what's coming [...] In the future, I would bet that tomorrow's Michael Jackson is not a person. It's a machine that will be able to talk to millions of people at the same time. Tomorrow's big superstar will be able to make custom songs Michael Jackson wasn't able to record. Tomorrow's superstar is going to be perfect." He has founded the AI-powered company RAiDiO.FYI that intends to be used for radio consumption.<ref name="william2" />
=== Other major influence === Various named him a main or major influence or have known to heavily reference him.
{{multiple image | direction = horizontal | align = center | footer = | image1 = Amy Winehouse f4962007 crop.jpg | image2 = Keri Hilson Supafest 3 - cropped.jpg | image3 = Kendrick Lamar White House 2016.jpg | image4 = Mariah Carey Adelaide 2014.png | image5 = Mark Ronson on Coke & Olympics, 2012.jpg | image6 = Rita Ora 1 (40251927980) (cropped).jpg | total_width = 400 | caption1 = [[Amy Winehouse]] | caption2 = [[Keri Hilson]] | caption3 = [[Kendrick Lamar]] | caption4 = [[Mariah Carey]] | caption5 = [[Mark Ronson]] | caption6 = [[Rita Ora]] | perrow = 4 | caption_align = center | header = | alt1 = | image7 = | caption7 = [[Tyla]] | image8 = Zayn Malik 2012.jpg | caption8 = [[Zayn Malik]] }}
*[[Adam Levine]] of [[Maroon 5]] contributed to a ''Rolling Stones'' column declaring that "I never met him, but he was probably the single most important musical influence for me."<ref name="adamlevine" /> *[[Amy Winehouse]], who said in a 2004 interview, "I could never decide whether I wanted to be Michael Jackson or marry him. I don't care what people say about him now because he's a fucking genius."<ref name="amywinehouse" /> *[[Ariana Grande]]'s early UK screen test for [[Nickelodeon]] contained a question on who would she trade places with for one day to which she chose Michael Jackson.<ref name="arianagrande1" /> Her tribute to him, "[[ABC (The Jackson 5 song)|ABC]]" she put in the description, "I have his music to inspire me."<ref name="arianagrande2" /> *[[Good Charlotte|Billy Martin]] of Good Charlotte, "I've got every Michael Jackson album [...] He is one of my major musical influences."<ref name="billymartin" /> *[[Bruno Mars]], a long-time fan, explains Jackson set the bar for music artists for his "attention to detail [...] Any artist, I don't care what genre you do, you should always aspire to be like Michael Jackson."<ref name="brunomars1" /> Mars wrote a dedication across on his social media platforms that Michael Jackson "will forever be the man that turned music into magic."<ref name="brunomars2" /> *South Korean band [[BTS]] has cited Jackson as a major influence, and the members also own various memorabilia of Jackson. Their dance cover of "Black or White" and "[[Love Never Felt So Good]]" were aired on television.<ref name="bts1" /><ref name="bts2" /> Their first English single "[[Dynamite (BTS song)|Dynamite]]" is an indirect homage to Jackson's dance moves while their second English single "[[Butter (song)|Butter]]" begins with a Smooth Criminal reference.<ref name="bts3" /> *[[Jay Kay]] of acid jazz band [[Jamiroquai]] mentioned Jackson in a statement, "I was hugely influenced by ''Off the Wall'' particularly. He will always be remembered for that level of brilliance which will doubtless never be replicated again."<ref name="jaykay" /> *[[Kendrick Lamar]] is influenced by Jackson and in a CBS Morning interview says he is his dream collaboration.<ref name="kendricklamar1" /> In an interview on ''[[The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon]]'', he called Michael Jackson "the legend".<ref name="kendricklamar2" /> Lamar's award-winning album ''[[To Pimp a Butterfly]]'' references Jackson twice, in "[[King Kunta]]" and "[[To Pimp a Butterfly|Mortal Man]]". *[[Keri Hilson]], "Michael Jackson has been an influence so much to why I love the stage. I remember particularly a performance of his song, "[[Dirty Diana]]". I was in the [[Nosebleed section|nosebleeds section]], but I felt so much a part of his whole show. That has never happened since, never happened before that, and don't think it will ever happen again."<ref name="ReferenceB">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-29 |title=Michael Jackson's Influence on the Next Generation |url=https://www.essence.com/news/michael-jacksons-influence-next-generation/#291624 |access-date=2025-01-02 |website=Essence |language=en-US}}</ref> *[[Lady Gaga]] has named Jackson as a source of her influence, both musically and fashionably.<ref name="ladygaga1" /> She owns around 400 pieces from his personal collection, buying them through [[auction]]. In 2016, she wore Jackson's jacket from his 1990 visit to the White House at Hillary Clinton's final campaign rally, during the 2016 U.S. presidential election.<ref name="ladygaga2" /> *[[Mariah Carey]] has mentioned Michael Jackson as one of the major influences who helped shape her artistry. When asked in an interview who her inspirations were, she said "Looking at the time I was little, Michael Jackson when he was a kid, and then when he did [[Motown 25: Yesterday, Today, Forever|Motown 25]] when he was there making history."<ref name="mariahcarey" /> *[[Mark Ronson]], producer of [[Uptown Funk]], is a fan of Jackson's music, and dedicated a tribute mixtape for him called "Mark Ronson Presents Rhymefest - Man In The Mirror".<ref name="markronson1" /> In [[Thriller 40 (film)|Thriller 40]] documentary he says, "Michael saw that he could touch greatness with ''Off The Wall''. Knew he was still going against racist radio and people that wanted to put him in a box. But you'd have to be in the rare .0001% to be able to project yourself to that."<ref name="markronson2" /> *[[Miley Cyrus]] has a "[[Bad (album)|Bad]]" font [[tattoo]] on her fingers in honor of her favorite album of Jackson, and expressed great admiration. "I wish I would have had some kind of determination to be like 'How can I get in touch with Michael Jackson and let him know how much he means to me?"<ref name="mileycyrus1" /><ref name="mileycyrus2" /> *[[Pete Wentz]] of [[Fall Out Boy]] stated, "Michael Jackson has been a part of my life for as long I have heard music. He in my mind is the ultimate entertainer of our generation. I can remember exact moments of my life based on Michael Jackson songs and videos."<ref name="petewentz" /> Fall Out Boy officially covered Jackson's music, releasing a studio version of "[[Beat It (Fall Out Boy song)|Beat It]]" on ''[[Live in Phoenix]]''. *[[Rita Ora]] references Jackson as her "king" and greatest inspiration: "I've always been a big fan but I didn't really understand how amazing he was until I got older. When you start to understand his music, it's like, "Whoa. What was he drinking? What was he breathing?" [[P.Y.T. (Pretty Young Thing)]] is my favourite song. He's an inspiration to me, and the whole world, because he was himself throughout his whole career. He wrote whatever he wanted to write and wore whatever he wanted to wear. I really admire anyone with that sort of confidence. You don't have to be famous; it's really admirable in anyone." On some concerts, she notes she wears Michael Jackson socks to keep up a high energy.<ref name="ritaora1" /><ref name="ritaora2" /> *Nigerian duo [[P-Square]] has named Jackson as their major music inspiration. In the late 1990s, the dancing group they belonged to majorly mimed Jackson's dance routines in Africa. The costumes for [[The Invasion (album)|''Invasion'']], which won Album of the Year in Africa was inspired by Jackson as well.<ref name="psquare" /> *[[Tyla]] in an interview for [[Vogue (magazine)|Vogue Japan]] on the artist who has the greatest influence on her is Michael Jackson, "He is loved by people of all ages all over the world and is an eternal presence."<ref name="tyla" /> [[Zayn Malik]] says Michael Jackson is his greatest inspiration.<ref name="zaynmalik1" /><ref name="zaynmalik2" /> Actress and singer [[Zendaya]] says Michael Jackson's lack of [[profanity]] in every song was admirable, "I've loved Michael Jackson since the minute I was born. He always had a positive way of doing what he did. He had such a love for the art of music and tried to make people feel better through that."<ref name="zendaya" />
=== Widening the craft === Michael Jackson is given mention by artists to have widened particular aspects of the music industry or expanded their view on their own ability.
{{multiple image | direction = horizontal | align = center | footer = | image1 = Chris Cornell.jpg | image2 = Chance the Rapper --- Red Rocks --- 05.02.17 (33630241313).jpg | image3 = Drake Live at Walmart Soundcheck (4636434364).jpg | image4 = Jason Mraz in Concert in Red Bank, NJ (32212495528).jpg | image5 = K. West (cropped) (blurred).jpg | image6 = Shawn Stockman cropped.jpg | total_width = 600 | caption1 = [[Chris Cornell]] | caption2 = [[Chance the Rapper]] | caption3 = [[Drake (musician)|Drake]] | caption4 = [[Jason Mraz]] | caption5 = [[Kanye West]] | caption6 = [[Shawn Stockman]] | perrow = 6 | caption_align = center | header = }}
*[[Chris Cornell]], the singer of [[Soundgarden]] and [[Audioslave]], "The next thing that had a clear impact was when I was already a musician, probably about 18 years old, and was working in restaurants but was also starting different bands at the time, and was obviously watching MTV all the time, just to see what was on it. I wasn't a fan of most of it. Then, "Thriller" happened, and to see that shift from pretty much an entirely white audience watching an entirely white music channel change because of this one guy [Michael Jackson]."<ref name="chriscornell" /> *[[Chance the Rapper]] did [[Michael Jackson impersonator|Michael Jackson impersonations]] at his school talent shows and exclusively only listened to him up until the fifth grade. Once becoming a musician, Michael Jackson's philanthropic side and social commentary greatly inspired him and helped shape his desire to make activism part of his artistry.<ref name="chancetherapper1" /><ref name="chancetherapper2" /> *[[Drake (musician)|Drake]] challenged the snub of "One Dance" in other categories following his 2017 [[Grammy Award for Best Rap Song|Grammys Award for Best Rap Song]] win for Hotline Bling, "There's pop obligations that [the Recording Academy] have," he said. "And I fluked out and got one of the biggest songs of the year, that is a pop song, and I'm proud of that. I love the rap world, and I love the rap community, but I write pop songs for a reason. I want to be like Michael Jackson."<ref name="drake1" /> ''[[Scorpion]]'' and ''[[Certified Lover Boy]]'' have featured or were inspired by Jackson posthumously.<ref name="drake2" /><ref name="drake3" /> *[[Jason Mraz]] who is known primarily for [[Acoustic guitar|acoustic songs]], says he wanted to make a dance album, "[Michael Jackson] That brilliant falsetto, the groovy songs, the harmony with his brothers. It dominated music through the 1970s and 1980s. It was how I learned to dance through his records."<ref name="jasonmraz1" /><ref name="jasonmraz2" /> *[[Kanye West]] interviewed with [[BBC Radio 1]]'s [[Zane Lowe]], "There would be no Kanye West without Michael Jackson [...] I would not be Kanye West were it not for Michael Jackson. He had to fight to get his video played because he was black."<ref name="kanyewest2" /> West has name dropped him in multiple songs, most controversially the line "[I am] The only rapper compared to Michael" for "[[I Am a God]]". West also credits Jackson for his inspiration to make his 2008 album ''[[808s & Heartbreak]]'' as he mentioned that when the pair met in New York City a year prior, West had played him "[[Good Life (Kanye West song)|Good Life]]" and said Jackson loved his singing voice.<ref name="kanyewest1" /> *[[Shawn Stockman]] of [[Boyz II Men]] said witnessing the achievements of Jackson was a massive inspiration to the [[Black people|black community]]. On June 27, 2009 in [[Atlantic City, New Jersey|Atlantic City]], they sang a tribute to him and following a playback of Billie Jean he ranted, "I have to say something about this song. This song right here revolutionized music as we know it. Here's the bug out. MTV did not want to play ([[Broadcasting|broadcast]]) this song. I remember that. For those that don't remember, there was a big controversy around this song. They didn't want to play it because it was R&B music." He also declared, "If there wasn't Michael Jackson, there would be no little dudes from South Philly like myself trying to emulate his moves [...] There would be no Boyz II Men."<ref name="shawnstockman" />
=== Overall impact === Michael Jackson has also been referenced by artists as a major influence on the music industry.
*[[Alicia Keys]] gave her thoughts, "I think Michael Jackson has influenced every performer on the face of the earth. What he really inspired me to do and influenced me to do is my best, and I feel like what he represented is quality and craftsmanship with his performance."<ref name="aliciakeys" /> *[[David Cook (singer)|David Cook]], winner of [[American Idol season 7]] highlighted Jackson, "I think the impact that Michael Jackson has on me as an artists is the same impact he's really had on everyone as an artist," says Cook. "He blew pop music wide open, you know, and made it bigger than just music."<ref name="passthetorch" /> *[[Ed Sheeran]] hailed Michael Jackson as the most influential artist of the past 25 years in an interview with [[Q (magazine)|Q Magazine]].<ref name="edsheeran" /> [[John Mayer]] wrote a ''[[Time (magazine)|Time]]'' article on Jackson's influence on himself and music as a whole, "I mean, what are the '80s? A Rubik's Cube, 3-D glasses and Michael Jackson. [...] As a musician, the man was one of the purest substances ever in music. [...] They don't want to remember that that kind of greatness is achievable because it skews the [[Normal distribution|bell curve]] completely."<ref name="johnmayer" /> [[Katy B]] described, "You can have the youngest boy who wanted to be Michael Jackson learning his dance routines, and the girliest girl [also]. I just think his music can just connect with different kinds of people. [...] I just think that's amazing."<ref name="katyb" />
== Academia == In academia, there are several courses available on Michael Jackson in universities. Librarians from [[Texas Tech University]] gathered a list of scholarly papers and peer-reviewed articles which found him referenced in psychology, medical, chemistry, mass communications and engineering journals. They gathered 100 databases information for a special issue of ''The Journal of Pan African Studies'' on Jackson, calling the breadth of his influence "truly astounding".<ref>{{Cite web |title=Librarians: Michael Jackson King of Academic Liter {{!}} Newswise |url=https://www.newswise.com/articles/librarians-michael-jackson-king-of-academic-literature |access-date=2025-01-03 |website=www.newswise.com |language=en}}</ref>

The American singer-songwriter [[Taylor Swift]] has influenced [[popular culture]] with [[Taylor Swift albums discography|her music]], artistry, [[List of Taylor Swift live performances|performances]], [[Public image of Taylor Swift|image]], [[Political impact of Taylor Swift|politics]], [[Public image of Taylor Swift#Fashion|fashion]], ideas and actions, collectively referred to as the '''Taylor Swift effect''' by publications. Debuting as a 16-year-old [[Independent record label|independent]] singer-songwriter in 2006, Swift steadily amassed fame, success, and public curiosity in her career, becoming a global [[Cultural icon|monocultural figure]].
One of the most prominent celebrities of the 21st century, Swift is recognized for her versatile musicality, [[List of songs by Taylor Swift|songwriting]] prowess, and business acuity that have inspired artists and entrepreneurs worldwide. She began in [[country music]], ventured into [[Pop music|pop]], and explored [[alternative rock]], [[indie folk]] and [[Electronic music|electronic]] styles, blurring [[music genre]] boundaries. Critics describe her as a cultural quintessence with a rare combination of chart success, critical acclaim, and [[Swifties|intense fan support]], resulting in her wide impact on and beyond the [[music industry]].
From the end of the [[album era]] to the rise of the [[Internet]], Swift drove the evolution of music distribution, perception, and consumption across the 2000s, 2010s, and 2020s, and has used [[social media]] to spotlight issues within the industry and society at large. Wielding a strong economic and political leverage, she prompted reforms to [[Recording contract|recording]], [[Music streaming service|streaming]], and [[Film distribution|distribution]] structures for greater artists' rights, increased awareness of creative ownership in terms of [[Taylor Swift masters dispute|masters]] and [[intellectual property]], and has led the [[vinyl revival]]. Her consistent commercial success is considered unprecedented by journalists, with simultaneous achievements in [[Record sales|album sales]], [[Music download|digital sales]], [[Music streaming service|streaming]], [[Radio format|airplay]], [[LP record|vinyl]] sales, [[record chart]]s, and [[Concert tour|touring]]. ''[[Bloomberg Businessweek]]'' stated Swift is "The Music Industry",<ref name="Bloomberg Businessweek-2014" /> one of her many [[Honorific nicknames in popular music|honorific sobriquets]]. ''[[Billboard (magazine)|Billboard]]'' described Swift as "an advocate, a style icon, a marketing wiz, a prolific songwriter, a pusher of visual boundaries and a record-breaking road warrior".<ref name=":14">{{Cite magazine |last=Schneider |first=Marc |date=July 24, 2023 |title=8 Ways Taylor Swift Has Changed the Music Business |url=https://www.billboard.com/lists/how-taylor-swift-changed-music-business/ |access-date=July 24, 2023 |magazine=Billboard |archive-date=July 24, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230724161024/https://www.billboard.com/lists/how-taylor-swift-changed-music-business/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Her [[The Eras Tour|Eras Tour]] (2023–2024) had [[Impact of the Eras Tour|its own impact]].
Swift is a subject of academic research, [[media studies]], and [[cultural analysis]], generally focused on concepts of [[Rockism and poptimism|poptimism]], [[feminism]], [[capitalism]], [[internet culture]], [[celebrity culture]], [[consumerism]], [[Americanism (ideology)|Americanism]], [[post-postmodernism]], and other [[Sociomusicology|sociomusicological]] phenomena. [[Academic institution]]s offer various [[Course (education)|courses]] on her. Scholars have variably attributed Swift's dominant cultural presence to her musical sensibility, [[artistic integrity]], global engagement, intergenerational appeal, public image, and marketing acumen. Several authors have used the [[adjective]] "Swiftian" to describe works reminiscent or derivative of Swift.
== Fame and stardom == [[Taylor Swift]] is one of the [[List of best-selling music artists|highest selling music artists of all time]].<ref name="Billboard-2022" /> She is the richest female musician and the first billionaire in history with music as the main source of income ({{Currency|1.6 billion}}, as of 2024).<ref name="Forbes-2023">{{Cite magazine |date=June 1, 2023 |title=2023 America's Self-Made Women Net Worth — Taylor Swift |url=https://www.forbes.com/profile/taylor-swift/ |magazine=Forbes |access-date=June 1, 2023 |archive-date=January 10, 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110110003752/http://www.forbes.com/profile/taylor-swift |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name=":23">{{Cite news |last1=Pendleton |first1=Devon |last2=Ballentine |first2=Claire |last3=Patino |first3=Marie |last4=Whiteaker |first4=Chloe |last5=Li |first5=Diana |date=October 26, 2023 |title=Taylor Swift Vaults Into Billionaire Ranks With Blockbuster Eras Tour |agency=Bloomberg News |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/graphics/2023-taylor-swift-net-worth-billionaire/ |access-date=October 26, 2023 |archive-date=October 26, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026232536/https://www.bloomberg.com/graphics/2023-taylor-swift-net-worth-billionaire/ |url-status=live }}</ref> She has released [[Taylor Swift albums discography|12 studio albums and four re-recorded albums]], supported by a number of [[Single (music)|singles]], apart from her non-album songs and collaborations.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Taylor Swift Albums and Discography |publisher=[[AllMusic]] |url=https://www.allmusic.com/artist/taylor-swift-mn0000472102/discography |access-date=May 8, 2023 |archive-date=May 14, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230514201537/https://www.allmusic.com/artist/taylor-swift-mn0000472102/discography |url-status=live }}</ref> All of her albums were commercially lucrative and positively received by music critics. ''Billboard'' noted that only a handful of artists have had Swift's trifecta of chart success, critical acclaim, and [[Swifties|fan support]], resulting in her widespread impact.<ref name="Billboard-2022">{{Cite magazine |date=March 23, 2022 |title=Taylor Swift's 40 Biggest Hot 100 Hits |url=https://www.billboard.com/lists/top-taylor-swift-songs-top-100-hits/ |magazine=Billboard |access-date=April 12, 2022 |archive-date=December 14, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221214072657/https://www.billboard.com/lists/top-taylor-swift-songs-top-100-hits/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
''[[i-D]]'' notes Swift's popularity and longevity as the kind of "ceaseless" fame and "global influence" unseen since the 20th century.<ref name="Cragg">{{Cite magazine |last=Cragg |first=Michael |title=Is Taylor Swift our last remaining real popstar? |url=https://i-d.co/article/how-did-taylor-swift-get-successful/ |magazine=i-D |access-date=December 3, 2022 |archive-date=May 6, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230506085436/https://i-d.vice.com/en/article/qjkakb/how-did-taylor-swift-get-successful |url-status=live }}</ref> To [[CNN]], Swift began the [[2010s in === Cultural presence === {{Quote box | quote = Taylor Swift is the biggest thing going in the [[entertainment industry]]. Turn on the TV or radio, scroll [[social media]], listen to talk on the street, and there she is. | author = Journalist Shirley McMarlin | source = ''[[Pittsburgh Tribune-Review]]''<ref name=":6"/> | width = 25% }} Journalists describe Swift as a cultural touchstone. ''[[The Guardian]]'' columnist [[Greg Jericho]] dubbed Swift a "cultural vitality" whose consistent popularity, accentuated by the era of [[internet]], surpassed that of [[the Rolling Stones]], [[Bob Dylan]], [[David Bowie]], [[Bruce Springsteen]] and [[U2]], who struggled to retain their cultural significance this far into their careers, whereas Swift continued to find success in the 18th year of hers with her tenth studio album, ''[[Midnights]]'' (2022). Jericho stated that only [[Drake (musician)|Drake]], [[Kanye West]] and [[Beyoncé]] could compete with Swift in terms of popularity, while deeming Swift the most popular female artist of the 21st Within [[celebrity culture]], Swift's music, life, and image are points of attention.{{sfn|Franssen|2022|p=90-92}}<ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Grady |first=Constance |date=October 12, 2023 |title=The Eras concert movie is Taylor Swift leveling up |url=https://www.vox.com/culture/23905279/taylor-swift-eras-concert-film-image-analysis |access-date=October 13, 2023 |website=Vox |archive-date=October 12, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231012224028/https://www.vox.com/culture/23905279/taylor-swift-eras-concert-film-image-analysis |url-status=live }}</ref> Swift became a [[teen idol]] upon the release of [[Taylor Swift (album)|her eponymous debut studio album]] in 2006,<ref>{{Cite news |date=November 2, 2012 |title=Taylor Swift: 'My Confidence Is Easy To Shake' |publisher=NPR |url=https://www.npr.org/2012/11/03/164186569/taylor-swift-my-confidence-is-easy-to-shake |access-date=May 20, 2021 |archive-date=April 28, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220428003156/https://www.npr.org/2012/11/03/164186569/taylor-swift-my-confidence-is-easy-to-shake |url-status=live }}{{Cite news |last=Thanki |first=Juli |date=September 24, 2015 |title=Taylor Swift: Teen idol to 'biggest pop artist in the world' |newspaper=[[The Tennessean]] |url=https://www.tennessean.com/sindtory/entertainment/music/2015/09/24/taylor-swift-teen-idol-biggest-pop-artist-world/72744548/ |access-date=May 13, 2021 }}{{Cite news |last=Yahr |first=Emily |date=June 16, 2016 |title=Taylor Swift's first song came out 10 years ago. |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2024/01/08/entertainment/taylor-swift-golden-globes-cec/index.html |access-date=January 9, 2024 |newspaper=CNN |archive-date=January 10, 2024 ''[[Newsweek]]'' remarked, "Swift has dominated the marketplace, a large portion of the cultural zeitgeist and media attention like no other artist before her".<ref name=Dunn>{{Cite magazine |last=Dunn |first=Billie |date=December 13, 2023 |title=Taylor Swift Breaks Astonishing Record |url=https://www.newsweek.com/taylor-swift-eras-tour-record-billion-dollars-highest-grossing-1851928 in time.<ref name=":31">{{Cite web |last=Chayka |first=Kyle |date=January 31, 2024 |title=America's Paranoid Taylor Swift Super Bowl MAGA Fever Dream === American symbol === [[File:YouTube Presents Taylor Swift 04.jpg|thumb|upright=0.6|Swift's early [[Girl next door|girl-next-door]] image made her "America's Sweetheart".|alt=Swift in 2011 addressing an audience while sitting]] Journalists associate Swift's fame with [[Americanism (ideology)|Americanism]]. According to Knibbs, with her second studio album ''[[Fearless (Taylor Swift album)|Fearless]]'' (2008), Swift had become a "countrified celebrity solidified into industrial-grade American fame" due to her craftsmanship.<ref name="Knibbs-2019" /> Jack Dickey of ''Time'' said, Swift became "America's most important musician" by 2014.<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Dickey |first=Jack |date=November 13, 2014 |title=The Power of Taylor Swift |url=https://time.com/3583129/power-of-taylor-swift-cover/ |access-date=May 12, 2023 |magazine=Time |archive-date=August 19, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200819085008/https://time.com/3583129/power-of-taylor-swift-cover/ |url-status=live }}</ref> ''[[Maxim (magazine)|Maxim]]'' called her career "a quintessential American success story".<ref name="Roy-2015">{{cite magazine |last=Roy |first=Jessica |date=May 18, 2015 |title=Taylor Swift Tops the 2015 Maxim Hot 100 |url=http://www.maxim.com/entertainment/taylor-swift-tops-2015-maxim-hot-100 |url-status=dead |magazine=[[Maxim (magazine)|Maxim]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201108192530/https://www.maxim.com/entertainment/taylor-swift-tops-2015-maxim-hot-100 |archive-date=November 8, 2020 |access-date=December 1, 2015}}</ref> [[Collider (website)|''Collider'']] contributor Shaina Weatherhead wrote that "Whether she likes it or not, Taylor Swift has become a pillar of the cultural [[zeitgeist]]", embodying [[love]], [[diligence]] and [[feminism]]. Weatherhead added Swift's fame turned her into a staple of [[Culture of the United States|American culture]] beyond just [[Music of the United States|American music]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Weatherhead |first=Shaina |date=April 20, 2023 |title=Taylor Swift's 'Miss Americana' Is Required Viewing Right Now |url=https://collider.com/taylor-swift-miss-americana-watch-now/ |access-date=April 24, 2023 |website=Collider |archive-date=April 23, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230423232752/https://collider.com/taylor-swift-miss-americana-watch-now/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
Journalists such as Ann Jamieson of ''[[Tampa Bay Times]],'' [[MSNBC]]'s [[Michael A. Cohen]] and [[NBC News]]' Kaetlyn Liddy have described Swift as an "American treasure",<ref>{{Cite news |last=Jamieson |first=Ann |date=November 28, 2023 |title=Why I admire Taylor Swift and you should, too |newspaper=Tampa Bay Times |url=https://www.tampabay.com/opinion/2023/11/28/why-i-admire-taylor-swift-you-should-too-letters/ |access-date=November 28, 2023 |archive-date=December 17, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231217130454/https://www.tampabay.com/opinion/2023/11/28/why-i-admire-taylor-swift-you-should-too-letters/ |url-status=live }}</ref> "the most famous and influential cultural icon" in the U.S.,<ref>{{Cite web |last=Cohen |first=Michael A. |date=December 25, 2023 |title=Taylor Swift could save Joe Biden in 2024. No, seriously. |url=https://www.msnbc.com/opinion/msnbc-opinion/taylor-swift-save-joe-biden-rcna130344 |access-date=December 25, 2023 |website=MSNBC |archive-date=December 27, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231227054047/https://www.msnbc.com/opinion/msnbc-opinion/taylor-swift-save-joe-biden-rcna130344 |url-status=live }}</ref> and "synonymous with American pop culture", respectively.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Liddy |first=Kaetlyn |date=December 27, 2023 |title=The art of studying Taylor Swift: College campuses embrace themed courses |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/pop-culture/taylor-swift-college-courses-review-rcna128236 |access-date=December 30, 2023 |newspaper=NBC News |archive-date=December 30, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231230081302/https://www.nbcnews.com/pop-culture/taylor-swift-college-courses-review-rcna128236 |url-status=live }}</ref> Writer Anna Mark felt that the Swift's hold on popular culture impacted American culture in the process.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Mark |first=Anna |title=Look What We Made Taylor Swift Do |date=January 4, 2024 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2024/01/04/opinion/taylor-swift-queer.html |access-date=January 10, 2024 |newspaper=The New York Times |archive-date=January 10, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240110015025/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/01/04/opinion/taylor-swift-queer.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In an [[op-ed]] for ''[[The Washington Post]]'', Eugene Robinson opined Swift and the [[Super Bowl]] are two of the most beloved phenomena in American culture.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Robinson |first=Eugune |title=Opinion Relax, MAGA bros — Taylor Swift is not the enemy |date=February 1, 2024 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2024/02/01/taylor-swift-outrage-nfl-kelce/ |access-date=February 2, 2024 |newspaper=The Washington Post |archive-date=April 3, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240403223252/https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2024/02/01/taylor-swift-outrage-nfl-kelce/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
''[[Vulture (website)|Vulture]]''{{'s}} Nate Jones considers Swift a cultural embodiment of [[American imperialism|American hegemony]],<ref name="Jones-2016">{{Cite web |last=Jones |first=Nate |date=July 21, 2016 |title=When Did the Media Turn Against Taylor Swift? |url=https://www.vulture.com/2016/07/when-did-the-media-turn-against-taylor-swift.html |access-date=May 8, 2023 |website=Vulture |archive-date=November 18, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211118092408/https://www.vulture.com/2016/07/when-did-the-media-turn-against-taylor-swift.html |url-status=live }}</ref> while ''[[The Wall Street Journal]]'' columnist [[Peggy Noonan]] felt Americans should be thankful to the "epic American story" of Swift's career.<ref name="ThanksTWSJ">{{Cite news |last=Noonan |first=Peggy |date=November 22, 2023 |title=We Should All Give Thanks for Taylor Swift |newspaper=The Wall Street Journal |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/we-should-all-give-thanks-for-taylor-swift-time-person-of-the-year-48d3b85a |access-date=November 24, 2023 |issn=0099-9660 |archive-date=December 23, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231223072526/https://www.wsj.com/articles/we-should-all-give-thanks-for-taylor-swift-time-person-of-the-year-48d3b85a |url-status=live }}</ref> Emily St. James of ''[[Vox (website)|Vox]]'' wrote that Swift tells the stories of American [[millennials]] through her songs in the manner Springsteen represented American [[baby boomers]].<ref name="VanDerWerff-2020">{{cite web |last=VanDerWerff |first=Emily |date=July 31, 2020 |title=Taylor Swift is the millennial Bruce Springsteen |url=https://www.vox.com/culture/2020/7/31/21340926/taylor-swift-folklore-millennial-bruce-springsteen |access-date=October 30, 2021 |website=Vox |archive-date=May 24, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220524211945/https://www.vox.com/culture/2020/7/31/21340926/taylor-swift-folklore-millennial-bruce-springsteen |url-status=live }}</ref> Writers at ''[[Financial Times]]'' described her as one of "America's most successful cultural symbols".<ref name="Politi">{{Cite news |last1=Politi |first1=James |last2=Chavez |first2=Steff |date=February 1, 2024 |title=Taylor Swift becomes a target in the US election culture wars |url=https://www.ft.com/content/8077e3cd-d45d-4eda-8133-bff297626a29 |access-date=February 1, 2024 |newspaper=[[Financial Times]] |archive-date=January 31, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240131222740/https://www.ft.com/content/8077e3cd-d45d-4eda-8133-bff297626a29 |url-status=live }}</ref> Swift also [[metaphor]]ically called herself as "Miss Americana" in her 2019 song "[[Miss Americana & the Heartbreak Prince]]",<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Willman |first=Chris |date=August 29, 2019 |title=Taylor Swift's 'Miss Americana' May Be the Great Protest Song of Our Time (Column) |url=https://variety.com/2019/music/opinion/taylor-swift-miss-americana-heartbreak-prince-opinion-1203317552/ |access-date=May 9, 2023 |magazine=Variety |archive-date=August 29, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190829031646/https://variety.com/2019/music/opinion/taylor-swift-miss-americana-heartbreak-prince-opinion-1203317552/ |url-status=live }}</ref> which also inspired the [[Miss Americana|namesake 2020 documentary]] about her life and career.<ref name="Variety2020">{{cite magazine |last=Willman |first=Chris |date=January 21, 2020 |title=Taylor Swift: No Longer 'Polite at All Costs' |url=https://variety.com/2020/music/features/taylor-swift-politics-sundance-documentary-miss-americana-1203471910/ |magazine=[[Variety (magazine)|Variety]] |access-date=January 21, 2020 |archive-date=January 21, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200121160238/https://variety.com/2020/music/features/taylor-swift-politics-sundance-documentary-miss-americana-1203471910/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
=== Tributes and honorifics === {{Further information|List of awards and nominations received by Taylor Swift}} [[File:Taylor Swift on Bloomberg Businessweek cover.jpeg|thumb|left|upright=0.6|''[[Bloomberg Businessweek]]'' proclaimed Swift "The Music Industry" on the magazine cover dated November 14, 2014.<ref name="Bloomberg Businessweek-2014">{{Cite magazine |date=November 13, 2014 |title=Cover Trail: Taylor Swift Is the Music Industry |magazine=Bloomberg Businessweek |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2014-11-12/businessweeks-taylor-swift-cover-how-we-made-it |access-date=May 12, 2023 |archive-date=August 28, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200828055521/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2014-11-12/businessweeks-taylor-swift-cover-how-we-made-it |url-status=live }}</ref>|alt=An very close shot of Swift with text reading "Bloomberg Businessweek" and "Taylor Swift Is The Music Industry".]] Swift received various [[Honorific nicknames in popular music|honorific titles and sobriquets]] recognizing her impact. "America's Sweetheart" is a title the media used for her in her early days, owing to her "all-American girl" image;<ref>{{Cite news |date=November 3, 2017 |title=Taylor Swift: The Rise Fall and Re-Invention of America's Sweetheart |newspaper=[[The Daily Telegraph]] |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/music/artists/taylor-swift-rise-fall-re-invention-americas-sweetheart/amp/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220111/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/music/artists/taylor-swift-rise-fall-re-invention-americas-sweetheart/amp/ |archive-date=January 11, 2022}}{{cbignore}}</ref><ref name="Telegraph.com">{{Cite news |date=February 7, 2010 |title=Taylor Swift: America's Sweetheart |newspaper=[[The Times]] |url=https://www.thetimes.com/sunday-times-rich-list/profile/article/taylor-swift-americas-sweetheart-l2b0fmg6x0j |access-date=November 9, 2017 |archive-date=February 3, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180203064730/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/taylor-swift-americas-sweetheart-l2b0fmg6x0j |url-status=live }}</ref> "Princess of Country" stemmed from her mainstream popularity as a country star.<ref>{{cite magazine |date=August 1, 2016 |title=Britney Spears Says She's Never Met Taylor Swift |url=https://www.teenvogue.com/story/britney-spears-taylor-swift-i-dont-know-her |access-date=November 23, 2020 |magazine=[[Teen Vogue]] |archive-date=September 18, 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160918022631/https://www.teenvogue.com/story/britney-spears-taylor-swift-i-dont-know-her |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |date=April 16, 2018 |title=Taylor Swift Makes her Move Back to Country |url=https://www.triplem.com.au/story/taylor-swift-makes-her-move-back-to-country-90113 |access-date=November 23, 2020 |publisher=[[Triple M]] |archive-date=January 1, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210101134612/https://www.triplem.com.au/story/taylor-swift-makes-her-move-back-to-country-90113 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |date=October 13, 2015 |title=Taylor Swift Tak Peduli Ocehan Orang Lain |trans-title=Taylor Swift Doesn't Care About Other People's Rattles |url=https://www.cnnindonesia.com/hiburan/20151013181711-227-84775/taylor-swift-tak-peduli-ocehan-orang-lain |access-date=November 23, 2020 |agency=[[CNN]] |language=id |archive-date=October 14, 2015 called her the "Pop Titan"<ref>{{Cite magazine |date=February 28, 2019 |title="We do not want pop to be generic": Taylor Swift opens up about her music in new essay |url=https://www.nme.com/news/music/not-want-pop-generic-taylor-swift-opens-2455126 |access-date=June 19, 2022 |magazine=NME |archive-date=April 6, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230406030003/https://www.nme.com/news/music/not-want-pop-generic-taylor-swift-opens-2455126 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=November 12, 2021 |title=Taylor Swift thanks fans as she releases re-recorded Red album |url=https://news.stv.tv/entertainment/taylor-swift-thanks-fans-as-she-releases-re-recorded-red-album |access-date=June 19, 2022 |publisher=STV News |archive-date=April 6, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230406201303/https://news.stv.tv/entertainment/taylor-swift-thanks-fans-as-she-releases-re-recorded-red-album |url-status=live }}</ref> or "Queen of Pop" due to her pop music dominance.<ref name="auto3">{{Cite magazine |date=September 3, 2019 |title=Taylor Swift is the Queen of Pop! Singer Ties Elvis Presley for 10th-Most Billboard Hot 100 Top 10s |magazine=[[People (magazine)|People]] |url=https://people.com/music/taylor-swift-ties-elvis-presley-10th-most-billboard-hot-100-lover/ |access-date=November 12, 2019 |archive-date=January 3, 2020 |access-date=November 10, 2021 |publisher=[[PopSugar]]}}</ref><ref name="TimeRodridgo">{{Cite magazine |last=Bruner |first=Raisa |date=May 24, 2021 |title=How Olivia Rodrigo Become America's Biggest New Pop Star |url=https://time.com/6050587/olivia-rodrigo-sour/ |magazine=Time |access-date=November 10, 2021 |archive-date=May 26, 2021 Swift's ability to compose well-received [[Bridge (music)|bridges]].<ref name="Nguyen" /><ref name="TimeRodridgo" /> "Queen of Easter Eggs" was coined once Swift became known for the [[Easter egg == Musicianship ==
===Country music revival=== [[File:Taylor Swift 5, 2012.jpg|thumb|upright=0.7|Swift performing "[[Mean (song)|Mean]]" on the [[Speak Now World Tour]] (2012) in [[Sydney]], Australia|alt=refer to caption]] Swift reshaped the 21st-century [[country music]] scene, and has been one of the most acclaimed country artists of her generation.<ref name="guardian country">{{cite news |last=Crummy |first=Colin |date=March 13, 2014 |title=Detwanging country music: how Nashville took the UK |url=https://www.theguardian.com/music/2014/mar/13/country-music-taylor-swift-nashville-radio-2 |access-date=September 23, 2021 |newspaper=The Guardian |archive-date=May 22, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522111223/https://www.theguardian.com/music/2014/mar/13/country-music-taylor-swift-nashville-radio-2 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Whitaker |first=Sterling |date=October 24, 2024 |title=18 Years Ago Today: Taylor Swift Releases Her Debut Album |url=https://tasteofcountry.com/taylor-swift-self-titled-debut-album/?ts24= |access-date=October 24, 2024 |website=Taste of Country |archive-date=January 22, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250122173116/https://tasteofcountry.com/taylor-swift-self-titled-debut-album/?ts24= |url-status=live }}</ref> According to Tom Roland of ''Billboard'', the country landscape is "much different today" due to Swift and her career that several critics regarded "unorthodox".<ref name="Roland-2016" /> Rosen described Swift as the first country act whose fame extended beyond the U.S. and marked internationally, as she offered "[[modernity]], [[cosmopolitanism]], [[youth]]" in a genre traditionally representing [[conservatism]], [[parochialism]] and older adults.<ref name="Rosen">{{cite magazine |last=Rosen |first=Jody |author-link=Jody Rosen |date=November 17, 2013 |title=Why Taylor Swift Is the Reigning Queen of Pop |url=https://www.vulture.com/2013/11/taylor-swift-reigning-queen-of-pop.html |url-access=limited |access-date=November 9, 2020 |magazine=[[New York (magazine)|New York]] |archive-date=November 19, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131119003714/https://www.vulture.com/2013/11/taylor-swift-reigning-queen-of-pop.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Her chart success extended to Asia and the U.K., where country music had not been popular.<ref name="guardian country" /><ref name="Rosen" /> As of February 2011, ''Fearless'' sold 400,000 copies in Hong Kong, Taiwan, Indonesia and the Philippines.<ref>{{Cite web|first=Min|last=Lee|date=February 22, 2011|title=Taylor Swift relishes Asian success|url=https://www.cleveland.com/music/2011/02/taylor_swift_relishes_asian_su.html|access-date=May 15, 2023|agency=[[Cleveland.com]]|via=Associated Press|archive-date=May 15, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230515104151/https://www.cleveland.com/music/2011/02/taylor_swift_relishes_asian_su.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Following her rise to fame, country record labels became interested in signing young singers capable of writing their own music.<ref>{{cite news |last=Tannembaum |first=Rob |date=September 12, 2014 |title=4 Ways Billboard Woman of the Year Taylor Swift Changed Country Music |magazine=Billboard |url=https://www.billboard.com/articles/news/6386008/how-billboard-woman-of-the-year-taylor-swift-changed-country-music |access-date=December 31, 2019 |archive-date=January 23, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123012548/https://www.billboard.com/articles/news/6386008/how-billboard-woman-of-the-year-taylor-swift-changed-country-music |url-status=live }}</ref> In 2008, [[Sasha Frere-Jones]] of ''The New Yorker'' called Swift a "preternaturally skilled student of established values". Frere-Jones wrote, as the [[opening act]] for [[Rascal Flatts]], Swift "[strutted] across stage platforms, performing a [[Percussion instrument|percussion]] duet on garbage cans, and switching gears without pause—her voice, all the while, light and breathy and without affectation—she returned the crowd's energy with the professionalism she has shown since the age of fourteen."<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Frere-Jones |first=Sasha |date=November 3, 2008 |title=Prodigy |url=https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2008/11/10/prodigy-pop-music-sasha-frere-jones |access-date=May 8, 2023 |magazine=The New Yorker |archive-date=October 21, 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161021165825/https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2008/11/10/prodigy-pop-music-sasha-frere-jones |url-status=live }}</ref> ''Rolling Stone'' said Swift's country music had a large impact on 2010s pop music.<ref>{{Cite magazine |last1=Martoccio |first1=Angie |last2=Spanos |first2=Brittany |last3=Blistein |first3=Jon |last4=Bernstein |first4=Jonathan |last5=Shaffer |first5=Claire |date=December 23, 2019 |title=The Biggest Influences on Pop in the 2010s |url=https://www.rollingstone.com/music/music-lists/biggest-pop-influences-on-2010s-927808/ |magazine=[[Rolling Stone]] |access-date=December 25, 2020 |archive-date=May 27, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220527053428/https://www.rollingstone.com/music/music-lists/biggest-pop-influences-on-2010s-927808/ |url-status=live }}</ref> The [[Country Music Association]] recognized Swift's "long-term positive impact on the appreciation of country music for generations to come" by awarding her with the second-ever Pinnacle Award in 2013.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Lewis |first=Randy |date=November 4, 2013 |title=Taylor Swift to receive rare Pinnacle Award at CMA Awards Nov. 6 |url=https://www.latimes.com/entertainment/music/la-xpm-2013-nov-04-la-et-ms-taylor-swift-cma-award-pinnacle-20131104-story.html |access-date=March 8, 2024 |newspaper=Los Angeles Times |archive-date=September 22, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200922124242/https://www.latimes.com/entertainment/music/la-xpm-2013-nov-04-la-et-ms-taylor-swift-cma-award-pinnacle-20131104-story.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
According to Roland, Swift insisted on writing her songs, mining inspiration from her real life. She entered country music, which has historically been "a place where adults sang grown-up songs for other adults".<ref name="Roland-2016" /> With her autobiographical songs of romance and heartbreak, Swift introduced the country genre to a relatable younger generation.<ref name="Rosen" /> Although the U.S. [[country radio]]'s target audience was between ages 25 and 54, listeners were generally limited to those older than 35 years. Various country music acts, label executives, and [[Broadcast programming|radio programmers]] have unsuccessfully attempted to lower this [[median]] age since the early 1980s, but with Swift's rise in the mid to late [[2000s in music|2000s]] the median age dropped below 25 with the genre attracting teenagers. According to programmer John Shomby, Swift "wrote for that specific age and was the first one to ever do that."<ref name="Roland-2016" /> Critic [[Gavin Edwards (writer)|Gavin Edwards]] opined in 2020 that although [[LeAnn Rimes]] achieved major commercial success as a teenage country singer in the 1990s, she could not have Swift's longevity as "most modern country radio stations are narrowly focused on women over thirty". He attributed Swift's rise to fame to "a rare blend of goofy teenager and polished saleswoman, which has let her tap into a huge market of country-loving teens".<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Edwards |first=Gavin |date=August 28, 2012 |title=2008's Country Lolita: Taylor Swift |url=https://www.rollingstone.com/music/music-news/2008s-country-lolita-taylor-swift-192759/ |access-date=May 16, 2024 |magazine=Rolling Stone |archive-date=November 29, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201129172316/https://www.rollingstone.com/music/music-news/2008s-country-lolita-taylor-swift-192759/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
